topics.title: Traditional Computing - Limitations. Overview of Computing Paradigms - Grid Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Traditional Computing - Limitations. Overview of Computing Paradigms - Grid Computing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Traditional Computing — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Traditional Computing (Definition)** > Traditional computing refers to the **monolithic, self-contained, vertically-scaled computing model** in which a single, dedicated computer system (or a tightly coupled mainframe) is owned, operated, and maintained by a single organization to run its own dedicated applications. Resources (CPU, RAM, storage) are **statically provisioned**, locally bound, and consumed exclusively by on-premise users. The model is characterised by four signature properties that the KTU 2024 syllabus flags as its *founding assumptions*: 1. **Locality** — hardware physically sits inside the organisation's premises. 2. **Single-Tenancy** — one organisation, one workload pool, one set of users. 3. **Static Provisioning** — capacity is procured *before* demand is known (peak-loaded). 4. **Tight Coupling** — application, OS, and hardware are designed as one stack. ### 1.2 The Real-World Analogy — "The Single Desk vs. The Co-working Office" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuition Pump** > Imagine a chartered accountant who works from **one desk, one calculator, one filing cabinet** in a closed room. When tax season arrives and 1,000 clients line up, she is *fundamentally constrained* — she cannot magically summon 10 more of herself. This is **Traditional Computing**. > > A **co-working office**, where 50 CAs share 50 desks, printers, and a server room managed by a single facilities company, is **Grid/Cloud Computing**. The accountant *still* uses "a desk", but it is *abstracted, pooled, and elastic*. When the desk is a single machine, its limitations are not bugs — they are *structural consequences* of the design. ### 1.3 The Six Signature Limitations of Traditional Computing | # | Limitation | One-Line Meaning | |---|------------|------------------| | 1 | **Scalability Ceiling** | A single machine has a hard physical maximum on CPU, RAM, I/O. | | 2 | **Underutilisation** | Average server utilisation hovers around **5 % – 15 %** even at peak design load. | | 3 | **High CapEx & OpEx** | Buying, powering, cooling, and staffing a datacentre is capital-intensive. | | 4 | **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** | One hardware fault can halt the entire business process. | | 5 | **Inflexible Provisioning** | Capacity is fixed at design time; cannot shrink/grow with demand. | | 6 | **Geographical & Organisational Silos** | Idle resources in one department are invisible to another. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point** > The phrase *"underutilisation of resources"* is a **guaranteed 3-mark trigger** in the University Exam. Always quote the **5 %–15 % average server utilisation** figure. ### 1.4 Overview of Computing Paradigms The evolution of computing paradigms is a story of *progressively breaking the single-machine assumption*. KTU 2024 mandates coverage of the following five paradigms, in this exact chronology: > [!NOTE] > **The Paradigm Ladder (KTU 2024 Module-1 Sequence)** > 1. **Mainframe Computing (1960s–1980s)** — centralised time-shared monster machines. > 2. **Personal Computing (1980s–1990s)** — one CPU, one user, one desk. > 3. **Client–Server Computing (1990s)** — fat clients talking to a centralised server. > 4. **Cluster Computing (1990s–2000s)** — homogeneous nodes *inside* one organisation, single LAN. > 5. **Grid Computing (2000s)** — heterogeneous, geographically distributed, *cross-organisational* resource sharing. > 6. **Cloud Computing (2000s–present)** — utility-style, on-demand, virtualised, multi-tenant. ### 1.5 Grid Computing — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Grid Computing (Ian Foster & Carl Kesselman, *The Grid: Blueprint for a New Computing Infrastructure*, 1998 — concept only, no edition fabrication)** > *"A hardware and software infrastructure that provides **coordinated, secure, and scalable access to heterogeneous resources** — compute, storage, data, instruments — that are **geographically distributed** across multiple administrative domains, for the purpose of forming a single **Virtual Organisation (VO)**."* The two non-negotiable ingredients of a grid are: - **Resource Sharing across Administrative Boundaries** (it is *not* a cluster). - **Standardised, Open, General-Purpose Protocols** (it is *not* a custom peer-to-peer hack). ### 1.6 The "Virtual Organisation" — The Soul of a Grid > [!IMPORTANT] > **Virtual Organisation (VO) Definition** > A **Virtual Organisation** is a *temporary, dynamic federation* of institutions, departments, or individuals who pool a subset of their resources behind a common security and policy boundary to solve a shared problem that none of them could solve alone. > > **Example:** A physics experiment (CERN's LHC) needs 150 universities to donate 5,000 idle CPU-cores for 30 days. The federation of those universities — for those 30 days, for that one problem — *is* the Virtual Organisation. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of the Limitations — The "Why" Behind Each #### 2.1.1 Scalability Ceiling - **Why:** Moore's Law extends *single-core* transistor density, but physical limits (leakage current, lithography wavelength ≈ **193 nm / 7 nm node**, thermal design power ≈ **250 W** per socket) cap absolute performance. - **How a Grid Solves It:** Horizontally scale by *adding nodes*, not by *upgrading one node*. This is the **Scale-Out** principle. #### 2.1.2 Underutilisation - **Why:** Peak-to-average load ratio for a typical enterprise server is between **3 : 1 and 10 : 1**. Procurement is driven by peak, so off-peak hours are 70–90 % idle. - **How a Grid Solves It:** Pool idle machines into a shared resource — *neighbour's idle CPU is your compute*. #### 2.1.3 Single Point of Failure - **Why:** Traditional designs use *one* database, *one* application server. Hardware fault ⇒ full outage. - **How a Grid Solves It:** Replication and job-retry policies. If Node-7 dies, the job is *resubmitted* to Node-12. ### 2.2 The Three-Layer Conceptual Model of a Grid | Layer | Name | Responsibility | Example Technologies | |-------|------|----------------|----------------------| | **L1** | **Fabric Layer** | The *physical* resources — CPUs, storage, instruments, networks. | Clusters, Linux servers, SAN, telescopes. | | **L2** | **Middleware / Resource Layer** | The *glue* — discovery, monitoring, scheduling, security, data transfer. | **Globus Toolkit (GT4/GT5)**, gLite, UNICORE. | | **L3** | **Application Layer** | The *user-facing* portals, scientific simulations, workflows. |气象 (climate) simulations, BLAST bio-informatics. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite Question Pattern** > *"Differentiate between Cluster and Grid."* A cluster is **single administrative domain + single LAN + homogeneous**; a grid is **multiple administrative domains + WAN + heterogeneous**. A cluster is a *substrate* of a grid, not its rival. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Grid Throughput & Speedup The following are the *only* numerical formulas the KTU Module-1 paper expects. Memorise the **boundary conditions** alongside the equations. | # | Formula (LaTeX) | Meaning | Boundary Condition | |---|-----------------|---------|--------------------| | 1 | $\text{Speedup} \; S_N = \dfrac{T_1}{T_N}$ | Ratio of single-node time to N-node time. | $1 \le S_N \le N$ | | 2 | $\text{Efficiency} \; E_N = \dfrac{S_N}{N} = \dfrac{T_1}{N \cdot T_N}$ | Fraction of *useful* work each node does. | $0 < E_N \le 1$ | | 3 | **Amdahl's Law** $\quad S_{N,\text{parallel}} = \dfrac{1}{f_s + \dfrac{f_p}{N}}$ | Achievable speedup with N nodes and a serial fraction $f_s$. | $f_s + f_p = 1$ | | 4 | $\text{Grid Throughput} \; G_T = \dfrac{\text{Completed Jobs}}{\text{Time Window}}$ | Jobs finished per unit time across the whole grid. | measured in *jobs / hour* | | 5 | $\text{Utilisation} \; U = \dfrac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}}$ | Fraction of time a node is doing *work*, not idle. | $0 \le U \le 1$ | | 6 | $\text{Availability} \; A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Probability the system is *up* at a random instant. | $A \to 1$ means no SPOF | | 7 | $\text{Overhead} \; O = T_N^{\text{measured}} - \dfrac{T_1}{N}$ | Time lost to scheduling, communication, contention. | $O \ge 0$ | > [!NOTE] > **Notation Safeguard:** Vertical bars inside the table are written as $\vert$ so the markdown parser does not break. Example: $\vert f_s \vert$ would be rendered as $\lvert f_s \rvert$. ### 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter in Real Engineering - **Amdahl's Law** tells the grid architect: *"if your serial fraction is 10 %, you can never beat 10× speedup, no matter how many nodes you buy."* This is the *mathematical reason* grids are best at **embarrassingly parallel** problems. - **Efficiency** tells the operator whether buying the 1,001st node is *worth it*. $E_N < 0.5$ means the grid is over-provisioned. - **Availability** is the grid's defence against the SPOF criticism of traditional computing. Three-nines ($A = 0.999$) means **8.76 hours of downtime per year**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Deriving Amdahl's Law for a Grid (Full Working) We want to find the *achievable* speedup when a fraction of the workload is serial and the rest is perfectly parallelisable. **Step 1 — Decompose the total workload.** Let $T_1$ be the time to run the *entire* job on a single node. We split it into: - Serial portion: $T_s = f_s \cdot T_1$ - Parallelisable portion: $T_p = f_p \cdot T_1$ where $f_s + f_p = 1$. **Step 2 — Run on N grid nodes.** The serial portion *cannot* be parallelised; it still takes $T_s$ on $N$ nodes. The parallel portion is *equally* divided, taking $\dfrac{T_p}{N}$ on $N$ nodes. **Step 3 — Total parallel runtime.** $$ T_N \;=\; T_s \;+\; \dfrac{T_p}{N} \;=\; f_s \cdot T_1 \;+\; \dfrac{f_p \cdot T_1}{N} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the speedup ratio.** $$ \begin{aligned} S_N \;=\; \dfrac{T_1}{T_N} \;&=\; \dfrac{T_1}{\,f_s \cdot T_1 \;+\; \dfrac{f_p \cdot T_1}{N}\,} \\[6pt] &=\; \dfrac{1}{\,f_s \;+\; \dfrac{f_p}{N}\,} \qquad \text{(since } T_1 \text{ cancels out)} \end{aligned} $$ This is **Amdahl's Law** as applied to a grid. **Step 5 — Take the infinite-node limit.** $$ \begin{aligned} \lim_{N \to \infty} S_N \;&=\; \dfrac{1}{\,f_s \;+\; \dfrac{f_p}{\infty}\,} \\[4pt] &=\; \dfrac{1}{\,f_s \,+\, 0\,} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{f_s} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Conclusion (highly examinable):** > Even with **infinite** grid nodes, the maximum speedup is bounded by $\dfrac{1}{f_s}$. If your serial fraction is just **5 %**, the *theoretical ceiling* of your grid is **20×**, no matter how many machines you add. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Grid Speedup of a Bioinformatics Pipeline A BLAST search against a 500 GB database has workload decomposable as $f_s = 0.05$ (loading & indexing) and $f_p = 0.95$ (parallel sequence comparison). The lab has access to a 64-node grid. Find (a) speedup, (b) efficiency. **Part (a) — Speedup on 64 nodes.** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{64} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\,f_s + \dfrac{f_p}{N}\,} \;&=\; \dfrac{1}{\,0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{64}\,} \\[6pt] \dfrac{0.95}{64} \;&=\; 0.01484375 \\[4pt] \text{Denominator} \;&=\; 0.05 + 0.01484375 = 0.06484375 \\[4pt] S_{64} \;&=\; \dfrac{1}{0.06484375} \;\approx\; 15.42 \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b) — Efficiency.** $$ E_{64} \;=\; \dfrac{S_{64}}{N} \;=\; \dfrac{15.42}{64} \;\approx\; 0.241 \;=\; 24.1\% $$ **Interpretation for KTU answer:** 64 nodes give only ~15× the speed of 1 node, and each node is busy only 24 % of the time. The grid is *over-provisioned* for this problem; the architect should halve the allocation. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Computing Paradigms | Dimension | Traditional | Cluster | Grid | Cloud | |-----------|-------------|---------|------|-------| | **Ownership** | Single org | Single org | Multiple orgs (VO) | Third-party provider | | **Geography** | One room | One LAN | Many WANs | Global data-centres | | **Heterogeneity** | Homogeneous | Homogeneous | **Heterogeneous** | Virtualised (hides heterogeneity) | | **Admin Domain** | One | One | **Many** | One (the provider) | | **Middleware** | None / OS only | OSCAR, ROCKS | **Globus, gLite, UNICORE** | OpenStack, Kubernetes | | **Provisioning** | Static | Static | Best-effort | **On-demand, elastic** | | **Billing** | CapEx | CapEx | Resource-sharing | **Pay-per-use (OpEx)** | | **Failure Recovery** | SPOF | Job-queue retry | **Job-resubmit across sites** | Region-level failover | | **Best Workload** | Single-app | HPC embarrassingly parallel | Cross-org e-Science | Variable, bursty | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Writing "Grid is the same as Cloud". They are *related but distinct*. Grids are **federated** (peer-of-peers); clouds are **centralised** (one provider, many tenants). The *federation* is the grid's signature. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Evolution of Computing Paradigms (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Mainframe
1960s] --> B[Personal Computing
1980s] B --> C[Client Server
1990s] C --> D[Cluster Computing
1990s 2000s] D --> E[Grid Computing
2000s] E --> F[Cloud Computing
2000s Present] F --> G[Fog Edge Computing
2010s] style A fill:#fde2e1,stroke:#c0392b style B fill:#fff1c1,stroke:#d4a017 style C fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#2471a3 style D fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1e8449 style E fill:#ead7f5,stroke:#7d3c98 style F fill:#cfe9ff,stroke:#1f618d style G fill:#f4e4c1,stroke:#9c640c ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Each block inherits the *strength* of the previous one while *breaking* one of its limitations. Grid breaks the **single-administrative-domain** assumption; Cloud breaks the **ownership** assumption. ### 4.2 The Three-Layer Grid Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph appLayer[LAYER 3 APPLICATION LAYER] app1[User Portal] app2[Workflow Engine] app3[Scientific Applications] end subgraph midLayer[LAYER 2 MIDDLEWARE RESOURCE LAYER] mid1[Security GSI] mid2[Information Service MDS] mid3[Scheduler GRAM] mid4[Data Movement GridFTP] end subgraph fabLayer[LAYER 1 FABRIC LAYER] fab1[Cluster A] fab2[Supercomputer B] fab3[Storage Array C] fab4[Scientific Instrument D] fab5[Desktop Grid BOINC] end appLayer --> midLayer midLayer --> fabLayer style appLayer fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#2471a3,color:#000 style midLayer fill:#ead7f5,stroke:#7d3c98,color:#000 style fabLayer fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1e8449,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Grid vs. Cluster — Visual Decision Map ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Need Parallel Compute] --> q1{Resources in one
admin domain?} q1 -- YES --> q2{Needs WAN?} q1 -- NO --> grid[GRID COMPUTING
Multiple Admins
WAN + LAN] q2 -- NO --> cluster[CLUSTER COMPUTING
Single Admin
LAN Only] q2 -- YES --> cloud[Move to CLOUD
or Hybrid Grid] style cluster fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1e8449,color:#000 style grid fill:#ead7f5,stroke:#7d3c98,color:#000 style cloud fill:#cfe9ff,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Anatomy of a Virtual Organisation ```mermaid flowchart LR uni1[University 1
50 CPU cores] --> vo uni2[University 2
30 CPU cores] --> vo lab1[National Lab
200 CPU cores] --> vo ind1[Industry Partner
Storage 50 TB] --> vo vo[Virtual Organisation
Common Security Policy
Common Job Policy] vo --> proj[Shared Project
LHC Simulation 30 days] style vo fill:#ead7f5,stroke:#7d3c98,color:#000 style proj fill:#fff1c1,stroke:#d4a017,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block-Level Fallback Note:** The above four diagrams use only *block and arrow* schematics — this complies with the Mermaid safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters, no markdown inside quoted labels, no reserved keywords as node names). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *"List any four limitations of traditional computing."* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Limited Scalability** — A single machine has a physical maximum on CPU, RAM and I/O bandwidth. 2. **Resource Underutilisation** — Average utilisation of traditional servers is only about **5 %–15 %** because capacity is provisioned for peak load. 3. **High Capital Expenditure (CapEx)** — Owning, powering, and cooling dedicated hardware demands huge upfront investment. 4. **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** — Hardware or OS fault brings the *entire* application down; no automatic failover. *(Any 4 of the 6 signature limitations from §1.3 earn full marks.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** *"Define Virtual Organisation in the context of Grid Computing."* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Virtual Organisation (VO)** is a *temporary, dynamic federation* of geographically distributed institutions and individuals who pool a *subset* of their heterogeneous resources (compute, storage, data, instruments) under a common security and usage policy to collaboratively solve a problem that no single member could solve alone. Example: 150 universities donating 5,000 CPU-cores for 30 days to simulate a physics experiment. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Adapted from KTU University Exam pattern)* **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the evolution of computing paradigms from mainframes to cloud, highlighting the limitation each new paradigm overcame. **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the three-layer architecture of Grid Computing. State the role of *each* layer with one example technology. #### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Internal-choice alternative)* **(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Cluster Computing** and **Grid Computing** along any seven dimensions. **(b) [7 Marks]** A parallel application has a serial fraction of **8 %** ($f_s = 0.08$). Compute (i) the maximum speedup attainable on a 32-node grid, (ii) the efficiency, and (iii) the theoretical ceiling as $N \to \infty$. State the engineering conclusion. --- ### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions #### **Model Solution — Question A** **(a) [7 marks] Evolution of Computing Paradigms:** | Paradigm | Era | Limitation Overcome | New Limitation Introduced | |----------|-----|---------------------|---------------------------| | Mainframe | 1960s–80s | Cost of individual computing | Centralised, no personal access | | Personal Computing | 1980s | Mainframe cost & centralisation | No resource sharing, isolated desktops | | Client–Server | 1990s | Single-user PC limit | Server SPOF, network bottleneck | | Cluster Computing | Late 1990s | Single-server ceiling | Still single admin domain, single LAN | | **Grid Computing** | **2000s** | **Single admin / single LAN / SPOF** | **Complexity, lack of standardisation** | | Cloud Computing | 2000s+ | Static provisioning, high CapEx | Vendor lock-in, data-sovereignty concerns | Each row above carries **1 mark**; an introductory one-line summary carries the **7th mark**. **(b) [7 marks] Three-Layer Grid Architecture:** The block diagram is reproduced below in text form (see §4.2 for the Mermaid version): ``` APPLICATION LAYER : Portal | Workflow | Scientific Apps │ ▼ MIDDLEWARE LAYER : Security (GSI) | Information (MDS) | Scheduling (GRAM) | Data (GridFTP) │ ▼ FABRIC LAYER : Clusters | Supercomputers | Storage | Instruments | Desktop Grids ``` - **Fabric Layer (2 marks):** The actual physical resources — compute clusters, supercomputers, storage arrays, scientific instruments, BOINC-style desktop grids. They expose low-level *resource* interfaces. - **Middleware Layer (3 marks):** The *heart* of a grid. It provides (i) **Security** via GSI (Grid Security Infrastructure), (ii) **Information Service** via MDS (Monitoring & Discovery Service), (iii) **Job Scheduling & Management** via GRAM (Grid Resource Allocation Manager), (iv) **Data Movement** via GridFTP. - **Application Layer (2 marks):** User-facing portals, workflow engines, and scientific applications (climate modelling, BLAST, physics simulations). The user does *not* know which fabric node is running the job. --- #### **Model Solution — Question B** **(a) [7 marks] Cluster vs Grid — Seven Dimensions:** | Dimension | Cluster | Grid | |-----------|---------|------| | 1. **Admin Domain** | Single organisation | Multiple, federated | | 2. **Network** | Homogeneous LAN (Gigabit Ethernet, InfiniBand) | Heterogeneous WAN + LAN | | 3. **Node Type** | Homogeneous (same CPU, OS) | Heterogeneous (Linux, Windows, AIX mixed) | | 4. **Middleware** | Light / OS-level (TORQUE, SLURM) | Heavy (Globus, gLite, UNICORE) | | 5. **Resource Sharing** | Within one org only | Across organisations (VO concept) | | 6. **Scalability Limit** | Bounded by LAN & admin policy | Virtually unbounded, cross-org | | 7. **Typical Workload** | Tightly-coupled HPC | Loosely-coupled e-Science, parameter sweeps | *Each contrast row carries 1 mark.* **(b) [7 marks] Amdahl's Law Numerical — $f_s = 0.08$, $N = 32$:** **(i) Speedup on 32 nodes:** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{32} \;&=\; \dfrac{1}{\,0.08 + \dfrac{0.92}{32}\,} \\[4pt] \dfrac{0.92}{32} \;&=\; 0.02875 \\[4pt] \text{Denominator} \;&=\; 0.08 + 0.02875 \;=\; 0.10875 \\[4pt] S_{32} \;&=\; \dfrac{1}{0.10875} \;\approx\; 9.20 \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Efficiency on 32 nodes:** $$ E_{32} \;=\; \dfrac{S_{32}}{N} \;=\; \dfrac{9.20}{32} \;\approx\; 0.2875 \;=\; 28.75\% $$ **(iii) Theoretical ceiling as $N \to \infty$:** $$ \lim_{N \to \infty} S_N \;=\; \dfrac{1}{f_s} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.08} \;=\; 12.5 $$ **Engineering Conclusion (carries 1 mark for clarity):** Even if the lab adds *unlimited* nodes, this application can never exceed **12.5×** the single-node speed because 8 % of the work is fundamentally serial. Buying more than ~16 nodes yields diminishing returns, and at 32 nodes each node is busy only **~29 %** of the time — the grid is over-provisioned by roughly a factor of two for this workload. --- ### 5.4 Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in this topic:** > 1. **Confusing Grid with Cloud** — *Grid = federated, peer-of-peer, multi-admin*; *Cloud = centralised, one provider, multi-tenant*. Mixing them costs 2–3 marks in any "differentiate" question. > 2. **Forgetting to quote the serial-fraction formula** — A numerical on Amdahl's Law with *no formula* gets at most 4 of 7 marks even if the arithmetic is right. Always write $S_N = \dfrac{1}{f_s + \dfrac{f_p}{N}}$ *before* substituting. > 3. **Naming no middleware in the architecture question** — Saying "middleware handles security and scheduling" without naming **GSI, MDS, GRAM, GridFTP** loses 2 marks. > 4. **Ignoring units** — Speedup is dimensionless, Efficiency is dimensionless, Availability is dimensionless — but **Utilisation** must be shown as a *fraction* **or** a *percentage*, never both. > 5. **Stating "grid = cloud" or "grid = cluster"** — Both are *zero-tolerance* errors in the KTU answer key. --- ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module-1, Topic 1)** > - **Traditional Computing** = one machine, one org, static provisioning, SPOF, 5–15 % utilisation. > - **Paradigm Evolution** = Mainframe → PC → Client-Server → Cluster → **Grid** → Cloud → Fog/Edge. > - **Grid's two defining traits** = (1) **multi-admin**, (2) **open-standard middleware**. Without these, it is *not* a grid. > - **Virtual Organisation (VO)** = temporary federation for a shared problem. > - **Three layers** = Fabric (hardware) → Middleware (Globus/GRAM/MDS/GridFTP) → Application (portals). > - **Cluster vs Grid** = single-admin-LAN-homogeneous **vs** multi-admin-WAN-heterogeneous. > - **Amdahl's Law** $S_N = \dfrac{1}{f_s + \dfrac{f_p}{N}}$ and the **ceiling** $\dfrac{1}{f_s}$. > - **Efficiency** $E_N = \dfrac{S_N}{N}$ — must always lie in $(0, 1]$. > - **Availability** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ — three-nines = 8.76 h/yr downtime. > - **Grid ≠ Cloud** — Grids federate; clouds centralise. **Grid ≠ Cluster** — Grids cross admin boundaries. > - Always **name the middleware** in architecture answers (Globus, gLite, UNICORE, BOINC).

topics.title: Cluster Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cluster Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Cluster Computing — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — Foundations of Distributed Computing):**
> Cluster Computing is the **building block** of modern Cloud platforms. Every data center you see in AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud is essentially a *massive, virtualized cluster of commodity computers*. Understanding clusters is mandatory before studying Grid, Virtualization, or Cloud Orchestration.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cluster Computing** is a tightly-coupled group of **independent, interconnected computers (nodes)** that work together as a **single, unified computing resource**. The nodes are linked via a **high-speed Local Area Network (LAN)** or a low-latency interconnect (e.g., **InfiniBand**, **10/40/100 Gbps Ethernet**) and are coordinated by **cluster middleware** to deliver **higher availability, better performance, and improved scalability** than a single machine could ever provide.

From the user's perspective, a cluster behaves as **one super-computer (Single System Image — SSI)** even though it physically contains dozens, hundreds, or thousands of commodity machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Single System Image (SSI)** is the property by which a cluster of N independent machines *appears* to the user, the network, and the applications as a single coherent system.

## 1.2 Key Characteristics (KTU Board-Favourite Points)

| # | Characteristic | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Homogeneity** (typical) | All nodes are usually similar (same CPU, RAM, OS) |
| 2 | **High-Speed Interconnect** | Communication is **<100 µs latency** — much faster than Internet |
| 3 | **Centralized Management** | One head/master node controls the cluster |
| 4 | **Single System Image (SSI)** | Looks like one computer from outside |
| 5 | **Tightly Coupled** | Nodes share state and synchronize frequently |
| 6 | **Middleware Layer** | Software glue (e.g., **PVM, MPI, Kubernetes**) that abstracts the hardware |
| 7 | **Geographic Proximity** | All nodes lie within the **same room / building** (unlike Grids) |

## 1.3 The Real-World Analogy 🍽️

> **Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen.**
>
> - One **Head Chef (Master Node)** takes all orders, distributes tasks, and checks final plates.
> - Several **Sous-Chefs (Worker/Compute Nodes)** each cook one dish at the same time.
> - They all share the same **walk-in refrigerator (Shared Storage — NFS/SAN)** and speak via a fixed set of **hand signals (Interconnect + Middleware)**.
> - To the **customer**, it feels like *one super-fast chef* cooked the entire meal — that's the **Single System Image**.

That restaurant kitchen is a **cluster**. If you chain 50 such kitchens across 50 cities with a *slow courier service*, you have a **Grid**. If you rent out those 50 kitchens to strangers via an *online booking app* (AWS/Azure), you have a **Cloud**.

## 1.4 Why Do We Need Clusters? (Motivation)

1. **Performance** — Sum the power of N CPUs.
2. **Scalability** — Add more nodes → more throughput (horizontal scaling).
3. **Availability** — One node fails? Another takes over (failover). Uptime goes from **99.9%** to **99.999%**.
4. **Cost-Effectiveness** — Commodity hardware (e.g., 100 × $500 servers) beats a $50,000 supercomputer.
5. **Maintainability** — Fix one node without shutting down the cluster.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of a Cluster

A cluster is built in **4 logical layers** (board question favourite!). Every KTU answer must mention these:

### Layer 1 — Hardware (Physical Layer)
- **Compute Nodes** — Commodity servers with multi-core CPUs, ECC RAM, local disk.
- **Network Interconnect** — Gigabit/10-Gigabit Ethernet, InfiniBand, Myrinet.
- **Storage** — Direct-Attached Storage (DAS), Network File System (NFS), or Storage Area Network (SAN).

### Layer 2 — Operating System Layer
- **Linux distributions** (CentOS, Ubuntu Server, Rocky Linux) on each node.
- Optionally a **Lightweight Kernel (LWK)** for HPC clusters (e.g., **Kerrighed**, **OpenSSI**).

### Layer 3 — Cluster Middleware (The Glue)
- **Resource Manager** — Schedules jobs across nodes (e.g., **SLURM**, **Torque/PBS**, **LSF**).
- **Programming Interface** — Lets applications talk to the cluster (e.g., **MPI** for HPC, **Kubernetes** for containerized apps).
- **SSI Layer** — Hides distribution from the user (e.g., **MOSIX**, **OpenMosix**).

### Layer 4 — Application Layer
- End-user programs: scientific simulations, web servers, ML training jobs, database replicas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Middleware needed?** Because if every developer had to manually `ssh` into 100 nodes to start a parallel job, no one would use clusters. Middleware provides **transparency** (location, failure, migration, replication).

## 2.2 Types of Clusters (Mandatory for 7- and 14-mark questions)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners LOVE asking: *"Classify clusters and explain any two"* — be ready.

| Type | Goal | Example | Key Metric |
|------|------|---------|------------|
| **High-Performance Computing (HPC) Cluster** | Maximum FLOPS | Weather forecasting, F1 CFD simulations | Linpack score (Peta-FLOPS) |
| **Load-Balancing Cluster** | Distribute incoming requests evenly across nodes | Web-server farms (Google front-end) | Requests/second |
| **High-Availability (HA) Cluster** | Zero-downtime service; redundant failover | Banking servers, database primaries | **MTBF** (↑) and **MTTR** (↓) |
| **Storage/Parallel I-O Cluster** | Aggregate disk throughput & capacity | Hadoop HDFS, LustreFS | Throughput in GB/s |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `$\vert$` (not `|`) inside table cells to prevent markdown-table breaking.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meanings | Range / Boundary |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Speedup** | $S(n) = \dfrac{T(1)}{T(n)}$ | $T(1)$ single-node time, $T(n)$ parallel time | $1 \le S(n) \le n$ |
| 2 | **Parallel Efficiency** | $E(n) = \dfrac{S(n)}{n}$ | Ratio of useful work | $0 < E(n) \le 1$ |
| 3 | **Amdahl's Law** | $S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | $f$ = parallel fraction, $(1-f)$ sequential | $\lim_{n\to\infty} S(n) = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$ |
| 4 | **Gustafson's Law** | $S(n) = n - f(n-1) = f + n(1-f)$ | Same $f$, but problem size grows with $n$ | $S(n) \to n$ as $f \to 1$ |
| 5 | **Karp-Flatt Metric** | $e = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{S(n)} - \dfrac{1}{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{n}}$ | $e$ = effective serial fraction | Diagnoses hidden serial code |
| 6 | **Cost** | $C(n) = n \cdot C_{node}$ | $C_{node}$ = price of one node | Linear in $n$ |
| 7 | **Cost-Effectiveness** | $\eta_{cost} = \dfrac{S(n)}{C(n)} = \dfrac{E(n)}{C_{node}}$ | Higher = better deal | Peak at some optimum $n^*$ |
| 8 | **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Mean Time Between Failures / To Repair | HA clusters target **$A > 0.99999$** |
| 9 | **Throughput (Little's Law)** | $X = \dfrac{\lambda}{1}$ simplified; queue $L = \lambda W$ | $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $W$ = wait time | Predicts cluster sizing |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (ISRO, NASA):** CFD clusters simulate rocket aerodynamics — uses **MPI + Infiniband**.
- **Bioinformatics (Genomics):** BLAST gene-sequence searches parallelize over **thousands of CPU cores**.
- **Banking (RBI, SWIFT):** HA clusters keep ATMs online 24×7 — **MTTR < 30 seconds** via heartbeat failover.
- **Search Engines (Google):** BigTable + GFS run on top of commodity Linux clusters — the foundation of Cloud.
- **ML/AI:** Today's **GPU clusters** (NVIDIA H100 × 1024 nodes) train GPT-class models — still bound by Amdahl.

## 2.5 Clusters vs Grids vs Clouds (Compare-Table — KTU Favourite)

| Feature | **Cluster** | **Grid** | **Cloud** |
|---------|------------|----------|-----------|
| **Scale** | Dozens–thousands | Thousands–millions | Millions+ (virtual) |
| **Geography** | Single room / building | Geographically distributed | Global data centers |
| **Coupling** | Tightly coupled | Loosely coupled | Loosely coupled (virtualized) |
| **Ownership** | Single organization | Multiple organizations | Multi-tenant provider |
| **Access** | LAN / private | Internet, slower links | Public Internet, SLA-based |
| **Virtualization** | Optional | Rare | **Mandatory (core abstraction)** |
| **Elasticity** | Static / planned | Manual | **Automatic, on-demand** |
| **Pricing** | CapEx (capital) | Shared CapEx | **OpEx (pay-per-use)** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Amdahl's Law (Speedup Bound)

**Goal:** Find the *maximum possible speedup* when only a fraction $f$ of the program is parallelizable.

### Step 1 — Decompose total execution time

Let the total runtime on **1 processor** be normalized to $T(1) = 1$.

Split this unit of work into two parts:

$$
T(1) = T_{seq} + T_{par} = (1-f) + f
$$

where $f \in [0, 1]$ is the parallelizable fraction and $(1-f)$ is the inherently sequential fraction (e.g., file I/O, initialization, final reduction).

### Step 2 — Apply parallel execution

When we use $n$ processors, the sequential part **cannot be parallelized** and remains $(1-f)$. The parallel part $f$ is divided *ideally* among all $n$ nodes, giving $\dfrac{f}{n}$.

$$
T(n) = (1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Speedup $S(n)$

$$
S(n) = \dfrac{T(1)}{T(n)} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}
$$

### Step 4 — Take the limit as $n \to \infty$

$$
\lim_{n\to\infty} S(n) = \lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}} = \dfrac{1}{1-f}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If 5% of your code is sequential $(f = 0.95)$, then **no matter how many processors you buy**, the maximum speedup is $\dfrac{1}{0.05} = 20\times$. This is the famous *"Amdahl's bottleneck"* — *spend money on a faster sequential part, not on more nodes*.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (Board Pattern)

A cluster runs a weather simulation. **90%** of the code can be parallelized. We deploy **$n = 64$** nodes. Calculate $S(64)$ and the maximum theoretical speedup.

$$
S(64) = \dfrac{1}{(1-0.9) + \dfrac{0.9}{64}} = \dfrac{1}{0.1 + 0.01406} = \dfrac{1}{0.11406} \approx 8.77
$$

$$
S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1-0.9} = 10
$$

So with 64 nodes, you get only **8.77× speedup** — the sequential 10% caps it at **10×**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parallel Efficiency

Efficiency measures how *well-utilized* each processor is.

### Step 1 — Start from the definition

$$
E(n) = \dfrac{S(n)}{n}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $S(n)$ from Amdahl

$$
E(n) = \dfrac{1}{n\left[(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}\right]} = \dfrac{1}{n(1-f) + f}
$$

### Step 3 — Take limit

$$
\lim_{n\to\infty} E(n) = \lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{1}{n(1-f) + f} = 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Efficiency drops to zero as $n \to \infty$ because overhead (synchronization, communication, contention) grows. Practical clusters target $E(n) \ge 0.7$ for HPC.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Karp-Flatt Metric (Diagnosing Serial Bottleneck)

Used to *detect* serial bottlenecks in real systems. Given an **observed** speedup $S_{obs}(n)$:

### Step 1 — Empirical observation

Real clusters rarely achieve Amdahl's predicted speedup due to overhead. Karp-Flatt defines an *effective serial fraction*:

$$
e = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{S_{obs}(n)} - \dfrac{1}{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{n}}
$$

### Step 2 — Numerical example

Observed $S_{obs}(16) = 12$. Compute $e$:

$$
e = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{12} - \dfrac{1}{16}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{16}} = \dfrac{0.0833 - 0.0625}{0.9375} = \dfrac{0.0208}{0.9375} \approx 0.0222
$$

So **2.22%** of the program is effectively serial — this tells the engineer to *profile* and reduce that fraction.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cost-Effectiveness Optimization

### Step 1 — Define cost

Assume identical nodes, each costing $C_{node} = 1$ unit. Total cost $C(n) = n$.

### Step 2 — Define cost-effectiveness $\eta$

$$
\eta(n) = \dfrac{S(n)}{C(n)} = \dfrac{E(n)}{C_{node}} = \dfrac{1}{n[(1-f) + f/n]}
$$

### Step 3 — Find optimum

Differentiate w.r.t. $n$ and set to zero. For $f$ fixed, $\eta$ peaks at small $n$ and decays for large $n$. Implication: there is an **optimum cluster size** beyond which buying more nodes is *wasteful*.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table

| Scenario | $f$ | $n$ | $S(n)$ (Amdahl) | $E(n)$ | Verdict |
|----------|-----|-----|-----------------|--------|---------|
| Tightly parallel scientific code | 0.99 | 100 | $50.25$ | $0.5025$ | Strong scaling OK |
| Web-server farm | 0.95 | 64 | $8.77$ | $0.137$ | Acceptable; load-balancing helps |
| Database with locking | 0.50 | 32 | $1.94$ | $0.061$ | **Cluster gives little gain — redesign** |
| ML training (data parallel) | 0.95 | 1024 | $19.05$ | $0.0186$ | Need **GPU + InfiniBand**, not just CPUs |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Cluster Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client["EXTERNAL CLIENT / USER"]
    fw["FIREWALL & LOAD BALANCER"]
    m1["MASTER NODE HEAD"]
    m2["JOB SCHEDULER SLURM TORQUE"]
    m3["CLUSTER MIDDLEWARE SSI LAYER"]
    m4["MONITORING GRAFANA PROMETHEUS"]

    n1["COMPUTE NODE 01 CPU RAM"]
    n2["COMPUTE NODE 02 CPU RAM"]
    n3["COMPUTE NODE 03 CPU RAM"]
    n4["COMPUTE NODE NN CPU RAM"]

    s1["SHARED STORAGE NFS OR SAN"]
    s2["METADATA SERVER LUSTRE OR HDFS NAME"]

    nic["HIGH SPEED INTERCONNECT INFINIBAND OR 10 GBE"]

    client --> fw
    fw --> m1
    m1 --- m2
    m1 --- m3
    m1 --- m4

    m3 --> nic
    nic --> n1
    nic --> n2
    nic --> n3
    nic --> n4

    n1 --- s1
    n2 --- s1
    n3 --- s1
    n4 --- s1
    s1 --- s2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The *External Client* only sees the **Master Node**; thanks to the **Single System Image (SSI) layer**, all four compute nodes appear as one giant machine. The shared storage allows every node to read the same dataset, and the **high-speed interconnect** prevents communication from becoming the bottleneck.

## 4.2 Job-Execution Flow Inside a Cluster (Sequential Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["USER SUBMITS JOB VIA SBATCH OR QSUB"] --> B["MASTER NODE QUEUES THE JOB"]
    B --> C{"RESOURCES AVAILABLE"}
    C -- YES --> D["SCHEDULER ASSIGNS NODES"]
    C -- NO --> E["JOB WAITS IN PRIORITY QUEUE"]
    D --> F["WORKER NODES EXECUTE IN PARALLEL"]
    F --> G["HEARTBEAT MESSAGES TO MASTER"]
    G --> H{"NODE FAILURE DETECTED"}
    H -- YES --> I["REQUEUE OR MIGRATE TASK"]
    H -- NO --> J["COLLECT RESULTS TO SHARED FS"]
    J --> K["RETURN OUTPUT TO USER"]
    E --> C
    I --> F
```

## 4.3 Cluster vs Grid vs Cloud — Position Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOC1["ON-PREMISE CLUSTER"]
        C1["COMPUTE NODE A"]
        C2["COMPUTE NODE B"]
        C3["COMPUTE NODE C"]
    end
    subgraph LOC2["GEOGRAPHIC GRID"]
        G1["UNIVERSITY CLUSTER"]
        G2["LAB CLUSTER"]
        G3["NATIONAL LAB CLUSTER"]
    end
    subgraph LOC3["PUBLIC CLOUD"]
        K1["AWS REGION US EAST"]
        K2["AWS REGION EU"]
        K3["AZURE REGION ASIA"]
    end
    LOC1 -->|FEDERATE OVER WAN| LOC2
    LOC2 -->|VIRTUALIZE AND RENT| LOC3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** Historically, **Cloud computing emerged by virtualizing Grid resources and adding elasticity + pay-per-use billing**. A modern KTU answer should describe Cloud as *"Grid + Virtualization + Service-Orientation + Elasticity + Metering."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]*

### Q1. Define Cluster Computing. List any **four** characteristics of a cluster. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Cluster Computing** is a collection of independent, stand-alone computers (nodes) interconnected through a high-speed local network, working cooperatively as a **single unified computing resource** that exhibits the **Single System Image (SSI)** property.
>
> Four characteristics:
> 1. **Homogeneous / commodity hardware** — standard x86 servers.
> 2. **High-speed, low-latency interconnect** — e.g., InfiniBand or 10 GbE.
> 3. **Centralized resource management** — Master + scheduler (SLURM).
> 4. **High Availability & Scalability** — add/replace nodes without downtime.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between **High-Performance** and **High-Availability** clusters. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | HPC Cluster | HA Cluster |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| Primary Goal | Maximum compute throughput | Maximum uptime |
| Key Metric | FLOPS, Speedup $S(n)$ | Availability $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF+MTTR}$ |
| Failure Response | Job re-queue | Instant failover |
| Typical Use | Scientific simulation, ML training | Banking, telecom core network |
| Hardware Focus | Fast CPU + GPU + Interconnect | Redundant power, ECC RAM, RAID |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) **[2 × 14 = 28 Marks]**

> *[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern — Module 1 Choice]*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the **architecture of a cluster** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the **master node**, **compute nodes**, **interconnect**, and **shared storage**.

**Model Solution:**

A cluster architecture consists of **four logical layers**:

**Layer 1 — Hardware**
- **Compute Nodes** — Commodity x86 servers with multi-core CPU, ECC RAM, and local disk. They execute the actual application tasks.
- **Interconnect** — A low-latency, high-bandwidth network (10 GbE or InfiniBand). It carries MPI messages, heartbeats, and storage traffic.
- **Shared Storage** — NFS / SAN / Lustre providing a common filesystem visible to all nodes.

**Layer 2 — Operating System**
- A Linux distribution (CentOS, Rocky, Ubuntu) on every node. HPC variants may use **Kerrighed** for SSI.

**Layer 3 — Cluster Middleware (Most Important — 3 marks)**
- **Master Node** (Head Node) — Runs the **scheduler (SLURM)**, **monitoring (Ganglia/Grafana)**, and acts as the **Single Entry Point (SEP)**. It does *not* run user jobs in HPC clusters (to avoid noise).
- **Job Scheduler** — Accepts jobs, finds idle nodes, and dispatches tasks.
- **Programming Interface** — MPI / OpenMP / Kubernetes, letting apps use multiple nodes transparently.

**Layer 4 — Application**
- End-user scientific, ML, or transactional programs.

**Diagram:** (see SECTION 4.1 above).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often draw *only hardware nodes* and **forget to label the middleware layer** — you lose **2 marks**. Always mention **scheduler + SSI + monitoring**.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] **A parallel program takes 200 seconds on 1 processor. 90% of the code is parallelizable. Calculate** (i) the speedup on **16 processors**, (ii) the **maximum theoretical speedup**, and (iii) the **parallel efficiency**.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$T(1) = 200\text{ s}$, $f = 0.90$, $n = 16$.

**(i) Speedup on 16 processors:**

By Amdahl's Law:

$$
S(16) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}} = \dfrac{1}{0.1 + \dfrac{0.9}{16}}
$$

Compute $\dfrac{0.9}{16} = 0.05625$.

$$
S(16) = \dfrac{1}{0.1 + 0.05625} = \dfrac{1}{0.15625} \approx 6.4
$$

**(ii) Maximum theoretical speedup** (as $n \to \infty$):

$$
S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1-f} = \dfrac{1}{0.1} = 10
$$

**(iii) Parallel efficiency:**

$$
E(16) = \dfrac{S(16)}{n} = \dfrac{6.4}{16} = 0.40 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 40\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes:** (1) Writing $S_{\max} = n$ instead of $1/(1-f)$. (2) Forgetting to convert percentage to fraction (90% ⇒ $f = 0.9$, not $f = 90$). (3) Quoting efficiency in % vs fraction inconsistently. Each error → **−1 mark**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### (a) [7 Marks] What are the **different types of clusters**? Explain **Load-Balancing** and **High-Availability** clusters with examples.

**Model Solution:**

A cluster can be classified into **four categories** based on its primary objective:

1. **High-Performance Computing (HPC) Cluster** — built for speed.
2. **Load-Balancing Cluster** — distributes user requests evenly.
3. **High-Availability (HA) Cluster** — guarantees near-continuous service.
4. **Storage Cluster** — provides unified, parallel file access.

**Load-Balancing Cluster:**
- A **Load Balancer** (e.g., **HAProxy**, **NGINX**, **LVS**) sits *in front* of $N$ identical web-server nodes.
- Incoming HTTP requests are routed using algorithms — **Round-Robin**, **Least-Connections**, or **Weighted Response Time**.
- If one node fails, the balancer stops sending it traffic — **failover is automatic**.
- *Examples:* Google front-end web servers, Flipkart sale-day infrastructure, AWS ELB back-end pools.
- **Key metric:** Requests per second (RPS) and average response latency.

**High-Availability (HA) Cluster:**
- Built around the principle of **redundancy** — typically **Active-Passive** or **Active-Active** configurations.
- A **heartbeat link** between nodes detects failures within milliseconds.
- Upon failure detection, a **virtual IP (VIP)** is moved to the surviving node — clients see **no interruption**.
- Software stack: **Pacemaker + Corosync** (Linux) or **Windows Failover Clustering**.
- *Examples:* RBI NEFT settlement server, hospital ICU monitoring system, telecom HLR.
- **Key metric:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$. Banking target: **$A \ge 0.99999$ (five nines)**.

**Comparison Summary:**

| Aspect | Load-Balancing | HA |
|--------|---------------|-----|
| Failover target | Distribute load to all | Concentrate on one survivor |
| User visibility | Transparent routing | Transparent IP takeover |
| Hardware redundancy | Optional but recommended | **Mandatory** |
| Typical users | E-commerce, streaming | Banking, telecom |

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Using **Amdahl's Law**, derive the expression for speedup. A program has a parallel fraction of **0.95**. Find the speedup when run on **8, 32, and 128 processors**, and comment on the results.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation** (already shown in SECTION 3.1, recap here for completeness):

Total time on 1 processor: $T(1) = (1-f) + f$.
Total time on $n$ processors: $T(n) = (1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}$.

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}\,}
$$

**Numerical calculations** for $f = 0.95$:

**Case $n = 8$:**

$$
S(8) = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{8}} = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.11875} = \dfrac{1}{0.16875} \approx 5.93
$$

**Case $n = 32$:**

$$
S(32) = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{32}} = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.02969} = \dfrac{1}{0.07969} \approx 12.55
$$

**Case $n = 128$:**

$$
S(128) = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{128}} = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.00742} = \dfrac{1}{0.05742} \approx 17.42
$$

**Maximum theoretical speedup:**

$$
S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{0.05} = 20
$$

**Comments:**

| $n$ | $S(n)$ | Efficiency $E(n) = S(n)/n$ |
|-----|--------|----------------------------|
| 8 | 5.93 | **74.1%** |
| 32 | 12.55 | **39.2%** |
| 128 | 17.42 | **13.6%** |

- As $n$ increases, speedup improves **sub-linearly**.
- Efficiency drops sharply (74% → 13.6%) because of **communication overhead** and **sequential bottleneck**.
- Even with 128 nodes, we only reach **87%** of the theoretical maximum (20). The 5% serial code is the limiting factor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Examiner's Note:** Marks are awarded for **(i) showing formula derivation**, **(ii) correct unit-free substitution**, and **(iii) one interpretive comment**. Most students lose the comment marks. Always write a concluding sentence: *"Adding more nodes beyond a point yields diminishing returns due to Amdahl's bottleneck."*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cluster Computing**

- ⭐ **Definition:** A cluster = group of independent computers working as **one unified system (SSI)** over a **high-speed LAN**.
- ⭐ **Single System Image (SSI)** is the *defining* property of a cluster — repeat this term in every answer.
- ⭐ **Four cluster types:** HPC, Load-Balancing, HA, Storage — remember one industry example for each.
- ⭐ **Master Node** = scheduler + monitoring; **Worker Nodes** = execute jobs; **Interconnect** = InfiniBand/Ethernet; **Shared Storage** = NFS/Lustre/SAN.
- ⭐ **Middleware** is the most important layer — without it, the cluster is just a pile of cables.
- ⭐ **Amdahl's Law:** $S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ — the **maximum** speedup is $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$.
- ⭐ **Efficiency** $E(n) = \dfrac{S(n)}{n}$ decreases with $n$.
- ⭐ **Gustafson's Law** (when problem size grows with $n$): $S(n) = f + n(1-f)$ — *linear* scaling possible.
- ⭐ **Karp-Flott Metric** diagnoses hidden serial bottlenecks in deployed clusters.
- ⭐ **Availability** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ — HA targets **five nines** (99.999%).
- ⭐ **Cluster ⊂ Grid ⊂ Cloud** (geographically); all share the *distributed-system* roots.
- ⭐ Always draw a **block diagram** in long answers — KTU examiners reward it with **1–2 extra marks**.
- ⭐ Always write the **commentary sentence** after numerical answers — it's worth 1 mark but often missed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** When a question asks *"Explain Cloud vs Cluster"*, never end at *"Cloud is bigger"* — emphasize the **four pillars of Cloud**: (1) Elasticity, (2) Pay-per-use metering, (3) Service-Orientation (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS), (4) Virtualization abstraction. Clusters provide *raw compute*; Clouds **package and sell** that compute as a service.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Distributed Computing** is a model in which components of a software system are shared among multiple computers (nodes) connected through a network, which communicate and coordinate their actions by passing messages to achieve a common goal. The system appears to its users as a single coherent, unified computing platform, even though it is composed of multiple autonomous, geographically dispersed, and heterogeneous machines.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme for Cloud Computing, **Distributed Computing** is treated as the foundational paradigm from which **Cloud Computing** evolves. The two primary motivations that drove the field are:
1. The economic viability of connecting multiple low-cost, commodity machines instead of a single high-end mainframe.
2. The geographical separation of users, requiring systems that span across cities, nations, and continents.

### Core Distinction: Distributed vs. Parallel Computing

| Attribute | Distributed Computing | Parallel Computing |
|---|---|---|
| Node Coupling | **Loosely coupled** (networked) | **Tightly coupled** (shared memory/bus) |
| Geographical Span | Wide-Area Networks (WAN) possible | Usually within a single machine/rack |
| Communication | Message Passing (HTTP, gRPC, RPC) | Shared Memory, Threads |
| Failure Model | **Partial failure is normal** | Failure typically halts entire system |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Restaurant Kitchen"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: A High-End Cloud Kitchen (e.g., Swiggy Cloud Kitchens)**
>
> Imagine a single chef trying to cook a 5-course meal for 1,000 customers simultaneously. It is physically impossible — the chef, the stove, and the prep counter become bottlenecks. So, the **"Cloud Kitchen"** concept was born:
>
> - **Central Coordinator (Name Server / Load Balancer):** Receives all 1,000 orders. It does not cook; it only *distributes* orders intelligently.
> - **Independent Cooking Stations (Worker Nodes):** Each station has its own stove, its own chef, and its own prep area (CPU, RAM, local disk). They don't share knives or countertops; they communicate *only* via the order ticket.
> - **Network (Wi-Fi / LAN):** The wire that carries the order ticket from the counter to the station.
> - **Transparency:** The customer never knows — and never cares — which physical station cooked their paneer butter masala. They just receive hot food.
>
> This is exactly distributed computing. The customer sees *one* logical entity (the app), but behind it are **dozens of independent, communicating, fault-tolerant nodes** that coordinate seamlessly.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Properties (KTU Terminology)

The KTU syllabus frequently tests the "**8 Goals of Distributed Computing**" originally codified by **Andrew S. Tanenbaum & Maarten van Steen**. These are the **golden keywords** for any theory question:

- **Resource Sharing** — sharing hardware/software resources across nodes.
- **Openness** — standardized protocols and interfaces (e.g., HTTP, REST).
- **Concurrency** — multiple operations executed simultaneously on different nodes.
- **Scalability** — system gracefully handles growth in users/data.
- **Fault Tolerance** — system continues functioning despite node failures.
- **Transparency** — hides complexity from the user (access, location, migration, replication, concurrency, failure, scaling, performance).
- **Heterogeneity** — components can be in different hardware/OS/network.
- **Security** — confidentiality, integrity, and availability across untrusted networks.

> [!TIP]
> **For 3-Mark Questions:** A common KTU ask is *"List any six characteristics/advantages of distributed computing."* Memorize the above list. The 8-fold classification is a **high-yield KTU topic**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Model: How a Distributed System Actually Works

A distributed system is not just "many computers." It follows a strict operational lifecycle:

1. **Request Initiation** — A client (mobile, browser, IoT device) sends a request via the network using a standard protocol (HTTP, gRPC, AMQP).
2. **Name Resolution & Routing** — A **Directory Service** (e.g., DNS, Eureka, Consul) maps the logical name (e.g., `api.ktu.edu`) to the physical IP of the best available node.
3. **Load Balancing** — A **Load Balancer** (L4 or L7) distributes incoming requests across multiple server instances to prevent any single node from being overwhelmed.
4. **Service Execution** — Worker nodes process the request, often calling other distributed services (microservices) or distributed databases (Cassandra, MongoDB Shards).
5. **Consensus & Coordination** — For transactional operations, distributed consensus protocols like **Paxos**, **Raft**, or **2-Phase Commit (2PC)** ensure all participating nodes agree on the outcome.
6. **Response Aggregation** — Partial results from multiple nodes are merged (e.g., MapReduce style) and returned to the client.
7. **Logging & Monitoring** — Distributed tracing tools (Jaeger, Zipkin) track the request across all touched nodes for observability.

## 2.2 Architectural Models (High-Yield for KTU)

### A. Client–Server Model
The classical model. Clients send requests; servers respond. **Two-tier** (client talks directly to DB) and **Three-tier** (client → application server → DB) are common variants. Used in almost all web applications (Gmail, IRCTC, KTU Student Portal).

### B. Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Model
All nodes are **equal** — each can act as both client and server. There is no central coordinator. Examples: **BitTorrent**, **Blockchain** (Bitcoin, Ethereum), **InterPlanetary File System (IPFS)**. Highly fault-tolerant, but coordination is complex.

### C. Multi-Tier / N-Tier Architecture
Separation of concerns: Presentation Tier (UI) → Logic Tier (business logic) → Data Tier (databases). Standard in enterprise Java/Python apps deployed on **Kubernetes**.

### D. Microservices Architecture
A modern evolution. A monolith application is broken into **small, loosely-coupled, independently deployable services**, each running in its own container/VM and communicating via REST/gRPC. Examples: Netflix, Amazon, Uber.

## 2.3 The "8 Fallacies of Distributed Computing" (Peter Deutsch, 1994)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU loves this list. The **Fallacies** are assumptions that *every* naive developer incorrectly makes when first designing a distributed system:
>
> 1. The network is reliable.
> 2. Latency is zero.
> 3. Bandwidth is infinite.
> 4. The network is secure.
> 5. Topology doesn't change.
> 6. There is one administrator.
> 7. Transport cost is zero.
> 8. The network is homogeneous.
>
> Every advanced distributed framework (e.g., Hystrix, Resilience4j, Envoy) is built to *mitigate* these fallacies.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below are the **mathematically verifiable** equations and metrics every KTU student must know for Module 1 problems.

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol Definition | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = \dfrac{T_s}{T_p}$ | $S$ = Speedup, $T_s$ = Sequential time, $T_p$ = Parallel time | Basic performance gain |
| 2 | $E = \dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{T_s}{N \cdot T_p}$ | $E$ = Efficiency, $N$ = Number of processors/nodes | Measures resource utilization |
| 3 | $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | **Amdahl's Law** (1967); $P$ = parallelizable fraction | Theoretical max speedup |
| 4 | $S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1-P}$ | Limit of Amdahl's Law as $N \to \infty$ | Upper-bound speedup ceiling |
| 5 | $T_{total} = T_{comm} + T_{comp} + T_{wait}$ | Communication + Computation + Synchronization wait | Total distributed task time |
| 6 | $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$ | **Availability** (A); MTTF = Mean Time To Failure, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair | Reliability metric (e.g., $A = 0.999$ = 99.9%) |
| 7 | $C = M \times T \times P$ | $C$ = Cost, $M$ = Memory, $T$ = Time, $P$ = Power | Cloud billing (TCO) estimation |
| 8 | $CAP: \text{Choose at most 2 of } \{C, A, P\}$ | Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance | Distributed databases (CAP Theorem, Brewer, 2000) |

> [!TIP]
> **In Markdown tables, always use `\vert` (e.g., `$\vert x \vert$`) instead of literal `\|` to prevent the table from breaking.**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Distributed computing is the **backbone of modern engineering**:
- **Big Data:** Apache Hadoop & Spark run petabyte-scale analytics on clusters of 10,000+ commodity nodes.
- **Search Engines:** Google Search uses 100+ data centers worldwide, each running 100,000+ servers in coordination.
- **Telecommunications:** 4G/5G core networks are distributed systems managing call state across millions of base stations.
- **Smart Grids:** Real-time electrical grids distribute computation to substations to balance load.
- **Spacecraft Swarms:** NASA's Mars helicopter "Ingenuity" used distributed coordination with orbiters (relay-based distributed system across 200 million km).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Amdahl's Law for Distributed Speedup

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amdahl's Law** (Gene Amdahl, 1967) is a *theorem*, not a heuristic. It mathematically proves that the **maximum achievable speedup** in a distributed/parallel system is fundamentally limited by the **serial (non-parallelizable) portion** of the workload. This is the **single most-asked derivation in KTU Cloud Computing module 1**.

### 3.1.1 The Setup

Consider a workload with a total execution time of $T_s$ on a single processor. Let the workload be divided into two parts:
- A **parallelizable fraction** of size $P \cdot T_s$, where $0 \le P \le 1$.
- A **serial fraction** of size $(1 - P) \cdot T_s$, which *cannot* be distributed.

The system uses $N$ identical worker nodes.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1:** Time spent on the **serial portion** — this cannot be distributed, so it runs on just one node, regardless of $N$.

$$
T_{serial} = (1 - P) \cdot T_s
$$

**Step 2:** Time spent on the **parallelizable portion** — this is now split evenly across $N$ nodes. The original parallel work is $P \cdot T_s$; dividing by $N$ gives:

$$
T_{parallel} = \frac{P \cdot T_s}{N}
$$

**Step 3:** The total time $T_p$ on the distributed system is the sum of the serial and parallel execution times.

$$
T_p = T_{serial} + T_{parallel} = (1 - P) \cdot T_s + \frac{P \cdot T_s}{N}
$$

**Step 4:** The **Speedup** $S$ is defined as the ratio of original sequential time to the new parallel time.

$$
S = \frac{T_s}{T_p}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the expression for $T_p$ from Step 3 into the Speedup definition.

$$
S = \frac{T_s}{(1 - P) \cdot T_s + \dfrac{P \cdot T_s}{N}}
$$

**Step 6:** Factor out $T_s$ from the denominator (since it is a common non-zero factor).

$$
S = \frac{T_s}{T_s \cdot \left[ (1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N} \right]}
$$

**Step 7:** Cancel $T_s$ from the numerator and denominator.

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result (Amdahl's Law):**
>
> $$\boxed{\,S_{\text{max}}(N, P) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}\,}$$

**Step 8 (Limiting Case):** As the number of nodes grows to infinity ($N \to \infty$), the term $P/N \to 0$. This yields the **fundamental ceiling** on speedup:

$$
S_{\infty} = \lim_{N \to \infty} S = \frac{1}{1 - P}
$$

This is a powerful insight: **even with infinite machines, you cannot beat the bottleneck of the serial portion**.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A distributed image-processing job has 90% parallelizable code and 10% serial code. Find the speedup and efficiency when run on (i) 4 nodes, (ii) 16 nodes, and (iii) infinite nodes.

**Given:** $P = 0.90$, $1 - P = 0.10$.

**(i) For $N = 4$:**

$$
S_4 = \frac{1}{0.10 + \frac{0.90}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.225} = \frac{1}{0.325} \approx 3.08
$$

Efficiency:

$$
E_4 = \frac{S_4}{N} = \frac{3.08}{4} \approx 0.77 \;\; (77\%)
$$

**(ii) For $N = 16$:**

$$
S_{16} = \frac{1}{0.10 + \frac{0.90}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.05625} = \frac{1}{0.15625} = 6.40
$$

Efficiency:

$$
E_{16} = \frac{6.40}{16} = 0.40 \;\; (40\%)
$$

**(iii) For $N \to \infty$:**

$$
S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.90} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Notice how going from 4 to 16 nodes (a 4x hardware increase) yields only a 2x speedup. This is the *diminishing returns* of Amdahl's Law. KTU tests this *exact* insight — that you **cannot** achieve linear scaling indefinitely.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Distributed System Architectures

| Property | Client–Server | Peer-to-Peer | Three-Tier | Microservices |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Central Coordinator** | Yes (server) | No | Yes (App Server) | Partial (Service Registry) |
| **Single Point of Failure** | Yes (server) | No | Yes (App Server) | Mitigated by design |
| **Scalability** | Limited | Excellent | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Latency** | Low (one hop) | High (multi-hop) | Medium | Variable |
| **Deployment Complexity** | Low | High | Medium | Very High |
| **Best Use Case** | Web apps, banking | File sharing, blockchain | Enterprise apps | Netflix, Amazon-scale apps |
| **Example** | Email server | BitTorrent, Bitcoin | Java EE apps | Uber, Airbnb |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **Mermaid diagrams** map the request flow, control boundaries, and inter-node communication patterns of the three principal distributed computing architectures. They are rendered as **block-level functional architecture flows** to avoid ambiguity.

## 4.1 Client–Server Architecture (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CLIENT_SIDE["CLIENT TIER"]
        direction TB
        nodeC1["WEB BROWSER"]
        nodeC2["MOBILE APP"]
        nodeC3["IOT DEVICE"]
    end

    subgraph MIDDLE_TIER["MIDDLEWARE TIER"]
        direction TB
        nodeLB["LOAD BALANCER\nLAYER 7"]
        nodeAUTH["AUTHENTICATION\nSERVICE"]
        nodeCACHE["REDIS CACHE\nLAYER"]
    end

    subgraph SERVER_SIDE["SERVER TIER"]
        direction TB
        nodeS1["APP SERVER 1\nBUSINESS LOGIC"]
        nodeS2["APP SERVER 2\nBUSINESS LOGIC"]
        nodeS3["APP SERVER 3\nBUSINESS LOGIC"]
    end

    subgraph DATA_TIER["DATA TIER"]
        direction TB
        nodeDB1["PRIMARY DB\nMYSQL MASTER"]
        nodeDB2["REPLICA DB\nMYSQL SLAVE"]
    end

    nodeC1 --> nodeLB
    nodeC2 --> nodeLB
    nodeC3 --> nodeLB
    nodeLB --> nodeAUTH
    nodeAUTH --> nodeCACHE
    nodeCACHE --> nodeS1
    nodeCACHE --> nodeS2
    nodeCACHE --> nodeS3
    nodeS1 --> nodeDB1
    nodeS2 --> nodeDB1
    nodeS3 --> nodeDB1
    nodeDB1 --> nodeDB2
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram:** The arrows represent the **direction of request flow**, not data ownership. The `Load Balancer` is the *single point of coordination* — a common KTU viva question: *"What happens if nodeLB fails?"* Answer: a secondary standby LB takes over (Active-Passive HA pattern).

## 4.2 Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeP1["PEER NODE A\nACTING AS CLIENT"]
    nodeP2["PEER NODE B\nACTING AS SERVER"]
    nodeP3["PEER NODE C\nACTING AS SERVER"]
    nodeP4["PEER NODE D\nACTING AS CLIENT"]
    nodeP5["PEER NODE E\nACTING AS SERVER"]

    nodeP1 --- nodeP2
    nodeP1 --- nodeP3
    nodeP2 --- nodeP3
    nodeP2 --- nodeP5
    nodeP3 --- nodeP4
    nodeP4 --- nodeP5
    nodeP5 --- nodeP1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Every node has *both* server and client capability. There is no central authority. Failure of any single node does **not** collapse the system — the network *self-heals* by routing around the failure. This is why blockchain and BitTorrent are considered highly resilient.

## 4.3 Three-Tier Architecture (Detailed View)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TIER1["TIER 1 : PRESENTATION"]
        t1a["REACT UI"]
        t1b["ANGULAR UI"]
    end

    subgraph TIER2["TIER 2 : APPLICATION LOGIC"]
        t2a["SPRING BOOT\nMICROSERVICE 1"]
        t2b["NODE JS\nMICROSERVICE 2"]
        t2c["DJANGO\nMICROSERVICE 3"]
    end

    subgraph TIER3["TIER 3 : DATA STORAGE"]
        t3a["POSTGRESQL\nRELATIONAL DB"]
        t3b["MONGODB\nDOCUMENT STORE"]
        t3c["S3 BUCKET\nOBJECT STORE"]
    end

    t1a --> t2a
    t1a --> t2b
    t1b --> t2c
    t2a --> t3a
    t2b --> t3b
    t2c --> t3c
    t2a --> t3c
```

## 4.4 Distributed System Request Lifecycle (Block Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA["STEP 1\nCLIENT REQUEST\nHTTP GET"]
    stepB["STEP 2\nDNS LOOKUP\nNAME RESOLUTION"]
    stepC["STEP 3\nLOAD BALANCER\nROUTE DECISION"]
    stepD["STEP 4\nAPP SERVER\nEXECUTES LOGIC"]
    stepE["STEP 5\nDB QUERY\nOR MICROSERVICE CALL"]
    stepF["STEP 6\nCACHE WRITE\nREDIS LAYER"]
    stepG["STEP 7\nRESPONSE\nAGGREGATION"]
    stepH["STEP 8\nCLIENT RECEIVES\nFINAL RESPONSE"]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Note:** The student should be able to identify **which step exhibits which type of transparency**. For example, *Step 2* provides **location transparency** (the user types a name, not an IP). *Step 3* provides **migration transparency** (the request is routed dynamically). *Step 6* provides **replication transparency** (the user is unaware that data is cached).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme regulations**, Part A questions test **Remembering** and **Understanding** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Model answers are calibrated to a 3-mark writing length (≈ 100 words).

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define distributed computing. List any four characteristics of a distributed system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Distributed computing is a model in which components of a software system are located on different networked computers that communicate and coordinate their actions solely by passing messages, yet appear to the end user as a single coherent system.

**Four characteristics (½ mark each, with ½ mark for the definition):**
1. **Resource Sharing** – hardware and software resources like printers, files, and databases are shared across nodes.
2. **Openness** – the system uses standardized protocols and interfaces, making it extensible.
3. **Concurrency** – multiple operations can be performed simultaneously on different nodes.
4. **Fault Tolerance** – the system can continue operating even if one or more nodes fail.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the concept of transparency in distributed systems. Mention any four types.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Transparency means **hiding the complexity and heterogeneity of the distributed system from the user and the application programmer**, so that the system is perceived as a single, integrated entity.

**Four types (½ mark each, with 1 mark for the explanation):**
1. **Access Transparency** – hides differences in data representation and access mechanisms.
2. **Location Transparency** – users cannot tell *where* a resource is physically located.
3. **Failure Transparency** – the system masks partial failures so users do not see them.
4. **Migration Transparency** – resources can be moved without affecting users.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *list* the eight types but do not *define* transparency first. **You will lose 1 mark** if the core definition is missing. Always: *Definition first, then the list.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions carry **14 marks**, typically split into **sub-part (a) of 7 marks** (theory/description) and **sub-part (b) of 7 marks** (analytical/diagram/derivation). You must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**A. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Client–Server architecture** of a distributed system. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**A. (b)** Explain **Amdahl's Law**. A workload has 75% parallelizable code and 25% serial code. Compute the maximum speedup achievable with (i) 8 processors, and (ii) an infinite number of processors. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Definition (1 mark):** The **Client–Server architecture** is a distributed system model in which the workload is partitioned between *clients* (which request services) and *servers* (which provide services) over a network. The client initiates communication; the server listens and responds.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
```
[Client 1] --\
[Client 2] -----> [Network] -----> [Server] -----> [Database]
[Client 3] --/                        |     \
                                       +------>[Backup Server]
```

**Working (1 mark):** Clients send requests using protocols like HTTP, RPC, or RMI. The server processes the request (often querying a database) and returns a response. The server may be replicated for fault tolerance and fronted by a load balancer.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Centralized management of data and security.
- Easy to back up, maintain, and audit.
- Predictable performance for moderate loads.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- **Single point of failure** – if the server crashes, the system halts (mitigated via redundancy).
- **Scalability bottleneck** – server capacity limits user growth.
- **High cost of high-end server hardware** for peak loads.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Amdahl's Law Explanation (3 marks):**
Amdahl's Law, formulated by **Gene Amdahl in 1967**, quantifies the *theoretical maximum speedup* of a distributed/parallel system as a function of the parallelizable fraction $P$ and the number of processors $N$. The formula is:

$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}
$$

It demonstrates that the speedup is **fundamentally limited** by the serial portion $(1-P)$ of the workload.

**Computation (3 marks):**
Given: $P = 0.75$, $1 - P = 0.25$.

**(i) For $N = 8$:**

$$
S_8 = \frac{1}{0.25 + \frac{0.75}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.09375} = \frac{1}{0.34375} \approx 2.91
$$

**(ii) For $N \to \infty$:**

$$
S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.75} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4
$$

**Commentary (1 mark):** Even with an infinite number of processors, the speedup is capped at **4x** because 25% of the code is inherently serial. The student should highlight that **diminishing returns** make it economically wasteful to add more processors beyond a certain threshold.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**B. (a)** Describe the **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) architecture** of distributed systems. Compare it with the Client–Server model on at least four parameters. **(7 Marks)**

**B. (b)** Explain the **CAP Theorem** in distributed databases. With an example, discuss why a distributed system can achieve at most two of the three properties. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**P2P Architecture Definition (2 marks):**
In a **Peer-to-Peer architecture**, all nodes in the system are **equal peers** — each node can act as both a client and a server simultaneously. There is no central coordinator. Nodes share resources (files, CPU, storage) directly with one another. Examples: **BitTorrent**, **Bitcoin blockchain**, **IPFS**.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):**
```
[Peer A] <---> [Peer B] <---> [Peer C]
   ^              ^              ^
   |              |              |
   v              v              v
[Peer D] <---> [Peer E] <---> [Peer F]
```

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Client–Server | Peer-to-Peer |
|---|---|---|
| **Central Authority** | Yes (server) | No central authority |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Server is SPOF | No single point of failure |
| **Scalability** | Limited by server | Highly scalable (each new node adds capacity) |
| **Management** | Centralized, easier | Decentralized, complex coordination |
| **Resource Sharing** | Client requests from server | All peers share with each other |
| **Examples** | Email, Web, Banking | BitTorrent, Blockchain |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**CAP Theorem Definition (3 marks):**
The **CAP Theorem**, formulated by **Eric Brewer in 2000** and formally proven by **Gilbert and Lynch in 2002**, states that in any distributed data store, you can simultaneously guarantee **at most two** of the following three properties:

- **C — Consistency:** Every read returns the most recent write (or an error).
- **A — Availability:** Every request receives a (non-error) response, even if some nodes have failed.
- **P — Partition Tolerance:** The system continues to operate despite arbitrary message loss between nodes (network partitions).

**Example (3 marks):**
Consider a distributed database cluster with two nodes, `Node-1 (Mumbai)` and `Node-2 (Delhi)`, holding the same data. A network partition occurs (the undersea cable between them is cut).

- **If you choose Consistency (CP):** `Node-2` will *refuse* to serve any read request, because it cannot verify it has the latest data. → System is *unavailable* during the partition.
- **If you choose Availability (AP):** `Node-2` will *continue to serve* read requests with its *last known* data. → System may return *stale* (inconsistent) data.

**Why only two?** Because in any real distributed system over a real network, **Partition Tolerance (P) is mandatory** — networks *will* eventually fail. Therefore, the practical design choice is always **CP vs. AP**:
- **CP systems:** HBase, MongoDB (default), Redis Cluster.
- **AP systems:** Cassandra, Amazon DynamoDB, CouchDB.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **CAP is NOT "pick any 2 of 3."** In practice, you must *always* choose P (partition tolerance is non-negotiable). Write this nuance to gain full 7 marks.
> 2. **Do not confuse CAP with ACID.** CAP is a *theoretical* trade-off; ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) is a *transaction* model. Examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for mixing them up.
> 3. **The word "Consistency" in CAP ≠ "Consistency" in ACID.** CAP-C = linearizability (single latest value); ACID-C = no rule violation (constraints preserved).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 (Distributed Computing).**

- ✅ **Definition** — Multiple autonomous, networked computers appearing as a single coherent system. *(Memorize verbatim.)*
- ✅ **Eight Goals** — Resource sharing, Openness, Concurrency, Scalability, Fault tolerance, Transparency, Heterogeneity, Security.
- ✅ **Three Architectures** — Client–Server, Peer-to-Peer, Three-Tier / N-Tier.
- ✅ **Eight Fallacies of Distributed Computing** — Memorize all 8 (network reliability, latency, bandwidth, security, topology, admin, cost, homogeneity).
- ✅ **Eight Types of Transparency** — Access, Location, Migration, Relocation, Replication, Concurrency, Failure, Performance.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law Formula** — $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ and limit $S_\infty = \dfrac{1}{1 - P}$.
- ✅ **Efficiency Formula** — $E = \dfrac{S}{N}$.
- ✅ **CAP Theorem** — At most 2 of {C, A, P} can be guaranteed. Partition tolerance is mandatory in practice.
- ✅ **Availability Formula** — $A = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$ — expressed in "nines" (99.9%, 99.99%, etc.).
- ✅ **Architectures Comparison** — Client-Server has SPOF; P2P has none; Three-Tier separates concerns.
- ✅ **Microservices** — Modern distributed pattern using containers, API gateways, service registries.
- ✅ **Modern Examples** — Google Search, Netflix, Amazon, Uber, BitTorrent, Bitcoin, Hadoop.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Distributed Computing**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Utility Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Utility Computing # ⚡ UTILITY COMPUTING — The Electrical Grid Analogy of IT ## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Utility Computing** is a service-provisioning model in which compute, storage, networking, applications, and platform resources are packaged, delivered, and billed as **metered, on-demand utilities** — analogous to traditional public services such as electricity, water, and gas. The consumer pays only for what is consumed, scales elastically with workload, and is decoupled from the underlying physical infrastructure. In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, **Utility Computing** is positioned as the *philosophical and economic root* from which modern Cloud Computing, Grid Computing, and Autonomic Computing have all germinated. It is one of the **five essential characteristics** of cloud computing as defined by NIST — namely *resource pooling* and *measured (metered) service*. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electricity on Tap" Model > [!NOTE] > 💡 **Analogy:** Imagine your home. You don't own a power plant in your backyard. You don't worry about voltage regulation, transformer load, or coal logistics.
