# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Q: List any THREE benefits of cloud computing. Explain the "Pay-as-you-go" pricing model in brief.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Three Benefits of Cloud Computing:**
1. **Cost Efficiency:** Eliminates upfront capital expenditure (CAPEX) on hardware and converts it to operational expenditure (OPEX) based on actual usage.
2. **Scalability and Elasticity:** Resources can be scaled up or down dynamically based on demand using auto-scaling mechanisms, ensuring optimal performance and cost.
3. **High Availability & Reliability:** Cloud providers offer Service Level Agreements (SLAs) with uptime guarantees (e.g., 99.99%), backed by geographically distributed data centers for disaster recovery.

**Pay-as-you-go Pricing Model (1 Mark):**
In this model, customers are billed only for the **actual resources consumed** (compute hours, storage GB, data transfer GB) over a billing cycle, similar to utility billing (electricity or water). This eliminates idle resource costs and aligns IT spending with business demand.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Q: What is "Vendor Lock-in" in cloud computing? Mention TWO strategies to mitigate it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Vendor Lock-in (1 Mark):**
Vendor lock-in is a situation where a customer becomes **dependent on a single cloud provider's proprietary technologies, APIs, and services**, making it difficult, costly, or impractical to switch to another provider without significant re-engineering efforts.

**Two Mitigation Strategies (2 Marks):**
1. **Adopt Open Standards and Portability Tools:** Use open-source technologies like **Kubernetes** (for orchestration), **Terraform** (for infrastructure-as-code), and **OpenStack** APIs to avoid proprietary lock-in.
2. **Multi-Cloud or Hybrid Cloud Strategy:** Distribute workloads across multiple cloud providers to reduce dependency on any single vendor. Use **cloud-agnostic data formats** (e.g., Parquet, JSON) and **containerization** (Docker) to ensure workload portability.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Q: (a)** Discuss the **FIVE essential characteristics of cloud computing** as defined by NIST, and explain how each delivers a measurable benefit to the user. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A startup company is evaluating migration to cloud. Their current on-premises TCO is **₹40,00,000/year** and projected cloud TCO is **₹26,00,000/year**. The cloud subscription includes auto-scaling that handles a **6x peak-to-baseline traffic ratio**.
   (i) Calculate the **TCO reduction percentage**.
   (ii) Calculate the **Elasticity Coefficient** and comment on its significance.
   (iii) If the cloud SLA is **99.9%**, calculate the **maximum annual downtime in minutes**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — NIST Five Essential Characteristics (7 Marks)

The **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** Special Publication 800-145 defines five essential characteristics of cloud computing:

1. **On-Demand Self-Service (1.4 Marks):**
   A consumer can **automatically provision** computing capabilities (server time, network storage) as needed **without requiring human interaction** with the service provider. *Benefit:* Provisioning time drops from weeks (traditional) to minutes, accelerating development and reducing IT bottlenecks.

2. **Broad Network Access (1.4 Marks):**
   Capabilities are available over the network and accessed through **standard mechanisms** (HTTP, REST APIs, mobile protocols) used by heterogeneous client platforms (laptops, smartphones, tablets). *Benefit:* Enables location-independent access, supporting global workforces and IoT devices.

3. **Resource Pooling (1.4 Marks):**
   Provider's computing resources are **pooled to serve multiple consumers** using a **multi-tenant model**, with physical and virtual resources dynamically assigned and reassigned according to consumer demand. *Benefit:* Enables **economies of scale** as the provider purchases hardware in bulk and passes cost savings to customers.

4. **Rapid Elasticity (1.4 Marks):**
   Capabilities can be **scaled out or in, often automatically**, to rapidly match demand. To the consumer, the available resources often appear **unlimited** and can be appropriated in any quantity at any time. *Benefit:* Eliminates over-provisioning; cost matches actual usage in real time.

5. **Measured Service (1.4 Marks):**
   Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging **metering at an appropriate level** (storage, processing, bandwidth, active users). *Benefit:* Provides **usage transparency** for both provider (billing accuracy) and consumer (cost optimization via FinOps).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $TCO_{on-prem} = ₹40,00,000/\text{year}$
- $TCO_{cloud} = ₹26,00,000/\text{year}$
- Elasticity ratio = 6 (peak : baseline)
- SLA = 99.9%

**(i) TCO Reduction Percentage (2.5 Marks):**

$$\Delta TCO = \dfrac{TCO_{on-prem} - TCO_{cloud}}{TCO_{on-prem}} \times 100\%$$

Substituting values:

$$\Delta TCO = \dfrac{40,00,000 - 26,00,000}{40,00,000} \times 100\%$$

$$\Delta TCO = \dfrac{14,00,000}{40,00,000} \times 100\% = 35\%$$

> **Interpretation:** A **35% TCO reduction** is well above the typical 20–40% benchmark, strongly favoring migration.

**(ii) Elasticity Coefficient (2 Marks):**

$$E = \dfrac{R_{peak}}{R_{baseline}} = 6$$

> **Significance:** An elasticity ratio of **6** means the system can scale to **6 times its baseline capacity** during peak load. This is particularly valuable for the startup if it experiences viral growth or seasonal traffic spikes, allowing seamless capacity expansion **without manual intervention**.

**(iii) Maximum Annual Downtime (2.5 Marks):**

Total minutes in a year:

$$T_{total} = 60 \times 24 \times 365 = 525,600 \text{ min}$$

Annual downtime:

$$T_{down} = (1 - 0.999) \times 525,600 = 0.001 \times 525,600 = 525.6 \text{ minutes}$$

> **Interpretation:** The cloud service is permitted to be down for a maximum of **525.6 minutes (~8.76 hours) per year**. If downtime exceeds this, the customer is entitled to **SLA credits** as compensation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the **shared responsibility model** in cloud security. With a neat diagram, describe the division of security responsibilities between the **Cloud Service Provider (CSP)** and the **customer** in **IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS** models. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss any **FOUR major challenges** of cloud computing in detail, with one **real-world example** for each. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Shared Responsibility Model (7 Marks)

The **Shared Responsibility Model** is a cloud security framework that **distributes security duties** between the Cloud Service Provider (CSP) and the customer. The CSP is always responsible for **security *of* the cloud** (infrastructure), while the customer is responsible for **security *in* the cloud** (their data, applications, and configurations).

**Division Across Service Models (1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 2.5 Marks for explanation):**

| Layer | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Applications** | Customer | Customer | CSP |
| **Data** | Customer | Customer | Customer |
| **Runtime / OS** | Customer | CSP | CSP |
| **Middleware** | Customer | CSP | CSP |
| **Virtualization** | CSP | CSP | CSP |
| **Servers / Storage** | CSP | CSP | CSP |
| **Networking** | CSP | CSP | CSP |
| **Physical Data Center** | CSP | CSP | CSP |

**IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service) — Customer Responsibility:**
Customer manages **OS, runtime, middleware, applications, and data**. CSP manages virtualization, servers, storage, and networking. *Example:* AWS EC2 — customer patches the guest OS; AWS secures the underlying hardware.

**PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service) — Customer Responsibility:**
Customer manages **applications and data only**. CSP manages runtime, OS, middleware, and infrastructure. *Example:* AWS Elastic Beanstalk — customer deploys code; AWS manages the platform stack.

**SaaS (Software-as-a-Service) — Customer Responsibility:**
Customer manages **only their data and access policies**. CSP manages everything else. *Example:* Microsoft 365 — Microsoft secures the application; customer configures user access.

> **Critical Insight:** As you move from IaaS → PaaS → SaaS, the **customer's security burden decreases** but so does **operational control**.

#### Part (b) — Four Major Challenges (7 Marks)

**1. Security and Data Breaches (1.75 Marks):**
Cloud environments are vulnerable to data breaches arising from misconfigured storage buckets, weak IAM policies, and API vulnerabilities. *Real-world example:* In 2017, **AWS S3 bucket misconfiguration** at a major credit-reporting agency exposed **147 million customer records**, including Social Security numbers, due to a publicly accessible storage bucket.

**2. Vendor Lock-in (1.75 Marks):**
Proprietary APIs, data formats, and tooling create switching costs. *Real-world example:* **Basecamp** famously migrated **3.2 petabytes of data from AWS back to on-premises** in 2019, citing vendor lock-in and rising AWS costs, but the migration took **2 years and significant engineering effort**.

**3. Compliance and Data Sovereignty (1.75 Marks):**
Storing data across borders may violate data residency laws. *Real-world example:* **Schrems II ruling (2020)** invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield, forcing companies to restructure how they transferred EU citizens' data to US-based cloud providers, requiring additional Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) and encryption.

**4. Cost Management and Bill Shock (1.75 Marks):**
Unmonitored cloud resources can accumulate runaway costs. *Real-world example:* In 2017, a company called **PCM** left an AWS test environment running, generating a **\$50,000 bill in 2 days** due to unmonitored auto-scaling. This led to the adoption of **FinOps** practices including budget alerts, resource tagging, and automated shut-down policies.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "of" and "in" the cloud** in the shared responsibility model. Use the exact phrasing: CSP secures *of*, customer secures *in*.
> 2. **Forgetting the formula for SLA downtime.** Memorize: $T_{down} = (1 - A) \times 525,600$ minutes.
> 3. **Writing generic "cloud is cheaper" statements** without numerical justification. Always compute TCO reduction percentage.
> 4. **Missing the difference between elasticity and scalability.** *Scalability* is the *ability* to scale; *elasticity* is the *automatic* scaling to match dynamic demand.
> 5. **Ignoring the "Four Nines" reference:** 99.99% = 52.56 min/year; 99.9% = 525.6 min/year; 99.95% = 262.8 min/year.
> 6. **Not linking benefits/challenges to specific service models** (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS) when asked.
> 7. **In 14-mark questions, failing to provide real-world examples** — always include at least one (e.g., Netflix, Spotify, Dropbox, Capital One) for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Benefits and Challenges**

### ✅ Core Benefits (Must Know)
- **Cost Benefit:** CAPEX → OPEX conversion; pay-per-use; economies of scale.
- **Scalability:** Vertical (scale up) and horizontal (scale out).
- **Elasticity:** Automatic scaling to match demand (auto-scaling groups).
- **High Availability:** 99.99% ("four nines") = 52.6 min downtime/year.
- **Global Reach:** Deploy in any region via a few clicks.
- **Disaster Recovery:** Built-in redundancy across geographic zones.
- **Resource Pooling:** Multi-tenancy enables shared infrastructure.
- **Measured Service:** Pay only for metered usage (CPU-hr, GB, API calls).

### ✅ Core Challenges (Must Know)
- **Security:** Data breaches, API vulnerabilities, IAM misconfigurations.
- **Privacy & Sovereignty:** GDPR, DPDP Act 2023 (India), US CLOUD Act.
- **Compliance:** HIPAA, PCI-DSS, IT Act 2000.
- **Vendor Lock-in:** Proprietary APIs; mitigate via multi-cloud, containers.
- **Performance:** Network latency, noisy neighbor, internet dependency.
- **Cost Unpredictability:** Data egress fees, idle resources, bill shock.
- **Reliability:** Single point of failure if not designed with redundancy.
- **Governance:** Limited visibility, shadow IT, provider dependency.

### ✅ Key Formulas (Numerical Questions)
- $T_{down} = (1 - A_n) \times 525,600$ minutes
- $E = R_{peak} \div R_{baseline}$
- $\Delta TCO = \dfrac{TCO_{on-prem} - TCO_{cloud}}{TCO_{on-prem}} \times 100\%$

### ✅ Key Acronyms (Board-Exam Gold)
- **NIST** — National Institute of Standards and Technology
- **CAPEX / OPEX** — Capital Expenditure / Operational Expenditure
- **SLA** — Service Level Agreement
- **IAM** — Identity and Access Management
- **CSP** — Cloud Service Provider
- **TCO** — Total Cost of Ownership
- **RTO / RPO** — Recovery Time/Point Objective
- **GDPR** — General Data Protection Regulation (EU)
- **DPDP** — Digital Personal Data Protection Act (India 2023)
- **HIPAA** — Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (US)

### ✅ Mnemonics
- **5 NIST Characteristics:** *"On Broad Resources, Rapidly Measured"* → **O**n-demand, **B**road network, **R**esource pooling, **R**apid elasticity, **M**easured service.
- **Shared Responsibility:** *"CSP secures **of**; Customer secures **in**."*
- **Challenges Acronym:** *"SPCVPRCG"* — Security, Privacy, Compliance, Vendor lock-in, Performance, Reliability, Cost, Governance.

> **Final Tip:** In KTU 14-mark answers, always structure as **Definition → Types/Categories → Diagram → Real-world Example → Conclusion** for full marks. Avoid one-liner answers; examiners reward **structured, diagram-supported, example-backed responses**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Roles and Boundaries. Cloud Delivery (Service) Models - Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Roles and Boundaries. Cloud Delivery (Service) Models - Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cloud Computing — Working Definition (NIST SP 800-145)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (NIST SP 800-145):**  
> *"Cloud computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction."*

This definition rests on **five essential characteristics** (On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, Measured service), **three service models** (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS), and **four deployment models** (Public, Private, Hybrid, Community).

---

## 1.2 Roles and Boundaries in Cloud Computing

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Roles and Boundaries" Matter:**  
> A cloud ecosystem is not a single monolithic vendor — it is an **interplay of independent actors**, each with clearly demarcated responsibilities, contracts, and liabilities. Misunderstanding these roles leads to security breaches, SLA disputes, and accountability gaps.

### 1.2.1 The Five Canonical NIST Roles

| Role | Acronym | One-Line Meaning |
|------|---------|------------------|
| **Cloud Consumer** | CC | Person/organization that *uses* cloud services |
| **Cloud Provider** | CP | Person/organization that *makes services available* |
| **Cloud Broker** | CB | Intermediary that *aggregates, integrates, customises* services from multiple CPs |
| **Cloud Carrier** | CCar | Network operator that *transports data* between CC and CP |
| **Cloud Auditor** | CA | Independent party that *evaluates* services against standards (security, privacy, performance) |

### 1.2.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cloud Airport"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (The Cloud Airport):**  
> - **You (the Passenger)** = **Cloud Consumer** — you book and fly.  
> - **The Airline (e.g., Indigo)** = **Cloud Provider** — owns the plane, crew, maintenance.  
> - **MakeMyTrip (the booking platform)** = **Cloud Broker** — sells tickets combining multiple airlines, adds insurance.  
> - **The Air Traffic Control / Airport Runway Owner** = **Cloud Carrier** — owns the *infrastructure* (the "pipes" — network, optical fibre) over which the data flies.  
> - **The DGCA Safety Auditor** = **Cloud Auditor** — independently checks whether the airline is safe.  
> 
> The **boundary** is the dotted line of the *contract* — *who is liable for what*.

### 1.2.3 The Concept of "Boundary" (Responsibility Demarcation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary = the contractually defined line of responsibility** between the Cloud Consumer and the Cloud Provider (and any intermediaries).  
> - It specifies **who manages** the OS, middleware, application, data, networking, and physical infrastructure.  
> - It is the **legal and operational demarcation** that survives even when the consumer cannot physically touch the hardware.

**Where does the boundary lie?**
- In **IaaS** → the boundary is *low* (consumer manages OS, runtime, middleware, apps; provider manages servers, storage, networking, hypervisor).
- In **PaaS** → the boundary shifts *upward* (provider also manages OS & runtime).
- In **SaaS** → the boundary sits at the *application layer* (consumer only configures the app).

---

## 1.3 Cloud Delivery (Service) Models — Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Service Model):**  
> A *service model* is a predefined packaging of cloud resources into a *consumer-ready capability*, with a corresponding responsibility boundary and SLAs.

The three canonical service models (NIST SP 800-145):

1. **IaaS — Infrastructure-as-a-Service** (raw VMs, storage, networks)
2. **PaaS — Platform-as-a-Service** (runtime + tools for developers)
3. **SaaS — Software-as-a-Service** (ready-to-use applications)

---

## 1.4 Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) — Core Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (NIST SP 800-145):**  
> *"IaaS is the capability provided to the consumer to provision processing, storage, networks, and other fundamental computing resources where the consumer is able to deploy and run arbitrary software, which can include operating systems and applications. The consumer does not manage or control the underlying cloud infrastructure but has control over operating systems, storage, and deployed applications; and possibly limited control of select networking components (e.g., host firewalls)."*

### 1.4.1 Intuitive Analogy — "Renting Empty Land"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**  
> Imagine you want to build a house but don't want to buy land outright.  
> - **IaaS = Renting a vacant plot of land with electricity, water, and sewerage connections already laid down.**  
> - You bring your own bricks, cement, architects, plumbers, painters.  
> - The **landlord (Cloud Provider)** maintains the plot, the boundary wall, the roads, and the utility connections.  
> - **You (Cloud Consumer)** decide what to build — a villa, a duplex, an office — and maintain it yourself.  
> - If you want to demolish and rebuild, you can — **elasticity**.  
> - If you want a bigger plot, you call the landlord — **scalability on demand**.

### 1.4.2 What IaaS Exposes to the Consumer

> [!NOTE]
> **IaaS gives the consumer direct control over:**
> - **Virtual Machines (VMs)** — compute capacity (CPU, RAM)
> - **Virtual Storage** — block storage, object storage, file storage
> - **Virtual Networks** — VPCs, subnets, firewalls, load balancers
> - **Operating System** (guest OS, of the consumer's choice)
> - **Middleware, Runtime, Applications, Data** (consumer-managed)
> 
> **The consumer does NOT directly control:** physical servers, switches, racks, datacenter cooling, power redundancy — these are abstracted and managed by the provider.

### 1.4.3 Key IaaS Metrics (Industry Standards)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardised IaaS Metrics (YCSO 800-145 / Cloud SLAs):**
> - **vCPU** = virtual Central Processing Unit (typically 1 vCPU = 1 hyper-thread).
> - **RAM** provisioned in **GiB** (Gibibytes, $1\,\text{GiB} = 2^{30}$ bytes).
> - **Storage** in **GB** or **TB** (1 TB = $10^{12}$ bytes for HDD marketing / $2^{40}$ bytes for GiB).
> - **Network Egress** measured in **Gbps** (Gigabits per second).
> - **SLA Uptime** typically **99.9% ("three nines")** to **99.99% ("four nines")**.

---

## 1.5 Visual Summary of the Topic

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          CLOUD COMPUTING ECOSYSTEM                   │
│                                                      │
│  Cloud Consumer ◄──┬──► Cloud Provider               │
│        ▲           │           ▲                      │
│        │           │           │                      │
│        └──── Cloud Broker ─────┘                      │
│                │                                     │
│        Cloud Carrier (network pipe)                  │
│                │                                     │
│        Cloud Auditor (independent)                   │
│                                                      │
│  ─── Boundary = contractual responsibility line ───  │
│                                                      │
│  In IaaS, the boundary sits LOW:                     │
│  Provider owns: Datacenter → Hypervisor              │
│  Consumer owns: OS → Middleware → App → Data         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Anatomy of the Five NIST Roles

### 2.1.1 Cloud Consumer (CC)

> **Definition:** The natural person or organisation that acquires and uses cloud services from a Cloud Provider through a formal contract (SLA).

**Responsibilities of CC:**
- Choose the appropriate service model, deployment model, and provider.
- Provision, configure, and use the cloud resources.
- Manage its own data, applications, identity, and access policies.
- Pay for resource consumption (typically pay-per-use).
- Ensure compliance with legal/regulatory obligations in its domain.

**Boundary obligations:** CC bears the *upstream* responsibility (what it builds *on top* of the cloud).

---

### 2.1.2 Cloud Provider (CP)

> **Definition:** The natural person, organisation, or entity responsible for making a cloud service available to interested parties (Cloud Consumers).

**Responsibilities of CP:**
- Procure and maintain the physical infrastructure (servers, storage, network fabric, datacenter facilities).
- Install, manage, and patch the **cloud infrastructure software** — this includes the hypervisor, virtualisation layer, management plane, and APIs.
- Provide *service interfaces* (web console, CLI, RESTful APIs) to expose resources to consumers.
- Define, advertise, and honour **SLAs** (Service Level Agreements).
- Implement multi-tenancy, isolation, billing, and metering.

**Boundary obligations:** CP bears the *downstream* responsibility (the raw substrate).

---

### 2.1.3 Cloud Broker (CB)

> **Definition:** An entity that manages the use, performance, and delivery of cloud services, and negotiates relationships between CPs and CCs.

**Why brokers exist:**
- **Aggregation** — combining multiple CPs (e.g., multi-cloud storage).
- **Arbitrage** — selecting the cheapest/best CP dynamically.
- **Intermediation** — adding value (e.g., security wrappers, performance enhancements).

> **Example:** A startup that offers "single-pane-of-glass" management over AWS, Azure, and GCP is acting as a Cloud Broker.

**Boundary obligations:** CB is liable for the *integration* and *value-add* layer.

---

### 2.1.4 Cloud Carrier (CCar)

> **Definition:** The intermediary that provides connectivity and transport of cloud services between CPs and CCs (typically a telecom or ISP).

**Responsibilities of CCar:**
- Provide *network bandwidth* and ensure *route availability* between consumer and provider.
- May offer *guaranteed bandwidth* or *best-effort* services.
- SLAs of CCar constrain the SLAs of CP and CC.

**Boundary obligations:** CCar owns the *data in transit* between consumer and provider datacenters.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The Carrier–Provider distinction is subtle. The **Provider** owns the *compute/storage fabric*; the **Carrier** owns the *long-haul data path*.

---

### 2.1.5 Cloud Auditor (CA)

> **Definition:** A party that can conduct independent assessment of cloud services, information system operations, performance, and security of a cloud implementation.

**Auditor evaluations typically cover:**
- **Security audits** — controls, encryption, vulnerability assessments.
- **Privacy audits** — GDPR, HIPAA, DPDP Act compliance.
- **Performance audits** — SLA compliance, latency, availability.

**Boundary obligations:** CA is a *third-party verifier* — not directly in the data path.

---

## 2.2 Boundary Demarcation — The Responsibility Stack

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Onion" of Cloud Responsibility (IaaS context):**

| Layer | Component | Owner in IaaS |
|-------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 (top) | **Data** | **Consumer** |
| 2 | **Application** | **Consumer** |
| 3 | **Middleware** | **Consumer** |
| 4 | **Guest OS** | **Consumer** |
| 5 | **Virtualisation / Hypervisor** | **Provider** |
| 6 | **Servers, Storage, Network Hardware** | **Provider** |
| 7 (bottom) | **Physical Datacenter (power, cooling, racks)** | **Provider** |

> The **boundary line** falls *between layers 4 and 5* in IaaS.

---

## 2.3 IaaS — Core Characteristics (NIST-Aligned)

1. **Resource Pooling** — multiple consumers share the same physical infrastructure, isolated via virtualisation.
2. **Broad Network Access** — services accessed over standard protocols (HTTP, SSH, RDP) from any device.
3. **Rapid Elasticity** — capacity scales out/in elastically with demand.
4. **Measured Service** — usage is metered; consumer pays for what is consumed.
5. **On-Demand Self-Service** — consumer provisions VMs, storage, networks *without human interaction* with the provider.

---

## 2.4 IaaS Service Components — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.4.1 Compute
- **Virtual Machines** (AWS EC2, Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine, DigitalOcean Droplets).
- **Bare-Metal Instances** (no hypervisor layer — dedicated hardware).
- **Auto-Scaling Groups** — elastic horizontal scaling.
- **Container-as-a-Service (CaaS)** — a *variant* of IaaS that deploys containers (e.g., AWS ECS, Azure Container Instances).

### 2.4.2 Storage
- **Block Storage** — raw volumes attached to VMs (e.g., AWS EBS — Elastic Block Store).
- **Object Storage** — flat namespace, accessed over HTTP (e.g., AWS S3, Azure Blob).
- **File Storage** — POSIX-compliant shared file systems (e.g., AWS EFS, Azure Files).
- **Archival Storage** — cold/cool tiers (e.g., AWS Glacier, Azure Archive).

### 2.4.3 Networking
- **Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)** — logically isolated network.
- **Subnets, Route Tables, NAT Gateways**.
- **Load Balancers** (L4: Network LB, L7: Application LB).
- **Virtual Firewalls / Security Groups**.
- **VPNs and Direct Connect / ExpressRoute** for hybrid connectivity.

### 2.4.4 Supporting Services (often grouped with IaaS)
- **Identity & Access Management (IAM)** — AWS IAM, Azure AD.
- **Key Management Service (KMS)** — encryption key handling.
- **Monitoring & Logging** — AWS CloudWatch, Azure Monitor.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **This topic is largely conceptual, but the following quantitative formulas appear in KTU numericals and short-answer questions.**

### 2.5.1 Cost Calculation Formulas

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i} (R_i \times P_i \times T_i)$ | Total cost = sum of (Resource units × Price per unit × Time used) |
| 2 | $C_{\text{monthly}} = N_{\text{vm}} \times P_{\text{vCPU-hr}} \times 24 \times 30$ | Monthly VM cost assuming continuous running |
| 3 | $C_{\text{reserved}} = C_{\text{upfront}} + N_{\text{hr}} \times P_{\text{hr}}$ | Reserved-instance pricing |
| 4 | $C_{\text{spot}} = P_{\text{spot}} \times T_{\text{used}}$ | Spot/preemptible instance cost |

### 2.5.2 SLA & Availability Formulas

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 5 | $A = \dfrac{U_{\text{time}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Availability percentage |
| 6 | $D_{\text{down}} = T_{\text{total}} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{A}{100}\right)$ | Allowed downtime in a period |
| 7 | $\text{Nines} = 1 - 10^{-n}$ | "n nines" availability → $n=3 \Rightarrow 99.9\%$ |

### 2.5.3 Storage & Throughput Formulas

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 8 | $T_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{bytes}}}{R_{\text{net}} \times \text{eff}}$ | Data transfer time (Bytes ÷ (Network rate × efficiency)) |
| 9 | $\text{IOPS} = \dfrac{N_{\text{op}}}{t}$ | Input/Output Operations Per Second |
| 10 | $L_{\text{latency}} = L_{\text{prop}} + L_{\text{trans}} + L_{\text{queue}}$ | Total latency = propagation + transmission + queuing |

> **Important:** All time units must be consistent (hours, days, months). Always convert GiB ↔ GB explicitly in KTU problems.

### 2.5.4 Resource Pooling & Tenancy

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 11 | $U_{\text{util}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{used}}}{R_{\text{allocated}}} \times 100\%$ | Resource utilisation ratio |
| 12 | $E_{\text{overhead}} = R_{\text{phy}} - R_{\text{alloc}}$ | Hypervisor overhead (Physical − Allocatable) |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of IaaS

| Industry Vertical | IaaS Use-Case |
|-------------------|---------------|
| **Startups** | Quick launch of web apps without CapEx on datacenters. |
| **AI / ML** | On-demand GPU/TPU clusters for training (AWS p4d, Azure ND A100). |
| **Big Data / Analytics** | Elastic Hadoop/Spark clusters that scale with the dataset. |
| **Disaster Recovery** | IaaS as a low-cost DR site (e.g., pilot-light in AWS). |
| **Dev/Test Environments** | Spin up/tear down ephemeral test environments. |
| **E-Commerce** | Auto-scaling during festive sales (Diwali, Black Friday). |

---

## 2.7 When to Choose IaaS (vs PaaS vs SaaS) — Decision Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Choose IaaS if:**
> - You need **full control** over the OS, runtime, and middleware.
> - You have **legacy applications** that cannot be containerised easily.
> - You have **compliance constraints** requiring custom security configurations.
> - You want to **optimise costs** using spot/reserved instances.
> - You are **migrating a lift-and-shift** workload from an on-premises datacenter.

> **Avoid IaaS if:**
> - Your application is purely web-based and stateless → **PaaS** is better.
> - You only need standard office tools → **SaaS** is better.
> - Your team lacks sysadmin expertise.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Roles to Service Models

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU question**. You may be asked to fill in a similar table in the exam.

| Layer / Responsibility | IaaS (Owner) | PaaS (Owner) | SaaS (Owner) |
|------------------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Data** | Consumer | Consumer | Consumer |
| **Application** | Consumer | Consumer | Provider |
| **Middleware** | Consumer | Provider | Provider |
| **Runtime / OS** | Consumer | Provider | Provider |
| **Virtualisation** | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| **Servers, Storage, Network** | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| **Physical Datacenter** | Provider | Provider | Provider |

> **Boundary line shifts upward** as we move from IaaS → PaaS → SaaS.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Cost of an IaaS Deployment

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical (KTU-style):**
> *A startup provisions 10 VMs in AWS EC2, each with 2 vCPUs and 4 GiB RAM. The on-demand price is ₹5 per vCPU-hour. The VMs run 24×7 for 30 days. Storage is 100 GB per VM at ₹2/GB-month. Outbound data transfer is 2 TB per VM at ₹5/GB. Calculate the monthly bill.*

### Step 1 — Compute Cost
Number of VMs = $N_{\text{vm}} = 10$  
vCPUs per VM = $n_{\text{cpu}} = 2$  
Hours in a month = $T = 24 \times 30 = 720\,\text{hours}$  
Price per vCPU-hour = $P_{\text{cpu}} = ₹5$

$$
C_{\text{compute}} = N_{\text{vm}} \times n_{\text{cpu}} \times P_{\text{cpu}} \times T
$$

Substituting:

$$
C_{\text{compute}} = 10 \times 2 \times 5 \times 720 = 72{,}000\,\text{₹}
$$

### Step 2 — Storage Cost
Storage per VM = $S_{\text{vm}} = 100\,\text{GB}$  
Price per GB-month = $P_{\text{st}} = ₹2$

$$
C_{\text{storage}} = N_{\text{vm}} \times S_{\text{vm}} \times P_{\text{st}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{storage}} = 10 \times 100 \times 2 = 2{,}000\,\text{₹}
$$

### Step 3 — Egress (Data Transfer) Cost
Data per VM = $D_{\text{vm}} = 2\,\text{TB} = 2 \times 1024 = 2048\,\text{GB}$  
Price per GB egress = $P_{\text{e}} = ₹5$

$$
C_{\text{egress}} = N_{\text{vm}} \times D_{\text{vm}} \times P_{\text{e}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{egress}} = 10 \times 2048 \times 5 = 102{,}400\,\text{₹}
$$

### Step 4 — Total Monthly Bill

$$
C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{compute}} + C_{\text{storage}} + C_{\text{egress}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 72{,}000 + 2{,}000 + 102{,}400 = 176{,}400\,\text{₹}
$$

> **Insight:** *Egress dominates the bill* — this is a common KTU exam takeaway: **egress (data leaving the cloud) is often the largest IaaS cost component**.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — SLA Downtime Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical:**  
> *An IaaS provider advertises 99.95% monthly uptime SLA. Calculate the maximum allowed downtime per month (30 days) and per year.*

### Step 1 — Convert percentage to fraction
Availability = $A = 99.95\% = 0.9995$

### Step 2 — Downtime fraction

$$
D_{\text{frac}} = 1 - A = 1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005
$$

### Step 3 — Total time in a month
$T_{\text{month}} = 30\,\text{days} \times 24 \times 60 = 43{,}200\,\text{minutes}$

### Step 4 — Allowed downtime per month

$$
D_{\text{month}} = T_{\text{month}} \times D_{\text{frac}}
$$

$$
D_{\text{month}} = 43{,}200 \times 0.0005 = 21.6\,\text{minutes/month}
$$

### Step 5 — Per year
$T_{\text{year}} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600\,\text{minutes}$

$$
D_{\text{year}} = 525{,}600 \times 0.0005 = 262.8\,\text{minutes/year} \approx 4.38\,\text{hours/year}
$$

> **Insight:** *99.95% sounds impressive but allows about 4.4 hours of downtime per year — a real KTU pitfall!*

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation — Storage Conversion (GB vs GiB)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical:**  
> *An IaaS provider quotes 500 GB of block storage. Convert to GiB.*

### Step 1 — Recall the definitions
- $1\,\text{GB} = 10^9\,\text{bytes}$ (decimal / SI)
- $1\,\text{GiB} = 2^{30}\,\text{bytes}$ (binary / IEC)

### Step 2 — Conversion

$$
S_{\text{GiB}} = \dfrac{S_{\text{GB}} \times 10^9}{2^{30}}
$$

$$
S_{\text{GiB}} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^9}{1{,}073{,}741{,}824} \approx 465.66\,\text{GiB}
$$

> **Insight:** *The provider's "500 GB" is actually only ~465.66 GiB as the OS sees it — a famous KTU exam "gotcha"*.

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation — Network Transfer Time

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical:**  
> *Transfer a 10 GB file over a 1 Gbps link with 80% efficiency. Find the transfer time.*

### Step 1 — Convert file size to bits
$D = 10\,\text{GB} = 10 \times 8 \times 10^9 = 8 \times 10^{10}\,\text{bits}$

### Step 2 — Effective bandwidth
$R_{\text{eff}} = 1\,\text{Gbps} \times 0.80 = 0.8 \times 10^9\,\text{bits/s}$

### Step 3 — Time

$$
T = \dfrac{D}{R_{\text{eff}}} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{10}}{0.8 \times 10^9} = 100\,\text{seconds}
$$

> **Insight:** *Effective throughput is always lower than the nominal bandwidth — account for protocol overhead (TCP, encryption).*

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS (Side-by-Side)

| Criterion | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|-----------|------|------|------|
| **Consumer manages** | OS → Data | App → Data | Only Data & usage config |
| **Provider manages** | Hardware → Hypervisor | Hardware → Runtime | Everything below app |
| **Flexibility** | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| **Time to deploy** | Hours–Days | Minutes–Hours | Instant |
| **Skill required** | SysAdmin + DevOps | Developer | End-user |
| **Cost model** | Pay per VM/Storage/Egress | Pay per app/runtime | Per-user / per-month |
| **Example** | AWS EC2, Azure VMs, GCE | AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku, Google App Engine | Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce |
| **Best for** | Lift-and-shift, custom infra | App development, CI/CD | End-user productivity |

---

## 3.7 Comparative Responsibility Allocation Matrix (Roles × Layers)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a typical 14-mark KTU question** — be ready to draw and explain.

| Layer | Cloud Consumer | Cloud Provider | Cloud Broker | Cloud Carrier | Cloud Auditor |
|-------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Data governance** | ✓ (primary) | — | — | — | Audit only |
| **Application logic** | ✓ | — | May add wrappers | — | Audit only |
| **OS / Middleware** | ✓ (in IaaS) | ✓ (in PaaS/SaaS) | — | — | — |
| **Virtualisation** | — | ✓ | Aggregates | — | Audit |
| **Network transport** | Uses | Provides | Routes | ✓ (owns) | — |
| **Physical infra** | — | ✓ | — | — | — |
| **Compliance reporting** | Partial | ✓ | ✓ (intermediary) | Limited | ✓ (primary) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture (Roles)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CC[Cloud Consumer]:::cc
    CB[Cloud Broker]:::cb
    CP[Cloud Provider]:::cp
    CCar[Cloud Carrier]:::ccar
    CA[Cloud Auditor]:::ca

    CC -- "Service usage<br>and SLA" --> CB
    CB -- "Aggregated<br>requests" --> CP
    CP -- "Data transport<br>and routing" --> CCar
    CCar -- "Connectivity" --> CC
    CA -- "Independent<br>audit of controls" --- CP
    CA -- "Independent<br>audit of controls" --- CC

    classDef cc fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cb fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cp fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ccar fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ca fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:**  
> - Solid arrows = primary service/data flow.  
> - Dashed arrow = audit relationship (passive, not in data path).  
> - The **Cloud Broker** sits *between* Consumer and Provider.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — IaaS Functional Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CONSUMER["CONSUMER LAYER (Cloud Consumer)"]
        direction TB
        APP["Application Code<br>e.g., Java / Python / Node"]
        MID["Middleware<br>e.g., Tomcat, RabbitMQ"]
        OS["Guest OS<br>e.g., Linux / Windows"]
    end

    subgraph PROVIDER["PROVIDER LAYER (Cloud Provider)"]
        direction TB
        HV["Hypervisor / VMM<br>e.g., KVM, VMware ESXi, Xen"]
        VMS["Virtual Machines<br>vCPU, vRAM, vDisk"]
        STORE["Virtual Storage<br>Block, Object, File"]
        NET["Virtual Network<br>VPC, Subnet, LB, FW"]
        PHY["Physical Infrastructure<br>Servers, Switches, Datacenter"]
    end

    OS --> HV
    HV --> VMS
    VMS --> STORE
    VMS --> NET
    HV --> PHY

    BOUNDARY["- - - - BOUNDARY LINE - - -"]:::bd

    OS -.- BOUNDARY
    HV -.- BOUNDARY

    classDef bd fill:none,stroke:#D32F2F,stroke-width:3px,stroke-dasharray:8 4,color:#D32F2F
```

> **Reading the diagram:**  
> - The **dashed red line** is the *responsibility boundary*.  
> - Everything **above** the line = Consumer-owned.  
> - Everything **below** the line = Provider-owned.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cloud Service Model Stack (Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SAA["SaaS Stack"]
        direction TB
        SAA1["Application (Provider)"]
        SAA2["Middleware (Provider)"]
        SAA3["OS / Runtime (Provider)"]
        SAA4["Virtualisation (Provider)"]
        SAA5["Hardware (Provider)"]
        SAA6["Data (Consumer)"]
    end

    subgraph PAA["PaaS Stack"]
        direction TB
        PAA1["Application (Consumer)"]
        PAA2["Middleware (Provider)"]
        PAA3["OS / Runtime (Provider)"]
        PAA4["Virtualisation (Provider)"]
        PAA5["Hardware (Provider)"]
        PAA6["Data (Consumer)"]
    end

    subgraph IAA["IaaS Stack"]
        direction TB
        IAA1["Application (Consumer)"]
        IAA2["Middleware (Consumer)"]
        IAA3["OS / Runtime (Consumer)"]
        IAA4["Virtualisation (Provider)"]
        IAA5["Hardware (Provider)"]
        IAA6["Data (Consumer)"]
    end
```

> **Reading the diagram:**  
> - *Orange/yellow rows* indicate Consumer responsibility.  
> - *Blue rows* indicate Provider responsibility.  
> - Notice the boundary climbs *upward* as we go IaaS → PaaS → SaaS.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — IaaS Provisioning Workflow (End-to-End)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Consumer logs in<br>via Web Console or API] --> B[Step 2: Select region,<br>instance type, image]
    B --> C[Step 3: Configure VPC,<br>subnet, security group]
    C --> D[Step 4: Launch VM<br>Hypervisor allocates vCPU/RAM]
    D --> E[Step 5: Attach block storage<br>and assign public IP]
    E --> F[Step 6: Install Guest OS<br>and middleware]
    F --> G[Step 7: Deploy application<br>and data]
    G --> H[Step 8: Metering begins<br>Billing per second/hour]
    H --> I[Step 9: Scale out / Scale in<br>Auto-scaling triggers]
    I --> J[Step 10: Terminate VM<br>Resources released to pool]
```

> **Reading the diagram:**  
> This is the **canonical IaaS lifecycle**. Be ready to redraw it from memory for KTU's 7-mark sub-questions.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Roles & Boundaries in Hybrid Cloud

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ON["On-Premises Datacenter<br>(Consumer-owned)"] -->|VPN / Direct Connect| CP
    AWS["AWS IaaS<br>(Provider-owned)"] --> CP
    AZ["Azure IaaS<br>(Provider-owned)"] --> CP
    GCP["GCP IaaS<br>(Provider-owned)"] --> CP
    CP[Cloud Broker<br>aggregates + routes]
    CA[Cloud Auditor<br>audits all parties]
    CP --> CC[Cloud Consumer<br>single pane of glass]
    CA -.-> CC
    CA -.-> CP
    CA -.-> ON
```

> **Reading the diagram:**  
> - The **Broker** abstracts the multi-cloud complexity from the Consumer.  
> - The **Auditor** has *read-only* relationships (dashed lines) with all entities.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**List and briefly explain the five main roles defined in the NIST cloud computing reference architecture.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The NIST Reference Architecture (SP 500-292) defines five roles:

1. **Cloud Consumer (CC):** The individual or organisation that uses cloud services procured from a provider. *(1 mark)*
2. **Cloud Provider (CP):** The entity responsible for making cloud services available; it manages the underlying infrastructure and provides service interfaces. *(1 mark)*
3. **Cloud Broker (CB):** An intermediary that manages the use, performance, and delivery of cloud services while negotiating relationships between CPs and CCs. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Cloud Carrier (CCar):** The network operator that provides connectivity and transport of cloud services between CPs and CCs. *(0.5 mark)*

> *(Note: Cloud Auditor, the 5th role, may be added if asked — describes a third party that performs independent audits of services, security, and performance.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Define IaaS. List any four characteristics of IaaS.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):** Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) is a cloud service model in which the consumer is provisioned with fundamental computing resources — processing, storage, networks — over which the consumer can deploy arbitrary software including operating systems and applications. The consumer does not manage the underlying cloud infrastructure but controls the OS, storage, and applications (NIST SP 800-145).

**Four Characteristics (0.5 mark each):**
1. **Resource Pooling** — multi-tenant sharing of physical resources, isolated via virtualisation.
2. **Broad Network Access** — services available over standard protocols from any client device.
3. **Rapid Elasticity** — capacity can be scaled out or in dynamically with workload.
4. **Measured Service** — usage is monitored, controlled, and reported, supporting pay-per-use billing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *Each Part B question has internal choice. Marks are split 7 + 7.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Aligned]**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of *Roles and Boundaries* in cloud computing. With a neat diagram, describe the responsibilities of each of the five NIST roles. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** What is Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)? Discuss its key characteristics, components, and advantages. List three real-world IaaS providers. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a) — Roles and Boundaries (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**  
In cloud computing, the *roles* are the distinct actors that participate in the cloud ecosystem, and the *boundaries* are the contractually defined lines of responsibility that demarcate who controls which layer of the technology stack.

**Detailed Explanation of the 5 Roles (1 mark each):**

1. **Cloud Consumer:** Acquires and uses cloud services. Owns data, applications, and access management. *Example:* a startup using AWS EC2.
2. **Cloud Provider:** Owns and operates the cloud infrastructure (servers, storage, hypervisor, datacenter). *Example:* Amazon (AWS), Microsoft (Azure).
3. **Cloud Broker:** Intermediary that aggregates, integrates, or customises services from multiple providers to meet consumer needs. Three sub-services — *Service Intermediation, Aggregation, Arbitrage*.
4. **Cloud Carrier:** The network operator (ISP/telecom) that transports data between consumer and provider. Owns the bandwidth and routes.
5. **Cloud Auditor:** Independent third party that evaluates the security, privacy, and performance of cloud services against standards (e.g., ISO 27001, GDPR).

**Diagram (1 mark):**  
Refer to the NIST Reference Architecture diagram in **Section 4.1**. *(In the exam, draw a block diagram with 5 boxes and arrows showing primary data flow between Consumer → Broker → Provider, plus dashed arrows from Auditor to all entities.)*

**Boundary (1 mark):**  
The boundary is the *SLA-defined responsibility demarcation* — it tells each party *what they own*. In IaaS, the boundary lies between the **Guest OS** (consumer-owned) and the **Hypervisor** (provider-owned).

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b) — IaaS Detailed (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**  
IaaS is the cloud service model that delivers virtualised computing, storage, and networking resources on demand over the internet. The consumer controls the OS, middleware, and applications, but not the underlying physical infrastructure.

**Key Characteristics (2 marks):**
- **On-Demand Self-Service** — VMs provisioned via API without human intervention.
- **Resource Pooling** — multi-tenant pooling of physical resources, isolated by virtualisation.
- **Rapid Elasticity** — capacity can scale horizontally and vertically on demand.
- **Measured Service** — pay-as-you-go metering.
- **Broad Network Access** — services accessible over standard protocols.

**Components of IaaS (2 marks):**
- **Compute:** Virtual Machines, Bare-Metal servers, Auto-scaling groups, Containers.
- **Storage:** Block storage (EBS), Object storage (S3), File storage (EFS), Archival storage.
- **Networking:** Virtual Private Cloud, Subnets, Load Balancers, Firewalls, VPN gateways.
- **Supporting:** IAM, KMS, Monitoring, Billing APIs.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Full control over OS and software stack.
- High flexibility and portability (lift-and-shift from on-premises).
- No CapEx; pay only for what you use.
- Global availability across regions/zones.

**Three Real-World IaaS Providers (1 mark):**
1. **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** — EC2, S3, VPC.
2. **Microsoft Azure** — Virtual Machines, Blob Storage, Virtual Network.
3. **Google Cloud Platform (GCP)** — Compute Engine, Cloud Storage, VPC.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Aligned]**
> **(a)** Compare and contrast the three cloud service models — IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS — using a responsibility matrix. Where does the *boundary* lie in each model? *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A startup provisions **20 VMs** on AWS EC2, each with **4 vCPUs and 8 GiB RAM**, running 24×7 for 30 days. The on-demand rate is **₹6 per vCPU-hour**. Block storage is **200 GB per VM at ₹1.5/GB-month**. Outbound egress is **5 TB per VM at ₹4/GB**. Calculate the total monthly bill and identify the largest cost component. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a) — Comparison Matrix (7 marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**  
The three service models differ in *who manages what*. The responsibility boundary shifts upward (toward the consumer) as we move from IaaS → PaaS → SaaS.

**Responsibility Matrix Table (3 marks):**

| Layer | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|-------|------|------|------|
| Data | Consumer | Consumer | Consumer |
| Application | Consumer | Consumer | **Provider** |
| Middleware | Consumer | **Provider** | Provider |
| Runtime / OS | Consumer | **Provider** | Provider |
| Virtualisation | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| Servers, Storage, Network HW | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| Physical Datacenter | Provider | Provider | Provider |

**Boundary Position (1 mark):**
- **IaaS boundary** → between *Guest OS* and *Hypervisor* (consumer manages upward).
- **PaaS boundary** → between *Application* and *Middleware* (provider manages runtime/OS).
- **SaaS boundary** → at the *Application* level (consumer only configures app behaviour).

**Comparison (2 marks):**
- **Flexibility:** IaaS > PaaS > SaaS.
- **Ease of use:** SaaS > PaaS > IaaS.
- **Skill required:** IaaS needs sysadmin; PaaS needs developer; SaaS needs end-user.
- **Cost control:** IaaS gives fine-grained control; SaaS is fixed-price per user.

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $N_{\text{vm}} = 20$
- $n_{\text{cpu}} = 4$ vCPUs per VM
- $T = 24 \times 30 = 720$ hours
- $P_{\text{cpu}} = ₹6$ per vCPU-hour
- $S_{\text{vm}} = 200$ GB per VM, $P_{\text{st}} = ₹1.5$ per GB-month
- $D_{\text{vm}} = 5\,\text{TB} = 5 \times 1024 = 5120$ GB per VM
- $P_{\text{e}} = ₹4$ per GB egress

**Step 1 — Compute Cost (2 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{compute}} = N_{\text{vm}} \times n_{\text{cpu}} \times P_{\text{cpu}} \times T
$$

$$
C_{\text{compute}} = 20 \times 4 \times 6 \times 720 = 3{,}45{,}600\,\text{₹}
$$

**Step 2 — Storage Cost (1.5 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{storage}} = 20 \times 200 \times 1.5 = 6{,}000\,\text{₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Egress Cost (1.5 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{egress}} = 20 \times 5120 \times 4 = 4{,}09{,}600\,\text{₹}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Monthly Bill (1 mark):**

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 3{,}45{,}600 + 6{,}000 + 4{,}09{,}600 = 7{,}61{,}200\,\text{₹}
$$

**Step 5 — Largest Cost Component (1 mark):**  
*Egress (₹4,09,600)* is the largest cost component (~53.8% of the total bill). *Compute is second (~45.4%); Storage is negligible (~0.8%).*  
**Engineering Insight:** Reduce egress by using **CDN, data compression, region co-location, or VPC endpoints** to avoid data leaving the cloud.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Carrier with Provider:** Many students write that the *network* is the provider's responsibility in all layers. The **Cloud Carrier** is a *separate* role — it owns the *WAN/transport*; the **Provider** owns the *datacenter fabric*.
> 2. **Forgetting the Boundary:** When asked to compare IaaS/PaaS/SaaS, students list features but forget to *explicitly state* where the boundary lies. Always mention the boundary in your comparison answer.
> 3. **Unit Confusion in Numericals:** Mixing **GB** (decimal, $10^9$) with **GiB** (binary, $2^{30}$). Convert consistently and show the conversion.
> 4. **Ignoring Egress:** In cost calculations, students often forget that **outbound data transfer is charged separately** and is usually the dominant cost.
> 5. **Vague "Advantages" Lists:** Don't just write *"flexible and scalable"* — quantify it. Example: *“Auto-scaling groups can scale from 2 to 200 instances in 5 minutes, paying only for actual vCPU-hours consumed.”*
> 6. **Mis-attributing Security:** In IaaS, **the consumer is responsible for OS patching, firewall rules inside the VM, and data encryption at rest** — this is a frequent exam trap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — Pin these for KTU Day-Before Exam**

### A. Roles & Boundaries
- ✅ **Five NIST Roles:** Consumer, Provider, Broker, Carrier, Auditor — remember the acronym **CP-BBC** *(Consumer, Provider, Broker, Carrier, Auditor)*.
- ✅ **Cloud Broker** has 3 sub-functions: **Service Intermediation, Aggregation, Arbitrage**.
- ✅ **Cloud Carrier** = the *network pipe*; **Cloud Provider** = the *compute/storage fabric*.
- ✅ **Cloud Auditor** is *out-of-band* — dashed arrows only, not in data path.
- ✅ **Boundary** = *contractual SLA line* dividing responsibilities; in IaaS it lies between **Guest OS** and **Hypervisor**.

### B. IaaS — Core Facts
- ✅ **NIST Definition:** Consumer controls *OS, storage, deployed applications*; *not* the underlying cloud infrastructure.
- ✅ **5 Essential Characteristics apply to IaaS:** on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service.
- ✅ **4 Component Categories:** Compute, Storage, Networking, Supporting Services (IAM, KMS, Monitoring).
- ✅ **Storage types:** Block (EBS), Object (S3), File (EFS), Archival (Glacier).
- ✅ **Top 3 IaaS providers:** AWS, Azure, GCP. (Remember: AWS = EC2/S3; Azure = VM/Blob; GCP = GCE/Cloud Storage.)

### C. Quantitative Must-Knows
- ✅ **Cost formula:** $C_{\text{total}} = \sum R_i \times P_i \times T_i$
- ✅ **SLA downtime:** $D = T \times (1 - A)$; for 99.9% over 30 days → ~43.2 min/month allowed.
- ✅ **GB → GiB:** divide by $2^{30} / 10^9 \approx 1.0737$.
- ✅ **Transfer time:** $T = D / (R \times \text{eff})$.
- ✅ **"N nines" availability:** $A = 1 - 10^{-n}$.

### D. Exam Strategy
- ✅ Always **draw the responsibility matrix** for IaaS/PaaS/SaaS comparison questions.
- ✅ Always **mention the boundary line** explicitly in your answers.
- ✅ In numericals, **show all unit conversions** (GiB↔GB, TB↔GB, hours↔seconds).
- ✅ **Egress cost is the silent killer** — never forget to include it in cloud-bill calculations.
- ✅ Memorise the **NIST roles and definitions verbatim** — they appear as 3-mark questions almost every semester.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of PaaS ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) **Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)** is a cloud computing service model that delivers a complete, ready-to-use development and deployment platform—including operating systems, programming-language runtimes, middleware, databases, development frameworks, and web servers—over the internet on a pay-per-use basis. The cloud provider abstracts and manages the underlying infrastructure (servers, storage, network, virtualization), allowing developers to focus exclusively on writing application code and managing data, without worrying about hardware provisioning, OS patching, or runtime configuration. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** PaaS is the cloud service layer that sits *between* IaaS (raw infrastructure) and SaaS (finished software). NIST SP 800-145 categorizes PaaS as one of the three canonical *Service Models* in cloud computing, distinct from *Deployment Models* (Public, Private, Hybrid, Community). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pizza Kitchen on Rent" Model Imagine you want to bake pizzas for a party, but you do not own an oven, do not want to buy flour, and do not want to clean up afterwards. - **On-Premise / Traditional IT:** You buy the oven, install gas, buy raw ingredients, knead dough, manage the kitchen, and clean the mess. *(You manage everything!)* - **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service):** You rent the oven and the gas connection, but you still bring your own dough, sauce, and toppings, and you bake it yourself. - **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service):** You walk into a **fully-equipped commercial kitchen** (the platform). The oven, mixer, prep counter, utilities, gas, and even the pizza dough base are *already prepared for you*. You only need to add your toppings, bake it, and take it out. The kitchen staff handles temperature, cleaning, and equipment maintenance. - **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service):** Someone else bakes the entire pizza and delivers it hot to your door. You just eat. > [!NOTE] > **In PaaS, the "kitchen" = Runtime + Middleware + OS + Virtualization. The "toppings" = Your Application Code + Data.** The chef (cloud provider) handles the oven (servers), gas (power), and cleanup (maintenance). ### 1.3 Key Characteristics (KTU Board-Weighted Points) | # | Characteristic | Explanation | |---|----------------|-------------| | 1 | **Provider-Managed Infrastructure** | Servers, storage, networking, and OS are abstracted away from the developer. | | 2 | **Pre-Configured Runtime Environment** | Languages (Java, Python, Node.js, .NET), frameworks (Spring, Django, Rails), and libraries are pre-installed. | | 3 | **Built-in Scalability & Load Balancing** | Automatic horizontal scaling based on traffic patterns. | | 4 | **Integrated DevOps Tooling** | CI/CD pipelines, version control hooks, automated testing built-in. | | 5 | **Multi-Tenancy** | Multiple developers/organizations share the same logical platform, isolated securely. | | 6 | **Pay-Per-Use Pricing** | Charged based on compute-seconds, memory-GB-hours, API calls, or app instances. | > [!TIP] > **Board Tip:** When asked to *define* PaaS in **3 marks**, always mention the three words: **"Platform", "Development/Deployment Environment", and "Pay-per-use"**. Examiners award marks for these trigger words. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ### 2.1 The PaaS Architecture Stack — Layer-by-Layer Breakdown PaaS is not a single technology; it is a **vertically integrated stack** of technologies managed by the cloud provider. Let us dissect it from the bottom (hardware) to the top (user application): #### Layer 1 — Physical Infrastructure (Hidden from User) - Bare-metal servers, storage arrays (SAN/NAS), and network switches housed in geographically distributed data centers. - Power redundancy (UPS, diesel generators), cooling systems, and physical security (biometrics, CCTV). #### Layer 2 — Virtualization & Hypervisor Layer - Hypervisors (VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM, Xen) create and manage **Virtual Machines (VMs)** or **containers** (Docker, containerd). - This is the magic that enables **multi-tenancy** and **elastic resource pooling**. #### Layer 3 — Operating System Layer - Standardized OS images (Linux distributions like Ubuntu, RHEL, CentOS; or Windows Server). - OS patching, security updates, and hardening are the provider's responsibility. #### Layer 4 — Middleware & Runtime Layer (THE HEART OF PaaS) - **Language Runtimes:** JVM (Java), CPython (Python), Node.js (JavaScript), CLR (.NET), BEAM (Erlang/Elixir). - **Web Servers / Application Servers:** Apache Tomcat, Nginx, JBoss, WebLogic, IIS. - **Message Brokers:** RabbitMQ, Apache Kafka, ActiveMQ. - **Caching Layers:** Redis, Memcached. #### Layer 5 — Data Services (PaaS-Managed Databases) - Managed RDBMS (Amazon RDS, Google Cloud SQL, Azure SQL Database). - NoSQL stores (Amazon DynamoDB, MongoDB Atlas, Azure Cosmos DB). - Object storage (S3, Google Cloud Storage, Azure Blob Storage). #### Layer 6 — Development & Deployment Tooling - **IDEs & CLIs:** Cloud9, VS Code integration, `gcloud`, `aws`, `az` command-line tools. - **CI/CD Pipelines:** AWS CodePipeline, Azure DevOps, Google Cloud Build, Heroku Pipelines. - **API Gateways & Service Meshes:** Kong, Istio, AWS API Gateway. #### Layer 7 — User Application Layer (Developer's Responsibility) - The developer's custom code, business logic, and proprietary algorithms. - **This is the ONLY layer the PaaS user fully controls.** ### 2.2 Analytical Justification — Why PaaS Exists > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Core "Why":** PaaS solves the **"Time-to-Market vs Operational Overhead" trade-off**. > > - Before PaaS: Developers spent ~**60–70%** of their time on infrastructure plumbing (server setup, OS config, runtime install, dependency management, scaling logic). > - With PaaS: That overhead drops to ~**10–15%**, freeing developers to focus on **business logic, UX, and innovation**. ### 2.3 PaaS High-Yield Reference Sheet (Board Exam Cheat Sheet) | **Concept** | **Definition / Formula / Boundary** | **Unit / Standard** | |-------------|--------------------------------------|---------------------| | Service Model Category | One of NIST's 3 service models: IaaS, PaaS, SaaS | — | | Primary User | Application Developers, DevOps Engineers | — | | Provider Manages | Hardware, Virtualization, OS, Middleware, Runtime, Database | — | | User Manages | Application Code, Data, Application Configuration | — | | Pricing Unit | vCPU-hour, GB-hour, API call, instance-month | USD/vCPU-hr | | Availability SLA | Typically **99.95%** to **99.99%** | % uptime/year | | Latency Target | < 100 ms intra-region, < 300 ms cross-region | milliseconds (ms) | | Throughput Metric | Requests per second (RPS), Queries per second (QPS) | req/s, qps | | Deployment Modes | Public PaaS, Private PaaS, Hybrid PaaS, Community PaaS | — | | Key Examples | AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine, Azure App Service, Heroku, Red Hat OpenShift | — | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL: LaTeX Pipe Rule** — In all equations and formulas below, the vertical bar $\vert$ is used *inside* math mode to prevent breaking markdown table syntax. Example: $C_{total} = C_{compute} + C_{storage} + C_{egress}$. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of PaaS PaaS is the **backbone of modern cloud-native development**: 1. **Startup Velocity:** A 2-person startup can deploy a production-grade web app in hours, not months. 2. **Microservices Architecture:** PaaS platforms natively support containerized microservices (via Kubernetes-based services like GKE, AKS, EKS). 3. **AI/ML Workloads:** Specialized PaaS offerings (Google AI Platform, Azure ML, AWS SageMaker) provide pre-configured environments for training and deploying machine learning models. 4. **API Economy:** PaaS makes it trivial to build, version, secure, and monetize RESTful APIs at scale. 5. **IoT Backends:** PaaS handles the bursty, unpredictable workloads of millions of IoT devices ingesting telemetry. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Comparative Matrix ### 3.1 Comparative Analytical Matrix — PaaS vs IaaS vs SaaS This is the **single most important table** in any cloud computing exam. Memorize the **boundaries of responsibility**. | **Layer / Component** | **On-Premise** | **IaaS** | **PaaS** | **SaaS** | |----------------------|----------------|----------|----------|----------| | Application Code & Data | User | User | User | Provider | | Application Configuration | User | User | User | Provider | | Middleware (e.g., Tomcat, Kafka) | User | User | **Provider** | Provider | | Runtime (JVM, Python) | User | User | **Provider** | Provider | | Operating System | User | User | **Provider** | Provider | | Virtualization | User | **Provider** | Provider | Provider | | Servers, Storage, Network | User | **Provider** | Provider | Provider | > [!TIP] > **Board Mnemonic: "PaaS is IaaS + (Middleware + Runtime + OS Management)."** This is the cleanest 1-line definition that scores full marks. ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation — PaaS Cost Calculation In a KTU exam, you may be asked to **compute the monthly cost** of a PaaS deployment. Let us derive the standard formula with a worked example. #### Given Problem Statement > *"An organization deploys a Java web application on a PaaS platform. The app runs on **4 instances**, each requiring **2 vCPUs and 4 GB RAM**. The PaaS provider charges **\$0.05 per vCPU-hour** and **\$0.012 per GB-hour of RAM**. Calculate the monthly cost assuming 30 days of continuous operation."* #### Step 1 — Identify the Cost Formula The total monthly PaaS cost is the sum of compute and memory costs across all instances: $$ C_{total} = N_{inst} \times \left[ (C_{vCPU} \times n_{vCPU} \times T) + (C_{RAM} \times m_{RAM} \times T) \right] $$ Where: - $C_{total}$ = Total monthly cost in USD - $N_{inst}$ = Number of instances (here, 4) - $C_{vCPU}$ = Cost per vCPU-hour (here, \$0.05) - $n_{vCPU}$ = vCPUs per instance (here, 2) - $C_{RAM}$ = Cost per GB-hour (here, \$0.012) - $m_{RAM}$ = RAM in GB per instance (here, 4) - $T$ = Total hours in the billing period #### Step 2 — Compute Total Hours $$ T = 30 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hours/day} = 720 \text{ hours} $$ > **Logic:** A month is conventionally taken as 30 days in cloud billing. 30 × 24 = 720 hours is the standard multiplier. #### Step 3 — Compute Compute Cost Component (vCPU) $$ C_{compute} = N_{inst} \times C_{vCPU} \times n_{vCPU} \times T $$ $$ C_{compute} = 4 \times 0.05 \times 2 \times 720 $$ $$ C_{compute} = 4 \times 0.05 = 0.20 $$ $$ C_{compute} = 0.20 \times 2 = 0.40 $$ $$ C_{compute} = 0.40 \times 720 = 288.00 \text{ USD} $$ > **Logic:** Multiply instance count × per-vCPU rate → get hourly rate per instance → multiply by vCPU count → multiply by hours. #### Step 4 — Compute Memory Cost Component (RAM) $$ C_{memory} = N_{inst} \times C_{RAM} \times m_{RAM} \times T $$ $$ C_{memory} = 4 \times 0.012 \times 4 \times 720 $$ $$ C_{memory} = 4 \times 0.012 = 0.048 $$ $$ C_{memory} = 0.048 \times 4 = 0.192 $$ $$ C_{memory} = 0.192 \times 720 = 138.24 \text{ USD} $$ > **Logic:** Same multiplication chain as compute, but with RAM pricing. #### Step 5 — Sum the Components $$ C_{total} = C_{compute} + C_{memory} $$ $$ C_{total} = 288.00 + 138.24 = 426.24 \text{ USD/month} $$ #### Final Answer Box > **Total Monthly PaaS Cost = \$426.24** #### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation - **Pure PaaS interpretation:** The user only worries about app code; all OS/runtime costs are bundled into the \$0.05/vCPU-hr rate. - **Cost optimization insight:** If we scale down to 2 instances during off-peak hours (say 12 hours/day), the cost drops proportionally. - **Vendor lock-in caveat:** This cost is fixed to the vendor's pricing; switching PaaS providers (e.g., Heroku → AWS Elastic Beanstalk) requires code refactoring in some cases. ### 3.3 PaaS Classification — Sub-Types Derivation PaaS can be classified along two orthogonal axes: #### Axis 1 — By Deployment Model | **Sub-Type** | **Description** | **Example** | |--------------|------------------|-------------| | **Public PaaS** | Hosted on public cloud, multi-tenant | Google App Engine, Heroku | | **Private PaaS** | Hosted in organization's own data center | Red Hat OpenShift on-premise, Pivotal Cloud Foundry | | **Hybrid PaaS** | Workload portability between public and private | IBM Cloud Private, Azure Stack | | **Community PaaS** | Shared by organizations with common requirements | Government cloud platforms | #### Axis 2 — By Application Domain | **Sub-Type** | **Purpose** | **Example** | |--------------|-------------|-------------| | **General-Purpose PaaS** | Web apps, REST APIs | AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service | | **Data PaaS (dPaaS)** | Analytics, big data processing | Google BigQuery, Azure Synapse | | **AI/ML PaaS (aPaaS)** | ML model training & deployment | AWS SageMaker, Azure ML | | **Integration PaaS (iPaaS)** | Enterprise system integration | MuleSoft, Dell Boomi | | **Blockchain PaaS (bPaaS)** | Smart contract & DApp development | IBM Blockchain Platform, Azure Blockchain Service | | **Communications PaaS (cPaaS)** | Real-time messaging, VoIP | Twilio, Vonage | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram 1 — PaaS in the Cloud Service Model Hierarchy This diagram shows where PaaS sits in the *Cloud Service Stack* and explicitly maps the **responsibility boundaries** between the cloud provider and the developer. This is a high-frequency Board exam question. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph userLayer["USER RESPONSIBILITY"] direction TB appCode["APPLICATION CODE AND DATA"] appConfig["APPLICATION CONFIGURATION
ENV VARIABLES SCALING RULES"] end subgraph providerLayer["PROVIDER MANAGED PAAS STACK"] direction TB middleware["MIDDLEWARE LAYER
TOMCAT KAFKA RABBITMQ"] runtime["RUNTIME LAYER
JVM PYTHON NODEJS DOTNET"] osLayer["OPERATING SYSTEM
LINUX WINDOWS SERVER"] virtLayer["VIRTUALIZATION AND CONTAINERS
KVM DOCKER KUBERNETES"] physicalLayer["PHYSICAL INFRASTRUCTURE
SERVERS STORAGE NETWORK"] end appCode -. DEVELOPER OWNS .-> appConfig appConfig --> middleware middleware --> runtime runtime --> osLayer osLayer --> virtLayer virtLayer --> physicalLayer classDef userBox fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef providerBox fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class appCode,appConfig userBox class middleware,runtime,osLayer,virtLayer,physicalLayer providerBox ``` **Reading the diagram:** The **dotted orange line** at the top represents the developer's domain. Everything below the line is fully managed by the PaaS provider. ### 4.2 Diagram 2 — PaaS Architecture & Request Flow This block-level functional architecture shows how a user request travels through a typical PaaS stack. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["DEVELOPER
WRITES CODE"] -->|GIT PUSH| B["CI CD PIPELINE
CODEBUILD OR JENKINS"] B -->|BUILD ARTIFACT| C["CONTAINER REGISTRY
DOCKER HUB OR ECR"] C -->|DEPLOY| D["ORCHESTRATOR
KUBERNETES OR DOCKER SWARM"] D -->|PROVISION| E["PAAS RUNTIME NODE
VIRTUAL MACHINE OR CONTAINER"] E -->|EXECUTE| F["APPLICATION INSTANCE
MICROSERVICE WEB APP"] F -->|READ WRITE| G["MANAGED DATABASE
RDS OR DYNAMODB"] F -->|CACHE| H["CACHE LAYER
REDIS OR MEMCACHED"] I["END USER"] -->|HTTPS REQUEST| J["LOAD BALANCER
ALB OR NGINX"] J --> F F -->|RESPONSE| I classDef devBox fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px classDef sysBox fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px classDef dataBox fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px classDef userBox fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px class A,B,C devBox class D,E,F,J sysBox class G,H dataBox class I userBox ``` **Reading the diagram:** A developer pushes code → CI/CD pipeline builds a container image → registry stores it → orchestrator deploys it onto a PaaS runtime node → application serves traffic from the load balancer → data is persisted in managed databases and cached in Redis. ### 4.3 Diagram 3 — PaaS Decision Flow (When to Use PaaS?) This flowchart helps students answer the classic Board question: *"When would you choose PaaS over IaaS?"* ```mermaid flowchart TD start(["START
NEW CLOUD PROJECT"]) --> q1{"NEED FULL
CONTROL OVER OS?"} q1 -->|YES| iaas["USE IAAS
AWS EC2 AZURE VM"] q1 -->|NO| q2{"FOCUS ON
APP CODE NOT
INFRASTRUCTURE?"} q2 -->|NO| saas["USE SAAS
OFFICE 365 SALESFORCE"] q2 -->|YES| q3{"STANDARD WEB
APP OR API?"} q3 -->|YES| paas["USE PAAS
ELASTIC BEANSTALK APP ENGINE"] q3 -->|NO COMPLEX CUSTOM| iaas2["USE IAAS OR CONTAINERS
WITH KUBERNETES"] classDef startNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF,stroke-width:3px classDef decNode fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 classDef paasNode fill:#32CD32,stroke:#006400,color:#FFF,stroke-width:3px classDef otherNode fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000 class start startNode class q1,q2,q3 decNode class paas paasNode class iaas,saas,iaas2 otherNode ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1: Define Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS). List any four characteristics of PaaS. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) is a cloud computing service model in which the cloud provider delivers a fully integrated platform—comprising hardware, operating system, middleware, runtime, and development tools—over the internet, enabling developers to build, test, deploy, and manage applications without managing the underlying infrastructure. **Four Characteristics of PaaS:** 1. **Provider-Managed Stack:** The provider manages servers, storage, networking, OS, virtualization, middleware, and runtime. 2. **Pre-Configured Development Environment:** Languages, frameworks, and tools are pre-installed and ready to use. 3. **Built-in Scalability:** Automatic horizontal and vertical scaling based on application load. 4. **Pay-Per-Use Pricing:** Users are billed based on actual consumption of compute, storage, and bandwidth resources. #### Question 2: Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS in terms of user-managed components. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]` **Model Answer:** | **Service Model** | **User Manages** | **Provider Manages** | |-------------------|------------------|----------------------| | **IaaS** | Application, Data, Middleware, Runtime, OS | Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Network | | **PaaS** | Application, Data, Application Configuration | Middleware, Runtime, OS, Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Network | | **PaaS** vs **SaaS**: | | | | **SaaS** | Nothing (only user data entry) | Entire stack including the application itself | > **Key Insight for Board:** In PaaS, the user manages only *Application + Data + Configuration*; in IaaS, the user manages everything down to the OS; in SaaS, the user only uses the software. --- ### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) #### Question A (Choice 1): Comprehensive PaaS Exposition [14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a) Explain the architecture of Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) with a neat block diagram. List the layers managed by the PaaS provider versus the layers managed by the user. [7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** The PaaS architecture is a **vertically integrated stack** of seven layers, as shown in the block diagram below: ``` +----------------------------------------------+ | USER LAYER | | Application Code + Data + Configuration | <-- USER MANAGES +----------------------------------------------+ | Middleware (Tomcat, Kafka, RabbitMQ) | <-- PROVIDER MANAGES +----------------------------------------------+ | Runtime (JVM, Python, Node.js, .NET CLR) | <-- PROVIDER MANAGES +----------------------------------------------+ | Operating System (Linux/Windows) | <-- PROVIDER MANAGES +----------------------------------------------+ | Virtualization (KVM, Hyper-V, Containers) | <-- PROVIDER MANAGES +----------------------------------------------+ | Servers, Storage, Network Hardware | <-- PROVIDER MANAGES +----------------------------------------------+ ``` **Step-by-Step Explanation of Each Layer:** 1. **Application Code & Data (User-Managed):** The developer writes the business logic in any supported language and defines the data schema. This is the *only* layer where the user has full creative control. 2. **Middleware Layer (Provider-Managed):** Web servers (Nginx, Apache), application servers (Tomcat, JBoss), and message brokers (Kafka) are pre-configured. The user simply deploys their app and the middleware handles request routing. 3. **Runtime Layer (Provider-Managed):** Language interpreters/compilers (JVM for Java, CPython for Python, V8 for Node.js) are pre-installed. The user uploads source code or compiled binaries. 4. **Operating System Layer (Provider-Managed):** A standardized Linux distribution (Ubuntu LTS, RHEL) or Windows Server image is maintained, patched, and hardened by the provider. 5. **Virtualization Layer (Provider-Managed):** Hypervisors and container runtimes (Docker, Kubernetes) abstract physical hardware into isolated, elastic compute units. 6. **Physical Infrastructure (Provider-Managed):** Bare-metal servers in geographically distributed data centers with redundant power, cooling, and networking. **Engineering Significance:** This layered abstraction enables **Developer Productivity** (no DevOps overhead), **Elastic Scalability** (provider auto-scales), and **Cost Efficiency** (pay only for what you use). --- **(b) With a suitable real-world example, explain how a startup company can leverage PaaS to launch a Minimum Viable Product (MVP). Discuss the cost and time benefits. [7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Real-World Scenario:** A 3-member startup "FreshBite" wants to launch a food delivery web app. They have limited capital and no DevOps team. **How PaaS Helps — Step-by-Step Workflow:** 1. **Platform Selection:** The team chooses **Heroku** (a popular PaaS provider). They sign up and create a new app in the Heroku dashboard. 2. **Code Deployment:** The lead developer writes the backend in **Node.js** (Express framework) and pushes the code via `git push heroku main`. Heroku automatically detects the language, provisions a runtime, installs dependencies (npm), and starts the server. 3. **Database Provisioning:** The team adds a **Heroku Postgres** add-on with a single click. A managed, backed-up, scalable relational database is instantly available — no manual installation. 4. **Scaling on Demand:** During the lunch-hour traffic spike (12 PM – 2 PM), Heroku auto-scales from 1 to 10 dynos (compute instances). After peak, it scales back down. 5. **CI/CD Integration:** The team connects their GitHub repository. Every `git push` triggers an automatic build, test, and deploy pipeline. **Cost & Time Benefit Analysis:** | **Aspect** | **Without PaaS (On-Premise/IaaS)** | **With PaaS (Heroku)** | |------------|------------------------------------|------------------------| | Time to launch MVP | 3–6 months | **2–4 weeks** | | DevOps hiring | 1–2 dedicated engineers needed | **Not required** | | Server provisioning | Manual: buy hardware, install OS, configure runtime | **One-click deployment** | | Scaling logic | Write custom scripts/load balancers | **Built-in auto-scaling** | | Monthly cost (MVP stage) | ~\$2,000–5,000 (servers + engineer salary) | **~\$50–200** (pay-per-dyno) | **Conclusion:** PaaS empowers startups to achieve **"Infrastructure-as-a-Code"** agility, reducing capital expenditure (CapEx) and shifting to operational expenditure (OpEx).
