## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A** (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five types of virtualization** with one real-world example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat **block diagram**, describe the **architecture of a Type 1 hypervisor**. Discuss CPU, memory, and I/O virtualization techniques used by it. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Five Types of Virtualization (7 marks)**

The five canonical types of virtualization, as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus, are:

1. **Server Virtualization (1.4 marks):** Partitioning one physical server into multiple isolated VMs. Example: VMware ESXi hosting 50 Linux VMs on a single rack server.
2. **Storage Virtualization (1.4 marks):** Pooling physical disks from multiple arrays into a single logical volume. Example: Amazon EBS aggregating multiple underlying SSDs into a single virtual disk.
3. **Network Virtualization (1.4 marks):** Abstracting physical NICs and switches into virtual switches and virtual NICs. Example: VMware vSwitch, Open vSwitch (OVS) used in OpenStack Neutron.
4. **Memory Virtualization (1.4 marks):** Mapping guest physical memory pages to machine physical memory using shadow page tables or Extended Page Tables (EPT). Example: Intel EPT in VMware ESXi.
5. **Desktop Virtualization / VDI (1.4 marks):** Hosting user desktops inside VMs in a data center, delivered over the network. Example: Citrix XenDesktop, Amazon WorkSpaces.

**Part (b) — Type 1 Hypervisor Architecture (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** *(Reproduce Architecture 1 from Section 4 of these notes.)*

The Type 1 hypervisor installs directly on the physical server's hardware. It hosts multiple guest VMs, each with its own guest OS and applications. The hypervisor sits between the VMs and the hardware, mediating every access.

**CPU Virtualization (1.5 marks):** Uses hardware-assisted virtualization (Intel VT-x or AMD-V). The CPU exposes a new "non-root" mode where guest instructions execute natively, except for privileged instructions that trap into the VMM (root mode). This avoids the costly binary translation of older approaches.

**Memory Virtualization (1.5 marks):** Uses **Extended Page Tables (EPT)** in Intel or **Nested Page Tables (NPT)** in AMD. The hypervisor maintains a two-level mapping: Guest Virtual → Guest Physical → Machine Physical. EPT/NPT hardware walks both levels in a single TLB miss, achieving near-native performance.

**I/O Virtualization (1 mark):** Achieved through three methods:
- **Emulation:** Software mimics a generic device (slowest, most portable).
- **Paravirtualization:** Guest uses special "front-end" drivers that talk to hypervisor "back-end" drivers (faster).
- **SR-IOV (Single Root I/O Virtualization):** A physical NIC exposes multiple virtual functions directly to VMs, bypassing the hypervisor for I/O (fastest).

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** A company has **120 physical servers** each running at **15% CPU utilization**. They plan to consolidate onto new hosts with **2 × 16-core CPUs, 256 GB RAM, and 4 GB hypervisor overhead per host**. Each consolidated VM requires **2 vCPUs and 8 GB vRAM**. Calculate the **Server Consolidation Ratio (SCR)**, the **maximum VMs per host** (memory-bound), and the **minimum number of new hosts** required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **key differences between full virtualization, paravirtualization, and hardware-assisted virtualization**. Mention the guest OS modification requirement for each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Numerical Consolidation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Number of VMs (0.5 mark):**
Since each legacy server becomes 1 VM, $N_{\text{VMs}} = 120$.

**Step 2 — vCPU requirement (0.5 mark):**
$$
\text{Total vCPUs} = 120 \times 2 = 240 \text{ vCPUs}
$$

**Step 3 — pCPU per new host (0.5 mark):**
$$
p\text{CPU per host} = 2 \times 16 = 32 \text{ cores}
$$

**Step 4 — CPU-bound VM capacity per host (1 mark):**
$$
D_{\text{CPU}} = \left\lfloor \frac{32}{2} \right\rfloor = 16 \text{ VMs per host}
$$

**Step 5 — Usable RAM per host (0.5 mark):**
$$
M_{\text{usable}} = 256 \text{ GB} - 4 \text{ GB} = 252 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 6 — Memory-bound VM capacity per host (1 mark):**
$$
D_{\text{Mem}} = \left\lfloor \frac{252}{8} \right\rfloor = 31 \text{ VMs per host}
$$

**Step 7 — Effective VM capacity per host (0.5 mark):**
$$
D_{\text{effective}} = \min(16, 31) = 16 \text{ VMs per host}
$$

**Step 8 — Minimum hosts required (0.5 mark):**
$$
N_{\text{hosts}} = \left\lceil \frac{120}{16} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 7.5 \right\rceil = 8 \text{ hosts}
$$

**Step 9 — Server Consolidation Ratio (1 mark):**
$$
\text{SCR} = \frac{120}{8} = 15 : 1
$$

**Final Answer (1 mark for the conclusion):** The company needs **8 new physical hosts** and achieves a **15:1 consolidation ratio**, reducing the data center footprint by ~93%.

---

**Part (b) — Virtualization Execution Modes (7 marks)**

1. **Full Virtualization (2.5 marks):**
   - The guest OS is **completely unmodified**.
   - All privileged guest instructions are **trapped** by the hypervisor using hardware CPU features (VT-x / AMD-V ring de-privileging).
   - Binary translation may be used for non-virtualizable instructions.
   - **Pros:** Zero guest modification; works with any OS. **Cons:** Some performance overhead.
   - **Example:** VMware ESXi (uses hardware-assisted full virtualization).

2. **Paravirtualization (2.5 marks):**
   - The guest OS is **explicitly modified** to use **hypercalls** instead of privileged instructions.
   - Hypercalls are explicit traps into the hypervisor for sensitive operations.
   - **Pros:** Higher performance (no trapping overhead). **Cons:** Requires OS source-code access — Windows cannot be paravirtualized.
   - **Example:** Early Xen, modern KVM with virtio drivers.

3. **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization (2 marks):**
   - Uses CPU hardware extensions (Intel VT-x, AMD-V) to **directly support** virtualization in silicon.
   - Introduces a new CPU execution mode (root/non-root) that traps automatically without software intervention.
   - Memory: EPT (Extended Page Tables) / NPT (Nested Page Tables) handle 2D page walks in hardware.
   - **Pros:** Near-native performance; unmodified guest OS. **Cons:** Requires CPU support (~10 years old or newer).
   - **Example:** All modern Type 1 hypervisors (ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "vCPU" with "physical core":** Always specify the vCPU:pCPU ratio AND the vCPU:logical-CPU ratio.
> 2. **Forgetting hypervisor memory overhead:** Deduct 150 MB + (0.5 MB × vCPU) when computing host memory availability.
> 3. **Calling VirtualBox a "Type 1" hypervisor:** It is Type 2. Only ESXi, Hyper-V, Xen, and KVM are Type 1.
> 4. **Drawing physical server pictures instead of block diagrams** in architecture questions.
> 5. **Using `|` inside markdown tables** (LaTeX) — this breaks PDF compilation in KTU answer sheets.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

- [ ] **Virtualization** = creating multiple logical resources from one physical resource using a **hypervisor (VMM)**.
- [ ] **Type 1 Hypervisor** = Bare-metal (ESXi, Hyper-V, Xen, KVM) — runs on hardware directly, **1–5% overhead**.
- [ ] **Type 2 Hypervisor** = Hosted (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation) — runs on host OS, **10–30% overhead**.
- [ ] **Five types of virtualization:** Server, Storage, Network, Memory, Desktop (VDI).
- [ ] **Server Consolidation Ratio (SCR)** = $N_{\text{old servers}} \div N_{\text{new hosts}}$; typical cloud ratio is **10:1 to 15:1**.
- [ ] **vCPU:pCPU ratio** of 2:1 to 4:1 is safe; 6:1+ is aggressive and only for batch jobs.
- [ ] **Hypervisor memory overhead rule:** $\approx 150 \text{ MB} + (0.5 \text{ MB} \times vCPU)$.
- [ ] **CPU Virtualization Techniques:** Full Emulation → Paravirtualization → Hardware-assisted (VT-x, AMD-V) — fastest to slowest.
- [ ] **Memory Virtualization** uses **EPT (Intel)** or **NPT (AMD)** for two-level page walks.
- [ ] **I/O Virtualization** methods: Emulation, Paravirtualization (virtio), SR-IOV passthrough.
- [ ] **Effective VM capacity per host** = $\min(D_{\text{CPU}}, D_{\text{Mem}}, D_{\text{IO}})$.
- [ ] **Key benefit:** Fault isolation, hardware independence, live migration, rapid provisioning, TCO reduction.
- [ ] **CPU Ready Time** should remain **< 5%** to avoid contention.
- [ ] **Live migration** (vMotion, KVM live migration) is the killer feature of virtualization.
- [ ] **AWS EC2** uses Xen (modified) / Nitro system; **Azure** uses Hyper-V; **GCP** uses KVM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a question says *"explain virtualization"*, always structure your answer in **three layers** — (1) the problem it solves, (2) the hypervisor mechanism, (3) the resulting VMs. This 3-layer structure is the marker for full marks in 14-mark KTU questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtual Machines (Machine Virtualization)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Virtual Machines (Machine Virtualization)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Virtual Machines (Machine Virtualization)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine Virtualization** is the process of creating a software-based (virtual) representation of physical computing resources — CPU, memory, storage, and network — that abstracts the underlying hardware and allows multiple, isolated execution environments (called **Virtual Machines**, or **VMs**) to run concurrently on a single physical host, mediated by a software layer known as the **Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM)** or **Hypervisor**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Outcome mapping **CO2 — Cloud Architecture & Virtualization**), virtualization is positioned as the **foundational abstraction layer** upon which every higher-order cloud service (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) is built.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **high-rise apartment building** sitting on one plot of land:

- The **land** = your **physical server (host machine)**.
- The **building structure and shared utilities** (plumbing, electricity meters, lobby) = the **Hypervisor (VMM)**.
- Each **independent flat** in the building = a **Virtual Machine (guest OS + applications)**.
- Each flat has its own locks, address, water supply, and electricity meter — the flat owner does not care how the building manages things internally. The flat cannot tap into a neighbour's electricity line.
- The **landlord (Hypervisor)** decides how to distribute water, power, and space fairly.

In the same way, the hypervisor partitions one physical server into many isolated VMs, each behaving exactly like an independent computer, while the hypervisor keeps them separated and fairly fed with CPU cycles, RAM, and disk I/O.

### 1.3 Standard Terminology (KTU Syllabus Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning | One-line Anchor |
|------|---------|-----------------|
| **Host** | The physical machine that runs the hypervisor | "The real metal" |
| **Guest** | The OS running inside a VM | "The virtual tenant" |
| **VMM / Hypervisor** | The software layer that creates and runs VMs | "The landlord" |
| **Full Virtualization** | Guest is unaware it is virtualized; runs unmodified | "Transparent tenant" |
| **Para-virtualization** | Guest OS is *modified* to call the hypervisor | "Cooperative tenant" |
| **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization** | CPU provides special instructions (VT-x, AMD-V) | "Building has dedicated lifts" |
| **Sensitive Instruction** | An instruction whose behaviour changes in user vs kernel mode | "Trap-prone command" |
| **Privileged Instruction** | An instruction that can only execute in kernel (ring 0) mode | "Management-only command" |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Emphasis**: Students are *expected* to know the difference between **System VMs** (provide a complete system environment — VMware, VirtualBox) and **Process VMs** (provide a sandboxed execution environment for a *single* process — JVM, .NET CLR). This distinction is a high-frequency short-answer question.

### 1.4 Why Machine Virtualization Matters in Cloud Computing

Virtualization is the **enabling technology** of IaaS clouds such as AWS EC2, Azure VMs, and Google Compute Engine. Without VMs, the "elastic, on-demand provisioning" promise of the cloud would be impossible because:

- **Hardware sharing** — Thousands of cloud tenants share millions of physical machines.
- **Fast provisioning** — Spinning up a VM takes seconds, not the hours needed to rack a server.
- **Isolation** — One noisy neighbour cannot crash another tenant's workload.
- **Portability** — VMs are encapsulated in files; they can be moved across data centres.
- **Utilization** — Idle CPU/RAM on a physical server can be repurposed into extra VMs.
- **Snapshotting & Cloning** — Easy backup, replication, and rapid deployment.

> [!TIP]
> A single metric to remember: **server utilization jumped from ~10–15 % (no virtualization) to ~70–85 % (with virtualization)** — a foundational justification for cloud economics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Popek & Goldberg Virtualization Conditions (1974)

This is the **most-asked theory** in KTU University Exams on this topic. The Popek-Goldberg theorem gives the *mathematical* criteria a CPU architecture must satisfy to be efficiently virtualizable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Popek & Goldberg (1974) Theorem** — An architecture is *efficiently virtualizable* if and only if its **sensitive instructions are a strict subset of its privileged instructions**.

### 2.1.1 Classification of CPU Instructions

CPU instructions are classified into three disjoint sets:

1. **Privileged Instructions** — Cause a *trap* (exception) if executed in user mode. Only the OS kernel (ring 0) may execute them. Example: `LDA` (load DMA address), `HLT` (halt), `MOV to CR3` (page table base register).
2. **Control-Sensitive Instructions** — Their behaviour depends on the contents of the *control registers* or *processor mode* (e.g., the interrupt-enable flag, processor mode bits). Toggling the mode changes what the instruction does.
3. **Behaviour-Sensitive Instructions** — Their effect (the *result* they produce) changes depending on the **current mode of execution** of the CPU, *not* only on the data they handle. Example: the `PUSH` of a sensitive register on mode change.

> **Sensitive Instructions** = **Control-Sensitive ∪ Behaviour-Sensitive**.

### 2.1.2 The Three Popek-Goldberg Conditions

For an architecture to be fully virtualizable:

1. **Equivalence / Fidelity** — Any program running under the VMM must behave *identically* to running on raw hardware (ignoring timing differences).
2. **Efficiency / Safety** — All *safe* (non-privileged) instructions execute natively on the CPU at native speed, without VMM intervention.
3. **Resource Control / Inspection** — The VMM must be able to *gain and retain* control over all privileged resources at will (i.e., any attempt by a guest to perform a privileged operation must trap to the VMM).

### 2.1.3 Verdict for the Famous Architectures

| Architecture | Sensitive ⊆ Privileged? | Virtualizable? | KTU-Compatible Term |
|--------------|------------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **IBM System/370** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Fully virtualizable |
| **Intel x86 (pre-VT-x, pre-2005)** | ❌ No — 17 problematic instructions | ❌ Not efficiently | Required **binary translation** |
| **Intel x86 (post-VT-x, 2005+)** | ✅ Yes (extended) | ✅ Yes | **Hardware-assisted** virtualization |
| **ARM with Virtualization Extensions** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Hardware-assisted |
| **RISC-V (with `H` extension)** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Modern cloud-friendly ISA |

> [!NOTE]
> The **17 problematic x86 instructions** (e.g., `SIDT`, `SGDT`, `SMSW`, `POPF`) were the historical reason VMware had to invent **binary translation** to virtualize x86 before Intel VT-x / AMD-V hardware assist was released.

## 2.2 The Three Implementation Strategies for x86

### 2.2.1 Full Virtualization (Software-Only, Pre-2005)

- Guest OS is **unmodified**.
- The VMM **dynamically rewrites** guest binary code at runtime — replacing privileged x86 instructions with safe, emulated sequences. This is called **Binary Translation** (invented by VMware).
- The VMM keeps *shadow page tables* to map guest-physical memory to real host-physical memory.
- **Pro**: Guest doesn't need any modification. **Con**: Translation overhead (~5–15 %).

### 2.2.2 Para-Virtualization

- Guest OS **is modified** to know it is being virtualized.
- Guest calls the VMM through well-defined **hypercalls** instead of executing privileged instructions.
- Example: the open-source **Xen hypervisor** pioneered this.
- **Pro**: Near-native speed. **Con**: Requires source access — Windows cannot be para-virtualized.

### 2.2.3 Hardware-Assisted Virtualization (Intel VT-x / AMD-V)

- The CPU itself supports a new execution mode: **VMX non-root mode** (guest) and **VMX root mode** (host).
- New instructions: `VMXON`, `VMXOFF`, `VMLAUNCH`, `VMRESUME`, `VMREAD`, `VMWRITE`, `VMCALL`.
- All sensitive instructions now *trap* to the VMM in hardware — no binary translation needed.
- Modern approach used by KVM, Hyper-V, ESXi, VirtualBox (recent).

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table** — it is the *single most important* condensed reference for the board exam.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Use Case | Unit |
|---|---------|----------------|----------|------|
| 1 | Popek-Goldberg virtualization criterion | $\text{Sensitive Instructions} \subseteq \text{Privileged Instructions}$ | Decide if an ISA is efficiently virtualizable | Set-theoretic |
| 2 | Total CPU time slice to $n$ VMs (equal share) | $t_i = \dfrac{T_{\text{quantum}}}{n}$ | Fair scheduling in VMM | $\text{ms}$ or $\mu s$ |
| 3 | Effective CPU share for VM $i$ (weighted) | $s_i = \dfrac{w_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j}$ | Weighted round-robin | Dimensionless ratio |
| 4 | VM total memory (ballooning-safe) | $M_{\text{real}} \ge M_{\text{assigned}} + M_{\text{overhead}}$ | Memory overcommitment | $\text{MiB / GiB}$ |
| 5 | CPU overcommitment ratio | $R_{\text{over}} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} vCPU_i}{pCPU_{\text{host}}}$ | How many virtual CPUs are assigned vs physical | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Effective MIPS share per VM | $MIPS_i = MIPS_{\text{host}} \times \dfrac{vCPU_i}{pCPU_{\text{host}}} \times \eta$ | Capacity planning | $\text{MIPS}$ |
| 7 | Hardware-assisted CPU support flag | $E_{VMX} = 1$ if Intel VT-x / AMD-V present | BIOS/UEFI check | Boolean (0 / 1) |
| 8 | VM density rule (server consolidation) | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{pCPU_{\text{host}} \times (1 - \rho)}{vCPU_{\text{avg}}}\right\rfloor$ | Max VMs per host | Integer count |
| 9 | Balloon-driver reclaiming | $M_{\text{reclaimed}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_{\text{target},i}^{\text{old}} - M_{\text{target},i}^{\text{new}}$ | Live memory reclaim | $\text{MB}$ |
| 10 | Migration downtime estimate | $T_{\text{down}} \propto \dfrac{M_{\text{dirty}} \times B_{\text{net}}^{-1}}{1}$ | Live VM migration | $\text{ms}$ |

Where the symbols used in the table mean:

- $T_{\text{quantum}}$ = Total scheduling quantum of the VMM.
- $w_i$ = weight of VM $i$.
- $vCPU_i$ = virtual CPUs assigned to VM $i$.
- $pCPU_{\text{host}}$ = number of physical CPU cores in the host.
- $\eta$ = scheduling efficiency factor ($0 < \eta \le 1$).
- $\rho$ = safety headroom reserve fraction.
- $M_{\text{dirty}}$ = amount of memory dirtied during pre-copy phase.
- $B_{\text{net}}$ = network bandwidth available for migration.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Virtualization is the *spinal cord* of:

- **Public Cloud IaaS** — AWS EC2, Azure VMs, GCP Compute Engine, DigitalOcean Droplets.
- **Private Cloud** — VMware vSphere, OpenStack (which uses KVM or Xen underneath).
- **DevOps & CI/CD** — Vagrant + VirtualBox spins up disposable test environments.
- **Disaster Recovery** — VM snapshots and live migration.
- **Sandboxing** — Process VMs (JVM) execute untrusted code safely.
- **Server Consolidation** — Reducing data-centre footprint and power costs by 5–10×.
- **Edge & Telecom (NFV)** — Network Functions Virtualization (5G core) runs on VMs/containers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Proving Whether x86 (Pre-2005) is Virtualizable

We must check the Popek-Goldberg condition:

$$
\text{Sensitive} \subseteq \text{Privileged}
$$

### Step 1 — Identify the set of Privileged Instructions on x86

In ring 3 (user mode), the following kinds of instructions trap to the kernel:

- `LIDT` (Load Interrupt Descriptor Table register)
- `LGDT` (Load Global Descriptor Table register)
- `LMSW` / `SMSW` (Load/Store Machine Status Word)
- `MOV to/from CR0`, `CR2`, `CR3`, `CR4` (Control Registers)
- `HLT` (Halt CPU)
- `IN`, `OUT` (Direct I/O)
- `CLI`, `STI` (Clear/Set Interrupt Flag — partly)

### Step 2 — Identify the Sensitive Instructions on x86

The famous 17 "non-virtualizable" x86 instructions are sensitive but **not** privileged. They include:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sensitive}_{\text{x86}} = \{ \text{SMSW},\ \text{SIDT},\ \text{SGST},\ \text{SLDT},\ \text{STR},\ \text{PUSHF},\ \text{POPF}, \\
\text{VERR},\ \text{VERW},\ \text{LAR},\ \text{LSL},\ \text{IN},\ \text{OUT},\ \text{INSB},\ \text{INSW},\ \text{OUTSB},\ \text{OUTSW} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Set-Theoretic Test

For each $x \in \text{Sensitive}_{\text{x86}}$, ask: "Does $x$ trap to the kernel when executed in ring 3?"

Take `SMSW` (Store Machine Status Word):

- In user mode, the **high-priority bits** of CR0 are *visible* to the guest.
- Therefore `SMSW` reads real hardware state even in user mode → it is **sensitive** (its result differs from what an OS-kernel-level execution would see).
- But `SMSW` in original 80386 did **not** trap in user mode → it is **NOT privileged**.

Hence:

$$
\exists\, x \in \text{Sensitive}_{\text{x86}} \quad \text{such that} \quad x \notin \text{Privileged}_{\text{x86}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{Sensitive}_{\text{x86}} \nsubseteq \text{Privileged}_{\text{x86}}
$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion

$$
\boxed{\text{Pre-2005 x86 is NOT efficiently virtualizable under Popek–Goldberg.}}
$$

This forces the use of **binary translation** (a runtime technique that rewrites such instructions into safe sequences that *do* trap). After 2005, Intel added **VT-x** with two new privilege rings (VMX root and VMX non-root), making all sensitive instructions privileged → x86 becomes fully virtualizable.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — VM Scheduling Quantum Allocation

Suppose a hypervisor has a scheduling quantum of $T = 80\ \text{ms}$ and the host has $p = 8$ physical cores. There are $n = 5$ VMs each pinned to a single vCPU.

### Step 1 — Determine the per-core quantum window

If using a simple round-robin per core, each core services its assigned VMs in a time-slice fashion. For VM $i$ on a particular core, the allocated slice is:

$$
t_i = \dfrac{T_{\text{quantum}}}{k_{\text{core}}}
$$

where $k_{\text{core}}$ is the number of VMs sharing that physical core.

If 2 of the 5 VMs share core 0, then:

$$
t_{i} = \dfrac{80\ \text{ms}}{2} = 40\ \text{ms}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the weighted share

If VM $i$ has weight $w_i$ on a core where the total weight is $W = \sum_j w_j$, the effective share is:

$$
s_i = \dfrac{w_i}{W} = \dfrac{w_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{k} w_j}
$$

For example, with three VMs of weights $w_1 = 2,\ w_2 = 1,\ w_3 = 1$ on the same core:

$$
s_1 = \dfrac{2}{2+1+1} = 0.5 \quad,\quad s_2 = s_3 = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

### Step 3 — Convert share to wall-clock time per second

$$
T_{i,\ \text{per sec}} = s_i \times 1\ \text{s}
$$

For VM 1:

$$
T_1 = 0.5 \times 1\ \text{s} = 500\ \text{ms / second}
$$

This means VM 1 gets the equivalent of half a physical core of CPU time, even though the host is "fully booked".

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — VM Density on a Host

A host has $pCPU_{\text{host}} = 16$ physical cores. The safety reserve $\rho = 0.20$ (keep 20 % of CPU unused for headroom and noisy-neighbour spikes). Average vCPU allocation per VM is $\overline{vCPU} = 2$.

Maximum VM density:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{pCPU_{\text{host}} \times (1 - \rho)}{\overline{vCPU}} \right\rfloor \\
  &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{16 \times (1 - 0.20)}{2} \right\rfloor \\
  &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{16 \times 0.80}{2} \right\rfloor \\
  &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{12.8}{2} \right\rfloor \\
  &= \left\lfloor 6.4 \right\rfloor \\
  &= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, **at most 6 VMs of size 2 vCPU can safely be packed onto this host** — the seventh would push utilisation above the safety headroom.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Virtualization Strategies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Table — KTU Frequently Asked Comparison.**

| Property | Full Virtualization (Software) | Para-Virtualization | Hardware-Assisted |
|----------|-------------------------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Guest OS modification** | None (unmodified) | Required (source needed) | None (unmodified) |
| **Performance overhead** | Medium (5–15 %) due to binary translation | Low (near native) | Very low (~1–3 %) |
| **Hardware support needed** | None (works on plain x86) | None | Intel VT-x / AMD-V required |
| **Sensitive-instruction handling** | Binary translation | Hypercalls | Hardware traps |
| **Example hypervisor** | VMware Workstation (early), VirtualBox | Xen (classic mode) | KVM, Hyper-V, ESXi, VirtualBox 6+ |
| **Windows guest support** | ✅ | ❌ (Windows cannot be modified) | ✅ |
| **Linux guest support** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **VM exit cost** | Medium | Low (cooperative) | Low–medium |
| **Ease of use** | High | Low (recompilation needed) | High |
| **Cloud relevance (2024)** | Legacy | Niche (containers dominate) | **Dominant** |

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Type 1 vs Type 2 Hypervisors

| Property | Type 1 (Bare-Metal) | Type 2 (Hosted) |
|----------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Where it runs** | Directly on hardware | On top of a host OS |
| **Performance** | High | Lower (extra OS layer) |
| **Examples** | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V (Server), Citrix XenServer, KVM | VMware Workstation, Oracle VirtualBox, Parallels Desktop |
| **Typical use** | Data centres, cloud, production | Desktop testing, dev, education |
| **Reliability** | Higher (no host OS to crash) | Lower (host OS failures affect VMs) |
| **Hardware access** | Direct | Indirect (through host OS) |
| **Cloud suitability** | ✅ Production | ❌ Generally not for cloud IaaS |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — High-Level Machine Virtualization Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef app fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1e3a8a
    classDef os fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#78350f
    classDef vmm fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#14532d
    classDef hw fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#7f1d1d

    App1["Guest App"]:::app
    App2["Guest App"]:::app
    App3["Guest App"]:::app

    GuestOS1["Guest OS VM 1"]:::os
    GuestOS2["Guest OS VM 2"]:::os
    GuestOS3["Guest OS VM 3"]:::os

    VMM["Hypervisor / VMM"]:::vmm

    CPU["Physical CPU"]:::hw
    RAM["Physical RAM"]:::hw
    Disk["Physical Storage"]:::hw
    NIC["Physical NIC"]:::hw

    App1 --> GuestOS1
    App2 --> GuestOS2
    App3 --> GuestOS3
    GuestOS1 --> VMM
    GuestOS2 --> VMM
    GuestOS3 --> VMM
    VMM --> CPU
    VMM --> RAM
    VMM --> Disk
    VMM --> NIC
```

**Reading guide:** The three guest operating systems on the right are completely independent; they only "touch" the real hardware through the hypervisor.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Type 1 vs Type 2 Hypervisor Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef t1 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#14532d
    classDef t2 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#1e3a8a
    classDef hw fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#7f1d1d
    classDef app fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#78350f

    subgraph Type1["TYPE 1 BARE METAL"]
        direction TB
        G1A["Guest OS A"]:::t1
        G1B["Guest OS B"]:::t1
        H1["Bare-Metal Hypervisor ESXi or KVM"]:::t1
        G1A --> H1
        G1B --> H1
        H1 --> HW1["Physical Hardware"]:::hw
    end

    subgraph Type2["TYPE 2 HOSTED"]
        direction TB
        G2A["Guest OS A"]:::t2
        G2B["Guest OS B"]:::t2
        H2["Hosted Hypervisor VirtualBox or VMware Workstation"]:::t2
        HostOS["Host OS Windows or Linux"]:::t2
        G2A --> H2
        G2B --> H2
        H2 --> HostOS
        HostOS --> HW2["Physical Hardware"]:::hw
    end
```

**Reading guide:** In Type 1, the hypervisor sits *directly* on the metal. In Type 2, the hypervisor is just another application of the host OS, so every VM trap must cross two software layers.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — VM Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created
    Created --> Stopped: VM Defined
    Stopped --> Starting: Power On
    Starting --> Running: Boot Complete
    Running --> Paused: Pause Request
    Paused --> Running: Resume Request
    Running --> Suspended: Save State to Disk
    Suspended --> Running: Restore State from Disk
    Running --> Migrating: Live Migration Initiated
    Migrating --> Running: Migration Complete on Target Host
    Running --> Stopped: Power Off
    Paused --> Stopped: Power Off
    Stopped --> [*]: VM Destroyed
```

**Reading guide:** A VM is a managed *resource* with a clearly defined lifecycle. Cloud portals (OpenStack Nova, AWS EC2) expose these as user actions: *Start*, *Stop*, *Reboot*, *Terminate*.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Full-Virtualization Binary Translation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef guest fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    classDef bt fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    classDef host fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
    classDef cpu fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c

    GV["Guest Code with Sensitive Instruction"]:::guest
    BT["Binary Translator in VMM"]:::bt
    Safe["Rewritten Safe Instruction Sequence"]:::bt
    Trap["Trap to VMM at Runtime"]:::host
    Emul["VMM Emulates Original Behaviour"]:::host
    Result["Correct Result Returned to Guest"]:::guest

    GV --> BT
    BT --> Safe
    Safe --> Trap
    Trap --> Emul
    Emul --> Result
    CPU["Physical CPU Executes Emulation"]:::cpu
    Emul --> CPU
```

**Reading guide:** Binary translation is *ahead-of-time* rewriting combined with *runtime* traps. The first time a sensitive x86 instruction is seen, the BT rewrites it; thereafter, the rewritten code runs natively, with a trap only on the very last instruction that touches privileged state.

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Hardware-Assisted Virtualization (Intel VT-x)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef guest fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    classDef host fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
    classDef cpu fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c

    GuestApp["Guest App"]:::guest
    GuestKernel["Guest Kernel Ring 0 in VMX Non Root"]:::guest
    VMCS["VMCS Hardware Structure VM Control Structure"]:::host
    VMM["VMM in VMX Root Mode"]:::host
    Exit["VM Exit Hardware Trap on Sensitive Instruction"]:::host
    Reentry["VM Entry Return to Guest"]:::host
    CPUCore["Physical CPU Core with VT x"]:::cpu

    GuestApp --> GuestKernel
    GuestKernel --> Exit
    Exit --> VMCS
    VMCS --> VMM
    VMM --> Reentry
    Reentry --> GuestKernel
    GuestKernel --> CPUCore
    VMM --> CPUCore
```

**Reading guide:** The hardware does the trapping for you. The VMCS (Virtual Machine Control Structure) is the data structure that saves/restores CPU state at every VM exit / VM entry boundary.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Set A]`
**Define machine virtualization. Differentiate between a System VM and a Process VM.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Machine Virtualization** is the abstraction of physical computing resources (CPU, memory, I/O) by a software layer called the *hypervisor*, so that multiple isolated virtual machines can run on one host.
>
> | Feature | System VM | Process VM |
> |---|---|---|
> | Purpose | Provides a complete system platform (its own kernel) | Provides a runtime for a *single* process |
> | Owns an OS? | Yes, hosts a full guest OS | No, it is invoked by a host process |
> | Started by | Hypervisor / cloud orchestrator | Application launcher (e.g., `java MyApp`) |
> | Lifetime | Days to months | Seconds to minutes (process lifetime) |
> | Examples | VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V | JVM, .NET CLR, BEAM (Erlang) |
> | Resource isolation | Strong (separate OS images) | Weak (shares host kernel) |

**[3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for 4 correct distinctions]**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Popek-Goldberg virtualization theorem and its three conditions.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Theorem:** A computer architecture is efficiently virtualizable if and only if its set of *sensitive instructions* is a *subset* of its *privileged instructions*.
>
> The three conditions are:
>
> 1. **Fidelity (Equivalence):** Programs running under the VMM must behave identically to those running on the bare hardware.
> 2. **Efficiency (Safety):** The VMM must not impose significant overhead; the bulk of instructions must run natively.
> 3. **Resource Control (Inspection):** The VMM must be able to maintain complete control over privileged resources; any unauthorised privileged access must trap to it.
>
> x86 (pre-2005) violated this because of 17 non-privileged yet sensitive instructions, forcing VMware to invent *binary translation*.

**[3 marks: 1 for theorem + 1 for three conditions + 1 for x86 comment]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Module 2 — Internal Choice Pair

> **Answer any ONE of the following:**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a **Type-1 (bare-metal) hypervisor** and a **Type-2 (hosted) hypervisor**. Compare them on at least **five** parameters. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain **Full Virtualization**, **Para-virtualization**, and **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization**. Discuss how the Popek-Goldberg theorem justifies the existence of *binary translation* on legacy x86 systems. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Architecture diagrams** (drawn neatly in the answer sheet, similar to *Diagram 2* in Section 4 above):

**Type-1 Hypervisor** (e.g., VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V Server):

- The hypervisor is installed *directly* on the physical hardware.
- The hardware layer is at the bottom.
- The hypervisor (VMM) sits above the hardware.
- Multiple guest OSes sit above the hypervisor.
- Each guest OS has its own user applications.

**Type-2 Hypervisor** (e.g., VMware Workstation, VirtualBox):

- The physical hardware is at the bottom.
- The *host operating system* (e.g., Windows 11, Ubuntu) sits on the hardware.
- The *hosted hypervisor* runs as a *process* inside the host OS.
- Guest OSes run *inside* the hypervisor, and they have their own applications.

**Comparison (any 5 of these 7 for full marks):**

| Parameter | Type 1 | Type 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Layer above hardware | Hypervisor | Host OS, then hypervisor |
| Performance | High | Lower (extra OS layer) |
| Reliability | Higher | Host OS crash ⇒ all VMs crash |
| Installation | Dedicated machine | Just an application |
| Hardware access | Direct | Indirect via host |
| Cloud suitability | ✅ (production) | ❌ (desktop/dev) |
| Examples | ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM | VirtualBox, Parallels, VMware Workstation |

**[7 marks = 2 for diagrams + 5 for comparison rows]**

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Full Virtualization** — Guest OS is *unmodified*. All sensitive instructions are intercepted and emulated by the VMM. On x86, this is achieved via **binary translation**: the VMM scans the guest's machine code blocks, finds sensitive-but-not-privileged instructions, and *rewrites* them into safe, trap-causing sequences at runtime. The guest "thinks" it is on bare metal. *Example: VMware Workstation (pre-VT-x).*

**Para-virtualization** — Guest OS is *modified* to know it is running in a VM. The guest kernel calls the VMM using **hypercalls** (e.g., Xen hypercall interface). Because both sides co-operate, performance is near-native. However, **closed-source OSes (Windows) cannot be para-virtualized** because their source is not available. *Example: Classic Xen with a custom Linux kernel.*

**Hardware-Assisted Virtualization** — The CPU itself supports virtualization via new instructions (Intel VT-x, AMD-V) and a new privilege mode. A new data structure called the **VMCS** (Virtual Machine Control Structure) holds guest state. The CPU automatically traps sensitive instructions to the VMM; the VMM emulates them and resumes the guest with `VMRESUME`. *Example: KVM, modern Hyper-V, modern ESXi.*

**Justification for Binary Translation via Popek-Goldberg:**

- The Popek-Goldberg theorem states that efficient virtualization requires `Sensitive ⊆ Privileged`.
- Pre-2005 x86 had **17 instructions that were sensitive but *not* privileged** (e.g., `SMSW`, `SIDT`, `POPF`).
- Therefore, `Sensitive ⊄ Privileged` on legacy x86 → x86 was *not* efficiently virtualizable.
- To get around this, **VMware invented binary translation**: a software technique that *rewrites* those 17 problematic instructions at runtime into safe sequences that *do* trap, thus artificially *making* the condition hold.
- This is a software patch for a hardware deficiency, and was the only way to virtualize x86 until Intel VT-x (2005) and AMD-V (2006) added hardware support.

**[7 marks = 2 for FV + 2 for PV + 2 for HAV + 1 for Popek-Goldberg binary-translation link]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What is a **Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM)**? Explain its **three classical functions**: *resource allocation*, *isolation*, and *interposition*. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **VM lifecycle** with a state diagram. Why is **live migration** important in cloud data centres? **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Definition:** A Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM), also called a **hypervisor**, is the software layer that creates, runs, and manages multiple virtual machines on a single physical host, presenting them with a virtualized view of the underlying hardware.

**Three classical functions:**

1. **Resource Allocation (Provisioning):**
   The VMM partitions the host's physical resources — CPU cycles, memory pages, disk blocks, network bandwidth — and assigns a share to each VM.
   - CPU: by time-slicing cores, weighted round-robin, or credit-based scheduling.
   - Memory: by maintaining shadow page tables that map guest-physical to host-physical memory.
   - Disk & network: by rate-limiting I/O queues and emulating virtual NICs / virtual disks.

2. **Isolation:**
   The VMM guarantees that one VM cannot read or modify the memory, disk, or CPU state of another VM, even if both VMs are on the same physical host.
   - Implemented through hardware page-table isolation, dedicated I/O channels, and privilege enforcement.
   - *Why important*: multi-tenant clouds must prevent malicious or buggy tenants from harming others.

3. **Interposition (Inspection & Mediation):**
   The VMM sits *between* the guest and the real hardware, so every privileged operation by a guest *must* pass through it.
   - This enables features such as VM snapshotting, live migration, debugging (record/replay), and security monitoring.
   - The *trap-and-emulate* mechanism makes interposition possible.

**[7 marks = 1 for definition + 2 for allocation + 2 for isolation + 2 for interposition]**

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**VM Lifecycle State Diagram** (as in *Diagram 3* of Section 4):

States:

- **Created (Defined):** VM metadata is registered (CPU, RAM, disk image). It exists as a *record* but uses no resources.
- **Stopped:** Disk image is allocated, but VM is *off*. No CPU or RAM is used.
- **Starting:** Hypervisor allocates RAM, attaches virtual disks, boots the guest kernel.
- **Running:** Guest is executing. Resources are being scheduled.
- **Paused:** VMM freezes CPU state; RAM and disk remain allocated. Useful for snapshots.
- **Suspended:** Entire CPU + RAM state is *serialized to disk*. Useful for long-term hibernation.
- **Migrating:** Live migration in progress — VM is being moved to another host.
- **Destroyed (Terminated):** All resources released. Disk image may be retained or deleted.

**Importance of Live Migration in Cloud Data Centres:**

1. **Zero-Downtime Maintenance:** Hosts can be patched, upgraded, or repaired by moving VMs away first.
2. **Load Balancing:** When a host becomes a hot-spot (e.g., CPU > 80 %), VMs can be transparently migrated to a cooler host.
3. **Power Management:** VMs can be consolidated onto fewer hosts at night so that unused hosts can be powered down (greener data centres).
4. **Disaster Avoidance:** Move VMs away from a host showing signs of hardware failure *before* it crashes.
5. **Data Centre Evacuation:** Move thousands of VMs off a flooded/burning data centre to a sibling facility.

Live migration uses a **pre-copy + stop-and-copy** strategy: pages are iteratively copied while the VM runs, then a final short pause copies dirty pages — downtime is often under a second.

**[7 marks = 3 for state diagram + 4 for five importance points]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically LOSE marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Sensitive vs Privileged instructions.** Sensitive = behaviour depends on mode. Privileged = traps in user mode. Most answer scripts *say* "sensitive = privileged" and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the year 2005.** When asked "why binary translation was invented", students forget to mention that pre-2005 x86 had 17 non-virtualizable instructions.
> 3. **Drawing wrong hypervisor diagrams.** Type 1 = hypervisor *directly* on hardware. Type 2 = hypervisor *on top of host OS*. Many students draw the *same* diagram twice.
> 4. **Writing "VirtualBox is Type 1" or "Hyper-V is Type 2".** Incorrect. VirtualBox = Type 2. Hyper-V Server = Type 1.
> 5. **Missing the Popek-Goldberg year (1974) and authors.** A 1-mark separate question is dedicated to naming them.
> 6. **Ignoring Process VMs.** If a question says "with examples", mentioning *only* VMware is incomplete — you should mention *both* System VMs and Process VMs (JVM, .NET CLR).
> 7. **Forgetting to state that para-virtualization cannot run Windows.** Always mention that source-modification is required.
> 8. **Not drawing the state diagram for VM lifecycle.** A description alone gets only partial credit — the *diagram* is worth at least 2 of 7 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for the 14-mark answer.**

- [x] **Virtualization** = running multiple isolated OS instances on one physical host via a software layer.
- [x] **Hypervisor (VMM)** is the landlord; **Guest OS** is the tenant; **Host** is the building.
- [x] **Type 1** = bare-metal (ESXi, Hyper-V Server, KVM) — direct on hardware, high performance, used in clouds.
- [x] **Type 2** = hosted (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation) — sits on host OS, lower performance, used on desktops.
- [x] **System VM** = full OS; **Process VM** = single process (JVM, .NET CLR).
- [x] **Popek-Goldberg (1974):** Sensitive ⊆ Privileged ⟺ efficiently virtualizable.
- [x] **Sensitive = Control-sensitive ∪ Behaviour-sensitive.**
- [x] **x86 pre-2005** had 17 non-privileged yet sensitive instructions → **not** virtualizable.
- [x] **Binary translation** = software fix to the 17 problematic x86 instructions.
- [x] **Full Virtualization** = unmodified guest, binary translation (or hardware assist).
- [x] **Para-virtualization** = modified guest, hypercalls, near-native, **cannot run Windows**.
- [x] **Hardware-Assisted** = Intel VT-x / AMD-V, VMCS structure, `VMLAUNCH`/`VMRESUME`.
- [x] **VM lifecycle states:** Created → Stopped → Starting → Running ↔ Paused ↔ Suspended → Migrating → Terminated.
- [x] **Live migration** enables maintenance without downtime, load balancing, power savings.
- [x] **Server consolidation** boosts utilization from 10–15 % to 70–85 %.
- [x] **VM density** $D = \left\lfloor pCPU \cdot (1 - \rho) \, / \, \overline{vCPU} \right\rfloor$.
- [x] **CPU share** $s_i = w_i / \sum_j w_j$.
- [x] **Three VMM functions:** resource allocation, isolation, interposition.
- [x] **Examples to memorize:** ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM, Xen, VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, JVM, .NET CLR.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Sensitive ⊆ Privileged, binary translation bridges the gap, hardware assist closes it for good, and the hypervisor plays landlord to every VM in the cloud."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Virtualized vs Virtualized Machine Environments. Types of VMs - Process VM vs System VM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-Virtualized vs Virtualized Machine Environments. Types of VMs - Process VM vs System VM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Non-Virtualized Machine Environment (Native / Bare-Metal Environment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> A *Non-Virtualized Machine Environment* (also called a *Native Environment* or *Bare-Metal Environment*) is a computing architecture in which a **single operating system** runs **directly on top of the physical hardware** without any intermediate abstraction layer. The OS kernel, device drivers, and applications share direct, exclusive control over the underlying CPU, memory, storage, and I/O subsystems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Single-Tenant House"**
> Imagine a physical house where only **one family** lives. The family has **direct access** to every brick, switch, and tap. There is no landlord, no property manager, no shared walls. If the family needs more rooms, they must **buy a new house**. Similarly, in a non-virtualized setup, if an application needs more compute power, you must provision a **new physical server**.

**Key Properties of a Non-Virtualized Environment:**
- **Direct hardware access** — the OS executes privileged instructions natively on the CPU.
- **One-to-one mapping** — one OS instance per physical machine.
- **Tightly coupled** — the OS kernel is compiled and tuned for that specific hardware SKU.
- **No hypervisor** — no Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM) layer.
- **Low overhead** — performance is near-native because there is no software emulation.
- **Static resource allocation** — resources (RAM, cores) are fixed at boot time and rarely change dynamically.

**Typical Workloads:** Legacy enterprise applications, high-performance computing (HPC) clusters that require direct RDMA access, real-time industrial controllers, and database servers requiring deterministic I/O latency.

---

### 1.2 Virtualized Machine Environment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> A *Virtualized Machine Environment* is a computing architecture in which a **software layer called the hypervisor (VMM)** is inserted between the physical hardware and one or more *Guest Operating Systems*. Each Guest OS runs inside an isolated container called a *Virtual Machine (VM)* and perceives the underlying hardware as a **dedicated, abstracted resource pool** carved out by the hypervisor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Apartment Building"**
> Picture a tall apartment building sitting on one plot of land. The building management (the *hypervisor*) divides the plot into multiple self-contained apartments (the *VMs*). Each apartment has its own door, kitchen, plumbing, and electricity meter. The tenants (*guest OS + applications*) feel as if they own the whole apartment, but in reality, the manager is slicing a single physical resource and leasing it out. You can add a new tenant simply by building another floor — no new land required.

**Key Properties of a Virtualized Environment:**
- **Hardware abstraction** — the hypervisor emulates/virtualizes CPU, memory, disk, and network.
- **Many-to-one mapping** — many VMs share one physical host.
- **Loose coupling** — guest OS is unaware of the real physical hardware; it interacts with *virtual* devices.
- **Hypervisor presence** — Type-1 (bare-metal: VMware ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM) or Type-2 (hosted: VirtualBox, VMware Workstation).
- **Higher overhead** — but modern **hardware-assisted virtualization** (Intel VT-x, AMD-V) reduces this to **2%–5%**.
- **Dynamic resource allocation** — live migration, hot-plug of vCPUs/RAM, vertical and horizontal scaling.

> [!TIP]
> **Why it matters for KTU:** Cloud Computing as a discipline is fundamentally built on **virtualization**. Without VMs, there would be no elastic IaaS, no multi-tenant public cloud, no live migration. The rest of Module 2 builds upon this foundation.

---

### 1.3 Side-by-Side Snapshot (Quick Reference)

| Dimension | Non-Virtualized | Virtualized |
|---|---|---|
| Abstraction Layer | None (OS → Hardware) | Hypervisor (OS → VMM → Hardware) |
| OS Instances per Host | **1** | **N (typically 10–100)** |
| Resource Utilization | Low (often **15%–20%**) | High (**60%–80%**) |
| Isolation | Process-level only | VM-level (strong) |
| Migration | Difficult (physical move) | Live migration supported |
| Boot Time | Hardware-dependent | Seconds to minutes |
| Failure Blast Radius | Entire host | Single VM (others unaffected) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Stack — What Changes When You Add Virtualization?

**Non-Virtualized Stack (Bottom-Up):**
1. **Physical Hardware** — CPU, RAM, Disk, NIC.
2. **Host Operating System Kernel** — directly schedules hardware.
3. **System Libraries & Runtime**.
4. **Application Processes** — share OS resources via system calls.

**Virtualized Stack (Bottom-Up):**
1. **Physical Hardware**.
2. **Hypervisor / VMM** — traps privileged instructions, schedules "virtual" CPUs, manages virtual memory.
3. **Virtual Hardware** — emulated/virtualized CPU, RAM, disk (VMDK), NIC (vNIC).
4. **Guest Operating System** — believes it owns the hardware exclusively.
5. **Guest Libraries & Runtime**.
6. **Guest Application Processes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Layer**
> The hypervisor exists to **solve three classical problems**:
> 1. **Resource Partitioning** — How do we divide one CPU among 20 guests?
> 2. **Isolation** — How do we ensure VM-A's kernel crash doesn't take down VM-B?
> 3. **Transparency** — How do we make a Linux VM feel "at home" on Intel hardware even when it was originally written for SPARC?

---

### 2.2 Types of Virtualization (Foundation for VM Classification)

- **Full Virtualization** — Guest OS is **unmodified**; binary translation + hardware-assisted virtualization handle privileged instructions. (e.g., VMware ESXi, KVM).
- **Para-Virtualization** — Guest OS is **modified** to issue *hypercalls* directly to the hypervisor. (e.g., Xen in classic mode).
- **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization** — Intel VT-x / AMD-V provide **new CPU rings (Ring -1)** so that guest privileged instructions can execute natively with a single context switch.
- **OS-Level Virtualization (Containers)** — Kernel is shared; only user-space is isolated (Docker, LXC). *Not a VM by strict definition.*

---

### 2.3 Types of VMs — The Two Grand Families

#### A. Process Virtual Machine (Application VM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**
> A *Process Virtual Machine* is a **runtime engine** that exists for the **lifetime of a single process**. It provides a **platform-independent abstract execution environment** for a specific program. The VM is instantiated when the process starts and terminated when the process exits.

**Core Characteristics:**
- **Single-process scope** — one VM instance = one application.
- **Language-specific** — designed for a particular language or bytecode instruction set.
- **Platform independence** — "Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA).
- **Provides higher-level abstractions** — memory management, garbage collection, exception handling, JIT compilation.
- **No full OS emulation** — the host OS still handles syscalls, file I/O, networking.

**Canonical Examples:**
| Process VM | Hosted Language | Developer |
|---|---|---|
| **JVM** (Java Virtual Machine) | Java, Scala, Kotlin, Groovy | Oracle / OpenJDK |
| **CLR** (Common Language Runtime) | C#, F#, VB.NET | Microsoft |
| **BEAM** (Erlang VM) | Erlang, Elixir | Ericsson |
| **Parrot** | Perl 6 (Raku), various | Open-source |
| **Dalvik / ART** | Android (DEX bytecode) | Google |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Personal Translator"**
> Think of a Process VM as a *UN-style simultaneous interpreter* sitting next to a diplomat. The diplomat (your Java code) speaks French; the interpreter converts every sentence into the local language of whatever country (Windows, Linux, macOS) the diplomat is visiting that day. The interpreter is born when the meeting starts and retires when it ends.

#### B. System Virtual Machine (Hardware VM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**
> A *System Virtual Machine* provides a **complete, self-contained replica of an entire physical computer** — including a virtual BIOS, virtual CPU, virtual RAM, virtual disks, and virtual I/O devices. It can host an **unmodified, full-fledged guest operating system** that, from its own perspective, believes it is running on bare metal.

**Core Characteristics:**
- **Full-system scope** — emulates the entire hardware platform.
- **OS-agnostic** — can run Windows, Linux, FreeBSD, Solaris simultaneously on one host.
- **Hypervisor-managed** — Type-1 (bare-metal) or Type-2 (hosted).
- **Strong isolation** — each VM has its own kernel, drivers, userspace.
- **Resource-pools** — vCPUs, vRAM, vDisk are carved from physical resources.

**Canonical Examples:**
| System VM | Hypervisor Type | Vendor |
|---|---|---|
| **VMware vSphere / ESXi** | Type-1 | VMware (Broadcom) |
| **Microsoft Hyper-V** | Type-1 (hybrid) | Microsoft |
| **KVM** (Kernel-based VM) | Type-1 (Linux kernel module) | Open-source (Red Hat) |
| **Xen** | Type-1 | Linux Foundation |
| **VirtualBox** | Type-2 | Oracle |
| **VMware Workstation / Fusion** | Type-2 | VMware |
| **QEMU** (with KVM) | Type-1 + emulation | Open-source |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Replica Office Building"**
> Imagine a *System VM* as a full-scale *architectural replica* of a real office building, built inside a warehouse. The replica has its own elevator (vCPU), its own electrical wiring (vRAM), its own front door (vNIC). A new company (guest OS) moves in, sets up its own furniture, hires its own staff (applications), and operates **as if it were in the real building**, completely unaware that the walls are plywood inside a steel warehouse.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Value | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **VMM** | Virtual Machine Monitor — software that creates and runs VMs | **Core terminology** |
| **Type-1 Hypervisor** | Runs directly on hardware (no host OS) | Asked every year |
| **Type-2 Hypervisor** | Runs as an application on a host OS | Asked every year |
| **Hypercall** | A syscall from guest OS directly to hypervisor (para-virtualization) | High-yield |
| **Ring -1** | New CPU privilege level introduced by Intel VT-x / AMD-V | Frequently tested |
| **Live Migration** | Moving a running VM from one host to another with zero downtime | Cloud-essentials |
| **vCPU** | Virtual CPU — a time-share of one or more physical cores | Capacity-planning |
| **Overcommitment** | Allocating more virtual resources than physical (e.g., 200 vCPUs on 64 cores) | Efficiency metric |
| **JVM** | Process VM for Java bytecode | Standard example |
| **WORA** | Write Once, Run Anywhere — Java's portability promise | Concept anchor |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why a Cloud Engineer Must Master This

- **Capacity Planning** — Determines how many tenants you can host per rack.
- **SLA Design** — VM isolation is the basis for multi-tenant SLAs.
- **Security Architecture** — VM-level isolation is stronger than container-level.
- **Cost Engineering** — Consolidation ratios directly drive TCO (Total Cost of Ownership).
- **DevOps Tooling** — Vagrant, Packer, Terraform all manipulate System VMs; JVM tuning is core to every backend microservice.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Consolidation Math

> [!NOTE]
> Although this topic is **architectural** rather than purely algebraic, the KTU 2024 Scheme frequently expects students to demonstrate *quantitative reasoning* about resource consolidation. Below is an exhaustive derivation of the standard cloud-capacity metrics.

### 3.1 Derivation of the VM Consolidation Ratio (CR)

The **Consolidation Ratio** quantifies how many virtual machines a single physical host can effectively sustain. This is the **single most important metric** for IaaS providers like AWS, Azure, and GCP.

**Step 1 — Define the variables.**

Let:
- $N_{VM}$ be the **total number of active VMs** running on a physical host.
- $N_{Host}$ be the **total number of physical hosts** in the cluster.

**Step 2 — State the basic formula.**

The naive (ideal) consolidation ratio is the ratio of VMs to hosts:

$$CR = \frac{N_{VM}}{N_{Host}}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the realistic adjustments.**

In practice, the hypervisor reserves a portion of physical resources for its own operation (memory for the VMM kernel, CPU cycles for I/O forwarding, etc.). Let:
- $R_{reserved}$ be the fraction of physical resources reserved for the hypervisor (typically $\approx 0.10$, i.e., **10%**).
- $S_{i}$ be the average resource demand of the $i$-th VM (CPU cores, RAM in GB).

**Step 4 — Compute the effective available capacity per host.**

For CPU (let $C_{phys}$ = physical core count):

$$C_{usable} = C_{phys} \times (1 - R_{reserved})$$

For Memory (let $M_{phys}$ = physical RAM in GB):

$$M_{usable} = M_{phys} \times (1 - R_{reserved})$$

**Step 5 — Determine the bottleneck-constrained VM count.**

The number of VMs that can fit is limited by whichever resource (CPU or RAM) saturates first.

$$N_{VM}^{CPU} = \left\lfloor \frac{C_{usable}}{\overline{S}_{CPU}} \right\rfloor$$

$$N_{VM}^{RAM} = \left\lfloor \frac{M_{usable}}{\overline{S}_{RAM}} \right\rfloor$$

where $\overline{S}_{CPU}$ is the **mean vCPU demand per VM** and $\overline{S}_{RAM}$ is the **mean RAM demand per VM**.

**Step 6 — Apply the min-function (Amdahl-style bottleneck resolution).**

$$N_{VM}^{max} = \min\!\left( N_{VM}^{CPU},\ N_{VM}^{RAM} \right)$$

**Step 7 — The realistic consolidation ratio.**

$$CR_{real} = \frac{N_{VM}^{max}}{N_{Host}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

*Problem:* A data-center rack has **4 physical hosts**, each with **64 CPU cores** and **256 GB RAM**. The hypervisor reserves **10%** of resources. Average per-VM demand: **2 vCPUs** and **8 GB RAM**.

*Solution walkthrough:*

**Step A — Compute usable resources per host.**

$$C_{usable} = 64 \times (1 - 0.10) = 64 \times 0.90 = 57.6\ \text{cores}$$

$$M_{usable} = 256 \times (1 - 0.10) = 256 \times 0.90 = 230.4\ \text{GB}$$

**Step B — Compute VM count limited by CPU.**

$$N_{VM}^{CPU} = \left\lfloor \frac{57.6}{2} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 28.8 \rfloor = 28\ \text{VMs}$$

**Step C — Compute VM count limited by RAM.**

$$N_{VM}^{RAM} = \left\lfloor \frac{230.4}{8} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 28.8 \rfloor = 28\ \text{VMs}$$

**Step D — Apply the bottleneck function.**

$$N_{VM}^{max} = \min(28,\ 28) = 28\ \text{VMs per host}$$

**Step E — Compute the realistic consolidation ratio across the rack.**

$$CR_{real} = \frac{28 \times 4}{4} = 28\ \text{VMs per host}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This perfectly balanced scenario gives 28 VMs per host. In real cloud environments, **RAM is the typical bottleneck**, so engineers over-provision RAM to lift the floor.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Virtualization Overhead

When a hypervisor is present, each VM consumes a small slice of "overhead" — CPU cycles and RAM that belong to the hypervisor's bookkeeping, I/O emulation, and shadow page tables.

**Step 1 — Total physical resource consumed by a VM.**

$$R_{total} = R_{workload} + R_{hypervisor}$$

where $R_{workload}$ is what the guest application needs and $R_{hypervisor}$ is the per-VM tax.

**Step 2 — Express overhead as a percentage.**

$$OH\% = \frac{R_{hypervisor}}{R_{total}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Substitute a typical value.**

For modern hardware-assisted virtualization, $R_{hypervisor} \approx 0.03 \times R_{total}$, giving:

$$OH\% = \frac{0.03 \times R_{total}}{R_{total}} \times 100\% = 3\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** Examiners love asking students to *compare* this **3% overhead** with the **15%–20% CPU wastage** in a non-virtualized environment. The math is straightforward but the *interpretation* is what scores marks.

---

### 3.3 Conceptual Derivation — How a Process VM Executes Java Bytecode

This is a **frequently asked 7-mark question** in KTU. Let's walk through it step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Compilation Phase.**

The Java compiler (`javac`) transforms `.java` source code into **platform-neutral bytecode**, stored in `.class` files. The bytecode instruction set is **identical on every CPU architecture**.

**Step 2 — Class Loading Phase.**

When the user runs `java HelloWorld`, the **Class Loader Subsystem** of the JVM:
- Loads `.class` files via the **Bootstrap ClassLoader** (from `rt.jar` / `jrt-fs`).
- Delegates to the **Extension ClassLoader** (legacy `lib/ext`).
- Finally the **Application ClassLoader** loads the user classpath.

**Step 3 — Bytecode Verification.**

The **Bytecode Verifier** statically checks that the bytecode does not violate JVM safety rules (no illegal casts, no stack underflow, no forged pointers). This is the **security gate** that makes Java "sandboxed".

**Step 4 — Execution Phase.**

The **Execution Engine** of the JVM then runs the bytecode using one (or a combination) of:
- **Interpreter** — executes bytecode line-by-line (slow start, no warm-up cost).
- **JIT (Just-In-Time) Compiler** — detects *hot methods* and compiles them to native machine code, caching the result in **Code Cache**. Common JIT: C1 (client), C2 (server), **Graal** (modern).
- **AOT (Ahead-of-Time) Compilation** — `jaotc` pre-compiles to native, used for fast startup.

**Step 5 — Runtime Data Areas.**

Memory is divided into:
- **Method Area** — class metadata, constant pool, static variables.
- **Heap** — all objects, managed by **Garbage Collectors** (G1, ZGC, Shenandoah, Parallel GC).
- **Stack** — one stack per thread, holding frames.
- **PC Register** — current instruction address per thread.
- **Native Method Stack** — for C/C++ `JNI` calls.

**Step 6 — Termination.**

When the `main()` method returns and all non-daemon threads terminate, the JVM tears down the runtime, releases memory back to the host OS, and the *Process VM* ceases to exist.

> [!NOTE]
> **Contrast with System VM:** A System VM, once started, persists as long as the guest OS is running — its lifetime is **minutes, hours, or years**. A Process VM's lifetime is **the duration of one process** — typically seconds to minutes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Non-Virtualized (Native) Environment — Architectural Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HW1[PHYSICAL HARDWARE] --> OS1[HOST OPERATING SYSTEM]
    OS1 --> LIB1[SYSTEM LIBRARIES AND RUNTIME]
    LIB1 --> APP1[APPLICATION PROCESS A]
    LIB1 --> APP2[APPLICATION PROCESS B]
    LIB1 --> APP3[APPLICATION PROCESS C]

    style HW1 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style OS1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style LIB1 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style APP1 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000
    style APP2 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000
    style APP3 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A single rectangle stack — Applications sit *directly* on the host OS which sits *directly* on the hardware. There is no intermediary. Each application shares the kernel's resources via system calls.

---

### 4.2 Virtualized Environment — System VM Stack (Type-1 Hypervisor)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HW2[PHYSICAL HARDWARE] --> HYP[HYPERVISOR VMM TYPE-1]
    HYP --> VHARD1[VIRTUAL HARDWARE VM1]
    HYP --> VHARD2[VIRTUAL HARDWARE VM2]
    HYP --> VHARD3[VIRTUAL HARDWARE VM3]

    VHARD1 --> GOS1[GUEST OS LINUX]
    VHARD2 --> GOS2[GUEST OS WINDOWS]
    VHARD3 --> GOS3[GUEST OS FREEBSD]

    GOS1 --> GAPPS1[GUEST APPLICATIONS]
    GOS2 --> GAPPS2[GUEST APPLICATIONS]
    GOS3 --> GAPPS3[GUEST APPLICATIONS]

    style HW2 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style HYP fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style VHARD1 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000
    style VHARD2 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000
    style VHARD3 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000
    style GOS1 fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000
    style GOS2 fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000
    style GOS3 fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000
    style GAPPS1 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000
    style GAPPS2 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000
    style GAPPS3 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The hypervisor (red band) sits between the hardware and multiple *parallel* guest stacks. Each guest believes it has exclusive ownership of the virtual hardware.

---

### 4.3 Process VM Internal Architecture (Java Virtual Machine Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC[.JAVA SOURCE] --> COMP[JAVAC COMPILER]
    COMP --> BC[BYTECODE .CLASS]
    BC --> CLS[CLASS LOADER SUBSYSTEM]
    CLS --> VER[BYTECODE VERIFIER]
    VER --> EE[EXECUTION ENGINE]
    EE --> INT[INTERPRETER]
    EE --> JIT[JIT COMPILER]
    EE --> GC[GARBAGE COLLECTOR]
    EE --> MEM[RUNTIME DATA AREAS]

    JIT --> NC[NATIVE MACHINE CODE]
    NC --> CPU[HOST CPU X86 ARM]

    style SRC fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000
    style COMP fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000
    style BC fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000
    style CLS fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000
    style VER fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000
    style EE fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style INT fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style JIT fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style GC fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style MEM fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follows the lifecycle of a Java program — from source compilation through to JIT-compiled native execution on the host CPU. The three purple blocks (Interpreter, JIT, GC) plus Runtime Data Areas constitute the **Execution Engine** sub-system.

---

### 4.4 Taxonomy of Virtual Machines — Master Classification Tree

```mermaid

    VM[VIRTUAL MACHINES]

    PVM --> ART[Android ART]

    SVM --> T1[TYPE-1 BARE METAL]
```
