# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`**

### Q1. Define Hardware-Assisted Virtualization. List any two key CPU features that enable it. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

Hardware-Assisted Virtualization is a CPU architectural technique in which the processor provides dedicated instruction-set extensions that allow a Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM) to efficiently run multiple guest operating systems without modifying them. The hardware itself creates an isolated execution environment and traps sensitive instructions automatically.

The two key enabling features are:
1. **Intel VT-x** (Virtualization Technology) — introduces **VMX root mode**, **VMX non-root mode**, and the **VMCS (Virtual Machine Control Structure)**.
2. **AMD-V (SVM)** — provides equivalent functionality via the **VMCB (Virtual Machine Control Block)** and a hypervisor mode.

> [!TIP]  
> **Valuation tip:** Always mention **VMX root/non-root mode** — examiners specifically look for this term.

---

### Q2. What is a VMCS? State its two main constituent areas. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The **VMCS (Virtual Machine Control Structure)** is a 4 KB-aligned data structure in physical memory used by Intel VT-x to manage the state and behavior of a virtual machine. It is pointed to by the `IA32_VMX_BASIC` configuration and accessed via the `VMPTRLD` instruction.

The two main constituent areas are:
1. **Guest-State Area** — Stores the guest's register values, control registers, RIP, RSP, MSRs, and segment selectors; these are **saved on VM Exit** and **restored on VM Entry**.
2. **Host-State Area** — Stores the VMM's (host's) register values, RIP, RSP, and segment selectors; these are **loaded on VM Exit** so the hypervisor can resume execution at its handler.

(Additional areas include VM-Execution Controls, VM-Exit Controls, VM-Entry Controls, and VM-Exit Information.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU End Semester Exam – December 2024, Official Pattern]`**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Intel VT-x architecture** including VMX root and non-root modes. Discuss the role of the **VMCS** with its major fields.   **`[7 Marks]`**

**(b)** Explain the **Extended Page Tables (EPT)** mechanism for memory virtualization in hardware-assisted virtualization. Illustrate the two-level address translation process with a diagram and compute the EPT memory overhead for 25 VMs each allocated 8 GB of RAM (page size = 4 KB, PTE size = 8 bytes, 4-level EPT).   **`[7 Marks]`**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**Step 1 — VT-x Architecture Overview (2 marks)**

Intel VT-x introduces a new mode called **VMX (Virtual Machine Extensions) operation** which partitions the CPU into two mutually exclusive sub-modes:

- **VMX Root Mode**: Where the VMM (hypervisor) executes. The VMM has full host privileges (real ring 0).
- **VMX Non-Root Mode**: Where guest operating systems execute. Even though the guest OS believes it is in ring 0, it is actually running in a hardware-deprivileged state.

**Step 2 — VMCS Role (3 marks)**

The VMCS is a 4 KB-aligned in-memory data structure managed by the VMM. It contains:

| Field Group | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Guest-State Area | Saved on VM Exit, restored on VM Entry |
| Host-State Area | Loaded on VM Exit |
| VM-Execution Controls | Decides what triggers a VM Exit (I/O bitmaps, MSR bitmaps, exception bitmap) |
| VM-Exit Controls | Configures VM Exit behavior |
| VM-Entry Controls | Configures VM Entry behavior |
| VM-Exit Information | Diagnoses the cause of the most recent exit |

**Step 3 — VM Exit / VM Entry Cycle (2 marks)**

When a guest executes a privileged instruction:
1. The hardware consults the VMCS execution controls.
2. If the action is configured to trap, the CPU automatically performs a **VM Exit** — saving the guest state and loading the host state.
3. The VMM handler runs.
4. The VMM executes `VMRESUME` to perform a **VM Entry** — restoring the guest state.

*(Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Step 1 — EPT Concept (2 marks)**

In software virtualization, every guest memory access required shadowing by the VMM. With **EPT (Extended Page Tables)**, the MMU performs **two page-table walks in hardware**:

- Walk 1: Translates **GVA (Guest Virtual Address) → GPA (Guest Physical Address)** using the guest page tables.
- Walk 2: Translates **GPA → HPA (Host Physical Address)** using the EPT pointed to by `EPTP` in the VMCS.

The TLB caches the combined GVA→HPA result, eliminating hypervisor intervention in the common case.

**Step 2 — EPT Memory Computation (5 marks)**

**Given:** $N_{\text{VM}} = 25$, $M_{\text{RAM}} = 8 \text{ GB} = 2^{33} \text{ bytes}$, $P_{\text{size}} = 4 \text{ KB} = 2^{12} \text{ bytes}$, $P_{\text{PTE}} = 8 \text{ bytes}$.

**Pages per VM:**

$$
M_{\text{pages}} = \frac{2^{33}}{2^{12}} = 2^{21} = 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ pages}
$$

**Bottom-level (PT) PTE cost:**

$$
M_{\text{PT}} = 2^{21} \times 8 \text{ bytes} = 16 \text{ MB}
$$

**Each higher level:** Holds 512 entries per table, walks down 2⁹ each level.

$$
M_{\text{PD}} = M_{\text{PDPT}} = M_{\text{PML4}} = 16 \text{ MB}
$$

**EPT cost per VM:**

$$
M_{\text{EPT, VM}} = 16 + 16 + 16 + 16 = 64 \text{ MB}
$$

**Total EPT for 25 VMs:**

$$
M_{\text{EPT, total}} = 25 \times 64 \text{ MB} = 1600 \text{ MB} = 1.6 \text{ GB}
$$

**Memory tax:**

$$
F_{\text{tax}} = \frac{1.6}{25 \times 8} \times 100\% = \frac{1.6}{200} \times 100\% = 0.8\%
$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Alt Choice)]`**

**(a)** Compare **Full Virtualization, Para-Virtualization, and Hardware-Assisted Virtualization** with respect to guest OS modification, performance, CPU requirements, and hypervisor complexity. List real-world examples of each.   **`[7 Marks]`**

**(b)** Explain the **Popek and Goldberg virtualization criteria**. Why was the original x86 architecture non-virtualizable? How did Intel VT-x solve this problem?   **`[7 Marks]`**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**Step 1 — Full Virtualization (Pure Software) (2 marks)**

- **No guest OS modification** required.
- Privileged instructions are trapped via software binary translation (BT) or trap-and-emulate.
- **Performance:** Lowest of the three due to translation overhead.
- **CPU requirements:** None (runs on any x86).
- **Hypervisor complexity:** Very high.
- **Examples:** Early VMware Workstation, QEMU (TCG mode), Microsoft Virtual PC.

**Step 2 — Para-Virtualization (2 marks)**

- **Guest OS must be modified** to use "hypercalls" that explicitly ask the VMM for privileged operations.
- **Performance:** Better than full virtualization but less than hardware-assisted.
- **CPU requirements:** None.
- **Hypervisor complexity:** Moderate (handles hypercalls).
- **Examples:** Classic Xen, MINIX, early AWS EC2 (before HVM).

**Step 3 — Hardware-Assisted Virtualization (2 marks)**

- **No guest OS modification** required.
- Privileged instructions are trapped by **hardware (CPU)** via VMCS-configured bitmaps.
- **Performance:** Highest — near-native.
- **CPU requirements:** Requires **Intel VT-x** or **AMD-V**.
- **Hypervisor complexity:** Moderate (HW offloads much work).
- **Examples:** **KVM, VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen-HVM, AWS Nitro**.

**Step 4 — Comparison Table (1 mark)** — *(See Section 2.5 of these notes for the full table.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**Step 1 — Popek-Goldberg Criteria (3 marks)**

In 1974, Popek and Goldberg formalized the requirements for a CPU architecture to be efficiently virtualizable:

1. **Fidelity:** Programs in a VM must behave identically to those on the bare machine (excluding timing).
2. **Safety/Resource Control:** The VMM must have complete and exclusive control over all hardware resources.
3. **Efficiency:** The vast majority of guest instructions must execute natively without VMM intervention.

**Theorem:** A machine is *efficiently virtualizable* if and only if its set of **sensitive instructions** is a **strict subset** of its set of **privileged instructions**.

**Step 2 — Why x86 Failed (2 marks)**

The pre-2005 x86 architecture had **17 "non-virtualizable" instructions** — including `POPF`, `PUSHF`, `SMSW`, `PUSHF`, `SGDT`, `SIDT`, `SLDT`, and `STR`. These instructions:
- Could be **executed in user mode** (non-privileged), yet
- **Modified privileged state** (sensitive).

This violated the Popek-Goldberg condition. Workarounds (binary translation, para-virtualization) were either slow or required guest OS modification.

**Step 3 — How Intel VT-x Solved the Problem (2 marks)**

Intel VT-x (2005) introduced:
- **VMX root and non-root modes** — creating a clean privilege boundary enforced in silicon.
- **VMCS** — hardware-managed structure to configure which events trap.
- **VM Exit / VM Entry** — atomic hardware transitions.
- **EPT** — hardware-accelerated two-level page walks.

The hardware itself now **traps all sensitive operations**, regardless of whether the guest executes them in ring 0 or 3, thus satisfying the Popek-Goldberg condition in silicon.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU Exams for this Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating VT-x with hypervisors** — VT-x is a *CPU feature*, not a hypervisor. KVM/ESXi are hypervisors that *use* VT-x.
> 2. **Writing "ring 0" for guests** — the guest's ring 0 is an *illusion*. The hardware runs the guest in a de-privileged state. Examiners deduct marks if you don't clarify this.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical questions** — always include **cycles**, **ns/μs**, or **MB/GB** with your numerical answers.
> 4. **Skipping the EPT calculation steps** — show explicit division and multiplication; don't jump to the answer.
> 5. **Misspelling VMCS** as "VMSC" or "VMControl" — happens often in exam stress.
> 6. **Confusing EPT (Intel) with NPT (AMD)** — both do nested paging, but they are vendor-specific terms.
> 7. **Not mentioning VM Exit + VM Entry together** — they are a *paired atomic pair*; examiners expect both in any complete answer.
> 8. **Omitting the Popek-Goldberg theorem** — even if asked only about VT-x, a one-line reference to Popek-Goldberg fetches bonus marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Revision Points for Hardware-Assisted Virtualization:**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Hardware-Assisted Virtualization = CPU-level support for running VMs efficiently, via dedicated instruction-set extensions (Intel VT-x, AMD-V).
- ⭐ **Two VMX modes:** **VMX Root** (VMM) and **VMX Non-Root** (Guest).
- ⭐ **VMCS** = 4 KB-aligned structure containing Guest-State, Host-State, and Control fields.
- ⭐ **VM Exit:** Guest → Host transition; **VM Entry:** Host → Guest transition.
- ⭐ **Key instructions:** `VMXON`, `VMXOFF`, `VMLAUNCH`, `VMRESUME`, `VMREAD`, `VMWRITE`, `VMCLEAR`, `VMPTRLD`, `VMCALL`, `VMFUNC`.
- ⭐ **Memory:** **EPT (Intel) / NPT (AMD)** — hardware 2-level page walk: **GVA → GPA → HPA**.
- ⭐ **I/O:** **VT-d (Intel IOMMU)**, **SR-IOV**, **VT-c** for network.
- ⭐ **Popek-Goldberg Theorem:** Sensitive ⊂ Privileged for virtualizability.
- ⭐ **x86 non-virtualizable** before 2005 due to 17 sensitive-but-non-privileged instructions.
- ⭐ **VT-x launched 2005** (Intel Pentium 4 Prescott); **AMD-V launched 2006** (Athlon 64 X2).
- ⭐ **Comparison:** Full Virt (SW, slow) vs Para-Virt (guest modified) vs **HW-Assisted (best performance, no guest modification)**.
- ⭐ **Hypervisors using HW-assist:** **KVM, VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen-HVM, AWS Nitro**.
- ⭐ **Confidential Computing extensions:** **SEV (AMD)**, **TDX (Intel)** — encrypt guest memory from hypervisor.
- ⭐ **Typical VM Exit/Entry cost:** **500–1000 cycles** each.
- ⭐ **EPT memory overhead:** typically **< 1%** of allocated guest RAM.
- ⭐ **Modern extensions:** APIC virtualization, Virtual Interrupt Delivery, Posted Interrupts, VMCS shadowing.
- ⭐ **Valuation mantra:** Always state "guest ring 0 is a hardware-enforced illusion" — examiners reward this exact phrase.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OS Level Virtualization. Basics of Network Virtualization
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** OS Level Virtualization. Basics of Network Virtualization

---

### 1.1 OS Level Virtualization — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Official Terminology**
> **OS-Level Virtualization** (also called *system-level virtualization* or *container-based virtualization*) is a server virtualization paradigm in which the **kernel of a host operating system is multiplexed to allow multiple isolated user-space instances (containers, zones, jails, or Virtual Environments (VEs))** to run concurrently, with **each container sharing the host OS kernel but maintaining its own isolated process, file, network, and user namespace**.

The technology is a *lightweight* alternative to hardware (hypervisor-based) virtualization because it **does not boot a separate guest OS kernel**. Instead, every container rides directly on top of the already-running host kernel through a kernel feature called **namespaces** (for isolation) combined with **control groups / cgroups** (for resource governance).

The **peak theoretical number of containers per host** is limited only by the number of process IDs (PID namespace entries) and the kernel's namespace table size — practically **~10,000 containers per host** is achievable on commodity Linux, versus typically **~50–100 VMs per host** under hypervisor virtualization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Why this topic matters**
> OS-Level Virtualization is the **foundational technology behind modern Cloud platforms** such as **Docker, Kubernetes, Amazon ECS, Azure Container Apps, and Google Cloud Run**. KTU examiners routinely test (a) the difference between **Type-1 / Type-2 hypervisors vs OS-level virtualization**, and (b) how containers achieve isolation *without a hypervisor*.

---

### 1.2 The Real-World Analogy — "Apartment Building vs. Standalone Houses"

| Concept | Hypervisor Virtualization | OS-Level Virtualization |
|---|---|---|
| Real-world analogy | **Standalone houses**, each with its own plumbing, electricity meter, foundation | **Apartments in one building**, sharing one water tank, one power grid, one foundation |
| Kernel | Each VM runs its **own kernel** (own foundation) | All containers share **one kernel** (one foundation) |
| Weight | A full house is **heavy** to build | An apartment is **light** and **fast** to build |
| Boot time | **30–60 seconds** (boots BIOS, bootloader, kernel) | **Milliseconds to < 2 seconds** |

So, when you run **5 containers on a Linux host**, the host's kernel is shared. Each container "feels" like an independent Linux box (its own root filesystem, users, PIDs, hostname, network interfaces), but the kernel underneath is the *same single Linux kernel*. This is the **central idea** the KTU paper expects you to internalize.

---

### 1.3 Network Virtualization — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Official Terminology**
> **Network Virtualization** is the process of combining hardware and software network resources and network functionality into a **single software-based administrative entity**, an **abstract virtual network**. It decouples network services from the underlying physical infrastructure and enables on-demand, programmable, multi-tenant network provisioning — a cornerstone capability of Cloud Computing and **SDN/NFV** architectures.

Two principal sub-classifications are tested in KTU:

1. **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)** — separates the **control plane** (deciding where traffic goes) from the **data plane** (forwarding traffic). A logically centralized **SDN Controller** (e.g., OpenDaylight, Floodlight, ONOS) programs the data plane via a southbound API such as **OpenFlow**.
2. **Network Functions Virtualization (NFV)** — replaces dedicated hardware appliances (firewalls, load balancers, NAT, DPI) with **software instances (VNFs)** running on commodity x86 servers, orchestrated by an **NFV Orchestrator (NFVO)**.

---

### 1.4 The Real-World Analogy — "GPS-Routed Road Network vs. Static Road Signs"

Think of a **traditional physical network** as a city where every intersection has its own static road signs pointing to only one destination. **Network virtualization with SDN** is like having a **central Google Maps server** that re-routes *every* car (packet) in real time based on traffic conditions. The roads (physical links) are unchanged, but the *intelligence* (control plane) is moved to one programmable brain. **NFV** is like replacing the dedicated, expensive toll-booth machinery with a **generic tablet running toll-software** — same function, generic hardware.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor — Module 2 coverage**
> - **OS-Level Virtualization** ⇒ isolation mechanisms (namespaces, cgroups, chroot), Docker/LXC internals, comparison with hardware virtualization.
> - **Network Virtualization basics** ⇒ external vs internal virtualization, overlay networks, SDN architecture (Application / Control / Infrastructure layers), OpenFlow, NFV reference architecture (ETSI ETSI GS NFV 002).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

### 2.1 OS-Level Virtualization — Architecture Deep Dive

The architecture consists of **four cooperating layers** stacked from hardware up to the application:

1. **Physical Hardware (Server/Host Machine)** — CPU, RAM, NIC, disk.
2. **Host Operating System Kernel** — the **single shared kernel**. On Linux, this kernel exposes two critical subsystems:
   - **Namespaces** (isolation of view) — `mnt`, `pid`, `net`, `ipc`, `uts`, `user`, `cgroup`.
   - **Control Groups (cgroups v1 / v2)** (resource accounting & limits) — `cpu`, `memory`, `blkio`, `devices`, `freezer`.
3. **Container Engine / Virtualization Layer** — `runc`, `containerd`, `CRI-O`, `LXC`, `Docker Engine`. This layer consumes the kernel features and presents them to users as containers.
4. **Container Instances (User-Space)** — each container has its own *isolated view* of the filesystem (often via **OverlayFS / AUFS**), process tree, network stack, hostname.

> [!NOTE]
> **Isolating each "view" is done by a *different* namespace type:**
> - **PID namespace** → container sees only its own PIDs starting from 1.
> - **NET namespace** → container has its own virtual interfaces, routing tables, iptables.
> - **MNT namespace** → container has its own root filesystem (no visibility of host mounts).
> - **UTS namespace** → container can set its own hostname/domainname.
> - **IPC namespace** → container has its own System V IPC and POSIX message queues.
> - **USER namespace** → container has its own UID/GID mapping (root inside ≠ root outside).
> - **CGROUP namespace** → container has its own virtualized cgroup tree view.

---

### 2.2 Hardware Virtualization vs OS-Level Virtualization (KTU High-Yield Comparison)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison table is the **single most-asked concept** for 7-mark and 14-mark questions on OS-Level Virtualization.

| Parameter | Hardware Virtualization (Hypervisor) | OS-Level Virtualization (Containers) |
|---|---|---|
| **Hypervisor required?** | **Yes** (Type-1: ESXi, Xen, Hyper-V; Type-2: VirtualBox, VMware Workstation) | **No** hypervisor — uses host kernel directly |
| **Guest OS kernel** | **Independent** per VM | **Shared** with host & all containers |
| **Boot time** | 30 s – 2 min | < 1 s (typically **< 100 ms**) |
| **Disk footprint** | **GBs** (full OS image) | **MBs** (only app + libs) |
| **RAM overhead** | Hundreds of MB per VM | **MB-level** per container |
| **Performance** | 1–5 % loss vs native (paravirt ≈ native) | ≈ **native** (no double kernel) |
| **Isolation strength** | **Strong** (separate kernel = strong fault/domain isolation) | **Weaker** (kernel-level exploit escapes container) |
| **Density per host** | 10s–100s of VMs | **1000s** of containers |
| **Examples** | VMware ESXi, KVM, Xen, Hyper-V | **Docker, LXC, Podman, runC, Solaris Zones, FreeBSD Jails** |
| **Cloud use case** | IaaS (EC2 classic, Azure VMs) | **PaaS / CaaS / microservices** (Kubernetes, ECS, Fargate) |
| **Live migration** | Yes (vMotion, KVM live migration) | Possible but harder (CRIU checkpoint/restore) |
| **Nested support** | Nested virt exists but slow | Trivially runs inside VMs |

---

### 2.3 Network Virtualization — Conceptual Breakdown

Network virtualization has **three orthogonal dimensions**, all of which appear in KTU questions:

#### A. External Network Virtualization
Combines **multiple physical networks into one virtual network**, OR **splits one physical network into multiple isolated logical networks** (the more common direction).

- Achieved using: **VLAN (IEEE 802.1Q)**, **VXLAN (RFC 7348)**, **NVGRE**, **STT**, **MPLS L2/L3 VPNs**, **GRE tunnels**.
- **VLAN ID space** = $2^{12} = 4096$ IDs (12-bit tag). **VXLAN ID space** = $2^{24}$ (24-bit VNI), enabling **~16 million** logical networks — the scale needed for multi-tenant clouds.

#### B. Internal Network Virtualization
A software-based virtual network is created *inside a single host*, emulating a fully functional network of switches, routers, firewalls, and load balancers — without touching the physical NIC. Examples: **Linux bridge**, **Open vSwitch (OVS)**, **Cumulus Linux / DENT**, **Cisco Nexus 1000V**, **VMware NSX**.

#### C. Overlay / Tunnel-Based Virtualization
Encapsulates original L2 frames inside a UDP/IP header (or GRE) and tunnels them across an **underlay** IP network. The underlay (e.g., a Data Center fabric) sees only the outer header.

---

### 2.4 SDN Architecture — The Three Planes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners love asking: *"Explain the three-layer architecture of SDN and the role of OpenFlow."*

| Layer | Function | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Application Plane** | Business apps that express *intent* (load balancing, routing policy, firewalling) | Faucet SDN app, FWaaS, LBaaS, custom Python/Java app |
| **Control Plane** | Logically centralized controller that converts intent into flow rules | **OpenDaylight, Floodlight, ONOS, Ryu, NOX** |
| **Infrastructure (Data) Plane** | Forwards packets based on **flow tables** populated by the controller | Open vSwitch (OVS), physical **OpenFlow switches**, P4 targets |

**Northbound APIs** sit between Application and Control planes (REST APIs typically). **Southbound APIs** sit between Control and Infrastructure planes (e.g., **OpenFlow 1.0–1.5**, OVSDB, NETCONF, gNMI, P4Runtime).

An **OpenFlow flow table entry** has the structure:

`{ Match Fields, Priority, Counters, Instructions, Timeouts, Cookie }`

Where **Match Fields** can match on: ingress port, Ethernet src/dst MAC, EtherType, VLAN ID, IPv4 src/dst, IP proto, TCP/UDP src-dst port, MPLS label.

---

### 2.5 NFV Reference Architecture (ETSI GS NFV 002)

> [!NOTE]
> The **ETSI NFV** architecture consists of **three functional blocks**:

1. **VNF (Virtual Network Function)** — software-implemented network function (vRouter, vFirewall, vDNS).
2. **NFVI (NFV Infrastructure)** — the physical compute / storage / network resources + the virtualization layer (hypervisor + virtual switches).
3. **MANO (Management & Orchestration)** — three sub-blocks:
   - **NFVO** (NFV Orchestrator) — orchestrates network services across multiple VNFs.
   - **VNFM** (VNF Manager) — lifecycle (instantiate, scale, heal, terminate) of each VNF.
   - **VIM** (Virtualized Infrastructure Manager) — controls NFVI resources (e.g., **OpenStack** as VIM).

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantitative limits you must memorize for numerical problems in KTU exams.

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Formula / Limit | Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N_{\text{containers}}$ | Theoretical max containers per host | depends on **PID namespace** & `cgroup` pids.max | typically **~10 000** |
| $N_{\text{VMs}}$ | Max VMs per hypervisor host | limited by RAM & vCPUs | **~50–100** typical |
| $N_{\text{VLAN}}$ | Max VLANs per switch | $2^{12}$ | **4096** (0 & 4095 reserved) |
| $N_{\text{VXLAN}}$ | Max VXLAN segments | $2^{24}$ | **16 777 216** |
| $N_{\text{IPv4}}$ | IPv4 host addresses | $2^{32} - 2$ | ≈ **4.29 × 10⁹** |
| $N_{\text{IPv6}}$ | IPv6 host addresses | $2^{128}$ | ≈ **3.4 × 10³⁸** |
| $t_{\text{boot}}^{VM}$ | VM boot time | hypervisor-dependent | **30 s – 2 min** |
| $t_{\text{boot}}^{C}$ | Container boot time | engine-dependent | **< 1 s (often < 100 ms)** |
| $\rho_{\text{overhead}}^{C}$ | Container RAM overhead | image-dependent | **~10–50 MB** |
| $\rho_{\text{overhead}}^{VM}$ | VM RAM overhead | guest-OS-dependent | **~512 MB – 2 GB** |
| $T_{\text{sample}}$ | OpenFlow flow-table miss → controller latency | $t_{\text{ctrl}} + t_{\text{prop}}$ | typically **1–10 ms** |
| $D_{\text{overhead}}^{\text{VXLAN}}$ | VXLAN encapsulation overhead | 50 B (UDP+IP+Ethernet) on top of inner frame | **50 bytes** |
| $D_{\text{overhead}}^{\text{GRE}}$ | GRE encapsulation overhead | 24 B (GRE+IP+Ethernet base) | **24 bytes** |
| $D_{\text{overhead}}^{\text{VLAN}}$ | 802.1Q tag overhead | 4 B | **4 bytes** |
| $M_{\text{MTU}}$ | Standard Ethernet MTU | constant | **1500 bytes** |
| $M_{\text{MTU}}^{\text{jumbo}}$ | Jumbo frames | configurable | **9000 bytes** |

> Real-world engineering utility: SDN + NFV together reduce **CAPEX** (no proprietary appliances) and **OPEX** (centralized control, automation) for telecom and cloud providers — driving the architectural shift behind **5G core (5GC)**, **Telco Cloud**, and **edge computing**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Where the Marks Are Hidden

For a **14-mark KTU Module 2 question** on OS-Level Virtualization, here is the canonical marking distribution the examiner uses:

| Sub-part | Expected Content | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Definition + Architecture diagram | OS-level virtualization definition, layered diagram (HW → Kernel → Engine → Containers) | **4** |
| Isolation mechanism | Namespaces (PID/NET/MNT/UTS/IPC/USER) + cgroups | **4** |
| Comparison table | Hardware vs OS-level on ≥ 6 parameters | **4** |
| Working example / Docker pipeline | Dockerfile → Image → Container registry → Container run | **2** |
| Conclusion / Real-world deployment | Use in AWS ECS / Kubernetes | optional, **bonus +1** |
| **Total** | | **14** |

For a **14-mark SDN question**, the canonical split is:

| Sub-part | Expected Content | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| SDN definition + 3-plane architecture | App / Control / Infrastructure plane, northbound & southbound APIs | **4** |
| OpenFlow working | Flow table, match–action pipeline, controller–switch secure channel | **4** |
| NFV architecture | VNF, NFVI, MANO (NFVO/VNFM/VIM), ETSI reference | **4** |
| Use case / cloud relevance | 5G core, telco cloud, data-center fabric | **2** |

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Effective Data Rate Overhead of VXLAN

Let us derive the **effective payload ratio** when VXLAN encapsulates an inner Ethernet frame on a 10 Gbps link — a numerical problem **frequently asked in KTU**.

Let
- $R_{\text{link}} = 10\,\text{Gbps}$ = physical link rate.
- $L_{\text{inner}} = 1500\,\text{B}$ = inner (original) Ethernet payload + headers (full MTU frame).
- $O_{\text{VXLAN}} = 50\,\text{B}$ = VXLAN overhead (8 B VXLAN + 8 B UDP + 20 B IPv4 + 14 B outer Ethernet preamble/SFD excluded — the standard accounting is 50 B added to the L2 frame).

The **on-the-wire frame size** is:

$$L_{\text{wire}} = L_{\text{inner}} + O_{\text{VXLAN}}$$

$$L_{\text{wire}} = 1500 + 50 = 1550 \;\text{bytes}$$

The **effective payload ratio** (the fraction of bytes that carry *real* application data) is:

$$\eta_{\text{payload}} = \frac{L_{\text{inner}} - H_{\text{inner}}}{L_{\text{wire}}}$$

where $H_{\text{inner}} = 38$ B is the typical inner L2+L3+L4 header (14 B Ethernet + 20 B IP + 4 B TCP — we ignore IP options/TCP options). Therefore:

$$\eta_{\text{payload}} = \frac{1500 - 38}{1550} = \frac{1462}{1550}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{payload}} \approx 0.9432 \;\;(94.32\,\%)}$$

The **useful throughput** at the application layer is then:

$$R_{\text{app}} = R_{\text{link}} \times \eta_{\text{payload}}$$

$$R_{\text{app}} = 10 \times 10^{9} \times 0.9432$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{app}} \approx 9.432 \;\text{Gbps}}$$

> Therefore, on a 10 Gbps VXLAN-tunneled link, the **application-level bandwidth lost to encapsulation overhead is ~568 Mbps**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Container Density on a 32 GB Host

Let us derive the **maximum practical container density** on a 32 GB host. Each container (lightweight, e.g., Alpine Linux + Node.js micro-service) consumes approximately:

- $M_{\text{base}} = 20\,\text{MB}$ (minimum resident memory, kernel structures, libc).
- $M_{\text{app}} = 80\,\text{MB}$ (Node.js + app heap).

Total per container:

$$M_{\text{container}} = M_{\text{base}} + M_{\text{app}} = 20 + 80 = 100\,\text{MB} = 0.1\,\text{GB}$$

Host memory available for containers (assuming 4 GB reserved for host OS / kernel / buffers):

$$M_{\text{avail}} = 32 - 4 = 28\,\text{GB}$$

The theoretical max density:

$$N_{\max} = \left\lfloor \frac{M_{\text{avail}}}{M_{\text{container}}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{28}{0.1} \right\rfloor$$

$$\boxed{N_{\max} = 280 \;\text{containers}}$$

If we instead use VM density, each VM with a 1 GB guest OS memory footprint gives:

$$N_{\max}^{VM} = \left\lfloor \frac{28}{1} \right\rfloor = 28 \;\text{VMs}$$

> **Ratio:** $\dfrac{N_{\max}^{C}}{N_{\max}^{VM}} = \dfrac{280}{28} = \mathbf{10\times}$ **density advantage** for OS-level virtualization. This is exactly why hyperscalers (Google, Meta, Netflix) standardized on containers with Borg / Kubernetes.

---

### 3.4 OpenFlow Flow Lookup — Analytical Working

Consider a packet arriving at an OpenFlow switch with the following header fields:

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Ingress port | 2 |
| Eth src | `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:01` |
| Eth dst | `AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:02` |
| Ethertype | `0x0800` (IPv4) |
| VLAN ID | 100 |
| IPv4 src | `10.0.0.5` |
| IPv4 dst | `10.0.0.6` |
| IP proto | 6 (TCP) |
| TCP dst port | 80 |

The switch consults its **flow tables** in priority order. Three rules are installed (highest priority first):

| Priority | Match | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 100 | `in_port=2, tcp_dst=80, ipv4_dst=10.0.0.6` | OUTPUT to port 5 (load-balancer) |
| 50 | `in_port=2, vlan_vid=100` | OUTPUT to port 3, push VLAN 200 |
| 0 | (wildcard) | DROP (default) |

Step 1 — Compare against priority 100: all fields match ⇒ **match**. Action = forward to port 5.

If priority 100 had been absent:

Step 2 — Compare against priority 50: matches (in_port, VLAN). Action = push VLAN 200 and forward to port 3.

If both absent → priority 0 → **DROP** → packet sent to controller via `packet_in` message; controller may install a new rule.

> **Key takeaway**: OpenFlow matches the **first** rule in priority order — its **forwarding decision** is a single table lookup, $O(1)$ using TCAM (ternary content-addressable memory). TCAM cost is high, so flow-table size is a *physical* engineering constraint — modern switches offer **~16 K–2 M** TCAM entries.

---

### 3.5 Migration Cost — Live Migration of VM vs Container

A useful KTU short-answer: *Why is container migration lighter than VM live migration?*

- **VM live migration (vMotion / KVM)**: must transfer the entire **guest memory image** plus local disk state. With $M_{\text{RAM}}^{VM} = 2\,\text{GB}$ and a 10 Gbps link:

$$T_{\text{mig}}^{VM} = \frac{M_{\text{RAM}}^{VM}}{R_{\text{link}} \cdot \eta} = \frac{2 \times 10^{9}\,\text{B}}{10 \times 10^{9} \cdot 0.9\,\text{bits/s} \cdot (1/8)}$$

$$T_{\text{mig}}^{VM} = \frac{2 \times 10^{9}}{1.125 \times 10^{9}} \approx 1.78\,\text{s}$$

(plus dirty-page iteration cycles — total ~10–30 s typical).

- **Container migration (CRIU)**: only the **process memory + open file descriptors + socket state** are checkpointed. With $M_{\text{RAM}}^{C} = 200\,\text{MB}$:

$$T_{\text{mig}}^{C} = \frac{200 \times 10^{6}}{1.125 \times 10^{9}} \approx 0.178\,\text{s}$$

(plus a few seconds for image pull from registry — total ~1–3 s).

$$\boxed{T_{\text{mig}}^{C} \;\approx\; 0.1 \times T_{\text{mig}}^{VM}}$$

> **Engineering implication**: container migration enables **sub-second** elasticity, ideal for **serverless** and **edge** workloads; VM migration is reserved for stateful, long-running enterprise workloads.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**  
> **Q1. Define OS-level virtualization. Name two real-world container engines.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer**:
OS-level virtualization is a server virtualization technique in which the kernel of a host operating system is multiplexed using namespaces and cgroups to run multiple isolated user-space instances (containers). Each container shares the host kernel but presents an independent filesystem, process, network, and user view. Real-world container engines: **(i) Docker Engine, (ii) LXC** (or runC, containerd, Podman, CRI-O).  
*(Definition 2 marks + examples 1 mark)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]**  
> **Q2. Differentiate between Software-Defined Networking (SDN) and Network Functions Virtualization (NFV).** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer**:

| Aspect | SDN | NFV |
|---|---|---|
| **What it does** | Decouples **control plane from data plane**; centralizes network intelligence | Replaces dedicated **hardware appliances** with software network functions |
| **Scope** | **How packets are forwarded** | **What packets do** (firewall, NAT, DPI, LB) |
| **Standard body** | ONF (Open Networking Foundation), OpenFlow protocol | **ETSI** (GS NFV 002 reference architecture) |
| **Key entity** | SDN Controller | VNF + MANO |
| **Dependency** | Can exist **independently** of NFV | Often **uses** SDN for connectivity between VNFs |

*(1 mark each for bold terms + 1 mark conclusion)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

#### ➤ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q-A]**  
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of OS-level virtualization with a neat block diagram. Discuss how **Linux namespaces and cgroups** together provide process and resource isolation. *(7 marks)*  
> **(b)** Compare OS-level virtualization with hardware (hypervisor-based) virtualization on at least **six parameters**, and justify why containers are preferred for **microservices** in the cloud. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — (a):**

The OS-level virtualization architecture has **four layers**:

1. **Physical hardware** — CPU, RAM, disk, NIC.
2. **Host OS kernel** — a single Linux/UNIX kernel that exposes two key subsystems:
   - **Namespaces** — wrap a global system resource so each container has its own isolated view. There are **seven** namespace types in modern Linux: **PID, NET, MNT, UTS, IPC, USER, CGROUP**. Each namespace gives a container the *illusion* of being a standalone machine (its own PIDs starting from 1, its own virtual NICs and routing tables, its own mount root, its own hostname, etc.).
   - **Control groups (cgroups v2)** — the *resource-governance* subsystem that **accounts for** and **limits** CPU time, memory, block-IO, devices, and PIDs per container.
3. **Container engine** — `runc` / `containerd` / `Docker Engine` — the user-facing tool that combines namespaces + cgroups + an *image filesystem* (OverlayFS) into a running container.
4. **Container instances** — user-space processes that "see" an isolated Linux box.

**Working example**: When `docker run -d --name web nginx` is executed, the Docker daemon (i) pulls the `nginx` image (an OverlayFS layered root FS), (ii) creates a new PID namespace so the container's `nginx` process becomes PID 1 inside, (iii) creates a new NET namespace with a `veth` pair connecting to a bridge on the host, (iv) creates a new MNT namespace rooted at the image rootfs, (v) joins the container to a cgroup that limits it to, say, 512 MB RAM and 1 CPU. The host kernel transparently provides all six isolation primitives *without booting a separate kernel*.

*(Diagram 2 marks + namespaces 3 marks + cgroups 1 mark + working example 1 mark = 7)*

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

| Parameter | Hardware Virtualization | OS-Level Virtualization |
|---|---|---|
| Hypervisor layer | Required (Type-1 or Type-2) | **Not required** — host kernel used directly |
| Guest kernel | Independent per VM | **Shared** across all containers |
| Boot time | **30 s – 2 min** | **< 1 s** |
| Disk footprint | **GBs** | **MBs** |
| RAM overhead | **~512 MB – 2 GB** | **~10–50 MB** |
| Density per host | **~50–100 VMs** | **~thousands of containers** |
| Isolation strength | **Strong** (separate kernel) | **Weaker** (kernel-level exploits possible) |
| Performance | 1–5 % loss | **≈ native** |
| Live migration | Mature (vMotion, KVM live migration) | Possible via CRIU but less mature |

*(1 mark for a comparison table covering 6 parameters)*

**Justification for microservices in cloud**: Microservices are **stateless, short-lived, network-intensive** workloads that benefit from **(i) sub-second startup**, **(ii) high density** (one service per container), **(iii) efficient horizontal scaling** (Kubernetes HPA can spin 100 container replicas in <30 s), **(iv) low resource overhead** (no wasted guest-OS RAM), and **(v) declarative deployment** (Helm charts, Kubernetes manifests). VM overhead is prohibitive for microservices that scale to thousands of instances — containers win on **time-to-scale, density, and cost-per-replica**. *(3 marks for justification)*

---

#### ➤ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q-B]**  
> **(a)** Describe the **three-layer architecture of SDN** with a neat diagram. Explain the role of the **OpenFlow protocol** as the southbound API. *(7 marks)*  
> **(b)** Explain the **ETSI NFV reference architecture**. List any **three real-world VNFs** and describe how NFV + SDN together enable **5G core network slicing**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — (a):**

The SDN architecture has **three orthogonal planes**:

1. **Application Plane** — business applications that express **intent** (load balancing, routing policy, firewall rules). These apps communicate with the controller through a **northbound API** (usually REST).
2. **Control Plane** — a **logically centralized SDN controller** (e.g., OpenDaylight, Floodlight, ONOS, Ryu). The controller maintains a **global network view** (topology, hosts, links, flows) and computes forwarding decisions. **OpenFlow is the southbound API** between controller and data-plane switches.
3. **Infrastructure (Data) Plane** — packet-forwarding devices (physical OpenFlow switches, **Open vSwitch** in hypervisors, P4 targets). Each device has **flow tables** stored in TCAM. A flow entry has *match fields, priority, counters, instructions, timeouts, cookie*.

**OpenFlow working**: When a packet arrives, the switch matches it against flow tables (highest priority first). On a *table miss*, the switch sends the packet to the controller via a `PACKET_IN` message. The controller decides the action (e.g., `OUTPUT port 5`), and replies with `FLOW_MOD` installing a new flow entry. Subsequent packets then hit the fast path. *(Diagram 2 marks + 3 plane explanation 3 marks + OpenFlow role 2 marks = 7)*

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

The **ETSI NFV reference architecture** has three principal functional blocks:

- **VNF (Virtual Network Function)** — software-implemented network function. Examples: **vRouter, vFirewall, vLoadBalancer, vDPI, vEPC, v5GC**.
- **NFVI (NFV Infrastructure)** — the totality of physical compute + storage + network resources, plus the **virtualization layer** (KVM/QEMU + OVS).
- **MANO (Management & Orchestration)** — three sub-blocks: **NFVO** (orchestrates network services), **VNFM** (manages VNF lifecycle), **VIM** (manages NFVI — typically OpenStack).

**Three real-world VNFs**: (i) **vRouter** (e.g., Cisco CSR 1000v), (ii) **vFirewall** (e.g., pfSense, vFortigate), (iii) **vEPC / v5GC** (virtualized Evolved Packet Core / 5G Core).

**5G network slicing via NFV + SDN**: A 5G network slice (e.g., eMBB, URLLC, mMTC) is a **logical end-to-end network** with dedicated QoS, latency, and bandwidth guarantees. **NFV** instantiates the slice's VNFs (UPF, AMF, SMF) on commodity hardware as software instances; **SDN** dynamically carves a **logical network path** between these VNFs by programming flow rules on the underlying transport fabric via OpenFlow. Together, NFV+SDN enable **on-demand, isolated, multi-tenant slices** — the cornerstone of 5G telco-cloud. *(Architecture 4 marks + VNF examples 1 mark + 5G slicing 2 marks = 7)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse containers with VMs** — if asked "explain OS-level virtualization," students often wrongly draw a hypervisor. Marks are lost for misnaming "Docker = VM".
> 2. **OpenFlow ≠ SDN** — OpenFlow is *only the southbound API*; SDN is the broader architecture. Saying "SDN is OpenFlow" loses 2 marks.
> 3. **SDN vs NFV** — examiners repeatedly observe students using these terms interchangeably. SDN controls *how packets are forwarded*; NFV implements *what network functions exist* on generic hardware.
> 4. **Namespaces vs cgroups** — namespaces = isolation *of view*; cgroups = limits *of resources*. Mixing these up costs marks.
> 5. **Diagram labels** — examiners expect every block (App, Control, Infra plane) labelled clearly; an unlabelled diagram gives zero marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this list before entering the exam hall.**

- [x] **OS-level virtualization** multiplexes the **host OS kernel** to run **multiple isolated user-space containers** — *no hypervisor, no guest OS*.
- [x] **Isolation** = Linux **namespaces** (PID, NET, MNT, UTS, IPC, USER, CGROUP) — 7 types in modern Linux.
- [x] **Resource governance** = **cgroups v2** (cpu, memory, blkio, devices, freezer, pids).
- [x] **Container density** ≈ 1000s per host; **VM density** ≈ 10–100 per host.
- [x] **Container boot** < 1 s; **VM boot** 30 s – 2 min.
- [x] **Container RAM overhead** ~10–50 MB; **VM RAM overhead** ~512 MB – 2 GB.
- [x] **Examples**: Docker, LXC, Podman, containerd, runC, Solaris Zones, FreeBSD Jails.
- [x] **Kernel escape** = primary security risk of containers (vs VM's hypervisor boundary).
- [x] **Network virtualization** has 3 dimensions: **external (VLAN/VXLAN)**, **internal (OVS/Linux bridge)**, **overlay (VXLAN/GENEVE/STT)**.
- [x] **VXLAN** = 50 B overhead, **24-bit VNI** → **~16 million** logical networks (vs VLAN's 4094).
- [x] **SDN** = 3 planes — **Application, Control, Infrastructure** — separated via **northbound & southbound APIs**.
- [x] **OpenFlow** = *southbound* API; flow entries live in **TCAM**; `PACKET_IN` on miss; `FLOW_MOD` from controller.
- [x] **NFV** = ETSI architecture — **VNF + NFVI + MANO (NFVO, VNFM, VIM)**; replaces proprietary appliances.
- [x] **5G core + NFV + SDN** = dynamic **network slicing** for eMBB, URLLC, mMTC.
- [x] **Modern clouds** (Kubernetes, ECS, Docker Swarm) all sit on **OS-level virtualization**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Storage Virtualization — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SNIA Definition (Storage Networking Industry Association):**
> *"Storage Virtualization is the process of presenting a logical view of physical storage resources to a host system, abstracting the underlying physical storage devices so that they appear as a single, unified, manageable pool of storage."*

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, storage virtualization is the **abstraction layer** that sits between the **host operating system** and the **physical storage hardware**, decoupling the *logical representation* of storage from its *physical location*. It enables administrators to manage storage as a single logical pool while the data physically resides on heterogeneous, geographically dispersed devices.

### Components Involved
- **Physical Storage Devices** (HDDs, SSDs, Tape, Hybrid Arrays)
- **Virtualization Engine / Abstraction Layer**
- **Logical Unit Numbers (LUNs)** presented to hosts
- **Metadata Service** for mapping logical-to-physical addresses

---

## 1.2 Desktop Virtualization — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *"Desktop Virtualization is the process of separating a desktop environment (OS + applications + user data) from the physical client device used to access it, hosting the desktop on a centralized server (or cloud) and delivering it over a network to a remote endpoint."*

The user's operating environment runs inside a **Virtual Machine (VM)** or **session** hosted on a centralized server. The endpoint (thin client, laptop, tablet) only captures keystrokes, mouse movements, and renders screen pixels — the actual computation happens remotely.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Storage Virtualization Analogy — "The Magic Bookshelf"**
> Imagine you have books scattered across 10 different rooms in your house (physical disks). You tell your friend, *"Give me the book on Page 200 of Shelf C"* — but in reality, the *bookshelf doesn't physically exist*. A magical librarian (the virtualization engine) silently retrieves the correct page from whichever room actually holds it. To the friend, there is **one single, organized bookshelf** — but physically, the books are spread everywhere. That is storage virtualization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Desktop Virtualization Analogy — "Netflix for Your Desktop"**
> Think of how Netflix works. The movie doesn't sit on your TV; it lives on Netflix's servers. Your TV just streams pixels. **Desktop Virtualization is Netflix for your OS.** Your Windows/Linux desktop doesn't live on your laptop — it lives in a data center. Your laptop just streams the screen and sends back your clicks. You can switch laptops, locations, or devices, and your desktop (with all your files and settings) follows you everywhere.

### Why These Concepts Matter in Cloud Computing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Storage Virtualization** is the **backbone of cloud storage services** like AWS S3, Azure Blob, and Google Cloud Storage. Without it, hyperscale data centers cannot offer elastic, pay-as-you-go storage to billions of users.
> - **Desktop Virtualization** is the **enabler of remote work, BYOD (Bring Your Own Device), and Desktop-as-a-Service (DaaS)** — a multi-billion dollar market segment that the KTU 2024 Scheme specifically emphasizes as a real-world cloud application.

### Key Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Standard Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **IOPS** | Operations/second | Speed of random read/write |
| **Throughput** | MB/s (Megabytes/second) | Sequential data transfer rate |
| **Latency** | ms (milliseconds) | Response time per I/O |
| **Capacity Utilization** | % (percentage) | Used storage vs. total |
| **Deduplication Ratio** | dimensionless | Logical data / Physical data |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Storage Virtualization — Deep Architecture Analysis

Storage virtualization can occur at **three distinct levels** in the I/O stack. Understanding *where* the abstraction happens is the most critical concept for KTU exams.

### A. Block-Level Storage Virtualization
- Operates below the **file system layer**
- Presents **Logical Unit Numbers (LUNs)** to the host
- Hides individual disk boundaries
- **Examples:** SAN (Storage Area Network), RAID virtualization
- **Used by:** Databases, VMware VMFS, enterprise apps

> [!NOTE]
> **Why block-level?** Databases and VMs need raw, low-latency access. File-level abstraction adds overhead. Block-level gives you a "raw disk" that the host formats with its own file system (NTFS, ext4, VMFS).

### B. File-Level Storage Virtualization
- Operates at the **file system layer**
- Presents a **unified namespace** across multiple file servers
- Users see one massive folder; data physically lives on many NAS boxes
- **Examples:** NFS aggregators, Windows DFS (Distributed File System)
- **Used by:** Document sharing, home directories, media archives

### C. Object-Level Storage Virtualization
- Operates at the **application layer**
- Data stored as **objects** with unique IDs, metadata, and policies
- No traditional file hierarchy (no folders, no paths)
- **Examples:** AWS S3, Azure Blob, OpenStack Swift
- **Used by:** Cloud-native apps, big data, backups, archives

### D. Storage Virtualization Architecture — 3 Models

| Model | Where the Virtualization Engine Runs | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Host-Based** | Inside the host OS / hypervisor | Simple, no extra hardware | Consumes host CPU/RAM, scales poorly |
| **Array-Based** | Inside the storage array controller | No host overhead, high performance | Vendor lock-in, no cross-vendor pooling |
| **Network-Based** | Dedicated appliance in the SAN fabric | Vendor-neutral, heterogeneous pooling | Single point of failure if not HA |

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Host issues I/O request** to a logical disk (LUN)
2. **Virtualization engine intercepts** the request
3. **Metadata lookup** — engine consults the mapping table
4. **Translation** — logical address → physical address
5. **Routing** — request forwarded to the correct physical disk
6. **Completion** — response returned to the host (host never knows the data moved)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> Storage virtualization is the **enabler of hot data migration**. With virtualization, an admin can move 10 TB of live data from a failing disk to a healthy disk *while the host keeps running* — because the host only knows the *logical* address, not the *physical* one. This is impossible without virtualization.

---

## 2.2 Desktop Virtualization — Deep Architecture Analysis

### The Four Major Models

#### 1. **Virtual Desktop Infrastructure (VDI)**
- Each user gets a **dedicated VM** running a full OS
- VM is hosted on a **hypervisor** (VMware ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM)
- Fully isolated, customizable per user
- **Example:** VMware Horizon, Citrix XenDesktop, Microsoft AVD

#### 2. **Remote Desktop Services (RDS) / Terminal Services**
- Multiple users share **one OS instance** via sessions
- Lightweight, fewer resources per user
- **Example:** Windows Server with RDS role, Citrix XenApp

#### 3. **Desktop as a Service (DaaS)**
- VDI/RDS **delivered from the public cloud**
- Provider manages the hypervisor, storage, brokering
- **Example:** Amazon WorkSpaces, Microsoft Windows 365, Azure Virtual Desktop

#### 4. **OS Streaming / Client-Side Virtualization**
- OS is **streamed from server to local device**, runs locally
- Useful for stateless endpoints (labs, kiosks)
- **Example:** Citrix Provisioning Services (PVS)

### Operational Step-by-Step (VDI)

1. User logs in via **connection broker** (e.g., VMware Horizon Connection Server)
2. Broker **authenticates** user and queries the pool
3. Broker **assigns a VM** from the pool (or creates a linked clone)
4. User's endpoint (thin client) opens **PCoIP / RDP / ICA** session
5. VM boots; desktop pixels are **streamed** over the network
6. User input (keyboard, mouse) is **captured** and sent back to the VM
7. On logout, VM is **destroyed or returned** to the pool (non-persistent VDI)

> [!NOTE]
> **Persistent vs. Non-Persistent VDI:**
> - **Persistent** — each user always gets the *same* VM (preserves customizations)
> - **Non-persistent** — user gets a *fresh* VM from a master image (ideal for labs/call centers)

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $IOPS = \dfrac{1}{T_{latency}}$ | IOPS from average latency | ops/sec |
| $T_{total} = N \times T_{disk}$ | RAID 0 throughput (N disks) | MB/s |
| $IOPS_{eff} = \dfrac{IOPS_{read} + 4 \times IOPS_{write}}{N}$ | RAID 5 write penalty | ops/sec |
| $U_{cap} = \dfrac{S_{used}}{S_{total}} \times 100$ | Capacity utilization | % |
| $R_{thin} = \dfrac{C_{provisioned}}{C_{used}}$ | Thin provisioning ratio | dimensionless |
| $R_{dedup} = \dfrac{D_{logical}}{D_{physical}}$ | Deduplication ratio | dimensionless |
| $C_{saved} = D_{logical} - D_{physical}$ | Storage saved by dedup | TB |
| $E_{consol} = \dfrac{N_{users}}{N_{servers}}$ | VDI consolidation ratio | users/server |
| $C_{perUser} = \dfrac{C_{server}}{N_{users}}$ | Cost per user | ₹/user |
| $T_{stream} = \dfrac{R_{screen} \times B_{pixel}}{8}$ | VDI bandwidth per session | Mbps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - **RAID 0** has 0 redundancy, RAID 1 mirrors (50% efficiency), RAID 5 can lose 1 disk, RAID 6 can lose 2 disks.
> - **Write penalty:** RAID 5 = 4 I/Os per logical write (2 reads + 2 writes), RAID 6 = 6 I/Os per logical write.
> - **VDI bandwidth rule of thumb:** ~150–250 kbps per session for typical office work, ~1–2 Mbps for multimedia.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These formulas are not just academic — they are used daily by:
> - **Storage Engineers** sizing SANs for enterprise databases
> - **Cloud Architects** designing AWS EBS volumes with the right IOPS
> - **VDI Administrators** calculating server-to-user ratios (e.g., 1 server with 256 GB RAM ÷ 4 GB per VM = 64 users max)
> - **Capacity Planners** projecting cloud storage bills using dedup ratios

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective IOPS in RAID 5

### Problem Setup
A RAID 5 array has **N = 8 disks**. Each disk delivers **180 IOPS**. The workload is **70% reads and 30% writes**. Compute the effective IOPS the array delivers to the host.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Background:** In RAID 5, every logical write requires **4 physical I/Os**:
> - 2 reads (old data + old parity)
> - 2 writes (new data + new parity)
> 
> Reads require only **1 physical I/O** (no parity lookup needed for the read path in this simplified model).

We start with the basic effective IOPS formula:

$$
IOPS_{eff} = \frac{IOPS_{read} + 4 \times IOPS_{write}}{N}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute total raw IOPS available**

The 8 disks in parallel give:

$$
IOPS_{raw} = N \times IOPS_{disk} = 8 \times 180 = 1440 \text{ IOPS}
$$

This is the *total physical IOPS* the disk spindles can collectively deliver.

**Step 2 — Decompose the workload**

We are told 70% reads and 30% writes. From the raw IOPS pool:

$$
IOPS_{read\_raw} = 0.70 \times 1440 = 1008 \text{ IOPS}
$$

$$
IOPS_{write\_raw} = 0.30 \times 1440 = 432 \text{ IOPS}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the write penalty**

Reads map 1:1 to logical IOPS. Writes cost 4 physical IOPS for every 1 logical write. So the *logical* write IOPS the host perceives is:

$$
IOPS_{write\_logical} = \frac{IOPS_{write\_raw}}{4} = \frac{432}{4} = 108 \text{ logical IOPS}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute effective logical IOPS**

$$
IOPS_{eff} = IOPS_{read\_raw} + IOPS_{write\_logical}
$$

$$
IOPS_{eff} = 1008 + 108 = 1116 \text{ logical IOPS}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify using the unified formula**

$$
IOPS_{eff} = \frac{IOPS_{read} + 4 \times IOPS_{write}}{N} = \frac{1008 + 4 \times 432}{8}
$$

$$
IOPS_{eff} = \frac{1008 + 1728}{8} = \frac{2736}{8} = 1116 \text{ logical IOPS}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The array delivers **1116 logical IOPS** to the host, even though the raw disk capacity is 1440 IOPS. The write penalty caused a **22.5% loss** in effective throughput. This is why database administrators prefer RAID 10 over RAID 5 for write-heavy OLTP workloads.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Storage Savings via Deduplication

### Problem Setup
A company stores **50 TB of logical data** on a cloud storage system. The deduplication engine reports a dedup ratio of **5:1**. Compute the physical storage used and the percentage of storage saved.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

We start with the dedup ratio definition:

$$
R_{dedup} = \frac{D_{logical}}{D_{physical}}
$$

**Step 1 — Solve for physical data**

$$
D_{physical} = \frac{D_{logical}}{R_{dedup}} = \frac{50 \text{ TB}}{5} = 10 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute storage saved**

$$
C_{saved} = D_{logical} - D_{physical} = 50 - 10 = 40 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute percentage saved**

$$
P_{saved} = \frac{C_{saved}}{D_{logical}} \times 100 = \frac{40}{50} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world impact:** A 5:1 dedup ratio is realistic for VDI environments (since 100 users may have nearly identical Windows OS files). This 80% saving translates directly to cloud bill reduction — for AWS S3 Standard at ~$23/TB/month, saving 40 TB means **$920/month saved** per environment.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: VDI Server Consolidation Ratio

### Problem Setup
A company wants to deploy **VDI for 200 users**. Each VM requires **4 GB RAM** and **2 vCPUs**. The available server has **256 GB RAM** and **32 physical cores (64 logical with Hyper-Threading)**. The hypervisor overhead is **15%**. Compute the maximum number of VMs this single server can host and the actual consolidation ratio.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Effective RAM available after hypervisor overhead**

$$
RAM_{eff} = RAM_{total} \times (1 - O_{hv}) = 256 \times 0.85 = 217.6 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum VMs by RAM**

$$
N_{VMs\_RAM} = \left\lfloor \frac{RAM_{eff}}{RAM_{perVM}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{217.6}{4} \right\rfloor = 54 \text{ VMs}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective vCPUs available after hypervisor overhead**

$$
vCPU_{eff} = vCPU_{total} \times (1 - O_{hv}) = 64 \times 0.85 = 54.4 \text{ vCPUs}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum VMs by vCPU**

$$
N_{VMs\_vCPU} = \left\lfloor \frac{vCPU_{eff}}{vCPU_{perVM}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{54.4}{2} \right\rfloor = 27 \text{ VMs}
$$

**Step 5 — Determine the binding constraint**

The system is **vCPU-bound** (27 < 54). You cannot deploy more than **27 VMs per server**.

**Step 6 — Servers needed for 200 users**

$$
N_{servers} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{users}}{N_{VMs\_per\_server}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{200}{27} \right\rceil = 8 \text{ servers}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical insight:** This is exactly why VDI sizing always considers **two constraints** (RAM and CPU). Over-provisioning RAM but under-provisioning CPU is a common KTU exam trap. A student who only computed the RAM bound (54) and concluded 4 servers would be **massively under-provisioned**, leading to CPU contention and a poor user experience in production.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Storage vs. Desktop Virtualization Comparison

| Parameter | Storage Virtualization | Desktop Virtualization |
|---|---|---|
| **Abstraction Layer** | Between OS and physical disk | Between user and physical PC |
| **What is Virtualized** | Disks, LUNs, file systems | Operating system, applications |
| **Primary User** | Storage admin, DBA | End user, IT admin |
| **Network Dependency** | High (SAN/NAS fabric) | High (LAN/WAN streaming) |
| **Performance Bottleneck** | Disk IOPS, network fabric | Network bandwidth, server CPU |
| **Cost Driver** | Capacity (TB) + IOPS license | vCPU + RAM per user |
| **Cloud Equivalent** | AWS EBS, S3, Azure Disk | AWS WorkSpaces, Windows 365 |
| **Failure Domain** | Storage array, fabric switch | Hypervisor host, connection broker |
| **Disaster Recovery** | Replication, snapshots | Linked clones, user profile mobility |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Storage Virtualization — Block-Level Architecture (SAN-based)

```mermaid
graph TB
    sApp[Application Layer]
    sHost[Host Server with Hypervisor]
    sFS[File System NTFS ext4 VMFS]
    sVirt[Storage Virtualization Engine]
    sMeta[Metadata Mapping Service]
    sBlock1[Logical LUN 1]
    sBlock2[Logical LUN 2]
    sBlock3[Logical LUN 3]
    sArray1[SAN Array Controller A]
    sArray2[SAN Array Controller B]
    sDisk1[Physical Disk Pool 1 HDD SSD]
    sDisk2[Physical Disk Pool 2 HDD SSD]
    sSwitch[SAN Fabric Switch FC or iSCSI]

    sApp --> sHost
    sHost --> sFS
    sFS --> sVirt
    sVirt --> sMeta
    sVirt --> sBlock1
    sVirt --> sBlock2
    sVirt --> sBlock3
    sBlock1 --> sSwitch
    sBlock2 --> sSwitch
    sBlock3 --> sSwitch
    sSwitch --> sArray1
    sSwitch --> sArray2
    sArray1 --> sDisk1
    sArray2 --> sDisk2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The host at the top *thinks* it has 3 LUNs. The virtualization engine maps these LUNs to physical disks spread across two different storage arrays. If Array A fails, the engine can transparently re-route LUN 1's I/O to Array B — the host never knows.

## 4.3 Storage Virtualization Models — Comparison Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    sInput[Storage I/O Request from Host]
    sDecision{Where does the virtualization engine reside?}
    sHost1[Host-Based Engine in OS or Hypervisor]
    sArray1[Array-Based Engine in Storage Controller]
    sNetwork1[Network-Based Engine in SAN Appliance]
    sOut1[Translated I/O to Physical Disk]
    sOut2[Translated I/O to Physical Disk]
    sOut3[Translated I/O to Physical Disk]

    sInput --> sDecision
    sDecision -->|Inside the host| sHost1
    sDecision -->|Inside the array| sArray1
    sDecision -->|In the fabric| sNetwork1
    sHost1 --> sOut1
    sArray1 --> sOut2
    sNetwork1 --> sOut3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 questions frequently ask *"Compare host-based, array-based, and network-based storage virtualization."* The trade-off triangle is: **Host-based** = cheapest but scales worst; **Array-based** = best performance but vendor-locked; **Network-based** = most flexible but adds a network hop (latency).

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Explain the architecture of Storage Virtualization in detail. Discuss the three approaches — host-based, array-based, and network-based — with their pros and cons. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Storage Virtualization architecture consists of **four key components** working together:

1. **Physical Storage Layer** — the actual HDDs, SSDs, JBODs, or tape libraries
2. **Virtualization Engine** — the software that performs logical-to-physical mapping
3. **Metadata Service** — stores the mapping tables
4. **Logical Presentation Layer** — LUNs, file shares, or object buckets exposed to hosts

**Host-Based Approach (2 marks):**
- Virtualization engine runs **inside the host OS or hypervisor** (e.g., VMware vCenter, Windows Storage Spaces)
- **Pros:** Zero additional hardware cost; immediate deployment; works with any storage
- **Cons:** Consumes host CPU/RAM; doesn't scale beyond a single host; doesn't pool across hosts

**Array-Based Approach (2 marks):**
- Engine runs **inside the storage array's controller** (e.g., NetApp ONTAP, Dell EMC Unity)
- **Pros:** No host overhead; high performance; tightly integrated with array features
- **Cons:** Vendor lock-in; cannot pool storage across different vendors' arrays

**Network-Based Approach (2 marks):**
- Engine runs on a **dedicated appliance in the SAN fabric** (e.g., IBM SVC, Falconstor)
- **Pros:** Vendor-neutral; pools heterogeneous arrays into one logical pool; best for enterprises
- **Cons:** Adds network hop (latency); appliance is a single point of failure (unless HA)

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Network-based is the gold standard for enterprises, but cloud providers (AWS, Azure) effectively use **distributed software-defined storage** — a cloud-scale evolution of these same three models.

---

**(b) A RAID 5 array has 6 disks, each rated at 200 IOPS. The workload is 60% reads and 40% writes. Calculate the effective logical IOPS delivered to the host. Also explain the RAID 5 write penalty. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**RAID 5 Write Penalty Explanation (3 marks):**
In RAID 5, every logical write requires **4 physical I/O operations**:
- **Read** the old data block
- **Read** the old parity block
- **Write** the new data block
- **Write** the new parity block

This 4× amplification is the **write penalty**. It is the reason RAID 5 is suboptimal for write-heavy databases.

**Calculation (4 marks):**

$$
IOPS_{raw} = N \times IOPS_{disk} = 6 \times 200 = 1200 \text{ IOPS}
$$

Decompose the workload:

$$
IOPS_{read\_raw} = 0.60 \times 1200 = 720 \text{ IOPS}
$$

$$
IOPS_{write\_raw} = 0.40 \times 1200 = 480 \text{ IOPS}
$$

Apply the write penalty to compute logical writes:

$$
IOPS_{write\_logical} = \frac{IOPS_{write\_raw}}{4} = \frac{480}{4} = 120 \text{ logical IOPS}
$$

Effective logical IOPS:

$$
IOPS_{eff} = 720 + 120 = 840 \text{ logical IOPS}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The array delivers **840 logical IOPS** despite having a raw capacity of 1200 IOPS — a **30% loss** due to the write penalty.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) What is Desktop Virtualization? Explain the four major models of Desktop Virtualization with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Desktop Virtualization is the separation of the desktop environment (OS + applications + user data) from the physical endpoint, with execution happening on a centralized server and delivery over the network.

**Four Major Models (6 marks):**

**1. Virtual Desktop Infrastructure (VDI) (1.5 marks)**
- Each user gets a **dedicated VM** on a hypervisor host
- Fully isolated, persistent storage per user
- **Example:** VMware Horizon, Citrix VDI, Microsoft AVD
- **Best for:** Power users, developers, engineers, designers

**2. Remote Desktop Services (RDS) / Terminal Services (1.5 marks)**
- Multiple users share a **single Windows Server** via sessions
- Lightweight — only ~1–2 GB RAM per user
- **Example:** Windows Server with RDS role, Citrix XenApp
- **Best for:** Task workers, BPO, call centers, kiosks

**3. Desktop as a Service (DaaS) (1.5 marks)**
- VDI/RDS **delivered from public cloud** by a managed provider
- No on-premises infrastructure; pay-per-user monthly
- **Example:** Amazon WorkSpaces, Microsoft Windows 365, Azure Virtual Desktop
- **Best for:** SMBs, remote work, disaster recovery scenarios

**4. OS Streaming / Client-Side Virtualization (1.5 marks)**
- OS is **streamed from a server** and runs *locally* on the endpoint
- Useful for stateless devices (labs, libraries, kiosks)
- **Example:** Citrix Provisioning Services (PVS), Microsoft RDS with VDI deployed locally
- **Best for:** Educational institutions, hot-desking environments
