$$PUE = \frac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}} = \frac{2{,}500 \text{ kW}}{1{,}800 \text{ kW}} = 1.3888 \approx \mathbf{1.39}$$

> Interpretation: For every **1.39 units** of utility power drawn, only **1 unit** reaches the IT load. The overhead loss is **0.39 units (~28%)**.

#### Part (b): DCiE
$$DCiE = \left(\frac{P_{IT}}{P_{total}}\right) \times 100\% = \left(\frac{1{,}800}{2{,}500}\right) \times 100\% = \mathbf{72\%}$$

#### Part (c): Annual Energy Lost to Overhead
$$P_{overhead} = P_{total} - P_{IT} = 2{,}500 - 1{,}800 = 700 \text{ kW}$$

Annual energy lost:
$$E_{lost} = 700 \text{ kW} \times 8{,}760 \text{ h/year} = 6{,}132{,}000 \text{ kWh/year} = \mathbf{6.132 \text{ GWh/year}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The "overhead power" includes **chillers, pumps, UPS losses (≈ 8–12%), PDU transformers (≈ 2–3%), lighting, and security systems**. Reducing any of these directly improves PUE.

---

### Worked Example 2: Cost Estimation Using PUE

**Problem:** A hyperscale cloud region operates **10,000 servers**, each averaging **400 W** of compute load. Electricity costs **₹7 per kWh**. The facility PUE is **1.5**. Calculate the annual electricity bill for IT equipment alone (excluding cooling overhead), and then the **total facility bill** including overhead.

#### Step 1 — Compute IT Power.
$$P_{IT} = 10{,}000 \times 0.400 \text{ kW} = 4{,}000 \text{ kW}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Total Facility Power (using PUE).
$$P_{total} = PUE \times P_{IT} = 1.5 \times 4{,}000 = 6{,}000 \text{ kW}$$

#### Step 3 — Annual energy consumption.
$$E_{IT} = 4{,}000 \times 8{,}760 = 35{,}040{,}000 \text{ kWh} = \mathbf{35.04 \text{ GWh}}$$

$$E_{total} = 6{,}000 \times 8{,}760 = 52{,}560{,}000 \text{ kWh} = \mathbf{52.56 \text{ GWh}}$$

#### Step 4 — Annual cost.
$$\text{Cost}_{IT} = 35{,}040{,}000 \times 7 = \mathbf{₹ 24.53 \text{ Crore}}$$

$$\text{Cost}_{total} = 52{,}560{,}000 \times 7 = \mathbf{₹ 36.79 \text{ Crore}}$$

#### Step 5 — Overhead cost.
$$\text{Cost}_{overhead} = 36.79 - 24.53 = \mathbf{₹ 12.26 \text{ Crore / year}}$$

> **Real-world takeaway:** Improving PUE from 1.5 to 1.2 saves **₹4+ Crore annually** on this single facility.

---

### Worked Example 3: Tier Comparison Downtime

**Problem:** Compare the annual downtime (in minutes) of a Tier II vs Tier IV facility.

#### Tier II: 99.741% availability
$$U_{II} = 1 - 0.99741 = 0.00259 = 2.59 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$D_{II} = 2.59 \times 10^{-3} \times 525{,}600 \approx \mathbf{1{,}361 \text{ minutes} \approx 22.7 \text{ hours}}$$

#### Tier IV: 99.995% availability
$$U_{IV} = 1 - 0.99995 = 5 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$D_{IV} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 525{,}600 \approx \mathbf{26.28 \text{ minutes}}$$

#### Ratio of improvement:
$$\frac{D_{II}}{D_{IV}} = \frac{1{,}361}{26.28} \approx 51.8 \times \text{ less downtime for Tier IV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule:** Moving from Tier II → Tier IV typically **doubles or triples** the infrastructure CAPEX (cooling, generators, UPS, redundant network paths) but is essential for financial services, defense, and healthcare systems.

---

### Worked Example 4: CUE and WUE — Sustainability Metrics

**Problem:** A data center consumes **5,000,000 kWh/year** of IT energy. The grid emits **0.7 kg CO₂ per kWh**. The facility uses **3,000,000 liters** of water annually for cooling. Compute CUE and WUE.

#### CUE
$$CUE = \frac{CO_2}{E_{IT}} = \frac{0.7 \text{ kg/kWh} \times 5{,}000{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{5{,}000{,}000 \text{ kWh}} = \mathbf{0.7 \text{ kgCO₂/kWh}}$$

#### WUE
$$WUE = \frac{W}{E_{IT}} = \frac{3{,}000{,}000}{5{,}000{,}000} = \mathbf{0.6 \text{ L/kWh}}$$

> **Benchmark:** Modern Google data centers achieve **CUE ≈ 0.35** and **WUE ≈ 0.12 L/kWh** using renewable PPAs and adiabatic free cooling.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Data Center Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UTL["Utility Power Grid 11kV"] --> TR["Step Down Transformer 11kV to 415V"]
    TR --> ATS["Automatic Transfer Switch ATS"]
    DG["Diesel Generator N+1"] --> ATS
    ATS --> UPS["UPS System with Battery Bank"]
    UPS --> PDU1["Primary PDU A Side"]
    UPS --> PDU2["Redundant PDU B Side"]
    PDU1 --> RACK1["Server Rack Group 1"]
    PDU1 --> RACK2["Server Rack Group 2"]
    PDU2 --> RACK1
    PDU2 --> RACK2
    RACK1 --> SWC["Core Switch Layer"]
    RACK2 --> SWC
    SWC --> SWA["Aggregation Switch"]
    SWA --> SWA2["Top of Rack ToR Switches"]
    SWA2 --> SRV1["Compute Servers VMs"]
    SWA2 --> SRV2["Storage Nodes"]
    SWA2 --> SRV3["GPU AI Workloads"]
    CRAC["CRAC Units N+1 Cooling"] --> RACK1
    CRAC --> RACK2
    CHILL["Chiller Plant"] --> CRAC
    FC["Free Cooling Economizer"] --> CHILL
    DCIM["DCIM Monitoring Layer"] -.-> UPS
    DCIM -.-> CRAC
    DCIM -.-> SWC
    SEC["Physical Security Biometric CCTV"] -.-> RACK1
    SEC -.-> RACK2
    FIRE["Fire Suppression FM200 Novec"] -.-> RACK1
    FIRE -.-> RACK2
    EXTFW["External Firewall Router BGP"] --> SWC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Solid arrows** represent active electrical or data flow.
> - **Dotted arrows (`.->`)** represent control/monitoring signals from the **DCIM (Data Center Infrastructure Management)** layer.
> - The **2N redundancy** is shown at the PDU stage — both paths feed every rack so that failure of either is non-disruptive.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — TIA-942 Tier Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["Tier I Basic 99.671 percent 28.8 hours downtime"]:::tier1
    T2["Tier II Redundant 99.741 percent 22 hours downtime"]:::tier2
    T3["Tier III Concurrently Maintainable 99.982 percent 1.6 hours downtime"]:::tier3
    T4["Tier IV Fault Tolerant 99.995 percent 26.3 minutes downtime"]:::tier4
    T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4
    classDef tier1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef tier2 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#FF6347,color:#000
    classDef tier3 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef tier4 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Hot Aisle / Cold Aisle Containment Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COLD["Cold Aisle Inlet 18 to 22 degrees Celsius"]
        CA1["Server Rack Front Faces Cold"]
    end
    subgraph HOT["Hot Aisle Exhaust 35 to 45 degrees Celsius"]
        HA1["Server Rack Back Faces Hot"]
    end
    CR["CRAC Supply Cold Air"] --> COLD
    COLD --> CA1
    CA1 --> HA1
    HA1 --> RET["Hot Air Return to CRAC"]
    RET --> CR
    EXT["Hot Exhaust Outside via Plenum"] --> HA1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hot Aisle / Cold Aisle?**
> Without separation, hot exhaust air from one rack mixes with cold inlet air of its neighbor — a phenomenon called **bypass airflow** — which forces CRAC units to overwork, raising PUE by **0.1–0.2**. Containment can slash energy costs by **20–30%**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE)" in a data center. What is its ideal value and why is it difficult to achieve in practice?**

**Model Answer:**
- **PUE** is defined by **The Green Grid consortium** as:
$$PUE = \frac{\text{Total Facility Power}}{\text{IT Equipment Power}}$$
- **Ideal value = 1.0** (i.e., 100% of incoming utility power is delivered to IT loads, with zero overhead).
- It is difficult to achieve because real facilities always incur losses in:
  - **UPS conversion (AC→DC→AC)** ≈ 8–12% loss,
  - **PDU transformers** ≈ 2–3% loss,
  - **Chillers, pumps, fans, lighting** for cooling.
- Google's best fleet average is **~1.10**; global industry average is **~1.58**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write the formula upside-down (IT Power / Total Power). Always double-check: PUE ≥ 1 always.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Tier III and Tier IV data centers according to the TIA-942 standard.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Tier III | Tier IV |
|---|---|---|
| **Availability** | 99.982% | 99.995% |
| **Annual Downtime** | ~1.6 hours | ~26.3 minutes |
| **Redundancy** | N+1 (full) | 2N or 2(N+1) — dual paths |
| **Concurrent Maintenance** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Fault Tolerance to single failure** | Partial (one component) | Full (any single failure tolerated) |
| **CAPEX** | Moderate | ~1.5–2× of Tier III |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions carry internal choice. **Answer ANY ONE** out of the two alternatives (A or B). Each Part B question is divided into (a) and (b), worth **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**Q.A.** *(a)* Explain in detail the **four TIA-942 Tier classifications** of data centers. Discuss the **availability, redundancy model, fault tolerance, and concurrent maintainability** of each tier with examples.

*(b)* A data center has a **total facility power of 4,500 kW** and an **IT load of 3,000 kW**. Compute (i) PUE, (ii) DCiE, (iii) overhead power, and (iv) annual energy lost to overhead. If electricity costs ₹8/kWh, compute the **annual cost of the overhead loss**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a): [7 Marks]

The **Telecommunications Industry Association (TIA-942)** is the most widely used standard that classifies data centers based on infrastructure robustness and uptime guarantees. Four tiers are defined:

1. **Tier I — Basic Site Infrastructure:**
   - Single non-redundant distribution path for power and cooling.
   - **Availability = 99.671%**, annual downtime ≈ **28.8 hours**.
   - No concurrent maintenance, no fault tolerance.
   - Example: Small office server room.

2. **Tier II — Redundant Site Infrastructure:**
   - Adds **N+1 redundancy** for power and cooling.
   - **Availability = 99.741%**, downtime ≈ **22 hours**.
   - Still no concurrent maintenance.
   - Example: Internet service provider PoP.

3. **Tier III — Concurrently Maintainable:**
   - **N+1 redundancy for ALL components**; includes redundant distribution paths.
   - **Availability = 99.982%**, downtime ≈ **1.6 hours**.
   - **Concurrent maintenance is allowed** — any component can be taken offline for repair without disrupting IT operations.
   - Example: AWS regional availability zone, Microsoft Azure region.

4. **Tier IV — Fault Tolerant:**
   - **2N (or 2(N+1)) redundancy** — every component has a completely independent duplicate.
   - **Availability = 99.995%**, downtime ≈ **26.3 minutes**.
   - Tolerates **any single planned or unplanned failure** without IT interruption.
   - Example: Banking data centers, defense installations, Google hyperscale regions.

**Diagram:**
$$\text{Tier I} \xrightarrow{+\text{N+1 redundancy}} \text{Tier II} \xrightarrow{+\text{redundant paths}} \text{Tier III} \xrightarrow{+\text{2N duplication}} \text{Tier IV}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b): [7 Marks]

**Given:** $P_{total} = 4{,}500 \text{ kW}$, $P_{IT} = 3{,}000 \text{ kW}$, electricity rate = ₹8/kWh.

**(i) PUE:**
$$PUE = \frac{4{,}500}{3{,}000} = \mathbf{1.50}$$

**(ii) DCiE:**
$$DCiE = \left(\frac{3{,}000}{4{,}500}\right) \times 100\% = \mathbf{66.67\%}$$

**(iii) Overhead Power:**
$$P_{overhead} = 4{,}500 - 3{,}000 = \mathbf{1{,}500 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iv) Annual Energy Lost:**
$$E_{lost} = 1{,}500 \times 8{,}760 = \mathbf{13{,}140{,}000 \text{ kWh/year} \approx 13.14 \text{ GWh}}$$

**Annual Cost of Overhead:**
$$\text{Cost} = 13{,}140{,}000 \times 8 = \mathbf{₹ 10{,}51{,}20{,}000 \approx ₹ 10.51 \text{ Crore}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **1 mark lost** if you forget to multiply by **8,760 hours/year**.
> - **2 marks lost** if you mis-state the PUE formula direction.
> - **1 mark lost** for not showing units clearly (kWh/year vs MWh vs GWh).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q.B.** *(a)* Describe the **major subsystems of a modern data center**: power infrastructure, cooling infrastructure, network architecture, and storage architecture. Mention key technologies in each.

*(b)* Compare and contrast the **Hot Aisle / Cold Aisle** containment layout with the **confined hot-aisle containment** strategy. How does each affect PUE?

#### Model Solution — Part (a): [7 Marks]

A modern data center is built from **four tightly coupled subsystems**:

**1. Power Infrastructure:**
- Utility power → Transformer → **Automatic Transfer Switch (ATS)** → **UPS** (with VRLA or Li-ion battery bank) → **PDU** → Server racks.
- Backup: **Diesel Rotary UPS (DRUPS)** or diesel generators with **N+1 / 2N redundancy**.
- Monitoring via **PDU-level smart meters**.

**2. Cooling Infrastructure:**
- **CRAC** (Computer Room Air Conditioner) units, **CRAH** (Computer Room Air Handler).
- **Hot Aisle / Cold Aisle** containment.
- **Free cooling** (economizers, adiabatic coolers) — uses outside air when ambient < 18 °C.
- **Rear-door heat exchangers**, **immersion cooling** for AI racks.

**3. Network Architecture:**
- **Three-tier model**: Core → Aggregation → Access (ToR).
- Modern **Spine-Leaf (Fat-Tree)** topology with **25/100/400 GbE** links.
- **VXLAN-EVPN** overlay for multi-tenant segmentation.
- **Border Leaf** connects to **Internet / WAN**.

**4. Storage Architecture:**
- **DAS** (Direct Attached Storage) — local disks inside servers.
- **NAS** (Network Attached Storage) — file-level access over Ethernet (NFS, SMB).
- **SAN** (Storage Area Network) — block-level access over **Fibre Channel** or **iSCSI**.
- Modern: **Hyperconverged Infrastructure (HCI)** — compute + storage in one node (e.g., Nutanix, VMware vSAN).

#### Model Solution — Part (b): [7 Marks]

| Feature | **Standard Hot/Cold Aisle Layout** | **Confined Hot-Aisle Containment** |
|---|---|---|
| **Cold air supply** | Open ceiling plenum above racks | Enclosed cold aisle with plastic strip curtains |
| **Hot air return** | Open ceiling plenum above hot aisle | **Enclosed duct** returns hot air directly to CRAC |
| **Bypass airflow** | Possible (~20–30%) | Eliminated (~0%) |
| **CRAC efficiency** | Moderate | Significantly improved |
| **PUE improvement** | Baseline to ~1.4 | Can achieve **1.1–1.2** |
| **CAPEX** | Low (no enclosure needed) | Moderate (enclosures, doors, sensors) |
| **Suitable for** | Low-density deployments | **High-density (15–30+ kW/rack)** AI/HPC |

**Key Insight:** In **standard layout**, hot exhaust air mixes with cold intake of the next rack → AC must cool already-warm air → wastes energy. **Confined hot-aisle containment** physically separates hot and cold streams, allowing CRAC to work against a smaller ΔT, raising the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of chillers and thereby reducing PUE.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (validated against past KTU answer scripts):**
> 1. **Confusing Tier III & IV redundancy** — Tier III has N+1 with redundant paths; Tier IV has 2N. Writing "Tier IV has N+1" loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Using the wrong constant** — 8,760 hours/year (non-leap) vs 5,256,000 minutes/year. Mixing them up in a numerical is a **3-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Forgetting the conversion factor** in PUE problems — students often forget to convert kW × hours to kWh. Always show units.
> 4. **Not mentioning TIA-942** in tier questions — the question literally says "according to TIA-942". Omitting this earns partial credit only.
> 5. **Writing DCiE as 1/PUE without the ×100%** — loses **1 mark** because DCiE is by definition a percentage.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Data Center Technology (Module 3)**

- ☐ **Data Center Definition**: Centralized facility housing critical IT infrastructure, governed by **TIA-942**.
- ☐ **PUE** = $P_{total} / P_{IT}$; ideal = **1.0**; industry average ≈ **1.58**.
- ☐ **DCiE** = $(1/PUE) \times 100\%$; ideal = **100%**.
- ☐ **Tier I** = 99.671% (28.8 h), **Tier II** = 99.741% (22 h), **Tier III** = 99.982% (1.6 h, N+1), **Tier IV** = 99.995% (26.3 min, **2N**).
- ☐ **Concurrent maintainability** appears from **Tier III** onward.
- ☐ **Redundancy Models**: N (none), N+1 (one extra), 2N (double), 2(N+1) (maximum).
- ☐ **Cooling**: Hot Aisle / Cold Aisle containment is the **#1 lever to reduce PUE**.
- ☐ **Network Topologies**: 3-tier (Core-Agg-Access) for traditional; **Spine-Leaf** for hyperscale.
- ☐ **Storage Models**: **DAS, NAS, SAN** — block vs file vs object; modern trend is **Hyperconverged (HCI)**.
- ☐ **Annual constants**: 8,760 hours/year; 525,600 minutes/year; 365 days.
- ☐ **Sustainability metrics**: **CUE** (CO₂/kWh), **WUE** (L/kWh) — both ideal = **0**.
- ☐ **Virtualization is enabled by** the resource pooling in modern data centers — a direct link to cloud computing.
- ☐ **Green data centers** integrate free cooling, renewable PPAs, and AI-driven workload scheduling to push PUE below **1.15**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Web Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Web Technology** is the comprehensive set of protocols, languages, application programming interfaces (APIs), and software frameworks that enable communication, data exchange, and service delivery over the **World Wide Web (WWW)** using the **Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP/HTTPS)**. In the context of **Cloud Computing**, web technology forms the foundational client-server interaction layer that allows end-users to access distributed, virtualized, and elastic computing resources through standard web browsers and lightweight client applications—without requiring local installation of specialized software.

The **W3C (World Wide Web Consortium)** standardizes web technologies, and the layered architecture is conventionally represented as:

$$\text{Web Technology Stack} = \text{Transport} \cup \text{Application} \cup \text{Presentation} \cup \text{Content Layer}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Web Technology is treated as the *gateway layer* of cloud computing. Every SaaS (Software as a Service) application you access via a browser (Gmail, Microsoft 365, Google Docs) is fundamentally a *web technology product* deployed over a cloud infrastructure.

### 1.2 Core Sub-Components of Web Technology

| Sub-Component | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **HTML / HTML5** | HyperText Markup Language – defines page structure | `<div>`, `<section>`, `<canvas>` |
| **CSS / CSS3** | Cascading Style Sheets – controls visual presentation | Color, layout, animation |
| **JavaScript (JS)** | Client-side scripting language for dynamic behavior | `React`, `Angular`, `Vue.js` |
| **HTTP / HTTPS** | Stateless request-response protocol | `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE` |
| **Web Services** | Machine-to-machine interaction over the web | **REST**, **SOAP** |
| **Data Formats** | Lightweight structured data interchange | **JSON**, **XML** |
| **AJAX** | Asynchronous JavaScript and XML | Gmail, Google Maps |
| **Web Server** | Software that serves HTTP requests | **Apache**, **Nginx**, **IIS** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Restaurant" Analogy**
>
> Imagine the **Web** as a giant restaurant chain:
> - The **Customer (Client/Browser)** is you, sitting at a table with a menu.
> - The **Menu (HTML)** lists what you can order — it defines *what* is available.
> - The **Presentation (CSS)** is how the food is plated — it defines *how* it looks.
> - The **Chef (JavaScript / Server-side logic)** is the dynamic worker who customizes your order in real time.
> - The **Kitchen (Web Server + Database)** is the back-end where actual preparation (data processing) happens.
> - The **Waiter (HTTP Protocol)** carries your request to the kitchen and brings back the response.
> - The **Restaurant Chain Itself (Cloud Infrastructure)** is the scalable, multi-location enterprise that can handle millions of customers simultaneously.
>
> Web technology is *the entire dining experience*; cloud computing is the *business model* that allows the restaurant chain to open 10 or 10,000 branches based on demand.

### 1.4 Evolution of the Web (Critical for KTU Theory)

The web has evolved through distinct generations, each adding new technological capabilities:

- **Web 1.0 (1990–2004):** *Static* – Read-only content. HTML pages with no interactivity. Example: Early Yahoo directory.
- **Web 2.0 (2004–present):** *Dynamic / Interactive* – User-generated content, AJAX, social media. Examples: Facebook, YouTube, Wikipedia.
- **Web 3.0 (Emerging):** *Semantic / Decentralized* – Linked data, blockchain, AI-driven, decentralized identifiers.

The standard HTTP request-response cycle is governed by:

$$\text{Request} \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP/HTTPS}} \text{Server} \xrightarrow{\text{Response}} \text{Client}$$

where the **Request** contains an HTTP method, headers, URI, and (optionally) a body; the **Response** contains a status code, headers, and the resource representation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Web Architecture: Layer-by-Layer Analysis

Web technology is best understood as a **multi-tier architecture**. Each tier has a distinct responsibility:

#### Tier 1 — Presentation Layer (Client-Side)
- **Technologies:** HTML5, CSS3, JavaScript, responsive frameworks (Bootstrap).
- **Responsibility:** Render the User Interface (UI), capture user input, validate data, communicate with the server asynchronously.
- **Why it matters in Cloud:** Reduces server load by handling logic in the browser; supports thin clients.

#### Tier 2 — Application / Logic Layer (Server-Side)
- **Technologies:** Node.js, Python (Django/Flask), Java (Spring), PHP, .NET.
- **Responsibility:** Business logic, authentication, session management, API orchestration.
- **Why it matters in Cloud:** Horizontally scalable via containers (Docker/Kubernetes) on IaaS platforms (AWS EC2, Azure VMs).

#### Tier 3 — Data Layer
- **Technologies:** SQL (MySQL, PostgreSQL), NoSQL (MongoDB, DynamoDB), in-memory caches (Redis).
- **Responsibility:** Persistent storage, retrieval, and integrity of data.
- **Why it matters in Cloud:** Often provisioned as managed PaaS services (Amazon RDS, Azure Cosmos DB).

### 2.2 HTTP Protocol Deep Dive

HTTP is **stateless**: every request is independent. State is preserved using **cookies**, **sessions**, or **tokens (JWT)**.

#### Standard HTTP Methods (Verbs)

| Method | Purpose | Idempotent? | Safe? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `GET` | Retrieve a resource | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| `POST` | Create a new resource | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| `PUT` | Replace a resource entirely | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| `PATCH` | Partial update of a resource | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| `DELETE` | Remove a resource | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| `HEAD` | Same as GET but no response body | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| `OPTIONS` | Describe communication options | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Idempotency** is a frequently tested KTU concept. An operation is *idempotent* if performing it multiple times produces the same result as performing it once. `GET`, `PUT`, `DELETE` are idempotent; `POST` is **not**.

#### HTTP Status Code Classes (KTU High-Yield)

$$\text{Status Code Class} = \begin{cases} 1xx & \text{Informational} \\ 2xx & \text{Success (200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content)} \\ 3xx & \text{Redirection (301 Moved, 304 Not Modified)} \\ 4xx & \text{Client Error (400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 404 Not Found)} \\ 5xx & \text{Server Error (500 Internal Server Error, 503 Service Unavailable)} \end{cases}$$

### 2.3 Web Services: SOAP vs REST (Most Tested Topic)

A **Web Service** is a software system designed to support **machine-to-machine interaction over a network**.

#### 2.3.1 SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol)

- **Protocol-based**, heavyweight.
- Uses **XML** exclusively for messaging.
- Transport-independent: works over HTTP, SMTP, TCP.
- Defines a strict contract via **WSDL (Web Services Description Language)**.
- Supports **WS-Security** for enterprise-grade encryption.

#### 2.3.2 REST (Representational State Transfer)

- **Architectural style** (defined by Roy Fielding, 2000), lightweight.
- Uses standard HTTP methods.
- Can return **JSON**, XML, YAML, plain text.
- Stateless, cacheable, uniform interface.
- Dominant choice for modern cloud-native APIs (e.g., AWS, Google Cloud, Azure APIs).

#### 2.3.3 REST Constraints (Fielding's 6 Constraints)

1. **Client-Server** separation
2. **Stateless** — no client context stored on the server
3. **Cacheable** — responses can declare cacheability
4. **Uniform Interface** — standardized resource URIs
5. **Layered System** — client cannot tell if connected to end server or proxy
6. **Code on Demand** *(optional)* — server can send executable code

### 2.4 AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML)

AJAX is **not a technology** but a *technique* that uses:
- The browser's built-in **`XMLHttpRequest`** object (or modern `fetch()` API).
- **JavaScript** to update the DOM without full page reload.
- Data formats: **JSON** (preferred) or **XML**.

The flow is:

$$\text{Browser} \xrightarrow{\text{Async Request}} \text{Web Server} \xrightarrow{\text{Async Response (JSON/XML)}} \text{Browser (partial DOM update)}$$

### 2.5 JSON vs XML (Data Interchange Formats)

| Property | JSON | XML |
|---|---|---|
| Stands for | JavaScript Object Notation | eXtensible Markup Language |
| Syntax | Key-value pairs | Custom tags |
| Verbosity | Lightweight | Verbose |
| Parsing | Native in JavaScript | Requires DOM parser |
| Data Types | Yes (string, number, boolean, null, array, object) | All data is text |
| Cloud Preference | **Preferred** (REST APIs) | Legacy (SOAP) |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Formula / Definition | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| HTTP Request-Response Cycle | $\text{Client} \rightarrow \text{Server (via HTTP)}$ | Foundation of web |
| Latency | $L = T_{\text{transmit}} + T_{\text{propagate}} + T_{\text{process}}$ | Performance evaluation |
| Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Data Size (bits)}}{\text{Time (sec)}}$ | Capacity planning |
| Cache Hit Ratio | $H = \frac{\text{Cache Hits}}{\text{Total Requests}}$ | CDN optimization |
| Page Load Time | $T_{\text{load}} = T_{\text{DNS}} + T_{\text{TCP}} + T_{\text{TLS}} + T_{\text{TTFB}} + T_{\text{render}}$ | Web performance |
| Stateless Protocol | $\text{Each Request} \perp \text{Previous Request}$ | HTTP, REST |
| Idempotent Operation | $f(x) = f(f(x))$ | PUT, DELETE, GET |
| REST API Endpoint | $\text{URI} = \text{Protocol} + \text{Base} + \text{Resource Path}$ | API design |
| JSON Data Size | $\vert S_{\text{JSON}} \vert \approx \vert S_{\text{XML}} \vert \times 0.4$ | Optimization |
| Concurrent Connections | $C = \frac{\text{Max Users}}{\text{Active Session Time}} \times \text{Total Time}$ | Server sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Every modern cloud SaaS product you interact with — from Salesforce to Zoom to Canva — uses REST APIs over HTTPS with JSON payloads, served by a web server (Nginx/Apache) fronted by a CDN, deployed on containerized infrastructure. The web technology stack is the *de facto standard interface* for cloud services.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Estimating Web Server Capacity (Concurrent Users)

A classic KTU analytical problem. Given user behavior statistics, derive the required number of concurrent connections a web server must support.

**Given:**
- Total registered users: $U = 10{,}000$
- Average user session duration: $T_{\text{session}} = 30$ minutes
- Observation window: $T_{\text{obs}} = 8$ hours (a working day)
- Peak concurrency factor: $k = 0.25$ (25% users active at peak)

**Step 1 — Calculate Average Concurrent Users**

Average concurrent users are those actively engaged with the server at any instant. The Little's Law variant for web systems is:

$$N_{\text{avg}} = \frac{U \times T_{\text{session}}}{T_{\text{obs}}}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$N_{\text{avg}} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 30 \text{ min}}{8 \times 60 \text{ min}}$$

$$N_{\text{avg}} = \frac{300{,}000 \text{ user-minutes}}{480 \text{ minutes}}$$

$$N_{\text{avg}} = 625 \text{ users (average)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Peak Concurrency Factor**

Peak load may exceed average load. The peak concurrent user count is:

$$N_{\text{peak}} = k \times N_{\text{avg}}$$

$$N_{\text{peak}} = 0.25 \times 625$$

$$N_{\text{peak}} = 156.25 \approx 157 \text{ users}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Concurrent Connections**

Assuming each user opens 1.2 simultaneous browser tabs/connections on average:

$$C_{\text{conn}} = N_{\text{peak}} \times n_{\text{tabs}}$$

$$C_{\text{conn}} = 157 \times 1.2 \approx 189 \text{ concurrent TCP connections}$$

**Step 4 — Derive Required Throughput**

If each user generates an average of 3 requests per session, the total request load is:

$$R_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{peak}} \times r_{\text{user}} = 157 \times 3 = 471 \text{ requests per peak instant}$$

If we assume a peak request rate of $\lambda = 471$ requests/min, the server's minimum throughput is:

$$\mu_{\min} = \frac{471}{60} \approx 7.85 \text{ requests/second (RPS)}$$

For safety margin (typically **3x**), provision:

$$\mu_{\text{design}} = 3 \times \mu_{\min} \approx 23.55 \text{ RPS}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** The derivation chains Little's Law (avg concurrency) → peak adjustment → per-connection expansion → throughput estimation → safety margin. Each step reflects a real engineering decision.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: SOAP vs REST (Decision Framework)

| Evaluation Criterion | SOAP | REST | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Format | XML only | JSON / XML / others | **High** |
| Transport | HTTP, SMTP, TCP | HTTP/HTTPS | Medium |
| State Management | Stateful (WS-*) | Stateless | **High** |
| Security | WS-Security, SSL | OAuth 2.0, JWT, TLS | **High** |
| Performance | Slower (XML parsing) | Faster (JSON) | Medium |
| Contract | WSDL (mandatory) | OpenAPI/Swagger (optional) | Medium |
| Use Case | Enterprise, banking, legacy | Public APIs, mobile, cloud-native | **High** |
| Tooling | Heavy (JAX-WS, WCF) | Light (curl, Postman, fetch) | Low |

### 3.3 Derivation: Page Load Time Decomposition

The total page load time is decomposed as:

$$T_{\text{load}} = T_{\text{DNS}} + T_{\text{TCP}} + T_{\text{TLS}} + T_{\text{TTFB}} + T_{\text{render}}$$

**Step 1 — DNS Lookup Time**
$$T_{\text{DNS}} \approx 20 \text{ to } 120 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — TCP Connection Time** (3-way handshake)
$$T_{\text{TCP}} = 3 \times \text{RTT} \approx 3 \times 50 \text{ ms} = 150 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — TLS Handshake Time** (for HTTPS)
$$T_{\text{TLS}} = 2 \times \text{RTT} \approx 100 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Time to First Byte (TTFB)**
$$T_{\text{TTFB}} = T_{\text{server-process}} \approx 200 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Render Time** (HTML parsing, CSS, JS execution, image load)
$$T_{\text{render}} \approx 300 \text{ ms}$$

**Total Load Time:**
$$T_{\text{load}} = 50 + 150 + 100 + 200 + 300 = 800 \text{ ms}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** For cloud-hosted web apps, the **TTFB** component is the only one controllable by server-side optimization. CDNs, edge caching, and HTTP/2 multiplexing primarily reduce $T_{\text{TCP}}$ and $T_{\text{TLS}}$ components.

### 3.4 RESTful URI Design (Idempotency Demonstration)

Show that a `DELETE` operation is idempotent:

$$\text{DELETE } /api/v1/users/42 \xrightarrow{} 204 \text{ No Content}$$

$$\text{DELETE } /api/v1/users/42 \xrightarrow{} 204 \text{ No Content (same result)}$$

$$\text{DELETE } /api/v1/users/42 \xrightarrow{} 204 \text{ No Content (idempotent)}$$

Mathematically, the idempotency property is:

$$f(x) = f(f(x)) = f(f(f(x))) = \ldots$$

For `POST` (NOT idempotent):

$$\text{POST } /api/v1/users \ \text{body:}\{name:"Alice"\} \xrightarrow{} 201 \text{ Created (User 1)}$$

$$\text{POST } /api/v1/users \ \text{body:}\{name:"Alice"\} \xrightarrow{} 201 \text{ Created (User 2 — duplicate!)}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Web Technology Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientA[User Browser Client Side]
    htmlA[HTML5 Structure Layer]
    cssA[CSS3 Presentation Layer]
    jsA[JavaScript Logic Layer]
    httpA[HTTP HTTPS Protocol]
    serverA[Web Server Nginx Apache]
    appA[Application Server Node js Java]
    apiA[REST API Gateway]
    dbA[Database SQL NoSQL]
    cloudA[Cloud Infrastructure IaaS PaaS]
    cdnA[CDN Edge Cache]
    secA[Security Layer OAuth JWT TLS]

    clientA --> htmlA
    clientA --> cssA
    clientA --> jsA
    jsA --> httpA
    httpA --> cdnA
    cdnA --> secA
    secA --> serverA
    serverA --> appA
    appA --> apiA
    apiA --> dbA
    appA --> cloudA
    dbA --> cloudA
    serverA --> cloudA
```

### 4.2 AJAX Request-Response Flow Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Browser
    participant JS as JavaScript Engine
    participant XHR as XMLHttpRequest
    participant S as Web Server
    participant DB as Database

    U->>JS: Click button event
    JS->>XHR: Create async request
    XHR->>S: HTTP GET POST PUT DELETE
    S->>DB: Query or update data
    DB-->>S: Return data
    S-->>XHR: HTTP 200 OK with JSON
    XHR-->>JS: Trigger onreadystatechange
    JS->>U: Update DOM partial page refresh
    Note over U,DB: No full page reload
```

### 4.3 SOAP vs REST Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SOAPStack [SOAP Architecture]
        soapC[Client Application]
        soapW[WSDL Contract]
        soapX[XML Message Envelope]
        soapH[HTTP SMTP Transport]
        soapC --> soapW
        soapW --> soapX
        soapX --> soapH
    end

    subgraph RESTStack [REST Architecture]
        restC[Client Application]
        restU[Uniform Resource URI]
        restJ[JSON or XML Payload]
        restH[HTTP Verbs GET POST PUT DELETE]
        restC --> restU
        restU --> restJ
        restU --> restH
    end
```

### 4.4 Cloud Web Hosting Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    geo[Global Users Geo Distributed]
    lb[Load Balancer Layer]
    web1[Web Server Instance 1]
    web2[Web Server Instance 2]
    web3[Web Server Instance N Elastic]
    cache[In Memory Cache Redis]
    queue[Message Queue]
    dbmaster[Primary Database]
    dbslave[Read Replica 1]
    dbslave2[Read Replica 2]
    storage[Object Storage S3 Buckets]

    geo --> lb
    lb --> web1
    lb --> web2
    lb --> web3
    web1 --> cache
    web2 --> cache
    web3 --> cache
    web1 --> queue
    web2 --> queue
    web3 --> queue
    web1 --> dbmaster
    web2 --> dbslave
    web3 --> dbslave2
    web1 --> storage
    web2 --> storage
    web3 --> storage
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** Each diagram represents a *functional architecture* — the actual physical deployment in a real cloud (AWS, Azure, GCP) mirrors these logical flows. KTU examiners reward diagrams that are clearly labeled and show the *flow of data*, not just boxes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Web Service". With a suitable example, explain how a RESTful web service differs from a traditional SOAP-based service.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Web Service** is a standardized software system designed to enable **machine-to-machine communication** over a network, typically using HTTP/HTTPS as the transport protocol. It exposes well-defined interfaces that other applications can invoke programmatically.

**REST (Representational State Transfer)** is an **architectural style** that uses standard HTTP methods (`GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`) and typically exchanges data in lightweight **JSON** format. Example: `GET https://api.weather.com/v1/cities/bangalore` returns weather data as JSON.

**SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol)** is a **protocol** that uses **XML** exclusively, requires a formal **WSDL** contract, and supports advanced features like **WS-Security** and **transactional reliability**. Example: Banking inter-bank transfer services.

**Key Differences:**
- REST is lightweight and stateless; SOAP is heavyweight and can be stateful.
- REST uses JSON (faster, smaller); SOAP uses XML (slower, more verbose).
- REST is preferred for public web/mobile APIs; SOAP dominates in enterprise/B2B systems.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for REST explanation, 1 mark for SOAP distinction.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is AJAX? Explain its advantages in modern web applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML)** is a *web development technique* (not a standalone technology) that enables web pages to send and receive data **asynchronously** from a server **without requiring a full page reload**. It uses the `XMLHttpRequest` object (or modern `fetch()` API) combined with JavaScript and DOM manipulation.

**Advantages:**
1. **Improved User Experience:** Only portions of the page update dynamically (e.g., live search suggestions, chat messages, stock tickers).
2. **Reduced Bandwidth:** Only necessary data is exchanged, not entire HTML pages.
3. **Increased Speed:** Asynchronous processing means the user can continue interacting while data loads in the background.
4. **Decoupled Architecture:** Separates presentation (frontend) from data (backend), enabling RESTful API design — a cornerstone of cloud-native applications.

**Example:** Gmail uses AJAX to load new emails instantly without refreshing the inbox page.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for components, 1 mark for advantages with example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **client-server architecture of a web application**. Discuss the role of the HTTP protocol and clearly differentiate between HTTP `GET` and `POST` methods. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **REST architectural style** in detail. List and briefly describe the **six constraints** of REST as defined by Roy Fielding. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Client-Server Architecture (7 marks):**

The **client-server model** partitions a web application into two logical entities:
- **Client:** The user's web browser (Chrome, Firefox, Edge) running on a personal device.
- **Server:** A remote machine hosting the web application and its database.

**Workflow:**
1. The user types a URL (e.g., `https://www.example.com`) into the browser.
2. The browser sends an **HTTP request** to the web server.
3. The server processes the request (queries the database, runs business logic).
4. The server returns an **HTTP response** containing the requested resource (HTML, JSON, etc.).
5. The browser renders the response visually.

**Role of HTTP:**
HTTP is a **stateless, application-layer protocol** that defines how messages are formatted and transmitted. It enables communication between client and server. State is maintained externally using **cookies**, **sessions**, or **JWT tokens**.

**Difference between GET and POST:**

| Property | GET | POST |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Retrieve data | Submit/Create data |
| Data Location | URL query string (`?id=42`) | Request body |
| Visibility | Visible in browser address bar | Not visible in URL |
| Idempotent | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Cacheable | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Security | Less secure (logged in history) | More secure (not in URL) |
| Data Limit | ~2048 characters (browser-dependent) | No strict limit |
| Use Case | Search queries, page loads | Login forms, file uploads, payment submissions |

**Example:**
- `GET /products?id=10` — Retrieves product with ID 10.
- `POST /products` with body `{ "name": "Laptop", "price": 50000 }` — Creates a new product.

*(Award 2 marks for architecture diagram, 2 marks for HTTP role, 3 marks for GET vs POST comparison table.)*

---

**(b) REST Architectural Style (7 marks):**

**REST (Representational State Transfer)** is an **architectural style** introduced by **Roy Fielding** in his 2000 doctoral dissertation. It defines a set of constraints that, when applied, yield a scalable, stateless, and uniform interface for distributed hypermedia systems.

**Six Constraints of REST:**

1. **Client-Server Separation:** The client (UI) and server (data storage) are independent. This separation allows them to evolve independently — e.g., the same backend API can serve a web app, a mobile app, and an IoT device.

2. **Statelessness:** Each request from the client must contain **all information** needed to process it. The server does not store any client session context between requests. This improves scalability since any server can handle any request.

3. **Cacheability:** Responses must explicitly indicate whether they are cacheable or not (via HTTP headers like `Cache-Control`). This reduces latency and server load. Example: A product image can be cached for 24 hours.

4. **Uniform Interface:** A standardized way of communicating between client and server using:
   - **Resource identification** (URIs: `/users/42`)
   - **Resource manipulation** (HTTP methods)
   - **Self-descriptive messages** (Content-Type: application/json)
   - **HATEOAS** (Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State)

5. **Layered System:** The architecture may consist of multiple layers (proxies, gateways, load balancers) that the client cannot distinguish from the end server. This enables scalability, security (firewalls), and legacy encapsulation.

6. **Code on Demand (Optional):** The server can temporarily extend client functionality by transferring executable code (e.g., JavaScript applets, scripts). This is the *only optional* constraint.

**REST Example Endpoints:**
- `GET /api/students` — List all students
- `GET /api/students/101` — Retrieve student 101
- `POST /api/students` — Create a new student
- `PUT /api/students/101` — Update student 101
- `DELETE /api/students/101` — Remove student 101

*(Award 1 mark for REST definition, 5 marks for explaining all six constraints, 1 mark for endpoint example.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain **JSON and XML** as data interchange formats in web services. Compare them on at least five parameters. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, describe the **multi-tier architecture of a web application** in a cloud environment. Explain how each tier can be independently scaled. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) JSON and XML (7 marks):**

**JSON (JavaScript Object Notation):**
A lightweight, text-based, language-independent data interchange format derived from JavaScript object syntax. It uses key-value pairs and is natively supported in JavaScript.

**Example:**
```json
{
  "name": "Alice",
  "age": 22,
  "courses": ["Cloud", "AI", "DBMS"]
}
```

**XML (eXtensible Markup Language):**
A markup language that defines rules for encoding documents in a human-readable and machine-readable format using custom tags.

**Example:**
```xml
<student>
  <name>Alice</name>
  <age>22</age>
  <courses>
    <course>Cloud</course>
    <course>AI</course>
  </courses>
</student>
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | JSON | XML |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | JavaScript Object Notation | SGML-derived markup language |
| Verbosity | Concise, less markup | Verbose, opening + closing tags |
| Parsing | Fast, native `JSON.parse()` in JS | Requires DOM/SAX parser |
| Data Types | Supports native types (string, number, boolean, array) | All data is text (string) |
| Namespace Support | No | Yes (useful in SOAP) |
| Comments | Not supported | Supported (`<!-- comment -->`) |
| Cloud-Native Use | **Preferred for REST APIs** | Used in SOAP, legacy systems |
| File Size (same data) | ~40% smaller | ~60% larger |

**Conclusion:** JSON has become the **de facto standard** for modern cloud-based REST APIs due to its smaller payload, faster parsing, and native JavaScript integration.

*(Award 2 marks for JSON definition+example, 2 marks for XML definition+example, 3 marks for comparison table.)*

---

**(b) Multi-Tier Architecture in Cloud (7 marks):**

The **multi-tier (n-tier) architecture** separates an application into logical layers, each with distinct responsibilities. A typical cloud web application has **3 to 4 tiers**.

**The Tiers:**

1. **Presentation Tier (Client Tier):**
   - Runs in the user's browser.
   - Built with HTML5, CSS3, JavaScript frameworks (React, Angular).
   - Communicates with the next tier via HTTPS/REST calls.

2. **Application / Logic Tier (Middleware):**
   - Implements business rules, validation, and workflow orchestration.
   - Built using Node.js, Java Spring, Python Django, .NET Core.
   - Deployed on cloud VMs (EC2) or containers (Kubernetes pods).

3. **Data Tier (Persistence):**
   - Stores structured (SQL) and unstructured (NoSQL) data.
   - Managed cloud services: Amazon RDS, Azure SQL, DynamoDB, MongoDB Atlas.
   - Replicated across multiple Availability Zones for durability.

4. **(Optional) Caching / CDN Tier:**
   - In-memory cache (Redis, Memcached) for hot data.
   - Content Delivery Network (CloudFront, Akamai) for static assets.

**Diagram (Block-Level):**
```
[Browser] → [CDN] → [Load Balancer] → [App Server Cluster] → [Cache] → [Database]
```

**Independent Scalability in Cloud:**

| Tier | Scaling Strategy | Cloud Service Example |
|---|---|---|
| Presentation | CDN caches static assets globally | CloudFront, Akamai |
| Application | Horizontal scaling with auto-scaling groups | AWS Auto Scaling, Kubernetes HPA |
| Database | Read replicas, sharding, managed service | RDS Multi-AZ, DynamoDB on-demand |
| Cache | Elasticache cluster with replication | AWS ElastiCache (Redis) |

**Advantages of Multi-Tier in Cloud:**
- **High Availability:** Failure in one tier does not crash the entire app.
- **Elastic Scalability:** Each tier scales independently based on its specific load.
- **Security:** Database tier is never directly exposed to the internet.
- **Maintainability:** Each tier can be updated independently.

*(Award 2 marks for tier explanation, 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for scaling strategies, 1 mark for advantages.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SOAP and REST:** Students often write "REST is a protocol." This is wrong — REST is an *architectural style*; SOAP is the protocol. Cost: **1–2 marks lost**.
> 2. **Mixing up HTTP verbs:** Writing `GET` to create resources or `POST` to retrieve them. Always remember: `GET = read`, `POST = create`, `PUT = replace`, `DELETE = remove`.
> 3. **Forgetting idempotency:** If asked "Which HTTP methods are idempotent?" — answer `GET`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, `HEAD`, `OPTIONS`. Do not list `POST` or `PATCH`.
> 4. **Skipping diagrams in 14-mark answers:** A neat labeled diagram carries **2–3 marks** by itself. Always include one.
> 5. **Missing Fielding's six constraints in REST:** Examiners expect **all six** listed by name with at least one-line explanations.
> 6. **JSON vs XML in 7-mark questions:** A *comparison table* is mandatory; textual comparison alone yields partial marks.
> 7. **Stateless vs Stateful confusion:** HTTP and REST are *stateless*; sessions and cookies are workarounds — not part of the protocol itself.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — WEB TECHNOLOGY**

- ✅ **Web Technology** = HTML + CSS + JavaScript + HTTP + Web Services + Data Formats.
- ✅ **HTTP is stateless**; state is preserved via cookies, sessions, JWT.
- ✅ **HTTP Methods (Verbs):** `GET` (read), `POST` (create), `PUT` (replace), `PATCH` (partial update), `DELETE` (remove), `HEAD`, `OPTIONS`.
- ✅ **Idempotent methods:** `GET`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, `HEAD`, `OPTIONS`. **Non-idempotent:** `POST`, `PATCH`.
- ✅ **HTTP Status Codes:** 2xx success, 3xx redirect, 4xx client error, 5xx server error.
- ✅ **SOAP** = protocol, XML-only, WSDL contract, heavyweight, used in enterprise.
- ✅ **REST** = architectural style (Roy Fielding, 2000), JSON preferred, stateless, cacheable.
- ✅ **REST's 6 constraints:** Client-Server, Stateless, Cacheable, Uniform Interface, Layered System, Code-on-Demand (optional).
- ✅ **AJAX** = technique, not a technology; uses `XMLHttpRequest` or `fetch()`; enables partial page updates.
- ✅ **JSON** is ~40% smaller than XML for equivalent data; preferred in cloud REST APIs.
- ✅ **Multi-tier architecture:** Presentation → Application → Data → Cache/CDN. Each tier scales independently in cloud.
- ✅ **Page Load Time = DNS + TCP + TLS + TTFB + Render.**
- ✅ **Web 1.0** = static, **Web 2.0** = dynamic/AJAX/social, **Web 3.0** = semantic/decentralized.
- ✅ **Web Server examples:** Apache, Nginx, IIS. **Frameworks:** React, Angular, Vue (frontend); Node.js, Spring, Django (backend).
- ✅ **Cloud integration:** Web technology is the *user-facing interface* of every SaaS cloud application.
- ✅ **For 14-mark questions:** Always include a *labeled diagram* and a *comparison table* wherever applicable.
- ✅ **Examiner's mantra:** "Definition → Characteristics → Diagram → Example → Application in Cloud."

**End of Module 3 — Web Technology Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multitenant Technology
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multitenant Technology

# Multitenant Technology — Foundational Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 3):** Multitenant Technology is one of the **architectural pillars** of Cloud Computing and is directly tied to the **SaaS (Software as a Service)** delivery model. KTU examiners routinely test this topic for **short definitions (3 marks)** and **architectural comparison questions (14 marks)**.

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Multitenancy** is a software architecture pattern in which a *single instance* of a software application running on a server serves **multiple customer organizations (tenants)**. Each tenant perceives the system as if it were exclusively dedicated to them, even though the underlying hardware, middleware, application code, and database resources are shared.

Mathematically, if $\mathcal{T} = \{T_1, T_2, T_3, \dots, T_n\}$ represents the set of **$n$ tenants**, and $\mathcal{I}$ represents a **single application instance**, then multitenancy can be expressed as the function:

$$f : \mathcal{T} \longrightarrow \mathcal{I} \quad \text{such that} \quad \forall \, T_i \in \mathcal{T}, \; \exists \, \text{isolated view}(T_i, \mathcal{I})$$

The phrase **"isolated view"** is critical — each tenant must have logically isolated *data, configuration, presentation, and performance* while sharing the physical infrastructure.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Standard Distinction:**
> - **Multi-instance model:** Each tenant gets a *separate* application and database instance.
> - **Multi-tenant model:** All tenants share *one* application and *one* database, but their data is logically partitioned.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Building"

Imagine a **high-rise apartment building**:

- The **building structure, plumbing, electrical wiring, and elevators** are *shared* by all residents — this is the **shared infrastructure**.
- Each resident (tenant) has their **own locked apartment** (isolated data and configuration).
- The **lobby, gym, and parking lot** are shared amenities — this is the **shared application layer**.
- The building manager (cloud provider) maintains everything for *all* residents at once — this represents **centralized maintenance and upgrades**.

Just as the apartment building owner saves cost by serving many families from one structure, a cloud provider saves cost by serving many organizations from one application stack. This is the **economic engine** behind SaaS platforms like Salesforce, Microsoft 365, and Google Workspace.

---

## 1.3 Why Multitenancy Matters in Cloud Computing

| Driver | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Resource Efficiency** | A single server can host hundreds of tenants, maximizing **CPU utilization** (often reaching **>80%** versus **5–10%** in single-tenant setups). |
| **Cost Reduction** | Operational costs (power, cooling, licensing) are amortized across tenants. |
| **Scalability** | New tenants can be onboarded with **near-zero incremental cost** (onboarding time reduced from days to minutes). |
| **Centralized Maintenance** | A single update/patch applies to all tenants simultaneously. |
| **Elasticity** | Resources scale dynamically based on **collective tenant demand**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Multitenancy is the *enabler* of the cloud's **"pay-as-you-go"** pricing model. Without it, providers could not offer the cost-efficiency required for SaaS economics.

---

## 1.4 Tenancy vs. Multi-User — Clearing a Common Confusion

| Feature | Multi-User System | Multi-Tenant System |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Users within **one organization** | Multiple **separate organizations** |
| **Data Isolation** | Shared database tables | Logically partitioned data per tenant |
| **Customization** | Limited | Per-tenant branding, workflows |
| **Example** | University ERP for students | Salesforce CRM for 1000s of companies |

---

## 1.5 Core Terminology — Must-Know for KTU Exams

- **Tenant:** A single customer organization subscribed to the SaaS application.
- **Tenant Isolation:** Logical/physical separation of tenant data and operations.
- **Tenant-Aware Application:** Code that dynamically determines the requesting tenant and applies tenant-specific rules.
- **Metadata Repository:** A central catalog storing tenant-specific configurations.
- **Shared Kernel / Shared Schema / Separate Schema:** Database strategies used to isolate tenant data.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis — Multitenant Architecture Layers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Expectation:** For 14-mark questions, students must demonstrate understanding of *layer-wise* multitenancy — not just a surface-level definition. Examiners look for **isolation mechanisms** at the **application, database, and infrastructure layers**.

---

## 2.1 The Three Layers of Multitenancy

Multitenant architecture operates at three distinct layers. Each layer introduces different **isolation guarantees, scaling behavior, and customization limits**.

### Layer 1: Infrastructure Layer (Virtualization-Based)
- Uses **virtual machines (VMs)** or **containers** to provide each tenant with an isolated operating environment.
- Highest isolation, lowest resource density.
- Example: A cloud provider gives Tenant A and Tenant B separate VMs running on a shared hypervisor.

### Layer 2: Application Layer (Shared Application)
- A **single application instance** serves multiple tenants.
- Tenant identity is established through **HTTP headers, session tokens, or subdomain mapping** (e.g., `tenantA.saas.com`, `tenantB.saas.com`).
- The application logic dynamically queries a **metadata repository** to apply tenant-specific rules.

### Layer 3: Database Layer (Shared Schema)
- A **single database instance** stores data from all tenants.
- Tenant discrimination happens through a **`tenant_id` column** in shared tables.

---

## 2.2 Isolation Strategies — The "Isolation Spectrum"

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The **degree of isolation** is a trade-off between **cost-efficiency** and **security/customization**. Examiners love asking this as a comparison.

| Strategy | Isolation Strength | Cost Efficiency | Customization | KTU-Marked As |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Shared Everything** (Shared DB + Schema + Instance) | Lowest | Highest | Lowest | Pure SaaS model |
| **Shared Schema, Separate DB** | Low-Medium | High | Medium | Common SaaS |
| **Separate Schema, Shared DB** | Medium | Medium | High | Mid-tier SaaS |
| **Separate DB, Shared Server** | High | Medium | High | Enterprise SaaS |
| **Separate Server (Multi-Instance)** | Highest | Lowest | Highest | Private cloud |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Multitenancy Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute value notation to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Metric | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tenant Density** | $D = \dfrac{N_T}{N_S}$ | Number of tenants per server | tenants / server |
| **Resource Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i^{used}}{R^{total}} \times 100\%$ | Aggregate usage vs. total capacity | % |
| **Cost per Tenant** | $C_T = \dfrac{C_{infra} + C_{ops}}{N_T}$ | Amortized cost per tenant | \$ / tenant |
| **Isolation Overhead** | $O_{iso} = \dfrac{T_{isolated} - T_{shared}}{T_{shared}} \times 100\%$ | Performance penalty of isolation | % |
| **Tenant Onboarding Time** | $T_{on} = T_{config} + T_{prov}$ | Time to add a new tenant | seconds |
| **Tenant Fairness Index** | $F = 1 - \dfrac{\sigma(R_i)}{\mu(R_i)}$ | Measures equitable resource distribution | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **Total Cost of Ownership Savings** | $S = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_{multi}}{C_{single}}\right) \times 100\%$ | Savings vs. single-tenant model | % |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Real-World Applications

| Domain | Use of Multitenancy |
|---|---|
| **CRM Software** | Salesforce, HubSpot — thousands of companies share one CRM platform. |
| **Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)** | SAP S/4HANA Cloud serves multiple corporations. |
| **E-Commerce Platforms** | Shopify hosts millions of merchants on shared infrastructure. |
| **Collaboration Tools** | Microsoft Teams, Slack, Zoom — multi-tenant SaaS. |
| **Healthcare** | Multi-tenant EHR (Electronic Health Record) systems with HIPAA-compliant isolation. |
| **Banking & FinTech** | Per-tenant encryption keys and audit trails. |

---

## 2.5 Advantages vs. Disadvantages (KTU Examiner's Comparison Table)

> [!NOTE]
> This table is a **high-frequency KTU question**. Memorize the structure.

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Lower infrastructure cost per tenant | Complex tenant isolation logic |
| Easier maintenance (single codebase) | Security risk if isolation fails |
| Faster tenant onboarding | Limited deep customization per tenant |
| Better resource utilization | "Noisy neighbor" problem |
| Centralized backups & disaster recovery | Complex upgrade coordination |
| Scalability on demand | Compliance audit complexity |

---

## 2.6 The "Noisy Neighbor" Problem

A defining challenge of multitenancy is that **one tenant's heavy workload can degrade performance for others**. If $T_a$ is consuming $R_a$ resources while $T_b$ needs $R_b$ resources, the **effective available resource** for $T_b$ becomes:

$$R_b^{effective} = R^{total} - \sum_{i=1, i \neq b}^{n} R_i^{used}$$

If $\sum R_i^{used}$ is high, $R_b^{effective}$ drops below the **service-level agreement (SLA)** threshold. Cloud providers counter this using:

- **Resource quotas** per tenant.
- **Container-level CPU/memory limits** (e.g., Kubernetes `requests` and `limits`).
- **Quality of Service (QoS)** scheduling classes.
- **Auto-scaling triggers** based on aggregate load.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Multitenant Resource Allocation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides **exhaustive derivations and worked examples** that KTU students can reproduce in 14-mark answers. No steps are skipped.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example — Cost Savings Calculation

**Problem:** A cloud provider wants to migrate **10 enterprise clients** from single-tenant deployments to a single multi-tenant platform.

**Given Data:**

- Cost of single-tenant server per client: $\$\,500$/month
- Operational overhead per single-tenant deployment: $\$\,200$/month
- Cost of single multi-tenant platform serving all 10: $\$\,3000$/month
- Operational overhead for multi-tenant platform: $\$\,800$/month

**Step 1 — Calculate total single-tenant cost.**

$$C_{single} = N_T \times (C_{server} + C_{ops})$$

$$C_{single} = 10 \times (500 + 200)$$

$$C_{single} = 10 \times 700 = \$\,7000 \text{ / month}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate total multi-tenant cost.**

$$C_{multi} = C_{platform} + C_{ops-multi}$$

$$C_{multi} = 3000 + 800 = \$\,3800 \text{ / month}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate percentage savings.**

$$S = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_{multi}}{C_{single}}\right) \times 100\%$$

$$S = \left(1 - \dfrac{3800}{7000}\right) \times 100\%$$

$$S = (1 - 0.5429) \times 100\%$$

$$S = 45.71\%$$

**Step 4 — Cost per tenant in multi-tenant model.**

$$C_T = \dfrac{C_{multi}}{N_T} = \dfrac{3800}{10} = \$\,380 \text{ / tenant / month}$$

> **Conclusion:** The cloud provider saves **45.71%** monthly, and each tenant's effective cost drops from $\$700$ to $\$380$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Tenant Density and Utilization

**Problem:** A multi-tenant SaaS platform runs on **5 application servers**. Each server has **32 GB RAM**. Tenants are provisioned **2 GB RAM** each on average.

**Step 1 — Compute theoretical maximum tenant density per server.**

$$D_{max} = \dfrac{R_{server}}{R_{tenant}} = \dfrac{32 \, \text{GB}}{2 \, \text{GB}} = 16 \, \text{tenants / server}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total platform tenant density.**

$$D_{total} = N_S \times D_{max} = 5 \times 16 = 80 \, \text{tenants}$$

**Step 3 — Compute utilization if currently serving 60 tenants with average 1.8 GB used.**

$$U = \dfrac{\sum R_i^{used}}{R^{total}} \times 100\%$$

$$U = \dfrac{60 \times 1.8}{5 \times 32} \times 100\%$$

$$U = \dfrac{108}{160} \times 100\%$$

$$U = 67.5\%$$

**Step 4 — Headroom for new tenants.**

$$N_{headroom} = D_{total} - N_{current} = 80 - 60 = 20 \text{ tenants}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Tenant Isolation Levels

> The matrix below is **directly answerable** as a 14-mark KTU question. Each cell must be supported with examples.

| Isolation Level | DB Strategy | Application Strategy | Security Strength | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **L0 — Shared Everything** | Shared schema with `tenant_id` column | Single shared app instance | Low | Public SaaS (low-sensitivity) |
| **L1 — Shared Schema, Hashed Partitions** | Shared schema + per-tenant hash | Single shared app instance | Low-Medium | Mid-tier SaaS |
| **L2 — Separate Schema** | One schema per tenant in shared DB | Single shared app instance | Medium | Regulated industries |
| **L3 — Separate Database** | One DB per tenant, shared server | Single shared app instance | High | Healthcare, Finance |
| **L4 — Separate VM** | One DB per tenant in separate VM | Per-tenant app instance | Very High | Government, Defense |
| **L5 — Dedicated Hardware** | Tenant-owned hardware | Tenant-owned software stack | Maximum | Private cloud / Hybrid |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step — Designing a Tenant-Aware Application

**Step 1:** Tenant identity is established at login via **OAuth tokens, SAML assertions, or subdomain mapping**.

**Step 2:** The application extracts the **`tenant_id`** from the token and attaches it to every database query.

**Step 3:** A **metadata repository** (often a key-value store like Redis) is consulted to apply tenant-specific:
- Branding (logos, colors)
- Feature flags (which modules are enabled)
- Workflow rules
- Localization (language, timezone)

**Step 4:** All database queries include a **`WHERE tenant_id = ?`** clause — this is the **fundamental isolation primitive**.

**Step 5:** Tenant-specific encryption keys are loaded from a **Key Management Service (KMS)** to encrypt/decrypt data at rest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Step 4 is non-negotiable:** A single missing `WHERE tenant_id = ?` clause causes a **cross-tenant data leak** — the most catastrophic failure mode of multi-tenant systems.

---

## 3.5 Mathematical Model — Tenant Fairness (Jain's Index Applied)

For $n$ tenants, the **Jain's Fairness Index** quantifies equitable resource distribution:

$$F = \dfrac{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i\right)^2}{n \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i^2}$$

- $F = 1$ → perfectly fair allocation.
- $F \to \dfrac{1}{n}$ → one tenant monopolizes resources.

**Example:** Three tenants receive **$R_1 = 4$, $R_2 = 4$, $R_3 = 4$** units.

$$F = \dfrac{(4+4+4)^2}{3 \times (16+16+16)} = \dfrac{144}{144} = 1.0$$

**Example:** Three tenants receive **$R_1 = 12$, $R_2 = 1$, $R_3 = 1$** units.

$$F = \dfrac{(12+1+1)^2}{3 \times (144+1+1)} = \dfrac{196}{438} \approx 0.447$$

> The closer $F$ is to **1**, the more equitable the multi-tenant resource distribution.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics — Multitenant Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following diagrams use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** and **clean uppercase text labels** to comply with Mermaid rendering safeguards.

---

## 4.1 High-Level Multitenant Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph clientLayer[CLIENT LAYER]
        T1[TENANT A BROWSER]
        T2[TENANT B BROWSER]
        T3[TENANT C BROWSER]
    end

    subgraph edgeLayer[EDGE LAYER]

        WAF[WEB APPLICATION FIREWALL]
    end

    subgraph appLayer[APPLICATION LAYER]

        APP2[APP INSTANCE 2]

    end

    subgraph dataLayer[DATA LAYER]
        DB[(SHARED DATABASE)]
        SCH1[SCHEMA TENANT A]
        SCH2[SCHEMA TENANT B]
        SCH3[SCHEMA TENANT C]
        KMS[KEY MANAGEMENT SERVICE]
    end

    T1 --> LB
    T2 --> LB
    T3 --> LB
    LB --> WAF
    WAF --> APP1
    WAF --> APP2

    APP2 --> MD
    APP1 --> DB
    APP2 --> DB

    DB --> SCH3

    APP2 --> KMS
```

---

## 4.2 Tenant Onboarding Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[TENANT REGISTRATION] --> B[VALIDATE IDENTITY]
    B --> C[CREATE TENANT METADATA]
    C --> D[ALLOCATE DATABASE SCHEMA]
    D --> E[GENERATE ENCRYPTION KEYS]
    E --> F[PROVISION RESOURCE QUOTAS]
    F --> G[ACTIVATE TENANT]
    G --> H[TENANT GOES LIVE]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff

    style E fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.3 Database Isolation Strategies Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph sharedDB[SHARED DATABASE INSTANCE]
        S0[STRATEGY 0 SHARED SCHEMA TENANT ID COLUMN]
        S1[STRATEGY 1 SHARED SCHEMA HASH PARTITION]
        S2[STRATEGY 2 SEPARATE SCHEMA PER TENANT]
        S3[STRATEGY 3 SEPARATE DATABASE PER TENANT]
        S4[STRATEGY 4 SEPARATE VM PER TENANT]
    end

    S0 -->|LOWER COST| S1
    S1 -->|HIGHER ISOLATION| S2
    S2 -->|HIGHER ISOLATION| S3
    S3 -->|HIGHER ISOLATION| S4

    style S0 fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff
    style S4 fill:#8c564b,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.4 Noisy Neighbor Mitigation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[TENANT REQUEST ARRIVES] --> B[CHECK TENANT QUOTA]
    B --> C{QUOTA EXCEEDED}
    C -->|YES| D[THROTTLE OR REJECT]
    C -->|NO| E[DISPATCH TO APP INSTANCE]
    E --> F[MONITOR RESOURCE USE]
    F --> G{USE WITHIN SLA}
    G -->|YES| H[SERVE RESPONSE]
    G -->|NO| I[TRIGGER AUTO SCALE]
    I --> E

    style C fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.5 Multi-Tenant Request Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as TENANT USER
    participant L as LOAD BALANCER
    participant A as APP INSTANCE
    participant M as METADATA STORE
    participant D as DATABASE
    participant K as KMS

    U->>L: HTTPS REQUEST WITH TENANT TOKEN
    L->>A: FORWARD TO APP INSTANCE
    A->>M: FETCH TENANT CONFIGURATION
    M-->>A: RETURN BRANDING FEATURE FLAGS
    A->>K: REQUEST TENANT ENCRYPTION KEY
    K-->>A: RETURN DECRYPTED KEY HANDLE
    A->>D: SQL QUERY WITH TENANT ID FILTER
    D-->>A: RETURN TENANT SCOPED DATA
    A-->>U: RENDERED TENANT SPECIFIC RESPONSE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Multitenancy. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Multitenancy is a software architecture in which a **single instance of an application** serves **multiple tenants (customer organizations)** simultaneously. Tenants share the underlying infrastructure, middleware, and application code, but their **data, configuration, and presentation** are logically isolated. Each tenant experiences the system as if it were exclusively their own. (3 marks)

### Question 2: List any three advantages of multitenancy. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Reduced infrastructure cost** due to resource sharing across tenants.
2. **Centralized maintenance** — single code update benefits all tenants.
3. **High resource utilization** and on-demand scalability.
(3 marks)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, Module 3 long-answer questions carry an **internal choice**. Below are **two alternative 14-mark questions** with sub-parts and complete model solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different **database isolation strategies** used in multitenant architectures with examples.

**Model Answer:**

In multitenant systems, tenant data can be isolated using the following strategies, ordered from lowest to highest isolation:

1. **Shared Schema with Tenant ID Column (L0):**
   A single database, single schema, single set of tables. Each row carries a `tenant_id` column. Every query includes `WHERE tenant_id = ?`. Example: early-stage SaaS CRMs. Cheapest but riskiest.

2. **Shared Schema with Hash Partitioning (L1):**
   Same as L0 but data is partitioned by hashing `tenant_id`. Improves performance but isolation logic still relies on `tenant_id` filtering.

3. **Separate Schema per Tenant (L2):**
   Each tenant has its own schema (set of tables) within a shared database instance. Application connects to the correct schema at runtime. Example: SAP Business ByDesign.

4. **Separate Database per Tenant (L3):**
   Each tenant has its own logical database on a shared DBMS server. Strong isolation; suited for regulated industries.

5. **Separate VM or Server per Tenant (L4):**
   Tenant gets an isolated virtual machine or physical hardware. Maximum isolation; minimal cost efficiency.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **"noisy neighbor" problem** in cloud multitenancy and propose **three mitigation techniques**.

**Model Answer:**

The **noisy neighbor problem** occurs when one tenant's workload consumes disproportionate resources, degrading performance for other tenants sharing the same infrastructure.

**Three Mitigation Techniques:**

1. **Resource Quotas and Limits:**
   Hard caps are enforced per tenant on CPU, memory, disk I/O, and network bandwidth. Kubernetes implements this via `ResourceQuota` and `LimitRange` objects.

2. **Quality of Service (QoS) Scheduling:**
   Tenants are assigned QoS classes (e.g., Guaranteed, Burstable, BestEffort) and the scheduler prioritizes traffic accordingly.

3. **Auto-Scaling and Load Monitoring:**
   Continuous telemetry monitors per-tenant resource use. When aggregate load exceeds thresholds, new application instances are spun up to redistribute load.

**Additional techniques:** Traffic shaping, container isolation (cgroups/namespaces), and per-tenant SLAs with penalties.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the **architecture of a multitenant SaaS application**.

**Model Answer:**

A multitenant SaaS application consists of the following layers:

- **Client Layer:** Tenants access the system via browsers or APIs. Each tenant may use a unique subdomain (`tenantA.saas.com`) or custom domain.
- **Edge Layer:** Load balancer distributes requests across multiple application servers. A Web Application Firewall (WAF) filters malicious traffic.
- **Application Layer:** A pool of stateless application instances serves all tenants. A metadata repository stores per-tenant configuration (branding, features).
- **Data Layer:** A shared database (or set of databases) stores tenant data with isolation strategies as described above.
- **Security Layer:** A KMS manages per-tenant encryption keys; an Identity Provider (IdP) issues tenant-scoped authentication tokens.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **multi-tenancy** with **multi-instance architecture** in terms of cost, isolation, customization, and maintenance.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Multi-Tenant | Multi-Instance |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Low — shared infrastructure | High — separate infrastructure per tenant |
| **Isolation** | Logical (DB-level) | Physical (separate VMs/servers) |
| **Customization** | Limited (per-tenant config) | Extensive (per-tenant code) |
| **Maintenance** | Single update deploys to all | Per-tenant updates required |
| **Resource Utilization** | High (>70%) | Low (5–15%) |
| **Onboarding Speed** | Fast (minutes) | Slow (days–weeks) |
| **Best Suited For** | SaaS at scale | Highly regulated enterprises |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing multi-user with multi-tenant** — multi-user is within one organization; multi-tenant is across organizations.
> 2. **Skipping the diagram** — for 14-mark questions, a labeled diagram is worth **at least 3 marks**. Always include one.
> 3. **Ignoring trade-offs** — never present multitenancy as purely beneficial. Always discuss the noisy-neighbor problem and isolation complexity.
> 4. **Forgetting formulas** — 14-mark questions often include a numerical sub-part. Memorize cost, density, and utilization formulas.
> 5. **Generic answers** — use the KTU terms: *tenant, tenant_id, metadata repository, isolation, noisy neighbor, SaaS*.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam Day**

- **Definition:** Multitenancy = **one application instance** serving **multiple tenants** with **logical isolation**.
- **Core Layers:** Infrastructure (VMs) → Application (shared instance) → Database (shared schema or isolated).
- **Five Isolation Strategies:** Shared Schema → Separate Schema → Separate DB → Separate VM → Separate Hardware.
- **Noisy Neighbor:** One tenant's heavy load degrades others; mitigated via **quotas, QoS, auto-scaling**.
- **Key Formulas:**
  - Cost Savings: $S = (1 - C_{multi}/C_{single}) \times 100\%$
  - Tenant Density: $D = N_T / N_S$
  - Utilization: $U = (\sum R_i^{used} / R^{total}) \times 100\%$
  - Fairness: Jain's Index $F = (\sum R_i)^2 / (n \sum R_i^2)$
- **Real-World Examples:** Salesforce, Microsoft 365, Shopify, SAP Cloud, Google Workspace.
- **Advantages:** Cost, scalability, maintenance, utilization, fast onboarding.
- **Disadvantages:** Isolation complexity, security risks, noisy neighbor, limited deep customization.
- **Tenant Awareness:** Every query must include `WHERE tenant_id = ?` — the **fundamental isolation primitive**.
- **SaaS Tie-In:** Multitenancy is the **architectural foundation** of SaaS economics.
- **Examiner's Mantra:** Always present a **diagram + table + formulas + example** in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Service Technology. Resource Provisioning Techniques - Static and Dynamic Provisioning. Open-Source Software Platforms for Private Cloud - OpenStack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Service Technology. Resource Provisioning Techniques - Static and Dynamic Provisioning. Open-Source Software Platforms for Private Cloud - OpenStack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Service Technology in Cloud Computing

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> **Service Technology** refers to the foundational set of protocols, architectural styles, virtualization mechanisms, and management frameworks that enable the on-demand delivery, discovery, composition, and orchestration of networked, loosely-coupled computing services over the Internet. It is the engineering substrate that turns raw infrastructure into billable, SLA-backed cloud offerings.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, *Service Technology* is treated as the **umbrella discipline** that covers three intertwined layers:
1. **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)** — design philosophy for modular services.
2. **Web Services & APIs** — SOAP, REST, gRPC, GraphQL contract languages.
3. **Virtualization & Containerization** — the runtime layer that abstracts physical resources into isolatable execution environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Question stems often begin with "Discuss the role of service technology in cloud delivery." You must explicitly mention the **three service models** (SaaS, PaaS, IaaS) and link each to a corresponding service technology (browser/HTTP, containers & PaaS runtimes, hypervisors & OpenStack).

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cloud Restaurant"

Imagine a large cloud kitchen serving thousands of customers every minute.
- The **menu (service catalog)** is what customers see — that's the *Service Interface* (REST API / WSDL).
- The **kitchen equipment (hypervisors, containers)** is the *Service Runtime* — it actually prepares the dish.
- The **supply-chain manager** who decides how many burners to light at lunchtime is the *Service Provisioning Engine*.
- The **waiter who takes orders and brings food** is the *Service Broker / Orchestrator*.

**Service Technology** is the *entire restaurant operating system* — recipes, kitchen layout, supply chain, and ordering app — all working together so that any customer can get any dish, on demand, at the right quality.

---

## 1.2 Resource Provisioning — The Heartbeat of the Cloud

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Glossary)**
> **Resource Provisioning** is the process of selecting, allocating, configuring, and releasing cloud resources (CPU, RAM, storage, network) to workloads in accordance with defined Service Level Agreements (SLAs) and Quality of Service (QoS) targets.

Provisioning answers the question: *"How many VMs, how much storage, and how much network bandwidth should be made available to this application right now?"*

### 1.3 Static vs Dynamic Provisioning — The Analogy

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Cloud Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Provisioning** | A bus that always leaves at 7 AM with 50 seats, no matter if 5 or 50 people show up. | Fixed pool of VMs reserved at deploy-time based on peak forecast. |
| **Dynamic Provisioning** | Uber Pool — cars are dispatched only when a rider requests a ride, scaling in real time. | Auto-scaling groups that launch/destroy VMs in response to live load metrics. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Static provisioning is also called **capacity planning based provisioning** or **peak-load provisioning**, while dynamic provisioning is also known as **elastic provisioning** or **auto-scaling provisioning**. Use all three synonyms in your answer for full marks.
