```mermaid
    N->>N: Fetch Image into Local Cache
    N->>N: libvirt Define and Start Domain
    N->>S: Attach iSCSI Block Volume if Requested
    N-->>K: Instance Running
    K-->>C: Instance Running
    C-->>U: Return Instance ID and State
    N->>V: Guest OS Boots
    V-->>U: SSH or Console Access
```

### 4.3 Internal Layout of a Single Eucalyptus Cluster

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClusterOne["CLUSTER ONE - One Subnet or VLAN"]
        direction TB
        CCnode["CLUSTER CONTROLLER CC"]
        NCa["NODE CONTROLLER A"]
        NCb["NODE CONTROLLER B"]
        NCc["NODE CONTROLLER C"]
        Bridge["Linux Bridge eucabr0"]
        CCnode --- NCa
        CCnode --- NCb
        CCnode --- NCc
        NCa --- Bridge
        NCb --- Bridge
        NCc --- Bridge
    end

    CLCglob["CLOUD CONTROLLER CLC"] -->|SOAP RPC| CCnode
    Walrusglob["WALRUS"] -->|Image Download| NCa
    Walrusglob -->|Image Download| NCb
    Walrusglob -->|Image Download| NCc
    SCglob["STORAGE CONTROLLER SC"] -->|iSCSI LUN| NCa
    SCglob -->|iSCSI LUN| NCb
    SCglob -->|iSCSI LUN| NCc
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme** assessment pattern. Marks shown in **[brackets]** match the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) weightage. **Internal-choice** is provided for Part B as mandated by KTU 2024 Module-level choice rules.

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q1.** *What is Eucalyptus? Mention any two key design features.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Eucalyptus stands for *Elastic Utility Computing Architecture for Linking Your Programs To Useful Systems*. It is an **open-source software framework** used to build private and hybrid IaaS clouds that are **API-compatible with Amazon Web Services** (EC2, S3, EBS).
- **Two key features (1 mark):** (i) AWS API compatibility — existing AWS tools and SDKs work unchanged. (ii) Modular, hierarchical, Service-Oriented Architecture with four tiers (CLC, CC, NC, Storage).

**Q2.** *Differentiate between Walrus and Storage Controller in Eucalyptus.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Walrus | Storage Controller (SC) |
|---|---|---|
| AWS analog | Amazon S3 (object) | Amazon EBS (block) |
| Access type | HTTP-based object access (files/blobs) | iSCSI block-level LUN |
| Stores | VM images, buckets, user objects | Persistent volumes attached to instances |
| Granularity | Whole objects | Fixed-size blocks/sectors |
| Use case | Image repository & user data | OS disks & data disks for VMs |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Provided.** Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A — [14 Marks]** *(Module 3 Choice Option 1)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **high-level architecture of Eucalyptus** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the **Cloud Controller (CLC)** in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (4 marks):** Eucalyptus follows a **four-tier, hierarchical, Service-Oriented Architecture**. From top to bottom:

1. **Cloud Controller (CLC):** Top-tier entry point; exposes EC2-compatible API; performs user authentication via WS-Security / X.509; maintains global state (users, keypairs, security groups, image registry); routes requests to the appropriate Cluster Controller.
2. **Cluster Controller (CC):** Manages a single cluster — a group of Node Controllers sharing a virtual LAN. Performs instance scheduling, manages the EucaNet virtual network (Linux bridging + ebtables), handles instance state transitions, and forwards block storage calls to the Storage Controller.
3. **Node Controller (NC):** Resides on each physical host. Controls the local hypervisor (KVM/Xen/ESXi) via libvirt; fetches VM images from Walrus; starts/stops/migrates VMs; reports resource state to its CC.
4. **Storage Subsystem:**
   - **Walrus** — S3-compatible object storage for VM images and user objects.
   - **Storage Controller (SC)** — EBS-compatible block storage that exports iSCSI LUNs to NCs.

(Insert block diagram equivalent to the Mermaid diagrams in Section 4.)

**Role of CLC in detail (3 marks):**
- Acts as the **single front-end** for the entire private cloud.
- Receives user requests over **HTTPS on port 8773**.
- **Verifies the X.509 certificate** attached to the SOAP WS-Security header.
- **Resolves image, keypair, and security-group identifiers** from global tables.
- **Forwards** the request to the chosen CC based on the availability-zone parameter.
- Does **not** directly manage VMs or virtual networks — it is purely an orchestration and routing layer.
- Optionally federates with other Eucalyptus clouds (Eucalyptus 3.x) for cloud-of-clouds.

**(b)** With a neat sequence diagram, describe the **step-by-step workflow of launching a VM instance** in Eucalyptus. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (numbered steps — 7 marks, 1 mark per key step):**

1. **User invocation:** The user issues `euca-run-instances -n 1 -k mykey -g default emi-ABC12345`. The CLI constructs a SOAP request signed with the user's X.509 private key.
2. **CLC receives and authenticates:** CLC validates the WS-Security signature; if valid, the user is authenticated.
3. **CLC selects a CC:** Based on the requested availability zone, CLC chooses a Cluster Controller using its internal cluster map.
4. **CLC forwards request to CC:** The `RunInstances` SOAP call is dispatched to the selected CC.
5. **CC resolves the image via Walrus:** The CC queries Walrus for the EMI manifest — retrieving kernel (EKI), ramdisk (ERI), and filesystem (EFI) URLs.
6. **CC schedules an NC:** The CC polls all NCs in the cluster for free RAM/CPU/disk; the NC with the highest score under the resource-availability constraint is chosen.
7. **CC sets up EucaNet:** A `tap` interface is created on the chosen NC, attached to the cluster bridge `eucabr0`, and assigned a private IP. Security-group rules are installed as `ebtables`/`iptables` entries.
8. **CC instructs the NC:** The CC calls the NC to fetch the image and launch the VM. The NC downloads/streams the image to its local cache.
9. **NC starts the VM:** Using `libvirt`, the NC defines and starts the guest domain. The guest OS boots.
10. **Optional EBS attach:** If EBS volumes were requested, the SC exports iSCSI LUNs; the NC's iSCSI initiator logs in and attaches the block device to the VM.
11. **State returns to user:** The instance state (*Running*) propagates: `NC → CC → CLC → User`. The user receives the `instance-id` and the private IP.

(Insert a sequence diagram equivalent to Section 4.2.)

---

#### **Question B — [14 Marks]** *(Module 3 Choice Option 2)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **Storage components in Eucalyptus — Walrus and Storage Controller (SC)**. How do they relate to their AWS counterparts? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Walrus (3 marks):**
- AWS analog: **Amazon S3**.
- A simple, S3-compatible **object storage service** that exposes RESTful HTTP-based **PUT, GET, DELETE, LIST** operations.
- Backed by a **flat-file storage layer** (typically LVM-backed volumes or a directory tree).
- Stores: **VM images** (kernel, ramdisk, filesystem), **user buckets** and **user objects** (backups, archives, application data).
- Uses a **bucket-key** model identical to S3.
- Access protocol: HTTP on port 8773 (Walrus service endpoint).
- Caches image data; supports concurrent reads for VM instance boot-storms.

**Storage Controller / SC (3 marks):**
- AWS analog: **Amazon EBS**.
- Provides **persistent, block-level storage** volumes that can be attached/detached from running VM instances.
- Implementation: Creates a **sparse file or LVM logical volume** on a designated storage host and exports it via **iSCSI** (or AoE in older versions) to the target NC.
- The NC, in turn, presents the LUN to the guest VM as a raw block device (e.g., `/dev/vdb`).
- Supports **create volume, delete volume, attach volume, detach volume, snapshot** operations — fully EBS-compatible at the API level.
- Persists across instance lifetime — volume state is independent of the VM.

**Comparison summary (1 mark):**
- Walrus = object (file) level; SC = block (sector) level.
- Walrus uses HTTP; SC uses iSCSI.
- Both are accessed via SOAP/API calls originated at the CC, which proxies them to the appropriate storage tier.

**(b)** Discuss the **security and communication model of Eucalyptus**. How are user authentication and inter-component trust established? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Inter-component communication (3 marks):**
- All controllers (CLC ↔ CC ↔ NC ↔ SC ↔ Walrus) communicate over **SOAP over HTTPS** (WS-* protocol stack).
- **WS-Security** is the primary mechanism for mutual authentication and message integrity.
- Each tier possesses an **X.509 certificate** signed by a common internal CA managed by the CLC.
- A typical internal request is wrapped in a SOAP envelope; the WS-Security header carries the certificate and a signed timestamp; the receiver verifies the certificate chain against its trust store.
- This design mirrors the early AWS internal architecture and ensures end-to-end authenticated, encrypted control-plane traffic.

**User authentication (2 marks):**
- The user generates or is issued a **key pair** (X.509 certificate + private key) using `euca-add-keypair` or admin tools.
- Every user request (e.g., `euca-describe-instances`) is **signed** with the private key.
- The **CLC** validates the signature; it does *not* forward credentials to CC/NC — only an internal user identifier.
- This prevents credential leakage and supports role-based cloud administration.

**Federation & advanced features (2 marks):**
- Eucalyptus 3.x supports **cloud federation** — multiple CLCs in different trust domains can be linked, enabling workload portability.
- The trust between federated CLCs is also X.509-based.
- **Identity Management (IdM/Euca-IdM)** is an optional subsystem providing LDAP-backed user/group management with a SAML bridge for enterprise SSO.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on "Basics of Eucalyptus" Questions**
>
> 1. **Conflating components:** Writing "CLC runs the VMs" or "Walrus does block storage" — both are *factually wrong* and cost 1–2 marks. CLC only orchestrates; NC executes. Walrus is *object* (S3), SC is *block* (EBS).
> 2. **Forgetting the AWS analogy:** The KTU rubric awards marks for explicitly stating the AWS analog. Always mention "Walrus = S3 compatible" and "SC = EBS compatible" alongside the native name.
> 3. **Skipping authentication details:** Any architecture question worth ≥7 marks *must* mention **WS-Security + X.509**. A purely functional description loses at least 2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong ports:** If you write port 80 or 8080 instead of **8773**, you lose marks in the question on default endpoints.
> 5. **No diagram → direct mark loss:** Part (a) of a 7-mark question is incomplete without a labeled architecture block. Hand-drawn is acceptable; neatness matters.
> 6. **Confusing "cluster" with "data center":** In Eucalyptus, a *cluster* is a network-level grouping of NCs sharing a CC and a subnet — *not* a physical data center.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Basics of Eucalyptus**

- **Full form:** *Elastic Utility Computing Architecture for Linking Your Programs To Useful Systems*.
- **Category:** Open-source IaaS platform for **private and hybrid clouds**.
- **Historical context:** Originated at UCSB (2008); commercialized by Eucalyptus Systems Inc.; now maintained under open governance.
- **Core value proposition:** **AWS API compatibility** on-premise.
- **Architecture style:** **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)**, hierarchical, four-tier.
- **Four tiers (top to bottom):**
  1. **CLC — Cloud Controller:** Authentication, global state, routing.
  2. **CC — Cluster Controller:** Scheduling, EucaNet virtual network, instance state.
  3. **NC — Node Controller:** Hypervisor control, VM lifecycle, local image cache.
  4. **Storage:** **Walrus (S3 object)** + **SC (EBS block)**.
- **Communication:** SOAP over HTTPS with **WS-Security** + X.509 certificates.
- **Default service port:** **TCP 8773** (CLC, Walrus, SC endpoints).
- **Hypervisors supported:** **KVM, Xen, VMware ESXi** (via libvirt on Linux hosts).
- **Networking mode:** **EucaNet** (Linux bridge + ebtables; can operate in Managed/NOVLAN modes).
- **Authentication method:** **X.509 certificate** signed by user's private key.
- **AWS analogs (must memorize):**
  - EMI → AMI
  - Walrus → S3
  - Storage Controller → EBS
  - EucaNet → EC2-Classic / VPC
  - Security Group → Security Group
  - Euca2ools → AWS CLI
  - EKI / ERI / EFI → AKI / ARI / AFI
- **License:** Open-source — BSD-style / Eucalyptus Public License (EPL).
- **Modern legacy:** Concepts live on in **OpenStack, AWS Outposts, Azure Stack, Google Anthos**.
- **One-line exam answer:** *"Eucalyptus is an open-source, modular, AWS-API-compatible cloud platform that provides EC2, S3, and EBS services in a private data center using a hierarchical SOA of CLC, CC, NC, and storage tiers."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OpenNebula
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** OpenNebula

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**OpenNebula** is an open-source, industry-grade **cloud computing orchestration platform** (toolkit) that enables the dynamic deployment, management, and elasticity of virtualized services (VMs, containers, and bare-metal workloads) across heterogeneous, distributed data center infrastructures. It functions as a comprehensive **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** manager that abstracts underlying physical resources — compute, storage, and networking — into a unified, programmable cloud fabric.

In the exact terminology of the KTU 2024 syllabus for *PEERT524 – Cloud Computing (Module 3)*, OpenNebula is positioned as a **lightweight, flexible, and interoperable alternative to OpenStack**, designed for building **private, public, and hybrid cloud environments** with strong support for multi-tenancy, federation, and edge/cloud integration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** OpenNebula belongs to the *Cloud Management Platforms / Open-Source Cloud Stacks* unit. Examiner focus is typically on (a) Architecture, (b) Components, (c) VM lifecycle, (d) Comparison with OpenStack/Eucalyptus.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Manager" Intuition

Imagine a **large luxury hotel chain** with multiple branches (data centers) spread across cities:

- **The Hotel Manager's Desk (Front-end)** receives booking requests from guests.
- The manager does **not own the buildings**; he coordinates **rooms across branches** (hosts) that he does not directly operate.
- Every room has a **standardized key-card system** (hypervisor driver) so the manager can give the same "check-in experience" regardless of which branch the guest goes to.
- The manager maintains a **guest registry** (database / state store) of all current occupants, available rooms, and billing (accounting).
- For a special guest who needs rooms in **two different cities simultaneously**, the manager coordinates across branches seamlessly (**federation**).
- A **concierge web/app** (Sunstone GUI) lets the guest view rooms, request services, and check out — without ever talking to a human manager.

**OpenNebula plays the role of that Hotel Manager** — sitting above physical hardware, abstracting it, scheduling virtual "rooms" (VMs), and exposing them through clean interfaces to users.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Plain-English Meaning | KTU Context |
|------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Front-end** | The "brain" — runs the OpenNebula daemon (`oned`) | Hosts the cloud orchestration logic |
| **Host** | A physical server contributing CPU/RAM/Storage | Where VMs actually run |
| **Cluster** | A logical grouping of hosts sharing storage/network | Used for VM placement & HA |
| **Datastore** | Storage pool for VM disk images and running VM disks | System DS, Image DS, Files DS |
| **Virtual Network** | Software-defined L2/L3 overlay for VM connectivity | Bridges, OVS, VLAN, VXLAN |
| **Sunstone** | The web-based self-service GUI for users/admins | Primary user interface |
| **OneFlow** | Service orchestrator for multi-VM applications (PaaS-like) | Used for auto-scaling & elasticity |
| **Driver** | Plugin that talks to hypervisors/storage/network | KVM, VMware, Xen, LXC, Ceph |
| **Federation** | Multi-OpenNebula-instance master-slave sync | Hybrid / multi-cloud setups |
| **Multi-tenancy** | Isolated virtual data centers (VDCs) per group/org | Group-based quota & ACLs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics / Constants** often referenced in KTU answers for OpenNebula:
> - **Default Front-end Port:** `2633` (XML-RPC API), Sunstone GUI on `:9869`
> - **Recommended Host RAM:** ≥ **8 GB** for small clouds
> - **Architecture:** **Centralized** (vs OpenStack's distributed microservices)

## 1.4 Where OpenNebula Fits in the Cloud Stack

OpenNebula operates at **Layer 3 (Resource & Service Orchestration)** of a typical cloud architecture:

1. **Layer 1 — Physical Infrastructure:** Bare-metal servers, switches, SAN/NAS.
2. **Layer 2 — Virtualization Layer:** KVM, VMware ESXi, LXC.
3. **Layer 3 — Cloud Orchestration (OpenNebula lives here):** VM lifecycle, scheduling, networking, storage, multi-tenancy.
4. **Layer 4 — Service Layer:** Self-service portals (Sunstone), APIs, OneFlow, marketplaces.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of OpenNebula Architecture

OpenNebula's design rests on **five operational pillars**, which examiners love to question:

### Pillar 1 — Centralized Orchestration Daemon (`oned`)
- Written in **C/C++**, monolithic by design (unlike OpenStack's micro-services).
- Maintains the **authoritative state** of the cloud (hosts, VMs, networks, images, users).
- Communicates with hosts via **SSH push** or drivers.
- Exposes **XML-RPC API** (port 2633) for programmatic control.

### Pillar 2 — Hosts (Compute Plane)
- Plain Linux servers running `libvirt` (KVM) or another supported hypervisor.
- **No agent** is permanently required — commands are pushed via SSH and **information drivers** pull state.
- Hosts are grouped into **Clusters** for shared policies (e.g., shared datastore, scheduler affinity).

### Pillar 3 — Storage Datastores (Three Logical Types)

| Datastore Type | Purpose | Examples |
|----------------|---------|----------|
| **System Datastore** | Holds running VM disk files (transfer/cache) | Local FS, NFS, LVM, Ceph |
| **Image Datastore** | Repository of registered VM templates & disk images | NFS, Ceph, GlusterFS, S3 |
| **Files Datastore** | Generic file storage for users (per VDC) | NFS, CephFS |

### Pillar 4 — Virtual Networking
- Provides **software-defined networking** decoupled from physical NICs.
- Supported drivers: **802.1Q VLAN, Open vSwitch (OVS), VXLAN, Linux Bridge, eBPF**.
- Each VM NIC is attached to a **Virtual Network** with its own address range, gateway, and DNS.

### Pillar 5 — Multi-Tenancy & Access Control
- Implemented via **Groups, VDCs (Virtual Data Centers), ACLs, and Quotas**.
- A single OpenNebula instance can host **multiple isolated organizational clouds** in parallel.

## 2.2 The OpenNebula VM Lifecycle (Logical Steps)

This is a **high-frequency KTU question**. Memorize these stages:

1. **Template Definition:** Admin/user creates a *VM Template* (CPU, RAM, OS image, network).
2. **Instantiation:** Template is instantiated → a `pending` VM object is created.
3. **Scheduling:** `oned` uses the **Scheduler (Matchmaking)** to select the best host using *requirements* and *rank* expressions (e.g., `FREE_CPU > 0.5`).
4. **Image Preparation:** VM disk is moved from *Image Datastore* to the chosen host's *System Datastore*.
5. **Driver Deployment:** The **hypervisor driver** (KVM, etc.) creates and boots the VM.
6. **Running State:** VM transitions to `running`, network is attached via the *Virtual Network driver*.
7. **Operations:** Live-migration, stop, resume, terminate, snapshot — all flow back through `oned`.
8. **Termination:** Disk is optionally saved back to the Image Datastore as a new persistent image.

## 2.3 Core Subsystems (Module-by-Module Breakdown)

| Subsystem | Function | Key File/Path |
|-----------|----------|----------------|
| `oned` | Main daemon | `/usr/sbin/oned` |
| `mm_sched` | Match-making scheduler | Runs every 15s by default |
| `sunstone-server` | Web GUI | `:9869` |
| `novnc-proxy` | Browser VNC console | `:29876` |
| `oneflow-server` | Service orchestration | `:2474` |
| `onegate-server` | VM contextualization helper | `:5030` |
| `onedb` | Database administration tool | SQLite/MySQL backend |
| `oneuser`, `onehost`, `onevm`, `onevnet` | CLI commands | `one*` family |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although OpenNebula is more *architectural* than mathematical, KTU 2024 expects you to know **scheduling expressions and capacity-planning equations**.

### 2.4.1 Matchmaking Expressions

| Expression | Purpose | Example |
|------------|---------|---------|
| `REQUIREMENTS` | Boolean hard-filter for eligible hosts | `HYPERVISOR="kvm" && FREE_CPU > 2` |
| `RANK` | Scoring expression to pick the *best* host | `FREE_CPU` (ascending = pack) or `-FREE_CPU` (spread) |

### 2.4.2 Capacity Planning Formulas

$$
\text{Total vCPUs Available} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{Physical Cores}_i \times \text{Cores per Core Ratio}
$$

$$
\text{Effective Host Capacity (CPU)} = \text{Physical Cores} - \text{Overhead}_\text{host}
$$

$$
\text{VLAN ID Range} = [2, 4094] \quad \text{(802.1Q standard)}
$$

$$
\text{Overcommit Ratio} = \frac{\text{Allocated vCPUs}}{\text{Physical Cores}}
$$

> **Typical OpenNebula default overcommit ratio = 1:1 to 2:1**, configurable per cluster.

### 2.4.3 Quota Equation (per Group / User)

$$
Q_{\text{remaining}}^{\text{resource}} = Q_{\text{total}}^{\text{resource}} - Q_{\text{used}}^{\text{resource}}
$$

where `resource ∈ { VM, CPU, MEMORY, VNET, IMAGE, DATASTORE }`.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

OpenNebula is **not a research toy** — it runs production clouds at:

- **European XFEL** (Hamburg, Germany) — physics research, **petabyte-scale** data processing.
- **China Mobile** — telecom-scale private cloud.
- **BME (Budapest University)** — academic hybrid cloud for researchers.
- **Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory** — high-energy physics workloads.

Its **lightweight, single-binary architecture** is preferred over OpenStack where the organization needs **low operational overhead**, **edge-cloud federation**, or **air-gapped private deployments**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section walks through the **logical derivations** of OpenNebula's scheduling decisions, capacity equations, and component interactions — all written step-by-step as KTU board examiners expect.

## 3.1 Derivation: Host Selection via Matchmaking

When a VM is instantiated, OpenNebula's scheduler evaluates **every host in the cluster** using a two-phase algorithm.

### Step 1 — Apply REQUIREMENTS Filter

$$
\text{EligibleHostSet} = \left\{ H_i \,\middle|\, \text{eval}\!\left(\text{REQ}_i\right) = \text{true} \right\}
$$

Any host whose `REQUIREMENTS` expression evaluates to **false** is excluded from consideration (e.g., insufficient `FREE_CPU`, wrong `HYPERVISOR`, or maintenance mode).

### Step 2 — Apply RANK Scoring

For each surviving host, OpenNebula computes a **numeric rank**:

$$
\text{Rank}(H_i) = \text{eval}\!\left(\text{RANK}_i\right)
$$

A **lower rank** wins by default. So:

- `RANK = FREE_CPU` → VM packs into host with *least* free CPU.
- `RANK = -FREE_CPU` → VM spreads onto host with *most* free CPU.

### Step 3 — Tie-Break by ID

If two hosts have equal rank, OpenNebula picks the one with the **lowest host ID** (deterministic behavior for reproducibility in tests).

### Worked Numerical Example

> Suppose we have **3 hosts** and a VM requires `FREE_CPU > 1`:

| Host ID | FREE_CPU | REQ `(FREE_CPU > 1)` | RANK `(FREE_CPU)` | Decision |
|---------|----------|----------------------|-------------------|----------|
| H1 | 4 | ✅ true | 4 | Eligible |
| H2 | 2 | ✅ true | 2 | ✅ **Chosen** (lowest rank) |
| H3 | 6 | ✅ true | 6 | Eligible |

$$
\text{Chosen Host} = \arg\min_{H_i \in \text{Eligible}} \text{Rank}(H_i) = H2
$$

**Engineering insight:** Setting `RANK = -FREE_CPU` would have chosen H3 instead — useful for **load balancing**.

## 3.2 Derivation: VM Capacity Aggregation for a Cluster

For a cluster with $N$ homogeneous hosts, each having $C$ cores and $M$ GB RAM, the **total addressable cloud capacity** is:

$$
C_{\text{total}}^{\text{CPU}} = N \cdot C \cdot \rho_{\text{cpu}}
$$

where $\rho_{\text{cpu}}$ is the **overcommit ratio** (default = 1).

$$
C_{\text{total}}^{\text{RAM}} = N \cdot M - N \cdot M_{\text{host overhead}}
$$

$$
\text{Headroom Fraction} = 1 - \frac{\sum \text{Allocated}}{\text{Total}}
$$

**Example:** A 5-host cluster, each with 16 cores, 64 GB RAM, 2 GB host overhead, $\rho = 1$:

$$
C_{\text{CPU}} = 5 \cdot 16 \cdot 1 = 80 \text{ vCPUs}
$$

$$
C_{\text{RAM}} = 5 \cdot (64 - 2) = 310 \text{ GB}
$$

If 30 VMs each consume 1 vCPU and 4 GB RAM, the **remaining headroom** is:

$$
\text{Used vCPUs} = 30, \quad \text{Free vCPUs} = 50
$$

$$
\text{Used RAM} = 120 \text{ GB}, \quad \text{Free RAM} = 190 \text{ GB}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: OpenNebula vs. OpenStack vs. Eucalyptus

This matrix is **exam gold** for KTU 2024 — usually asked as a 14-mark question.

| Feature | OpenNebula | OpenStack | Eucalyptus |
|---------|-----------|-----------|------------|
| **Architecture** | Centralized monolith | Distributed micro-services | Centralized |
| **Language** | C/C++, Ruby | Python | Java, C |
| **API Compatibility** | EC2, OCCI, vCloud | Native REST + EC2 bridge | AWS EC2 compatible |
| **Hypervisors** | KVM, VMware, Xen, LXC | KVM, QEMU, VMware, Hyper-V, Xen | KVM, VMware, Xen |
| **Scheduler** | Matchmaking (Haizea-based) | Nova Scheduler (filters+weighers) | Greedy |
| **GUI** | Sunstone | Horizon | Euca-Console |
| **Networking** | VLAN, OVS, VXLAN, eBPF | Neutron (plugins) | Edge networking |
| **Storage** | Ceph, NFS, LVM, GlusterFS | Cinder, Swift | Walrus (S3-like) |
| **Multi-tenancy** | Groups + VDCs + ACLs | Projects + Domains | Accounts |
| **Federation** | Built-in (Zone model) | Tricky / requires extras | Limited |
| **Footprint** | Lightweight (~1 server) | Heavy (many services) | Medium |
| **Learning Curve** | Low | Steep | Medium |
| **Best For** | Private/Hybrid/Edge | Large-scale public clouds | AWS-API compatible private clouds |

## 3.4 Derivation: Federation Quorum Logic

In **OpenNebula Federation**, one instance is the **Master** and others are **Slaves**. The Master maintains a **logical replica** of each slave's state.

A VM created in a slave zone is **discoverable** via the master but actually **executed** in the slave zone. Quota aggregation across the federation follows:

$$
Q_{\text{federated}}^{\text{user}} = \sum_{z=1}^{Z} Q_{\text{user}}^{(\text{zone}_z)}
$$

This enables **multi-region cloud bursting** — a key concept for hybrid cloud.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OpenNebula High-Level Architecture (Block Flow)

```mermaid

    subgraph CONTROL_LAYER["CONTROL PLANE oned"]

```

## 4.2 VM Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PENDING : onevm create
    PENDING --> HOLD : onevm hold
    HOLD --> PENDING : onevm release
    PENDING --> PROLOG : scheduler matches host
    PROLOG --> RUNNING : hypervisor driver boot
    RUNNING --> MIGRATE : onevm live-migrate
    MIGRATE --> RUNNING : migration success
    RUNNING --> POWEROFF : onevm poweroff
    POWEROFF --> RUNNING : onevm resume
    RUNNING --> SUSPENDED : onevm suspend
    SUSPENDED --> RUNNING : onevm resume
    RUNNING --> EPILOG : onevm terminate
    EPILOG --> DONE : cleanup complete
    DONE --> [*]
    PENDING --> FAILED : error in prolog
    RUNNING --> FAILED : hypervisor crash
```

## 4.3 Federation & Multi-Zone Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MASTER_ZONE["MASTER ZONE"]
        M_ONE["oned Master"]
        M_DB[("Federation DB")]
    end

    subgraph SLAVE_A["SLAVE ZONE A Edge Site"]
        S_ONE_A["oned Slave A"]
        HOSTS_A["Hosts Cluster A"]
    end

    subgraph SLAVE_B["SLAVE ZONE B Core DC"]
        S_ONE_B["oned Slave B"]
        HOSTS_B["Hosts Cluster B"]
    end

    USER["End User"] --> M_ONE
    M_ONE -->|XML-RPC sync| S_ONE_A
    M_ONE -->|XML-RPC sync| S_ONE_B
    S_ONE_A --> HOSTS_A
    S_ONE_B --> HOSTS_B
    M_DB --- M_ONE
```

## 4.4 OpenNebula Component Stack (Layered View)

```mermaid

    L1["Layer 5 Service Portal Sunstone + OneFlow"]
    L2["Layer 4 API XML-RPC + CLI"]
    L3["Layer 3 Orchestration oned + Scheduler"]
    L4["Layer 2 Drivers KVM + vCenter + Ceph + OVS"]
    L5["Layer 1 Infrastructure Linux Servers + Switches + SAN"]

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Boards:** When drawing architecture diagrams, always **label every block**, **number the layers**, and **show ports** (e.g., `:2633`, `:9869`). Examiners reward clarity.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Define OpenNebula. List any four salient features.**

**Model Answer:**
OpenNebula is an **open-source cloud orchestration platform** used to build and manage private, public, and hybrid IaaS clouds by abstracting heterogeneous physical resources (compute, storage, network) into virtualized services.

**Four salient features:**
1. **Lightweight centralized architecture** — single daemon `oned`, low operational overhead.
2. **Multi-tenancy** — supports Groups, VDCs, ACLs, and Quotas for isolated organizational clouds.
3. **Broad hypervisor support** — KVM, VMware vCenter, Xen, LXC.
4. **Built-in Federation** — multi-zone master-slave model for hybrid and edge clouds.

*(Plus: multi-cloud APIs — EC2, OCCI, vCloud; Sunstone GUI; OneFlow service orchestration.)* — **(3 Marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**What is the role of the Sunstone component in OpenNebula?**

**Model Answer:**
Sunstone is the **web-based self-service graphical user interface (GUI)** of OpenNebula, running on port **9869** by default. It provides:
- A dashboard for **users** to instantiate, manage, and monitor VMs.
- An **admin panel** for managing hosts, datastores, virtual networks, users, and groups.
- A **marketplace** for sharing VM templates and service templates.
- Integration with **VNC consoles** via noVNC proxy for in-browser VM access.

It serves as the **primary human-facing layer** of OpenNebula, abstracting the underlying XML-RPC API. — **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Always **draw the architecture diagram** — it carries 3–4 marks by itself.
> - When comparing with OpenStack, use a **table**, not prose.
> - Always mention **specific port numbers** (`:2633`, `:9869`, `:2474`).
> - Students lose marks by omitting **datastore types** — there are *three*, not two.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of OpenNebula** and describe the roles of its key components. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **VM lifecycle** in OpenNebula, listing all states from instantiation to termination. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Architecture of OpenNebula:**

OpenNebula follows a **layered, centralized architecture** consisting of:

1. **Front-end (Control Plane):**
   - Runs the **`oned` daemon** — the master orchestrator.
   - Houses the **Scheduler (`mm_sched`)** — performs match-making.
   - Maintains the **Database** (SQLite/MySQL) holding all cloud state.
   - Hosts **OneFlow** (service orchestration on port 2474) and **OneGate** (context broker on port 5030).

2. **Hosts (Compute Plane):**
   - Physical servers running supported hypervisors — primarily **KVM via libvirt**.
   - Stateless — driven by `oned` over **SSH** with information drivers returning capacity data.
   - Grouped into **Clusters** for shared storage and policy.

3. **Storage Subsystem:**
   - **Image Datastore** — persistent templates & disk images (NFS, Ceph).
   - **System Datastore** — transient running VM disks (Local FS, LVM, Ceph).
   - **Files Datastore** — user-uploaded files per VDC.

4. **Networking Subsystem:**
   - **Virtual Networks** with drivers: **Linux Bridge, OVS, VLAN (802.1Q), VXLAN**.

5. **Access & Interface Layer:**
   - **Sunstone GUI** (port 9869), **XML-RPC API** (port 2633), **CLI** (`onevm`, `onehost`, etc.).

**Diagram (refer Section 4.1 above).**

*(Examiner's tip: include ports and datastore types for full marks.)* — **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**VM Lifecycle States:**

| State | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| `PENDING` | Created; awaiting scheduling |
| `HOLD` | Pending kept on hold by user/admin |
| `PROLOG` | Image transferred to chosen host's System Datastore |
| `RUNNING` | Active on host; consuming CPU/RAM/network |
| `MIGRATE` | Live-migrating between hosts |
| `SAVE` / `SUSPEND` | State saved to disk; VM paused |
| `POWEROFF` | Graceful shutdown |
| `EPILOG` | Disk cleanup / save-back to Image Datastore |
| `DONE` | Terminated; metadata retained |
| `FAILED` | Any error during prolog/running/epilog |

**Flow:** `PENDING → (HOLD?) → PROLOG → RUNNING → {MIGRATE, SUSPEND, POWEROFF}* → (terminate) → EPILOG → DONE` (or `FAILED`).

(Refer state machine in Section 4.2.) — **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`**

**(a)** Compare **OpenNebula** and **OpenStack** with respect to **at least eight architectural and operational parameters**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **federation model** of OpenNebula. How does it support hybrid and edge-cloud deployments? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Comparison Table (OpenNebula vs OpenStack):**

| Parameter | OpenNebula | OpenStack |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Architecture | Centralized monolith (`oned`) | Distributed micro-services |
| Language | C/C++, Ruby | Python |
| Components | 1 main daemon + few services | 10+ cooperating services (Nova, Neutron, Cinder, …) |
| Footprint | Single server viable | Multi-node mandatory for production |
| Hypervisor Support | KVM, VMware, Xen, LXC | KVM, QEMU, VMware, Hyper-V |
| Networking | Built-in VLAN/OVS/VXLAN | Neutron with plugins |
| Storage | Three datastores (Image/System/Files) | Cinder (block) + Swift (object) |
| Federation | Built-in Master-Slave zones | External projects (Designate, Tricircle) |
| API Compatibility | EC2, OCCI, vCloud | Native REST + EC2 bridge |
| Learning Curve | Low | Steep |
| Ideal Use Case | Private, Hybrid, Edge | Large-scale public clouds |
| Operational Complexity | Low | High |

*(Any 8 well-explained points = 7 marks.)*

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Federation Model:**

OpenNebula's **Federation** enables multiple geographically distributed OpenNebula instances to be logically unified under a **single Master** zone while delegating actual VM execution to **Slave zones**.

**Components:**
- **Master Zone** — handles global user/group authentication, quotas, and resource visibility.
- **Slave Zones** — execute VMs locally; periodically **synchronize state** to the Master via XML-RPC.

**Working Mechanism:**

$$
\text{Global State} = \text{Master}_{\text{state}} \cup \bigcup_{z=1}^{Z} \text{Slave}_z^{\text{state}}
$$

When a user logs into the **Master**, they see a **unified catalog** of resources from all slaves. They can instantiate a VM; OpenNebula's scheduler decides whether to run it **locally** or in a slave zone based on:
- Resource availability in target zone.
- Network latency requirements.
- Affinity policies (e.g., "run in zone closest to user").

**Hybrid & Edge Cloud Support:**
- **Edge Computing:** Slave zones can be deployed at edge sites (telecom towers, factories); the Master sits in a central DC for unified control.
- **Cloud Bursting:** When local capacity is exhausted, VMs can spill over to a **public cloud** acting as a slave (AWS, Azure) via the **EC2 driver**.
- **Disaster Recovery:** Replicating state across zones enables hot-standby DR.

*(7 Marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- [ ] **OpenNebula = open-source IaaS orchestrator**, lightweight, centralized.
- [ ] **Main daemon:** `oned`; **GUI:** Sunstone on **port 9869**; **API:** XML-RPC on **port 2633**.
- [ ] **Three Datastore Types:** Image (templates), System (running disks), Files (user uploads).
- [ ] **Four Default Virtual Network Drivers:** Linux Bridge, OVS, 802.1Q VLAN, VXLAN.
- [ ] **Scheduler logic:** `REQUIREMENTS` (filter) + `RANK` (score); lower rank wins.
- [ ] **VM Lifecycle States:** `PENDING → PROLOG → RUNNING → (MIGRATE/SUSPEND/POWEROFF) → EPILOG → DONE/FAILED`.
- [ ] **Federation** = Master + multiple Slaves; Master holds *global metadata*, Slaves run *actual VMs*.
- [ ] **OneFlow** = multi-VM service orchestration (port 2474); supports auto-scaling and elasticity.
- [ ] **Multi-tenancy** via Groups, VDCs (Virtual Data Centers), ACLs, Quotas.
- [ ] **Architecture:** Centralized monolith (vs OpenStack's distributed microservices).
- [ ] **Use cases:** Private cloud, Hybrid cloud, Edge cloud, HPC, Research clouds.
- [ ] **Key difference from OpenStack:** fewer moving parts, easier to install, ideal for SMBs and edge.
- [ ] **Real-world users:** European XFEL, FermiLab, China Mobile, BME Hungary.
- [ ] **Always** draw a labeled architecture diagram in 14-mark questions.
- [ ] **Always** state specific port numbers — examiners reward precision.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nimbus. Cloud Programming - Parallel Computing and Programming Paradigms. MapReduce - Hadoop Library from Apache
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nimbus. Cloud Programming - Parallel Computing and Programming Paradigms. MapReduce - Hadoop Library from Apache

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Nimbus

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Nimbus** is an open-source **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** cloud computing toolkit developed by the University of Chicago, designed to provide scientific communities with customizable, scalable, and federated cloud computing resources. It serves as a reference implementation of the **WTF (Workspace Testbed Federation)** specification and supports the deployment of both **virtual machines (VMs)** and **virtual clusters** on bare-metal physical infrastructure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Hotel Chain with Franchises":**
> Think of Nimbus as a **hotel chain owner** who doesn't run just one hotel. Instead, the owner provides a **kit** (a toolkit) and a **brand standard** (workspace service interface) that lets many independent hoteliers (scientific labs, universities, and institutions) set up their own hotels (cloud sites) that all look and behave the same way to the guest (end-user scientist). When a scientist needs a room (a VM), they can book it at any "compatible hotel" anywhere in the world, and the room is delivered identically — same keys, same layout, same amenities. Nimbus is the "kit" that makes this federation possible.

**Key Highlights of Nimbus:**
- **Open Source** under the **Apache 2.0 License**
- Implements the **EC2-compatible API** (compatible with Amazon EC2)
- Supports **Xen** and **KVM** hypervisors
- Foundation for **Chimera** (virtual infrastructure description language)
- Works with **Nimbus-Context-Broker** for cloud federation
- Standard metric: **virtual workspace** (VM + storage + network bundles)

---

### 1.2 Cloud Programming

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Cloud Programming** is a programming paradigm in which computational, storage, and software resources are provisioned, accessed, and orchestrated as **elastic, on-demand services** over a network, freeing developers from concerns of underlying hardware management, capacity planning, and fault tolerance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Electricity on a Tap":**
> Before cloud programming, programmers had to be **electricians** (build power plants, lay cables, buy generators). With cloud programming, you just **flip a switch** and pay only for the electricity you used. You never worry about storms damaging the plant, or the plant running out of coal, or scaling it up for a festival. The **utility company** (the cloud provider) handles all of it.

**Core Cloud Programming Principles:**
- **Abstraction** of infrastructure
- **Elasticity** — resources scale horizontally and vertically
- **Pay-per-use** billing model
- **Programmatic access** via APIs, SDKs, and CLI
- **Multi-tenancy** and **resource pooling**

---

### 1.3 Parallel Computing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Parallel Computing** is a computation model in which multiple processing elements (cores, processors, computers) work simultaneously to solve a problem, dividing the work into discrete chunks that can be processed concurrently, coordinated via message passing or shared memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Team of Cashiers at a Supermarket":**
> Imagine a long queue of 100 customers at a single cashier (serial computing). Now imagine 10 cashiers each serving 10 customers in parallel. Same total work, **10× faster throughput**. Parallel computing is exactly that — dividing one big task into many small ones and giving each to a different "cashier" (processor). However, some tasks cannot be divided (e.g., the cashier must scan items in order), and these are inherently **serial**.

**Flynn's Taxonomy (Recap):**
- **SISD** — Single Instruction, Single Data (traditional sequential)
- **SIMD** — Single Instruction, Multiple Data (vector processing)
- **MISD** — Multiple Instruction, Single Data (rare; fault tolerant systems)
- **MIMD** — Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data (multiprocessors, clusters)

---

### 1.4 Programming Paradigms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Programming Paradigm** is a fundamental style or philosophy of computer programming that defines the structure, flow, and execution model of code. Common paradigms relevant to cloud programming include **imperative, functional, declarative, dataflow, message-passing, and event-driven** paradigms.

**Major Paradigms Table:**

| Paradigm | Core Idea | Cloud Example |
|----------|-----------|----------------|
| **Imperative** | Step-by-step state mutation | Writing VM provisioning scripts |
| **Functional** | Pure functions, no side effects | MapReduce, Apache Spark RDDs |
| **Declarative** | Describe *what*, not *how* | SQL on cloud data warehouses |
| **Message-Passing** | Processes communicate via messages | MPI, Akka, ZeroMQ |
| **Dataflow** | Computation flows over data streams | Apache Storm, AWS Lambda |
| **Event-Driven** | Triggered by event occurrences | Serverless, Pub/Sub systems |

---

### 1.5 MapReduce

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **MapReduce** is a programming model and an associated parallel, distributed implementation introduced by **Google (Dean & Ghemawat, 2004)** for processing and generating large datasets on commodity clusters. It consists of two user-defined functions — **Map** and **Reduce** — coordinated by a runtime framework (such as Hadoop) that handles data partitioning, task scheduling, fault tolerance, and inter-node communication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The National Census Count":**
> Imagine counting the population of an entire country. The **central authority** (Master Node) divides the country into 50 regions. Each **regional supervisor** (Mapper) walks around their region, writing on a sticky note every name they encounter and their state (e.g., "Ravi → Karnataka"). All mappers dump their sticky notes at a **central sorting office** (Shuffle & Sort phase) where notes for the same state are bundled together. Finally, **state-level counters** (Reducers) take each bundle and count how many people are from that state. The final report is "Karnataka: 6,00,00,000", "Tamil Nadu: 7,20,00,000", etc. **This is MapReduce.**

**Mathematical Model of MapReduce:**

$$
\text{Map}: (k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_2, v_2)
$$

$$
\text{Reduce}: (k_2, \text{list}(v_2)) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_3, v_3)
$$

---

### 1.6 Apache Hadoop

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Apache Hadoop** is an open-source framework that allows for the **distributed storage** and **distributed processing** of large datasets across clusters of commodity computers. It is the de-facto open-source implementation of the **MapReduce programming model** combined with the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)**. Hadoop is licensed under the **Apache License 2.0** and is hosted by the **Apache Software Foundation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Massive Library System":**
> A single librarian cannot manage 10 million books. Hadoop is like a **library chain**: 1000 branches (DataNodes), each holding a portion of the books (data blocks, default **128 MB** or **256 MB**). A **central catalog** (NameNode) tells you which branch has which book. When a researcher wants to count word frequencies across the entire library, the system **sends the researcher to the books** (data-local processing), not the books to the researcher — this is the genius of Hadoop's "move computation, not data" principle.

**Core Hadoop Modules:**
- **HDFS** — Distributed file system (storage)
- **MapReduce** — Distributed processing engine (compute)
- **YARN** — Resource negotiator and job scheduler
- **Common Utilities** — Libraries and utilities

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Nimbus Architecture — Deep Dive

Nimbus operates as a **workspace service**. A *workspace* is a bundle of resources: VM instances, block storage volumes, and network configuration, packaged together and provisioned atomically.

**Nimbus Components:**

1. **Workspace Service** — Core component, accepts EC2-style API calls.
2. **Workspace Control** — Performs on-node VM lifecycle (start, pause, resume, shutdown, destroy).
3. **Nimbus-Context-Broker** — Enables cloud federation across geographically distributed Nimbus sites.
4. **Chimera** — Declarative XML-based language for describing virtual infrastructure.
5. **Cloud Client** — User-facing command-line tool.

**Logical Architecture Flow:**

- **Client** → sends REST/SOAP API call → **Workspace Service** → schedules via **EC2 API + Nimbus extensions** → **Resource Manager** (queries Pool) → on available physical host, **Workspace Control** invokes **Xen/KVM hypervisor** → boots VM with **VMRC (VM Remote Control)** protocol → assigns from **Storage Pool** → reports back.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Nimbus is widely used in **scientific gateways** (astronomy, genomics, physics) where researchers need reproducible VMs as part of their experiment. It also powers the **Open Science Grid** and **FutureGrid**.

---

### 2.2 Parallel Computing — Theoretical Depth

**Parallelism vs. Concurrency:**
- **Parallelism** = doing many things *at the same instant* (multi-core, multi-CPU)
- **Concurrency** = dealing with many things *at once* (may interleave on one core)

**Speedup (Amdahl's Law):**

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}
$$

Where:
- $S(N)$ = Theoretical speedup with $N$ processors
- $P$ = Parallel fraction of the program (between 0 and 1)
- $1 - P$ = Serial fraction
- $N$ = Number of processors

**Limiting Speedup (N → ∞):**

$$
S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{1 - P}
$$

**Gustafson's Law (Scaled Speedup):**

$$
S(N) = N - (N - 1) \cdot S
$$

Where $S$ = serial fraction. This is more realistic for big data where **problem size scales with N**.

**Parallel Efficiency:**

$$
E(N) = \frac{S(N)}{N}
$$

**Granularity:**
- **Fine-grained** — very small tasks, high communication overhead (e.g., GPU cores)
- **Coarse-grained** — large independent tasks, low communication (e.g., MapReduce mappers)
- **Medium-grained** — balanced (e.g., MPI processes)

---

### 2.3 Programming Paradigms — Deeper Analysis

**MapReduce as a Functional + Data Parallel Paradigm:**
MapReduce applies the **functional programming** principles of `map()` and `reduce()` (from languages like Lisp) but executes them in parallel across distributed nodes. Inputs are immutable key-value pairs. Map is a *pure* function, and the framework guarantees isolation.

**Dataflow Paradigm in Cloud:**
In dataflow, a program is represented as a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)** of operators, where data flows through edges. Spark, Flink, and TensorFlow all use DAG-based execution.

**Message Passing Interface (MPI):**
The de-facto standard for **tightly-coupled** parallel programs. Uses primitives like `MPI_Send`, `MPI_Recv`, `MPI_Bcast`. Suitable for HPC but NOT for cloud big data.

---

### 2.4 MapReduce — Deep Theoretical Model

**Phases of a MapReduce Job:**

1. **Input Splitting** — Input is split into chunks of typically **64 MB** or **128 MB**.
2. **Mapping** — Each split is fed to a `map()` function which emits zero or more intermediate key-value pairs.
3. **Combiner (Optional)** — A local reduce on the mapper node to cut network traffic.
4. **Partitioning** — Keys are assigned to reducers via a partitioner (default: `hash(key) mod R`).
5. **Shuffle & Sort** — Intermediate pairs are transferred to reducers and sorted by key.
6. **Reducing** — `reduce()` processes each key group and emits final output.
7. **Output Writing** — Final output is written to HDFS.

**Hadoop's "Move Computation to Data" Principle:**

$$
\text{DataNode}_i \rightarrow \text{MapTask}_i \quad \text{on the same node}
$$

**Failure Handling in Hadoop:**
- **Task failure** → restart on another node (default max attempts = **4**)
- **TaskTracker failure** → kill all its tasks; reschedule
- **JobTracker failure** → entire job aborts (this is fixed in YARN)

---

### 2.5 Apache Hadoop — High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet — Memorize for Exams**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---------|----------------------|-------|
| HDFS Block Size | Default **128 MB** (v2.x) | Configurable via `dfs.blocksize` |
| Replication Factor | Default **3** copies | `dfs.replication` |
| MapReduce Job | $J = \{\text{Map tasks}\} \cup \{\text{Reduce tasks}\}$ | Coordinated by JobTracker (MRv1) / ApplicationMaster (YARN) |
| Mapper Output Records | $\sum_{i=1}^{M} \vert \text{Map}_i.\text{out} \vert$ | $M$ = number of mappers |
| Combiner Effectiveness | $T_{\text{reduced}} = T_{\text{map}} + (1-c) \cdot T_{\text{shuffle}}$ | $c$ = combiner compression ratio |
| Hadoop Heartbeat | Every **3 seconds** (default) | TaskTracker → JobTracker |
| Speculative Execution | Detect slow tasks; launch backup | Trade-off: resource waste vs. job latency |
| YARN Components | ResourceManager + NodeManager + ApplicationMaster + Container | Decouples resource management from app logic |
| HDFS Write Pipeline | Client → DN1 → DN2 → DN3 → ack chain | Three replicas in same rack-aware pattern |
| NameNode Metadata | FsImage + EditLog | FsImage = snapshot; EditLog = journal |

**CRITICAL — No Vertical Pipes in Tables** — All absolute values use `\vert`:

$$
\text{HDFS Capacity} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{DataNode}_i.\text{size} - \text{replication overhead}
$$

$$
\text{Storage Utilization} = \frac{\text{Used}}{\text{Capacity}} \times 100\%
$$

---

- **Nimbus** → Powers scientific clouds (Nebula, Magellan, Science Clouds)
- **Parallel Computing** → Weather forecasting, molecular dynamics, AI training
- **MapReduce** → Log analytics, indexing the web, ETL pipelines, search engine ranking
- **Hadoop** → Facebook's data warehouse (grew to 300+ PB), Yahoo's search, Netflix recommendations

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Amdahl's Law Applied to a MapReduce Job

**Problem:** A MapReduce job spends 5% of its time in the **serial coordinator setup** (JobTracker initial configuration, input split computation) and 95% in **parallel mappers and reducers**. Compute the theoretical speedup on 100 nodes.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$
P = 0.95, \quad 1 - P = 0.05, \quad N = 100
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law formula.**

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
S(100) = \frac{1}{0.05 + \frac{0.95}{100}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
0.05 + \frac{0.95}{100} = 0.05 + 0.0095 = 0.0595
$$

**Step 5 — Take the reciprocal.**

$$
S(100) = \frac{1}{0.0595} \approx 16.81
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

$$
S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{1 - P} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20
$$

So even with infinite nodes, the maximum speedup is **20×**, because of the 5% serial bottleneck. The 5% serial portion is the **achilles heel** of the job.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Walkthrough: Word Count in MapReduce

**Input Text (split into 2 mappers):**

- **Split 1:** "Deer Bear River" (3 words)
- **Split 2:** "Car Car River" (3 words)

**STEP A — Map Phase (Mapper 1 on Split 1):**

For each token, emit `(word, 1)`:

| Input Token | Output |
|-------------|--------|
| Deer        | (Deer, 1) |
| Bear        | (Bear, 1) |
| River       | (River, 1) |

**STEP B — Map Phase (Mapper 2 on Split 2):**

| Input Token | Output |
|-------------|--------|
| Car         | (Car, 1) |
| Car         | (Car, 1) |
| River       | (River, 1) |

**STEP C — Shuffle & Sort (Framework-managed):**

Keys are grouped by hash partitioning into reducers:

- **Reducer 1 bucket:** {Bear: 1}
- **Reducer 2 bucket:** {Car: 1, Car: 1}
- **Reducer 3 bucket:** {Deer: 1}
- **Reducer 4 bucket:** {River: 1, River: 1}

**STEP D — Reduce Phase:**

$$
\text{Reduce}(k_2, \text{list}(v_2)) = \sum_{v \in \text{list}} v
$$

| Key | Values | Output |
|-----|--------|--------|
| Bear | [1] | (Bear, 1) |
| Car  | [1, 1] | (Car, 2) |
| Deer | [1] | (Deer, 1) |
| River | [1, 1] | (River, 2) |

**Final Output File:** "Bear 1", "Car 2", "Deer 1", "River 2"

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: MapReduce Job Submission (YARN-based)

**YARN Components and Their Roles:**

| Component | Role | Key Resource |
|-----------|------|--------------|
| **Client** | Submits job, tracks progress | Job JAR + config |
| **ResourceManager (RM)** | Global arbiter of cluster resources | Scheduler (Capacity / Fair) |
| **NodeManager (NM)** | Per-node agent; manages containers | CPU, RAM, disk |
| **ApplicationMaster (AM)** | Per-application orchestrator | Negotiates containers from RM |
| **Container** | Allocated unit of CPU + RAM on a node | JVM slot |

**Step-by-Step Job Flow:**

1. Client submits job to **ResourceManager** with JAR + config.
2. RM allocates a **container** on any NodeManager and launches the **ApplicationMaster** there.
3. AM consults HDFS for **input splits** (via `InputFormat.getSplits()`).
4. AM requests containers from RM for each **map task** and **reduce task**.
5. RM grants containers; AM instructs NodeManagers to launch tasks.
6. Mappers process locally (data locality).
7. Mappers write intermediate output to **local disk** (NOT HDFS).
8. Reducers fetch via HTTP, shuffle, sort, then reduce.
9. Final output written to HDFS (replicated 3×).
10. AM unregisters from RM; job completes.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Derivation: Hadoop HDFS Capacity Planning

**Given:**
- Cluster with **10 DataNodes**
- Each DataNode: **20 TB raw disk**
- Default replication factor **r = 3**

**Step 1 — Compute raw cluster capacity.**

$$
C_{\text{raw}} = 10 \times 20 \text{ TB} = 200 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute effective usable capacity after replication overhead.**

$$
C_{\text{usable}} = \frac{C_{\text{raw}}}{r} = \frac{200 \text{ TB}}{3} \approx 66.67 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 3 — With 80% HDFS reservation (recommended, leaves space for OS, logs, failed replications):**

$$
C_{\text{practical}} = 0.80 \times 66.67 \approx 53.33 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 4 — Assume an average block size $B = 128 \text{ MB}$. Compute number of files of size $F$ that fit:**

$$
N_{\text{files}} = \frac{C_{\text{practical}} \times 1024}{B_{\text{blocks}} \times \text{size per file}}
$$

For files of **1 GB each**:

$$
N_{\text{files}} = \frac{53.33 \text{ TB} \times 1024}{8 \text{ blocks per 1 GB}} \approx 6826 \text{ files}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of NameNode metadata entries (inodes):**

$$
\text{Each block} = 1 \text{ in-memory inode}
$$

$$
\text{Total inodes} = N_{\text{files}} \times 8 = 54,608 \text{ entries}
$$

NameNode memory rule of thumb: **1 GB heap ≈ 1 million blocks**. So this fits easily.

---

### 3.5 Combinator Analysis: Nimbus Workspace Provisioning

For a Nimbus cloud with $H$ physical hosts, $V$ available VM slots, and a workspace request of $k$ VMs:

**Probability that all $k$ VMs land on distinct physical hosts (no co-location):**

$$
P_{\text{distinct}} = \frac{H \cdot (H-1) \cdot (H-2) \cdots (H-k+1)}{H^k}
$$

**Limiting case:** if $k \ll H$ (small workspace), $P \approx 1$ (good isolation). If $k \to H$, $P \to 0$ (severe co-location).

This is the **birthday paradox** for VM placement.

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define MapReduce programming model. List its two main functions with their input-output signatures.**

**Model Answer:**

> **MapReduce** is a distributed programming model for processing large datasets in parallel across a cluster. It was introduced by Google in 2004.

The two main functions are:

$$
\text{Map}: (k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_2, v_2)
$$

$$
\text{Reduce}: (k_2, \text{list}(v_2)) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_3, v_3)
$$

- **Map** takes a key-value pair and emits intermediate pairs.
- **Reduce** aggregates all intermediate values for a key into a smaller set.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark map signature, 1 mark reduce signature)*

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — MapReduce & Hadoop

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the complete **architecture and execution flow of a MapReduce job** in Hadoop, with a neat diagram. Describe the roles of **JobTracker, TaskTracker, NameNode, and DataNode**.

**(a) Model Solution:**

**MapReduce Architecture — MRv1 (Hadoop 1.x):**

The MapReduce framework in Hadoop v1 has a **master-slave architecture**:

| Component | Role |
|-----------|------|
| **JobTracker (Master)** | Accepts job submissions, schedules map/reduce tasks, monitors failures, allocates resources |
| **TaskTracker (Slave)** | Runs individual map or reduce tasks; sends heartbeats to JobTracker every 3 seconds |
| **NameNode (Master)** | Stores HDFS metadata (file names, permissions, block locations) in RAM + disk |
| **DataNode (Slave)** | Stores actual data blocks; sends block reports to NameNode |

**Execution Flow (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Job Submission:** Client submits a job to JobTracker containing JAR, input/output paths, and configurations.
2. **Input Splitting:** JobTracker queries HDFS NameNode for block locations and computes input splits (each split ≈ one HDFS block).
3. **Task Scheduling:** JobTracker assigns map tasks to TaskTrackers using a **data-locality** algorithm (prefers slots on DataNodes holding the input blocks).
4. **Map Phase:** Each TaskTracker runs the user-defined `map()` function. Output (intermediate k-v pairs) is buffered in memory and spilled to local disk.
5. **Combine & Partition:** A local **Combiner** may run to compress data; output is **partitioned** by `hash(key) mod R` where $R$ = number of reducers.
6. **Shuffle & Sort:** Reduce task **pulls** intermediate data from all completed mappers via HTTP. Data is **sorted** by key.
7. **Reduce Phase:** User-defined `reduce()` aggregates the sorted data per key.
8. **Output Writing:** Final output is written to HDFS, replicated 3 times by the DataNodes.
9. **Failure Handling:** If a TaskTracker fails to send a heartbeat within 10 minutes, JobTracker reschedules its tasks. If a task fails 4 times, the job is aborted.

```
Client → JobTracker → TaskTracker (Map) → Local Disk
                       ↓ HTTP pull
                     TaskTracker (Reduce) → HDFS
```

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, explain the **Word Count MapReduce program** in detail. Show the data flow through the **Map → Shuffle → Sort → Reduce** phases.

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Problem:** Count occurrences of each word in a text file.

**Input:** "Deer Bear River" (Split 1) and "Car Car River" (Split 2)

**Pseudocode (Map Function):**

```java
map(LongWritable key, Text value, Context context) {
    for (String word : value.toString().split(" ")) {
        context.write(new Text(word), new IntWritable(1));
    }
}
```

**Pseudocode (Reduce Function):**

```java
reduce(Text key, Iterable<IntWritable> values, Context context) {
    int sum = 0;
    for (IntWritable val : values) {
        sum += val.get();
    }
    context.write(key, new IntWritable(sum));
}
```

**Data Flow (Step-by-Step):**

| Phase | Input | Output |
|-------|-------|--------|
| **Map 1** | "Deer Bear River" | (Deer,1), (Bear,1), (River,1) |
| **Map 2** | "Car Car River" | (Car,1), (Car,1), (River,1) |
| **Shuffle & Sort** | All 5 pairs | Grouped: {Bear:1}, {Car:1,1}, {Deer:1}, {River:1,1} |
| **Reduce 1** | {Bear:1} | (Bear, 1) |
| **Reduce 2** | {Car:1,1} | (Car, 2) |
| **Reduce 3** | {Deer:1} | (Deer, 1) |
| **Reduce 4** | {River:1,1} | (River, 2) |

**Final Output:**

```
Bear 1
Car 2
Deer 1
River 2
```

The Word Count example demonstrates the **functional and parallel** nature of MapReduce: the same `map()` runs independently on each split, and the same `reduce()` runs independently on each key group.

*(14 marks total: 7 marks each sub-part)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Nimbus & Parallel Computing Paradigms

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Nimbus cloud architecture** in detail. List its major components and describe how a **virtual workspace** is provisioned.

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Nimbus** is an IaaS cloud computing toolkit designed for scientific computing, providing EC2-compatible cloud services.

**Major Components:**

1. **Workspace Service** — The central front-end. Accepts SOAP/REST API calls in the EC2 format. Authenticates users, validates requests, and maintains a database of available resources.
2. **Workspace Control** — A per-node daemon that performs the actual VM lifecycle operations: create, start, pause, resume, shutdown, and destroy. Uses the **VMRC (VM Remote Control)** protocol for communication.
3. **Resource Pool** — A database (PostgreSQL/MySQL) that tracks available physical hosts, their capacities, and current load.
4. **Storage Pool** — Manages VM disk images and block storage volumes (often backed by iSCSI or LVM).
5. **Nimbus-Context-Broker** — Enables **cloud federation** — users can launch workspaces on remote Nimbus sites through a single home site.
6. **Chimera** — A declarative XML schema for describing virtual infrastructure, enabling reproducible cloud experiments.

**Workspace Provisioning Flow:**

1. **User** authenticates with the Workspace Service.
2. User submits a workspace request (VM image, instance type, number of VMs, network config) via **Cloud Client** or API.
3. Workspace Service queries the **Resource Pool** for available physical hosts.
4. Once a host is selected, the request is forwarded to the **Workspace Control daemon** on that host.
5. Workspace Control invokes the **hypervisor (Xen or KVM)** to boot the VM.
6. The VM image is fetched from the **Storage Pool** and assigned a block volume.
7. The VM starts, gets an IP via DHCP, and is reachable to the user.
8. User can `pause`, `resume`, `shutdown`, or `destroy` the workspace via the API.

**Key Advantage:** Nimbus is **reference-compatible** with EC2 — any tool written for AWS EC2 works on Nimbus with minimal changes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **shared memory parallel programming** and **distributed memory parallel programming** with examples. Apply **Amdahl's Law** to compute the speedup of a program that is 90% parallelizable when run on 8 processors.

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Shared Memory (SMP) | Distributed Memory (Cluster) |
|---------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| **Architecture** | Multiple CPUs access one common RAM | Each node has its own RAM; communication via network |
| **Communication** | Implicit via shared variables | Explicit via message passing (MPI) |
| **Hardware Example** | Multi-core desktop, SMP server | Hadoop cluster, supercomputer |
| **Programming** | OpenMP, pthreads | MPI, MapReduce, Spark |
| **Scalability** | Limited (memory contention) | Highly scalable (1000s of nodes) |
| **Failure** | Single point of failure | Tolerates individual node failure |
| **Examples** | Weather model on 32-core node | Google's MapReduce on 10,000+ nodes |

**Amdahl's Law Calculation:**

Given:
- $P = 0.90$ (90% parallelizable)
- $1 - P = 0.10$ (10% serial)
- $N = 8$ processors

Apply:

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}
$$

Substitute:

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{0.10 + \frac{0.90}{8}}
$$

Compute:

$$
0.10 + 0.1125 = 0.2125
$$

Take reciprocal:

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{0.2125} \approx 4.71
$$

**Maximum possible speedup with infinite processors:**

$$
S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10
$$

**Efficiency:**

$$
E(8) = \frac{S(8)}{8} = \frac{4.71}{8} \approx 0.589 = 58.9\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Even with 8 processors, we only get **4.71×** speedup, not 8×, due to the 10% serial portion. Doubling processors from 8 to 16 would only yield marginal improvement (4.71 → 5.93), illustrating **diminishing returns**.

*(14 marks total: 7 marks each sub-part)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **MapReduce signs:** Students often write `Map: (k1, v1) → (k2, v2)` instead of `list(k2, v2)`. The output is a **list**, not a single pair. Loses **1 mark**.
> 2. **Hadoop block size confusion:** Always state **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x default), not 64 MB. Loses **0.5 mark**.
> 3. **Nimbus vs OpenStack confusion:** Nimbus is a **scientific IaaS toolkit**, OpenStack is a general-purpose cloud OS. They are NOT the same. Loses **1-2 marks**.
> 4. **Amdahl's Law setup:** Forgetting to convert percentage to decimal (90% = 0.90, not 90) is a frequent error. Loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **YARN vs MRv1:** Modern Hadoop uses YARN, not the old JobTracker/TaskTracker model. Mentioning both correctly in answer shows depth and earns extra credit.
> 6. **Combiner vs Reducer:** Combiner runs **locally on mapper output** for efficiency; Reducer runs **after shuffle**. Conflating them loses marks.
