> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exams**

- [ ] **Nimbus** = open-source IaaS toolkit, EC2-compatible, scientific clouds, supports Xen/KVM, has **Nimbus-Context-Broker** for federation.
- [ ] **Cloud Programming** = elasticity + on-demand + pay-per-use + abstraction + multi-tenancy.
- [ ] **Parallel Computing** uses **Flynn's Taxonomy**: SISD, SIMD, MISD, MIMD.
- [ ] **Amdahl's Law** formula:

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}
$$

- [ ] **Gustafson's Law** assumes problem size **scales with N** (more realistic for big data).
- [ ] **Parallelism vs Concurrency**: simultaneous execution vs. dealing-with-many-things (may interleave).
- [ ] **Programming Paradigms** relevant to cloud: Functional (MapReduce), Dataflow (Spark), Message-Passing (MPI), Event-Driven (Lambda).
- [ ] **MapReduce model**:

$$
\text{Map}: (k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_2, v_2)
$$

$$
\text{Reduce}: (k_2, \text{list}(v_2)) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_3, v_3)
$$

- [ ] **MapReduce phases** (memorize the 7): Input Splitting → Mapping → Combining → Partitioning → Shuffle & Sort → Reducing → Output Writing.
- [ ] **Combiner** = local reducer on mapper side, reduces network traffic.
- [ ] **Partitioner** = `hash(key) mod R` where $R$ = number of reducers.
- [ ] **Hadoop 1.x (MRv1)** = JobTracker + TaskTracker (master-slave).
- [ ] **Hadoop 2.x (YARN)** = ResourceManager + NodeManager + ApplicationMaster + Container.
- [ ] **HDFS default block size** = **128 MB**; default **replication factor = 3**.
- [ ] **NameNode stores metadata in RAM** = scalability bottleneck; each block ≈ 150 bytes of RAM.
- [ ] **Data Locality** principle: "Move computation to data, not data to computation."
- [ ] **Speculative Execution** = detect stragglers, launch duplicates (trade-off: extra work for less latency).
- [ ] **Apache Hadoop** = open-source (Apache 2.0), founded by Doug Cutting & Mike Cafarella, originally from **Nutch** project, inspired by Google GFS + MapReduce papers (2003-2004).
- [ ] **Hadoop ecosystem** = HDFS, MapReduce, YARN, HBase, Hive, Pig, Spark, Sqoop, Flume, ZooKeeper, Oozie.
- [ ] **MapReduce word count** is the canonical example — practice the table-trace on paper.
- [ ] **Hadoop heartbeats** every **3 seconds**; task timeout default **600 seconds** (10 minutes).
- [ ] **Failure types**: Task failure (4 retries) → TaskTracker failure (reschedule) → JobTracker failure (job abort in v1) → NameNode failure (HDFS down — HA solves in v2).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HDFS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** HDFS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** is a distributed, scalable, and fault-tolerant file system designed to store very large files (terabytes to petabytes) across multiple commodity machines in a Hadoop cluster. It follows a **master/slave (NameNode/DataNode) architecture**, uses a **write-once, read-many (WORM)** access model, and provides high-throughput access to application data by streaming the data in large blocks (default **128 MB** in Hadoop 2.x/3.x).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, HDFS is positioned under the Big Data / Cloud Storage umbrella and is the foundational storage layer for the **MapReduce**, **YARN**, and **Spark** processing engines.

### Key Terminology Lockdown (Exam-Critical Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition (verbatim syllabus) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **NameNode** | The master daemon that maintains the metadata (namespace tree, file-to-block mapping) of the entire file system. |
| **DataNode** | The slave daemon that stores the actual data blocks on the local disk. |
| **Block** | The smallest unit of read/write in HDFS. Default size = **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x onward), earlier was **64 MB**. |
| **Replication Factor** | The number of copies maintained for each block. Default = **3**. |
| **Rack Awareness** | A policy that uses the rack topology to place replicas intelligently across racks for fault tolerance. |
| **Secondary NameNode** | A helper node that periodically merges the **fsimage** with **edit logs** to prevent edit logs from growing unbounded. (It is **not** a standby NameNode.) |
| **fsimage** | A snapshot/checkpoint of the filesystem metadata stored on disk. |
| **Edit Log** | A transaction log recording every metadata change made to the filesystem. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library of Alexandria on a Budget"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Pump (Memorize this analogy):** Imagine HDFS as a **gigantic public library** that stores millions of books, but instead of keeping one complete book on one shelf, it **tears every book into fixed-size chunks (blocks)** and scatters these chunks across **many branch libraries (DataNodes)** in different cities.

- The **Head Librarian (NameNode)** only knows *which chunk is in which branch* — never the content.
- Every chunk is photocopied **3 times (replication factor = 3)** and stored in different branches to survive fire/flood.
- When you ask for a book, the Librarian tells your assistant (the client) the exact addresses of the chunks, and your assistant walks to those branches and reads them in parallel.
- This is **why HDFS gives high throughput**: parallel reads from many machines.
- This is **why HDFS is write-once**: once a book is shredded, re-shredding it (random writes) is messy, expensive, and against library policy.

This analogy underpins nearly every KTU 2-mark and short-answer question on HDFS.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold and Lock)

The following values are **standard KTU-board-expected defaults**. Examiners will assume you know them.

- **Default Block Size:** **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x and 3.x)
- **Default Replication Factor:** **3**
- **Default Heartbeat Interval:** **3 seconds**
- **Block Report Interval:** **6 hours** (default)
- **fsimage + EditLog Checkpoint Interval:** **1 hour** (or 1 million transactions, whichever first)
- **HDFS Replica Placement Policy:** 1 replica on local node (for writers), 1 on a different node in the same rack, 1 on a node in a different rack.

> [!TIP]
> If a numerical question in KTU 2024 does **not** specify block size or replication factor, **always use 128 MB and 3** unless the question hints otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HDFS Architecture — The Master/Slave Paradigm

HDFS follows a strict **master/slave (now sometimes called Active/Standby + Workers)** topology consisting of three primary daemon types:

### 2.1.1 NameNode (Master)

- **In-Memory Metadata Store:** The entire filesystem namespace (directory tree, file names, block-to-file mapping, block locations, permissions, quotas) is held in **RAM** for fast access.
- **Disk Files Maintained:**
  * `fsimage` — the most recent checkpoint of the metadata.
  * `edits` (or `edits_inprogress`) — the transaction log of recent metadata changes.
- **Never touches user data.** It only handles metadata.
- **Single Point of Failure (SPOF) in classic Hadoop 1.x.** Solved in Hadoop 2.x+ via **HA (High Availability)** using a **Standby NameNode** + **ZooKeeper** + **Shared EditLog (NFS / Quorum Journal Manager)**.

### 2.1.2 DataNode (Slave / Worker)

- Stores **actual data blocks** on its local disks.
- On startup, connects to the NameNode via TCP and performs a **handshake**.
- Periodically (every **3 seconds**) sends a **heartbeat** to the NameNode, signaling "I am alive."
- Periodically (every **6 hours**, by default) sends a **Block Report** — a complete list of all blocks stored on that DataNode.
- Serves read/write requests directly from clients (data path bypasses the NameNode).
- Can be configured with multiple storage directories (volumes).

### 2.1.3 Secondary NameNode (SNN)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Misconception:** The Secondary NameNode is **NOT** a backup NameNode. It does **NOT** serve requests, and it does **NOT** provide automatic failover. Its sole job is to merge `fsimage` + `edits` periodically to create a new checkpoint, thus keeping edit log size bounded. In modern Hadoop 2.x/3.x, this role is often replaced by the **CheckpointNode** or **BackupNode**.

## 2.2 The Block Abstraction — Why 128 MB?

Files in HDFS are split into **fixed-size blocks**. Why a fixed size?

- **Decouples file metadata from file size** — NameNode only stores `~150 bytes` per block in memory, regardless of block content size.
- **Enables simplified storage management** — a DataNode only needs to know "I have N blocks" without caring about which file they belong to.
- **Optimized for streaming reads** — large blocks reduce the seek-overhead vs. data-transfer time, yielding high sequential throughput.

### The Memory-Bound Constraint of NameNode

$$ M_{NN} \approx B \times 150 \text{ bytes} $$

where $M_{NN}$ is the RAM required at the NameNode and $B$ is the total number of blocks in the cluster. This is why **HDFS is unsuitable for millions of tiny files** — the NameNode would run out of memory.

## 2.3 Replica Placement Policy (Rack Awareness)

HDFS uses a **rack-aware replica placement strategy** to balance **reliability** (don't lose data on rack failure) against **write bandwidth** (don't write all replicas across racks — slow).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Default Replica Policy (Hadoop Default — assume for KTU):**
> - **Replica 1:** Placed on the **local node** of the writer (if the writer is a DataNode); otherwise on a random DataNode in the same rack.
> - **Replica 2:** Placed on a **different node in the same rack** as Replica 1.
> - **Replica 3:** Placed on a **different node in a different rack**.

This policy guarantees:
- **Rack failure tolerance** — losing an entire rack still leaves ≥1 replica.
- **Intra-rack write aggregation** — replica 1 and 2 use the same rack switch (faster).
- **Read locality flexibility** — clients can read from the closest replica.

## 2.4 Fault Tolerance & Heartbeat Protocol

- The NameNode considers a DataNode **dead** if it misses **10 consecutive heartbeats** (i.e., no heartbeat for **30 seconds** by default).
- Upon detecting a dead DataNode, the NameNode **triggers re-replication** of all blocks that were stored on that node, until the replication factor is restored.
- **Heartbeat doubles as a command channel** — the NameNode piggybacks instructions (replicate, delete, re-register, shutdown) onto the heartbeat response.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> The following table summarizes every KTU-board-relevant formula for HDFS. All vertical bars in mathematical expressions use `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Number of Blocks in a File** | $B_f = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{FileSize}}{\text{BlockSize}} \right\rceil$ | $B_f$ = blocks, FileSize in MB, BlockSize in MB | Calculate metadata overhead. |
| 2 | **Total Blocks in Cluster** | $B = \sum_{i=1}^{F} B_{f_i}$ | $F$ = number of files | Estimate NameNode memory. |
| 3 | **NameNode RAM Estimate** | $M_{NN} \approx 150 \text{ B} \times B$ | $M_{NN}$ in bytes, $B$ = total blocks | Sizing NameNode hardware. |
| 4 | **Total Physical Storage Required** | $S_{total} = \text{LogicalSize} \times R$ | $S_{total}$ in bytes, $R$ = replication factor | Disk capacity planning. |
| 5 | **Per-DataNode Storage** | $S_{DN} = \dfrac{S_{total}}{N_{DN}} \times \text{LoadFactor}$ | $N_{DN}$ = number of DataNodes | Uniform distribution planning. |
| 6 | **Failure Detection Time** | $T_{FD} = T_{HB} \times 10$ | $T_{HB}$ = heartbeat interval (s), $T_{FD}$ = failure detection time (s) | Bound on unavailability. |
| 7 | **Re-replication Trigger** | When $R_{actual} < R_{target}$ for any block | $R_{actual}$ = current replicas, $R_{target}$ = 3 (default) | Data durability guarantee. |
| 8 | **Theoretical Read Throughput** | $\Theta_{read} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{DN}} B_w \times \eta_{net}$ | $B_w$ = per-node disk bandwidth, $\eta_{net}$ = network efficiency | Cluster sizing for analytics. |
| 9 | **Write Pipeline Throughput** | $\Theta_{write} \approx \min(B_{w1}, B_{w2}, B_{w3}) \times 3$ | Bottleneck of the 3-node pipeline | Bound on ingestion rate. |
| 10 | **Block Report Memory at DN** | $M_{BR} = B_{DN} \times 150 \text{ B}$ | $B_{DN}$ = blocks per DataNode | DataNode RAM sizing. |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HDFS powers every major Hadoop deployment** — Yahoo, Facebook (now Meta), Twitter, LinkedIn — for log analytics, recommendation systems, and data warehousing.
- It is the storage backbone of the **data lake** paradigm, where raw, semi-structured, and unstructured data is stored in native format for later processing.
- It is increasingly replaced/augmented by **object stores** (Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake Storage, MinIO) in cloud-native Hadoop deployments, but the architecture, block model, and replication semantics are conceptually identical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 3 Mandate:** All derivations below are exhaustive. Every algebraic step is shown, every assumption is stated, and every transformation is justified. The format follows a "given → formula → substitution → result" pattern, which is the KTU board examiner's gold standard.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Blocks from File Size

**Given:**
- File size $F = 512 \text{ MB}$
- Block size $B_s = 128 \text{ MB}$ (Hadoop 2.x/3.x default)
- Replication factor $R = 3$ (default)

**Goal:** Compute the number of logical blocks $B_f$ and the total physical storage footprint $S_{total}$.

### Step 1 — State the floor/ceiling relationship

Because a file must be split into **whole, non-overlapping** blocks of fixed size, the last block may be **partial**. Hence we use the **ceiling** function.

$$ B_f = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B_s} \right\rceil $$

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$ B_f = \left\lceil \frac{512 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil $$

### Step 3 — Compute the ratio

$$ \frac{512}{128} = 4.0 $$

### Step 4 — Apply the ceiling

Since 4.0 is already an integer, the ceiling is 4.

$$ B_f = 4 \text{ blocks} $$

### Step 5 — Compute total physical storage with replication

$$ S_{total} = F \times R $$

$$ S_{total} = 512 \text{ MB} \times 3 = 1536 \text{ MB} $$

### Step 6 — Sanity check

Total physical storage = $4 \text{ blocks} \times 128 \text{ MB/block} \times 3 = 1536 \text{ MB}$. Matches Step 5. ✔

> **Final Answer:** $B_f = 4$ blocks, $S_{total} = 1536 \text{ MB} = 1.5 \text{ GB}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — NameNode Memory Sizing

**Given:**
- Cluster stores $N_F = 10{,}000$ files
- Average file size $= 1 \text{ GB} = 1024 \text{ MB}$
- Block size $B_s = 128 \text{ MB}$
- Replication factor $R = 3$

**Goal:** Estimate the NameNode RAM requirement $M_{NN}$.

### Step 1 — Compute blocks per file

$$ B_f = \left\lceil \frac{1024 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil = 8 \text{ blocks/file} $$

### Step 2 — Compute total blocks in cluster

$$ B_{total} = N_F \times B_f = 10{,}000 \times 8 = 80{,}000 \text{ blocks} $$

### Step 3 — Note on replication in metadata

> The NameNode tracks the **first occurrence** of each block (the metadata is per-block, not per-replica). The replicas are tracked as a list, but the dominant memory cost is the per-block object.

### Step 4 — Apply the NameNode memory formula

$$ M_{NN} \approx 150 \text{ bytes} \times B_{total} $$

$$ M_{NN} \approx 150 \times 80{,}000 $$

$$ M_{NN} \approx 12{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} $$

### Step 5 — Convert to MB

$$ M_{NN} = \frac{12 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes}}{1024 \times 1024 \text{ bytes/MB}} \approx 11.44 \text{ MB} $$

### Step 6 — Engineering reality check

> In practice, the NameNode also stores file inodes, directory entries, transaction logs in memory, and the **JVM overhead** is significant. The standard production rule-of-thumb is to provision **1 GB of RAM per 1 million blocks**, giving a much more practical estimate of **~80 MB** of metadata + JVM overheads of **~4–8 GB** total. The 150-byte figure is a **theoretical per-block object size**.

> **Final Answer (theoretical):** $M_{NN} \approx 11.44 \text{ MB}$. **Production estimate:** Provision **~5–8 GB** of NameNode RAM for a 1-million-block cluster.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Failure Detection Time

**Given:**
- Default heartbeat interval $T_{HB} = 3 \text{ s}$
- Missed-heartbeat threshold $\theta = 10$ (default)

**Goal:** Maximum time before a DataNode is declared dead.

### Step 1 — Define the failure detection rule

The NameNode marks a DataNode as **dead/stale** if $\geq \theta$ consecutive heartbeats are missed.

$$ T_{FD} = T_{HB} \times \theta $$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$ T_{FD} = 3 \text{ s} \times 10 = 30 \text{ s} $$

### Step 3 — Interpret

During these 30 seconds, the "dead" DataNode may serve partial requests to clients. The NameNode will only start re-replicating the lost blocks **after** the timeout expires.

$$ \boxed{T_{FD} = 30 \text{ seconds}} $$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Write Pipeline Throughput

**Given:** 3 DataNodes in a write pipeline with per-node disk write bandwidths:
- Node A: $B_{w1} = 100 \text{ MB/s}$
- Node B: $B_{w2} = 80 \text{ MB/s}$
- Node C: $B_{w3} = 120 \text{ MB/s}$

**Goal:** Compute effective write throughput $\Theta_{write}$.

### Step 1 — Recognize the pipeline bottleneck

HDFS write pipeline is a **chain**: data flows A → B → C. The throughput is gated by the **slowest link**.

$$ \Theta_{write} = \min(B_{w1}, B_{w2}, B_{w3}) \times \text{(overhead factor)} $$

### Step 2 — Identify the minimum

$$ \min(100, 80, 120) = 80 \text{ MB/s} $$

### Step 3 — Account for network and protocol overhead

Typically, network efficiency $\eta_{net} \approx 0.8$ (TCP/IP overhead, ACKs, packet loss).

$$ \Theta_{write} \approx 80 \text{ MB/s} \times 0.8 = 64 \text{ MB/s} $$

### Step 4 — Result

$$ \boxed{\Theta_{write} \approx 64 \text{ MB/s effective}} $$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Total Physical Storage with Heterogeneous Block Sizes

> A common KTU trick: a file of 600 MB split into 128 MB blocks. Naively: 600/128 = 4.6875 → 5 blocks. Last block = 600 − 4×128 = 600 − 512 = **88 MB**.

**Given:** File size $F = 600 \text{ MB}$, $B_s = 128 \text{ MB}$, $R = 3$.

### Step 1 — Number of blocks

$$ B_f = \left\lceil \frac{600}{128} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 4.6875 \right\rceil = 5 \text{ blocks} $$

### Step 2 — Verify last block size

$$ B_{last} = F - (B_f - 1) \times B_s = 600 - 4 \times 128 = 600 - 512 = 88 \text{ MB} $$

### Step 3 — Total physical storage (with replication)

$$ S_{total} = 4 \times 128 \text{ MB} \times 3 + 88 \text{ MB} \times 3 = 1536 + 264 = 1800 \text{ MB} $$

### Step 4 — Alternative cross-check (formula-based)

$$ S_{total} = F \times R = 600 \times 3 = 1800 \text{ MB} \checkmark $$

> **Final Answer:** $B_f = 5$, last block = 88 MB, $S_{total} = 1800 \text{ MB}$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Number of Blocks for Common KTU Problem Sizes

This table is the kind of "shortcut lookup" a topper uses to skip arithmetic errors in the exam.

| File Size | Block Size | # Logical Blocks | Last Block Size | Total Physical (R=3) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 64 MB | 128 MB | 1 | 64 MB | 192 MB |
| 128 MB | 128 MB | 1 | 128 MB | 384 MB |
| 256 MB | 128 MB | 2 | 128 MB | 768 MB |
| 512 MB | 128 MB | 4 | 128 MB | 1536 MB |
| 600 MB | 128 MB | 5 | 88 MB | 1800 MB |
| 1 GB (1024 MB) | 128 MB | 8 | 128 MB | 3072 MB |
| 1 TB | 128 MB | 8192 | 128 MB | 3072 GB = 3 TB |
| 1 PB | 128 MB | 8,388,608 | 128 MB | 3 PB |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDFS High-Level Master/Slave Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ClientA[Client Application 1]
    ClientB[Client Application 2]
    ClientC[Client Application 3]
    
    subgraph NameNodeCluster[NameNode Master Tier]
        NN[Active NameNode]
        SNN[Standby NameNode]
        ZK[ZooKeeper Ensemble]
        QJM[Quorum Journal Manager]
    end
    
    subgraph Rack1[Rack 1]
        DN1[DataNode 1]
        DN2[DataNode 2]
        DN3[DataNode 3]
    end
    
    subgraph Rack2[Rack 2]
        DN4[DataNode 4]
        DN5[DataNode 5]
        DN6[DataNode 6]
    end
    
    subgraph Rack3[Rack 3]
        DN7[DataNode 7]
        DN8[DataNode 8]
        DN9[DataNode 9]
    end
    
    ClientA --> NN
    ClientB --> NN
    ClientC --> NN
    NN <--> QJM
    SNN <--> QJM
    NN <--> ZK
    SNN <--> ZK
    NN --> DN1
    NN --> DN2
    NN --> DN3
    NN --> DN4
    NN --> DN5
    NN --> DN6
    NN --> DN7
    NN --> DN8
    NN --> DN9
    DN1 -.Heartbeat 3s.-> NN
    DN4 -.Heartbeat 3s.-> NN
    DN7 -.Heartbeat 3s.-> NN
    DN1 --- DN2
    DN4 --- DN5
    DN7 --- DN8
    Rack1 --- Rack2
    Rack2 --- Rack3
```

> **Reading guide:** Solid arrows = control plane (metadata RPC). Dashed arrows = heartbeat channel. Horizontal links = intra-rack switch (fast). Long links = inter-rack switch (slower, fault-isolated).

## 4.2 HDFS File Write Pipeline (Block-Level)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant NN as NameNode
    participant DN1 as DataNode 1
    participant DN2 as DataNode 2
    participant DN3 as DataNode 3

    C->>NN: create() request for file
    NN-->>C: block locations (DN1, DN2, DN3)
    C->>DN1: write block to pipeline stage 1
    DN1->>DN2: forward packet to stage 2
    DN2->>DN3: forward packet to stage 3
    DN3-->>DN2: ACK packet
    DN2-->>DN1: ACK packet
    DN1-->>C: ACK packet
    C->>NN: complete() file close
    NN-->>C: file write success
```

> **Explanation:** The client pushes data to DN1, which pipelines to DN2, then DN3. Acknowledgements flow back. The NameNode is **not in the data path** — it only tells the client *where* to write.

## 4.3 HDFS Block Replica Placement Strategy (Default Policy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Writer Client] --> R1[Replica 1: Same Node as Writer]
    R1 --> R2[Replica 2: Different Node Same Rack]
    R2 --> R3[Replica 3: Different Node Different Rack]
    
    subgraph SameRack[Rack A]
        W
        R1
        R2
    end
    
    subgraph DiffRack[Rack B]
        R3
    end
```

## 4.4 Read Operation Flow (Block-Level)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant NN as NameNode
    participant DN1 as DataNode with Replica
    participant DN2 as DataNode with Replica
    participant DN3 as DataNode with Replica

    C->>NN: open() file read request
    NN-->>C: list of block locations sorted by proximity
    C->>DN2: read block (closest replica)
    DN2-->>C: block data stream
    C->>DN1: read next block
    DN1-->>C: block data stream
    C->>NN: close() file
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern: 3-mark Part A short-answer questions, and 14-mark Part B questions with internal choice. Each question is tagged with a simulated past-year reference.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define HDFS. List any four features of HDFS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**

> **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** is a distributed, scalable, and fault-tolerant file system designed to store very large files across commodity machines in a Hadoop cluster.
>
> **Four key features:**
> 1. **Distributed Storage** — data is split into blocks and stored across multiple machines.
> 2. **Fault Tolerance** — blocks are replicated (default 3 copies) so data survives node failure.
> 3. **High Throughput** — optimized for streaming reads/writes, not low-latency random access.
> 4. **Scalability** — easily scales horizontally by adding more DataNodes.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a NameNode? Why is it called the master of HDFS?**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~70 words):**

> The **NameNode** is the master daemon of HDFS. It maintains the **metadata** of the entire filesystem — directory tree, file-to-block mapping, block locations, and access permissions — in main memory and on disk (`fsimage` and `edits`).
>
> It is called the master because **all client requests** (open, read, write, close, rename) are first routed to the NameNode for metadata decisions. DataNodes are its slaves that store the actual data and report back via heartbeats. The NameNode **never handles actual file data**, only metadata.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Convention):** Each Part B question is split into **two sub-parts: (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks**. You answer either Question A **or** Question B (internal choice).

---

### **Question A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **HDFS architecture** with a neat diagram. Describe the roles of **NameNode, DataNode, and Secondary NameNode** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **block replication and rack-awareness policy** in HDFS. How does HDFS ensure fault tolerance? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**HDFS Architecture (Master/Slave):**
HDFS follows a master/slave architecture comprising three key daemons:

1. **NameNode (Master):**
   - Stores all **metadata** (file names, directory structure, block-to-file mapping, replica locations, permissions) in RAM for fast access.
   - On-disk files: `fsimage` (snapshot) and `edits` (transaction log).
   - Receives heartbeat signals from all DataNodes every 3 seconds.
   - Never stores actual user data.
   - Acts as the central coordinator for all read/write metadata decisions.

2. **DataNode (Slave/Worker):**
   - Stores **actual data blocks** on local disk.
   - On startup, registers with the NameNode.
   - Sends **heartbeat every 3 seconds** to signal liveness.
   - Sends a **Block Report every 6 hours** listing all blocks it stores.
   - Serves read/write requests directly from clients (bypasses NameNode for data).
   - A cluster typically has hundreds/thousands of DataNodes.

3. **Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Helper):**
   - **Not a backup** NameNode. It does not serve client requests.
   - Periodically (default 1 hour or 1M transactions) downloads `fsimage` and `edits` from the NameNode, merges them to create a new `fsimage`, and uploads it back.
   - This **bounds the size of the edit log** and reduces NameNode recovery time on restart.
   - In modern Hadoop, replaced by **CheckpointNode** or **Standby NameNode** in HA mode.

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 architecture diagram.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Block Replication Policy (Default):**
HDFS replicates every block **3 times** by default. The replicas are placed as follows:
- **Replica 1:** On the **local DataNode** where the writer is running (or a random node in the same rack).
- **Replica 2:** On a **different node in the same rack** as Replica 1.
- **Replica 3:** On a **node in a different rack** from Replicas 1 and 2.
- Any additional replicas (R > 3) are placed on random nodes in the cluster.

**Why this policy?**
- **Write performance:** Two of three replicas are on the same rack → uses the faster intra-rack switch bandwidth.
- **Fault tolerance:** Replicas span two distinct racks → losing one entire rack still leaves ≥1 surviving replica.
- **Read locality:** Clients can read from the closest replica.

**Fault Tolerance Mechanism:**
1. **Heartbeat-based detection** — DataNode is marked dead after missing 10 heartbeats (30 seconds).
2. **Re-replication** — NameNode initiates re-replication of all under-replicated blocks on a dead node until the count returns to 3.
3. **Block integrity check** — DataNodes periodically compute **checksums** of stored blocks; corrupted blocks are reported and replaced.
4. **Rack awareness** — as above, distributes replicas across racks to survive rack-level failures (power, switch, cooling).

---

### **Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **HDFS file read and write operations** with sequence diagrams. How is the NameNode involved in each? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Hadoop cluster stores **1 million files**, each of size **2 GB**. The block size is **128 MB** and the replication factor is **3**.
   - (i) Calculate the **total number of blocks** in the cluster. **(2 marks)**
   - (ii) Calculate the **total physical storage** required. **(2 marks)**
   - (iii) Estimate the **NameNode memory requirement** using the 150-byte per-block rule. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**HDFS Write Operation (Step-by-step):**
1. Client calls `DistributedFileSystem.create()` → contacts the NameNode.
2. NameNode performs metadata checks (file doesn't exist, permissions OK) and creates an entry in the edit log. NameNode does **not** specify DataNode locations yet.
3. Client gets an `FSDataOutputStream`. The first block's data is chunked into **64 KB packets**.
4. Client calls `NameNode.addBlock()` → NameNode allocates **3 DataNodes** for the pipeline (based on rack-awareness).
5. Client pushes the first packet to **DataNode 1** (head of pipeline).
6. DataNode 1 forwards to **DataNode 2**, which forwards to **DataNode 3**.
7. Each DataNode stores the packet in a temporary file and sends an **ACK** backwards.
8. After ACK reaches the client, the next packet is sent.
9. When the block is fully written, DataNodes pipeline-confirm to the NameNode.
10. Client calls `NameNode.complete()` → NameNode finalizes the file metadata.

**HDFS Read Operation (Step-by-step):**
1. Client calls `DistributedFileSystem.open()` → contacts the NameNode.
2. NameNode returns the **list of blocks** for the file along with the **DataNode locations** for each block (sorted by network proximity).
3. Client gets an `FSDataInputStream` and connects to the **closest DataNode** holding the first block.
4. Data is streamed from the DataNode in 64 KB packets.
5. When block 1 finishes, client closes that connection, opens connection to closest DataNode for block 2, and so on.
6. Client calls `close()` on the stream; NameNode metadata is **not** updated for reads.

> **Key Insight:** The NameNode is **only in the control path** (metadata RPCs). The **data path** flows directly between Client and DataNodes, never through the NameNode. This is what allows HDFS to scale.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:**
- Number of files: $N_F = 1{,}000{,}000$
- File size: $F = 2 \text{ GB} = 2048 \text{ MB}$
- Block size: $B_s = 128 \text{ MB}$
- Replication factor: $R = 3$

**(i) Total Number of Blocks:**

$$ B_f = \left\lceil \frac{2048}{128} \right\rceil = 16 \text{ blocks per file} $$

$$ B_{total} = N_F \times B_f = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 16 = 16{,}000{,}000 \text{ blocks} $$

**(ii) Total Physical Storage:**

$$ S_{total} = \text{Total logical data} \times R = (1{,}000{,}000 \times 2048 \text{ MB}) \times 3 $$

$$ S_{total} = 2{,}048{,}000{,}000 \text{ MB} \times 3 = 6{,}144{,}000{,}000 \text{ MB} $$

Converting to PB: $\dfrac{6.144 \times 10^9 \text{ MB}}{1024^2 \text{ MB/PB}} \approx 5722 \text{ PB} \approx 5.6 \text{ EB}$.

In GB: $S_{total} = 6{,}144{,}000 \text{ GB} = 6.144 \text{ EB}$.

**(iii) NameNode Memory Estimate:**

$$ M_{NN} = 150 \text{ bytes/ block} \times 16{,}000{,}000 \text{ blocks} = 2{,}400{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} $$

$$ M_{NN} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^9}{1024 \times 1024 \times 1024} \text{ GB} \approx 2.24 \text{ GB (theoretical)} $$

> **Engineering Note:** Add JVM overhead, file inodes, and directory entries. Production provisioning: **at least 8–16 GB of NameNode RAM** for a cluster of this size. The 2.24 GB is the *raw per-block* footprint only.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Places Students Lose Marks on HDFS Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Secondary NameNode with a Standby NameNode.** The SNN is a **checkpoint helper**, not a hot standby. HA uses the **Standby NameNode** + ZooKeeper + QJM. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting that the NameNode does NOT handle data traffic.** Many students draw a write pipeline passing *through* the NameNode. Wrong. The NameNode is **control-plane only**; data flows **Client → DN1 → DN2 → DN3**.
> 3. **Using the wrong block size.** Pre-Hadoop-2 used 64 MB. Hadoop 2.x+ uses **128 MB**. Always state your assumption.
> 4. **Mis-stating the rack-awareness default.** The default is **3 replicas**: 1 on writer's node, 1 on different node same rack, 1 on a different rack. Not "all on different racks" (that wastes bandwidth) and not "all on same rack" (no fault tolerance).
> 5. **Forgetting the ceiling function in block count.** $600/128 = 4.6875$ → **5 blocks** (ceiling), not 4.7 or 4. Last block = 88 MB.
>
> **Bonus Pitfall:** In the heartbeat formula, the threshold is **10 missed heartbeats**, not 10 seconds. So failure detection time = 10 × 3 s = **30 s**, not just 10 s.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **HDFS — Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam)**

- [x] HDFS = **Hadoop Distributed File System**; master/slave, write-once-read-many, high throughput.
- [x] **Three daemons:** NameNode (master), DataNode (slave/worker), Secondary NameNode (checkpoint helper — **NOT a backup**).
- [x] **Default block size = 128 MB**; **Default replication factor = 3**.
- [x] **Heartbeat interval = 3 s**; **Failure detection threshold = 10 missed heartbeats (30 s)**.
- [x] **Block Report = every 6 hours.**
- [x] **Rack Awareness Default:** Replica 1 = local/same-rack node, Replica 2 = different node same rack, Replica 3 = different rack.
- [x] **Write Pipeline:** Client → DN1 → DN2 → DN3 (chain). ACKs flow backwards.
- [x] **Read Path:** Client gets block locations from NameNode, reads directly from nearest DataNode. **NameNode is not in data path.**
- [x] **NameNode memory formula:** $M_{NN} \approx 150 \text{ B} \times B_{total}$.
- [x] **Total physical storage:** $S_{total} = \text{LogicalSize} \times R$.
- [x] **Number of blocks:** $B_f = \lceil F / B_s \rceil$.
- [x] **HDFS is NOT for:** small files, low-latency random access, multiple writers to the same file.
- [x] **HDFS IS for:** batch analytics, log processing, data warehousing, write-once read-many workloads.
- [x] **High Availability (HA):** Active NameNode + Standby NameNode + ZooKeeper + Quorum Journal Manager (QJM) — replaces the SPOF of classic HDFS.
- [x] **fsimage** = metadata snapshot on disk; **edits** = metadata transaction log; SNN merges these periodically.
- [x] **Checksums:** DataNodes verify block integrity via checksums; corrupted blocks are re-replicated automatically.

> **Final KTU Tip:** When answering a 14-mark question on HDFS, always draw a **labelled diagram** (3–4 marks reserved for it). Mention the **numerical defaults** (128 MB, 3 replicas, 3 s heartbeat). State the **rack-awareness rule** explicitly. These three habits alone can move your grade from B to A+.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pig Latin High-Level Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pig Latin High-Level Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🐷 Pig Latin High-Level Languages — Module 3 Deep Dive

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pig Latin** is a **dataflow language** used to express data analysis operations on large, semi-structured, and unstructured datasets within the **Apache Pig** framework — a sub-project of the **Apache Hadoop ecosystem**. It is designed to bridge the gap between the low-level procedural MapReduce Java API and the declarative nature of SQL, enabling rapid development of **ETL (Extract, Transform, Load)**, ad-hoc analysis, and iterative data processing pipelines.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context for Cloud Computing (PEERT524), Pig Latin is studied under **Module 3: Cloud Programming & Data-Intensive Computing Models**, where it represents the **high-level abstraction layer** over the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS) and the MapReduce computation paradigm.

### 1.2 Core Architectural Position in the Hadoop Stack

Pig Latin sits **above** HDFS and **MapReduce** but **below** higher-level tools like Hive (which is SQL-based) and Spark (which uses Scala/Python). The compiler that converts Pig Latin scripts into MapReduce jobs is called the **Pig Engine** (or **Grunt Shell** in interactive mode).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary for KTU Board Exam:**
> - **Pig Engine** → converts Pig Latin statements into executable MapReduce jobs.
> - **Grunt** → the interactive JavaScript-like shell of Pig.
> - **Pig Latin Script (.pig file)** → a sequence of Pig Latin statements executed in batch mode.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory Assembly Line" Intuition

Imagine you own a **car manufacturing factory** (this is your Hadoop cluster):

| Factory Element | Pig Latin Equivalent | Real Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Raw materials arriving at the loading dock | `LOAD` statement | Data ingestion from HDFS |
| Quality inspection & sorting station | `FILTER`, `DISTINCT` | Removing bad rows, deduplication |
| Component assembly line | `FOREACH ... GENERATE` | Field-level transformations |
| Grouping similar parts into bins | `GROUP`, `COGROUP` | Aggregation by key |
| Combining parts from two different suppliers | `JOIN`, `CROSS` | Multi-dataset combination |
| Final packaging & dispatch | `STORE` or `DUMP` | Output to HDFS or screen |
| The Factory Manager | **Pig Engine** | Translates your wish-list (script) into shop-floor (MapReduce) tasks |

### 1.4 The Intuition in One Sentence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pig Latin = "Tell me WHAT data you want, not HOW to compute it."** It is a declarative-yet-procedural hybrid that lets you write **what** transformations to perform on data, while the Pig Engine automatically decides **how** to break them into Map and Reduce phases — the opposite philosophy of writing raw MapReduce Java code.

### 1.5 Why Pig Latin Was Invented (The Pain It Solves)

Writing raw MapReduce jobs in Java requires approximately **200 lines of code** for a simple Word Count program. The same logic in Pig Latin takes roughly **10 lines**. This **~95% reduction in code volume** is the central value proposition of Pig Latin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Highlighted in Bold:**
> - **~95% code reduction** compared to raw MapReduce Java.
> - **Multi-query optimization**: a single Pig script can chain multiple operations, optimizing across the entire pipeline (something naive MapReduce cannot do).
> - **Built-in UDF support** (User-Defined Functions) in Java, Python, JavaScript, Ruby, and Groovy.

---

## 1.6 Pig Latin Data Model — The Five Atomic Building Blocks

Pig Latin operates on a **nested relational data model**. Every data structure in Pig is built from the following primitives:

| # | Data Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Atom** | A simple atomic value (string, int, float, double, long, byte[], chararray) | `'Alice'`, `42`, `3.14` |
| 2 | **Tuple** | An ordered set of fields (like a database row) | `('Alice', 42, 3.14)` |
| 3 | **Bag** | An **unordered** collection of tuples (allows duplicates) | `{('Alice', 42), ('Bob', 33)}` |
| 4 | **Map** | A set of key-value pairs (keys must be chararray) | `['name'#'Alice', 'age'#'42']` |
| 5 | **Relation** | The outermost bag — the "table" in Pig | A bag of tuples with a schema |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schema in Pig is OPTIONAL.** Unlike SQL, where every table must have a fixed schema, Pig Latin supports **schema-on-read** — you can process data without pre-defining its schema. This is crucial for handling semi-structured and unstructured cloud data (logs, JSON, clickstreams).

### 1.7 Pig Latin Statement Structure — The Anatomy

Every Pig Latin statement follows a strict grammatical pattern:

$$ \text{Statement} \rightarrow \text{Alias} = \text{Operator} \text{ ( Input\_Relations ) } [ \text{Schema} ] ; $$

- **Alias** → a logical name given to the result (like a variable).
- **Operator** → the action verb (LOAD, FILTER, JOIN, etc.).
- **Input_Relations** → the source data references.
- **Schema** → optional type declaration.
- Semicolon `;` → mandatory terminator.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The Two Execution Modes of Pig Latin

Pig Latin scripts can be executed in **two distinct modes**, and understanding this distinction is a guaranteed **2-mark KTU question**:

| Feature | **Local Mode** | **MapReduce Mode (Hadoop Mode)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Execution Engine** | Single JVM (local Java process) | Full Hadoop cluster via MapReduce |
| **Data Source** | Local filesystem (`/home/user/`) | HDFS (`hdfs://namenode:9000/`) |
| **Use Case** | Development, debugging, small datasets | Production-scale big data processing |
| **Command** | `pig -x local script.pig` | `pig script.pig` (default) |
| **Parallelism** | None (single thread) | Distributed across cluster nodes |
| **Performance** | Slow for large data | Scalable to petabytes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Examiners often ask: *"What happens internally when you execute a Pig Latin script in MapReduce mode?"* The answer is: the **Pig Compiler** parses the script → generates a **logical plan** → optimizes it → converts to a **physical MapReduce plan** → submits it as **one or more MapReduce jobs** to the Hadoop YARN resource manager.

---

## 2.2 The Complete Operator Taxonomy (High-Yield for KTU)

Pig Latin operators fall into **six functional categories**. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasizes the following groups:

### 📥 Category 1 — Loading & Storing Operators

| Operator | Syntax Pattern | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `LOAD` | `alias = LOAD 'path' [USING PigStorage(',')] AS (schema);` | Read data into Pig |
| `STORE` | `STORE alias INTO 'path' [USING PigStorage()];` | Write data to filesystem |
| `DUMP` | `DUMP alias;` | Print to console (debug only) |

### 🔍 Category 2 — Diagnostic & Filtering Operators

| Operator | Syntax Pattern | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `FILTER` | `X = FILTER A BY condition;` | Row-level filtering (WHERE in SQL) |
| `DISTINCT` | `X = DISTINCT A;` | Remove duplicate tuples |
| `SAMPLE` | `X = SAMPLE A 0.1;` | Random sampling (10%) |
| `LIMIT` | `X = LIMIT A n;` | Take first n rows |
| `ORDER BY` | `X = ORDER A BY col [ASC/DESC];` | Sort (note: single sort key only) |

### 🔄 Category 3 — Transformation Operators (The Core of Pig Latin)

| Operator | Syntax Pattern | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `FOREACH ... GENERATE` | `X = FOREACH A GENERATE field1, expr2;` | Column projection / transformation |
| `MAPREDUCE` | `X = MAPREDUCE 'jar' LOAD ... STORE ...;` | Embed raw MapReduce jar |
| `STREAM` | `X = STREAM A THROUGH 'script';` | Call external script (Python, Perl) |
| `NESTED FOREACH` | `X = FOREACH B { S1 = FILTER A BY ...; GENERATE group, S1; }` | Nested row processing |

### 🤝 Category 4 — Joining & Combining Operators

| Operator | Syntax Pattern | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `JOIN` (inner) | `X = JOIN A BY col1, B BY col2;` | Inner join |
| `LEFT OUTER JOIN` | `X = JOIN A BY col LEFT, B BY col;` | Left outer |
| `RIGHT OUTER JOIN` | `X = JOIN A BY col RIGHT, B BY col;` | Right outer |
| `FULL OUTER JOIN` | `X = JOIN A BY col FULL, B BY col;` | Full outer |
| `COGROUP` | `X = COGROUP A BY col1, B BY col2;` | Group both inputs (outer join + group) |
| `CROSS` | `X = CROSS A, B;` | Cartesian product |
| `UNION` | `X = UNION A, B;` | Bag concatenation |

### 📊 Category 5 — Grouping & Aggregation Operators

| Operator | Syntax Pattern | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `GROUP` | `X = GROUP A BY col;` | Group rows by key |
| `GROUP ALL` | `X = GROUP A ALL;` | Single group (for global aggregates) |
| Built-in Aggregations | `COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`, `TOKENIZE`, `SIZE`, `CONCAT` | Applied inside FOREACH |

### 📐 Category 6 — Schema & Utility Operators

| Operator | Syntax Pattern | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `DESCRIBE` | `DESCRIBE alias;` | Show schema |
| `EXPLAIN` | `EXPLAIN alias;` | Show execution plan (logical + physical + MapReduce) |
| `ILLUSTRATE` | `ILLUSTRATE alias;` | Show sample data execution |
| `REGISTER` | `REGISTER 'path/to/udf.jar';` | Load UDF library |
| `DEFINE` | `DEFINE myFunc my.package.MyUDF();` | Alias for UDF |
| `SPLIT` | `SPLIT A INTO B IF cond1, C IF cond2;` | Conditional branching |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Pig Latin Quick Reference

> [!NOTE]
> The table below condenses the **entire Pig Latin vocabulary** that a KTU 2024 student must memorize. The "Marks Weightage" column is an approximate guide based on previous years' question paper patterns.

| Operator / Concept | Syntax Skeleton | Category | Marks Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| `LOAD` | `X = LOAD 'file' AS (s:int, n:chararray);` | Load | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `FILTER BY` | `Y = FILTER X BY s > 50;` | Diagnostic | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `FOREACH ... GENERATE` | `Z = FOREACH Y GENERATE n, s*2;` | Transform | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `GROUP BY` | `G = GROUP Y BY n;` | Aggregation | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `JOIN` | `J = JOIN X BY id, Y BY id;` | Join | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `ORDER BY` | `O = ORDER X BY s DESC;` | Sort | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `DISTINCT` | `D = DISTINCT X;` | Diagnostic | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `COGROUP` | `C = COGROUP X BY k1, Y BY k2;` | Combine | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `STORE` / `DUMP` | `STORE Z INTO '/out';` / `DUMP Z;` | Output | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `UNION` | `U = UNION A, B;` | Combine | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| `SPLIT` | `SPLIT X INTO A IF c1, B IF c2;` | Utility | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| `SAMPLE` | `S = SAMPLE X 0.1;` | Diagnostic | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| `LIMIT` | `L = LIMIT X 100;` | Diagnostic | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| `CROSS` | `C = CROSS A, B;` | Join | ⭐⭐ |
| `FLATTEN` | `F = FOREACH X GENERATE FLATTEN(group);` | Transform | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `UDF Register` | `REGISTER 'udf.jar';` | Utility | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `EXPLAIN` | `EXPLAIN X;` | Diagnostic | ⭐⭐ |
| `Local vs MR Mode` | `pig -x local` vs `pig` | Execution | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

### 2.4 Built-In Aggregation Functions (Cheat Sheet)

| Function | Input | Output | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `COUNT(A)` | Bag | Long | `COUNT(B)` → number of tuples |
| `SUM(A)` | Bag | Double/Long/Int | `SUM(B.salary)` |
| `AVG(A)` | Bag | Double | `AVG(B.marks)` |
| `MIN(A)` | Bag | Same as input | `MIN(B.age)` |
| `MAX(A)` | Bag | Same as input | `MAX(B.score)` |
| `TOKENIZE(A)` | Chararray | Bag of tokens | `TOKENIZE(line)` |
| `SIZE(A)` | Any | Int | `SIZE(bag1)` |
| `CONCAT(A, B)` | Chararray × 2 | Chararray | `CONCAT(first, last)` |
| `IsEmpty(A)` | Bag | Boolean | `IsEmpty(B)` |
| `DIFF(A, B)` | Two bags | Bag | `DIFF(A, B)` |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pig Latin is used industrially in:
> 1. **Log Analytics** — Twitter processes **billions of tweets daily** using Pig for trend detection.
> 2. **ETL Pipelines** — Yahoo! famously ran **30+ Pig scripts daily** on 100,000+ CPUs to power its ad-targeting.
> 3. **Clickstream Analysis** — E-commerce companies analyze user navigation paths.
> 4. **Sensor Data Processing** — IoT pipelines ingesting telemetry from millions of devices.
> 5. **Recommendation Pre-processing** — Joining user profiles with item catalogs before feeding ML models.

The **multi-query optimization** feature of Pig is its biggest industrial advantage — when you chain `LOAD → FILTER → JOIN → GROUP → STORE` in a single script, the optimizer fuses operations to **minimize the number of MapReduce jobs** (often from 5 to just 1 or 2), saving massive I/O overhead.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example #1 — Word Count in Pig Latin vs MapReduce

### Problem Statement

Given a text file `input.txt` stored on HDFS at `/data/input.txt`, count the frequency of each word.

---

### 🐢 Solution in Raw MapReduce (Java) — For Contrast

The standard MapReduce WordCount requires:
1. A `Mapper` class (~30 lines) that tokenizes lines and emits `(word, 1)`.
2. A `Reducer` class (~25 lines) that sums up counts.
3. A `Driver` class (~30 lines) with `Job` configuration.
4. **Total: ~85–100 lines of Java + XML config.**

---

### 🐷 Step-by-Step Solution in Pig Latin (4 lines)

**Step 1: Load the input data as a bag of lines (chararray tuples).**

$$
\text{lines} = \text{LOAD} \; \text{'/data/input.txt'} \; \text{AS} \; (\text{line} : \text{chararray});
$$

**Step 2: Tokenize each line into a bag of words using the built-in TOKENIZE function.**

$$
\text{words} = \text{FOREACH} \; \text{lines} \; \text{GENERATE} \; \text{FLATTEN}(\text{TOKENIZE}(\text{line}));
$$

**Explanation of logic:**
- `TOKENIZE(line)` splits the line into a bag: `{('hello'), ('world'), ('pig')}`. It defaults to splitting on whitespace and discards empty tokens.
- `FLATTEN()` **un-nests** the bag, producing one row per word. So `{('hello'), ('world')}` becomes two rows: `('hello')` and `('world')`.

**Step 3: Group all identical words together.**

$$
\text{grouped} = \text{GROUP} \; \text{words} \; \text{BY} \; \text{word};
$$

**Explanation:** After this step, each tuple becomes `(word, {(word1), (word2), ...})` — a key with a bag of all matching values.

**Step 4: Count the size of each inner bag to get the word frequency.**

$$
\text{counts} = \text{FOREACH} \; \text{grouped} \; \text{GENERATE} \; \text{group} \; \text{AS} \; \text{word}, \; \text{COUNT}(\text{words}) \; \text{AS} \; \text{frequency};
$$

**Step 5: Store the result back to HDFS.**

$$
\text{STORE} \; \text{counts} \; \text{INTO} \; \text{'/data/output\_wc'};
$$

---

### 🔢 Final Complete Script (Concatenated)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{lines}   = \text{LOAD} \; \text{'/data/input.txt'} \; \text{AS} \; (\text{line:chararray}); \\
&\text{words}   = \text{FOREACH} \; \text{lines} \; \text{GENERATE} \; \text{FLATTEN}(\text{TOKENIZE(line)}); \\
&\text{grp}     = \text{GROUP} \; \text{words} \; \text{BY} \; \text{word}; \\
&\text{counts}  = \text{FOREACH} \; \text{grp} \; \text{GENERATE} \; \text{group} \; \text{AS} \; \text{word}, \; \text{COUNT(words)} \; \text{AS} \; \text{freq}; \\
&\text{STORE} \; \text{counts} \; \text{INTO} \; \text{'/data/output\_wc'};
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Lines: 5** vs **~100 in Java** → ~95% reduction.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example #2 — Student Performance Analysis Pipeline

### Problem Statement

Given two files in HDFS:
- `students.txt` → columns: `id, name, dept` (comma-separated)
- `marks.txt` → columns: `id, subject, score` (comma-separated)

**Tasks:**
1. Load both files with explicit schemas.
2. Join them on `id`.
3. Filter only students with score > 70.
4. Group by department.
5. Compute the average score per department.
6. Store the result ordered by average score descending.

---

### 🔍 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Load `students.txt` with schema.**

$$
\text{students} = \text{LOAD} \; \text{'/data/students.txt'} \; \text{USING PigStorage(',')} \; \text{AS} \; (\text{id:int}, \; \text{name:chararray}, \; \text{dept:chararray});
$$

**Step 2: Load `marks.txt` with schema.**

$$
\text{marks} = \text{LOAD} \; \text{'/data/marks.txt'} \; \text{USING PigStorage(',')} \; \text{AS} \; (\text{id:int}, \; \text{subject:chararray}, \; \text{score:int});
$$

**Step 3: Inner join on `id`.**

$$
\text{joined} = \text{JOIN} \; \text{students} \; \text{BY} \; \text{id}, \; \text{marks} \; \text{BY} \; \text{id};
$$

**After join, schema is:** `(id, name, dept, id, subject, score)` — note the duplicate `id` field from both relations.

**Step 4: Filter score > 70.**

$$
\text{passed} = \text{FILTER} \; \text{joined} \; \text{BY} \; \text{score} > 70;
$$

**Step 5: Project only relevant fields to clean schema.**

$$
\text{clean} = \text{FOREACH} \; \text{passed} \; \text{GENERATE} \; \text{students::name} \; \text{AS} \; \text{name}, \; \text{dept}, \; \text{subject}, \; \text{score};
$$

**Explanation:** Using `students::name` resolves the ambiguity since both relations might have a `name` field (here only students does, but the namespace qualifier is good practice).

**Step 6: Group by department.**

$$
\text{by\_dept} = \text{GROUP} \; \text{clean} \; \text{BY} \; \text{dept};
$$

**Step 7: Compute average score per department.**

$$
\text{avg\_by\_dept} = \text{FOREACH} \; \text{by\_dept} \; \text{GENERATE} \; \text{group} \; \text{AS} \; \text{department}, \; \text{AVG(clean.score)} \; \text{AS} \; \text{avg\_score};
$$

**Step 8: Sort descending by average score.**

$$
\text{ranked} = \text{ORDER} \; \text{avg\_by\_dept} \; \text{BY} \; \text{avg\_score} \; \text{DESC};
$$

**Step 9: Store output.**

$$
\text{STORE} \; \text{ranked} \; \text{INTO} \; \text{'/data/dept\_ranking'} \; \text{USING PigStorage(',')};
$$

---

### 📊 Analytical Matrix — Operator Selection Cheat Sheet

| If you need to… | Use this operator | SQL Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Read a file | `LOAD ... AS (schema)` | `FROM table` |
| Write a file | `STORE ... INTO 'path'` | `INSERT INTO` |
| Print to screen | `DUMP` | `SELECT` (debug) |
| Filter rows | `FILTER ... BY` | `WHERE` |
| Pick columns | `FOREACH ... GENERATE` | `SELECT col1, col2` |
| Group rows | `GROUP ... BY` | `GROUP BY` |
| Sort rows | `ORDER ... BY` | `ORDER BY` |
| Combine datasets (vertical) | `UNION` | `UNION ALL` |
| Combine datasets (horizontal) | `JOIN` | `JOIN` |
| Group both sides | `COGROUP` | No direct equivalent |
| Cartesian product | `CROSS` | `CROSS JOIN` |
| Remove duplicates | `DISTINCT` | `DISTINCT` |
| Random sample | `SAMPLE 0.1` | `TABLESAMPLE` |
| Top-N rows | `LIMIT n` | `LIMIT n` / `TOP` |
| Compute count | `COUNT(bag)` | `COUNT(*)` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🏗️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pig Latin Execution Pipeline — End-to-End Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how a `.pig` script is transformed all the way from source code to executed MapReduce jobs on a Hadoop cluster.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["A: Pig Latin Script File .pig"] --> B["B: Grunt Interactive Shell or Pig Batch Mode"]
    B --> C["C: Pig Compiler Parser"]
    C --> D["D: Logical Plan DAG"]
    D --> E["E: Logical Optimizer"]
    E --> F["F: Physical Plan"]
    F --> G["G: MapReduce Job Generator"]
    G --> H["H: YARN Resource Manager Submit"]
    H --> I["I: HDFS DataNodes Storage Layer"]
    H --> J["J: Map Phase Mappers"]
    J --> K["K: Shuffle and Sort Phase"]
    K --> L["L: Reduce Phase Reducers"]
    L --> M["M: Output to HDFS or DUMP to Console"]
    M --> N["N: Final Result Relation"]
```

### 🔍 Stage-by-Stage Explanation

- **Stage A→B**: The user submits a `.pig` file either via the `grunt` shell (interactive REPL) or via `pig script.pig` (batch mode).
- **Stage B→C**: The **Parser** validates syntax and builds a **Parse Tree** (Abstract Syntax Tree).
- **Stage C→D**: The parse tree is converted into a **Logical Plan** — a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) of logical operators.
- **Stage D→E**: The **Logical Optimizer** applies rules like *predicate pushdown*, *projection pushdown*, and *combiner inlining*.
- **Stage E→F**: Logical plan → **Physical Plan** (e.g., `POJoin`, `POMergeJoin`, `POMapOnly`).
- **Stage F→G**: Each physical operator is converted to a **MapReduce Job**.
- **Stage G→H**: Jobs are submitted to **YARN** (Yet Another Resource Negotiator).
- **Stage H→L**: YARN schedules **Mapper** and **Reducer** tasks across cluster nodes.
- **Stage L→N**: Reduced output is written back to HDFS or displayed via `DUMP`.

---

## 4.2 Pig Latin Data Model — Nested Relational Hierarchy

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **containment hierarchy** of Pig Latin data types.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["R: Relation outermost bag with schema"] --> B["B: Bag unordered collection allows duplicates"]
    B --> T["T: Tuple ordered set of fields"]
    T --> F1["F1: Atom primitive value"]
    T --> F2["F2: Tuple nested tuple"]
    T --> F3["F3: Map set of key value pairs"]
    T --> F4["F4: Bag nested bag"]
    F1 --> S["S: Atom can be chararray int long float double bytearray"]
    F3 --> K1["K1: key must be chararray"]
    F3 --> V1["V1: value can be any pig type"]
```

---

## 4.3 Pig Latin Statement Flow — Logical Data Pipeline

The following block diagram shows how data **flows** through a typical multi-stage Pig Latin pipeline (matching the Worked Example #2 above).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["S1: LOAD students.txt schema id name dept"] --> J["J: JOIN on id"]
    S2["S2: LOAD marks.txt schema id subject score"] --> J
    J --> F["F: FILTER score greater than 70"]
    F --> C["C: FOREACH project name dept subject score"]
    C --> G["G: GROUP BY department"]
    G --> A["A: FOREACH AVG score per group"]
    A --> O["O: ORDER BY avg score DESC"]
    O --> ST["ST: STORE into dept ranking path"]
    O --> DU["DU: DUMP for verification optional"]
```

---

## 4.4 Local vs MapReduce Mode — Comparative Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LM["LM: Local Mode Single JVM Process"]
        L1["L1: User script.pig"] --> L2["L2: pig x local script.pig"]
        L2 --> L3["L3: Pig Engine in process"]
        L3 --> L4["L4: Read from local filesystem"]
        L4 --> L5["L5: No HDFS involvement"]
        L5 --> L6["L6: Output to local or DUMP"]
    end
    subgraph MR["MR: MapReduce Mode Hadoop Cluster"]
        M1["M1: User script.pig"] --> M2["M2: pig script.pig default"]
        M2 --> M3["M3: Pig Engine submits to YARN"]
        M3 --> M4["M4: HDFS distributed read"]
        M4 --> M5["M5: Mappers across DataNodes"]
        M5 --> M6["M6: Reducers aggregate"]
        M6 --> M7["M7: Write back to HDFS"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Tip for KTU Lab Exams:** Always test your script in **Local Mode first** (`pig -x local`) using a small sample file. This catches 90% of syntax and logic errors in seconds rather than waiting minutes for cluster execution.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Pig Latin. List any four Pig Latin data types with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Pig Latin** is a high-level, data-flow oriented scripting language used in the Apache Pig platform to analyze large, semi-structured datasets in Hadoop. It abstracts the underlying MapReduce complexity, allowing users to write transformations without writing Java code.

**Four Pig Latin Data Types:**

1. **Atom** → A single, simple value such as a string or integer (e.g., `'Alice'`, `42`).
2. **Tuple** → An ordered sequence of fields forming a record (e.g., `('Alice', 42)`).
3. **Bag** → An unordered collection of tuples, the outermost container of a relation (e.g., `{('Alice', 42), ('Bob', 33)}`).
4. **Map** → A set of key-value pairs where keys are character arrays (e.g., `['name'#'Alice']`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scoring Note:** Mentioning **Relation** as the fifth type with "outermost bag with schema" earns a bonus mark.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between Local Mode and MapReduce Mode of Apache Pig execution.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Local Mode | MapReduce Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Command | `pig -x local script.pig` | `pig script.pig` |
| Execution Environment | Single JVM on local machine | Distributed Hadoop cluster |
| Filesystem | Local disk | HDFS |
| Speed | Fast for small data | Scales for big data |
| Use Case | Development & testing | Production workloads |

**Key Distinction:** In Local Mode, all MapReduce jobs are simulated within one process; in MapReduce Mode, actual distributed Mappers and Reducers run across cluster nodes coordinated by YARN.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### 🔹 Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a) Explain the architecture of Apache Pig with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the Pig Compiler and Optimizer in detail.** **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b) Write a Pig Latin script to perform the following on a sales dataset stored in HDFS at `/data/sales.csv` with columns `(txn_id:int, product:chararray, category:chararray, amount:double, region:chararray)`:**
1. Load the data with proper schema.
2. Filter transactions where `amount > 1000`.
3. Group by `region` and compute the total sales per region.
4. Sort regions by total sales in descending order and store the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

**Apache Pig Architecture — Major Components:**

1. **Parser** → Performs syntactic and semantic validation. Builds a parse tree from Pig Latin statements. Errors here produce immediate compilation errors (e.g., `Syntax error at FILTER`).

2. **Optimizer (Logical Plan Optimizer)** → Applies transformation rules such as:
   - **Predicate Pushdown** → moves `FILTER` operators as close to the `LOAD` as possible, reducing I/O.
   - **Projection Pushdown** → eliminates unused columns early.
   - **Map-Side Aggregation** → enables combiners in MapReduce.
   - **Join Reordering** → reorders joins for efficiency.

3. **Compiler** → Converts the optimized logical plan into a **physical plan** consisting of physical operators (e.g., `POLoad`, `POFilter`, `POJoin`).

4. **Execution Engine** → Submits the physical plan as one or more **MapReduce jobs** to the Hadoop cluster via YARN.

5. **Grunt Shell** → Interactive JavaScript-like shell for ad-hoc queries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Role of Optimizer:** It is the **brain** of Pig. Without optimization, a 5-step script would generate 5 sequential MapReduce jobs. With optimization, it often fuses them into 1–2 jobs — a **5× I/O reduction** in production.

**Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.1):** The execution pipeline Parser → Logical Plan → Optimizer → Physical Plan → MapReduce Job Generator → YARN.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

**Complete Pig Latin Script with Explanations:**

```pig
-- Step 1: Load sales.csv with explicit schema
sales = LOAD '/data/sales.csv' USING PigStorage(',') 
        AS (txn_id:int, product:chararray, category:chararray, 
            amount:double, region:chararray);

-- Step 2: Filter transactions where amount > 1000
high_value = FILTER sales BY amount > 1000;

-- Step 3: Group by region
grouped = GROUP high_value BY region;

-- Step 4: Compute total sales per region using SUM aggregation
totals = FOREACH grouped GENERATE 
            group AS region, 
            SUM(high_value.amount) AS total_sales;

-- Step 5: Sort descending by total sales
ranked = ORDER totals BY total_sales DESC;

-- Step 6: Store the result back to HDFS
STORE ranked INTO '/data/region_sales_ranking' USING PigStorage(',');

-- Optional: Verify with DUMP
-- DUMP ranked;
```

**Output Schema:** `(region:chararray, total_sales:double)` sorted from highest to lowest revenue.

---

### 🔹 Question B (Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: (a) Explain the Pig Latin data model in detail with examples for Atom, Tuple, Bag, and Map. Why does Pig Latin support optional schema?** **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b) Consider two datasets:**
- `employees.txt` → `(emp_id:int, name:chararray, dept_id:int)`
- `departments.txt` → `(dept_id:int, dept_name:chararray, location:chararray)`

**Write a Pig Latin script to:**
1. Load both files with schemas.
2. Perform an inner join on `dept_id`.
3. Filter employees in the 'CSE' department.
4. Group by `location` and count employees per location.
5. Store the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

**Pig Latin Data Model — Detailed Explanation:**

The data model is a **nested relational model**, meaning data can contain other data (like bags inside tuples inside bags). This is fundamentally different from SQL's **flat relational model**.

| Type | Syntax Example | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| Atom | `42`, `'hello'`, `3.14` | The simplest value; no internal structure |
| Tuple | `('Alice', 42, 'CSE')` | A fixed-order record of fields; positional |
| Bag | `{('Alice', 42), ('Bob', 33)}` | Unordered collection of tuples; the schema of a Relation |
| Map | `['name'#'Alice', 'age'#'42']` | Key-value pairs; keys must be chararray |

**Why Optional Schema?**
1. **Schema-on-Read Flexibility** — Real-world big data (logs, JSON, tweets) is often semi-structured. Forcing a schema upfront would require expensive ETL.
2. **Rapid Prototyping** — Analysts can explore unknown datasets without pre-commitment.
3. **Late Binding** — The schema is enforced only when the data is actually read, allowing schema evolution.
4. **Nested Data Support** — Unlike SQL's flat rows, Pig bags naturally represent nested JSON/XML structures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** When schema is omitted in `LOAD`, all fields default to **bytearray** (opaque). You can later **cast** them using `(int)field` syntax or apply schema via `AS` clauses downstream.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

```pig
-- Step 1: Load employees
employees = LOAD '/data/employees.txt' USING PigStorage(',') 
            AS (emp_id:int, name:chararray, dept_id:int);

-- Step 2: Load departments
departments = LOAD '/data/departments.txt' USING PigStorage(',') 
              AS (dept_id:int, dept_name:chararray, location:chararray);

-- Step 3: Inner join on dept_id
joined = JOIN employees BY dept_id, departments BY dept_id;

-- Step 4: Filter CSE department only
cse_emp = FILTER joined BY dept_name == 'CSE';

-- Step 5: Group by location
grouped_loc = GROUP cse_emp BY location;

-- Step 6: Count employees per location
loc_count = FOREACH grouped_loc GENERATE 
                group AS location, 
                COUNT(cse_emp.emp_id) AS emp_count;

-- Step 7: Store result
STORE loc_count INTO '/data/cse_location_count' USING PigStorage(',');
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on String Comparison:** Pig Latin uses `==` for equality (not SQL's `=`). Strings are compared **case-sensitively**. To do case-insensitive matching, use `LOWER(field) == 'cse'` or a regex `MATCHES '.*cse.*'`.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically LOSE MARKS in Pig Latin questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the Semicolon `;`** at the end of each statement → causes parser failure (-2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing `==` (Pig equality) with `=` (SQL assignment)** — using `=` for comparison gives a syntax error.
> 3. **Incorrect namespace qualification after JOIN** → using `name` instead of `students::name` when both relations have the same field name (-1 mark per occurrence).
> 4. **Using `LIMIT` before `ORDER BY`** — order of operations matters; `LIMIT` must come **after** sorting, otherwise results are non-deterministic.
> 5. **Not flattening after TOKENIZE** — `TOKENIZE` returns a bag; without `FLATTEN`, you get a nested bag of bags (a common bug in WordCount-style questions).
> 6. **Confusing `UNION` with `UNION ALL`** — Pig's `UNION` is actually `UNION ALL` (no deduplication). To deduplicate, you must apply `DISTINCT` separately.
> 7. **Misusing `COGROUP` vs `GROUP`** — `GROUP` acts on one relation; `COGROUP` groups multiple relations side-by-side (similar to outer join + group).

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU)

- ✅ Pig Latin is the **language**, **Apache Pig** is the platform.
- ✅ It is a **dataflow language** (not procedural, not purely declarative — a hybrid).
- ✅ Compiled into **MapReduce jobs** by the **Pig Engine**.
- ✅ Two execution modes: **Local** (single JVM) and **MapReduce / Hadoop Mode** (distributed).
- ✅ Five data types: **Atom, Tuple, Bag, Map, Relation** — with **Bag** being the outermost.
- ✅ Schema is **optional** (schema-on-read philosophy).
- ✅ Statements end with **semicolon `;`** (mandatory).
- ✅ **Equality operator is `==`**, not `=`.
- ✅ `LOAD ... AS (schema)` → `FILTER ... BY cond` → `FOREACH ... GENERATE` → `GROUP ... BY` → `STORE ... INTO` is the **canonical pipeline**.
- ✅ `TOKENIZE` needs `FLATTEN` to produce one row per token.
- ✅ `UNION` in Pig ≈ `UNION ALL` in SQL (does not deduplicate).
- ✅ `ORDER BY` takes **only one key** in Pig Latin (use nested sort via UDF for multi-key).
- ✅ `JOIN` syntax: `JOIN A BY x, B BY y` — note the comma between key pairs.
- ✅ After `GROUP`, the grouping column becomes `group` (a reserved alias).
- ✅ After `JOIN`, use `relation_name::field_name` to disambiguate duplicate column names.
- ✅ `EXPLAIN alias` shows logical + physical + MapReduce plan (great debugging tool).
- ✅ `REGISTER 'udf.jar'` + `DEFINE myFunc my.package.MyClass()` for **User-Defined Functions**.
- ✅ **95% code reduction** vs raw MapReduce Java is the headline advantage.
- ✅ **Multi-query optimization** chains operations to minimize I/O.
- ✅ Pig Latin supports **nested FOREACH** for complex per-group processing.
- ✅ Industrial users: **Yahoo, Twitter, LinkedIn, Netflix** (for ETL and ad analytics).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked *"Compare Pig Latin with SQL"*, always mention these **3 differences**:
> 1. **Schema is optional** in Pig Latin, mandatory in SQL.
> 2. Pig Latin supports **nested data** (bags of bags), SQL requires joins and normalization.
> 3. Pig Latin has **procedural pipeline** style (step-by-step), SQL is **declarative** (one big query).

---

### 📚 Quick Reference Summary Table — Pig Latin vs MapReduce vs SQL

| Feature | Pig Latin | MapReduce (Java) | SQL |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lines of Code (WordCount) | ~5 | ~100 | ~3 |
| Schema | Optional | Not enforced | Mandatory |
| Data Model | Nested relational | Key-value pairs | Flat relational |
| Procedural/Declarative | Hybrid procedural | Imperative | Declarative |
| Learning Curve | Low | High | Low |
| Optimization | Automatic | Manual | Automatic (query planner) |
| UDF Support | Yes (Java, Python, JS) | Yes (Java only) | Limited (stored procs) |
| Execution Engine | MapReduce / Tez / Spark | MapReduce only | RDBMS engine |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
