### 1.5 Physical / Standard Metrics

- **Default Root Volume Size for Amazon Linux 2 AMI:** **8 GiB** (General Purpose SSD `gp2`).
- **Maximum EBS Volume Size:** **64 TiB** per volume.
- **Maximum Number of Block Devices per Instance:** Determined by instance type (e.g., `m5.large` supports up to **27 EBS volumes** including the root).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an AMI — Structured Decomposition

### Step 1 — The Root Volume Template
The root volume template is essentially a **snapshot of the boot disk**. When the EC2 instance boots, the root volume is hydrated from this template. There are two storage backends:

1. **EBS-Backed AMI (Recommended)**
   - Root volume is an **EBS snapshot**.
   - Supports **Stop / Start**, **Reboot**, and **Termination** lifecycle operations.
   - Boot time: typically **under 1 minute** (warm EBS volume).
   - Data persists independently of instance lifecycle.

2. **Instance Store-Backed AMI (Legacy / S3-backed)**
   - Root volume is stored as a **template in Amazon S3**.
   - **Cannot be stopped** — only *rebooted* or *terminated*.
   - Data is **ephemeral** — lost on termination, hardware failure, or stop.
   - Boot time: typically **under 5 minutes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** In the 2024 Scheme, AWS has effectively deprecated instance store-backed AMIs for general use. If a KTU question asks *"compare the two types,"* always emphasize that **EBS-backed is the modern default** and instance store is for specialized, high-I/O, ephemeral workloads only.

### Step 2 — Launch Permissions
Launch permissions form the **access control layer** of the AMI.

- **Public:** Any AWS account worldwide can launch the AMI (requires explicit `public` flag).
- **Explicit Launch Permission:** Granted to specific AWS account numbers via `ModifyImageAttribute` API.
- **Shared via AWS RAM:** Shared across accounts within an AWS Organization.

### Step 3 — Block Device Mapping
This is the **storage orchestration plan**. It specifies:

- The root device (`/dev/sda1` or `/dev/xvda` for Linux).
- Additional EBS volumes to attach (`/dev/sdb`, `/dev/sdc`, …).
- Whether volumes should be **deleted on instance termination**.
- The **size, type (`gp3`, `io1`, `st1`, `sc1`), and IOPS/throughput** configuration at launch time.

## 2.2 AMI Lifecycle States

| State | Description | API Call |
|-------|-------------|----------|
| **Pending** | AMI is being created; not yet available | `CreateImage` (in progress) |
| **Available** | AMI is ready for launching instances | `CreateImage` (complete) |
| **Deregistered** | AMI removed from service; existing instances unaffected | `DeregisterImage` |

## 2.3 Types of AMIs

1. **AWS Quick Start AMIs** — Published by AWS (e.g., Amazon Linux 2, Ubuntu LTS, Windows Server). Free to use, pay only for instance.
2. **AWS Marketplace AMIs** — Third-party pre-configured software (e.g., databases, ML frameworks). Charged **per hour** in addition to EC2.
3. **Community AMIs** — Shared publicly by other AWS users. Use with caution — no SLAs.
4. **Custom AMIs** — Created by *you* from a configured instance. Fully owned and controlled.

## 2.4 Virtualization Types

| Type | Full Name | Use Case | Boot Mode Compatibility |
|------|-----------|----------|------------------------|
| `hvm` | **Hardware Virtual Machine** | Modern workloads; required for GPU instances; uses hardware-assisted virtualization | UEFI + Legacy BIOS |
| `paravirtual` | **PV** (legacy) | Older instance types; uses special PV drivers | Legacy BIOS only |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** For any instance launched **after 2016** (i.e., virtually all current-gen instance families like `t3`, `m5`, `c5`, `r5`), the virtualization type is **`hvm`**. PV is effectively historical knowledge.

## 2.5 AMI Creation Workflow — The "Why" Behind Each Step

1. **Launch a base instance** from an existing public or custom AMI.
2. **Connect via SSH/RDP** and customize: install packages, configure security, copy data, run hardening scripts.
3. **Snapshot the root EBS volume** (or bundle for instance store).
4. **Register the snapshot as a new AMI** using `aws ec2 register-image` (or console → *Actions → Image and templates → Create image*).
5. **Test the new AMI** by launching a fresh instance from it.
6. **Tag, share, or copy** as needed.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Default Amazon Linux 2 root volume | **8 GiB** | gp2 / gp3 |
| 2 | Max EBS volume size | **64 TiB** | Per single volume |
| 3 | Max IOPS per gp3 volume | **16,000** IOPS baseline | Provisioned independently |
| 4 | Max throughput per gp3 volume | **1,000 MiB/s** | Provisioned independently |
| 5 | Boot time — EBS-backed | **< 1 min** | After first launch |
| 6 | Boot time — Instance store-backed | **< 5 min** | S3 hydration |
| 7 | AMI region scope | **1 region only** | Copy required for cross-region |
| 8 | Stop / Start supported | **Only EBS-backed** | Instance store: Reboot / Terminate only |
| 9 | Terminate protection | Depends on `DeleteOnTermination` flag in **block device mapping** | Boolean per volume |
| 10 | Deregister AMI | Does **NOT** delete the underlying EBS snapshot | Snapshots persist; billable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Custom AMIs are the cornerstone of **Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC)** pipelines. Tools like **Packer** (HashiCorp), **EC2 Image Builder** (AWS-native), and **Terraform `aws_ami` data sources** all revolve around automated AMI creation. In enterprise DevOps, the "Golden Image" pattern — a hardened, patched, security-scanned base AMI — is a **mandatory compliance artifact** for PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and SOC 2 environments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Cost of an AMI-Based Deployment

While AMI itself is **free** (no charge for registering/deregistering), the cost structure is derived from the **storage of underlying snapshots** and the **running instance hours**. Let us derive the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** for a multi-instance deployment using a custom AMI.

### Given Parameters

- **N** = number of EC2 instances launched from the AMI
- **S_ami** = size of the AMI's root snapshot in **GiB**
- **S_data** = size of attached data EBS volume in **GiB** (per instance)
- **H** = total running hours per month
- **C_inst** = instance-hour price (region/instance-type specific)
- **C_ebs** = EBS **gp3** price = **$0.08 per GiB-month**
- **C_snap** = EBS Snapshot price = **$0.05 per GiB-month**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the cost components.**
A running EC2 deployment based on a custom AMI incurs:
1. Compute (instance-hour cost)
2. Root EBS volume storage (per instance)
3. Data EBS volume storage (per instance)
4. AMI snapshot storage (one-time, shared across all N instances)

**Step 2 — Compute compute cost.**

$$C_{\text{compute}} = N \times H \times C_{\text{inst}}$$

*Explanation:* Each of the **N** instances runs for **H** hours at the per-hour price **C_inst**.

**Step 3 — Compute per-instance EBS storage cost.**

$$C_{\text{ebs\_per\_instance}} = S_{\text{data}} \times C_{\text{ebs}}$$

*Explanation:* The attached data volume of size **S_data** GiB is billed monthly at the gp3 rate of **$0.08/GiB-month**.

**Step 4 — Compute total EBS storage cost across all instances.**

$$C_{\text{ebs\_total}} = N \times S_{\text{data}} \times C_{\text{ebs}}$$

*Explanation:* Multiply the per-instance cost by **N** because each instance has its own copy of the data volume.

**Step 5 — Compute AMI snapshot storage cost (singular, not multiplied by N).**

$$C_{\text{snapshot}} = S_{\text{ami}} \times C_{\text{snap}}$$

*Explanation:* The AMI is a single snapshot stored once. Even if 1000 instances are launched from it, the snapshot is still **one** entity — so we do **not** multiply by **N**.

**Step 6 — Compute the grand total monthly TCO.**

$$C_{\text{TCO}} = C_{\text{compute}} + C_{\text{ebs\_total}} + C_{\text{snapshot}}$$

*Explanation:* Sum all three cost streams to obtain the true monthly bill.

### Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a startup launches **N = 10** instances, each with **S_data = 100 GiB** data volume, running **H = 720** hours/month, instance price **C_inst = $0.0416/hr** (t3.medium in `ap-south-1`), AMI snapshot size **S_ami = 8 GiB**.

**Step 1 — Compute cost.**

$$C_{\text{compute}} = 10 \times 720 \times 0.0416 = 299.52 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 2 — EBS storage cost.**

$$C_{\text{ebs\_total}} = 10 \times 100 \times 0.08 = 80.00 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 3 — AMI snapshot cost.**

$$C_{\text{snapshot}} = 8 \times 0.05 = 0.40 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 4 — Total TCO.**

$$C_{\text{TCO}} = 299.52 + 80.00 + 0.40 = 379.92 \text{ USD/month}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — EBS-Backed vs Instance Store-Backed AMI

| Parameter | EBS-Backed AMI | Instance Store-Backed AMI | Engineering Implication |
|-----------|----------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Root storage location | EBS snapshot | S3 object | EBS = direct block access; S3 = object hydration overhead |
| Stop operation | ✅ Supported | ❌ Not supported | EBS allows graceful shutdowns, cost savings |
| Data persistence | ✅ Independent of instance | ❌ Lost on termination | EBS is the only choice for databases |
| Boot time | **< 1 min** | **< 5 min** | EBS wins for autoscaling responsiveness |
| Max instance volume size | **64 TiB** | **10 GB** (legacy) per instance store | EBS offers virtually unlimited root size |
| Default root type | `gp2`/`gp3` (SSD) | Magnetic (legacy) | EBS gives modern I/O performance |
| Typical use case | Web servers, databases, microservices | Caching layers, scratch space, HPC | Choose by workload criticality |
| Current AWS recommendation | **Strongly preferred** | Legacy / niche | 2024 Scheme answer must lean EBS-backed |

## 3.3 Boot Process Derivation — How an AMI Becomes a Running Instance

**Step 1 — User invokes `RunInstances` API with the chosen AMI ID.**
The API call specifies: AMI ID, instance type, subnet, key pair, security group, IAM role, user data.

**Step 2 — AWS Control Plane locates the AMI in the region's metadata store.**
The region-local AMI registry returns the root snapshot reference and block device map.

**Step 3 — AWS provisions compute hardware.**
A physical host is selected (or a hypervisor partition is carved out). For Nitro System instances, this is an **enclave on a bare-metal Nitro card**.

**Step 4 — EBS volume(s) are created from snapshot(s).**
For each entry in the block device map, a new EBS volume is initialized by **lazy-loading blocks** from the snapshot. This is why the first launch is slower — subsequent boots of the *same instance type* in the *same AZ* are faster due to EBS warm pool.

**Step 5 — Hypervisor attaches the root volume and triggers boot.**
The instance boots into the OS defined by the root volume. For HVM, hardware virtualization emulates a full x86_64 boot environment — UEFI or Legacy BIOS as configured.

**Step 6 — User data scripts execute (if provided).**
`cloud-init` reads the **user data** field and runs bootstrap scripts (e.g., `yum install nginx`).

**Step 7 — Instance reaches the `running` state.**
The instance is now accessible via its **private IP** (and public IP if subnet has auto-assign enabled).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AMI Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of how an AMI is decomposed into its components and how an EC2 instance is materialized from it.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AMI ID: ami-0a1b2c3d4e5f] --> B[AMI Registry Entry]
    B --> C[Root Volume Template]
    B --> D[Launch Permissions ACL]
    B --> E[Block Device Mapping]
    C --> C1[EBS Snapshot: snap-01ab]
    C --> C2[OS Filesystem: Amazon Linux 2]
    D --> D1[Owner: AWS Account 1234-5678]
    D --> D2[Public Flag: false]
    D --> D3[Shared Accounts: 9012-3456]
    E --> E1[Root: /dev/xvda gp3 20 GiB]
    E --> E2[Data: /dev/sdb gp3 100 GiB]
    E --> E3[DeleteOnTermination: true]
    F[RunInstances API Call] --> G{Region: ap-south-1}
    G --> H[Match AMI to Region]
    H --> I[Provision Nitro Compute Enclave]
    I --> J[Hydrate EBS Volumes from Snapshot]
    J --> K[Attach Block Devices per Mapping]
    K --> L[Hypervisor Boot: UEFI/Legacy]
    L --> M[cloud-init Executes User Data]
    M --> N[EC2 Instance: i-0abc123def456 State: Running]
    style A fill:#FF9900,stroke:#232F3E,color:#fff
    style N fill:#3F8624,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#fff
    style J fill:#146EB4,stroke:#0B3D7A,color:#fff
    style M fill:#7D8998,stroke:#424650,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 AMI Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Pending : CreateImage API
    Pending --> Available : Snapshot Registered
    Available --> Available : CopyImage to other region
    Available --> Available : Modify Launch Permissions
    Available --> Deregistered : DeregisterImage API
    Available --> [*] : Deregistered by Owner
    Deregistered --> [*] : Final State
    note right of Available
        Running EC2 instances launched
        from this AMI continue to run
        even after deregistration
    end note
    note right of Deregistered
        Underlying EBS snapshots
        are NOT deleted automatically
    end note
```

## 4.3 AMI Provisioning Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User as Cloud Engineer
    participant Console as AWS Console / CLI
    participant API as EC2 Control Plane
    participant EBS as EBS Service
    participant Host as Nitro Compute Host
    User->>Console: Select AMI ami-0xyz
    Console->>API: RunInstances (AMI, Type, Subnet, Key)
    API->>API: Validate AMI exists in region
    API->>EBS: CreateVolume from Snapshot
    EBS-->>API: Volume ID vol-0abc
    API->>Host: Allocate compute enclave
    Host->>EBS: Attach volumes per BlockDeviceMap
    Host->>Host: Power on VM (HVM/UEFI)
    Host->>Host: Execute cloud-init with UserData
    Host-->>User: Instance State = running
    Note over User,Host: Time to ready: ~30-60 sec for EBS-backed
```

## 4.4 Block Device Mapping Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EC2["EC2 Instance: web-server-01"]
        direction TB
        OS[Operating System Layer]
        APP[Application Layer: nginx + node.js]
        DATA[Data Layer: PostgreSQL]
    end
    subgraph BDM["Block Device Mapping"]
        direction TB
        R1["/dev/xvda : Root EBS gp3 20 GiB DeleteOnTermination=true"]
        R2["/dev/sdb : Data EBS gp3 200 GiB DeleteOnTermination=false"]
        R3["/dev/sdc : Swap io1 4 GiB 1000 IOPS DeleteOnTermination=true"]
    end
    R1 --> OS
    R2 --> DATA
    R3 --> OS
    style EC2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style BDM fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define AMI. List its three main components.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
An **Amazon Machine Image (AMI)** is a pre-configured template containing the software configuration (OS, application server, and applications) needed to launch an EC2 instance. The three main components are:
1. **Root Volume Template** — a snapshot of the OS boot disk (EBS snapshot or S3 template).
2. **Launch Permissions** — the AWS account(s) authorized to launch the AMI.
3. **Block Device Mapping** — specifies the EBS and instance store volumes to attach on launch, including size, type, and the `DeleteOnTermination` flag.

### Question 2: Differentiate between EBS-backed and instance store-backed AMIs in two points.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | EBS-Backed | Instance Store-Backed |
|-----------|------------|------------------------|
| Storage backend | EBS snapshot | Amazon S3 |
| Stop operation | Supported | Not supported |
| Data persistence | Independent of instance life | Lost on termination |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions for a single module carry **14 marks** and offer **internal choice**. Below are two alternative 14-mark questions.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Part (a) — [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an Amazon Machine Image. List and briefly describe the three core components of an AMI.

**Part (b) — [7 Marks]** Discuss the AMI lifecycle. What happens to running instances when an AMI is deregistered? Also explain the cost implications of deregistration on the underlying EBS snapshots.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Define AMI.
An Amazon Machine Image (AMI) is a region-scoped, read-only template used to launch EC2 instances. It encapsulates the OS, application stack, launch permissions, and storage topology.

**Step 2:** Draw the block diagram (refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid architecture).

**Step 3:** Describe the three components in depth:
1. **Root Volume Template:** An EBS snapshot containing the OS, libraries, and pre-installed software. For instance store-backed AMIs, this is an S3-stored bundled template.
2. **Launch Permissions:** AWS IAM-style ACL defining which accounts (`AccountId` list) or the general public can use the AMI. Configured via `ModifyImageAttribute`.
3. **Block Device Mapping:** A list of storage volumes to attach at launch. Each entry specifies device name, type (`gp3`, `io1`, `st1`, `sc1`, `standard`), size, IOPS, throughput, encryption status, and the `DeleteOnTermination` boolean.

**Step 4:** Mention that the AMI is **region-bound**; cross-region usage requires `CopyImage`.

*(7 marks — distributed: 2 for definition, 2 for diagram, 3 for component description)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** AMI lifecycle has three states: **Pending → Available → Deregistered**.

**Step 2:** Explain each state with the corresponding API call (`CreateImage`, `DeregisterImage`).

**Step 3:** What happens to running instances on deregistration?
- **The instances are NOT affected.** They continue to run normally because the AMI is only a *launch template*, not a live reference for the instance's root volume. The instance's root volume is an independent EBS volume created *from* the snapshot at launch time.
- However, **new launches** from the AMI's ID will fail because the AMI no longer exists in the registry.

**Step 4:** Cost implications on underlying snapshots:
- **Deregistration does NOT delete the underlying EBS snapshot.** The snapshot continues to exist and accrue storage charges at **$0.05 per GiB-month**.
- To stop billing, the user must **manually delete** the snapshot via `DeleteSnapshot` API or the EC2 console's *Snapshots* section.
- This is a **classic AWS cost-optimization pitfall** — many students in KTU exams forget that deregistration ≠ snapshot deletion.

*(7 marks — distributed: 2 for lifecycle diagram, 2 for instance behavior, 3 for cost implication)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Part (a) — [7 Marks]** Compare EBS-backed and Instance Store-backed AMIs across **six technical parameters** with appropriate justifications.

**Part (b) — [7 Marks]** Describe the step-by-step procedure to create a custom AMI from an existing EC2 instance. Also explain how you would copy this AMI to a different AWS region and why this might be necessary.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The comparison matrix from **Section 3.2** serves as the model answer. Students must present it as a **table** with at least **six parameters**: storage backend, stop support, persistence, boot time, max root size, and current AWS recommendation. Each parameter must carry a **one-line justification** (e.g., "Boot time: EBS-backed is < 1 min because volumes are pre-allocated EBS volumes; instance store requires full S3 hydration which takes ~5 min").

*(7 marks — 1 per parameter × 6, plus 1 mark for concluding statement)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** **Launch a base instance** from any AMI (e.g., Amazon Linux 2). Connect via SSH.

**Step 2:** **Customize the instance**: `sudo yum update -y`, install application packages, copy application code, set up users, configure `/etc/fstab` for any data volumes.

**Step 3:** **Create the AMI**:
- Console: *EC2 → Instances → Select instance → Actions → Image and templates → Create image*. Provide a name and description; choose "No reboot" only if the application supports it (otherwise data corruption risk).
- CLI: `aws ec2 create-image --instance-id i-0abc123 --name "MyApp-v1.0" --description "Golden image for MyApp" --no-reboot`

**Step 4:** **Wait for the AMI state to become `Available`**. Monitor via `aws ec2 describe-images --image-ids ami-0xyz`.

**Step 5:** **Copy to another region** (e.g., from `us-east-1` to `eu-west-1`):
`aws ec2 copy-image --source-region us-east-1 --source-image-id ami-0xyz --region eu-west-1 --name "MyApp-v1.0-EU"`

**Step 6:** **Why copy to another region?**
- **Disaster Recovery:** A region-wide outage in `us-east-1` does not affect `eu-west-1`.
- **Latency Optimization:** Launch instances closer to end-users (e.g., EU customers → `eu-west-1`).
- **Data Sovereignty / Compliance:** GDPR and other regulations may mandate that data and compute reside within specific geographic regions.
- **Cost Optimization:** Some regions have lower pricing for specific instance types.
- **Global Application Deployment:** Multi-region active-active architectures.

*(7 marks — 2 for creation procedure, 2 for copy command/syntax, 3 for justification of cross-region copy)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on AMI Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting that AMI is region-specific** — a global AMI-ID assumption will cost you at least **2 marks**.
> 2. **Assuming deregistration deletes the snapshot** — this is the **#1 cost-related question trap**. Always state explicitly that **the snapshot persists** and bills at $0.05/GiB-month until manually deleted.
> 3. **Confusing "stop" with "terminate"** for instance store-backed AMIs — they support only **reboot** and **terminate**, not stop.
> 4. **Stating that AMI itself is billed** — the AMI is **free**; you are billed for the **underlying snapshot storage** and the **running instance hours**, not the AMI itself.
> 5. **Omitting `DeleteOnTermination` flag** in block device mapping explanations — this is a high-frequency **2-mark** question in KTU exams.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Amazon Machine Images**

- ⭐ **AMI = Template** (not a running instance); contains OS + apps + permissions + storage plan.
- ⭐ **Three components:** Root Volume Template, Launch Permissions, Block Device Mapping.
- ⭐ **Region-bound:** Same AMI ID in two regions = two different entities.
- ⭐ **EBS-backed = modern default**; supports Stop/Start/Reboot/Terminate; root persists in EBS.
- ⭐ **Instance store-backed = legacy**; supports only Reboot/Terminate; data is ephemeral.
- ⭐ **Default Amazon Linux 2 root size = 8 GiB**; max EBS volume = **64 TiB**.
- ⭐ **Virtualization type:** `hvm` for all current-gen instances (post-2016); `paravirtual` is legacy.
- ⭐ **AMI ID format:** `ami-` followed by 8 or 17 hex characters (e.g., `ami-0a1b2c3d4e5f67890`).
- ⭐ **AMI lifecycle:** Pending → Available → Deregistered.
- ⭐ **Deregistration does NOT delete the underlying EBS snapshot** — manual cleanup required to stop billing.
- ⭐ **Deregistration does NOT affect running instances** — only new launches are blocked.
- ⭐ **Cross-region workflow:** Use `CopyImage` API; required for DR, latency, and compliance.
- ⭐ **Custom AMI creation:** Customize instance → `CreateImage` → wait for `Available` → test launch.
- ⭐ **Golden Image pattern:** Hardened, patched, security-scanned base AMI used in IaC pipelines (Packer, EC2 Image Builder).
- ⭐ **Block device mapping controls:** device path, size, type (`gp3`/`io1`/`st1`/`sc1`), IOPS, throughput, encryption, `DeleteOnTermination`.
- ⭐ **Public vs Explicit launch permission:** Public flag exposes the AMI to all AWS accounts globally; explicit shares are scoped to specific account IDs.
- ⭐ **No separate charge for AMI itself** — billed for snapshot storage ($0.05/GiB-month for standard S3-tier snapshots) and instance usage.
- ⭐ **Boot time:** EBS-backed ~30-60 sec; instance store-backed ~5 min.
- ⭐ **KTU exam weightage:** AMI is consistently asked in Module 4 (EC2) for 3-14 marks across KTU 2023 and 2024 papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2)** is a web service provided by Amazon Web Services (AWS) that delivers **secure, resizable compute capacity in the cloud**. It is designed to make web-scale cloud computing easier for developers by providing virtualized computing environments, known as **instances**, that can be launched, configured, and terminated on demand through a simple web service interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** EC2 is an IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service) offering that provides virtualized server instances, elastic IP addresses, block storage, security groups, key pairs, AMIs, and auto-scaling capabilities — all exposed via RESTful APIs.

### Key Characteristics of EC2

| Characteristic | Description |
|---|---|
| **Elasticity** | Capacity can be scaled up or down within minutes |
| **Complete Control** | Root access via SSH; instance behavior is fully user-controlled |
| **Flexible Cloud Hosting** | Choice of OS, instance type, storage, and networking |
| **Designed for Integration** | Tightly integrated with nearly all AWS services (S3, RDS, VPC, IAM) |
| **Reliable** | SLA commitment of **99.99%** monthly uptime per region |
| **Secure** | Built-in VPC, Security Groups, IAM roles, EBS encryption |
| **Inexpensive** | Pay-as-you-go pricing; no upfront capital expenditure |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electricity Grid" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of EC2 as the **"Electricity Grid for Computers."**

Before electricity grids existed, every factory had to build its own generator. Maintenance was expensive, capacity was fixed, and scaling meant buying a bigger generator. Then the central grid arrived: factories simply plugged in, paid only for what they consumed, and could add or remove machines without worrying about generation capacity.

**EC2 is exactly this for servers:**
- **Pre-EC2 era:** Buy a physical server, rack it, pay for power, cooling, and idle capacity.
- **EC2 era:** Click a button → a virtual server appears in 60 seconds → pay only for the seconds it runs → terminate it when done.

### Mapping the Analogy

| Electricity Grid | Amazon EC2 |
|---|---|
| Power socket | API endpoint / Console |
| kWh meter | Instance-hours billing |
| Voltage/Phase | Instance type (CPU, RAM, Network) |
| Appliance | Operating System + Application |
| Wiring topology | Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) and Subnets |
| Fuse/Circuit breaker | Security Group |

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following service-level metrics are industry-standard for EC2:

- **Region:** A physical geographical location (e.g., `us-east-1`, `ap-south-1`).
- **Availability Zone (AZ):** One or more discrete data centers within a region.
- **Instance Hour:** Billing unit — running for **3600 seconds** = **1 instance-hour**.
- **Minimum Billing Increment (Linux):** **1 minute** (billed per second thereafter, with a 60-second minimum).
- **Minimum Billing Increment (Windows):** **1 hour** regardless of usage.
- **EBS Bandwidth:** Up to **128,000 IOPS** on provisioned IOPS SSD (`io2`).
- **VPC CIDR Block Range:** `/16` is the default size for an EC2-VPC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A Region contains **at least two Availability Zones**, and AZs are connected by low-latency, high-bandwidth private links. This is the foundation of EC2's high-availability design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The EC2 Operational Stack — Layered Breakdown

EC2 is not a single product; it is an **ecosystem of tightly integrated components**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to understand each layer:

### Layer 1 — Compute Plane (The Server Itself)

1. **Amazon Machine Image (AMI):**
   - A **template** containing the OS, application server, and applications.
   - Functions as the "blueprint" from which instances are launched.
   - AMI components: Root volume template (EBS snapshot or instance-store-backed), launch permissions, and a block device mapping.

2. **Instance Type:**
   - Defines the hardware: **vCPU count, memory (GiB), storage, and network performance**.
   - Naming convention: `[family][generation].[size]` — Example: `m5.large`
     - `m` → General purpose family
     - `5` → 5th generation
     - `large` → Size within the family

3. **Instance Lifecycle States:**
   - `pending` → `running` → `stopping` → `stopped` → `shutting-down` → `terminated`

### Layer 2 — Storage Plane

| Storage Type | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **EBS (Elastic Block Store)** | Persistent block-level storage attached via network | Boot volumes, databases |
| **Instance Store** | Local SSD physically attached to host | Temporary data, caches |
| **EFS (Elastic File System)** | Shared NFS file system across many instances | Shared content, web farms |
| **S3 (via AWS CLI)** | Object storage with REST API access | Backups, AMIs, static assets |

### Layer 3 — Network Plane

1. **Elastic IP Address:** A static, public IPv4 address designed for dynamic cloud computing.
2. **Security Groups:** A **virtual stateful firewall** controlling inbound and outbound traffic at the **instance level**.
3. **Network ACLs (NACLs):** A **stateless firewall** controlling traffic at the **subnet level**.
4. **Elastic Network Interface (ENI):** A virtual network card that can be attached/detached between instances.
5. **Placement Groups:** Logical grouping of instances to influence communication:
   - **Cluster:** Low-latency, high-bandwidth (HPC)
   - **Spread:** Distinct underlying hardware (critical apps)
   - **Partition:** Grouped by logical partitions (Hadoop, Kafka)

### Layer 4 — Scaling and High Availability Plane

1. **Auto Scaling Group (ASG):** Maintains a desired count of healthy instances.
2. **Elastic Load Balancer (ELB):** Distributes traffic across instances.
   - Application Load Balancer (ALB) — Layer 7
   - Network Load Balancer (NLB) — Layer 4
   - Gateway Load Balancer (GLB) — Layer 3
3. **Launch Template / Launch Configuration:** The configuration blueprint for ASG-spawned instances.

### Layer 5 — Security Plane

1. **Key Pairs:** RSA key pairs used for SSH login — public key stored by AWS, private key downloaded once.
2. **IAM Roles:** Allow instances to call AWS APIs without embedding credentials.
3. **EBS Encryption:** AES-256 encryption using AWS KMS keys.

## 2.2 Pricing Models — The Heart of KTU Questions

EC2 offers **four primary pricing models**, and **selecting the correct one is a frequently tested concept**.

| Pricing Model | Discount | Commitment | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **On-Demand** | None | Pay by the second/hour | Short-term, spiky, unpredictable workloads |
| **Reserved Instances (RI)** | Up to **72%** off | 1-year or 3-year term | Steady-state, predictable usage |
| **Savings Plans** | Up to **72%** off | 1-year or 3-year term, $/hour commit | Flexible long-term workloads |
| **Spot Instances** | Up to **90%** off | No commitment; can be reclaimed with 2-min notice | Fault-tolerant, batch, stateless jobs |
| **Dedicated Hosts** | Premium pricing | Pay per host | Compliance, BYOL licensing |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cost Calculation Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Most numerical EC2 problems in KTU tests revolve around **monthly cost calculation** under one or more pricing models.

Let:
- $H$ = Total usage hours per month
- $r_{od}$ = On-Demand hourly rate
- $r_{ri}$ = Reserved Instance effective hourly rate
- $r_{spot}$ = Spot hourly rate (variable)
- $C_{upfront}$ = Upfront fee for RI
- $T_{term}$ = Term length in hours (1 year $= 8760$, 3 years $= 26280$)
- $U$ = Utilization percentage of RI

$$
\text{Monthly On-Demand Cost} \;=\; H \times r_{od}
$$

$$
\text{Total Reserved Cost} \;=\; C_{upfront} + (T_{term} \times r_{ri})
$$

$$
\text{Reserved Utilization (U)} \;=\; \frac{\text{Hours Actually Used RI}}{\text{Total RI Hours Available}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Effective Hourly Cost of RI} \;=\; \frac{C_{upfront}}{T_{term}} + r_{ri}
$$

$$
\text{Spot Cost (estimate)} \;=\; H \times \bar{r}_{spot}
$$

where $\bar{r}_{spot}$ is the **average** spot price over the usage window.

> **Decimal Handling Note (Table-Safe):** The above expressions avoid the vertical bar `|` character to remain compatible with markdown table rendering. Use `$\vert$` only in inline math, never inside table cells.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of EC2

EC2 underpins nearly every modern scalable internet service. Common real-world use cases:

- **Web application hosting** — Combines EC2 + ELB + ASG + RDS for three-tier architectures.
- **Big data analytics** — Hadoop/Spark clusters on Spot Instances.
- **Batch processing** — Genomics, financial risk modeling, image transcoding.
- **Disaster recovery** — Pilot light and warm standby topologies.
- **Machine learning training** — P-family and G-family GPU instances.
- **Microservices** — Each microservice deployed on its own ASG behind a service mesh.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Elastic"** in EC2 means two distinct things: (1) **vertical elasticity** — resizing instance type, and (2) **horizontal elasticity** — adding/removing instances via Auto Scaling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Monthly On-Demand Cost Computation (Worked Example)

### Given:
- Instance type: `t3.medium`
- Hourly rate: **$0.0416** (Linux, US East, on-demand)
- The instance runs continuously for **30 days** in a month.

### Find:
The total monthly bill for this single instance.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the billing model.**
This is a pure on-demand workload. The relevant formula is:

$$
\text{Monthly On-Demand Cost} \;=\; H \times r_{od}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total hours in 30 days.**

$$
H \;=\; 30 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hours/day}
$$

$$
H \;=\; 720 \text{ instance-hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the formula.**

$$
\text{Cost} \;=\; 720 \times 0.0416
$$

$$
\text{Cost} \;=\; \$29.952
$$

**Step 4 — Round to currency precision.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Cost} \;\approx\; \$29.95 \text{ per month}}
$$

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Reserved Instance TCO Comparison

### Given:
- 1-year Standard Reserved Instance for `m5.large` (Linux, US East):
  - Hourly rate: **$0.096** (effective)
  - Upfront fee: **$0** (No Upfront option)
- Equivalent On-Demand hourly rate: **$0.096**
- Wait — that doesn't yield a discount. Use these realistic values:
  - **No Upfront RI hourly:** $0.06
  - **All Upfront RI one-time:** $430
  - **On-Demand hourly:** $0.096
  - **Term:** 1 year = 8760 hours

### Find:
Compare the three TCO scenarios.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — On-Demand Total (Baseline).**

$$
\text{TCO}_{\text{OD}} \;=\; 8760 \times 0.096 \;=\; \$840.96
$$

**Step 2 — No Upfront RI Total.**

$$
\text{TCO}_{\text{NoUp}} \;=\; 0 + (8760 \times 0.06) \;=\; \$525.60
$$

**Step 3 — All Upfront RI Total.**

$$
\text{TCO}_{\text{AllUp}} \;=\; 430 + (8760 \times 0) \;=\; \$430.00
$$

**Step 4 — Discount vs. On-Demand (No Upfront).**

$$
\text{Discount}_{\text{NoUp}} \;=\; \frac{840.96 - 525.60}{840.96} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Discount}_{\text{NoUp}} \;=\; \frac{315.36}{840.96} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Discount}_{\text{NoUp}} \;\approx\; 37.5\%}
$$

**Step 5 — Discount vs. On-Demand (All Upfront).**

$$
\text{Discount}_{\text{AllUp}} \;=\; \frac{840.96 - 430.00}{840.96} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Discount}_{\text{AllUp}} \;=\; \frac{410.96}{840.96} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Discount}_{\text{AllUp}} \;\approx\; 48.86\%}
$$

## 3.3 Problem 3 — Spot vs. On-Demand Cost Analysis

### Given:
- A fault-tolerant image-processing job uses `c5.xlarge` Spot Instances.
- Spot average price: **$0.045/hour**
- On-Demand price: **$0.204/hour**
- Job requires **500 instance-hours** per month.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute Spot cost.**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{Spot}} \;=\; 500 \times 0.045 \;=\; \$22.50
$$

**Step 2 — Compute On-Demand cost.**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{OD}} \;=\; 500 \times 0.204 \;=\; \$102.00
$$

**Step 3 — Compute absolute savings.**

$$
\text{Savings} \;=\; 102.00 - 22.50 \;=\; \$79.50
$$

**Step 4 — Compute percentage savings.**

$$
\text{Savings\%} \;=\; \frac{79.50}{102.00} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Savings\%} \;\approx\; 77.94\%}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Pricing Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This decision matrix is a high-yield KTU exam topic.** Examiners frequently ask: *"Given a workload scenario, which pricing model would you recommend?"*

| Workload Characteristic | Recommended Model | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Predictable, runs 24/7 for 3 years | **Standard 3-year RI (All Upfront)** | Maximum discount (~60%+) |
| Predictable, runs 24/7 for 1 year | **1-year RI (No Upfront)** | Lower commitment risk |
| Predictable compute spend (\$/hour), flexible family | **Compute Savings Plan** | Balance discount + flexibility |
| Spiky traffic during business hours | **On-Demand + ASG** | Pay only when needed |
| Batch jobs, can be interrupted | **Spot Instances** | Up to 90% discount |
| Regulatory compliance / BYOL | **Dedicated Host** | Per-socket/per-core licensing |
| Unpredictable, dev/test environment | **Spot + On-Demand mix** | Cost-efficient + reliable |
| Long-running dev sandbox | **EC2 Savings Plan** | Predictable discount |

## 3.5 Problem 4 — Auto Scaling Calculation

### Given:
- ASG desired capacity: **4 instances**
- Scale-out threshold: CPU > 70%
- Scale-in threshold: CPU < 30%
- Current state: 2 healthy instances at 95% CPU

### Find:
What action will the ASG take?

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Evaluate current condition against scale-out rule.**

$$
\text{CPU}_{\text{avg}} \;=\; 95\% \;>\; 70\% \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Trigger policy.**

The ASG will launch additional instances until either:
- The desired capacity (4) is reached, OR
- The cooldown period has not yet expired.

**Step 3 — Compute new instance count.**

$$
\text{New Count} \;=\; \min(\text{Desired}, \text{Current} + \Delta)
$$

$$
\text{New Count} \;=\; \min(4, 2 + 2) \;=\; 4
$$

**Step 4 — Result.**

$$
\boxed{\text{The ASG scales out by launching } 2 \text{ new instances to reach the desired capacity of 4.}}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EC2 Component Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    User([User or Client Application])
    Console[AWS Management Console]
    CLI[AWS CLI or SDK]
    API[EC2 REST API Endpoint]
    
    User --> Console
    User --> CLI
    Console --> API
    CLI --> API
    
    API --> AMI[Amazon Machine Image]
    API --> InstType[Instance Type Selector]
    API --> SG[Security Group Firewall]
    API --> KP[Key Pair Authentication]
    
    AMI --> Inst1[EC2 Instance VM]
    InstType --> Inst1
    SG --> Inst1
    KP --> Inst1
    
    Inst1 --> EBS[Elastic Block Store Volume]
    Inst1 --> EIP[Elastic IP Address]
    Inst1 --> ENI[Elastic Network Interface]
    Inst1 --> Role[IAM Instance Role]
    
    EBS --> Snap[EBS Snapshot in S3]
    
    Inst1 --> ASG[Auto Scaling Group]
    ASG --> LB[Elastic Load Balancer]
    LB --> Inst1
    
    Inst1 --> CW[CloudWatch Metrics]
    CW --> ASG
    
    Inst1 --> User
```

## 4.2 EC2 Instance Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Pending : Launch via API or Console
    Pending --> Running : Instance boots successfully
    Running --> Stopping : User issues stop command
    Stopping --> Stopped : Hypervisor releases host resources
    Stopped --> Pending : User issues start command
    Running --> ShuttingDown : User issues terminate command
    ShuttingDown --> Terminated : Instance is destroyed
    Terminated --> [*] : State persists briefly then purged
    Stopped --> Terminated : User issues terminate command
```

## 4.3 EC2 Pricing Model Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Workload Requirement]) --> Q1{Predictable 24x7 usage for 1+ year?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Need instance family flexibility?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Fault tolerant to interruptions?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> SP[Compute Savings Plan]
    Q2 -- No --> RI[Reserved Instance 1y or 3y]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Spot[Spot Instances up to 90 percent off]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Strict compliance or BYOL needed?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> DH[Dedicated Host]
    Q4 -- No --> OD[On Demand pay per second]
```

## 4.4 EC2 High Availability Multi-Tier Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Internet[Public Internet]
        DNS[Route 53 DNS]
    end
    
    DNS --> ELB[Application Load Balancer]
    
    subgraph Region[AWS Region Two Availability Zones]
        ELB --> AZ1Web[Web Tier AZ1 EC2 Instances]
        ELB --> AZ2Web[Web Tier AZ2 EC2 Instances]
        
        AZ1Web --> AZ1App[App Tier AZ1 EC2 ASG]
        AZ2Web --> AZ2App[App Tier AZ2 EC2 ASG]
        
        AZ1App --> RDSPrimary[RDS Primary DB]
        AZ2App --> RDSStandby[RDS Standby DB Multi AZ]
        
        RDSPrimary -. Replication .-> RDSStandby
    end
    
    ASGPolicy[CloudWatch Alarm CPU gt 70] -. Triggers Scale Out .-> AZ1App
    ASGPolicy -. Triggers Scale Out .-> AZ2App
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — EC2 Request Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClientSide[Client Side]
        A1[Developer writes code or script]
        A2[Authenticate using IAM credentials]
        A3[Construct API request signed with SigV4]
    end
    
    subgraph AWSSide[AWS Backend]
        B1[API Gateway receives signed request]
        B2[EC2 Control Plane validates request]
        B3[Scheduler selects host hardware]
        B4[Hypervisor boots the chosen AMI]
        B5[EBS volume attached as root device]
        B6[Security Group rules applied to ENI]
        B7[Public or Private IP allocated]
        B8[Instance enters running state]
        B9[CloudWatch emits metric data]
        B10[Billing service starts accruing instance hours]
    end
    
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B6 --> B7 --> B8 --> B9 --> B10
    B8 -. SSH or RDP .-> A1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** List and briefly explain any **three pricing models** offered by Amazon EC2.

### Model Answer:
Amazon EC2 offers four main pricing models:

1. **On-Demand Instances:** Pay-per-second (Linux) or pay-per-hour (Windows) with no upfront commitment. Suitable for short-term, irregular workloads that cannot be interrupted. The most expensive option but offers maximum flexibility.

2. **Reserved Instances (RIs):** Commit to a 1-year or 3-year term for up to **72% discount** versus On-Demand. Available in three payment options — No Upfront, Partial Upfront, and All Upfront. Best for steady-state workloads.

3. **Spot Instances:** Bid for unused EC2 capacity at up to **90% discount**. AWS can reclaim instances with a **2-minute** interruption notice when demand rises. Ideal for fault-tolerant, stateless, or batch workloads.

4. **Dedicated Hosts:** A physical EC2 server dedicated to a single customer, allowing address of regulatory requirements and use of existing per-socket, per-core, or per-VM software licenses.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention **Savings Plans** in their answer. While RIs lock you to instance families, **Savings Plans** offer similar discounts with family flexibility. Mention at least 3 models clearly to secure full marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is an **Amazon Machine Image (AMI)**? List its **three main components**.

### Model Answer:
An **Amazon Machine Image (AMI)** is a pre-configured template that contains the information required to launch an EC2 instance, including the operating system, application server, and applications.

**Three Main Components of an AMI:**

1. **A template for the root volume** — The contents of the root file system (e.g., OS, applications, configurations).
2. **Launch permissions** — Specifies which AWS accounts can use the AMI to launch instances (public, explicit, or owner-only).
3. **A block device mapping** — Defines the EBS volumes and instance-store volumes to attach to the instance at launch.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **EC2 instance lifecycle** with the help of a state transition diagram. Describe what happens to EBS-backed and Instance Store-backed volumes during the `stop` and `terminate` transitions.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A startup runs **three `t3.medium` instances** continuously for an entire month (**30 days**). The On-Demand hourly rate is **$0.0416**, and the 1-year No Upfront Reserved Instance rate is **$0.026**. Compute:
- (i) Monthly On-Demand cost for all three instances.
- (ii) Annual On-Demand cost.
- (iii) Annual Reserved cost.
- (iv) Annual savings in absolute dollars and percentage.

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — EC2 Instance Lifecycle

The EC2 instance lifecycle has **six states**:

| State | Description |
|---|---|
| `pending` | Instance is being prepared; not yet running |
| `running` | Instance is booted and incurring charges |
| `stopping` | Transition state during a user-initiated stop |
| `stopped` | Instance is shut down; can be restarted |
| `shutting-down` | Transition state during termination |
| `terminated` | Instance is permanently deleted |

**EBS-Backed vs. Instance Store-Backed Behavior:**

- **EBS-Backed Instances:** Can be **stopped** and restarted. The root EBS volume persists in `available` state. By default, the root volume is **deleted on termination**, but this behavior is configurable per-volume.
- **Instance Store-Backed Instances:** **Cannot** be stopped — only **terminated or rebooted**. Any data on instance store is **lost permanently** upon termination.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Pending
    Pending --> Running : launch
    Running --> Stopping : stop EBS only
    Stopping --> Stopped
    Stopped --> Running : start
    Running --> ShuttingDown : terminate
    Running --> Rebooting : reboot
    Rebooting --> Running
    ShuttingDown --> Terminated
    Terminated --> [*]
    Stopped --> Terminated : terminate
```

#### Part (b) — Cost Calculations

**Given Data:**
- $n = 3$ instances (each `t3.medium`)
- $r_{od} = \$0.0416$/hour
- $r_{ri} = \$0.026$/hour (No Upfront, 1-year)
- Month duration: 30 days → 720 hours/month
- Year duration: 8760 hours

**(i) Monthly On-Demand Cost:**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{month}} \;=\; n \times H \times r_{od}
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{month}} \;=\; 3 \times 720 \times 0.0416
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{month}} \;=\; \$89.86
$$

**(ii) Annual On-Demand Cost:**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{year,OD}} \;=\; 3 \times 8760 \times 0.0416
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{year,OD}} \;=\; \$1093.25
$$

**(iii) Annual Reserved Cost:**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{year,RI}} \;=\; 3 \times 8760 \times 0.026
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{year,RI}} \;=\; \$683.28
$$

**(iv) Annual Savings:**

**Absolute Savings:**

$$
\text{Savings}_{\$} \;=\; 1093.25 - 683.28 \;=\; \$409.97
$$

**Percentage Savings:**

$$
\text{Savings}_{\%} \;=\; \frac{409.97}{1093.25} \times 100 \;\approx\; 37.50\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Annual Savings} \;\approx\; \$409.97 \;\text{or}\; 37.50\%}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, describe the **Elastic Load Balancer (ELB)** types in AWS and explain how **Auto Scaling Groups (ASG)** interact with ELB to provide high availability.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare **Security Groups** and **Network ACLs** across any **five dimensions**. Why are Security Groups considered **stateful** while NACLs are **stateless**?

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — ELB and ASG Interaction

**Three Types of Elastic Load Balancers:**

1. **Application Load Balancer (ALB):**
   - Operates at **Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS)**.
   - Supports content-based routing (path, host, header).
   - Ideal for microservices and containerized apps.
   - Supports WebSocket and HTTP/2.

2. **Network Load Balancer (NLB):**
   - Operates at **Layer 4 (TCP/UDP)**.
   - Handles millions of requests per second with ultra-low latency.
   - Preserves the source IP address.

3. **Gateway Load Balancer (GLB):**
   - Operates at **Layer 3 (Transparent Network Gateway)**.
   - Used to deploy third-party virtual appliances (firewalls, IDS/IPS).

**ASG + ELB Interaction Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    User([User Traffic]) --> DNS[Route 53]
    DNS --> ALB[Application Load Balancer]
    ALB -->|Health Check Probe| TG[Target Group]
    TG --> Inst1[Instance 1 in ASG]
    TG --> Inst2[Instance 2 in ASG]
    TG --> Inst3[Instance N in ASG]
    
    CW[CloudWatch Alarm] -->|Scale Out Trigger| ASG
    ASG -. Add or Remove .-> Inst1
    ASG -. Add or Remove .-> Inst2
    ASG -. Add or Remove .-> Inst3
    
    TG -. Unhealthy Instance .-> ASG
    ASG -. Terminate and Replace .-> TG
```

**How ASG + ELB Provide High Availability:**
- ASG registers every new instance with the ELB's target group.
- ELB performs continuous health checks; unhealthy instances are deregistered.
- ASG terminates unhealthy instances and launches replacements.
- This creates a **self-healing, self-scaling** architecture with zero downtime.

#### Part (b) — Security Groups vs. Network ACLs

| Dimension | Security Group (SG) | Network ACL (NACL) |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope (Operates at)** | **Instance level (ENI)** | **Subnet level** |
| **Statefulness** | **Stateful** — return traffic is auto-allowed | **Stateless** — return traffic must be explicitly allowed |
| **Rule Type** | **Allow rules only** | **Allow and Deny rules** |
| **Rule Evaluation** | All rules evaluated; most permissive wins | Rules evaluated by **rule number** (lowest first) |
| **Default Behavior** | **Deny all inbound**, allow all outbound | **Allow all inbound and outbound** |
| **Association** | Can be applied to multiple instances | Auto-applied to all instances in the subnet |

**Why Security Groups are Stateful and NACLs are Stateless:**

A **stateful** firewall tracks the state of network connections. When an outbound request is made from an instance, the Security Group remembers the connection (via the 5-tuple: source IP, source port, destination IP, destination port, protocol) and automatically allows the corresponding inbound reply — even if no inbound rule explicitly permits it.

A **stateless** firewall inspects each packet independently without context. A Network ACL does not track connections; therefore, if you allow inbound TCP port 80, you must **also explicitly allow** the corresponding ephemeral outbound replies on ports 1024–65535, otherwise return traffic will be dropped.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall on Q.B(b):** A common error is writing "Security Groups work on the subnet." This is **incorrect** — Security Groups attach to **ENIs (Elastic Network Interfaces)**, not subnets. Always quote the correct scope. Also remember: SGs have **allow rules only**, while NACLs support both **allow and deny**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — EC2**

- ✅ **EC2 = Elastic Compute Cloud** — virtualized, on-demand compute capacity in AWS.
- ✅ **Instance** = a virtual server. **AMI** = the blueprint/template.
- ✅ **Instance Type Naming:** `[family][generation].[size]` — Example: `m5.large`.
- ✅ **Pricing Models (4):** On-Demand, Reserved, Savings Plans, Spot, Dedicated Hosts.
- ✅ **Spot Instances can be reclaimed with a 2-minute notice.**
- ✅ **On-Demand Linux billing:** per-second after a 60-second minimum.
- ✅ **EBS = persistent block storage**; **Instance Store = ephemeral local SSD.**
- ✅ **Security Groups** = stateful, instance-level, allow-only. **NACLs** = stateless, subnet-level, allow + deny.
- ✅ **Elastic IP** = static public IPv4 address that can be remapped between instances.
- ✅ **Key Pairs** = RSA public-key cryptography for SSH login. Private key is downloaded once and never stored by AWS.
- ✅ **Auto Scaling Group (ASG)** maintains desired, minimum, and maximum instance counts.
- ✅ **ELB Types:** ALB (L7), NLB (L4), GLB (L3 Gateway).
- ✅ **Placement Groups:** Cluster (HPC), Spread (HA), Partition (Big Data).
- ✅ **EC2 SLA = 99.99%** monthly uptime per region.
- ✅ **Bootstrapping** uses **user data** scripts executed at first launch.
- ✅ **Instance Metadata** is accessible at `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/`.
- ✅ **Stop vs. Terminate:** Stop = EBS-backed instances only, data preserved. Terminate = permanent deletion.
- ✅ **High Availability pattern** = Multi-AZ deployment with ELB + ASG + EBS Multi-Attach or RDS Multi-AZ.
- ✅ **For TCO problems:** Always convert days → hours using $24$ hours/day and $1 \text{ year} = 8760$ hours.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** When answering EC2 questions in the exam, structure your response in **three layers** — (1) Define the concept in one sentence, (2) List key components/features as bullet points, (3) Provide a small diagram or example to demonstrate real-world relevance. This maps directly to KTU's outcome-based marking scheme targeting CO1 (Understanding), CO2 (Applying), and CO3 (Analyzing).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Compute Services. Storage Services - Simple Storage System (Amazon S3)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advanced Compute Services. Storage Services - Simple Storage System (Amazon S3)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Advanced Compute Services — Definition

**Advanced Compute Services** in the AWS cloud ecosystem refer to a category of managed, highly abstracted, serverless or container-based compute offerings that allow developers and enterprises to run code, orchestrate workflows, and process data **without provisioning, managing, or scaling underlying virtual machines or physical servers**. These services abstract away infrastructure management so that engineers can focus exclusively on application logic and business outcomes.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (Course: PEERT524 – Cloud Computing)**, Advanced Compute Services include — but are not limited to — the following AWS offerings:

| Service | Full Form | Primary Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **AWS Lambda** | AWS Lambda (Serverless Functions) | Event-driven, function-as-a-service (FaaS) execution |
| **AWS Fargate** | AWS Fargate | Serverless container orchestration with ECS/EKS |
| **AWS Batch** | AWS Batch | Batch computing workloads at any scale |
| **AWS Lightsail** | AWS Lightsail | Simplified virtual private servers for small workloads |
| **AWS Outposts** | AWS Outposts | Hybrid on-premises AWS infrastructure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition for KTU Exams:**
> *Advanced Compute Services are serverless or highly managed compute abstractions that dynamically allocate compute resources on-demand, billed by millisecond-level consumption or workload, eliminating the operational burden of server administration.*

## 1.2 Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3) — Definition

**Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service)** is an AWS **object storage service** that offers industry-leading scalability, data availability, security, and performance. It is designed to store and retrieve any amount of data from anywhere on the web, making it the foundational storage backbone of cloud-native architectures.

Formally, according to the **AWS official documentation and the KTU 2024 syllabus**:

> [!NOTE]
> *Amazon S3 is a regional, web-based object storage service that stores data as **objects** inside **buckets**. An object is composed of:*
> 1. ***Data*** *— the actual file content (any type: images, videos, logs, backups, binaries).*
> 2. ***Key*** *— the unique identifier (file name) within a bucket.*
> 3. ***Metadata*** *— descriptive information about the object (size, content-type, custom tags).*
> 4. ***Version ID*** *— a unique identifier assigned when versioning is enabled.*

The minimum storage unit is the **object**, the maximum single object size is **5 TB**, and the maximum single PUT upload size is **5 GB** (larger uploads use **multi-part upload**).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Analogy 1: Amazon S3 as a "Bank of Lockers"

Imagine walking into a **huge, secure bank vault** that has an **unlimited number of lockers**:

- The **bank vault itself** is your **AWS Account / Region**.
- Each **locker** is a **Bucket** (a container for objects).
- Each **locker can hold any item** — from a small document to a massive filing box (up to 5 TB). The item is the **Object**.
- Each item has a **label sticker** with a unique locker number — that label is the **Key** (file name).
- The **sticker also has extra info** like "fragile", "created on 12-Jan", "owner: Alice" — that extra info is the **Metadata**.
- The vault has **security guards** (IAM policies, bucket policies), **cameras** (access logs), and **firewalls** (encryption at rest/in-transit).

You don't worry about buying more lockers if you run out — the bank just **adds infinite lockers on demand**. This is **elastic, virtually unlimited scalability**.

### Analogy 2: Advanced Compute as "Ordering Pizza vs. Cooking at Home"

- **Traditional EC2** = Cooking at home: you buy groceries (provision servers), preheat the oven (configure OS), cook the dish (deploy app), and clean up afterward.
- **Lambda / Fargate** = Ordering pizza: you just say "I want a Margherita", and someone else (AWS) handles the kitchen, oven, and cleanup. You pay only for the pizza you ate (per-invocation billing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always relate advanced compute services to *abstraction*, *auto-scaling*, and *pay-per-use* in exam answers — these are the three hallmark differentiators from traditional IaaS.

## 1.4 Core Terminology Summary

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bucket** | A top-level container for objects in S3; globally unique name within AWS partition | Logical grouping unit |
| **Object** | The fundamental entity stored in S3, consisting of data, key, metadata, and version ID | Atomic storage unit |
| **Key** | The unique identifier (path) for an object within a bucket | Equivalent to file name |
| **Region** | The geographic AWS region where the bucket resides | Determines latency & data sovereignty |
| **Storage Class** | A tier of storage with different cost/availability trade-offs | Cost optimization lever |
| **Versioning** | Keeping multiple variants of an object in the same bucket | Data protection mechanism |
| **Lifecycle Policy** | Rules that automatically transition objects between storage classes or expire them | Cost automation |
| **Replication** | Automatic async copying of objects across buckets/regions | Disaster recovery |
| **Pre-signed URL** | A time-limited URL granting temporary access to an S3 object | Secure sharing mechanism |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive: Advanced Compute Services

### 2.1.1 AWS Lambda (Function-as-a-Service)

AWS Lambda lets you run code **without provisioning or managing servers**. You upload your code (called a **Lambda function**), define a trigger, and AWS handles the rest.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Event-driven**: Triggered by HTTP requests (via API Gateway), S3 events, DynamoDB streams, SQS, SNS, CloudWatch schedules, etc.
- **Stateless**: Each invocation is independent — no shared memory between invocations.
- **Auto-scaling**: Concurrent executions scale horizontally (default quota: 1,000 concurrent executions per region).
- **Billed by**: Number of requests + compute time (GB-seconds), with a free tier of **1 million requests/month** and **400,000 GB-seconds/month**.

**Lambda Configuration Parameters:**
- **Memory allocation**: 128 MB to 10,240 MB (in 1 MB increments).
- **Maximum execution time (timeout)**: 900 seconds (15 minutes).
- **Ephemeral disk space (`/tmp`)**: Up to 10,240 MB.
- **Package size limits**: 50 MB (zipped, direct upload) / 250 MB (unzipped, including layers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory vs. CPU Relationship:** Lambda allocates **CPU power proportionally to memory**. If you double the memory, you get roughly double the vCPU allocation. This is a critical exam point for performance tuning.

### 2.1.2 AWS Fargate (Serverless Containers)

Fargate is a **serverless compute engine for containers** that works with **Amazon ECS** (Elastic Container Service) and **Amazon EKS** (Elastic Kubernetes Service). It removes the need to choose, provision, or manage EC2 instances for container workloads.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **No cluster management**: AWS manages the underlying infrastructure.
- **Pay-per-resource**: Billed by vCPU and memory consumed per second.
- **Isolation**: Each task/pod runs in its own kernel runtime environment.
- **Use cases**: Microservices, batch processing, machine learning inference, web applications.

### 2.1.3 Comparison Table for KTU Exams

| Feature | EC2 (IaaS) | Lambda (FaaS) | Fargate (Containers) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Abstraction Level** | Low (VM) | Highest (function) | Medium (container) |
| **Scaling Model** | Manual / Auto Scaling Groups | Automatic, instant | Automatic via ECS/EKS |
| **Pricing Unit** | Per-hour (instance) | Per-request + GB-sec | Per-vCPU + GB-sec |
| **Max Runtime** | Indefinite | 15 minutes | Configurable |
| **State Management** | Stateful possible | Stateless only | Stateful possible |
| **Use Case** | Legacy apps, full control | Event handlers, APIs | Microservices, ML |

## 2.2 Deep Dive: Amazon S3 Architecture

### 2.2.1 The Bucket

A **bucket** is a flat container for objects. Despite the visual illusion of folders, S3 has **no real folder hierarchy** — what looks like a `/` in the key is simply a character in the object's name. For example:

- Object Key: `photos/2024/january/photo1.jpg`
- This is a single object whose key contains `/` characters, not a folder structure.

**Bucket Naming Rules (KTU High-Yield):**
- **3 to 63 characters** long.
- Can contain lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens (`-`), and periods (`.`).
- **Must start and end with a letter or number**.
- **Must be globally unique** across all AWS accounts worldwide.
- Cannot be formatted as an IP address (e.g., `192.168.1.1`).

### 2.2.2 The Object

Each object stored in S3 has:
1. **Data** — up to **5 TB** in size.
2. **Key** — the object's name (UTF-8 encoded).
3. **Metadata** — system-defined (last modified, size, ETag) and user-defined (custom tags).
4. **Version ID** — assigned when versioning is enabled.
5. **ETag** — a hash of the object used for integrity verification.

### 2.2.3 S3 Storage Classes

S3 offers multiple storage classes optimized for different use cases, forming a **cost vs. availability vs. durability** tradeoff.

| Storage Class | Use Case | Availability | Min Storage Duration | Retrieval Fee |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **S3 Standard** | Frequently accessed data | 99.99% | None | None |
| **S3 Intelligent-Tiering** | Unknown/Changing access patterns | 99.9% | None | Monitoring fee |
| **S3 Standard-IA** | Infrequently accessed, rapid access | 99.9% | 30 days | Per-GB |
| **S3 One Zone-IA** | Infrequently accessed, non-critical replicas | 99.5% | 30 days | Per-GB |
| **S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval** | Archive, ms retrieval | 99.9% | 90 days | Per-GB |
| **S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval** | Archive, 1 min–12 hr retrieval | 99.99% | 90 days | Per-GB + request |
| **S3 Glacier Deep Archive** | Long-term archive, 12–48 hr retrieval | 99.99% | 180 days | Per-GB + request |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Durability Note:** All S3 classes offer **99.999999999% (11 nines) durability**, except **S3 One Zone-IA**, which has **99.999999999%** within a single AZ (i.e., data is lost if the AZ is destroyed). This is a frequent KTU MCQ topic.

### 2.2.4 S3 Data Consistency Model

A critical concept for KTU exams:

- **For new PUTs (overwrites):** S3 provides **strong read-after-write consistency**.
- **For all other operations:** S3 provides **eventual consistency for the old version** during the time it takes to propagate.

In 2020, AWS upgraded S3 to be **strongly consistent for all operations** — but students should mention this evolution in their answers.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Rule | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Lambda Cost** | $C = (N_{req} \times P_{req}) + (T \times M \times P_{GBs})$ | $N_{req}$ = requests, $P_{req}$ = \$0.20 per 1M, $T$ = duration (s), $M$ = memory (GB), $P_{GBs}$ = \$0.0000166667 per GB-s |
| **S3 Object Max Size** | $S_{obj,max} = 5$ | TB |
| **S3 PUT Max Size (single)** | $S_{PUT,max} = 5$ | GB |
| **S3 Buckets per Account** | $N_{buckets} \leq 100$ | Soft limit, can be increased |
| **S3 Durability (Standard)** | $D = 99.999999999\%$ | i.e., 11 nines, $= 1 - 10^{-11}$ |
| **S3 Availability (Standard)** | $A = 99.99\%$ | 4 nines of uptime |
| **Multi-part Upload Parts** | $N_{parts} \leq 10{,}000$ | Per object |
| **Lambda Memory Range** | $128 \leq M_{lambda} \leq 10240$ | MB |
| **Lambda Timeout** | $T_{max} = 900$ | seconds |
| **Pre-signed URL Expiry** | $T_{exp} \in [1, 604800]$ | seconds (1 second to 7 days) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Lambda's cost formula is widely used in **FinOps (Financial Operations)** dashboards to forecast serverless spend. S3's storage class selection is used in **data lifecycle engineering** to optimize TCO (Total Cost of Ownership).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Lambda Cost Calculation

This is a **typical KTU numerical problem** on Advanced Compute Services.

**Problem Statement:**
An application uses AWS Lambda to process images. Each invocation uses **512 MB** of memory and runs for an average of **2 seconds**. If the application receives **5 million requests per month**, calculate the monthly bill (excluding free tier).

**Step 1: Identify the Formula**

$$C_{total} = (N_{req} \times P_{req}) + (T \times M \times P_{GBs} \times N_{req})$$

**Step 2: Substitute Known Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{req} &= 5 \times 10^{6} \text{ requests} \\
T &= 2 \text{ seconds} \\
M &= 512 \text{ MB} = 0.5 \text{ GB} \\
P_{req} &= \$0.20 \text{ per 1 million requests} \\
P_{GBs} &= \$0.0000166667 \text{ per GB-second}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Request Charges**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{req} &= N_{req} \times P_{req} \\
C_{req} &= 5 \times 10^{6} \times \frac{\$0.20}{10^{6}} \\
C_{req} &= \$1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Compute Charges**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{compute} &= T \times M \times P_{GBs} \times N_{req} \\
C_{compute} &= 2 \times 0.5 \times 0.0000166667 \times 5 \times 10^{6} \\
C_{compute} &= 1.0 \times 0.0000166667 \times 5 \times 10^{6} \\
C_{compute} &= 0.0000166667 \times 5 \times 10^{6} \\
C_{compute} &= \$83.3335
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Total Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= C_{req} + C_{compute} \\
C_{total} &= \$1.00 + \$83.33 \\
C_{total} &= \$84.33 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is the **monthly Lambda bill for the image-processing workload** at the given scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** This kind of calculation is used by cloud architects during the **pricing comparison** between Lambda and EC2 — often, Lambda wins for **spiky, low-traffic workloads**, while EC2 is cheaper for **steady-state, high-traffic workloads**.

## 3.2 Derivation: S3 Storage Cost Analysis with Lifecycle Policies

**Problem Statement:**
A company stores **10 TB of data in S3 Standard** for the first 30 days, then transitions it to **S3 Standard-IA** for the next 60 days, and finally archives it to **S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval** for 2 years. Calculate the total cost per object (assuming a 1 GB file for simplicity and ignoring request costs).

**Standard Pricing (Approximate, USD, US-East-1 region):**
- S3 Standard: $0.023 per GB-month
- S3 Standard-IA: $0.0125 per GB-month
- S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval: $0.0036 per GB-month
- Transition fee: $0.01 per 1,000 objects (negligible for 1 object)

**Step 1: Convert to GB**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{File size} &= 1 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Cost in S3 Standard (First 30 Days)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{standard} &= 1 \text{ GB} \times \$0.023 \times 1 \text{ month} \\
C_{standard} &= \$0.023
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Cost in S3 Standard-IA (Next 60 Days = 2 Months)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{ia} &= 1 \text{ GB} \times \$0.0125 \times 2 \text{ months} \\
C_{ia} &= \$0.025
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Cost in S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval (2 Years = 24 Months)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{glacier} &= 1 \text{ GB} \times \$0.0036 \times 24 \text{ months} \\
C_{glacier} &= \$0.0864
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Total Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= C_{standard} + C_{ia} + C_{glacier} \\
C_{total} &= \$0.023 + \$0.025 + \$0.0864 \\
C_{total} &= \$0.1344 \text{ over the 26.5 months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Comparison — Without Lifecycle Policy (All in S3 Standard for 26.5 months)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{no\_policy} &= 1 \text{ GB} \times \$0.023 \times 26.5 \text{ months} \\
C_{no\_policy} &= \$0.6095
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Savings**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= C_{no\_policy} - C_{total} \\
\text{Savings} &= \$0.6095 - \$0.1344 \\
\text{Savings} &= \$0.4751 \\
\text{Savings \%} &= \frac{0.4751}{0.6095} \times 100 = 77.95\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A well-designed **lifecycle policy** yields a **~78% cost reduction** for archival data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This is a classic **Part B (14-mark) question pattern**: students must show lifecycle transitions, justify the storage class choice, and quantify savings.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing Between Lambda, Fargate, and EC2

| Workload Characteristic | Best Service | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Short, event-driven, sporadic traffic | **Lambda** | Pay only per invocation; instant scale |
| Long-running containerized microservice | **Fargate** | No server management; container-native |
| Stateful legacy application needing full OS control | **EC2** | Persistent instance, custom AMI, full root access |
| Bursty ML inference with unpredictable load | **Lambda** or **Fargate** | Both scale to zero / scale out automatically |
| High-performance computing, GPU workloads | **EC2 (P/G instances)** | Lambda doesn't support GPU; Fargate only recently added limited GPU |
| WebSocket-based real-time app | **Fargate** | Lambda's 15-min timeout and statelessness are limiting |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: AWS Lambda Invocation Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how AWS Lambda is triggered by an event from Amazon S3 and processes the event with downstream services.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Uploads File] --> B[Amazon S3 Bucket]
    B --> C{S3 Event Notification}
    C --> D[Amazon EventBridge / S3 Trigger]
    D --> E[AWS Lambda Function]
    E --> F[Lambda Execution Environment]
    F --> G{Process Object}
    G --> H[Write to DynamoDB]
    G --> I[Send SNS Notification]
    G --> J[Log to CloudWatch]
    F --> K[IAM Role for Permissions]
    E --> L[Billed: Requests + GB-Seconds]
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
- **Node A:** Represents the originating user action (e.g., a developer or application uploads a file).
- **Node B:** The S3 bucket stores the uploaded object.
- **Node C:** A bucket notification event is emitted when the PUT completes.
- **Node D:** EventBridge (or direct S3 trigger) routes the event to Lambda.
- **Node E, F:** The Lambda function is invoked inside a managed execution environment.
- **Node G:** Business logic processes the object (e.g., resize image, parse log).
- **Nodes H, I, J:** The function interacts with other AWS services.
- **Node K:** IAM roles authorize the function's access to downstream services.
- **Node L:** Cost accumulates per request and per compute-second.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Amazon S3 Architecture and Storage Class Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client / Application] --> B[AWS Account Region]
    B --> C[Bucket: Globally Unique Name]
    C --> D1[Object 1: Key=data.csv]
    C --> D2[Object 2: Key=img.jpg]
    C --> D3[Object 3: Key=log.txt]
    C --> D4[Object N: ...]
    C --> E[Versioning Enabled?]
    E -- Yes --> F[Multiple Version IDs]
    E -- No --> G[Single Latest Object]
    C --> H[Lifecycle Policy Rules]
    H --> I1[S3 Standard: 0-30 days]
    H --> I2[S3 Standard-IA: 30-90 days]
    H --> I3[S3 Glacier Flexible: 90+ days]
    H --> I4[S3 Deep Archive: 365+ days]
    I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4
    C --> J[Replication Rules]
    J --> K1[Same-Region Replication]
    J --> K2[Cross-Region Replication]
    J --> K3[Cross-Account Replication]
    C --> L[Access Control]
    L --> M1[Bucket Policies]
    L --> M2[IAM Policies]
    L --> M3[ACLs]
    L --> M4[Pre-signed URLs]
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
- **Node A:** External client (web app, mobile, IoT device) initiates interaction.
- **Node B:** Bucket resides in a specific AWS region (e.g., `ap-south-1`).
- **Node C:** Bucket is the root container with a globally unique name.
- **Nodes D1–D4:** Objects are stored with unique keys within the bucket.
- **Node E:** Versioning toggle determines if multiple variants of the same key are kept.
- **Node H:** Lifecycle policies automate transitions across storage classes.
- **Nodes I1–I4:** Sequential cost-optimization tiers based on data age.
- **Node J:** Replication provides redundancy and disaster recovery.
- **Node L:** Multiple access control layers secure the bucket and its objects.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Comparison — EC2 vs. Lambda vs. Fargate (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EC2[EC2 IaaS Model]
        E1[User Provisions EC2 Instance] --> E2[User Manages OS]
        E2 --> E3[User Installs Runtime]
        E3 --> E4[User Deploys App]
        E4 --> E5[User Configures Auto Scaling]
    end
    subgraph Lambda[Lambda FaaS Model]
        L1[User Uploads Code] --> L2[AWS Manages Runtime]
        L2 --> L3[AWS Auto-Scales Invocations]
        L3 --> L4[Billed Per Invocation]
    end
    subgraph Fargate[Fargate Container Model]
        F1[User Builds Container Image] --> F2[User Defines Task in ECS/EKS]
        F2 --> F3[AWS Provisions Infra for Containers]
        F3 --> F4[AWS Scales Containers]
        F4 --> F5[Billed Per vCPU + GB-sec]
    end
```

**Explanation:**
- The **left subgraph (EC2)** shows maximum user responsibility and lowest abstraction.
- The **middle subgraph (Lambda)** shows maximum AWS abstraction and minimum user responsibility.
- The **right subgraph (Fargate)** is a balanced middle ground: users control the container but not the host.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram for KTU:** Examiners love visual comparisons. Drawing such a diagram in your answer sheet (even by hand) demonstrates **deep conceptual clarity** and earns higher marks.

## 4.4 Diagram 4: S3 Data Lifecycle Stages

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Uploaded: Object PUT to S3 Standard
    Uploaded --> InfrequentAccess: After 30 days, Lifecycle Rule
    InfrequentAccess --> GlacierFlexible: After 90 days, Lifecycle Rule
    GlacierFlexible --> DeepArchive: After 365 days, Lifecycle Rule
    DeepArchive --> Expiration: After N years, Lifecycle Rule
    Expiration --> [*]: Object Deleted
    Uploaded --> Deleted: Manual Delete or Versioning Purge
```

**Explanation:** The state diagram shows the **progressive transition** of objects across storage classes as they age, ending in expiration or manual deletion.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Advanced Compute Service" in the context of cloud computing. List two examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Advanced Compute Services are a class of highly abstracted, serverless or managed compute offerings provided by cloud providers that allow users to execute workloads (functions, containers, batches) without provisioning or managing the underlying servers. They are characterized by **automatic scaling, pay-per-use billing, and zero server administration**.

**Two Examples:**
1. **AWS Lambda** — Function-as-a-Service for event-driven code execution.
2. **AWS Fargate** — Serverless container orchestration with ECS/EKS.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing "EC2 is an advanced compute service" will lose you full marks. EC2 is **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** — it is *not* advanced compute. Advanced compute = **higher-level abstraction beyond VMs**.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is an S3 bucket? What are the constraints on bucket naming?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** An **S3 bucket** is a top-level logical container in Amazon S3 that holds objects (files). Each bucket must have a globally unique name across all AWS accounts and is associated with a specific AWS region.

**Bucket Naming Constraints:**
1. **Length:** 3 to 63 characters.
2. **Allowed characters:** Lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens (`-`), and periods (`.`).
3. **Must start and end with a letter or number** (not a hyphen or period).
4. **Cannot be an IP address format** (e.g., `192.168.1.1` is invalid).
5. **Must be globally unique** across the entire AWS namespace.
6. **No uppercase letters, underscores, or special characters** allowed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Saying "buckets are folders" — S3 has **no real folder hierarchy**. S3 buckets are **flat namespaces**; the `/` in object keys is purely cosmetic.

---
