## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of Amazon S3**, detailing the concepts of buckets, objects, keys, metadata, and versioning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **various S3 storage classes** with their use cases, availability, and minimum storage duration. How does choosing the right class impact cost? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a): Architecture of Amazon S3**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Amazon S3 is a regional, web-based object storage service offered by AWS. It is designed for **99.999999999% (11 nines) durability** and **99.99% availability** of objects.

**Bucket (1.5 marks):**
- A **bucket** is a top-level container for objects stored in S3.
- Each bucket has a **globally unique name** and is tied to a specific AWS **region** at creation time.
- An AWS account can have up to **100 buckets** by default (soft limit, can be increased).
- A bucket stores an **unlimited number of objects**.

**Object (1.5 marks):**
- An **object** is the fundamental entity in S3, composed of:
  - **Data** — the actual binary content (0 bytes to 5 TB).
  - **Key** — the unique identifier for the object within the bucket (e.g., `documents/report.pdf`).
  - **Metadata** — descriptive information (system metadata: `Content-Type`, `Last-Modified`; user metadata: custom key-value pairs).
  - **Version ID** — assigned automatically if versioning is enabled.

**Keys and Pseudo-Hierarchies (1 mark):**
- Keys are **flat strings**; S3 does not have a true folder structure.
- A key like `photos/2024/jan/img1.jpg` looks like a folder path, but `/` is just a character in the name.
- Tools like the S3 Console and CLI simulate folders using prefix-based listing.

**Versioning (1 mark):**
- **Versioning** is a bucket-level feature that keeps **multiple variants of the same object** in the same bucket.
- When enabled, S3 assigns a unique **Version ID** to each variant.
- Protects against **accidental deletion or overwrites**.
- Once enabled, versioning **cannot be suspended — only further versioning disabled**.

**Additional Features (1 mark):**
- **Lifecycle policies** for automated class transitions.
- **Replication** for cross-region or cross-account copies.
- **Access control** via IAM, bucket policies, ACLs, and pre-signed URLs.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b): S3 Storage Classes**

**Overview (1 mark):**
S3 provides **7 storage classes** designed to balance **cost, availability, durability, and retrieval latency**.

**Detailed Table (4 marks):**

| Storage Class | Use Case | AZs | Availability | Min Duration | Retrieval Time |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **S3 Standard** | Frequently accessed | ≥3 | 99.99% | None | Milliseconds |
| **S3 Intelligent-Tiering** | Changing access patterns | ≥3 | 99.9% | None | Milliseconds |
| **S3 Standard-IA** | Infrequently accessed, rapid access | ≥3 | 99.9% | 30 days | Milliseconds |
| **S3 One Zone-IA** | Non-critical infrequent data | 1 | 99.5% | 30 days | Milliseconds |
| **S3 Glacier Instant** | Archive, millisecond retrieval | ≥3 | 99.9% | 90 days | Milliseconds |
| **S3 Glacier Flexible** | Archive, hours-long retrieval | ≥3 | 99.99% | 90 days | Minutes to hours |
| **S3 Glacier Deep Archive** | Long-term cold archive | ≥3 | 99.99% | 180 days | 12–48 hours |

**Cost Impact (2 marks):**
- **Hot data (Standard)** costs ~$0.023/GB-month.
- **Cold data (Glacier Deep Archive)** costs ~$0.00099/GB-month — **~95% cheaper**.
- **Lifecycle policies** automate transitions, yielding **up to 70-80% savings** on long-term storage.
- **Trade-off:** Lower-cost classes have **higher retrieval fees and minimum storage duration penalties**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention the **minimum storage duration** penalty. If you store an object in Standard-IA for 20 days and delete it, you are **still charged for 30 days**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this omission.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **working of AWS Lambda** and its event-driven architecture. Mention key configuration parameters like memory, timeout, and concurrency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **EC2, AWS Lambda, and AWS Fargate** as cloud compute services. In which scenario would you recommend each? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a): AWS Lambda Working**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
AWS Lambda is a **serverless, event-driven compute service** that runs your code in response to events without requiring server management.

**Working with Diagram (3 marks):**

The flow is as follows:
1. **User uploads code** (zip or container image) to Lambda.
2. **Event source** (S3, API Gateway, DynamoDB, SQS, etc.) triggers the function.
3. Lambda's **execution environment** is spun up in milliseconds.
4. The function **executes** with allocated memory and CPU.
5. Logs are sent to **CloudWatch Logs**.
6. **IAM role** authorizes the function to access other AWS resources.
7. User is **billed per request and per GB-second of compute**.

**Key Configuration Parameters (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Range / Value | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Memory** | 128 MB – 10,240 MB | Determines RAM and proportional CPU |
| **Timeout** | 1 s – 900 s (15 min) | Max execution time |
| **Ephemeral Storage** | 512 MB – 10,240 MB | Temporary `/tmp` disk space |
| **Package Size** | 50 MB (zip) / 250 MB (unzipped) | Deployment artifact limit |
| **Concurrency** | 1,000 default (soft limit) | Parallel executions |
| **Environment Variables** | 4 KB total | Configuration injection |
| **Runtime** | Node.js, Python, Java, Go, .NET, Ruby, custom | Supported language |

---

#### **Model Solution for (b): Comparison of EC2, Lambda, Fargate**

**Tabular Comparison (4 marks):**

| Feature | EC2 | Lambda | Fargate |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Service Model** | IaaS | FaaS (Serverless) | Containers (Serverless) |
| **Unit of Deployment** | VM | Function | Container |
| **Abstraction** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Scaling** | Manual / ASG | Automatic (per request) | Automatic (per task) |
| **Pricing** | Per-hour instance | Per-request + GB-sec | Per-vCPU + GB-sec |
| **Max Runtime** | Unlimited | 15 minutes | Configurable |
| **State** | Stateful | Stateless | Stateful possible |
| **OS Control** | Full | None | Container-level |
| **Startup Time** | Minutes | Milliseconds | Seconds |

**Scenario-Based Recommendation (3 marks):**

1. **Use EC2** when:
   - You need full control over the OS, kernel, or hardware.
   - Workloads are long-running and stateful (e.g., databases, legacy enterprise apps).
   - You require GPU acceleration for HPC or ML training.

2. **Use Lambda** when:
   - Workloads are event-driven, short-lived (e.g., image resizing, log processing, IoT data).
   - Traffic is unpredictable or spiky.
   - You want to minimize operational overhead and pay only for execution time.

3. **Use Fargate** when:
   - You have containerized microservices that need to run for hours or days.
   - You want container benefits without managing EC2 clusters.
   - You are migrating from Kubernetes (EKS) or Docker (ECS) and need serverless orchestration.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Examiners often include a follow-up like *"Why is Fargate more expensive than EC2 for steady-state workloads?"* — students must mention that Fargate bills **per-second with no Reserved Instance discounts** equivalent to EC2's pricing model.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Topic-Wise

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes Students Make on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing S3 with EBS or EFS** — S3 is **object storage** (HTTP-based, not mounted), EBS is **block storage** (mounted to EC2), EFS is **file storage** (NFS-based). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Writing "S3 has folders"** — S3 has **flat namespaces**; what looks like a folder is a key prefix.
> 3. **Ignoring the 11 nines of durability** — Always quote the **99.999999999% durability** for Standard tier.
> 4. **Forgetting minimum storage duration penalties** — Standard-IA = 30 days, Glacier = 90 days, Deep Archive = 180 days.
> 5. **Calling Lambda "the same as EC2"** — Lambda is **FaaS**, stateless, max 15 minutes, billed per invocation. EC2 is **IaaS**, stateful, billed per hour.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### **Advanced Compute Services — Key Takeaways**

- **Lambda** is event-driven, stateless, max **15 minutes** runtime, billed per **GB-second**.
- **Lambda memory** is **128 MB – 10,240 MB**, and CPU scales proportionally with memory.
- **Fargate** is serverless container orchestration (with ECS/EKS) — bills per **vCPU-second + GB-second**.
- Advanced compute services are differentiated by **abstraction, auto-scaling, and per-use billing**.
- The **Lambda cost formula** is:
$$C = (N_{req} \times P_{req}) + (T \times M \times P_{GBs} \times N_{req})$$

### **Amazon S3 — Key Takeaways**

- **S3 = Object Storage**, not block or file storage.
- **11 nines of durability** for Standard tier = **99.999999999%**.
- **Bucket** = top-level container; **Object** = data + key + metadata + version ID.
- **Bucket names** = 3–63 chars, lowercase, globally unique, no IP format.
- **Object max size** = **5 TB**; **single PUT max** = **5 GB**; use multi-part upload for larger.
- **7 Storage Classes**: Standard, Intelligent-Tiering, Standard-IA, One Zone-IA, Glacier Instant, Glacier Flexible, Glacier Deep Archive.
- **Minimum storage duration**: 30 days (IA), 90 days (Glacier), 180 days (Deep Archive).
- **S3 versioning** protects against accidental overwrites/deletes.
- **Lifecycle policies** automate class transitions for cost optimization.
- **Pre-signed URLs** grant time-limited access (1 second to 7 days).
- **S3 replication** supports same-region, cross-region, and cross-account modes.
- **Consistency model**: S3 is now **strongly consistent** for all read-after-write operations (since 2020).
- **Access control** layers: **IAM policies, Bucket policies, ACLs, Pre-signed URLs, S3 Access Points**.

### **High-Frequency KTU Keywords to Memorize**

- Object, Bucket, Key, Metadata, Versioning, Lifecycle Policy, Replication
- Pre-signed URL, Storage Class, Lifecycle Rule, Multipart Upload
- Lambda, FaaS, Event-Driven, Concurrency, Cold Start
- Fargate, EKS, ECS, Container-as-a-Service
- 11 nines, 99.99% availability, Flat Namespace

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For any S3 question, **always draw a diagram** showing the bucket-object relationship, list 2–3 storage classes with their use cases, and conclude with a one-line cost/performance trade-off statement. This guarantees full marks in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS). Google Cloud Platform - IaaS Offerings: Compute Engine (GCE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — STORAGE & IaaS COMPUTE SERVICES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Amazon Elastic Block Store (EBS)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
Amazon Elastic Block Store (EBS) is a high-performance, **block-level storage service** designed for use with Amazon EC2 instances. EBS provides persistently attached raw block storage volumes that can be formatted with a file system, mounted as a device, and offer low-latency, high-throughput access to data across an **Availability Zone (AZ)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** EBS is **AZ-scoped**, meaning a volume created in `us-east-1a` cannot be attached to an instance in `us-east-1b` without first creating a snapshot and copying it across AZs.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Persistent USB Drive"** 🔌
Think of an EC2 instance as a **laptop** and EBS as a **high-speed, durable USB SSD** plugged into it.
- The **instance's local storage (instance store)** is like the laptop's RAM/SSD — fast, but *vanishes the moment you power off the laptop*.
- An **EBS volume** is like a USB SSD — it survives shutdowns, can be detached from one laptop, and reattached to another.
- **Snapshots** are like *incremental backups* of that USB drive sent to a vault (S3), allowing point-in-time recovery.
- The catch? You can only plug the USB into a laptop sitting in the **same rack (Availability Zone)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Properties of EBS:**
> - **Block-level** — operates at the block device layer (not object/file).
> - **Persistent** — independent of EC2 instance lifecycle.
> - **AZ-scoped** — confined to a single Availability Zone.
> - **Replicated within AZ** — automatically replicates within the AZ for durability.
> - **Encryption** — supports AES-256 encryption at rest and in transit.

---

### 1.2 Google Compute Engine (GCE)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
Google Compute Engine (GCE) is the **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** compute offering of Google Cloud Platform (GCP) that delivers secure, configurable **virtual machines (VMs)** running in Google's global data centers. GCE supports custom machine types, live migration, preemptible/spot VMs, and integration with Google’s global fiber network.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Modular, Hot-Swappable Server Rack"** 🖥️
Imagine Google’s data center as a **gigantic, climate-controlled server warehouse**. GCE lets you rent:
- A **specific rack slot** (a VM) with the exact number of CPU cores, RAM, and disks you want.
- You can **swap in extra RAM, GPUs, or TPUs** at any time (custom machine types).
- If the physical host needs maintenance, your VM is **lifted mid-flight** to another host without restarting — this is **Live Migration**, like a chef being moved to a different stove *without* dropping the pan.
- You can also rent **discounted "spot" VMs** that Google may reclaim in seconds — perfect for batch jobs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Properties of GCE:**
> - **IaaS** — full control over OS, middleware, runtime.
> - **Per-second billing** — with a 1-minute minimum.
> - **Live migration** — VMs move across physical hosts during maintenance.
> - **Global VPC networking** — VMs can communicate across regions privately.
> - **Custom machine types** — choose exact vCPU and RAM.
> - **Sustained Use & Committed Use Discounts** — automatic cost reductions.

---

### 1.3 The Big Picture: Why These Services Matter

| Feature | Amazon EBS | Google Compute Engine |
|---|---|---|
| **Service Type** | Block Storage (used *with* EC2) | IaaS Compute (creates VMs) |
| **Scope** | AZ-scoped | Global (regions/zones) |
| **Persistence** | Highly persistent (99.999% durability) | VMs are ephemeral by default; persistent disks available |
| **Analogy** | Detachable USB SSD | Modular server rack with hot-swap VMs |
| **Sibling Service** | EC2 (compute) | Persistent Disk (storage) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pointer:** The student must clearly distinguish that **EBS is a storage service** providing volumes *to* compute instances, whereas **GCE is a compute service** that *consumes* storage (Persistent Disk) and network resources.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Amazon EBS — Architecture & Volume Types

### 2.1.1 Architectural Components

EBS operates through a **distributed, replication-based architecture**:

- **EBS Volume:** A virtual block device exposed to EC2 as `/dev/sdX` (Linux) or `/dev/xvdX` (Windows).
- **EBS Backend Infrastructure:** Each volume is automatically replicated within its AZ (typically across multiple physical servers) to prevent data loss.
- **EBS Snapshot:** An **incremental, point-in-time copy** of a volume stored in Amazon S3. Only changed blocks since the previous snapshot are saved.
- **EBS Encryption:** Uses **AES-256-XTS** algorithm. Encryption keys managed via **AWS KMS** (Key Management Service).
- **EBS Direct APIs:** Allow uploading raw blocks to EBS without EC2.
- **EBS Multi-Attach:** Allows attaching a single provisioned IOPS SSD volume to up to **16 EC2 Nitro instances** in the *same AZ* (for cluster-aware applications).

### 2.1.2 EBS Volume Types (5 Main Categories)

| Volume Type | Use Case | Volume Size | Max IOPS/Volume | Max Throughput/Volume | Latency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **gp3 (General Purpose SSD)** | Boot volumes, dev/test, low-latency apps | 1 GiB – 16 TiB | 16,000 (baseline 3,000) | 1,000 MiB/s | Single-digit ms |
| **io2 Block Express** | Mission-critical, 64,000 IOPS, highest durability | 4 GiB – 64 TiB | 256,000 (with Block Express) | 4,000 MiB/s | Sub-ms |
| **io1 (Provisioned IOPS SSD)** | I/O-intensive databases (Oracle, SQL) | 4 GiB – 16 TiB | 64,000 | 1,000 MiB/s | Single-digit ms |
| **st1 (Throughput Optimized HDD)** | Big data, log processing, data warehouses | 125 GiB – 16 TiB | 500 | 500 MiB/s | ms-range |
| **sc1 (Cold HDD)** | Infrequently accessed data, lowest cost | 125 GiB – 16 TiB | 250 | 250 MiB/s | ms-range |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** SSD-backed volumes (gp3, io1, io2) are billed on **IOPS provisioned**; HDD-backed volumes (st1, sc1) are billed on **throughput provisioned**. Boot volumes are always gp2/gp3.

### 2.1.3 EBS Performance Formulas (High-Yield for Numerical Problems)

> [!NOTE]
> **EBS IOPS Calculation:**
>
> $$\text{IOPS}_{\text{actual}} = \min\left( \text{IOPS}_{\text{provisioned}},\ \frac{\text{Throughput}_{\text{actual}} \times 1024}{\text{I/O Size in KiB}} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **EBS Throughput Calculation (for SSD volumes):**
>
> $$\text{Throughput}_{\text{max}} = \min\left( \text{Volume Throughput Limit},\ \frac{\text{IOPS} \times \text{I/O Size in KB}}{1024} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **EBS Cost Calculation (Monthly):**
>
> $$C_{\text{monthly}} = (S_{\text{GB}} \times P_{\text{GB-month}}) + (I_{\text{provisioned}} \times P_{\text{IOPS-month}}) + (T_{\text{MiB/s}} \times P_{\text{MiB/s-month}})$$

Where:
- $S_{\text{GB}}$ = Storage size in GB
- $P_{\text{GB-month}}$ = Price per GB-month (e.g., \$0.10 for gp3 in US-East-1)
- $I_{\text{provisioned}}$ = Provisioned IOPS above baseline
- $T_{\text{MiB/s}}$ = Provisioned throughput above baseline
- $P_{\text{IOPS-month}}$, $P_{\text{MiB/s-month}}$ = Respective unit prices

### 2.1.4 EBS Snapshots — How They Work

1. **First snapshot:** Copies **all** blocks of the volume to S3.
2. **Subsequent snapshots:** Copy only **changed blocks** (incremental).
3. **Deletion logic:** Deleting an *intermediate* snapshot does **not** remove data; it merely removes the reference. The data is retained until *all* snapshots referencing it are deleted.
4. **Cross-region/cross-AZ:** Snapshots can be **copied** across regions and AZs to enable global data distribution.
5. **Fast Snapshot Restore (FSR):** Eliminates the I/O latency penalty when restoring large snapshots (at extra cost).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why incremental?** Reduces storage costs and snapshot creation time drastically. Example: A 1 TB volume with 50 GB changes takes only 50 GB of snapshot storage (plus metadata).

---

## 2.2 Google Compute Engine (GCE) — Machine Types & Architecture

### 2.2.1 GCE Machine Family Taxonomy

| Family | Code Letter | Best For | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **General Purpose** | E2, N2, N2D, T2D | Balanced workloads, web servers, app servers | e2-standard-4, n2-standard-8 |
| **Compute-Optimized** | C2, C2D, H3 | Gaming, HPC, scientific computing | c2-standard-16, c2d-highmem-32 |
| **Memory-Optimized** | M1, M2, M3, M4 | In-memory DBs (SAP HANA, Redis), analytics | m1-ultramem-40, m2-ultramem-208 |
| **Accelerator-Optimized** | A2, A3, G2 | ML training, inference, GPU-bound workloads | a2-highgpu-1g, a3-highgpu-8g |
| **Storage-Optimized** | Z3 | Local SSD-heavy, streaming, log processing | z3-highmem-8-ssd |

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming Convention:** `[family]-[series]-[vCPUs]` with optional suffixes.
> - `n2-standard-4` → N2 family, standard series, **4 vCPUs, 16 GB RAM**.
> - `c2d-highcpu-56` → C2D family, high-CPU series, **56 vCPUs, 56 GB RAM**.

### 2.2.2 GCE Pricing Models (3-Form Discount Structure)

| Discount Type | Description | Discount Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Sustained Use Discount (SUD)** | Automatic discount for VMs running a significant portion of a month | Up to **20% for N1**, up to **52.5% for E2** |
| **Committed Use Discount (CUD)** | Contract-based commitment (1-yr or 3-yr) for predictable workloads | Up to **57%** for 3-yr commitment on N2 |
| **Spot VMs (Preemptible)** | Excess inventory, can be reclaimed with 30-sec notice | Up to **80–91% off** standard pricing |

> [!NOTE]
> **GCE Cost Formula (Effective Hourly Rate):**
>
> $$\text{Effective Rate} = \text{List Price} \times (1 - \text{SUD}\%) \times (1 - \text{CUD}\%) \times \text{Spot Factor}$$
>
> Where Spot Factor $\in [0.10,\ 0.30]$ for typical workloads.

### 2.2.3 GCE Live Migration — How It Works

Live migration is a **black-art** of hypervisor engineering that Google pioneered:

1. A physical host signals maintenance (hardware fault, OS patch, cooling).
2. The hypervisor copies the running VM's **memory pages** to a new host *while the VM continues running*.
3. Modified pages are tracked via a **pre-copy** technique — pages modified during copy are re-copied iteratively.
4. Once memory converges to a near-zero delta, the VM is **paused for ~60–300 ms** (the "freeze time"), the final state is synced, and the VM resumes on the new host.
5. The IP address, MAC address, and active connections are **preserved** — no client-side reconnect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Live migration requires no downtime and is **transparent to running applications**. It differs from VM snapshots, which capture state to disk.

### 2.2.4 Preemptible / Spot VMs

- **Preemptible VMs** (older term) run for **max 24 hours**.
- **Spot VMs** (newer) have no 24-hr cap but can be preempted at any time.
- Google provides a **30-second warning** before reclamation.
- Cannot live-migrate, cannot auto-restart, and aren't covered by SLAs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Use Cases:** Batch processing, fault-tolerant microservices, CI/CD build workers, ML hyperparameter sweeps, distributed rendering.

---

## 2.3 Comparative Analysis: EBS vs Persistent Disk (GCE's Storage Backbone)

| Aspect | Amazon EBS | Google Persistent Disk |
|---|---|---|
| **Min Size** | 1 GiB (gp3), 4 GiB (io1) | 10 GB |
| **Max Size/Volume** | 64 TiB (io2 Block Express) | 64 TB (Extreme PD), 100 TB (Hyperdisk) |
| **Snapshot Storage** | S3 (incremental) | Cloud Storage (incremental) |
| **Multi-Attach** | Up to 16 instances (io1/io2) | Multi-writer mode for regional PD |
| **Encryption** | AES-256 (KMS-managed) | AES-128/256 (KMS-managed) |
| **Live Resize** | Yes (modifying volumes) | Yes (no detach required) |
| **Regional Replication** | Cross-AZ snapshots | Synchronous regional PD (zonal + regional) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: EBS Cost Estimation (7-Mark Standard)

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
A startup deploys an application on EC2 with the following requirements:
- 2 TB EBS volume of type `io1` (provisioned IOPS SSD)
- 10,000 provisioned IOPS
- Located in `us-east-1`
- Assume pricing:
  - \$0.125 per GB-month for io1 storage
  - \$0.065 per provisioned IOPS-month

Calculate the **monthly EBS cost**.

---

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Convert storage size to GB.**

$$S_{\text{GB}} = 2 \text{ TB} \times 1024 \text{ GB/TB} = 2048 \text{ GB}$$

*Explanation:* EBS billing uses **decimal** when measured in TB but **binary** when measured in GiB. The official pricing unit is GB (1 GB = $10^9$ bytes), so we use the $1 \text{ TB} = 1024 \text{ GB}$ conversion as per AWS documentation practice.

**Step 2: Calculate the storage cost component.**

$$C_{\text{storage}} = S_{\text{GB}} \times P_{\text{GB-month}} = 2048 \times \$0.125$$

Let us expand this multiplication:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{storage}} &= 2048 \times 0.125 \\
&= 2048 \times \left(\frac{1}{8}\right) \\
&= \frac{2048}{8} \\
&= \$256.00 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* The factor **0.125 = 1/8** simplifies the arithmetic. This is the storage cost for keeping 2 TB on io1.

**Step 3: Calculate the provisioned IOPS cost component.**

$$C_{\text{IOPS}} = I_{\text{provisioned}} \times P_{\text{IOPS-month}} = 10{,}000 \times \$0.065$$

Let us expand this multiplication:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{IOPS}} &= 10{,}000 \times 0.065 \\
&= 10{,}000 \times \left(\frac{65}{1000}\right) \\
&= \frac{10{,}000 \times 65}{1000} \\
&= 10 \times 65 \\
&= \$650.00 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* This covers the guaranteed IOPS (input/output operations per second) that AWS reserves for the volume regardless of whether you use them.

**Step 4: Sum the two components.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total}} &= C_{\text{storage}} + C_{\text{IOPS}} \\
&= \$256.00 + \$650.00 \\
&= \$906.00 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** The monthly EBS cost is **\$906.00** (excluding data transfer, snapshot, and any additional throughput charges).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: EBS Throughput Bound Calculation (Analytical)

**Problem Statement:**
A `gp3` EBS volume is provisioned with **6,000 IOPS** and **250 MiB/s throughput**. The application issues I/O requests of **16 KiB** each. Calculate the **achievable IOPS** limited by throughput.

---

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Recall the throughput-bound IOPS formula.**

$$\text{IOPS}_{\text{throughput-bound}} = \frac{\text{Throughput}_{\text{MiB/s}} \times 1024 \text{ KiB/MiB}}{\text{I/O Size in KiB}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IOPS}_{\text{throughput-bound}} &= \frac{250 \text{ MiB/s} \times 1024 \text{ KiB/MiB}}{16 \text{ KiB}} \\
&= \frac{256{,}000 \text{ KiB/s}}{16 \text{ KiB}} \\
&= 16{,}000 \text{ IOPS}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compare with provisioned IOPS to find the actual achievable IOPS.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IOPS}_{\text{actual}} &= \min(\text{IOPS}_{\text{provisioned}},\ \text{IOPS}_{\text{throughput-bound}}) \\
&= \min(6000,\ 16000) \\
&= 6000 \text{ IOPS}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Conclusion.**
The volume is **IOPS-bound**, not throughput-bound. The application can fully achieve 6,000 IOPS at 16 KiB per I/O without hitting the throughput ceiling.

$$\text{Actual Throughput} = 6000 \times \frac{16 \text{ KiB}}{1024 \text{ KiB/MiB}} = 93.75 \text{ MiB/s}$$

This is well below the 250 MiB/s ceiling, confirming IOPS is the bottleneck.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: GCE Cost with Sustained Use Discount

**Problem Statement:**
A company runs **4 × n2-standard-8** VMs continuously for an entire month (720 hours) in `us-central1`. The list price is **\$0.4713/hour per vCPU** and **\$0.0631/hour per GB RAM**.

For an **n2-standard-8**: 8 vCPUs + 32 GB RAM.

Compute the **monthly cost with Sustained Use Discount (SUD)**, which gives **20% off** for full-month N2 usage.

---

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Compute the vCPU hourly list price for one VM.**

$$P_{\text{vCPU, hour}} = 8 \text{ vCPUs} \times \$0.4713/\text{vCPU-hour} = \$3.7704/\text{hour}$$

**Step 2: Compute the RAM hourly list price for one VM.**

$$P_{\text{RAM, hour}} = 32 \text{ GB} \times \$0.0631/\text{GB-hour} = \$2.0192/\text{hour}$$

**Step 3: Compute the total hourly cost per VM.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{VM, hour}} &= P_{\text{vCPU, hour}} + P_{\text{RAM, hour}} \\
&= \$3.7704 + \$2.0192 \\
&= \$5.7896 \text{ per hour}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute monthly list price for 4 VMs over 720 hours.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{monthly, list}} &= 4 \text{ VMs} \times 720 \text{ hours} \times \$5.7896/\text{hour} \\
&= 2880 \times \$5.7896 \\
&= \$16{,}673.97
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Apply the 20% Sustained Use Discount.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{monthly, with SUD}} &= \$16{,}673.97 \times (1 - 0.20) \\
&= \$16{,}673.97 \times 0.80 \\
&= \$13{,}339.18
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Annualized cost.**

$$C_{\text{annual}} = \$13{,}339.18 \times 12 = \$160{,}070.16 \text{ per year}$$

**Conclusion:** The sustained use discount saves approximately **\$3,334.79/month** for this workload — significant savings without any commitment.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Choose Which Volume / Machine Type

| Application Workload | Recommended AWS EBS | Recommended GCE Machine | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MySQL OLTP database** | io1 / io2 Block Express | N2-highmem + SSD PD | Predictable low-latency IOPS |
| **Big data log analytics** | st1 | Z3 family with local SSD | High throughput, sequential I/O |
| **Web server boot volume** | gp3 | E2-standard | Cost-effective, balanced |
| **In-memory cache (Redis)** | gp3 (small) | M1-ultramem | RAM-intensive |
| **ML training with GPU** | io2 (data volume) | A2-highgpu / A3 | GPU-accelerated, high IOPS for dataset streaming |
| **Cold archival data** | sc1 | Archive PD (cheapest) | Lowest cost, infrequent access |
| **Mission-critical Oracle RAC** | io2 Block Express + Multi-Attach | M2-ultramem (cluster) | Cluster-aware, high IOPS, sub-ms latency |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EBS Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph region["AWS Region us-east-1"]
        subgraph azA["Availability Zone us-east-1a"]
            ec2A1["EC2 Instance 1 Nitro Hypervisor"]
            ec2A2["EC2 Instance 2"]
            volA1["EBS Volume gp3 1 TB"]
            volA2["EBS Volume io1 500 GB"]
            volA3["EBS Volume st1 4 TB"]
        end
        subgraph azB["Availability Zone us-east-1b"]
            ec2B1["EC2 Instance 3"]
            volB1["EBS Volume gp2 100 GB"]
        end
    end
    s3["Amazon S3 Snapshot Storage"]
    kms["AWS KMS Encryption Keys"]
    cw["CloudWatch Monitoring Metrics"]

    ec2A1 --> volA1
    ec2A2 --> volA2
    ec2A1 --> volA3
    ec2B1 --> volB1

    volA1 -.-> s3
    volA2 -.-> s3
    volA3 -.-> s3
    volB1 -.-> s3

    volA1 -.encrypt.-> kms
    volA2 -.encrypt.-> kms

    volA1 -.metrics.-> cw
    volA2 -.metrics.-> cw

    azA -.cross-AZ snapshot copy.-> azB
    s3 -.restore.-> volA1
```

**Diagram Explanation:**
- EC2 instances within the **same AZ** can attach EBS volumes.
- Snapshots are stored in **S3** (a separate, region-wide service).
- Cross-AZ usage requires **snapshot copy + new volume creation**.
- **CloudWatch** monitors IOPS, throughput, and queue depth.

---

## 4.2 GCE Architecture — Compute Engine Resource Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    org["GCP Organization Node"]
    folder["Folder Project Group"]
    proj["GCP Project pay-billing-account"]
    region["Region us-central1"]
    zone1["Zone us-central1-a"]
    zone2["Zone us-central1-b"]

    org --> folder --> proj
    proj --> region
    region --> zone1
    region --> zone2

    subgraph zone1detail["Zone us-central1-a Resources"]
        mig["Managed Instance Group MIG"]
        vm1["VM n2-standard-4"]
        vm2["VM n2-standard-4"]
        vm3["VM c2-highcpu-8"]
        pd1["Persistent Disk SSD 200 GB"]
        pd2["Local SSD 375 GB"]
    end

    subgraph zone2detail["Zone us-central1-b Resources"]
        vm4["VM n2-standard-8"]
        pd3["Regional Persistent Disk 500 GB"]
    end

    zone1 --> zone1detail
    zone2 --> zone2detail

    mig --> vm1
    mig --> vm2
    mig --> vm3
    vm1 --> pd1
    vm2 --> pd1
    vm3 --> pd2
    vm4 --> pd3

    net["VPC Network custom-subnet"]
    lb["Cloud Load Balancer HTTP S"]
    net -. attaches .-> vm1
    net -. attaches .-> vm2
    net -. attaches .-> vm3
    net -. attaches .-> vm4
    lb -. distributes to .-> mig
```

**Diagram Explanation:**
- GCP resources are organized in a **4-level hierarchy**: Organization → Folder → Project → Resource.
- A **VPC network** spans all zones in a region (and can be global).
- **Managed Instance Groups (MIGs)** provide auto-scaling and auto-healing.
- **Local SSD** is physically attached to the host (faster, but ephemeral).
- **Regional Persistent Disk** provides synchronous replication across two zones.

---

## 4.3 EBS Snapshot Lifecycle (Incremental Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vol["EBS Volume Original 100 GB"]
    s1["Snapshot S1 Day 1 All 100 GB"]
    s2["Snapshot S2 Day 2 Changed 10 GB"]
    s3["Snapshot S3 Day 3 Changed 5 GB"]
    s4["Snapshot S4 Day 4 Changed 20 GB"]
    s5["Snapshot S5 Day 5 Changed 8 GB"]

    vol --> s1
    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4
    s4 --> s5

    s1 -.contains all data.-> s2
    s2 -.references s1 plus changes.-> s3
    s3 -.references s2 plus changes.-> s4
    s4 -.references s3 plus changes.-> s5

    delS3["Delete Snapshot S3"]
    s3 --> delS3
    delS3 -.merges blocks into S4 and S5.-> s4
```

**Explanation:** Each snapshot only stores the **delta** from the previous one. Deleting a middle snapshot does not destroy data — the next snapshot "absorbs" the orphaned blocks, ensuring no data loss.

---

## 4.4 GCE Live Migration Process (Stepwise Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    hostA["Physical Host A Maintenance Required"]
    hostB["Physical Host B Healthy Target"]
    vm["VM Running on Host A"]
    mem1["Memory Page Tracking Begins"]
    mem2["Iterative Pre-copy of Memory Pages"]
    mem3["Final Dirty Page Sync Paused for 60-300 ms"]
    mem4["VM Resumes on Host B Resume Traffic"]

    hostA --> vm
    vm --> mem1 --> mem2 --> mem3 --> mem4
    mem4 --> hostB
```

**Explanation:** Live migration is **transparent** — the VM's IP, MAC, and active TCP sessions are preserved. The brief pause (sub-second) is typically undetectable by applications.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Modeled on KTU 2024 Scheme Part A pattern — concise, definition-based questions.**

---

### Q1. Differentiate between Amazon EBS and EC2 Instance Store. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Amazon EBS | EC2 Instance Store |
|---|---|---|
| **Persistence** | Data persists after instance termination | Data is **lost** when instance stops/terminates |
| **Scope** | Network-attached, survives instance failure | Physically attached to the host machine |
| **Performance** | Moderate (network-based) | Very high (local NVMe) |
| **Use Case** | Databases, boot volumes, persistent state | Temporary buffers, caches, scratch space |
| **Backup** | Supported via snapshots to S3 | No built-in backup |
| **Elasticity** | Can be resized and re-attached | Fixed size tied to instance type |

**One-line:** EBS provides **durable**, network-attached block storage; Instance Store provides **ephemeral**, high-speed local storage.

---

### Q2. What is a Spot VM (Preemptible VM) in Google Compute Engine? Mention any two use cases. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Spot VM** (formerly Preemptible VM) in GCE is a virtual machine instance that uses Google's **excess spare compute capacity** at a significantly reduced price (up to **80–91% discount**). However, Google can **reclaim the VM at any time with a 30-second warning**. These VMs are **not** covered by the GCE SLA and cannot live-migrate.

**Two Use Cases:**
1. **Batch processing jobs** — e.g., nightly log analysis that can be re-run on failure.
2. **CI/CD build workers** — short-lived compilation/test tasks where a restart is acceptable.
3. (Bonus) **ML hyperparameter tuning** — distributed training of small models where fault tolerance is built in.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — EBS Architecture & Performance

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of Amazon EBS with a neat block diagram. List the different types of EBS volumes and their key characteristics.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An EC2 instance uses a 1 TB `gp3` EBS volume with 4,000 provisioned IOPS and 500 MiB/s throughput. The application issues random I/O of 32 KiB per request. Calculate:
1. The IOPS limit imposed by the throughput.
2. The actual achievable IOPS.
3. Whether the workload is IOPS-bound or throughput-bound.
4. Suggest an optimization if the workload is bound by IOPS.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a):

**EBS Architecture (Block Diagram — refer Section 4.1):**

Amazon EBS architecture consists of:
- **EBS Volumes:** Block storage devices attached to EC2 instances over a high-speed internal network.
- **EBS Backend Service:** Replicates volume data within an Availability Zone (AZ) for fault tolerance.
- **EBS Snapshot Service:** Stores incremental backups of volumes in Amazon S3.
- **EBS Encryption Module:** Uses AES-256-XTS with keys from AWS KMS.
- **CloudWatch Metrics:** Monitors IOPS, throughput, queue depth, and latency.

**Key EBS Volume Types (3 Marks):**

| Type | Backend | Use Case | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **gp3** | SSD | Boot, dev/test, low-latency apps | Baseline 3,000 IOPS free |
| **io1 / io2 Block Express** | SSD | I/O-intensive DBs | Provisioned IOPS, low latency |
| **st1** | HDD | Big data, log processing | High throughput (500 MiB/s) |
| **sc1** | HDD | Cold data, archive | Lowest cost |

**Connectivity Constraints (1 Mark):**
- Volumes are **AZ-scoped**.
- Multi-Attach allows up to 16 Nitro instances in **same AZ** for io1/io2.
- Cross-AZ requires snapshot copy and new volume creation.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b):

**Given:**
- Volume type: gp3, Size: 1 TB, Provisioned IOPS: 4,000, Throughput: 500 MiB/s, I/O size: 32 KiB.

**Step 1: Apply the throughput-bound IOPS formula.**

$$\text{IOPS}_{\text{throughput-bound}} = \frac{\text{Throughput} \times 1024 \text{ KiB/MiB}}{\text{I/O Size}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IOPS}_{\text{throughput-bound}} &= \frac{500 \times 1024}{32} \\
&= \frac{512{,}000}{32} \\
&= 16{,}000 \text{ IOPS}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Find the actual achievable IOPS.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IOPS}_{\text{actual}} &= \min(\text{IOPS}_{\text{provisioned}},\ \text{IOPS}_{\text{throughput-bound}}) \\
&= \min(4000,\ 16000) \\
&= 4000 \text{ IOPS}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Determine bottleneck.**

Since $\text{IOPS}_{\text{actual}} = \text{IOPS}_{\text{provisioned}} = 4000$ (the throughput allows 16,000 IOPS, but only 4,000 are provisioned), the workload is **IOPS-bound** (or rather, capped at the provisioned limit).

**Step 5: Optimization suggestion.**

- **Increase provisioned IOPS** to a higher value (e.g., 10,000) to leverage the available 500 MiB/s throughput.
- After increasing, recalculate: at 10,000 IOPS × 32 KiB = 320,000 KiB/s = **312.5 MiB/s**, still under 500 MiB/s ceiling. So 10,000 IOPS is achievable.
- Alternative: Migrate to **io1 or io2** for higher guaranteed IOPS if the workload demands it.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — GCE Machine Types & Pricing

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different machine families in Google Compute Engine with suitable examples. Discuss the concept of live migration in GCE.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A research lab runs 10 × `n2-highmem-8` VMs for a 2-year ML training project. The VMs run 24/7. Compute the monthly cost assuming:
- List price: \$0.5572/hour per vCPU, \$0.0743/hour per GB RAM.
- `n2-highmem-8`: 8 vCPUs, 64 GB RAM.
- 720 hours/month.
- **Sustained Use Discount** of 20% (full month N2).
- **3-year Committed Use Discount** of 55% (replacing the SUD for full term).

Compare the two discount scenarios and recommend the best approach.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a):

**GCE Machine Families (5 Marks):**

1. **General Purpose (E2, N2, N2D, T2D):** Balanced compute/memory ratio.
   - Example: `n2-standard-4` — 4 vCPUs, 16 GB RAM.
   - Use: Web servers, app servers, microservices.

2. **Compute-Optimized (C2, C2D, H3):** Highest performance per core.
   - Example: `c2-standard-30` — 30 vCPUs, 120 GB RAM.
   - Use: Gaming, scientific simulations, HPC.

3. **Memory-Optimized (M1, M2, M3, M4):** High RAM-to-vCPU ratio (up to 12 GB/vCPU).
   - Example: `m2-ultramem-208` — 208 vCPUs, 5,888 GB RAM.
   - Use: SAP HANA, in-memory databases, big analytics.

4. **Accelerator-Optimized (A2, A3, G2):** GPU/TPU-bound.
   - Example: `a2-highgpu-1g` — 12 vCPUs, 85 GB RAM, 1× A100 GPU.
   - Use: ML training, deep learning inference.

5. **Storage-Optimized (Z3):** High local SSD, sequential throughput.
   - Example: `z3-highmem-8-ssd` — 8 vCPUs, 64 GB RAM, local SSD.
   - Use: Streaming, log processing, data analytics.

**Live Migration in GCE (2 Marks):**

Live migration is the transparent movement of a running VM from one physical host to another without service interruption. The process uses an **iterative pre-copy** of memory pages while the VM continues serving traffic. The VM is **paused for ~60–300 ms** during the final state sync, then resumed on the target host. IP address, MAC, and active TCP sessions are preserved. Triggers include hardware maintenance, kernel patches, and capacity rebalancing.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b):

**Given:**
- 10 × `n2-highmem-8` (8 vCPUs, 64 GB RAM per VM)
- Pricing: \$0.5572/vCPU-hr, \$0.0743/GB-RAM-hr
- 720 hours/month

**Step 1: Compute per-VM hourly list price.**

**vCPU cost per hour:**
$$P_{\text{vCPU}} = 8 \times \$0.5572 = \$4.4576/\text{hour}$$

**RAM cost per hour:**
$$P_{\text{RAM}} = 64 \times \$0.0743 = \$4.7552/\text{hour}$$

**Per-VM hourly list price:**
$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{VM, hour}} &= \$4.4576 + \$4.7552 = \$9.2128/\text{hour}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute monthly list price for 10 VMs (720 hours).**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{monthly, list}} &= 10 \times 720 \times \$9.2128 \\
&= 7200 \times \$9.2128 \\
&= \$66{,}332.16
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute cost with Sustained Use Discount (SUD = 20%).**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{SUD}} &= \$66{,}332.16 \times (1 - 0.20) \\
&= \$66{,}332.16 \times 0.80 \\
&= \$53{,}065.73 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}$$

Savings: \$66,332.16 − \$53,065.73 = **\$13,266.43/month**.

**Step 4: Compute cost with 3-year Committed Use Discount (CUD = 55%).**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{CUD}} &= \$66{,}332.16 \times (1 - 0.55) \\
&= \$66{,}332.16 \times 0.45 \\
&= \$29{,}849.47 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}$$

Savings: \$66,332.16 − \$29,849.47 = **\$36,482.69/month**.

**Step 5: Comparison Table.**

| Discount Type | Monthly Cost | Annual Cost | 2-Year Cost | Flexibility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **List Price (no discount)** | \$66,332.16 | \$795,985.92 | \$1,591,971.84 | Maximum |
| **Sustained Use (20%)** | \$53,065.73 | \$636,788.76 | \$1,273,577.52 | High (automatic) |
| **3-Year Committed (55%)** | \$29,849.47 | \$358,193.64 | \$716,387.28 | **Locked in** |

**Step 6: Recommendation.**

- For a **2-year ML training project with stable, predictable workloads**, the **3-year CUD is the best choice** — it offers the deepest discount (55% off) and the workload is long enough to justify the commitment.
- The SUD is preferable if the project might be **scaled down or terminated early**.
- A **hybrid strategy** could be: CUD for 70% of the capacity + on-demand (with SUD) for 30% burst capacity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing EBS with S3:** EBS is **block storage** (raw devices), S3 is **object storage** (files via HTTP API). Many students lose 1–2 marks by interchanging them.
> 2. **Forgetting AZ-Scoped nature of EBS:** Saying "EBS volumes can be attached to any EC2 instance" loses 1 mark. Always specify "in the same AZ."
> 3. **Wrong cost formula units:** Confusing GB-month with GiB-month. AWS bills in **GB** (1 GB = $10^9$ bytes), not GiB.
> 4. **Spot VM vs Preemptible VM:** Modern GCE uses "Spot VMs" (no 24-hr cap). The 24-hr limit applies only to *preemptible* VMs (older).
> 5. **Live Migration vs Snapshots:** Snapshots save state to disk (downtime). Live migration moves memory in real-time (no downtime).
> 6. **SUD vs CUD:** SUD is **automatic** but capped (max 20–52.5%). CUD requires a **contract** but goes up to **57%**. Students often claim SUD offers CUD-level discounts.
> 7. **Snapshot incremental logic:** Saying "deleting a snapshot deletes its data" is **wrong** — the next snapshot absorbs the blocks.
> 8. **Multi-Attach constraints:** Multi-Attach requires **io1/io2** volumes and **Nitro instances** in the **same AZ**. Generic EC2 instances do not support it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exams):**

**🔹 Amazon EBS (Storage Service):**
- Block-level, persistent, AZ-scoped storage for EC2.
- 5 volume types: **gp3, io1, io2 Block Express, st1, sc1**.
- Boot volumes are always **gp2/gp3**.
- Snapshots are **incremental**, stored in **S3**.
- Encryption: **AES-256-XTS** with **AWS KMS**.
- **Multi-Attach:** Up to 16 Nitro instances (io1/io2) in same AZ.
- **Fast Snapshot Restore (FSR):** Eliminates I/O latency on snapshot restore.

**🔹 EBS Cost Formula:**
$$C = (S_{\text{GB}} \times P_{\text{GB}}) + (I_{\text{IOPS}} \times P_{\text{IOPS}}) + (T_{\text{MiB/s}} \times P_{\text{MiB/s}})$$

**🔹 EBS Throughput-Bound IOPS Formula:**
$$\text{IOPS}_{\text{throughput-bound}} = \frac{\text{Throughput (MiB/s)} \times 1024}{\text{I/O Size (KiB)}}$$

**🔹 Google Compute Engine (IaaS Compute):**
- IaaS offering, **per-second billing** (1-min minimum).
- 5 machine families: **General (N2), Compute (C2), Memory (M2), Accelerator (A2), Storage (Z3)**.
- **Custom machine types** allow picking exact vCPU/RAM.
- **Live migration:** Transparent, ~60–300 ms pause, preserves IP/MAC/TCP sessions.
- **Sustained Use Discount (SUD):** Automatic, up to 20–52.5%.
- **Committed Use Discount (CUD):** 1-yr or 3-yr contract, up to 57%.
- **Spot VMs:** Up to 80–91% off, 30-second reclamation warning, no SLA.

**🔹 GCE Effective Rate Formula:**
$$\text{Effective Rate} = \text{List Price} \times (1 - \text{SUD}) \times (1 - \text{CUD}) \times \text{Spot Factor}$$

**🔹 Key Comparisons to Memorize:**
- EBS vs Instance Store → Persistent vs Ephemeral.
- EBS Snapshots vs AMIs → Snapshots = volume backup, AMIs = instance template + root volume.
- GCE Spot VMs vs AWS Spot Instances → Similar concept; GCE gives 30-sec warning.
- GCE Live Migration vs AWS Live Migration → Both exist; GCE pioneered it.

**🔹 Numeric Constants to Remember:**
- 1 TB = 1024 GB (binary) for storage size.
- gp3 baseline: **3,000 IOPS** and **125 MiB/s** free.
- EBS Multi-Attach: **max 16 instances**.
- CUD 3-yr maximum discount: **57%**.
- GCE Spot VM discount: **up to 80–91%**.

**🔹 Exam Day Mantra:**
- Always state the **service category** (block storage vs IaaS compute).
- Always mention **scope** (AZ vs Region vs Global).
- Always include a **diagram** (Mermaid or block-level) — it earns 1–2 free marks.
- Always **show units** in numeric calculations (MiB/s, IOPS, GB-month).
- Always use the **constraint comparison** `min()` when computing actual IOPS.

---

**End of Module 4 — EBS & GCE Section** ✅

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cloud Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Cloud Storage** is a service model in which digital data is stored, managed, and backed up on remote physical or virtual servers that are hosted by a third-party provider and made available to users over a network (typically the public Internet or a private network connection). In the context of the KTU 2024 Cloud Computing curriculum, cloud storage is treated as a foundational **Storage-as-a-Service (STaaS)** delivery paradigm that abstracts the physical storage infrastructure from the consumer, providing **elastic capacity, measured service, pay-per-use billing**, and **on-demand provisioning** as its four cardinal properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Phrasing):** *"Cloud storage is a model of networked enterprise storage where data is maintained, managed, and backed up remotely and made available to users over a network, with the underlying physical storage pooled into a virtualized resource pool and provisioned on-demand with metered billing."*

The architectural foundation rests on four pillars defined by the NIST SP 800-145 reference model, which the KTU syllabus directly inherits:

1. **On-demand self-service** — The consumer can unilaterally provision storage capacities automatically without requiring human interaction with the service provider.
2. **Broad network access** — Storage capabilities are available over the network and accessed through standard mechanisms used by heterogeneous client platforms (mobile, laptop, IoT).
3. **Resource pooling** — The provider's storage resources are pooled to serve multiple consumers using a multi-tenant model, with physical storage dynamically assigned and reassigned according to consumer demand.
4. **Rapid elasticity** — Storage capacity can be elastically provisioned and released, in some cases automatically, to scale rapidly outward and inward commensurate with demand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Measured Service:** Cloud storage systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging metering at a level appropriate to the type of service. The user pays for the **GB-month** consumed plus **I/O operations** and **data transfer out** (the three primary billable units in AWS S3, Azure Blob, and GCS).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cloud Warehouse" Mental Model

Imagine you are a shop owner who previously kept all your inventory in a small backroom of your shop. You could only store a limited number of boxes, expansion required buying a bigger shop, and if there was a fire, you would lose everything. You then sign a contract with a massive climate-controlled warehouse run by a logistics company:

- **You do not know (or care) which physical shelf your box is on** → this is **abstraction of physical location**.
- **The warehouse can instantly give you 10 more boxes or take 10 away** → this is **elastic provisioning**.
- **You pay only for the boxes you used, counted daily** → this is **metered / pay-per-use billing**.
- **The warehouse keeps 3 photocopies of your box in 3 different cities** → this is **geographic redundancy (replication)**.
- **If a courier is bringing you the box, you do not need it immediately, you can have it delivered overnight** → this is **storage tiering (Standard vs. Infrequent Access vs. Archive)**.

**The shop owner = the cloud consumer (application/user). The warehouse = the cloud storage provider (AWS, Azure, GCP). The boxes = data objects/blobs/files. The contracts = SLAs (Service Level Agreements).**

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Cloud Storage (KTU-Exam-Relevant)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Storage Capacity | $C$ | **GB**, **TB**, **PB** | Total volume of logical data stored |
| Availability | $A$ | **% uptime (e.g., 99.999 %)** | Fraction of time the service is reachable |
| Durability | $D$ | **nines (e.g., 99.999999999 % — "11 nines")** | Probability that data is not lost over a year |
| Latency | $L$ | **milliseconds (ms)** | Time to first byte after a request |
| Throughput | $T$ | **MB/s, IOPS** | Read/write bandwidth per second |
| Cost | $\kappa$ | **$ per GB-month** | Recurring charge per unit storage per month |
| Replication Factor | $r$ | integer ≥ 1 | Number of physical copies maintained |

> [!TIP]
> **Why "11 nines" of durability?** AWS S3 Standard claims $D = 99.999999999\,\%$ durability. Statistically, if you store **10 million objects (10⁷)**, you can expect to lose **one object every 10,000 years**. The board loves this number in Part A questions.

### 1.4 The Three Fundamental Cloud Storage Service Models (Exam-Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Object Storage vs. Block Storage vs. File Storage** — The tri-class taxonomy of cloud storage. KTU examiners almost always include a 3-mark direct question asking the student to compare these three.

| Feature | **Object Storage** | **Block Storage** | **File Storage** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Unit | Object (blob + metadata + key) | Fixed-size block (e.g., 512 B – 4 MB) | Hierarchical file/folder tree |
| Access Method | REST API (HTTP GET/PUT) | iSCSI, FC, direct-attached | NFS, SMB, CIFS |
| Scalability | Virtually unlimited (exabytes) | Limited to volume size | Limited to filesystem |
| Performance | High throughput, moderate latency | **Lowest latency, highest IOPS** | Moderate |
| Metadata | Rich, customizable | None (just block address) | File system attributes |
| Use Case | Backups, media, logs, Big Data | Databases, OS boot volumes | Home directories, CMS |
| AWS Example | Amazon S3 | Amazon EBS | Amazon EFS |
| Azure Example | Azure Blob Storage | Azure Managed Disks | Azure Files |
| Google Example | Google Cloud Storage | Persistent Disk | Filestore |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of a Cloud Storage System

A cloud storage system is best understood as a **four-layer logical stack** from the bottom (physical media) to the top (user-facing API):

**Layer 4 — Application / User Interface Layer**
RESTful APIs (HTTP/HTTPS), SDKs, web consoles, CLI tools. The user-facing surface; examples: S3 PUT Object, Azure Blob Upload, GCS gsutil cp.

**Layer 3 — Service Management Layer**
Handles authentication (OAuth 2.0, IAM), authorization (bucket policies, ACLs), metering, billing, quota management, and SLA enforcement. This is the "front desk" of the warehouse.

**Layer 2 — Storage Virtualization & Logical Abstraction Layer**
Maps logical object/block IDs to physical device locations. Performs deduplication, compression, encryption (at-rest with AES-256, in-transit with TLS 1.3), striping, and erasure coding. This is the "warehouse inventory management system".

**Layer 1 — Physical Storage Layer**
The actual hardware: HDD arrays, SSDs, NVMe drives, JBODs, distributed clusters spanning multiple data centers and geographic regions.

### 2.2 Core Theoretical Operations — The "Why" and the "How"

#### 2.2.1 Data Replication (The Simplest Redundancy Strategy)
- **What it is:** Maintaining $r$ identical copies of every data unit across different physical nodes/availability zones.
- **Why it is used:** Provides the strongest durability and lowest read latency (you can read from the closest replica). It is also the easiest to understand and implement.
- **How it works on write:** The primary node writes the data locally, then synchronously (or asynchronously) fans out the new block to $r - 1$ other replica nodes. The write is acknowledged as successful only after the **write quorum** $W$ is satisfied.
- **Storage overhead:** $r \times$ the logical data size. A replication factor of 3 means you physically store **3 GB for every 1 GB of logical data**, so storage efficiency is only $1/r$.

#### 2.2.2 Erasure Coding (The Space-Efficient Redundancy Strategy)
- **What it is:** A forward-error-correction technique that splits a data object into $k$ data chunks and computes $m$ parity chunks using a Reed–Solomon or Cauchy matrix over a Galois Field $GF(2^w)$, giving a total of $n = k + m$ chunks.
- **Why it is used:** Provides the same (or better) durability as 3× replication at a fraction of the storage cost. Standard RAID-6 is a special case with $k = 16, m = 2$ in HDFS / Ceph.
- **How it works:** Any $k$ of the $n$ chunks are sufficient to reconstruct the original data. So the system tolerates the loss of up to $m$ chunks simultaneously.
- **Storage efficiency:** $k / n = k / (k+m)$, far superior to replication. For $k = 10, m = 4$ (RS(10,4)) the efficiency is $10/14 \approx 71\,\%$, whereas 3× replication is only $33\,\%$.

#### 2.2.3 Storage Virtualization
- **What it is:** The process of abstracting logical storage from physical storage so that a single logical volume may span multiple physical devices, and multiple logical volumes may share the same physical pool.
- **Why it matters:** Enables live migration, capacity pooling, thin provisioning (allocate logical space larger than physical space until actually used), and non-disruptive hardware refresh.
- **How it works:** A hypervisor or storage controller maintains a **mapping table** (logical block address → physical block address). I/O requests are intercepted, translated, and dispatched.

#### 2.2.4 Consistency Models
- **Strong consistency:** A read is guaranteed to return the most recent write. AWS S3 now provides strong read-after-write consistency for all GET, PUT, LIST operations (since 2020 — a frequently asked board question).
- **Eventual consistency:** A read may temporarily return stale data, but the system guarantees convergence within bounded time. Classic example: Amazon DynamoDB's tunable consistency, Cassandra.
- **Causal consistency:** Operations that are causally related are seen in the same order by all nodes, but concurrent operations may be seen in different orders.

### 2.3 Storage Tiering (Hot → Cold → Archive)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** "Explain the different storage tiers with example use cases." You must know at least three tiers and their typical retrieval latency.

| Tier | Example Service | Access Latency | Cost per GB-month | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hot / Standard** | S3 Standard, Azure Hot Blob | **ms** | ~$0.021 | Frequently accessed data, websites |
| **Cool / Infrequent Access** | S3 Standard-IA, Azure Cool | ms | ~$0.0125 | Disaster recovery, backups (≥ 30 days) |
| **Cold / Archive** | S3 Glacier Deep Archive | **hours** | ~$0.00099 | Long-term compliance, 7-10 year retention |

### 2.4 The CAP Theorem Applied to Cloud Storage

For any distributed cloud storage system, only **two** of the following three guarantees can be simultaneously satisfied under network partition:

- **C** — **Consistency:** every read receives the most recent write.
- **A** — **Availability:** every request receives a (non-error) response.
- **P** — **Partition tolerance:** the system continues to operate despite arbitrary network message loss.

Because network partitions **will** happen in any real cloud datacenter, P is non-negotiable. Therefore the choice is between **CP** (e.g., HBase, etcd — gives up availability during partition) and **AP** (e.g., Amazon Dynamo, Cassandra — gives up strong consistency).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` in all LaTeX to protect markdown table syntax.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Replication Storage Overhead** | $\displaystyle S_{\text{phys}} = r \cdot S_{\text{logic}}$ | $S_{\text{phys}}$ physical storage (GB), $S_{\text{logic}}$ logical data (GB), $r$ replica count | $r = 3$ is the industry default |
| 2 | **Replication Storage Efficiency** | $\displaystyle \eta_{\text{rep}} = \frac{1}{r} \times 100\,\%$ | $\eta$ in % | $33\,\%$ for $r = 3$ |
| 3 | **Erasure Coding Storage Efficiency** | $\displaystyle \eta_{\text{ec}} = \frac{k}{k+m} \times 100\,\%$ | $k$ data chunks, $m$ parity chunks | RS(10,4) → $71.4\,\%$ |
| 4 | **Erasure Coding Fault Tolerance** | $f = m$ | $f$ tolerable chunk failures | RS(10,4) tolerates any 4 failures |
| 5 | **Quorum Condition (R + W > N)** | $\displaystyle R + W > N$ | $R$ read quorum, $W$ write quorum, $N$ replica count | Required for strong consistency |
| 6 | **Annual Availability → Downtime** | $\displaystyle D_{\text{year}} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | $A$ availability fraction, $D$ downtime in minutes | For $A = 99.9\,\%$ → 525.6 min/yr |
| 9 | **Nines-to-Downtime Conversion** | $\displaystyle D_{\text{yr}} = \frac{525\,600}{10^{n}}$ | $n$ = number of 9s, $D$ in minutes/year | "5 nines" = 5.26 min/yr; "4 nines" = 52.6 min/yr |
| 8 | **Mean Time To Data Loss (MTTDL)** | $\displaystyle \text{MTTDL} \approx \frac{\text{MTBF}^2}{N \cdot \text{MTTR}}$ | MTBF mean time between failures, MTTR mean time to repair, $N$ disks | Lower for larger arrays; mitigated by replication |
| 9 | **Annual Data Loss Probability** | $\displaystyle P_{\text{loss}} = 1 - D$ | $D$ durability (fraction) | S3 Standard: $P \approx 10^{-11}$ |
| 10 | **Throughput (Sequential)** | $\displaystyle T = \frac{S}{t}$ | $S$ data size (MB), $t$ elapsed time (s) | T in MB/s |
| 11 | **IOPS Aggregation (RAID-0 striping)** | $\displaystyle \text{IOPS}_{\text{array}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{IOPS}_i$ | $N$ disks in stripe | Linear in number of disks |
| 12 | **Thin Provisioning Saving** | $\displaystyle \text{Saving} = \frac{L_{\text{alloc}} - L_{\text{used}}}{L_{\text{alloc}}} \times 100\,\%$ | $L_{\text{alloc}}$ logical allocated, $L_{\text{used}}$ actual written | Reduces unused capacity waste |
| 13 | **Storage Cost (Monthly)** | $\displaystyle \text{Cost} = C \cdot \kappa \cdot t_m$ | $C$ capacity (GB), $\kappa$ unit price ($/GB-month), $t_m$ months | Add I/O and egress costs separately |
| 14 | **RAID-5 Write Penalty** | 4 I/O per logical write | — | 1 read-modify-write of data + parity; 2 reads + 2 writes |
| 15 | **RAID-6 Write Penalty** | 6 I/O per logical write | — | Dual parity requires 3 reads + 3 writes |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization priority for KTU Part A (3-mark) questions:** (i) Replication factor formula, (ii) Erasure coding efficiency, (iii) Nines-to-downtime conversion, (iv) R + W > N quorum rule, (v) Quorum condition.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cloud storage underpins virtually every modern Internet service. **Netflix** uses Amazon S3 to store its entire video library (multiple petabytes) and uses **AWS S3 Glacier** for cold backups. **Dropbox** uses a custom storage system called "Magic Pocket" built on top of sharded erasure-coded data spread across multiple data centers. **WhatsApp** uses a heavily sharded, erasure-coded blob store for message media. **Genomics projects** (e.g., the 100,000 Genomes Project) use cloud object storage to hold hundreds of petabytes of raw DNA sequencing reads. The choice of storage tier and redundancy strategy directly determines the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** and the **RTO / RPO** (Recovery Time / Recovery Point Objective) of any cloud-deployed system.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Converting "Nines of Availability" to Annual Downtime

This is a derivation KTU examiners love because it is short, mechanical, and tests unit awareness.

**Given:** A cloud storage SLA promises $A = 99.999\,\%$ ("five nines") of availability per year.

**To find:** The maximum tolerable annual downtime in minutes.

**Step 1 — Express availability as a fraction of total time per year.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0.99999 \\
A_{\text{fraction}} &= 1 - A = 0.00001
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of minutes in a non-leap year (the standard KTU assumption).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minutes per year} &= 60 \,\text{min/h} \times 24 \,\text{h/day} \times 365 \,\text{days} \\
&= 525\,600 \,\text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the unavailability fraction by the total minutes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{year}} &= (1 - A) \times 525\,600 \\
&= 0.00001 \times 525\,600 \\
&= 5.256 \,\text{minutes/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to seconds for context (a follow-up question often asked).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{seconds}} &= 5.256 \,\text{min} \times 60 \,\text{s/min} \\
&\approx 315.36 \,\text{seconds/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification (general formula check):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{yr}} &= \frac{525\,600}{10^{n}} \,\text{minutes} \\
\text{For } n=5: \quad D &= \frac{525\,600}{10^{5}} = 5.256 \,\text{min} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering insight:** "Five nines" sounds luxurious but still allows **5 minutes of downtime per year** — equivalent to one fairly long coffee break. For mission-critical workloads (e.g., hospital ICU monitoring), we need **six nines** (31.5 seconds/year) or better, typically achieved via active-active multi-region replication.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Replication vs. Erasure Coding Trade-off Analysis

**Problem statement:** You need to store **$S = 100\,\text{GB}$** of critical log data with a **tolerance of 2 simultaneous disk failures**. Compare:
- **Option A:** 3× replication ($r = 3$)
- **Option B:** RS(6,2) erasure coding ($k = 6, m = 2$), with each chunk stored on a separate disk

**Step 1 — Compute physical storage for Option A (3× replication).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{phys}}^{(\text{rep})} &= r \cdot S_{\text{logic}} \\
&= 3 \times 100\,\text{GB} \\
&= 300\,\text{GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute physical storage for Option B (RS(6,2) erasure coding).**

In RS(6,2) the object is split into $k = 6$ data chunks, and $m = 2$ parity chunks are computed, totaling $n = k + m = 8$ chunks. If the original object is 100 GB and is split into 6 equal data chunks, each data chunk is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{chunk}_{\text{data}} &= \frac{S}{k} = \frac{100\,\text{GB}}{6} \approx 16.67\,\text{GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each parity chunk is the same size as a data chunk, so the total physical storage is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{phys}}^{(\text{ec})} &= n \cdot \text{chunk}_{\text{data}} = (k+m) \cdot \frac{S}{k} \\
&= 8 \times 16.67\,\text{GB} \\
&= 133.33\,\text{GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute storage efficiency for each option.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{rep}} &= \frac{S_{\text{logic}}}{S_{\text{phys}}^{(\text{rep})}} = \frac{100}{300} = 33.33\,\% \\
\eta_{\text{ec}} &= \frac{S_{\text{logic}}}{S_{\text{phys}}^{(\text{ec})}} = \frac{100}{133.33} = 75.00\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the storage cost savings of Option B vs. Option A.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Saving} &= \frac{S_{\text{phys}}^{(\text{rep})} - S_{\text{phys}}^{(\text{ec})}}{S_{\text{phys}}^{(\text{rep})}} \times 100\,\% \\
&= \frac{300 - 133.33}{300} \times 100\,\% \\
&\approx 55.56\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the fault-tolerance equivalence.**

Both options tolerate the loss of any **2 disks**:
- **Replication:** With $r = 3$, any single replica can be lost, but if both replicas on a single physical node are lost simultaneously, data is unavailable. With proper placement across 3 independent racks/AZs, 2 disk failures are tolerated.
- **Erasure coding RS(6,2):** Tolerates loss of up to $m = 2$ of the 8 chunks, hence 2 disk failures are tolerated with **mathematical guarantee**.

**Step 6 — Write-cost trade-off (extra analysis).**

| Operation | Replication $r=3$ | Erasure Coding RS(6,2) |
|---|---|---|
| Single write | 3 network writes | 8 chunk writes (≈ 2.67× more) |
| Reconstruction | N/A (just use remaining replica) | Read 6 surviving chunks + recompute |
| Read latency | Lower (read nearest replica) | Higher (need to fetch k = 6 chunks in parallel) |

**Conclusion:** Erasure coding is preferred for **cold / archival / write-rare workloads** (e.g., backups, object storage). Replication is preferred for **hot / read-heavy / latency-sensitive workloads** (e.g., database primary copies). This is exactly why **S3 Standard uses replication** and **Glacier uses erasure coding** internally.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Quorum Intersection for Strong Consistency

**Problem statement:** A cloud key-value store replicates every key across $N = 3$ nodes. The user specifies a read quorum $R = 2$ and a write quorum $W = 2$. Determine whether strong consistency is guaranteed.

**Step 1 — Recall the quorum intersection theorem.**

For strong consistency, every read quorum must intersect every write quorum in at least one node that participated in the most recent successful write:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R + W > N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R + W &= 2 + 2 = 4 \\
N &= 3 \\
R + W &> N \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 4 > 3 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Geometric interpretation.**

The set of 2 nodes handling a write (say {A, B}) and the set of 2 nodes handling the subsequent read (say {A, C}) share node **A** in common. This shared node holds the latest version, so the read returns the most recent write.

**Step 4 — Counter-example (what if R = 1, W = 1)?**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R + W &= 1 + 1 = 2 \\
2 &> 3 \;\;\text{is FALSE} \;\;\times
\end{aligned}
$$

The read and write may touch disjoint nodes; staleness is possible. Hence consistency is sacrificed — this is the classic **AP (Available, Partition-tolerant)** configuration of Dynamo and Cassandra.

**Step 5 — Cost analysis.**

| Configuration | Reads per logical read | Writes per logical write | Total R + W | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fast-read ($R=1, W=3$) | 1 | 3 | 4 | Read-heavy, write-rare (caches) |
| Fast-write ($R=3, W=1$) | 3 | 1 | 4 | Write-heavy, no read load (logs) |
| Balanced ($R=2, W=2$) | 2 | 2 | 4 | General purpose (default) |
| Sloppy ($R=1, W=1$) | 1 | 1 | 2 | Eventual consistency only |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Major Cloud Storage Providers (KTU Industry-Readiness)

| Feature | **Amazon S3** | **Azure Blob Storage** | **Google Cloud Storage** | **MinIO / Ceph (Open Source)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| API Style | REST + S3 API (de-facto standard) | REST + Azure SDK | REST + JSON / gRPC | S3-compatible |
| Strong Consistency | Yes (since Dec 2020) | Yes (default) | Yes (default, strong global) | Configurable |
| Max Object Size | **5 TB** | 4.75 TB (block blob) / 200 GB (append) | 5 TB | 5 TB |
| Default Durability | **99.999999999 %** (11 nines) | 99.999999999 % (11 nines, RA-GRS) | 99.999999999 % (multi-region) | Depends on config |
| Erasure Coding | Yes (S3 Standard-IA, Glacier) | Yes (LRS / ZRS / GRS / RA-GRS) | Yes | Yes (Reed-Solomon) |
| Replication | Yes (Cross-Region Replication) | Yes (GRS, RA-GRS) | Yes (multi-region buckets) | Yes (CRUSH map) |
| Tiering | Standard, IA, One Zone-IA, Glacier, Glacier DA | Hot, Cool, Archive | Standard, Nearline, Coldline, Archive | Custom policies |
| Server-Side Encryption | AES-256 (SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, SSE-C) | AES-256 (Microsoft-managed or CMK) | AES-256 (Google-managed or CMEK) | AES-256 (KMS) |
| Primary Use Case | General object storage | Microsoft ecosystem, hybrid | GCP, Big Data, AI/ML | Private cloud, on-prem |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define cloud storage. List and briefly explain the three primary cloud storage service types.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Cloud storage is a cloud computing model in which data is stored, managed, and backed up on remote, virtualized storage infrastructure accessed over a network, with on-demand provisioning, elastic capacity, and metered pay-per-use billing.

**Three service types (2 marks):**

1. **Object Storage** — Data is stored as discrete objects, each consisting of the data payload, a unique identifier (key), and rich customizable metadata. Accessed via RESTful HTTP APIs. Highly scalable to exabytes. Examples: Amazon S3, Azure Blob Storage, Google Cloud Storage. Best for unstructured data, backups, media, logs.

2. **Block Storage** — Data is stored in fixed-size blocks (typically 512 B to 4 MB) on a logical volume. Accessed at the OS level as a raw block device via iSCSI, Fibre Channel, or direct attachment. Provides the lowest latency and highest IOPS. Examples: Amazon EBS, Azure Managed Disks, Google Persistent Disk. Best for databases, boot volumes, and transactional workloads.

3. **File Storage** — Data is organized in a hierarchical folder/file structure and accessed over standard file protocols like NFS (Linux) or SMB/CIFS (Windows). Provides shared file system semantics. Examples: Amazon EFS, Azure Files, Google Filestore. Best for shared home directories, content management systems, and lift-and-shift legacy applications.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Differentiate between data replication and erasure coding in cloud storage. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | **Replication** | **Erasure Coding** |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism (1 mark) | Maintains $r$ full identical copies of the data on different physical nodes | Splits data into $k$ chunks and computes $m$ parity chunks using Reed–Solomon codes; only $k$ of $n = k + m$ chunks needed to reconstruct |
| Storage Overhead | $r \times$ logical size (e.g., $3 \times = 300\,\%$) | $((k+m)/k) \times$ logical size (e.g., RS(10,4) = 140 %) |
| Advantage (1 mark) | Simple to implement; **low read latency** (read from nearest replica); fast recovery (just promote a replica) | **High storage efficiency**; tolerates more failures per unit storage; ideal for cold/archival data |
| Disadvantage (1 mark) | Very expensive in storage cost at high $r$ | Higher write amplification (more I/O for encoding); longer reconstruction time; higher read latency (must fetch $k$ chunks in parallel) |

**Example:** Amazon S3 Standard uses replication internally; S3 Glacier uses erasure coding.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) regulations, students answer **one full question of 14 marks** from a choice of two. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each, OR one 14-mark main question with internal subdivisions.

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the four essential characteristics of cloud storage as defined by the NIST reference model. Briefly describe the three cloud storage service delivery models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) with one storage-related example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cloud storage SLA promises **99.99 %** availability ("four nines") per year and **99.999999999 %** ("11 nines") durability. Compute:
   (i) The maximum tolerable annual downtime in minutes and seconds.
   (ii) The probability of losing at least one object in a year if you store 10 million objects.
   (iii) Justify why cloud providers can offer 11 nines of durability even though individual disks have an annual failure rate (AFR) of about 2 %. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**The Four NIST Essential Characteristics of Cloud Storage (4 marks):**

1. **On-demand self-service** — A consumer can automatically provision storage capacity (e.g., create a 5 TB S3 bucket) without human interaction with the provider. *(1 mark)*

2. **Broad network access** — Storage services are reachable over the network and accessible from diverse client platforms (laptop, smartphone, IoT edge device) through standard protocols (HTTP, REST). *(1 mark)*

3. **Resource pooling** — The provider's physical storage resources (HDDs, SSDs) are pooled to serve multiple consumers using a multi-tenant model, with virtual resources dynamically reassigned based on demand. *(1 mark)*

4. **Rapid elasticity** — Storage capacity can be elastically scaled up or down, often automatically. From the consumer's perspective, the storage appears to be unlimited and can be provisioned in any quantity at any time. *(1 mark)*

*(Plus metered service as a fifth often-listed property.)*

**The Three Service Delivery Models (3 marks):**

- **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service):** Provides raw storage infrastructure (virtual disks, object stores) that the consumer rents. The consumer manages the OS and data. *Storage example:* **Amazon EBS** (block), **Amazon S3** (object). *(1 mark)*

- **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service):** Provides a managed platform including storage, runtime, and middleware. The consumer focuses on application code and data only. *Storage example:* **Azure SQL Database** (managed database with built-in storage), **Google App Engine Datastore**. *(1 mark)*

- **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service):** The consumer uses a complete application hosted by the provider; storage is fully managed and invisible. *Storage example:* **Google Drive**, **Dropbox**, **Microsoft 365 OneDrive**. *(1 mark)*

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**(i) Maximum annual downtime for $A = 99.99\,\%$: (3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total minutes per year} &= 60 \times 24 \times 365 = 525\,600 \text{ min} \\
\text{Unavailability fraction} &= 1 - 0.9999 = 0.0001 \\
D_{\text{min}} &= 0.0001 \times 525\,600 = 52.56 \text{ minutes} \\
D_{\text{sec}} &= 52.56 \times 60 = 3\,153.6 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark for total minutes, 1 mark for unavailability fraction, 1 mark for final answer in both units.)*

**(ii) Probability of at least one object lost out of $N = 10^7$ objects, given durability $D = 0.99999999999$: (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{per object}} &= 1 - D = 1 - 0.99999999999 = 10^{-11} \\
P_{\text{at least one loss}} &\approx N \times P_{\text{per object}} \quad \text{(for small } p \text{)} \\
&\approx 10^{7} \times 10^{-11} = 10^{-4} = 0.0001 = 0.01\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

So even with 10 million objects, there is only a **0.01 % chance** of losing any object in a given year. *(1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final answer.)*

**(iii) Justification of 11 nines despite 2 % AFR per disk: (2 marks)**

A single disk has an AFR ≈ 2 %, which is "only" **1.8 nines** of reliability ($98\,\%$). The provider achieves 11 nines (a factor of $10^{9}$ better) by combining **five layers of mitigation**:
- **Erasure coding** across many disks (typically RS(10,4) or similar) so that losing one disk is harmless.
- **Cross-AZ replication** (synchronous copies in 3+ Availability Zones).
- **Cross-region backup** (asynchronous copies in geographically separated regions).
- **Continuous monitoring and self-healing** (a "scrubber" detects and reconstructs degraded chunks in the background, well before they are lost).
- **End-to-end data integrity checks** (cryptographic hashes verified on read and during audits).

The probability of data loss is the **product of the probabilities of multiple independent failures** happening simultaneously (disk fail + AZ fail + region fail + reconstruction lag), which drops the joint probability to $\sim 10^{-11}$.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Object Storage, Block Storage, and File Storage** across the following dimensions: data access method, scalability, typical latency, and ideal use cases. Provide one real cloud service example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Reed–Solomon Erasure Coding** technique in detail. A cloud object store uses **RS(10, 4)** to protect a **500 GB** dataset. Calculate:
   (i) The size of each data chunk and the size of each parity chunk.
   (ii) The total physical storage required.
   (iii) The storage efficiency in percentage.
   (iv) The maximum number of chunk failures that can be tolerated, and explain how the original data is reconstructed when chunks are lost. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) [7 marks]**

| Dimension (1.75 marks each row) | **Object Storage** | **Block Storage** | **File Storage** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Access Method** | RESTful HTTP API (GET, PUT, DELETE), unique key-based | iSCSI, Fibre Channel, NVMe-oF; raw block-level I/O | NFS, SMB, CIFS; standard POSIX file system calls |
