| **Scalability** | Virtually unlimited (exabytes); horizontally scalable | Limited to volume size (TB scale); vertical scaling | Limited by file system metadata servers; moderate scalability |
| **Typical Latency** | Moderate (tens of ms) | **Lowest** (sub-ms to few ms) | Moderate (few ms); higher due to metadata lookups |
| **Ideal Use Cases** | Backups, archives, big data lakes, media, log files | Databases (SQL, NoSQL), OS boot volumes, transactional apps | Shared home directories, CMS, lift-and-shift legacy enterprise apps |
| **Cloud Example** | **Amazon S3** | **Amazon EBS** | **Amazon EFS** |

*(Award 1.75 marks per correctly filled row; full 7 marks for all four rows filled with correct example.)*

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Explanation of Reed–Solomon Erasure Coding (Intro for context):**

Reed–Solomon (RS) erasure coding is a forward-error-correction technique widely used in cloud object stores (e.g., Microsoft Azure, Facebook's f4 storage, Ceph). A data object is conceptually divided into $k$ equal-sized data chunks. An encoder applies a generator matrix over a Galois Field $GF(2^w)$ (typically $GF(2^8)$) to produce $m$ additional parity chunks. The $n = k + m$ chunks are distributed across distinct failure domains (disks, servers, racks, AZs). The original object can be **mathematically reconstructed from any $k$ of the $n$ chunks** by solving a system of linear equations (matrix inversion over the Galois Field).

**(i) Chunk sizes (1.5 marks):**

The dataset $S = 500$ GB is split into $k = 10$ equal data chunks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Chunk size (data)} &= \frac{S}{k} = \frac{500\,\text{GB}}{10} = 50\,\text{GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each parity chunk is the **same size** as a data chunk (in RS coding, all chunks are uniform in size):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Chunk size (parity)} &= 50\,\text{GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total physical storage (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{phys}} &= n \times \text{chunk size} = (k + m) \times 50\,\text{GB} \\
&= (10 + 4) \times 50\,\text{GB} \\
&= 14 \times 50\,\text{GB} \\
&= 700\,\text{GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Storage efficiency (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{S_{\text{logic}}}{S_{\text{phys}}} \times 100\,\% = \frac{k}{n} \times 100\,\% \\
&= \frac{10}{14} \times 100\,\% \\
&\approx 71.43\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Fault tolerance and reconstruction (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{max}} &= m = 4 \text{ simultaneous chunk losses}
\end{aligned}
$$

The system can tolerate the loss of **any 4 of the 14 chunks** (e.g., 4 disk failures, 4 server crashes, or even 4 simultaneous AZ outages if chunks are AZ-distributed).

**Reconstruction procedure (explanation for marks):**

1. The storage controller detects the failed/missing chunks via periodic "scrubbing" jobs that verify checksums.
2. The controller reads any $k = 10$ **surviving** chunks from the remaining 10 healthy locations.
3. The decoder constructs a $k \times k$ submatrix of the original generator matrix corresponding to the available chunks and **inverts it over the Galois Field**.
4. Multiplying the inverse matrix by the vector of $k$ available chunks yields the original $k$ data chunks.
5. The lost parity chunks are then recomputed, and the system returns to a fully healthy state of $n = 14$ chunks.
6. The reconstruction is done in the background, so users continue to see normal availability throughout.

**Comparison note (1 bonus mark if asked):** RS(10,4) provides the same fault tolerance as **3× replication** (tolerating up to 2 simultaneous failures) **plus more**, while using only **71.4 % storage** vs. replication's **33.3 %** — a **2.14× storage saving**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically LOSE marks in the Cloud Storage question:**
>
> 1. **Confusing availability with durability.** Availability = "% of time the service is reachable". Durability = "% probability the data is not lost over a year". AWS S3 has 99.99 % availability and 99.999999999 % durability — students often swap them. *(Lose 1–2 marks.)*
>
> 2. **Writing $r = 2$ for replication factor** instead of the **correct replica count** (e.g., $r = 3$ for AWS default). Always state the *number of copies*, not the "extra copies".
>
> 3. **Forgetting the units** in downtime calculations. "5.256 minutes" — examiners specifically check whether you wrote "minutes" or just "5.256".
>
> 4. **Saying RS(10,4) is "more reliable than 3× replication"** — it is **more space-efficient**, not necessarily more reliable. Both tolerate 2 failures; RS(10,4) tolerates 4. Don't conflate.
>
> 5. **In the quorum question, students write $R + W \geq N$** instead of the strict inequality $R + W > N$. The strict ">" is required for the read set to *guarantee* intersection with the write set.
>
> 6. **Listing cloud storage types but not giving a service example** — Part A "define" questions ALWAYS want an AWS/Azure/GCP name. Without it, lose 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 7. **Mixing up "Hot/Cold" tiers with "Standard/IA" tiers** — Hot = Standard; Cold = Archive/Glacier; Cool = Standard-IA. Use the correct cloud-vendor term in your answer.
>
> 8. **Skipping the "why" in 14-mark questions** — Examiners want analysis, not just definitions. Always pair every fact with a *reason* and a *real-world consequence*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cloud Storage (Module 4)**

**Core Definitions (must know verbatim):**
- ☐ Cloud Storage = remote virtualized storage with on-demand provisioning, elastic capacity, metered billing, multi-tenancy.
- ☐ Object / Block / File — the three fundamental service types.
- ☐ Availability = uptime %; Durability = survival % of data per year.

**Essential Numbers to Memorize:**
- ☐ AWS S3 Standard durability = **11 nines** (99.999999999 %).
- ☐ S3 default replication factor $r = 3$.
- ☐ HDFS default replication factor = **3**.
- ☐ S3 Standard-IA / IA storage minimum storage duration = **30 days**.
- ☐ S3 Glacier retrieval time = **minutes to hours**; Deep Archive = **up to 12 hours**.
- ☐ Five nines = 5.256 min downtime/year; Four nines = 52.56 min/year; Three nines = 525.6 min/year (8.76 hours).

**Critical Formulas (all derivable in <30 seconds):**
- ☐ Storage efficiency of replication: $\eta = 1/r$.
- ☐ Storage efficiency of erasure coding: $\eta = k/(k+m)$.
- ☐ Quorum for strong consistency: $R + W > N$.
- ☐ Annual downtime in minutes: $D = (1 - A) \times 525\,600$.
- ☐ Erasure coding total physical storage: $S_{\text{phys}} = (k+m) \times S/k$.

**Architecture & Concepts:**
- ☐ Four NIST characteristics: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity (+ measured service).
- ☐ Four-layer architecture: User → API → Service Management → Virtualization → Physical.
- ☐ CAP theorem: in a partition, choose CP (consistency) or AP (availability).
- ☐ Strong vs. eventual consistency — S3 is strongly consistent since Dec 2020.

**Comparisons You Must Be Able to Draw:**
- ☐ Object vs. Block vs. File storage (table memorized).
- ☐ Replication vs. Erasure Coding (overhead, fault tolerance, use case).
- ☐ Hot vs. Cool vs. Cold vs. Archive tiers (latency, cost, use case).
- ☐ AWS S3 vs. Azure Blob vs. GCS vs. Ceph/MinIO (one example each).
- ☐ RAID-5 (single parity) vs. RAID-6 (dual parity) — write penalties of 4 and 6.

**Real-World Cloud Provider Names (must use in answers):**
- ☐ Object: **Amazon S3, Azure Blob, Google Cloud Storage**.
- ☐ Block: **Amazon EBS, Azure Managed Disks, Google Persistent Disk**.
- ☐ File: **Amazon EFS, Azure Files, Google Filestore**.

**Quick Triggers for Exam Answers:**
- ☐ If a question asks "lowest latency" → answer **Block Storage**.
- ☐ If a question asks "unlimited scalability, REST API" → answer **Object Storage**.
- ☐ If a question asks "shared folder over NFS" → answer **File Storage**.
- ☐ If a question asks "cheapest for cold data" → answer **Archive tier + Erasure Coding**.
- ☐ If a question asks "R + W > N" → answer **Strong consistency via quorum**.

**Mnemonics to Remember:**
- ☐ **"OBF"** = **O**bject, **B**lock, **F**ile (the three storage types in alphabetical order).
- ☐ **"EC = Reed–Solomon = k + m = n, any k suffice"** = Erasure Coding quick rule.
- ☐ **"11 nines = 1 lost object per 10,000 years per 10 million objects"** = S3 durability pitch.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** In any 14-mark cloud storage question, structure your answer in **three layers** — (1) **Definition/Concept** (2 marks), (2) **Architecture/Working with diagram or table** (5–6 marks), (3) **Numerical/comparative analysis with formula and final boxed answer** (5–6 marks). This mirror the KTU valuation key's marking distribution and guarantees full marks if your content is correct.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PaaS Offerings: Google App Engine (GAE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** PaaS Offerings: Google App Engine (GAE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Google App Engine (GAE)** is a fully managed **Platform as a Service (PaaS)** offering by Google Cloud that allows developers to build, deploy, and scale web applications and mobile back-ends on Google's infrastructure without managing the underlying hardware, operating system, runtime, or middleware. It provides automatic scaling, built-in security, and a rich set of cloud-native services such as Datastore, Cloud SQL, Memcache, Task Queues, and Endpoints.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> "PaaS Offerings – Google App Engine (GAE): Architecture, Application Lifecycle, Execution Environments, Scaling, and Service Ecosystem."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are opening a **pop-up restaurant** in a busy market.

- The **building, kitchen equipment, gas, plumbing, and electricity** are all handled by the landlord.
- You only focus on the **recipe (code), ingredients (data), and the menu (UI)**.
- When the crowd swells, the landlord **automatically opens more counters** (auto-scaling).
- When the crowd thins, the **extra counters close** (scale down).
- The landlord even provides **storage lockers (Datastore)**, **waiters (Task Queues)**, and a **cash register (Billing)**.

That "landlord" is **Google App Engine**. You bring your code; Google brings the planet-scale infrastructure, runtime, and operational tooling.

### 1.3 Core Properties of GAE (as required by KTU)

| Property | GAE Native Value |
|---|---|
| Service Model | **PaaS** (Platform as a Service) |
| Deployment Unit | Application Version (per service) |
| Supported Languages (Standard) | Python, Java, Node.js, Go, PHP, Ruby |
| Supported Languages (Flexible) | Any language via Docker container |
| Default Persistence | Datastore (NoSQL, schemaless) |
| Auto-Scaling Granularity | Per-instance / per-version |
| Hard Limit (Free Tier Quota) | **28 instance-hours/day** (historical reference) |
| Maximum Request Timeout | **60 seconds** (Standard env.) |
| Compute Pricing Unit | **Instance-hours (vCPU + GB memory)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GAE is NOT an IaaS** — you never SSH into a VM, never patch an OS, never configure load balancers manually. Everything is **declarative** via `app.yaml`.

### 1.4 Why GAE Matters in KTU Examinations

GAE is one of the **two canonical PaaS case studies** (the other being AWS Elastic Beanstalk) that examiners repeatedly use to test a student's understanding of:

- The boundary between **PaaS vs IaaS vs SaaS**
- **Auto-scaling policies** and how they map to cloud cost
- **Stateless vs stateful** application design (because GAE Statelessness is mandatory)
- **Service decoupling** using Task Queues, Cron, Memcache, and Cloud Endpoints

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 GAE High-Level Architecture

GAE's architecture is composed of **five logical tiers**, each of which has appeared in past KTU question papers as a 7-mark sub-question.

#### Tier 1 — Front-End (Edge Layer)

- Terminates **HTTPS traffic** at Google's global edge.
- Performs **load balancing** and **DDoS protection**.
- Routes requests to the appropriate **service → version → instance**.

#### Tier 2 — Application Master / Scheduler

- Decides **which instance handles the request**.
- Monitors health of every instance via **health checks** (HTTP `/_ah/health`).
- Implements the **scaling policy** (automatic, manual, basic).

#### Tier 3 — Application Instances (Compute Tier)

- Where your **code actually runs** inside a secure sandbox.
- Standard Environment: **gVisor-based sandbox** (language-locked).
- Flexible Environment: **Docker container** on a managed VM.

#### Tier 4 — Managed Services Tier

| Service | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Datastore (Firestore in Datastore mode)** | Schemaless NoSQL, ACID transactions, strongly consistent reads |
| **Cloud SQL** | Managed relational DB (MySQL/PostgreSQL) |
| **Memcache** | In-memory key-value cache, low-latency lookups |
| **Task Queues** | Asynchronous work execution (push & pull) |
| **Cron / Scheduled Tasks** | Time-triggered jobs |
| **Cloud Endpoints** | REST/RPC API gateway with API keys & OAuth |
| **Search API** | Full-text search over documents |
| **Blobstore / Cloud Storage** | Large binary objects (images, video) |

#### Tier 5 — Operations Layer

- **Cloud Monitoring** (Stackdriver) for metrics, logs, traces.
- **Cloud Build** for CI/CD of app versions.
- **IAM** for fine-grained access.

### 2.2 The Two Execution Environments (Most Repeated 14-Mark Topic)

| Feature | Standard Environment | Flexible Environment |
|---|---|---|
| Language Support | Fixed runtimes (Python 3, Java 11/17, Node.js, Go, PHP, Ruby) | **Any** language packaged in Docker |
| Source of Truth | Source code pushed directly | Docker image in Artifact Registry / Container Registry |
| Startup Time | **Milliseconds** (sandbox warm-up) | **Seconds to minutes** (container boot) |
| Scaling | Aggressive, near-instant (0 → N) | Slower; boots VMs |
| Access to Local Filesystem | **Sandboxed, read-only** | Read-write inside container |
| SSH / Debug | Not allowed | Allowed (debug mode) |
| Pricing Model | Per instance-hour (granular) | Per vCPU + GB memory per second |
| Underlying Infra | gVisor sandbox on Borg | Managed VM (Compute Engine) |
| Use Case | Spiky microservices, REST APIs | Legacy apps, custom runtimes, long-running workloads |

### 2.3 The Four Scaling Types (Killer Exam Question)

GAE offers **four scaling modes** declared in `app.yaml` via the `automatic_scaling`, `manual_scaling`, `basic_scaling`, and `max_instances` parameters.

#### 2.3.1 Automatic Scaling

The platform decides when to add/remove instances based on request rate, latency, and CPU. Default for Standard Environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Explain the auto-scaling parameters of GAE."*
> The mandatory parameters are:
> - `min_instances` — floor (cost floor for low traffic)
> - `max_instances` — ceiling (cost ceiling & DoS protection)
> - `target_cpu_utilization` — soft target (default **0.65**)
> - `target_throughput_utilization` — target RPS/instance
> - `max_concurrent_requests` — requests per instance (default **50** for HTTP/2)
> - `min_idle_instances` — pre-warmed warm pool
> - `min_pending_latency` — threshold to add a new instance
> - `max_pending_latency` — deadline before a request is rejected

#### 2.3.2 Manual Scaling

The developer explicitly declares the **fixed number of instances**; no auto-scaling occurs. Useful for steady, predictable workloads.

#### 2.3.3 Basic Scaling

Instances are created when a request arrives and **shut down after a configurable idle timeout** (`idle_timeout`, default **15 min**). Cheaper for spiky workloads, but each cold start has latency.

#### 2.3.4 Idle vs Always-On

- **Idle instances** are allowed in Standard Env. — they reduce cold-start latency.
- **Always-on** (legacy) reserved at least one instance running 24/7.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quantitative Aspects of GAE

Although GAE is conceptual, a few quantitative relationships are testable.

| Formula / Relationship | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| $$T_{\text{instance}} = \frac{R}{N \cdot \mu}$$ | Per-instance request load, where $R$ = total request rate, $N$ = instance count, $\mu$ = per-instance throughput capacity | Determining if auto-scaler will add an instance |
| $$\text{Scale-out trigger} \iff T_{\text{instance}} > \mu \cdot \rho_{\text{target}}$$ | New instance added when load exceeds target utilization $\rho$ | Auto-scaling logic |
| $$\text{Cost}_{\text{GAE}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i \cdot p_{\text{class}_i}$$ | Total cost = sum of (instance-hours × instance-class price) | Cost estimation questions |
| $$\text{Cold-start latency}_{\text{std}} \approx 5\text{–}100 \text{ ms}$$ | Sandbox warm-up time for Standard Env. | Justifying use of `min_idle_instances` |
| $$\text{Cold-start latency}_{\text{flex}} \approx 15\text{–}60 \text{ s}$$ | VM/Container boot time for Flexible Env. | Justifying use of `manual_scaling` |
| $$\text{Availability} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{k}(1 - a_i)$$ | Composite availability across $k$ redundant zones | SLA reasoning (SLA = **99.95%** in Standard) |
| $$R_{\text{rejected}} = R \cdot \mathbb{1}[L_{\text{pending}} > L_{\text{max}}]$$ | Requests rejected when pending latency exceeds maximum | DoS / capacity planning |

> [!NOTE]
> Always write `\vert` for vertical bar in tables, never the raw pipe character, to preserve the markdown table.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of GAE in Engineering

- **Startups** deploy MVPs in hours instead of weeks, paying only for actual traffic.
- **Mobile back-ends** (Firebase + GAE) handle authentication, push notifications, and APIs without operating system administration.
- **IoT pipelines** ingest millions of device events via Pub/Sub → GAE Task Queues → Datastore.
- **Enterprise APIs** are exposed via Cloud Endpoints with API-key throttling and OAuth2.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Auto-Scaler Decision Logic

A KTU favourite is *"Explain how the GAE auto-scaler decides to add or remove an instance."* Below is a complete, step-by-step analytical derivation.

#### Step 1 — Define the System Variables

Let the system at time $t$ have:

- $R(t)$ = aggregate inbound request rate (req/s)
- $N(t)$ = current number of live instances
- $\mu$ = maximum sustainable request rate per instance (req/s)
- $\rho_{\text{target}}$ = target utilization (default **0.65**)
- $L_{\text{pending}}(t)$ = oldest pending request age
- $L_{\text{max}}$ = max pending latency (default **30 s**)
- $L_{\text{min}}$ = min pending latency (default automatic)

#### Step 2 — Compute Per-Instance Load

The average load offered to each instance is:

$$
T_{\text{load}}(t) = \frac{R(t)}{N(t)}
$$

This is the simple division of total request rate by current instances.

#### Step 3 — Test Scale-Out Condition (Upper Threshold)

A new instance is **added** if **either** condition holds:

$$
T_{\text{load}}(t) \;>\; \mu \cdot \rho_{\text{target}}
$$

or

$$
L_{\text{pending}}(t) \;>\; L_{\text{min}}
$$

The first condition is utilization-driven; the second is latency-driven. The OR-coupling ensures the system reacts to **either** CPU saturation or user-visible slowness.

#### Step 4 — Test Scale-In Condition (Lower Threshold)

An instance is **removed** if the load is persistently low and no warm pool is required. The condition is:

$$
T_{\text{load}}(t) \;<\; \alpha \cdot \mu \cdot \rho_{\text{target}}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the hysteresis factor (default ≈ **0.4**) — this **prevents flapping** (rapid scale-out/scale-in oscillation).

#### Step 5 — Test Hard Reject Condition

If even the maximum allowed instances cannot keep up:

$$
L_{\text{pending}}(t) \;>\; L_{\text{max}} \quad \wedge \quad N(t) \;=\; N_{\text{max}}
$$

then incoming requests are rejected with **HTTP 503** or **429**. This protects the platform from cascading failure.

#### Step 6 — Closed-Loop Equilibrium

At steady state, the auto-scaler drives the system to a fixed point where:

$$
T_{\text{load}}^{*} = \mu \cdot \rho_{\text{target}}
$$

Solving for the equilibrium number of instances:

$$
N^{*} = \left\lceil \frac{R}{\mu \cdot \rho_{\text{target}}} \right\rceil
$$

This $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ ceiling operator is why a small spike may force one extra instance above the theoretical value.

#### Step 7 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

Suppose an app receives $R = 1200$ req/s, each instance sustains $\mu = 100$ req/s, target utilization is $\rho = 0.65$, and $L_{\text{min}} = 5$ s.

First compute the target load per instance:

$$
\mu \cdot \rho = 100 \cdot 0.65 = 65 \text{ req/s}
$$

Then compute the equilibrium number of instances:

$$
N^{*} = \left\lceil \frac{1200}{65} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 18.46 \right\rceil = 19 \text{ instances}
$$

Verify the achieved utilization per instance:

$$
T_{\text{load}} = \frac{1200}{19} \approx 63.16 \text{ req/s}
$$

Compare to the threshold:

$$
63.16 \;<\; 65 \;\;\checkmark \text{ (no scale-out)}
$$

If a traffic spike pushes $R$ to $1400$ req/s, recompute:

$$
N^{*} = \left\lceil \frac{1400}{65} \right\rceil = \lceil 21.54 \rceil = 22 \text{ instances}
$$

So the auto-scaler will provision **3 additional instances** to absorb the spike.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — `app.yaml` Configuration

A typical `app.yaml` block — the **single most-asked KTU question** — is shown below in a structured analytical matrix.

| Directive | Sample Value | Meaning / Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `runtime` | `python311` | Declares language runtime (Standard Env.) |
| `service` | `api` | Microservice name within the application |
| `entrypoint` | `gunicorn -b :$PORT main:app` | Process started inside the sandbox |
| `instance_class` | `F4` | vCPU + memory tier (e.g. F4 = 2.4 GHz, 1024 MB) |
| `automatic_scaling.target_cpu_utilization` | `0.65` | CPU target for scaling decisions |
| `automatic_scaling.min_instances` | `2` | Always-on warm pool floor |
| `automatic_scaling.max_instances` | `50` | Hard ceiling |
| `automatic_scaling.max_concurrent_requests` | `80` | HTTP/2 fan-out per instance |
| `automatic_scaling.min_idle_instances` | `1` | Reserved idle to cut cold starts |
| `env_variables` | `KEY=value` | Injected at boot |
| `handlers` (list) | URL → script routing | Maps paths to WSGI/Go entry points |
| `health_check.path` | `/healthz` | Endpoint for liveness probes |
| `readiness_check.path` | `/ready` | Endpoint for traffic gating |
| `vpc_access_connector` | `projects/.../connectors/x` | Serverless VPC connector for private resources |

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix — GAE Standard vs Flexible (Detailed)

| Dimension | Standard Env. | Flexible Env. |
|---|---|---|
| Hardware isolation | Shared sandbox (gVisor) | Dedicated VM per instance |
| Cold start | **~5–100 ms** | **~15–60 s** |
| Burst scaling | Yes (0 → thousands in seconds) | No (boots VMs) |
| Network egress | Egress charges apply | Same |
| Persistent disk | None | Yes (10 GB scratch) |
| WebSockets | Limited | Full |
| Custom binaries | Not allowed | Allowed (Dockerfile) |
| Cost per idle second | Almost zero (scales to 0) | Cost of VM hour (minimum 10 min) |
| Max instance lifetime | Stateless (no SLA on retention) | Configurable; supports long jobs |
| Best for | APIs, microservices, HTTP workloads | Legacy apps, custom ML models, persistent state |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of GAE

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client1[Client Browser or Mobile App]
    edge1[Google Global Edge and Load Balancer]
    router1[GAE Front End Router]
    sched1[Instance Scheduler and Auto Scaler]
    instA[App Instance 1]
    instB[App Instance 2]
    instC[App Instance N]
    ds[Datastore NoSQL]
    sql[Cloud SQL]
    cache[Memcache]
    queue[Task Queue]
    storage[Cloud Storage]
    monitor[Cloud Monitoring Stackdriver]
    iam[IAM and Security]

    client1 --> edge1 --> router1 --> sched1
    sched1 --> instA
    sched1 --> instB
    sched1 --> instC

    instA --> ds
    instA --> sql
    instA --> cache
    instA --> queue
    instA --> storage

    instB --> ds
    instB --> cache
    instC --> queue

    instA --> monitor
    instB --> monitor
    sched1 --> monitor

    sched1 --> iam
    router1 --> iam
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The client request flows from edge → router → scheduler → chosen instance, which in turn consults managed backing services (Datastore, Cloud SQL, Memcache, etc.). All components emit telemetry to Cloud Monitoring, and IAM guards every hop.

### 4.2 Application Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LocalDev
    LocalDev --> Build: gcloud app deploy
    Build --> Uploaded: Version uploaded to GAE
    Uploaded --> Staging: Health and Readiness checks
    Staging --> Serving: Traffic routed
    Serving --> ServingOld: New version deployed
    ServingOld --> Stopped: No traffic threshold met
    Stopped --> [*]
    Serving --> Scaled: Auto scaler adds or removes instances
    Scaled --> Serving
    Serving --> Stopped: Version manually stopped
```

### 4.3 Auto-Scaling Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Incoming Request at t]
    measure1[Measure R t and L pending t]
    check1{R load per instance greater than target}
    check2{Pending latency greater than L min}
    check3{Instance count less than max}
    scaleout[Add one instance]
    check4{Pending latency greater than L max}
    reject[Return HTTP 503 or 429]
    check5{Utilization below hysteresis alpha}
    scalein[Remove one instance]
    route[Route request to existing instance]

    start --> measure1
    measure1 --> check1
    check1 -- Yes --> check3
    check1 -- No --> check2
    check3 -- Yes --> scaleout --> route
    check3 -- No --> check4
    check4 -- Yes --> reject
    check4 -- No --> route
    check2 -- Yes --> check3
    check2 -- No --> check5
    check5 -- Yes --> scalein --> route
    check5 -- No --> route
```

### 4.4 Deployment Workflow Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dev1[Developer Machine]
    code1[Application Source Code]
    yaml1[app yaml Configuration]
    build1[Cloud Build Pipeline]
    image1[Docker Image for Flexible Env]
    registry1[Artifact Registry]
    gae1[GAE Deployment Service]
    ver1[Version v1]
    ver2[Version v2]
    split1[Traffic Split 90 10]
    promote1[Promote to 100 percent]

    dev1 --> code1
    dev1 --> yaml1
    code1 --> build1
    yaml1 --> build1
    build1 --> image1
    image1 --> registry1
    registry1 --> gae1
    code1 --> gae1
    gae1 --> ver1
    gae1 --> ver2
    ver1 --> split1
    ver2 --> split1
    split1 --> promote1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This block diagram models GAE's CI/CD-style lifecycle: code + manifest → Cloud Build → Docker image → Artifact Registry → GAE Deployment Service → multiple versions coexisting → traffic split → final promotion to 100%.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]**
*Define Platform as a Service. Mention any three characteristics of Google App Engine that classify it as a PaaS offering.*

**Model Answer:**

**Platform as a Service (PaaS)** is a cloud service model in which the provider delivers a complete runtime environment — including the operating system, middleware, runtime, and managed services — so that the developer only provides the application code and data.

Three characteristics of GAE that classify it as PaaS are:

1. **No server management** — Google handles the OS, runtime, patching, and security updates; the developer does not provision or maintain VMs.
2. **Auto-scaling and load balancing** are declarative and platform-managed through `app.yaml`, removing the need for manual infrastructure tuning.
3. **Built-in managed services** (Datastore, Memcache, Task Queues, Cloud Endpoints) are provided as out-of-the-box platform capabilities rather than installed by the developer.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Differentiate between the Standard Environment and the Flexible Environment of Google App Engine. (Any 3 points)*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Standard Environment | Flexible Environment |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Supports only fixed runtimes (Python, Java, Node.js, Go, PHP, Ruby) | Supports **any** language that can be packaged in a Docker image |
| 2 | Sandboxed on gVisor — no local disk write, no SSH, no custom binaries | Runs inside a Docker container on a dedicated VM with read-write filesystem and SSH access |
| 3 | Cold start is in **milliseconds**; auto-scales to **zero** instances | Cold start is in **seconds-to-minutes**; instances are VMs with a minimum runtime |
| 4 | Pricing is per **instance-hour** of a small number of predefined instance classes | Pricing is per **vCPU + GB memory per second** of the underlying VM |
| 5 | Best suited for spiky REST APIs and microservices | Best suited for legacy applications, custom runtimes, and long-running workloads |

### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks.

---

#### ❑ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **high-level architecture of Google App Engine** with a neat block diagram. List the responsibilities of each tier.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* With the help of relevant formulas, explain **how the GAE auto-scaler decides to scale out and scale in** an application running in the Standard Environment. Use a numerical example in your explanation.

##### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

The architecture of Google App Engine is divided into **five logical tiers**:

**1. Edge / Front-End Tier:** Terminates HTTPS at Google's global edge, performs TLS offloading, DDoS protection, and global load balancing. It receives the inbound request and dispatches it into the Google network.

**2. Router / Scheduler Tier:** Decides which **service → version → instance** should handle the request. It also performs health checks against the `/_ah/health` endpoint and gates traffic using the readiness check.

**3. Instance Tier (Compute):** This is where the application code actually runs.
- *Standard Environment:* inside a **gVisor sandbox** on shared infrastructure.
- *Flexible Environment:* inside a **Docker container** on a dedicated VM.

**4. Managed Services Tier:** Backing services used by the application.
- **Datastore** — NoSQL document store.
- **Cloud SQL** — managed MySQL/PostgreSQL.
- **Memcache** — in-memory cache.
- **Task Queue** — asynchronous work dispatcher.
- **Cloud Storage / Blobstore** — large object storage.
- **Cloud Endpoints** — API gateway with auth and quotas.

**5. Operations Tier:** Provides **Cloud Monitoring (Stackdriver)** for logs, metrics, and traces; **Cloud Build** for CI/CD; and **IAM** for security and authorization.

> **Block diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 for the complete Mermaid schematic.

##### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

The auto-scaler in the GAE Standard Environment operates as a **closed-loop controller** that observes three key variables at every polling interval:

- $R(t)$ — aggregate request rate
- $L_{\text{pending}}(t)$ — age of the oldest request waiting in the queue
- $N(t)$ — current number of live instances

**Scale-out decision (add an instance)** is taken when **either** of the following is true:

$$
T_{\text{load}}(t) = \frac{R(t)}{N(t)} \;>\; \mu \cdot \rho_{\text{target}}
$$

or

$$
L_{\text{pending}}(t) \;>\; L_{\text{min}}
$$

**Scale-in decision (remove an instance)** is taken when the load is **persistently low**:

$$
T_{\text{load}}(t) \;<\; \alpha \cdot \mu \cdot \rho_{\text{target}}
$$

where $\alpha$ is a **hysteresis factor** (≈ 0.4) that prevents flapping.

**Hard reject decision** is taken when:

$$
L_{\text{pending}}(t) \;>\; L_{\text{max}} \;\;\wedge\;\; N(t) = N_{\text{max}}
$$

At equilibrium, the number of instances is:

$$
N^{*} = \left\lceil \frac{R}{\mu \cdot \rho_{\text{target}}} \right\rceil
$$

**Numerical example:**
Given $R = 1200$ req/s, $\mu = 100$ req/s, $\rho = 0.65$:

$$
\mu \cdot \rho = 65 \text{ req/s}
$$

$$
N^{*} = \left\lceil \frac{1200}{65} \right\rceil = 19 \text{ instances}
$$

If a traffic spike raises $R$ to $1400$ req/s:

$$
N^{*} = \left\lceil \frac{1400}{65} \right\rceil = 22 \text{ instances}
$$

Therefore, the auto-scaler will provision **3 additional instances** to maintain the target utilization.

---

#### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** *(7 marks)* List and explain the **four scaling modes** of Google App Engine. State the `app.yaml` configuration block for each.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **application lifecycle** of GAE from local development to production. List the key `gcloud` CLI commands used at each stage.

##### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

The four scaling modes of GAE are:

**1. Automatic Scaling** — The platform continuously measures load and provisions instances to keep utilization around a configurable target.

```yaml
automatic_scaling:
  min_instances: 2
  max_instances: 50
  target_cpu_utilization: 0.65
  min_idle_instances: 1
  max_concurrent_requests: 80
```

**2. Manual Scaling** — The developer declares a **fixed number of instances**; the platform does not scale automatically.

```yaml
manual_scaling:
  instances: 5
```

**3. Basic Scaling** — An instance is **created when a request arrives** and **shut down** after a configurable `idle_timeout` of inactivity (default **15 minutes**).

```yaml
basic_scaling:
  max_instances: 10
  idle_timeout: 10m
```

**4. (Always-on, legacy)** — Reserves at least one instance running 24/7 to eliminate cold starts. Available only in older Standard runtimes.

**Comparison table:**

| Mode | Auto-Scale | Cold Start | Cost Floor | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Automatic | Yes | Mitigated by `min_idle` | `min_instances` × $/hr | APIs, microservices |
| Manual | No | None if `instances ≥ 1` | `instances` × $/hr | Steady workload |
| Basic | Yes (per request) | **Yes** (every request) | Zero when idle | Spiky low-traffic |
| Always-on | No | None | 1 instance × 24 hr | Legacy apps |

##### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

The application lifecycle of a GAE application consists of the following stages:

**Stage 1 — Local Development**
The developer codes locally and tests using the local SDK emulator.
- *Command:* `dev_appserver.py app.yaml` (legacy) or `gcloud beta emulators appengine`.

**Stage 2 — Build**
Source code is compiled (for compiled languages) and packaged. In Flexible Environment, a Docker image is built via `gcloud builds submit`.

**Stage 3 — Upload / Deploy**
The version is uploaded to the GAE Deployment Service.
- *Command:* `gcloud app deploy app.yaml --version=v1 --no-promote`

The `--no-promote` flag means the version is uploaded but does not yet receive traffic.

**Stage 4 — Staging & Health Checks**
The platform boots instances of the new version, runs **liveness (`/_ah/health`)** and **readiness** checks. Only healthy instances are added to the load-balancer pool.

**Stage 5 — Traffic Splitting**
Multiple versions can co-exist. Traffic is split by percentage for **canary** or **blue-green** deployments.
- *Command:* `gcloud app services set-traffic default --splits=v2=0.1,v1=0.9`

**Stage 6 — Promotion**
Once verified, the new version receives 100% traffic.
- *Command:* `gcloud app services set-traffic default --splits=v2=1.0`

**Stage 7 — Stop / Rollback**
The old version can be stopped or deleted.
- *Command:* `gcloud app versions delete v1`

**Stage 8 — Auto-Scaling at Runtime**
The auto-scaler continuously adjusts the instance count as described in Q.A (b).

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in GAE questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PaaS with IaaS:** Do NOT mention SSH, OS patching, or VM provisioning as features of GAE. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this.
> 2. **Mixing Standard and Flexible parameters:** Writing `instance_class` (Standard-only) inside a Flexible Environment block is a frequent blunder. The Flexible Env. uses `resources` (CPU/memory) in `app.yaml`.
> 3. **Forgetting the hysteresis factor:** A student who explains scale-out but not the `alpha` hysteresis will lose a mark, because the examiner expects to see **flapping prevention** discussed.
> 4. **Confusing `min_idle_instances` with `min_instances`:** The former is a **warm pool** (cuts cold-start latency); the latter is the **absolute floor**. This distinction is a classic 1-mark differentiator.
> 5. **Omitting cold-start cost in the numerical example:** A 14-mark numerical question on auto-scaling without acknowledging cold-start latency is considered incomplete.
> 6. **Forgetting the `app.yaml` block in scaling questions:** The examiner expects to see at least the **YAML snippet**, not just prose.
> 7. **Treating Datastore as a relational DB:** It is a **schemaless, NoSQL, document-like** store with **entity groups and ancestor queries**. Calling it SQL is an automatic 1-mark cut.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for "PaaS Offerings: GAE":**
>
> - ☐ GAE is a **PaaS** offering by Google Cloud.
> - ☐ Two execution environments: **Standard** (gVisor sandbox, fixed runtimes) and **Flexible** (Docker container, any runtime).
> - ☐ Standard is for spiky APIs; Flexible is for legacy apps and custom runtimes.
> - ☐ Four scaling modes: **Automatic, Manual, Basic, Always-on (legacy)**.
> - ☐ Auto-scaling parameters: `min_instances`, `max_instances`, `target_cpu_utilization` (default **0.65**), `min_idle_instances`, `max_concurrent_requests`, `min_pending_latency`, `max_pending_latency`.
> - ☐ Hysteresis factor `alpha ≈ 0.4` prevents scale-out/scale-in flapping.
> - ☐ Equilibrium instance count: $N^{*} = \lceil R \,/\, (\mu \rho) \rceil$.
> - ☐ Five-tier architecture: Edge → Router → Instances → Managed Services → Operations.
> - ☐ Key managed services: **Datastore, Cloud SQL, Memcache, Task Queue, Cloud Storage, Cloud Endpoints**.
> - ☐ Cold start: **~5–100 ms** (Standard) vs **~15–60 s** (Flexible).
> - ☐ Deployment is via `gcloud app deploy app.yaml`; traffic split via `gcloud app services set-traffic`.
> - ☐ GAE is **stateless**; persistent state goes to **Datastore / Cloud SQL / Memcache**.
> - ☐ SLA: **99.95%** for Standard Env., **99.9%** for Flexible Env.
> - ☐ Pricing unit: **instance-hours** (Standard) or **vCPU-second + GB-second** (Flexible).
> - ☐ Health checks: `/_ah/health` (liveness) and configurable readiness endpoint.
> - ☐ App structure: `app.yaml` is the single source of truth for runtime, scaling, and routing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Storage Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud Storage Services** are **on-demand, network-accessible, elastic, and metered data storage subsystems** delivered as a managed service over the Internet. They abstract the underlying physical storage hardware (HDDs, SSDs, tapes) and present logical storage primitives — **Object, Block, and File storage** — to consumers via standardized APIs (REST/SOAP) or industry protocols (iSCSI, NFS, SMB).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme Cloud Computing syllabus (PEERT524, Module 4)**, *Storage Services* is positioned as the data-persistence backbone of every IaaS/PaaS/SaaS offering. The formal definition, per the syllabus outcome **CO4**, is:

*"A logically pooled, geographically distributed, fault-tolerant storage fabric that delivers **5 nines (99.999%)** durability, multi-region replication, pay-per-GB billing, and policy-driven access control to tenants over standard network protocols."*

Key terminologies mandated by the KTU syllabus:
- **Object Storage** – Data managed as discrete, addressable units (objects) inside flat namespaces called *buckets* or *containers*.
- **Block Storage** – Raw, fixed-size storage volumes (typically **512 bytes – 16 MB blocks**) attached to a single VM instance.
- **File Storage** – Hierarchical POSIX/NTFS-style file systems shared across multiple clients using NFS/SMB.
- **Storage Tier** – Cost-vs-performance class (Hot, Cool/Cold, Archive).
- **Data Consistency Model** – Strong, eventual, or read-after-write.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Warehouse" Model

Imagine you run a **national logistics company**. Instead of owning warehouses, you rent space from a giant, automated warehouse provider (AWS, Azure, GCP). You never visit the warehouse; you send a courier (API call) with a barcode label (object key) and either:
- **Drop a box** (Object Storage — `S3 / Azure Blob`) — perfect for photos, videos, backups.
- **Rent a private locker bolted to your office desk** (Block Storage — `EBS / Azure Disk`) — perfect for databases that need low latency.
- **Rent a shared filing cabinet with folders** (File Storage — `EFS / Azure Files`) — perfect when multiple employees need to edit the same documents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary for First-Time Learners:**
> Cloud storage is *just a hard drive that lives on the Internet*. The "cloud" part means you don't own the disk, you rent it by the hour or by the gigabyte, and it automatically copies itself to multiple physical locations so a meteor could hit one data center and your data would still survive.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics You Must Memorize for KTU Exams

The following constants appear in nearly every KTU Numerical/Descriptive question on Module 4:

- **Durability of Object Storage:** **99.999999999 % (11 nines)** — industry-standard for top-tier cloud object stores.
- **Availability SLA:** Typically **99.9 %** (3 nines) to **99.99 %** (4 nines).
- **Maximum single object size:** **5 TB** (AWS S3 / Azure Blob).
- **Minimum billable block size:** **1 GB-month** for cold tiers; **per-request** for hot tiers.
- **Default block size for cloud block storage:** **4 KB – 16 MB** (configurable).
- **Replication factor (default):** **3 copies** across **3 Availability Zones**.


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Cloud Storage — Operational Breakdown

#### Pillar 1: Object Storage (e.g., AWS S3, Azure Blob, GCP Cloud Storage)

**Operational Logic (Why & How):**
- *Why?* Traditional file systems collapse when files exceed billions because directory trees become bottlenecks. Object stores use a **flat namespace** — every object has a unique key (URL) and an associated **metadata map** (size, MIME type, custom tags).
- *How?* When a `PUT` request arrives, the storage controller:
  1. Computes an MD5/SHA-256 hash for integrity.
  2. Generates a globally unique object key.
  3. Stores the object on **one of N storage nodes** using a **Consistent Hashing** ring.
  4. Replicates to **N+1 or N+2 nodes** based on durability class.
  5. Updates the index/metadata database (DynamoDB-style).

> [!NOTE]
> **Object Storage is ideal for:** static websites, backups, log archival, big-data lakes (data used by Spark/Hadoop), disaster recovery, and media streaming.

#### Pillar 2: Block Storage (e.g., AWS EBS, Azure Managed Disks, GCP Persistent Disk)

**Operational Logic:**
- *Why?* Operating systems and databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB) require **raw, low-latency, randomly writable byte-addressable** storage. Object storage is eventually consistent and not random-writable.
- *How?* A block volume is a virtual disk carved from a **RAID array of SSDs/HDDs** in the hypervisor's storage area network (SAN). It is exposed as `/dev/sda`, `/dev/xvdf`, etc., to the guest VM via iSCSI or NVMe-over-Fabrics.

**Key Properties:**
- **Single-tenant attachment** (one volume = one VM at a time, except for shared-NVMe clusters).
- **IOPS-bound** — performance measured in **Input/Output Operations Per Second** and **Throughput (MB/s)**.
- **Snapshots** are incremental, copy-on-write, point-in-time copies.

#### Pillar 3: File Storage (e.g., AWS EFS, Azure Files, GCP Filestore)

**Operational Logic:**
- *Why?* Legacy enterprise applications (SAP, Oracle E-Business Suite, content management systems) expect a **POSIX-compliant** shared file system that multiple VMs can mount simultaneously.
- *How?* Implemented as a **distributed parallel file system** (e.g., Lustre, GPFS, custom NFSv4 cluster). Data is striped across many storage nodes using a metadata server (MDS) + data server (DS) architecture.

### 2.2 Storage Service Tier Matrix

| Tier Class | Access Frequency | Retrieval Latency | Typical Use Case | Relative Cost (per GB/month) |
|------------|------------------|-------------------|------------------|------------------------------|
| **Hot / Standard** | Frequent (daily) | Milliseconds | Web content, analytics | **Highest** (~$0.023) |
| **Cool / Infrequent Access** | 1× per month | Milliseconds–seconds | Disaster recovery, backups | Medium (~$0.0125) |
| **Cold / Archive** | 1–2× per year | Minutes–hours | Compliance, long-term archival | **Lowest** (~$0.004) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *"How does the cost model of cold storage differ from hot storage?"*, always mention **retrieval fees** and **minimum storage duration penalties** (e.g., 90 days for S3 IA, 180 days for Glacier).

### 2.3 Consistency Models — A KTU Favourite

| Model | Definition | Example Service |
|-------|------------|-----------------|
| **Strong Consistency** | All clients see the same data immediately after a write completes | AWS EBS, Azure Disk |
| **Read-After-Write Consistency** | A read immediately after a write returns the new value (only for the writer) | AWS S3 (post-2020) |
| **Eventual Consistency** | Replicas converge within seconds; temporary staleness is acceptable | AWS S3 (older), DynamoDB |
| **Causal Consistency** | Operations causally related are seen in order | Some Azure Cosmos DB modes |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **exam-critical** for any KTU 2024 Scheme numerical on storage services.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Effective Storage Capacity** | $C_{\text{effective}} = (N_{\text{disks}} \times S_{\text{disk}}) \times \eta_{\text{RAID}}$ | $\eta_{\text{RAID}} = 0.5$ (RAID-1), $0.75$ (RAID-5), $1.0$ (RAID-0) |
| 2 | **Replication Factor Cost** | $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{logical}} \times R$ | $R = 3$ default in AWS S3, Azure Blob |
| 3 | **Durability (N-nines)** | $D = 1 - (1 - p)^{n}$ | $p$ = single-object loss probability, $n$ = number of replicas |
| 4 | **Availability (uptime %)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100$ | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair |
| 5 | **Annual Downtime** | $T_{\text{down}} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | Minutes/year for given availability |
| 6 | **Monthly Cost (Object Store)** | $C_{\text{monthly}} = (S_{\text{GB}} \times P_{\text{storage}}) + (N_{\text{req}} \times P_{\text{req}})$ | $P_{\text{storage}}$ per GB-month, $P_{\text{req}}$ per 10,000 requests |
| 7 | **IOPS Requirement** | $\text{IOPS}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{IOPS}_{i}$ | Sum across all VMs attached to a volume |
| 8 | **Throughput** | $T = \text{IOPS} \times \dfrac{B_{\text{block}}}{1024^2}$ | MB/s = IOPS × Block size in MB |
| 9 | **Erasure Coding Overhead** | $E = \dfrac{k}{k+m}$ | $k$ data shards, $m$ parity shards (e.g., RS 6+3 → $E = 0.667$) |
| 10 | **RTO / RPO Definitions** | $\text{RTO} = \text{Recovery Time Objective}$, $\text{RPO} = \text{Recovery Point Objective}$ | RPO measured in data loss tolerance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The durability equation $D = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$ is used by AWS/Azure engineers to **prove compliance with 11 nines** ($D \geq 0.99999999999$) during SLA negotiations with enterprise customers (banks, hospitals).

### 2.5 Content Delivery Networks (CDN) — Extension of Storage

A CDN is a **geographically distributed cache layer** sitting *in front of* object storage. When a user requests `/video.mp4`, the CDN edge server nearest to the user delivers the bytes, drastically reducing latency.

- **Cache Hit Ratio (CHR):** $\text{CHR} = \dfrac{\text{Cache Hits}}{\text{Cache Hits} + \text{Cache Misses}}$
- **Origin Shield:** A dedicated tier of caching between edge and origin (S3) to reduce origin load.
- **Examples:** AWS CloudFront, Azure CDN, GCP Cloud CDN, Akamai, Cloudflare.


<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Durability from Replication

**Problem Statement:** A cloud object store replicates every object across **$n = 3$** independent data centers. Each data center has an annual failure probability of $p = 0.001$ (i.e., 0.1 % chance of losing a copy). What is the probability that **all replicas** are simultaneously lost?

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Probability that a single replica survives:**
A single replica survives if the data center does NOT fail. Therefore:

$$
P_{\text{survive}} = 1 - p
$$

Substituting $p = 0.001$:

$$
P_{\text{survive}} = 1 - 0.001 = 0.999
$$

**Step 2 — Probability that ALL $n$ replicas survive independently:**

Since the data centers fail independently (no common-mode failure considered), the joint survival probability is:

$$
P_{\text{all survive}} = (1 - p)^{n}
$$

Substituting $n = 3$:

$$
P_{\text{all survive}} = (0.999)^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation:**
Expanding the cube:

$$
P_{\text{all survive}} = (0.999)^{3} = 0.999 \times 0.999 \times 0.999
$$

Multiplying step by step:

$$
P_{\text{all survive}} = 0.998001 \times 0.999 = 0.997002999
$$

**Step 4 — Probability of TOTAL data loss (all 3 replicas fail simultaneously):**

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = 1 - P_{\text{all survive}} = 1 - 0.997002999
$$

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = 0.002997001 \approx 0.003 \text{ (or 0.3 %)}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to "nines" notation (Durability):**

$$
D = 1 - P_{\text{loss}} = 0.997002999
$$

This is only **2.97 nines**, far below the cloud standard of **11 nines**. To reach 11 nines, replication must increase to **$n = 6$ or use erasure coding**.

**Verification for $n = 6$:**

$$
P_{\text{all survive}} = (0.999)^{6}
$$

Using logarithms $\ln(0.999) \approx -0.0010005$:

$$
\ln(P) = 6 \times (-0.0010005) = -0.006003
$$

$$
P_{\text{all survive}} = e^{-0.006003} \approx 0.99398
$$

This still gives only ~2.99 nines, proving that **simple replication cannot achieve 11 nines for high $p$**. Industry instead uses **erasure coding (Reed-Solomon)** + multi-region replication.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Storage Capacity with RAID

**Problem:** A cloud provider configures an **EBS-backed block store** using **8 disks of 1 TB each** in a **RAID-5** array (striping with single parity). What is the **effective usable capacity**?

**Step 1 — Identify RAID-5 efficiency:**

For RAID-5 with $N$ disks, usable capacity = $(N-1)$ disks worth of space because 1 disk is parity:

$$
\eta_{\text{RAID-5}} = \dfrac{N-1}{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute raw capacity:**

$$
C_{\text{raw}} = N_{\text{disks}} \times S_{\text{disk}} = 8 \times 1\ \text{TB} = 8\ \text{TB}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply RAID efficiency:**

$$
C_{\text{effective}} = C_{\text{raw}} \times \eta_{\text{RAID-5}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{effective}} = 8\ \text{TB} \times \dfrac{7}{8} = 7\ \text{TB}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for filesystem overhead (~5 %):**

$$
C_{\text{usable}} = 7\ \text{TB} \times 0.95 = 6.65\ \text{TB}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Monthly Cost Calculation (Composite Bill)

**Problem:** A startup stores **5 TB** in S3 Standard for 30 days, makes **10 million PUT requests** and **50 million GET requests**. Compute the monthly bill. Assume (illustrative rates):

- Storage: **\$0.023 per GB-month**
- PUT: **\$0.005 per 1,000 requests**
- GET: **\$0.0004 per 1,000 requests**

**Step 1 — Storage cost:**

Convert 5 TB to GB: $5 \times 1024 = 5120\ \text{GB}$.

$$
C_{\text{storage}} = 5120\ \text{GB} \times 0.023\ \$/\text{GB} = \$117.76
$$

**Step 2 — PUT request cost:**

10,000,000 / 1,000 = 10,000 units.

$$
C_{\text{PUT}} = 10{,}000 \times 0.005 = \$50.00
$$

**Step 3 — GET request cost:**

50,000,000 / 1,000 = 50,000 units.

$$
C_{\text{GET}} = 50{,}000 \times 0.0004 = \$20.00
$$

**Step 4 — Total monthly bill:**

$$
C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{storage}} + C_{\text{PUT}} + C_{\text{GET}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 117.76 + 50.00 + 20.00 = \$187.76
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — IOPS-to-Throughput Conversion

**Problem:** A gp3 EBS volume provides **3,000 IOPS** with a block size of **16 KB**. Compute throughput.

**Step 1 — Block size in MB:**

$$
B_{\text{block}} = \dfrac{16\ \text{KB}}{1024} = 0.015625\ \text{MB}
$$

**Step 2 — Throughput formula:**

$$
T = \text{IOPS} \times B_{\text{block}}
$$

$$
T = 3{,}000 \times 0.015625\ \text{MB/s}
$$

$$
T = 46.875\ \text{MB/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Mbps:**

$$
T_{\text{Mbps}} = 46.875 \times 8 = 375\ \text{Mbps}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Which Storage to Choose?

| Workload | Recommended Storage | Reason |
|----------|--------------------|--------|
| MySQL database on a single VM | **Block (EBS)** | Needs low-latency random writes |
| Static website images | **Object (S3)** + **CDN** | Cheap, infinite scale, cacheable |
| Shared home directories for HPC cluster | **File (EFS/Lustre)** | POSIX, parallel, multi-client |
| 7-year compliance archive | **Cold Object (Glacier Deep Archive)** | Cheapest per-GB |
| Boot disk of a VM | **Block (EBS)** | OS requires raw block device |
| Video streaming (Netflix-scale) | **Object (S3) + CloudFront CDN** | High throughput, edge caching |
| Big-data lake (Spark/Hive input) | **Object (S3) with Hadoop-compatible layer** | Decouples compute & storage |


<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cloud Storage System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ClientApp["Client Application"]
    APIGateway["REST API Gateway"]
    AuthSvc["Authentication and IAM Service"]
    MetaDB["Metadata Index Database"]
    ConsHash["Consistent Hashing Ring"]
    StorageNodeA["Storage Node AZ1"]
    StorageNodeB["Storage Node AZ2"]
    StorageNodeC["Storage Node AZ3"]
    ReplicationMgr["Replication Manager"]
    ErasureEncoder["Erasure Coding Encoder"]
    BackupVault["Backup and Archive Vault"]
    CDNEdge["CDN Edge Cache"]

    ClientApp --> APIGateway
    APIGateway --> AuthSvc
    AuthSvc --> MetaDB
    APIGateway --> ConsHash
    ConsHash --> StorageNodeA
    ConsHash --> StorageNodeB
    ConsHash --> StorageNodeC
    ReplicationMgr --> StorageNodeA
    ReplicationMgr --> StorageNodeB
    ReplicationMgr --> StorageNodeC
    ErasureEncoder --> StorageNodeA
    ErasureEncoder --> StorageNodeB
    ErasureEncoder --> StorageNodeC
    StorageNodeA --> BackupVault
    ClientApp --> CDNEdge
    CDNEdge --> StorageNodeA
```

### 4.2 Storage Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartNew["Need to Store Data"]
    Q1{"Random low latency writes required"}
    Q2{"Multiple VMs share files simultaneously"}
    Q3{"Massive unstructured data backup or media"}
    BlockChoice["Use Block Storage EBS or Azure Disk"]
    FileChoice["Use File Storage EFS or Azure Files"]
    ObjectChoice["Use Object Storage S3 or Azure Blob"]
    Q4{"Frequent access"}
    HotTier["Hot Tier Standard"]
    ColdTier["Cold Tier Glacier or Archive"]
    EndNode["Storage Selected"]

    StartNew --> Q1
    Q1 -->|Yes| BlockChoice
    Q1 -->|No| Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| FileChoice
    Q2 -->|No| Q3
    Q3 -->|Yes| ObjectChoice
    ObjectChoice --> Q4
    Q4 -->|Yes| HotTier
    Q4 -->|No| ColdTier
    BlockChoice --> EndNode
    FileChoice --> EndNode
    HotTier --> EndNode
    ColdTier --> EndNode
```

### 4.3 Tiered Storage Lifecycle Policy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DataCreate["Object Created in Standard"]
    Day0["Day 0 to 30"]
    MoveIA["Transition to Infrequent Access"]
    Day90["Day 30 to 90"]
    MoveGlacier["Transition to Glacier"]
    Day365["Day 90 to 365"]
    MoveDeep["Move to Deep Archive"]
    Expire["Lifecycle Expiry and Delete"]

    DataCreate --> Day0
    Day0 --> MoveIA
    MoveIA --> Day90
    Day90 --> MoveGlacier
    MoveGlacier --> Day365
    Day365 --> MoveDeep
    MoveDeep --> Expire
```

### 4.4 RAID Levels — Visual Comparison (Block Composition)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1["Disk1"]
    D2["Disk2"]
    D3["Disk3"]
    D4["Disk4"]
    RAID0["RAID 0 Striping No Redundancy Usable 100 percent"]
    RAID1["RAID 1 Mirroring Usable 50 percent"]
    RAID5["RAID 5 Striping Plus Parity Usable N minus 1 over N"]
    RAID6["RAID 6 Double Parity Usable N minus 2 over N"]

    D1 --> RAID0
    D2 --> RAID0
    RAID0 --> RAID1
    D1 --> RAID1
    D2 --> RAID1
    RAID1 --> RAID5
    D1 --> RAID5
    D2 --> RAID5
    D3 --> RAID5
    RAID5 --> RAID6
    D4 --> RAID6
```

### 4.5 CDN Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    User["End User in Kerala"]
    EdgeMumbai["Edge POP Mumbai"]
    EdgeChennai["Edge POP Chennai"]
    Origin["Origin S3 Bucket Mumbai Region"]
    Shield["Regional Origin Shield Cache"]

    User -->|First Request| EdgeMumbai
    EdgeMumbai -->|Cache Miss| Shield
    Shield -->|Cache Miss| Origin
    Origin -->|Video Bytes| Shield
    Shield -->|Video Bytes| EdgeMumbai
    EdgeMumbai -->|Video Bytes| User
    User -->|Second Request| EdgeMumbai
    EdgeMumbai -->|Cache Hit| EdgeMumbai
    EdgeChennai -.Backup Edge.-> Origin
```


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between Object Storage and Block Storage. Give one real-world cloud example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criteria | Object Storage | Block Storage |
|----------|----------------|---------------|
| Data Unit | Whole object (file + metadata) | Fixed-size blocks (4 KB – 16 MB) |
| Access Protocol | REST API (HTTP `PUT/GET/DELETE`) | iSCSI / NVMe-oF (mounted as raw disk) |
| Use Case | Unstructured data, backups, media | Databases, OS boot disks |
| Example | **AWS S3** | **AWS EBS** |

Object storage treats data as discrete units in a flat namespace, optimized for **scale and durability (11 nines)** but not for random writes. Block storage exposes raw volumes to a VM for **low-latency, transactional workloads**.

*(Award 1 mark for correct definition, 1 mark for the table comparison, 1 mark for valid cloud examples.)*

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is "Eventual Consistency" in cloud object stores? Why is it used instead of Strong Consistency?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Eventual Consistency** is a consistency model where, after a write, all replicas of an object **converge to the same value within a short, bounded time** (typically < 1 second), but a read immediately following the write may return the **stale value** for some replicas.

**Why used instead of Strong Consistency?**
1. **Availability & Partition tolerance (CAP theorem):** Strong consistency requires a quorum on every write, which becomes impossible during network partitions. Eventual consistency keeps the service available.
2. **Lower write latency** — no synchronous replication across regions.
3. **Higher throughput** — writes are acknowledged as soon as one replica persists.

AWS S3 uses read-after-write consistency for new objects since 2020, but **updates and deletes still exhibit eventual consistency**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three categories of cloud storage** (Object, Block, File) with diagrams. For each, mention one typical AWS service and one ideal workload.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A company stores **20 TB** of archival data in **AWS S3 Glacier Deep Archive**. Compute the **annual storage cost** if the rate is **\$0.00099 per GB-month**. If retrieval incurs **\$0.02 per GB**, what is the cost to restore the entire 20 TB once?

#### (a) Model Solution [7 marks]

**1. Object Storage (2 marks):**
Data is stored as **objects** in a **flat bucket** with a unique key + metadata. Accessed over **REST/HTTPS**.
- *AWS Service:* **S3 (Simple Storage Service)**
- *Workload:* Static website assets, video backups, log archive.

**2. Block Storage (2 marks):**
Data is split into **fixed-size blocks** and exposed as a **raw disk** to a single VM.
- *AWS Service:* **EBS (Elastic Block Store)**
- *Workload:* Relational databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL), OS boot volumes.

**3. File Storage (2 marks):**
A **POSIX-compliant shared file system** mounted by multiple clients via NFS.
- *AWS Service:* **EFS (Elastic File System)** for Linux, **FSx for Windows** for SMB.
- *Workload:* Content management, shared home directories, HPC scratch.

**4. Comparative Diagram (1 mark):**
A simple 3-column table contrasting access method, attachment, and protocol as shown in SECTION 2 must be reproduced.

#### (b) Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Convert TB to GB (1 mark):**

$$
20\ \text{TB} = 20 \times 1024\ \text{GB} = 20{,}480\ \text{GB}
$$

**Step 2 — Monthly storage cost (2 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{monthly}} = 20{,}480 \times 0.00099 = \$20.2752
$$

**Step 3 — Annual storage cost (2 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{annual}} = C_{\text{monthly}} \times 12 = 20.2752 \times 12 = \$243.30
$$

**Step 4 — One-time retrieval cost (2 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{retrieval}} = 20{,}480 \times 0.02 = \$409.60
$$

**Final Answer (in bold for emphasis):**
**Annual Storage Cost ≈ \$243.30**, **One-time Retrieval Cost ≈ \$409.60**, **Total ≈ \$652.90.**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss **Replication** and **Erasure Coding** as redundancy strategies in cloud storage. Compare their **storage overhead** and **failure tolerance** with an example of $(k=4, m=2)$ Reed-Solomon coding vs **3-way replication**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cloud block-storage service uses **RAID-6 across 10 disks of 2 TB each**. Calculate the **effective usable capacity** and the **maximum number of simultaneous disk failures** the array can survive.

#### (a) Model Solution [7 marks]

**Replication (2 marks):**
Each object is copied $R$ times (typically $R=3$) across independent failure domains (Availability Zones).
- **Overhead:** $R \times$ original size → **3× overhead for 3-way replication**.
- **Failure Tolerance:** Can survive $R - 1$ failures (i.e., 2 in 3-way replication).

**Erasure Coding (3 marks):**
Data is split into $k$ data shards + $m$ parity shards. Original data can be reconstructed from any $k$ of the $k+m$ shards.
- **Overhead:** $\dfrac{k+m}{k}$ → for $(k=4, m=2)$, overhead $= 6/4 = 1.5\times$.
- **Failure Tolerance:** Can survive any $m$ simultaneous failures (2 in this case).

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | 3-way Replication | Erasure Coding (4,2) |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Storage Overhead | **300 %** (3× raw data) | **150 %** (1.5× raw data) |
| Failure Tolerance | 2 disks/zones | 2 shards |
| Read Latency | Fast (read from nearest replica) | Slower (decode required) |
| Best For | Hot data, small objects | Cold data, large objects (e.g., 100 MB+) |

#### (b) Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — RAID-6 efficiency formula (2 marks):**

For RAID-6 with $N$ disks, **2 disks are parity**:

$$
C_{\text{effective}} = (N - 2) \times S_{\text{disk}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (2 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{effective}} = (10 - 2) \times 2\ \text{TB} = 8 \times 2 = 16\ \text{TB}
$$

**Step 3 — Failure tolerance (2 marks):**

RAID-6 can survive **simultaneous failure of any 2 disks** without data loss.

**Step 4 — Final answer (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{effective}} = 16\ \text{TB},\quad \text{Tolerable Failures} = 2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In Part A, students often confuse **Block** and **File** storage. Remember: *Block = raw disk to one VM*, *File = shared folder to many VMs*.
> 2. In RAID numericals, **forgetting to subtract parity disks** is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks. Always state the formula before substituting.
> 3. When asked about *erasure coding*, **explicitly mention the decode/reconstruction latency** as a drawback — many students only state the storage savings and lose a mark.
> 4. In cost-calculation questions, **do not forget retrieval fees** for cold/archive tiers. Examiners frequently hide a "restore" sub-question worth 2 marks.
> 5. Avoid writing "S3 is free" or "cloud storage has unlimited capacity" — these statements lose accuracy marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Three storage types:** Object (S3), Block (EBS), File (EFS) — know AWS, Azure, and GCP equivalents.
- [ ] **Object storage** = flat namespace, REST API, 11 nines durability, 5 TB max object size.
- [ ] **Block storage** = raw volume to one VM, IOPS-bound, snapshots are incremental.
- [ ] **File storage** = POSIX-compliant, multi-client, NFS/SMB protocol, used for legacy enterprise apps.
- [ ] **Consistency models:** Strong, Read-After-Write, Eventual, Causal — S3 uses RAWR for new objects.
- [ ] **Storage tiers:** Hot, Cool/IA, Cold/Archive — each with different retrieval latency and minimum-storage-day penalties.
- [ ] **Replication factor** default = **3** across **3 AZs**; Erasure Coding (e.g., RS 6+3) used for cold data to reduce overhead.
- [ ] **Durability formula:** $D = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$ — must understand for numericals.
- [ ] **Availability formula:** $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ and annual downtime in minutes.
- [ ] **RAID efficiency:** RAID-0 = 100 %, RAID-1 = 50 %, RAID-5 = $(N-1)/N$, RAID-6 = $(N-2)/N$.
- [ ] **IOPS-to-Throughput:** $T = \text{IOPS} \times B_{\text{block}}$ in MB/s.
- [ ] **Cost formula:** $C = (S_{\text{GB}} \times P_{\text{storage}}) + (N_{\text{req}} \times P_{\text{req}}) + C_{\text{retrieval}}$.
- [ ] **CDN** = distributed cache layer in front of origin storage; metric = **Cache Hit Ratio**.
- [ ] **RTO vs RPO:** RTO = how fast you recover; RPO = how much data you can afford to lose.
- [ ] **Lifecycle policies** automate transitions Standard → IA → Glacier → Deep Archive → Delete.
- [ ] Always **convert TB → GB by × 1024**, not × 1000, in KTU numericals (1 TB = 1024 GB).


<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Application Services** in cloud computing refer to the on-demand delivery of software applications, application platforms, integration middleware, and related programming services over a network (typically the Internet) through a cloud provider's infrastructure. According to the **NIST SP 800-145** reference model adopted in the KTU 2024 syllabus, Application Services broadly encompass the **SaaS (Software as a Service)** and **PaaS (Platform as a Service)** service models, in addition to supporting services such as **Web Services (REST/SOAP)**, **API Gateways**, **Microservices frameworks**, and **Message-Oriented Middleware (MOM)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Strict KTU Formulation):**
> *Application Services are centrally hosted, multi-tenant software components that are provisioned, scaled, metered, and managed by a Cloud Service Provider (CSP) and consumed by clients via thin clients (browsers, mobile apps, or APIs) without the need to install or maintain the underlying application stack locally.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electricity + Appliance" Model

Think of cloud Application Services as the modern **"Electricity Grid + Smart Appliances"** analogy:

| Real World | Cloud Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Electricity from the grid | Compute, Storage, Network from IaaS |
| Ready-made appliances (AC, Geyser) | **SaaS applications** (Gmail, Office 365) |
| Power sockets & wiring infrastructure | **PaaS platforms** (Google App Engine) |
| Utility bill (pay-per-use) | Usage-based billing ($/hour, $/request) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Just as you do not build a power plant to run a fan, in the cloud you do not build a server farm to run an application — you simply *subscribe* to an application service. The provider handles availability, patching, scaling, and security, while you focus purely on **business logic** and **user experience**.

## 1.3 Why Application Services Matter in Modern Engineering

- **Elastic Scaling:** A B.Tech startup can launch a product serving 10 users today and 10 million users tomorrow **without rewriting the application**.
- **CapEx to OpEx shift:** Capital expenditure becomes predictable operational expenditure.
- **Continuous Delivery:** Providers roll out updates transparently — clients always have the latest version.
- **Global Reach:** Edge POPs (Points of Presence) ensure low latency worldwide.

## 1.4 Categories of Application Services (KTU Taxonomy)

```
Application Services
├── 1. SaaS  (Software as a Service)
├── 2. PaaS  (Platform as a Service)
├── 3. Web Services (WSDL/SOAP, REST)
├── 4. API Gateway Services
├── 5. Message-Oriented Middleware (MOM)
└── 6. Microservices & Serverless Backends
```

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU Module 4 syllabus, **SaaS and PaaS receive the highest weightage** in examinations. Memorize their characteristics, advantages, limitations, and key providers (AWS, Azure, GCP).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SaaS — Software as a Service (The "End-User" Layer)

### 2.1.1 Operational Characteristics

1. **Single Instance, Multi-Tenant Architecture** — One application instance serves multiple customers (tenants).
2. **Centralized Update & Patch Management** — Provider pushes updates; users always get the newest version.
3. **Thin-Client Access** — Delivered through **web browsers**, mobile apps, or API calls.
4. **Subscription / Usage-Based Billing** — Monthly, per-user, or per-transaction pricing.
5. **Built-in Elasticity** — Auto-scaling to handle traffic spikes.
6. **High Availability (typically 99.9% to 99.99% SLA)**.

### 2.1.2 Multi-Tenancy Models (High-Yield Topic)

| Model | Description | Isolation Level | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shared Everything** | Single DB & app instance for all tenants | Low | Lowest |
| **Shared App, Separate DB** | App shared; per-tenant DB schema | Medium | Medium |
| **Shared App, Shared Schema** | Single DB, tenant_id column | Low-Medium | Low |
| **Separate App & DB per Tenant** | Full isolation per tenant | High | Highest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 frequently asks: *"Compare SaaS multi-tenancy models."* Always draw a comparison table covering **isolation, cost, scalability, and customization**.

### 2.1.3 Advantages and Limitations of SaaS

- **Pros:** Zero installation; low upfront cost; automatic updates; accessible anywhere; predictable OPEX.
- **Cons:** Limited customization; vendor lock-in; data residency concerns; requires internet; performance depends on network.

## 2.2 PaaS — Platform as a Service (The "Developer" Layer)

PaaS provides a **runtime environment, OS, middleware, development tools, and DBMS** so that developers can build, test, and deploy applications without managing the underlying infrastructure.

### 2.2.1 Core Components of a PaaS Stack

1. **Runtime Frameworks** — Java (Tomcat), Node.js, Python (Django), .NET Core.
2. **Managed Databases** — Amazon RDS, Google Cloud SQL, Azure Cosmos DB.
3. **Middleware Services** — Message queues, caches, search engines.
4. **DevOps Toolchain** — CI/CD pipelines, version control hooks.
5. **Auto-Scaling & Load Balancing** — Built-in elasticity controllers.

### 2.2.2 PaaS Deployment Models

| Model | Use Case | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Public PaaS** | General web/mobile apps | AWS Elastic Beanstalk |
| **Private PaaS** | Enterprise compliance | Red Hat OpenShift on-prem |
| **Hybrid PaaS** | Burst to public during peak | IBM Bluemix |
| **Communications PaaS (CPaaS)** | Real-time messaging/voice | Twilio |
| **Mobile Backend as a Service (MBaaS)** | Mobile app backends | Firebase |

## 2.3 Web Services & API Services

### 2.3.1 SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol)
- **XML-based**, heavy, **WSDL** describes the service.
- Strong **WS-Security**, ACID transactions, formal contracts.
- Used in **enterprise, banking, telecom** integrations.

### 2.3.2 REST (Representational State Transfer)
- **Lightweight, stateless**, uses HTTP verbs (`GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`).
- Data formats: **JSON (preferred)**, XML, YAML.
- Backbone of modern cloud APIs and **microservices**.

### 2.3.3 gRPC & GraphQL (Emerging in KTU 2024)
- **gRPC:** High-performance RPC framework using **Protocol Buffers**.
- **GraphQL:** Query language letting clients request exactly the data they need.

### 2.3.4 REST vs SOAP — The KTU Comparison Table

| Feature | SOAP | REST |
|---|---|---|
| Data Format | XML only | JSON, XML, YAML |
| Transport | HTTP, SMTP, TCP | HTTP/HTTPS only |
| State | Stateful or stateless | Stateless |
| Security | WS-Security, SAML | OAuth 2.0, JWT, TLS |
| Performance | Slower (heavy payload) | Faster (lightweight) |
| Contract | WSDL | OpenAPI/Swagger |
| Use Case | Banking, ERP | Web/Mobile APIs |

## 2.4 Service Level Agreements (SLA) — The Contractual Heart

An **SLA** is a formal contract defining measurable service guarantees. The most critical metric is **availability**.

### 2.4.1 Availability Tiers (Industry Standard)

| Availability % | Downtime/Year | Tier |
|---|---|---|
| 99% | 3.65 days | Two 9s |
| 99.9% | 8.76 hours | Three 9s |
| 99.95% | 4.38 hours | Three-and-half 9s |
| 99.99% | 52.56 minutes | Four 9s |
| 99.999% | 5.26 minutes | Five 9s (Carrier-grade) |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all mathematical formulas and metrics examinable under KTU PEERT524 Module 4 — Application Services.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Service Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | % | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair |
| 2 | Annual Downtime | $D_{yr} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | minutes | Used for SLA calculations |
| 3 | Monthly Downtime (99.9%) | $D_{mo} = \dfrac{8.76}{12}$ | hours | ≈ 43.8 minutes |
| 4 | Cost per User per Month | $C_{upm} = \dfrac{T_{monthly}}{N_{users}}$ | ₹ / $ / user | SaaS pricing model |
| 5 | Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) | $TCO = C_{capex} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} C_{opex,t}$ | currency | Discounted over n years |
| 6 | Tenancy Density | $\rho_{t} = \dfrac{N_{tenants}}{V_{resource}}$ | tenants / unit | Measures multi-tenancy efficiency |
| 7 | Throughput | $T = \dfrac{N_{req}}{\Delta t}$ | requests / sec | Cloud service capacity |
| 8 | Response Time (Average) | $R_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum R_i}{N}$ | ms | Includes network + compute |
| 9 | Utilization | $U = \dfrac{B_{used}}{B_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | Resource efficiency |
| 10 | Scaling Efficiency | $\eta_{scale} = \dfrac{\Delta T / T_{base}}{\Delta R / R_{base}}$ | ratio | Ideal = 1.0 (linear) |
| 11 | Energy Efficiency (PUE) | $PUE = \dfrac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}}$ | unitless | Data-center metric |
| 12 | Service Composition Latency | $L_{comp} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} L_i + \sum_{j=1}^{m} L_{network,j}$ | ms | For composite SaaS workflows |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The **availability formula** ($A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$) appears in nearly every Module 4 question paper. Master it along with the **downtime conversion**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations — SLA Availability & Downtime

### 3.1.1 From Availability Percentage to Annual Downtime

**Step 1: Define Availability in decimal form.**
$$A_{dec} = \dfrac{A_{\%}}{100}$$

**Step 2: Compute unavailability.**
$$U_{dec} = 1 - A_{dec}$$

**Step 3: Convert to minutes per year.**
$$D_{min} = U_{dec} \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$$

**Step 4: Convert minutes to days/hours/minutes as needed.**

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A cloud provider advertises **99.95% availability**. Calculate the maximum permissible downtime per year.

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{dec} &= \dfrac{99.95}{100} = 0.9995 \\
U_{dec} &= 1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005 \\
D_{min} &= 0.0005 \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \\
&= 0.0005 \times 525{,}600 \\
&= 262.8 \text{ minutes} \\
&= 4 \text{ hours } 22 \text{ minutes } 48 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic:** The "0.0005" means 5 parts in 10,000 fail. Multiply by total minutes in a year (525,600) to get allowable downtime.

### 3.1.2 From MTBF/MTTR to Availability

**Problem:** A SaaS application has $MTBF = 800$ hours and $MTTR = 2$ hours. Find availability.

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{800}{800 + 2} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{800}{802} \times 100\% \\
&= 99.7506\% \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Annual Downtime:**
$$\begin{aligned}
D_{yr} &= (1 - 0.997506) \times 525600 \\
&= 0.002494 \times 525600 \\
&\approx 1310.86 \text{ minutes} \approx 21.85 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> In a 14-mark question, KTU expects you to compute **both availability AND downtime** and then comment on whether the SLA is met. Always finish with a 1-2 line engineering interpretation.

### 3.1.3 TCO Derivation (Total Cost of Ownership)

For a SaaS deployment over **n years**:

$$\begin{aligned}
TCO &= C_{capex} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} C_{opex,t} \\
    &= C_{capex} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \left(C_{license,t} + C_{infra,t} + C_{admin,t} + C_{downtime,t}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Discounted TCO (Net Present Value):**
$$TCO_{NPV} = C_{capex} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_{opex,t}}{(1 + r)^{t}}$$

where $r$ is the discount rate (typically 8-12%).

**Worked Example:**
Initial capex = ₹ 5,00,000; annual opex = ₹ 1,20,000; $n = 5$ years; $r = 10\%$.

$$\begin{aligned}
TCO_{NPV} &= 5{,}00{,}000 + \sum_{t=1}^{5} \dfrac{1{,}20{,}000}{(1.10)^{t}} \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 \times \left(\dfrac{1 - (1.10)^{-5}}{0.10}\right) \\
&= 5{,}00{,000 + 1{,}20{,}000 \times 3.7908 \\
&= 5{,}00{,000 + 4{,}54{,}896 \\
&= \text{₹ } 9{,}54{,}896
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — SaaS vs PaaS vs IaaS

| Dimension | SaaS | PaaS | IaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Who manages what?** | Provider manages everything | Provider manages infra + platform; user manages app | Provider manages only infra |
| **Target User** | End-users | Developers | Sysadmins / Architects |
| **Flexibility** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Control** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
