| **Examples** | Gmail, Salesforce, Office 365 | AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku, Google App Engine | AWS EC2, Azure VMs, GCP Compute Engine |
| **Skill Required** | Browser only | App development | OS, networking, security |
| **Time to Deploy** | Minutes | Hours-Days | Days-Weeks |
| **KTU 2024 Weightage** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ (covered in Mod 2/3) |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Choose What?

| Scenario | Recommended Service Model | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| A startup building a quick MVP | **PaaS** | Rapid deployment, no infra management |
| A hospital needing HIPAA-compliant email | **SaaS** (e.g., G Suite with BAA) | Compliance + low overhead |
| A bank running legacy Oracle DB | **IaaS** | Full control for regulatory audits |
| A gaming app with seasonal traffic spikes | **PaaS / Serverless** | Auto-scaling built-in |
| An ML training workload | **IaaS** (with GPU VMs) | Need raw hardware access |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cloud Application Service Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClientLayer["CLIENT LAYER"]
        Browser["Web Browser"]
        MobileApp["Mobile App"]
        DesktopClient["Desktop Client"]
    end

    subgraph AccessLayer["ACCESS & DELIVERY LAYER"]
        APIGateway["API Gateway"]
        LoadBalancer["Load Balancer"]
        CDN["CDN Edge Cache"]
    end

    subgraph AppLayer["APPLICATION SERVICE LAYER"]
        SaaS1["SaaS Module A"]
        SaaS2["SaaS Module B"]
        PaaS1["PaaS Runtime Engine"]
        PaaS2["Microservices Mesh"]
    end

    subgraph DataLayer["DATA & STORAGE LAYER"]
        SQLDB["Managed SQL DB"]
        NoSQLDB["Managed NoSQL DB"]
        ObjectStore["Object Storage S3"]
        Cache["In-Memory Cache"]
    end

    subgraph InfraLayer["INFRASTRUCTURE LAYER"]
        VM1["Virtual Machines"]
        VM2["Container Orchestrator K8s"]
        VM3["Serverless Functions"]
    end

    Browser --> APIGateway
    MobileApp --> APIGateway
    DesktopClient --> APIGateway
    APIGateway --> LoadBalancer
    LoadBalancer --> CDN
    LoadBalancer --> SaaS1
    LoadBalancer --> SaaS2
    LoadBalancer --> PaaS1
    LoadBalancer --> PaaS2
    SaaS1 --> SQLDB
    SaaS2 --> NoSQLDB
    PaaS1 --> ObjectStore
    PaaS2 --> Cache
    SaaS1 --> VM1
    PaaS2 --> VM2
    SaaS1 --> VM3
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram represents the **functional flow of requests** from client to infrastructure in a typical cloud-deployed application service. Each layer abstracts the complexity of the one below it.

## 4.2 Multi-Tenancy Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SharedApp["SHARED APPLICATION INSTANCE"]
        AppCore["Application Core Engine"]
    end

    subgraph TenantDBs["TENANT DATABASES"]
        T1["Tenant 1 DB"]
        T2["Tenant 2 DB"]
        T3["Tenant N DB"]
    end

    subgraph Tenants["TENANT USERS"]
        U1["User Group A"]
        U2["User Group B"]
        U3["User Group C"]
    end

    U1 --> AppCore
    U2 --> AppCore
    U3 --> AppCore
    AppCore --> T1
    AppCore --> T2
    AppCore --> T3
```

## 4.3 SaaS Request Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as End User Browser
    participant LB as Load Balancer
    participant APP as SaaS App Server
    participant AUTH as Auth Service
    participant DB as Tenant Database
    participant MON as Monitoring

    U->>LB: HTTPS Request (Login)
    LB->>APP: Forward to available instance
    APP->>AUTH: Validate JWT Token
    AUTH-->>APP: Token Valid
    APP->>DB: Query tenant_id = X
    DB-->>APP: Return tenant data
    APP-->>LB: JSON Response
    LB-->>U: Rendered HTML / JSON
    APP->>MON: Emit metrics (latency, errors)
```

## 4.4 Deployment Model Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Need Application Service?"])
    Start --> Q1{"Need to control OS / Runtime?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> IaaS["Choose IaaS"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Want to focus only on code?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> PaaS["Choose PaaS"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Need ready-made software?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> SaaS["Choose SaaS"]
    Q3 -- No --> Serverless["Choose Serverless FaaS"]
    IaaS --> End(["Provisioned"])
    PaaS --> End
    SaaS --> End
    Serverless --> End
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Note:** KTU 2024 ESE Part A contains 5 questions of 3 marks each. Two representative questions are given below.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between SaaS and PaaS with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | SaaS | PaaS |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Software as a Service | Platform as a Service |
| **Target User** | End-users | Application developers |
| **What is provided** | Ready-to-use software applications | Runtime + development platform to build apps |
| **Examples** | Gmail, Microsoft Office 365, Salesforce | Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku |
| **User Responsibility** | Use the software | Develop, test, deploy own apps |
| **Control Level** | Minimal (configuration only) | Moderate (code + app config) |

SaaS delivers **complete applications** over the Internet, while PaaS delivers a **platform** to develop and deploy those applications. (1 mark)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Service Level Agreement (SLA). List any four SLA parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** An SLA is a **formal negotiated contract** between a Cloud Service Provider (CSP) and the customer that defines the **measurable level of service**, responsibilities, and penalties for non-compliance.

**Four SLA Parameters (0.375 each):**
1. **Availability / Uptime** (e.g., 99.9%)
2. **Response Time / Latency**
3. **Throughput** (transactions per second)
4. **Recovery Time Objective (RTO)** and **Recovery Point Objective (RPO)**
5. **Support Response Time** (e.g., 24x7 email/phone)
6. **Data Durability** (e.g., 99.999999999% — 11 nines for S3)
7. **Security & Compliance** certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries a 14-mark question with **internal choice** (either/or). Two alternative questions are provided.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Choice-1]**

**A. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various **multi-tenancy models** used in SaaS application design. Compare their advantages and limitations with neat diagrams.

**A. (b) [7 Marks]** A cloud service reports $MTBF = 1500$ hours and $MTTR = 1$ hour. Calculate its **availability percentage** and the **permissible annual downtime** in hours. Comment on whether this meets a typical 99.9% SLA.

---

#### **Solution A(a) — Multi-Tenancy Models [7 Marks]**

Multi-tenancy is the architectural principle where a **single application instance serves multiple customer organizations (tenants)**, with logical data isolation. The four models are:

**1. Shared Application, Shared Database, Shared Schema (3 Marks for the comparison)**
- A single database and schema serves all tenants; tenants are differentiated by a `tenant_id` column.
- **Pros:** Lowest cost, highest density, easiest to scale.
- **Cons:** Security risk if query layer is buggy; difficult to customize per tenant.

**2. Shared Application, Separate Database**
- A single application instance connects to **per-tenant databases**.
- **Pros:** Better data isolation; easier backup/restore per tenant; supports tenant-specific schemas.
- **Cons:** Higher cost; more complex to manage.

**3. Shared Application, Shared Database, Separate Schema**
- One database instance but **a different schema per tenant**.
- **Pros:** Balance between isolation and cost; tenant-specific customizations possible.
- **Cons:** Schema migration becomes complex with many tenants.

**4. Separate Application, Separate Database (Full Isolation)**
- A **dedicated app instance and database per tenant**.
- **Pros:** Maximum security, customization, and compliance.
- **Cons:** Highest cost; lowest density; complex operations.

**Diagrammatic representation (refer Section 4.2 above for Mermaid).**

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The choice depends on **tenant size, regulatory needs, and budget**. Most public SaaS providers use the **shared-everything** model with robust query-layer security.

---

#### **Solution A(b) — Availability & Downtime Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $MTBF = 1500$ hours, $MTTR = 1$ hour

**Step 1: Apply the availability formula (3 marks).**
$$A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$$
$$A = \dfrac{1500}{1500 + 1} \times 100\%$$
$$A = \dfrac{1500}{1501} \times 100\% = 99.9334\%$$

**Step 2: Calculate unavailability (2 marks).**
$$U = 1 - 0.999334 = 0.000666$$
$$D_{yr} = 0.000666 \times 365 \times 24 = 5.834 \text{ hours per year}$$

**Step 3: Convert to minutes for clarity (1 mark).**
$$D_{yr} = 5.834 \times 60 = 350.04 \text{ minutes} \approx 5\text{h } 50\text{min}$$

**Step 4: Compare with 99.9% SLA (1 mark).**
- SLA allows: $D_{SLA} = 0.001 \times 8760 = 8.76$ hours/year.
- Actual downtime: **5.834 hours < 8.76 hours**.
- **Conclusion:** The service **MEETS** the 99.9% SLA requirement. ✅

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice-2]**

**B. (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a PaaS cloud environment**. List its key characteristics and any four popular PaaS platforms.

**B. (b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **REST architectural style** for cloud web services. Compare it with **SOAP** along at least six parameters.

---

#### **Solution B(a) — PaaS Architecture [7 Marks]**

**Definition (1 mark):** PaaS provides a **cloud-hosted platform** containing runtime environments, middleware, databases, and DevOps tools, enabling developers to **build, test, deploy, and scale** applications without managing underlying infrastructure.

**Architecture Block Diagram (3 marks):** *(Refer to the functional flow in Section 4.1.)*

Layers from bottom to top:
1. **Infrastructure Layer** — Physical servers, storage, network, virtualization.
2. **Platform Layer** — OS, runtime (Java/Node/Python), middleware, services.
3. **Application Layer** — User-deployed applications.
4. **User/Developer Access Layer** — Web console, CLI, APIs, IDE plugins.

**Key Characteristics (2 marks):**
- Automatic resource provisioning and scaling.
- Built-in load balancing and fault tolerance.
- Multi-language and multi-framework support.
- Integrated DevOps toolchain (CI/CD, monitoring, logging).
- Pay-per-use billing based on compute, storage, and request metrics.

**Four Popular PaaS Platforms (1 mark):**
1. **Google App Engine (GAE)** — Java, Python, Node.js, Go, PHP.
2. **AWS Elastic Beanstalk** — Java, .NET, Python, Ruby, Node.js.
3. **Microsoft Azure App Service** — .NET, Java, PHP, Node.js, Python.
4. **Heroku** — Polyglot platform with add-on marketplace.

---

#### **Solution B(b) — REST vs SOAP [7 Marks]**

**REST (Representational State Transfer) — 3.5 marks:**

REST is an **architectural style** defined by Roy Fielding (2000) for designing networked applications. It treats data as **resources** identified by URIs and manipulated using **standard HTTP verbs**.

**Key Principles:**
- **Stateless:** Each request contains all info; no client state stored on server.
- **Client-Server:** Separation of UI and data storage.
- **Cacheable:** Responses can declare cacheability.
- **Uniform Interface:** Standardized resource URIs.
- **Layered System:** Client unaware of intermediary proxies.
- **Code on Demand (optional):** Server can send executable code.

**SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol) — 0.5 mark for context:**

SOAP is a **protocol** for exchanging structured XML messages, using **WSDL** for service description and typically HTTP/SMTP/TCP as transport.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | REST | SOAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Architectural style | Protocol |
| **Data Format** | JSON (preferred), XML, YAML | XML only |
| **Transport** | HTTP/HTTPS | HTTP, SMTP, TCP, JMS |
| **Statefulness** | Stateless | Can be stateful |
| **Security** | HTTPS, OAuth 2.0, JWT | WS-Security, SAML, XML Encryption |
| **Performance** | Lightweight, fast | Heavier due to XML parsing |
| **Contract** | OpenAPI / Swagger | WSDL |
| **Error Handling** | HTTP status codes | SOAP Fault element |
| **Use Case** | Web/Mobile APIs, microservices | Enterprise, banking, telecom |
| **Tooling** | curl, Postman, browser | SOAP UI, code generators |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing SaaS and PaaS** — Many students write "PaaS provides software" or "SaaS provides a platform." Always state: *SaaS = ready-made software; PaaS = platform to build software.*
> 2. **Availability formula errors** — Forgetting to convert decimal to percentage in the final step. Always show $A \times 100\%$.
> 3. **Time unit mismatches in downtime** — Mixing hours and minutes. Stick to one unit throughout the calculation.
> 4. **Skipping the engineering interpretation** — KTU awards 1 mark for concluding whether the SLA is met. Do not stop at numerical computation.
> 5. **Omitting diagrams in multi-tenancy questions** — A 7-mark question on multi-tenancy without a diagram loses 2-3 marks easily. Use box diagrams (shared vs separate).
> 6. **In REST vs SOAP answers** — Do not just list differences; briefly define BOTH first, then compare.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Application Services**

- ✅ **Application Services = SaaS + PaaS + Web Services + APIs + Middleware** (broad definition per KTU 2024 syllabus).
- ✅ **SaaS = end-user-facing**, **PaaS = developer-facing**, **IaaS = sysadmin-facing** — remember this hierarchy.
- ✅ **Multi-tenancy** has 4 models: shared-everything, shared-app-separate-DB, shared-app-shared-schema, fully-isolated.
- ✅ **Availability Formula:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ — memorize and practice.
- ✅ **99.9% SLA = 8.76 hours downtime/year; 99.99% = 52.56 minutes/year.**
- ✅ **REST is an architectural style (HTTP + JSON), SOAP is a protocol (XML + WSDL)** — key distinction.
- ✅ **PaaS components:** runtime + middleware + DB + DevOps toolchain + auto-scaling.
- ✅ **TCO formula:** $TCO_{NPV} = C_{capex} + \sum C_{opex,t} / (1+r)^t$.
- ✅ **Tenancy density** and **utilization** are key SaaS efficiency metrics.
- ✅ **Cloud web service flavors:** SOAP, REST, gRPC, GraphQL — be ready to compare.
- ✅ **SLA parameters to memorize:** availability, response time, throughput, RTO, RPO, data durability, support hours.
- ✅ **Providers to remember:** AWS (Elastic Beanstalk), Azure (App Service), GCP (App Engine), Heroku, Red Hat OpenShift, Firebase (MBaaS), Twilio (CPaaS).
- ✅ **NIST Service Models:** SaaS, PaaS, IaaS — adopted in KTU 2024 curriculum.
- ✅ **Composite service latency** = sum of component latencies + network latencies.
- ✅ **Use case mapping:** Start-up MVP → PaaS; Enterprise email → SaaS; ML training → IaaS; Real-time chat → Serverless.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as **Definition (2 marks) + Diagram (3 marks) + Explanation (5 marks) + Examples/Comparison (3 marks) + Conclusion (1 mark)**. This pattern is what KTU examiners reward with full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compute Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Compute Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Compute Services — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Compute Services** in cloud computing refer to the on-demand, scalable, and metered provisioning of **processing power, memory, storage, and networking resources** delivered as a service over the internet. These services abstract the underlying physical hardware and allow users to deploy and run applications without managing the physical servers.

According to the **NIST SP 800-145** definition, compute services form the **Processing & Computation layer** of the cloud stack, providing the execution environment for workloads ranging from simple web applications to high-performance computing (HPC) and artificial intelligence/machine learning (AI/ML) tasks.

The major compute service models in KTU 2024 syllabus scope are:
- **Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)** — Virtual Machines
- **Container as a Service (CaaS)** — Container Orchestration
- **Function as a Service (FaaS) / Serverless** — Event-driven execution
- **Platform as a Service (PaaS)** — Managed application runtimes

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electricity Grid" Model

Think of compute services like the **electricity grid** in your home:
- You don't build your own power plant. You just plug in a device.
- You pay only for the units (kWh) you consume.
- When you need more power (AC + Geyser + Oven), the grid automatically scales up.
- When you unplug everything, consumption drops to zero.

Similarly, **Compute Services** let you "plug in" your workload and consume CPU/RAM on-demand:
- Launch a VM in **seconds** (not weeks for procurement)
- Scale from **1 instance to 1,000 instances** automatically
- Pay per **second** (or millisecond for serverless)

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Compute Services

| Service Model | Abstraction Level | User Manages | Provider Manages | Example |
|---------------|-------------------|--------------|-----------------|---------|
| **IaaS (VMs)** | Hardware (Virtual) | OS, Middleware, Runtime, App, Data | Hardware, Virtualization, Networking | Amazon EC2, Azure VM, GCP Compute Engine |
| **CaaS (Containers)** | OS Image + Runtime | App, Data, Container config | OS, Runtime, Orchestration | Amazon ECS/EKS, Azure AKS |
| **PaaS** | Application Platform | App, Data | Everything else | AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service |
| **FaaS (Serverless)** | Function | Function code + trigger | Entire stack | AWS Lambda, Azure Functions |

## 1.4 Key Characteristics (KTU Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Pillars of Cloud Compute Services (Per KTU 2024 Module 4):**
> 1. **Elasticity** — Resources expand/contract with workload
> 2. **Pay-per-use** — Metered billing (per second/minute/invocation)
> 3. **Multi-tenancy** — Shared physical hardware, isolated virtual resources
> 4. **Self-service provisioning** — No human intervention from provider
> 5. **Service Level Agreement (SLA)** — Guaranteed uptime (e.g., **99.99%** for EC2)

## 1.5 Industry Reference Implementations

- **Amazon Web Services (AWS):** EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, Fargate
- **Microsoft Azure:** Virtual Machines, Azure Functions, AKS, Container Instances
- **Google Cloud Platform (GCP):** Compute Engine, Cloud Run, GKE, Cloud Functions
- **Open Source:** OpenStack (IaaS), Kubernetes (CaaS), Knative (Serverless)

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU examinations, AWS terminology is typically the **default reference** unless otherwise specified, as it dominates the global cloud market with approximately **31% market share (as of 2024)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Virtual Machines (EC2 — Elastic Compute Cloud)

### 2.1.1 Concept & Architecture

An **EC2 instance** is a virtualized computing environment that runs on a hypervisor (typically **Xen** in older AWS or **Nitro System** in current AWS). Each instance is launched from an **Amazon Machine Image (AMI)**, which contains the OS, application server, and applications.

**Operational Flow:**
1. User selects an **AMI** (template)
2. Chooses an **Instance Type** (CPU, RAM, storage, network)
3. Configures **Security Group** (firewall rules)
4. Launches → Hypervisor provisions VM on bare-metal host
5. User gets **Public/Private IP** + **DNS name**

### 2.1.2 Instance Type Families (AWS Reference)

| Family | Full Form | Use Case | Example Type |
|--------|-----------|----------|--------------|
| **T** | Burstable | Low-traffic web, dev/test | t3.micro, t3.medium |
| **M** | General Purpose | Balanced workloads | m5.large, m6i.xlarge |
| **C** | Compute Optimized | CPU-intensive tasks | c5.4xlarge, c7i.2xlarge |
| **R** | Memory Optimized | In-memory databases | r5.2xlarge |
| **X** | Memory Optimized (Extreme) | SAP HANA, large caches | x1.32xlarge |
| **P / G** | Accelerated (GPU) | ML training, graphics | p4d.24xlarge, g5.xlarge |
| **I** | Storage Optimized | NoSQL, data warehousing | i3.16xlarge |

### 2.1.3 Pricing Models (Critical for KTU 14-Mark Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4 EC2 Pricing Models:**
> 1. **On-Demand** — Pay per second/minute, no commitment. Most expensive. Best for short-term, unpredictable workloads.
> 2. **Reserved Instances (RI)** — 1-year or 3-year commitment. Up to **72% discount**. Best for steady-state workloads.
> 3. **Savings Plans** — Flexible commitment (1-3 years) across regions. Up to **66% discount**.
> 4. **Spot Instances** — Bid for spare capacity. Up to **90% discount**. Can be terminated with **2-minute notice**. Best for fault-tolerant, batch processing.

### 2.1.4 High Availability Concepts

- **Regions** — Geographic areas (e.g., `us-east-1`, `ap-south-1`)
- **Availability Zones (AZs)** — Isolated data centers within a region (typically **3 AZs per region**)
- **Edge Locations** — CDN endpoints for low-latency content delivery

> **Why AZs matter:** AZs are physically separate but connected with **low-latency links (< 1 ms)**. Deploying across multiple AZs provides **99.99% SLA**.

## 2.2 Containers & Orchestration

### 2.2.1 Container Fundamentals

A **container** is a lightweight, standalone, executable package that includes:
- Application code
- Runtime (e.g., Node.js, Python)
- System libraries
- Dependencies
- Configuration

Unlike VMs, containers **share the host OS kernel** and run as isolated processes.

| Feature | Virtual Machine | Container |
|---------|----------------|-----------|
| **Startup Time** | Minutes | Seconds/Milliseconds |
| **Size** | Gigabytes | Megabytes |
| **OS Overhead** | Full guest OS | Shares host kernel |
| **Isolation** | Strong (hypervisor) | Process-level |
| **Density per Host** | 10-20 VMs | 100-1000 containers |
| **Boot Time** | 30-60 seconds | < 1 second |

### 2.2.2 Container Services in Cloud

- **Amazon ECS** — AWS's proprietary container orchestrator (uses Docker)
- **Amazon EKS** — Managed **Kubernetes** service
- **AWS Fargate** — Serverless containers (no EC2 management)
- **Azure AKS / GCP GKE** — Managed Kubernetes on other clouds

> **Kubernetes** (often abbreviated **K8s**) is the de-facto standard for container orchestration, handling **auto-scaling, self-healing, service discovery, and rolling updates**.

## 2.3 Serverless Computing (FaaS)

### 2.3.1 Core Concept

In serverless, the cloud provider:
- **Dynamically allocates** compute resources on demand
- **Charges only for actual execution time** (e.g., per 100 ms)
- **Automatically scales** from zero to thousands of concurrent invocations

The user only provides:
- Function code
- Trigger event (HTTP request, queue message, file upload, schedule)

> [!NOTE]
> **"Serverless" does NOT mean "no servers"** — it means **"no server management for the user"**. Servers still exist; the provider handles all infrastructure.

### 2.3.2 AWS Lambda (Reference Implementation)

**Key Limits (must remember for KTU):**
- **Maximum execution time:** 15 minutes
- **Memory allocation:** 128 MB to **10,240 MB**
- **Deployment package size:** 50 MB (zipped), 250 MB (unzipped)
- **Concurrent executions:** 1,000 (default, can be increased)

**Billing Formula:**

$$ \text{Cost} = (\text{Number of invocations}) \times (\text{Price per invocation}) + (\text{GB-seconds}) \times (\text{Price per GB-second}) $$

**Where:**
- 1 GB-second = 1 GB of memory provisioned × 1 second of execution

### 2.3.3 Cold Start Problem

When a Lambda function is invoked for the first time (or after idle), the platform must:
1. Download the code
2. Start the runtime
3. Initialize the function

This latency (typically **100 ms to several seconds**) is called the **Cold Start**. Subsequent invocations are **"warm"** with much lower latency.

## 2.4 Auto Scaling & Load Balancing

### 2.4.1 Auto Scaling Types

| Type | Mechanism | Use Case |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **Reactive (Dynamic) Scaling** | Scale based on metrics (CPU > 70%) | Handling traffic spikes |
| **Predictive Scaling** | ML-based forecast of load | Anticipated events |
| **Scheduled Scaling** | Time-based (e.g., scale up at 9 AM) | Known business hours |

**AWS Auto Scaling Group (ASG) Components:**
- **Launch Template/Configuration** — What to launch
- **Min/Max/Desired capacity** — Boundaries
- **Scaling Policies** — When and how to scale
- **Health Checks** — Replace unhealthy instances

### 2.4.2 Load Balancers (ELB)

| Type | Layer | Protocol | Use Case |
|------|-------|----------|----------|
| **Application Load Balancer (ALB)** | Layer 7 | HTTP/HTTPS | Web apps, microservices, path-based routing |
| **Network Load Balancer (NLB)** | Layer 4 | TCP/UDP | High-performance, low-latency |
| **Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB)** | Layer 3 | IP | Third-party virtual appliances |
| **Classic Load Balancer (CLB)** | Layer 4/7 | TCP/HTTP | Legacy (being deprecated) |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Compute Services:**

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|-------------------|----------|---------------|
| **Lambda Cost** | $C = N \times P_{inv} + M \times T \times P_{GB\text{-}s}$ | N = invocations, M = GB memory, T = seconds |
| **GB-Seconds** | $GB\text{-}s = \frac{Memory_{MB}}{1024} \times Duration_{s}$ | Always normalize to GB |
| **SLA Uptime (3 nines)** | $\text{Downtime} = 365.25 \times 24 \times 60 \times (1 - 0.999) = 52.6 \text{ min/year}$ | 99.9% = 8.77 hours/year |
| **SLA Uptime (4 nines)** | $\text{Downtime} = 365.25 \times 24 \times 60 \times (1 - 0.9999) = 5.26 \text{ min/year}$ | 99.99% = 52.6 min/year |
| **Spot Savings** | $Savings\% = \frac{P_{OD} - P_{Spot}}{P_{OD}} \times 100$ | Up to 90% |
| **RI Discount** | $Discount\% = \frac{P_{OD} - P_{RI}}{P_{OD}} \times 100$ | Up to 72% for 3-year |
| **Concurrent Users Capacity** | $Capacity = \frac{Instances \times vCPU_{per\_inst}}{vCPU_{per\_user}}$ | Theoretical limit |
| **Cost per Hour (EC2)** | $C_{hr} = C_{compute} + C_{storage} + C_{data}$ | Sum all components |

> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN RULE:** All absolute values and pipe symbols have been replaced with `\vert` for table compatibility. For example, write $\vert x \vert$ instead of `|x|`.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Startups:** Use Spot + Lambda to keep infrastructure cost below **\$50/month**
- **Enterprises:** Use Reserved Instances for steady-state + Spot for batch processing
- **ML Workloads:** Use P/G instances for training, Lambda for inference
- **E-commerce:** Auto-scaling handles 10x traffic on Black Friday
- **IoT:** Serverless functions process millions of sensor events
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: EC2 Cost Calculation (14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A startup runs **3 m5.large** instances **24/7 for 30 days**. Calculate the monthly cost using:
> - On-Demand pricing: **\$0.096/hour**
> - 1-year Reserved (No Upfront): **\$0.062/hour**
> - Spot pricing: **\$0.038/hour**
>
> Which option is cheapest? What is the savings percentage?

### Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate total hours per month**

$$ H = 3 \text{ instances} \times 24 \text{ hr/day} \times 30 \text{ days} = 2160 \text{ instance-hours} $$

**Step 2: On-Demand Cost**

$$ C_{OD} = 2160 \times 0.096 = \$207.36 $$

**Step 3: Reserved Instance Cost (1-year)**

$$ C_{RI} = 2160 \times 0.062 = \$133.92 $$

**Step 4: Spot Instance Cost**

$$ C_{Spot} = 2160 \times 0.038 = \$82.08 $$

**Step 5: Savings Comparison**

$$ Savings_{RI} = \frac{207.36 - 133.92}{207.36} \times 100 = 35.42\% $$

$$ Savings_{Spot} = \frac{207.36 - 82.08}{207.36} \times 100 = 60.42\% $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Spot is cheapest at **\$82.08/month (60.42% savings)**, but carries the risk of 2-minute termination notice. RI offers **35.42% savings** with guaranteed capacity. The choice depends on workload fault-tolerance.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: AWS Lambda GB-Seconds Calculation

> **Problem:** A Lambda function is configured with **512 MB** memory. It runs **100,000 times** in a month, with each execution averaging **200 ms**.
> - Invocation price: **\$0.20 per 1 million**
> - Compute price: **\$0.0000166667 per GB-second**
>
> Calculate the total monthly bill.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Convert memory to GB**

$$ M_{GB} = \frac{512}{1024} = 0.5 \text{ GB} $$

**Step 2: Calculate execution time in seconds**

$$ T_s = \frac{200}{1000} = 0.2 \text{ seconds per invocation} $$

**Step 3: Calculate GB-seconds per invocation**

$$ GB\text{-}s_{single} = 0.5 \times 0.2 = 0.1 \text{ GB-seconds} $$

**Step 4: Total GB-seconds in month**

$$ GB\text{-}s_{total} = 100{,}000 \times 0.1 = 10{,}000 \text{ GB-seconds} $$

**Step 5: Invocation cost**

$$ C_{inv} = \frac{100{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 0.20 = \$0.02 $$

**Step 6: Compute cost**

$$ C_{compute} = 10{,}000 \times 0.0000166667 = \$0.1667 $$

**Step 7: Total monthly cost**

$$ C_{total} = 0.02 + 0.1667 = \$0.1867 $$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: SLA Downtime Calculation

> **Problem:** A compute service offers **99.95% SLA**. How much downtime is allowed per year?

### Solution:

**Step 1: Identify downtime percentage**

$$ Availability = 99.95\% \implies Downtime\% = 100 - 99.95 = 0.05\% $$

**Step 2: Convert to decimal**

$$ Downtime_{decimal} = \frac{0.05}{100} = 0.0005 $$

**Step 3: Calculate minutes in a year**

$$ Min_{year} = 365.25 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}960 \text{ minutes} $$

**Step 4: Calculate allowed downtime**

$$ D_{allowed} = 525{,}960 \times 0.0005 = 262.98 \text{ minutes} \approx 4.38 \text{ hours/year} $$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: VM vs Container vs Serverless

| Parameter | EC2 (IaaS) | ECS/EKS (CaaS) | Lambda (FaaS) |
|-----------|------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Startup Time** | 30-60 s | 1-5 s | 100 ms (warm), 1-3 s (cold) |
| **Max Runtime** | Unlimited | Unlimited | 15 minutes |
| **Control Level** | Full OS access | Container-level | Function-only |
| **Scaling Speed** | Minutes (with ASG) | Seconds (K8s HPA) | Milliseconds |
| **Pricing Granularity** | Per hour/second | Per second (Fargate) | Per 100 ms |
| **Idle Cost** | Yes (always running) | Yes (always running) | **Zero** |
| **Best For** | Stateful, long-running | Microservices, hybrid | Event-driven, sporadic |
| **Example Use** | Database server | API gateway backend | Image resize on upload |

## 3.5 Decision Tree: When to Use What?

```
┌─ Is the workload event-driven and short-lived (< 15 min)?
│    ├─ YES → Use AWS Lambda (Serverless)
│    └─ NO  → Continue ↓
│
├─ Does the app need OS-level customization (specific kernel modules, drivers)?
│    ├─ YES → Use EC2 (IaaS)
│    └─ NO  → Continue ↓
│
├─ Is the app containerized and needs orchestration?
│    ├─ YES → Use ECS / EKS (CaaS)
│    └─ NO  → Use Elastic Beanstalk (PaaS)
│
└─ Is it a steady-state long-running workload?
     ├─ YES → EC2 with Reserved Instances
     └─ NO with fault-tolerance → EC2 Spot Instances
```

## 3.6 Common KTU 14-Mark Problem Patterns

1. **Cost Comparison:** "Compare On-Demand vs Reserved vs Spot for X instances over Y months"
2. **Lambda Billing:** "Calculate monthly cost given memory, invocations, and duration"
3. **SLA Calculation:** "Compute downtime for a given SLA percentage"
4. **Scaling Strategy:** "Design an auto-scaling policy for given CPU thresholds"
5. **Architecture Design:** "Choose appropriate compute service for given scenario"
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Serverless Computing. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Serverless Computing** is a cloud execution model in which the cloud provider dynamically allocates compute resources on-demand, and the user is charged only for the actual execution time of their code. The user writes and uploads **functions** (event-driven code snippets) and the provider handles all server provisioning, scaling, patching, and maintenance. The term "serverless" is misleading — servers still exist, but the user has **zero server management responsibility**. Examples include **AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, and Google Cloud Functions**. Key characteristics are:
> 1. **Event-driven execution** (triggered by HTTP, queue, schedule)
> 2. **Automatic scaling from zero to thousands**
> 3. **Pay-per-invocation billing** (typically per 100 ms)
> 4. **Built-in high availability and fault tolerance**

### Question 2: List any three EC2 instance type families with their use cases. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1. **T-family (Burstable):** T3, T4g — For low-traffic websites, development environments, and small databases with baseline CPU performance and burst capability.
> 2. **C-family (Compute Optimized):** C5, C6i, C7i — For CPU-intensive workloads such as batch processing, scientific modeling, gaming servers, and high-performance web servers.
> 3. **R-family (Memory Optimized):** R5, R6i — For memory-intensive applications such as in-memory databases (Redis, Memcached), real-time big data analytics, and high-performance caching.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **Question A: Compute Services — Pricing and SLA Analysis** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
> Explain the **four EC2 pricing models** with their discount percentages and ideal use cases. A company wants to run **5 m5.large instances 24/7 for 12 months** with predictable steady-state load. Recommend the most cost-effective model with justification.

**Model Answer (a):**

The four EC2 pricing models are:

**1. On-Demand Instances (No Discount):**
- Pay per second (Linux) or per hour (Windows)
- No long-term commitment
- Highest cost (baseline pricing)
- **Use case:** Short-term, unpredictable, spiky workloads; development and testing

**2. Reserved Instances (Up to 72% discount):**
- 1-year or 3-year commitment
- Three payment options: All Upfront, Partial Upfront, No Upfront
- Capacity reserved in specific AZ
- **Use case:** Steady-state workloads with predictable usage (databases, always-on apps)

**3. Savings Plans (Up to 66% discount):**
- 1-year or 3-year commitment based on dollar amount
- Flexibility across instance families, regions, and operating systems
- **Use case:** Workloads that may shift between instance types but with predictable spend

**4. Spot Instances (Up to 90% discount):**
- Bid for spare AWS capacity
- Can be terminated with **2-minute notice** by AWS
- **Use case:** Fault-tolerant, flexible workloads (batch jobs, CI/CD, ML training)

**Cost Calculation for 5 m5.large × 24/7 × 12 months:**

$$ H_{total} = 5 \times 24 \times 365 = 43{,}800 \text{ instance-hours} $$

| Model | Rate/hr | Annual Cost |
|-------|---------|-------------|
| On-Demand | \$0.096 | \$4,204.80 |
| 1-yr Reserved (No Upfront) | \$0.062 | \$2,715.60 |
| 3-yr Reserved (No Upfront) | \$0.043 | \$1,883.40 |
| Spot (variable) | \$0.038 | \$1,664.40 |

> **Recommendation:** For a **predictable steady-state load**, **3-year Reserved Instances (No Upfront)** are most cost-effective, providing **55.21% savings** over On-Demand while guaranteeing capacity. Spot is cheaper but inappropriate due to termination risk. 1-year RI offers 35.42% savings if 3-year commitment is undesirable.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
> A cloud service advertises **99.95% SLA**. Calculate the **maximum allowed annual downtime** in minutes. Explain the **multi-AZ deployment strategy** with a diagram and justify why it is critical for compute services.

**Model Answer (b):**

**SLA Calculation:**

$$ Downtime_{year} = 525{,}960 \times (1 - 0.9995) = 525{,}960 \times 0.0005 = 262.98 \text{ minutes} $$

This equates to approximately **4 hours 22 minutes 58 seconds per year**.

**Multi-AZ Deployment Strategy:**

A **Region** contains multiple **Availability Zones (AZs)**. Each AZ is one or more physically separate, independent data centers with redundant power, networking, and connectivity. AZs within a region are connected by **low-latency private fiber links** (typically < 1 ms RTT).

**Deployment Steps:**
1. Deploy EC2 instances across **at least 2 AZs** (e.g., AZ-1a and AZ-1b)
2. Place an **Application Load Balancer (ALB)** in front of all instances
3. Configure **Route 53** for DNS-based failover
4. Use **RDS Multi-AZ** for synchronous database replication
5. Enable **Auto Scaling** across AZs for elasticity

**Why It Is Critical:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Single-AZ deployment risks:**
> - Power outage in the data center → complete service downtime
> - Natural disaster (flood, earthquake) → service unavailable
> - Network failure → users disconnected
> - Hardware failure → no failover

> **Multi-AZ benefits:**
> - **High availability** — If one AZ fails, traffic reroutes to the other
> - **99.99% SLA** — Multi-AZ deployments qualify for higher SLA than single-AZ
> - **Zero-downtime maintenance** — AWS can patch underlying infrastructure
> - **Disaster recovery** — Built-in protection against localized failures

**Architecture Diagram (refer to Section 4.5):** The diagram shows users connecting via Route 53 to an ALB, which distributes traffic to EC2 instances spread across multiple AZs, with a primary-standby RDS configuration and S3 cross-region replication for backups.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

### **Question B: Container and Serverless Architectures** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
> Compare **Virtual Machines (EC2) and Containers (ECS/EKS)** across **six key parameters**. Explain the role of **Kubernetes** in container orchestration with at least **four core functions**.

**Model Answer (a):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | EC2 (Virtual Machines) | ECS/EKS (Containers) |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------|
| **Isolation** | Hardware-level (hypervisor) | Process-level (kernel namespaces + cgroups) |
| **Startup Time** | 30-60 seconds | 1-5 seconds |
| **Image Size** | 10-20 GB (full OS) | 50-500 MB (app + libs) |
| **Density per Host** | 10-20 VMs | 100-1000 containers |
| **OS Overhead** | Full guest OS per instance | Shared host kernel |
| **Boot/Update Speed** | Minutes | Seconds |

**Kubernetes (K8s) — Core Functions:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kubernetes is the de-facto industry standard for container orchestration.**

1. **Auto-Scaling (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler):** Automatically adjusts the number of running container instances (pods) based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics.

2. **Self-Healing:** Restarts failed containers, replaces unresponsive pods, and reschedules workloads to healthy nodes automatically.

3. **Service Discovery & Load Balancing:** Provides built-in DNS and load balancing so containers can find and communicate with each other without hard-coded IPs.

4. **Rolling Updates & Rollbacks:** Performs zero-downtime deployments by gradually replacing old container versions with new ones, with automatic rollback on failure.

5. **Storage Orchestration:** Automatically mounts persistent storage (EBS, EFS, S3) to containers as needed.

6. **Secret & Config Management:** Manages sensitive data (API keys, passwords) and configuration files without exposing them in container images.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
> Explain **AWS Lambda's billing model** with a worked example. A function is configured with **1024 MB memory**, invoked **500,000 times/month**, with each execution lasting **3 seconds**. Pricing: **\$0.20 per 1M invocations, \$0.0000166667 per GB-second.** Calculate the monthly bill.

**Model Answer (b):**

**AWS Lambda Billing Model:**

AWS Lambda charges for two components:

$$ C_{total} = C_{invocations} + C_{compute} $$

**1. Invocation Charges:** Per number of function executions
- \$0.20 per 1 million requests
- Free tier: 1 million requests/month

**2. Compute Charges:** Per GB-second (memory × duration)
- \$0.0000166667 per GB-second
- Free tier: 400,000 GB-seconds/month

**Where:**
$$ GB\text{-}s = \frac{Memory_{MB}}{1024} \times Duration_{seconds} $$

**Worked Example Calculation:**

**Step 1: Convert memory to GB**

$$ M_{GB} = \frac{1024}{1024} = 1.0 \text{ GB} $$

**Step 2: Compute GB-seconds per invocation**

$$ GB\text{-}s_{single} = 1.0 \times 3 = 3.0 \text{ GB-seconds} $$

**Step 3: Total GB-seconds per month**

$$ GB\text{-}s_{total} = 500{,}000 \times 3.0 = 1{,}500{,}000 \text{ GB-seconds} $$

**Step 4: Invocation cost**

$$ C_{inv} = \frac{500{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 0.20 = \$0.10 $$

**Step 5: Compute cost**

$$ C_{compute} = 1{,}500{,}000 \times 0.0000166667 = \$25.00 $$

**Step 6: Total monthly bill**

$$ C_{total} = 0.10 + 25.00 = \$25.10 $$

> **Note:** This exceeds the free tier (400,000 GB-seconds), so the full amount is charged. The key insight is that **memory is the most significant cost driver** in Lambda — doubling memory doubles the GB-second cost.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Compute Services Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include ALL cost components:** EC2 cost is not just per-hour rate — students forget to add **EBS storage, data transfer, and Elastic IP** costs. Always state: *"Assuming standard EBS and minimal data transfer..."*
>
> 2. **Confusing Spot and Reserved pricing:** Spot offers up to **90% discount** but NO capacity guarantee. Reserved offers up to **72% discount** with capacity guarantee. Examiners mark this distinction carefully.
>
> 3. **Mixing up GB-Seconds formula:** Many students compute **MB × seconds** instead of **GB × seconds**. Always divide memory by 1024 to convert MB to GB.
>
> 4. **SLA calculation errors:** Use **365.25 days** (accounting for leap years), not 365. For **99.99% SLA**, downtime is **52.6 minutes/year** — memorize this.
>
> 5. **Lambda limits:** Forgetting the **15-minute maximum execution time** and **10 GB memory cap** is a common error.
>
> 6. **Container vs VM comparison:** Always mention **kernel sharing** for containers vs **separate kernels** for VMs. Examiners specifically test this.
>
> 7. **Auto Scaling terminology:** "Horizontal scaling" (add more instances) is different from "Vertical scaling" (add more CPU/RAM to existing instance). EC2 Auto Scaling is **horizontal only**.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Compute Services:**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] **EC2 = IaaS** (Virtual Machines, full OS control)
- [ ] **ECS/EKS = CaaS** (Containers, shared OS kernel)
- [ ] **Lambda = FaaS** (Serverless, event-driven, max 15 min runtime)
- [ ] **Beanstalk = PaaS** (Managed platform for app deployment)
- [ ] **Fargate = Serverless Containers** (no EC2 management)

### Key Numerical Limits
- [ ] Lambda **max execution:** 15 minutes
- [ ] Lambda **max memory:** 10,240 MB (10 GB)
- [ ] Lambda **deployment size:** 50 MB zipped, 250 MB unzipped
- [ ] Spot **termination notice:** 2 minutes
- [ ] RI **max discount:** 72% (3-year, all upfront)
- [ ] Spot **max discount:** Up to 90%
- [ ] Typical **3-AZ** region architecture

### Critical Formulas
- [ ] **Lambda cost:** $C = N \times P_{inv} + GB \times s \times P_{GB\text{-}s}$
- [ ] **GB-Seconds:** $GB\text{-}s = \frac{MB}{1024} \times seconds$
- [ ] **SLA Downtime:** $D = 525{,}960 \times (1 - SLA)$
- [ ] **Discount %:** $\frac{P_{base} - P_{new}}{P_{base}} \times 100$

### Service Selection Mnemonic — "LECA"
- [ ] **L**ong-running + OS control → **E**C2
- [ ] **E**vent-driven + short tasks → **L**ambda
- [ ] **C**ontainerized + microservices → **E**CS/**E**KS
- [ ] **A**pp deployment without infra mgmt → Elastic **B**eanstalk (PaaS)

### Pricing Models Mnemonic — "ORSS"
- [ ] **O**n-Demand (full price, no commitment)
- [ ] **R**eserved (1-3 yr, up to 72% off)
- [ ] **S**avings Plans (flexible commitment)
- [ ] **S**pot (up to 90% off, 2-min termination)

### Quick Definitions to Memorize
- [ ] **Hypervisor:** Software that creates and runs VMs (e.g., AWS Nitro)
- [ ] **AMI:** Amazon Machine Image — template for EC2 instances
- [ ] **AZ:** Availability Zone — isolated data center within a region
- [ ] **ASG:** Auto Scaling Group — manages fleet of EC2 instances
- [ ] **ALB:** Application Load Balancer — Layer 7 HTTP/HTTPS
- [ ] **NLB:** Network Load Balancer — Layer 4 TCP/UDP
- [ ] **Cold Start:** First-time Lambda invocation latency
- [ ] **EFA:** Elastic Fabric Adapter — for HPC and ML
- [ ] **Placement Group:** Cluster of EC2 instances for low-latency communication
- [ ] **Dedicated Host:** Physical server dedicated to single customer
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Database Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Database Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Database Services in Cloud Computing** refer to the on-demand provisioning, management, scaling, and maintenance of database systems by a cloud service provider, delivered over the internet under a pay-as-you-go or subscription-based model. This paradigm is formally known as **Database-as-a-Service (DBaaS)** and forms a sub-class of the broader Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) layer in the cloud computing reference model.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, **Database Services** encompass three architectural categories:

1. **Relational Cloud Databases (SQL/RDBMS)**: Managed instances of traditional relational databases such as MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, and Microsoft SQL Server, offered via services like **Amazon RDS**, **Google Cloud SQL**, and **Azure SQL Database**.
2. **NoSQL Cloud Databases**: Distributed, schema-less data stores designed for horizontal scalability, including key-value (Amazon DynamoDB), document (MongoDB Atlas), column-family (Google Bigtable, Amazon Redshift), and graph databases (Amazon Neptune).
3. **Cloud Data Warehouses**: Analytical, column-oriented systems optimized for OLAP workloads, such as **Amazon Redshift**, **Google BigQuery**, and **Snowflake**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 4):** Database services are evaluated primarily through their consistency guarantees (ACID vs BASE), distribution models (sharding and replication), the CAP theorem implications, and the architectural trade-offs between SQL and NoSQL paradigms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library on Rent" Model

Imagine a **traditional library** (an on-premise database):

- You must buy or rent the entire building (server hardware).
- You hire librarians (DBAs) to manage the books (data).
- If more people come, you must build a new wing (vertical scaling).
- If a water pipe bursts, the whole library closes (single point of failure).

Now imagine a **digital library subscription** (a cloud database service):

- You pay only for the books (storage) and the reading rooms (compute) you actually use.
- The librarian service (provider) maintains cataloging, indexing, backups, and security.
- During exam season, the system automatically adds more reading rooms (auto-scaling).
- If one server room floods in one city, another in a different city takes over (multi-region replication).

> [!TIP]
> **Student Takeaway:** Cloud Database Services shift the database from being a *capital expenditure (CapEx)* product you own to an *operational expenditure (OpEx)* service you consume — identical in philosophy to how electricity is consumed from a power grid instead of owning a generator.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Parameters

The following metrics are standardized benchmarks for evaluating any cloud database service:

| Metric | Standard Value/Unit | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | Milliseconds (ms), often 1–10 ms p99 | Time to fetch a single record |
| **Throughput** | Queries per second (QPS) / IOPS | Volume of operations handled |
| **Replication Factor** | 3 (industry default) | Number of copies maintained |
| **Consistency Window** | Milliseconds to seconds | Time taken for replicas to converge |
| **Sharding Key Cardinality** | $10^6$ to $10^9$ partitions | Granularity of horizontal partitioning |
| **Storage Elasticity** | Petabytes (PB) — $10^{15}$ bytes | Maximum scalable data volume |
| **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** | Seconds to minutes | Maximum tolerable downtime |
| **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** | Seconds (with synchronous replication) | Maximum tolerable data loss |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — RTO vs RPO:** **RTO** is the maximum acceptable duration of service unavailability, while **RPO** is the maximum acceptable period in which data may be lost due to a major incident. Both are SLA-bound in cloud database contracts.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Classification of Cloud Database Services

Cloud database services are operationally classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

### Axis 1: Data Model
- **Relational (SQL)**: Schema-first, tabular, ACID-compliant.
- **Document**: JSON/BSON documents, flexible schema (e.g., MongoDB).
- **Key-Value**: Hash-table-style, ultra-low latency (e.g., DynamoDB).
- **Column-Family**: Sparse columns grouped into families (e.g., Bigtable).
- **Graph**: Nodes and edges for relationship queries (e.g., Neptune).

### Axis 2: Deployment Topology
- **Single-Region, Multi-AZ**: High availability within one geographic region.
- **Multi-Region, Active-Passive**: Disaster recovery across continents.
- **Multi-Region, Active-Active**: Global low-latency reads and writes.

### Axis 3: Consistency Model
- **Strong Consistency**: Linearizability, all reads see the latest write.
- **Eventual Consistency**: Replicas converge asynchronously.
- **Causal Consistency**: Reads respect cause-effect ordering.
- **Read-Your-Writes**: A client always sees its own latest write.

### Axis 4: Workload Optimization
- **OLTP (Online Transaction Processing)**: High-frequency short transactions (RDS, DynamoDB).
- **OLAP (Online Analytical Processing)**: Complex analytical queries (Redshift, BigQuery).
- **HTAP (Hybrid Transactional/Analytical Processing)**: Unified transactional and analytical engine (Google Spanner, TiDB).

## 2.2 The CAP Theorem — Cornerstone of Distributed Database Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAP Theorem (Brewer's Conjecture, proved by Gilbert and Lynch, 2002):** In any distributed data store, it is impossible to simultaneously guarantee all three of the following properties — **C**onsistency, **A**vailability, and **P**artition tolerance. At most two of the three can be satisfied under a network partition.

| Property | Formal Meaning | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Consistency (C)** | Every read returns the most recent committed write. | Requires inter-node coordination, increasing latency. |
| **Availability (A)** | Every request receives a non-error response. | Cannot block for cross-node synchronization. |
| **Partition Tolerance (P)** | System continues operating despite arbitrary network message loss. | Mandatory in real distributed systems. |

Since network partitions are an inevitability in any real cloud deployment, the practical CAP trade-off reduces to **CP vs AP**:
- **CP Systems**: Sacrifice availability during partitions (e.g., Google Spanner, Apache HBase, MongoDB with majority write concern).
- **AP Systems**: Sacrifice strong consistency for availability (e.g., Amazon DynamoDB, Cassandra, CouchDB).

> [!NOTE]
> **Extended CAP — PACELC Theorem:** When there is no partition (P), the system trades off between **A**vailability and **L**atency vs **C**onsistency (ELC). This adds nuance beyond the binary CAP model.

## 2.3 ACID vs BASE — The Consistency Spectrum

### ACID Properties (Traditional RDBMS)
- **Atomicity**: All operations in a transaction succeed or none do.
- **Consistency**: Database transitions from one valid state to another.
- **Isolation**: Concurrent transactions do not interfere.
- **Durability**: Committed data survives system failures.

### BASE Properties (NoSQL, distributed)
- **Basic Availability**: The database appears to work most of the time.
- **Soft State**: The state of the system may change over time, even without input.
- **Eventual Consistency**: The system will become consistent given enough time.

## 2.4 Sharding and Replication Mathematics

### Replication Storage Cost
If a single data record of size $S$ bytes is replicated $R$ times, the total physical storage consumed is:

$$T = S \times R$$

For a database with $N$ total records and average record size $\bar{s}$, the total storage is:

$$T_{total} = N \times \bar{s} \times R$$

### Sharding Distribution
If data is divided into $K$ shards and a hash function $h(k)$ distributes keys, the load on shard $i$ is:

$$L_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\big[h(k_j) = i\big]$$

For a uniform hash function, the expected load per shard is:

$$\mathbb{E}[L_i] = \frac{N}{K}$$

### Quorum-Based Consistency Rule
For a replicated database with $N$ replicas, read quorum $R$, and write quorum $W$, **strong consistency** is maintained if and only if:

$$R + W > N$$

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Example:** DynamoDB by default uses $N = 3$, $R = 2$, $W = 2$. Then $R + W = 4 > 3 = N$, so strong consistency is guaranteed. If $W = 1$ (eventual consistency mode), then $R + W = 3$ is not strictly greater than $N = 3$, so only **eventual consistency** is provided.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total replicated storage | $T = N \cdot \bar{s} \cdot R$ | Capacity planning |
| 2 | Uniform shard load | $\mathbb{E}[L_i] = N / K$ | Predicting skew |
| 3 | Strong consistency quorum | $R + W > N$ | Read/write tuning |
| 4 | Availability quorum | $W \leq N$ and $R \leq N$ | Ensuring fault tolerance |
| 5 | Mean time to data loss (MTTDL) | $\text{MTTDL} = \frac{\text{MTBF}^R}{R \cdot \text{MTTR}^{R-1}}$ | Reliability engineering |
| 6 | Partitioning probability | $P_{hot} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot t / K}$ | Hotspot detection |
| 7 | Cost per GB-month | $C = (S_{GB} \times P_{storage}) + (R \times P_{compute})$ | TCO analysis |
| 8 | Replication lag (steady state) | $\tau_{lag} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ | For M/M/1 replication queue |
| 9 | Throughput (Little's Law) | $\text{QPS} = \frac{\text{Concurrency}}{\text{Latency}}$ | Capacity planning |
| 10 | Storage elasticity bound | $S_{max} = S_0 \times 2^n$ (for $n$ scaling events) | Auto-scaling ceiling |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cloud database services underpin mission-critical applications across industries:

- **E-Commerce (Amazon DynamoDB)**: Powers the shopping cart of amazon.com, handling > 10 trillion requests per year with single-digit millisecond latency.
- **Streaming (Netflix Cassandra)**: Tracks viewing state for > 260 million subscribers across multiple AWS regions.
- **Finance (Google Spanner)**: Provides globally distributed ACID transactions for Google Ads billing.
- **Analytics (Snowflake)**: Decouples storage and compute, enabling near-infinite analytical queries over petabytes of data.
- **IoT (Amazon Timestream)**: Time-series optimized store ingesting millions of sensor events per second.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The choice of database service is **not** about which is "best" but which is **best-fit** for a given consistency, latency, scale, and cost profile. KTU exams reward students who articulate the *trade-off reasoning*, not just feature lists.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Storage Cost of a Replicated Cloud Database

**Problem Statement:** A cloud database holds $N = 5 \times 10^9$ user records, each of average size $\bar{s} = 2$ KB. The service is configured with a replication factor of $R = 3$ across multiple availability zones. The cloud provider charges $P_{storage} = \$0.023$ per GB-month. Compute:
1. The total logical storage.
2. The total physical (replicated) storage.
3. The monthly storage cost.

### Step 1 — Compute Logical Storage
Logical storage is the uncompressed, unreplicated size of all unique data.

$$S_{logical} = N \times \bar{s}$$

$$S_{logical} = 5 \times 10^9 \times 2 \text{ KB} = 1 \times 10^{10} \text{ KB}$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Gigabytes
1 GB = $10^6$ KB (using SI decimal convention) or $2^{20}$ KB (binary). For cloud billing, the **decimal SI** convention is used.

$$S_{logical} = \frac{1 \times 10^{10}}{10^6} = 10{,}000 \text{ GB} = 10 \text{ TB}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Replication Factor
$$S_{physical} = S_{logical} \times R$$

$$S_{physical} = 10{,}000 \times 3 = 30{,}000 \text{ GB} = 30 \text{ TB}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Monthly Cost
$$C_{monthly} = S_{physical} \times P_{storage}$$

$$C_{monthly} = 30{,}000 \text{ GB} \times \$0.023 / \text{GB-month} = \$690 / \text{month}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Although the user's "logical" data is only 10 TB, the cloud provider physically stores 30 TB. This **3x overhead** is the cost of high availability and is *invisible* to the user in a DBaaS model — the provider absorbs the storage cost and the user pays per logical unit billed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Quorum-Based Consistency Validation

**Problem Statement:** A distributed key-value store has $N = 5$ replicas. The system uses $W = 3$ for writes and $R = 3$ for reads. Determine:
1. Is strong consistency guaranteed?
2. What happens if $W = 2$ and $R = 2$?
3. What is the minimum $W$ for $R = 3$ that preserves strong consistency?

### Step 1 — Apply the Quorum Rule
Strong consistency requires:

$$R + W > N$$

For $R = 3$, $W = 3$, $N = 5$:

$$3 + 3 = 6 > 5$$

**Result:** Strong consistency is guaranteed because at least one node is common between any read and write set.

### Step 2 — Test the Weaker Configuration
For $R = 2$, $W = 2$, $N = 5$:

$$2 + 2 = 4 \not> 5$$

**Result:** Strong consistency is **not** guaranteed. A read could return stale data because the read and write quorums may be disjoint.

### Step 3 — Determine the Minimum $W$
For $R = 3$ and $N = 5$, the minimum $W$ satisfying $R + W > N$ is:

$$W_{min} = N - R + 1 = 5 - 3 + 1 = 3$$

$$\therefore W_{min} = 3$$

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Real-world tie-in:** Amazon DynamoDB uses $N = 3$ (default), with **strong consistency reads** setting $R = 2$ and **eventual consistency reads** setting $R = 1$. The write quorum $W = 2$ is fixed. Validation: $2 + 2 = 4 > 3$ ✓ (strong) and $1 + 2 = 3 \not> 3$ (eventual).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Shard Load Skew Analysis

**Problem Statement:** A database is sharded into $K = 16$ shards using a hash function $h(k)$. The total number of records is $N = 8 \times 10^7$. The hash function is approximately uniform. Compute:
1. The expected load per shard.
2. The probability that a specific shard receives more than $1.5 \times$ the expected load (hotspot detection).
3. The standard deviation of the load.

### Step 1 — Expected Load per Shard
Using a uniform hash distribution:

$$\mathbb{E}[L_i] = \frac{N}{K} = \frac{8 \times 10^7}{16} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ records per shard}$$

### Step 2 — Hotspot Probability
Each record lands in shard $i$ with probability $p = 1/K = 1/16$. The number of records in shard $i$ follows a Binomial distribution:

$$L_i \sim \text{Binomial}(N, p)$$

For large $N$, we use the normal approximation:

$$L_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$$

where $\mu = Np = 5 \times 10^6$ and $\sigma^2 = Np(1-p)$.

$$\sigma^2 = 8 \times 10^7 \times \frac{1}{16} \times \frac{15}{16} = 4{,}687{,}500$$

$$\sigma \approx 2164.97 \approx 2165 \text{ records}$$

The threshold for a hotspot is $1.5 \mu = 7.5 \times 10^6$. The z-score is:

$$z = \frac{7.5 \times 10^6 - 5 \times 10^6}{2165} \approx 1154.7$$

Since $z \gg 3$, the probability is approximately:

$$P(L_i > 1.5\mu) \approx 0$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation
> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A well-designed hash-based sharding scheme virtually eliminates hotspots. The 99.99% confidence interval for shard load is $[\mu - 4\sigma, \mu + 4\sigma] = [4.99 \times 10^6, 5.01 \times 10^6]$ — a deviation of less than 0.1%. This is why cloud databases prefer **consistent hashing** over modulo-based sharding.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SQL vs NoSQL

| Evaluation Criterion | SQL (RDBMS) | NoSQL | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Model** | Tabular, predefined schema | Document / KV / Column / Graph | Mention CAP trade-off |
| **Schema** | Rigid, schema-on-write | Flexible, schema-on-read | Emphasize evolution |
| **Scaling** | Vertical (scale-up) | Horizontal (scale-out) | Add nodes vs upgrade |
| **Consistency** | Strong (ACID) | Tunable (BASE) | Always mention both |
| **Transactions** | Multi-row, multi-table | Single-document or limited | MongoDB 4.0+ supports |
| **Query Language** | Standardized SQL | Proprietary (MongoQL, GQL) | Vendor lock-in issue |
| **Joins** | Native and efficient | Limited or absent | Performance impact |
| **Use Cases** | Banking, ERP, CRM | IoT, gaming, social, analytics | Match by data shape |
| **Examples (Cloud)** | RDS, Cloud SQL, Azure SQL | DynamoDB, Cosmos DB, Bigtable | Always cite 2+ examples |
| **Cost Model** | Per instance-hour | Per request or per RCU/WCU | OpEx vs CapEx |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Cloud Database Service Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a **DBaaS platform** mediates between application clients and the underlying distributed storage substrate.

```mermaid
graph TD
    clientA[Client Application 1] --> loadbal[Cloud Load Balancer]
    clientB[Client Application 2] --> loadbal
    clientC[Client Application 3] --> loadbal
    loadbal --> proxy1[Query Proxy Node 1]
    loadbal --> proxy2[Query Proxy Node 2]
    proxy1 --> router[Query Router and Planner]
    proxy2 --> router
    router --> shard1[(Shard 1 Primary)]
    router --> shard2[(Shard 2 Primary)]
    router --> shard3[(Shard 3 Primary)]
    router --> shard4[(Shard 4 Primary)]
    shard1 --> replica1a[(Replica 1A)]
    shard1 --> replica1b[(Replica 1B)]
    shard2 --> replica2a[(Replica 2A)]
    shard2 --> replica2b[(Replica 2B)]
    shard3 --> replica3a[(Replica 3A)]
    shard3 --> replica3b[(Replica 3B)]
    shard4 --> replica4a[(Replica 4A)]
    shard4 --> replica4b[(Replica 4B)]
    router --> meta[Metadata Service]
    meta --> router
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Clients never connect to shards directly. They send queries to a **stateless proxy**, which consults a **metadata service** for shard topology and then routes to the correct primary. Each primary asynchronously replicates to two standby replicas for fault tolerance.

## 4.2 CAP Theorem Triangle — Practical Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    cp[CP Systems] --> spanner[Google Spanner]
    cp --> hbase[Apache HBase]
    cp --> mongo[Consistent MongoDB]
    ap[AP Systems] --> cassandra[Apache Cassandra]
    ap --> dynamo[Amazon DynamoDB]
    ap --> couchdb[Apache CouchDB]
    ca[CA Systems Theoretical] --> rdbms[Traditional RDBMS]
    rdbms -.->|not partition tolerant| note1[Not viable in multi-region cloud]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** Pure **CA** systems (traditional single-node RDBMS) are theoretically consistent and available but cannot tolerate partitions. In modern multi-region cloud deployments, **CA is impractical**, so the real choice is **CP vs AP**.

## 4.3 Database Service Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    start([New Application Requirement]) --> q1{Data has rigid schema?}
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{Need ACID transactions?}
    q1 -->|No| q3{Data shape is document-like?}
    q2 -->|Yes| q4{Workload is OLTP or OLAP?}
    q2 -->|No| q5{Eventually consistent OK?}
    q4 -->|OLTP| ans1[Choose Amazon RDS or Cloud SQL]
    q4 -->|OLAP| ans2[Choose Redshift or BigQuery]
    q3 -->|Yes| ans3[Choose MongoDB Atlas or DocumentDB]
    q3 -->|No| q6{Need ultra low latency?}
    q6 -->|Yes| ans4[Choose DynamoDB or Cosmos DB]
    q6 -->|No| q7{Time series or graph data?}
    q7 -->|Time Series| ans5[Choose Timestream or InfluxDB]
    q7 -->|Graph| ans6[Choose Amazon Neptune or Neo4j]
    q5 -->|Yes| ans3
    q5 -->|No| ans1
```

## 4.4 Data Flow in a Cloud OLTP Service (Amazon RDS-Style)

```mermaid
graph TD
    app[Application Tier] --> conn[Connection Pool]
    conn --> primary[(Primary DB Instance)]
    primary --> sync[Synchronous Replication]
    sync --> standby1[(Standby 1 Same AZ)]
    sync --> standby2[(Standby 2 Different AZ)]
    primary --> async[Asynchronous Replication]
    async --> readreplica[(Read Replica)]
    primary --> backup[Automated Daily Backup to S3]
    backup --> snapshot[Point in Time Snapshot]
    standby1 --> failover[Automatic Failover]
    standby2 --> failover
    failover --> primary
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Note:** Synchronous replication to a standby in another Availability Zone (AZ) ensures **RPO ≈ 0** during failover. Asynchronous read replicas serve read-heavy workloads and can lag by **seconds to minutes**, acceptable for analytics but not for financial ledgers.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Database-as-a-Service (DBaaS). List any four key characteristics of cloud database services.**

**Model Answer:**

Database-as-a-Service (DBaaS) is a cloud computing service model in which a cloud provider hosts, manages, and maintains a database engine on behalf of the customer, exposing it as a network-accessible service. The customer does not install, patch, back up, or scale the database infrastructure manually.

**Four key characteristics:**

1. **On-Demand Self-Service**: Databases can be provisioned automatically without human interaction with the provider.
2. **Elastic Scalability**: Both storage and compute can scale up or down based on workload, often transparently.
3. **Pay-Per-Use Billing**: The customer is billed only for the storage consumed, queries executed, or instance-hours used.
4. **High Availability and Disaster Recovery**: Multi-AZ and multi-region replication are built-in, with automatic failover and point-in-time recovery.
5. **Managed Maintenance**: The provider handles patching, backups, monitoring, and security hardening.

*(Any four well-explained points fetch full marks.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between ACID and BASE properties in the context of cloud databases.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | ACID | BASE |
|---|---|---|
| **Acronym Meaning** | Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability | Basic Availability, Soft state, Eventual consistency |
| **Consistency Model** | Strong, immediate consistency | Eventual consistency |
| **Transaction Support** | Full multi-operation transactions | Limited or single-record atomicity |
| **Performance** | Slower due to locking and coordination | Higher throughput and lower latency |
| **Suitable For** | Banking, ERP, financial systems | Social media, IoT, analytics, gaming |
| **Example Systems** | PostgreSQL, MySQL, Google Spanner | Cassandra, DynamoDB, Cosmos DB |

ACID is preferred when **data correctness is paramount**, while BASE is preferred when **availability and scale** dominate the requirements.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the CAP theorem in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss how distributed cloud databases make trade-offs among the three properties.**

**Model Answer:**

The **CAP theorem**, formally conjectured by Eric Brewer in 2000 and proved by Gilbert and Lynch in 2002, states that in any distributed data store, only two of the three guarantees — **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, and **Partition Tolerance (P)** — can be simultaneously achieved in the presence of a network partition.

**Definition of Properties:**

- **Consistency**: Every read request from any client receives the result of the most recent write or an error. This is the same as linearizability in distributed systems.
- **Availability**: Every non-failing node returns a response for every request (read or write) within a bounded time, even if some nodes are down.
- **Partition Tolerance**: The system continues to operate despite an arbitrary number of messages being dropped or delayed between nodes due to network failure.

**CAP Triangle Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    cp[CP] --> sp[Spanner HBase]
    ap[AP] --> dp[Dynamo Cassandra]
    ca[CA] --> rdb[RDBMS Theoretical]
```

**Trade-offs in Cloud Databases:**

- **CP Systems (e.g., Google Spanner, MongoDB with majority writes)**: When a partition occurs, the system rejects writes on the minority side, sacrificing availability to preserve consistency. Once the partition heals, the system reconciles and resumes full operation.
- **AP Systems (e.g., Amazon DynamoDB, Apache Cassandra)**: When a partition occurs, all nodes continue to accept reads and writes, but different partitions may diverge. Consistency is restored via **anti-entropy protocols** (e.g., Merkle tree sync) or **read repair** after the partition heals.
- **CA Systems**: Theoretically consistent and available, but cannot tolerate partitions. They are limited to single-node or tightly coupled deployments and are **not viable** in real multi-region cloud environments.

**Why Partition Tolerance is Non-Negotiable:**
In any real cloud deployment spanning multiple data centers, network failures are inevitable. A system that cannot tolerate partitions is unsuitable for production cloud workloads. Hence the real-world choice is always between CP and AP.

**Conclusion:** The CAP theorem is a foundational design principle for cloud database architects. It does not provide a universal "winner" but a framework for making informed consistency vs availability decisions aligned with business requirements.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare and contrast SQL and NoSQL databases in detail. Discuss at least two real-world cloud services for each category.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | SQL (Relational) | NoSQL (Non-Relational) |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Model** | Tables with rows and columns | Documents, key-value pairs, columns, or graphs |
| **Schema** | Rigid, predefined | Dynamic, flexible |
| **Scaling** | Primarily vertical | Primarily horizontal |
| **Consistency** | Strong (ACID) | Tunable (BASE / eventual) |
| **Query Language** | Standardized SQL | Vendor-specific APIs |
| **Joins** | Native and efficient | Limited or absent |
| **Transactions** | Full multi-row ACID | Single-document or limited multi-row |
| **Performance** | Optimized for complex joins | Optimized for simple high-throughput queries |

**Real-World Cloud SQL Services:**
1. **Amazon RDS**: Managed relational database supporting MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, MariaDB. Provides automated patching, Multi-AZ failover, and read replicas.
2. **Google Cloud SQL**: Fully managed PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQL Server with automatic backups, replication, and encryption at rest.

**Real-World Cloud NoSQL Services:**
1. **Amazon DynamoDB**: Serverless key-value and document database with single-digit millisecond latency at any scale, used by Amazon.com, Lyft, and Snapchat.
2. **Google Bigtable**: Wide-column NoSQL store optimized for analytical and operational workloads, used by Google Search, Maps, and Analytics.
3. **MongoDB Atlas**: Managed document database with native JSON support, full-text search, and global clusters.

**Use-Case Driven Selection:**
- Choose **SQL** for: financial systems, ERP, inventory management, scenarios with strong relational integrity.
- Choose **NoSQL** for: IoT telemetry, social feeds, gaming leaderboards, real-time analytics, scenarios with massive horizontal scale.

**Conclusion:** The SQL vs NoSQL decision is not a question of superiority but of **fit-for-purpose**. Modern cloud architectures increasingly adopt **polyglot persistence** — using multiple database types within a single application to leverage the strengths of each.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the different types of NoSQL databases with examples. Discuss their data models and suitable use cases.**

**Model Answer:**

NoSQL databases are categorized into four primary types, each optimized for a specific data shape and access pattern.

**1. Key-Value Databases:**
- **Data Model**: A hash map of unique keys mapped to opaque values (binary blobs, JSON, etc.).
- **Examples**: Amazon DynamoDB, Redis, Aerospike.
- **Use Cases**: Session storage, shopping carts, leaderboards, caching, feature flags. Operations are typically O(1) lookups by key.
- **Strengths**: Highest throughput and lowest latency (sub-millisecond possible).
- **Weaknesses**: No secondary indexes, limited querying by value.

**2. Document Databases:**
- **Data Model**: Collections of JSON or BSON documents, each with a flexible internal schema.
- **Examples**: MongoDB Atlas, Amazon DocumentDB, Couchbase.
- **Use Cases**: Content management, catalogs, user profiles, event logging.
- **Strengths**: Schema flexibility, rich query language, native JSON alignment with modern web apps.
- **Weaknesses**: Joins are application-side; large documents can hurt performance.

**3. Column-Family (Wide-Column) Databases:**
- **Data Model**: Sparse, multi-dimensional sorted map; rows have dynamic column families.
- **Examples**: Google Bigtable, Apache Cassandra, HBase, Amazon Redshift (OLAP variant).
- **Use Cases**: Time-series data, sensor telemetry, write-heavy analytical workloads, recommendation engines.
- **Strengths**: Massive write throughput, efficient compression, excellent for time-window queries.
- **Weaknesses**: Complex data modeling, limited transaction support.

**4. Graph Databases:**
- **Data Model**: Nodes (entities), edges (relationships), and properties (attributes).
- **Examples**: Amazon Neptune, Neo4j, JanusGraph, Azure Cosmos DB (Gremlin API).
- **Use Cases**: Social networks, fraud detection, knowledge graphs, network topology, recommendation systems.
- **Strengths**: Optimized for traversing deeply connected data, constant-time edge lookups.
- **Weaknesses**: Not suitable for tabular or hierarchical bulk data.

**Summary Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    nosql[NoSQL Databases] --> kv[Key Value]
    nosql --> doc[Document]
    nosql --> col[Column Family]
    nosql --> gr[Graph]
    kv --> ex1[DynamoDB Redis]
    doc --> ex2[MongoDB Atlas DocumentDB]
    col --> ex3[Bigtable Cassandra]
    gr --> ex4[Neptune Neo4j]
```

**Conclusion:** Choosing the right NoSQL type is dictated by the **shape of the data** and the **access patterns** of the application. A well-designed polyglot system may employ multiple NoSQL types side-by-side.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the architecture and key features of Amazon DynamoDB. Explain how it provides high availability and scalability.**

**Model Answer:**

**Amazon DynamoDB** is a fully managed, serverless, key-value and document NoSQL database service provided by AWS, designed for single-digit millisecond latency at any scale.

**Core Architecture:**

1. **Partitioning**: DynamoDB automatically partitions data across multiple physical partitions based on the **partition key** (hash key) and optional **sort key** (range key). Each partition handles a slice of the key space.
2. **Replication**: Every partition is replicated **three times** (default) across multiple Availability Zones within a region using a **leaderless replication** protocol. The replica set is dynamic and can shift with traffic.
3. **Storage Layer**: Uses **Log-Structured Merge (LSM) trees** for high write throughput. Writes are first appended to an in-memory buffer and periodically flushed to immutable SSTable files on SSD.
4. **Request Routing**: A **request router** maps the partition key to the correct partition set using a consistent hash ring. Routing is decentralized and automatic.

**Key Features:**

- **Serverless**: No servers to manage. Capacity is provisioned via **Read Capacity Units (RCU)** and **Write Capacity Units (WCU)**, or via **on-demand mode** for unpredictable workloads.
- **Auto Scaling**: DynamoDB Auto Scaling dynamically adjusts provisioned capacity in response to actual traffic patterns.
- **Global Tables**: Multi-region, multi-active replication with sub-second replication latency. Write to any region, read from any region.
- **DynamoDB Streams**: A time-ordered sequence of item-level modifications, useful for event-driven architectures, replication, and analytics.
- **Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR)**: Restore to any second within the last 35 days.
- **ACID Transactions**: Since 2018, DynamoDB supports cross-item, cross-table ACID transactions.

**How DynamoDB Achieves High Availability:**

- **Multi-AZ Replication**: Three replicas across AZs ensure survival of one entire AZ failure.
- **Sloppy Quorums**: If a coordinator cannot reach the primary replica set, it writes to a healthy "sloppy" quorum to maintain availability, with anti-entropy repairing inconsistencies later.
- **Hinted Handoff**: Temporary storage on a healthy node when a target replica is down, replayed once the target recovers.
- **Merkle Tree Sync**: Periodic background verification to detect and repair replica divergence.

**How DynamoDB Achieves Scalability:**

- **Elastic Partitioning**: The service automatically splits and merges partitions as data volume or throughput changes.
- **Stateless API Endpoints**: Clients can connect to any endpoint; routing is dynamic.
- **No Capacity Planning Required** (on-demand mode): DynamoDB adapts instantly to workload spikes.

**Conclusion:** DynamoDB is a benchmark implementation of an AP system in the CAP model, trading off strict consistency for unmatched availability and scale. Its serverless model exemplifies the **opex-driven** philosophy of cloud-native database services.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in KTU Database Services Questions:**
>
> 1. **CAP Theorem Confusion**: Students often state "CAP lets you choose any two" without explaining that **partition tolerance is mandatory** in cloud environments, reducing the real choice to **CP vs AP**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **No Real-World Examples**: Listing SQL vs NoSQL features without citing specific cloud services (DynamoDB, Bigtable, Spanner) is considered incomplete. Always mention **at least two real services** for full marks.
> 3. **Confusing Replication with Backup**: Replication provides **availability**; backups provide **durability**. They are not interchangeable. Examiners deduct marks when these are conflated.
