# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define Azure Virtual Machine. List any four VM families available in Azure with their primary use case.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Azure Virtual Machine (VM)** is an on-demand, scalable computing resource provided by Microsoft Azure as part of its Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) offering. It emulates a physical server with virtualized CPU, memory, storage, and networking resources, running a guest operating system on the Azure Hyper-V hypervisor.

| Family | Primary Use Case |
|--------|-----------------|
| **B-Series (Burstable)** | Dev/Test environments, low-traffic web servers, small databases |
| **D-Series (General Purpose)** | Balanced workloads — web servers, application servers, enterprise apps |
| **E-Series (Memory Optimized)** | In-memory databases (SAP HANA, Redis), large caching, big-data analytics |
| **F-Series (Compute Optimized)** | Batch processing, gaming servers, CPU-intensive scientific workloads |
| **N-Series (GPU)** | AI/ML training, 3D rendering, video transcoding using NVIDIA GPUs |

*(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between Availability Set and Availability Zone in Azure.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Availability Set | Availability Zone |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Definition** | Logical grouping of VMs within a **single data center** that protects against hardware failures (rack-level) | Physically separate data centers within an **Azure region**, each with independent power, cooling, and networking |
| **Fault Domain (FD)** | VMs are spread across up to **3 FDs** | N/A — zones themselves are independent |
| **Update Domain (UD)** | VMs are spread across up to **20 UDs** for safe patching | N/A |
| **SLA Uptime** | **99.95%** | **99.99%** |
| **Latency between VMs** | Low (within same DC) | Slightly higher (cross-zone, sub-millisecond) |
| **Cost** | **Free** | VMs in different zones are charged standard rates (no extra zone fee, but egress may apply) |
| **Use Case** | Within-DC redundancy, cost-effective HA | Mission-critical workloads, regional DR |

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the various components of an Azure Virtual Machine in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the Azure Fabric Controller and Hyper-V hypervisor in VM provisioning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the composite SLA for the following application architecture and determine the monthly allowable downtime in minutes. Two Azure VMs are deployed in **parallel** across two Availability Zones, each with an individual SLA of 99.99%. The application also uses an Azure SQL Database (Single Server) with a 99.99% SLA in the data path. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Components of an Azure VM:**

1. **Compute (vCPU and Memory):** The VM is allocated virtual CPU cores (1 to 128) and RAM (1 GiB to 4 TiB) from the physical host. The size (SKU) defines this — e.g., Standard_D4s_v5 = 4 vCPUs, 16 GiB RAM.

2. **Storage (Managed Disks):** The VM uses three disk types:
   - **OS Disk:** Contains the bootable operating system (typically Premium SSD, 127 GiB minimum for Gen 2).
   - **Data Disks:** Persistent volumes for application data (up to 64 TB per disk, up to 64 disks per VM).
   - **Temporary Disk:** Non-persistent, high-performance local storage (D: on Windows, /dev/sdb on Linux).

3. **Networking (vNIC, VNet, Subnet, Public IP, NSG):** Each VM has at least one vNIC attached to a subnet within a VNet. NSGs enforce traffic rules.

4. **OS Image:** The template (Windows Server, Ubuntu, RHEL, custom image, or shared image gallery) used to bootstrap the VM.

5. **Extensions:** Post-deployment scripts and agents (Custom Script Extension, DSC, Diagnostics, Log Analytics).

6. **Boot Diagnostics & Monitoring:** Console output screenshots and performance metrics.

**Role of Azure Fabric Controller:**
The Fabric Controller (FC) is the **brain of Azure Compute**. It is a distributed application responsible for:
- Allocating physical hosts to VMs.
- Managing VM lifecycle (create, start, stop, deallocate, delete).
- Monitoring host health and triggering **auto-heal** (live migration of failed VMs to healthy hosts).
- Enforcing SLAs and updating domains for patching.
- Coordinating with the Network Controller and Storage Controller.

**Role of Hyper-V Hypervisor:**
- Azure uses the **Windows Server Hyper-V hypervisor** (Type-1, bare-metal).
- It creates and runs **isolated partitions** on the physical server, each acting as an independent VM.
- Provides **CPU virtualization** (via VT-x / AMD-V), **memory isolation** (via Extended Page Tables), **I/O virtualization** (SR-IOV), and **virtual networking** (vSwitch).
- Enforces **security isolation** between tenants.

*(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1 — Identify components and their SLAs:**
- VM 1: $S_1 = 99.99\% = 0.9999$
- VM 2: $S_2 = 99.99\% = 0.9999$
- Azure SQL Database: $S_{SQL} = 99.99\% = 0.9999$

**Step 2 — Compute parallel composite SLA for the two VMs.**

Failure probability per VM:
$$P_{fail} = 1 - 0.9999 = 0.0001$$

Joint failure probability (both down):
$$P_{both\_fail} = 0.0001 \times 0.0001 = 10^{-8}$$

Parallel composite SLA of the VM layer:
$$S_{VMs} = 1 - 10^{-8} = 0.99999999 \approx 99.99999\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute composite SLA with Azure SQL in series.**

In a series path, all components must be available. For independent components in series:
$$S_{comp} = S_{VMs} \times S_{SQL}$$

$$S_{comp} = 0.99999999 \times 0.9999 = 0.99989999 \approx 99.99\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute monthly allowable downtime.**

Total minutes in 30 days:
$$T_{month} = 30 \times 24 \times 60 = 43{,}200 \text{ minutes}$$

Downtime allowance:
$$D_{month} = 43{,}200 \times (1 - 0.99989999) = 43{,}200 \times 1.0001 \times 10^{-4} \approx 4.32 \text{ minutes/month}$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{S_{comp} = 99.99\% \text{ ; Monthly Downtime} \approx 4.32 \text{ minutes}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Azure SQL SLA dominates the composite because the VM layer's redundancy (99.99999%) is far higher than the database SLA (99.99%). The series chain is only as strong as the weakest link.

*(7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: (a)** With neat diagrams, explain the three Azure VM pricing models — Pay-As-You-Go, Reserved Instances, and Spot VMs. Compare them based on cost, commitment, and workload suitability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company deploys **Standard_D8s_v5** VMs (16 vCPUs, 64 GiB RAM) for its production web tier. The hourly PAYG rate is **\$0.384/hour**. Compute the monthly cost under (i) Pay-As-You-Go, (ii) 1-Year Reserved Instance with 40% savings, and (iii) 3-Year Reserved Instance with 62% savings. If the company uses 4 such VMs continuously, what is the **3-year total cost** under each model? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Three Azure VM Pricing Models:**

1. **Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG):**
   - Charged per second or per hour of usage.
   - **No upfront commitment**, can stop/start anytime.
   - Best for short-term, unpredictable workloads, dev/test.
   - Highest per-hour cost.

2. **Reserved Instances (RI):**
   - 1-year or 3-year commitment in exchange for up to **72% discount**.
   - Payment options: All Upfront, Partial Upfront, No Upfront.
   - Best for **steady-state production workloads** with predictable usage.
   - Can be exchanged or refunded (with limits).

3. **Spot VMs:**
   - Use Azure's **unused compute capacity** at discounts up to **90%**.
   - Can be **evicted** by Azure with 30-second notice when capacity is needed.
   - Best for **fault-tolerant, stateless, batch jobs** (e.g., ML training, rendering).
   - **Not suitable** for mission-critical production.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | PAYG | Reserved Instances | Spot VMs |
|-----------|------|-------------------|----------|
| Discount | 0% | 40-72% | 60-90% |
| Commitment | None | 1 or 3 years | None |
| Eviction Risk | None | None | High (30-sec notice) |
| Workload Fit | Dev/Test, short-term | Production, steady-state | Batch, fault-tolerant |
| Billing Granularity | Per second | Pre-paid or monthly | Per second |

*(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1 — Compute monthly cost for ONE Standard_D8s_v5 VM at PAYG.**

$$C_{PAYG,1} = 0.384 \times 730 = 280.32 \text{ USD/month}$$

**Step 2 — Compute monthly cost for ONE VM under 1-Year RI (40% off).**

$$C_{RI1,1} = 280.32 \times (1 - 0.40) = 280.32 \times 0.60 = 168.19 \text{ USD/month}$$

**Step 3 — Compute monthly cost for ONE VM under 3-Year RI (62% off).**

$$C_{RI3,1} = 280.32 \times (1 - 0.62) = 280.32 \times 0.38 = 106.52 \text{ USD/month}$$

**Step 4 — Scale up to 4 VMs.**

$$C_{PAYG,4} = 280.32 \times 4 = 1{,}121.28 \text{ USD/month}$$
$$C_{RI1,4} = 168.19 \times 4 = 672.77 \text{ USD/month}$$
$$C_{RI3,4} = 106.52 \times 4 = 426.09 \text{ USD/month}$$

**Step 5 — Compute 3-year total cost (36 months).**

$$T_{3yr,PAYG} = 1{,}121.28 \times 36 = 40{,}366.08 \text{ USD}$$
$$T_{3yr,RI1} = 672.77 \times 36 = 24{,}219.72 \text{ USD}$$
$$T_{3yr,RI3} = 426.09 \times 36 = 15{,}339.24 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 6 — Compute savings of 3-yr RI vs PAYG.**

$$\text{Savings} = 40{,}366.08 - 15{,}339.24 = 25{,}026.84 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 7 — Final Answer Table:**

$$\boxed{
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Model} & \textbf{Monthly (4 VMs)} & \textbf{3-Year Total} & \textbf{Savings vs PAYG} \\
\hline
\text{PAYG} & \$1{,}121.28 & \$40{,}366.08 & - \\
\hline
\text{1-Year RI} & \$672.77 & \$24{,}219.72 & \$16{,}146.36 \\
\hline
\text{3-Year RI} & \$426.09 & \$15{,}339.24 & \$25{,}026.84 \\
\hline
\end{array}
}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 3-year Reserved Instance saves the company **\$25,026.84** over 3 years compared to PAYG — a **62% reduction** in compute cost. This is why Reserved Instances are the recommended choice for steady-state production workloads.

*(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cause Mark Deductions in Azure VM Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Stopped" vs "Deallocated" states:** Stopped VMs **continue to incur compute charges**; only **Deallocated** releases the lease. Examiners frequently deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by 730 hours** in monthly cost calculations. Use the standard: **1 month = 730 hours = 43,200 minutes**.
>
> 3. **Mixing up SLA formula direction:** Composite SLA in **series** = **product** of individual SLAs. Composite SLA in **parallel** = **1 − product of failure probabilities**. Wrong direction = **−3 marks**.
>
> 4. **Wrong discount percentages:** Don't assume 50% for RIs. Azure's actual ranges are **40-50% for 1-yr** and **60-72% for 3-yr** RIs. Use the values **given in the question**.
>
> 5. **Spot VM eviction ignorance:** Students often recommend Spot VMs for production. Examiners will deduct 2 marks for not mentioning the **30-second eviction notice** and fault-tolerance requirement.
>
> 6. **Confusing vCPU:RAM ratios across families:** General Purpose (D) = 1:4, Compute Opt (F) = 1:2, Memory Opt (E/M) = 1:8 or higher. Wrong family recommendation = **−1 mark**.
>
> 7. **Not labeling Mermaid diagrams** properly: Examiners expect the diagram to show **VNet, Subnet, VM, NSG, Disk, and Public IP** components explicitly. Vague diagrams lose 1-2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision Before Entering the Exam Hall — Memorize These Points:**

- [x] **Azure VM = IaaS compute primitive** running on **Hyper-V (Type-1) bare-metal hypervisor**, managed by the **Azure Fabric Controller**.
- [x] **VM Components:** vCPU + RAM + OS Disk + Data Disks + Temporary Disk + vNIC + VNet/Subnet/NSG + Public/Private IP.
- [x] **VM Families Quick Mnemonic:**
  - **B** = Burstable (dev/test)
  - **D** = General Purpose (1:4)
  - **E/M** = Memory Optimized (1:8+)
  - **F** = Compute Optimized (1:2)
  - **N** = GPU (AI/ML)
  - **L** = Storage Optimized (high IOPS)
  - **H** = HPC (RDMA, InfiniBand)
- [x] **SLA Hierarchy:** Single VM (Premium SSD) = **99.9%** < Availability Set = **99.95%** < Availability Zone = **99.99%**.
- [x] **Monthly SLA Downtime Formula:** $D = 43{,}200 \times (1 - S_{decimal})$ minutes.
- [x] **Cost Formulas:**
  - $C_{monthly,PAYG} = R_{hourly} \times 730$
  - $C_{monthly,RI} = C_{PAYG} \times (1 - d)$ where $d$ = discount rate
- [x] **Composite SLA Rules:**
  - **Series:** $S = \prod S_i$ (product — weakest-link principle)
  - **Parallel:** $S = 1 - \prod(1 - S_i)$ (redundancy boost)
- [x] **Pricing Models:** PAYG (no commitment) < RI 1-yr (~40% off) < RI 3-yr (~62% off) < Spot VMs (~60-90% off, but evictable).
- [x] **Availability:** Availability Set = within-DC redundancy (**free, 99.95%**); Availability Zone = cross-DC redundancy (**99.99%**).
- [x] **Azure VM States:** Creating → Running → Stopped (still billed) / Deallocated (not billed) → Deleted.
- [x] **Burstable B-Series:** Accumulates credits at rate = vCPU count; bank max = 120 credits for B2s; full recharge from empty = 60 hours.
- [x] **Managed Disks vs Unmanaged Disks:** Managed = Azure manages storage account (recommended); Unmanaged = you manage the storage account (legacy, not for production).
- [x] **NSG (Network Security Group):** Stateful firewall at subnet/NIC level; rules are based on **5-tuple** (source IP, source port, dest IP, dest port, protocol).
- [x] **Azure Hypervisor:** Windows Server **Hyper-V** (Type-1, bare-metal), supports **nested virtualization** in Dv5 and Ev5 series for running VMs inside VMs.
- [x] **VM Extensions:** Post-deployment agents for configuration, monitoring, patching (e.g., Custom Script Extension, Azure Monitor Agent, Dependency Agent).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Module 4 — Azure VM:**
> *"Azure VMs are **IaaS**, billed by the **second** (with a 1-minute minimum), running on **Hyper-V hypervisor**, and they form the **backbone of cloud workloads** — choosing the right family, SLA, and pricing model is the key engineering decision."*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compute Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Compute Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Compute Services — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Compute Services** in cloud computing refer to the on-demand, scalable, and metered provisioning of **processing power, memory, storage, and networking resources** delivered as a service over the internet. These services abstract the underlying physical hardware and allow users to deploy and run applications without managing the physical servers.

According to the **NIST SP 800-145** definition, compute services form the **Processing & Computation layer** of the cloud stack, providing the execution environment for workloads ranging from simple web applications to high-performance computing (HPC) and artificial intelligence/machine learning (AI/ML) tasks.

The major compute service models in KTU 2024 syllabus scope are:
- **Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)** — Virtual Machines
- **Container as a Service (CaaS)** — Container Orchestration
- **Function as a Service (FaaS) / Serverless** — Event-driven execution
- **Platform as a Service (PaaS)** — Managed application runtimes

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electricity Grid" Model

Think of compute services like the **electricity grid** in your home:
- You don't build your own power plant. You just plug in a device.
- You pay only for the units (kWh) you consume.
- When you need more power (AC + Geyser + Oven), the grid automatically scales up.
- When you unplug everything, consumption drops to zero.

Similarly, **Compute Services** let you "plug in" your workload and consume CPU/RAM on-demand:
- Launch a VM in **seconds** (not weeks for procurement)
- Scale from **1 instance to 1,000 instances** automatically
- Pay per **second** (or millisecond for serverless)

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Compute Services

| Service Model | Abstraction Level | User Manages | Provider Manages | Example |
|---------------|-------------------|--------------|-----------------|---------|
| **IaaS (VMs)** | Hardware (Virtual) | OS, Middleware, Runtime, App, Data | Hardware, Virtualization, Networking | Amazon EC2, Azure VM, GCP Compute Engine |
| **CaaS (Containers)** | OS Image + Runtime | App, Data, Container config | OS, Runtime, Orchestration | Amazon ECS/EKS, Azure AKS |
| **PaaS** | Application Platform | App, Data | Everything else | AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service |
| **FaaS (Serverless)** | Function | Function code + trigger | Entire stack | AWS Lambda, Azure Functions |

## 1.4 Key Characteristics (KTU Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Pillars of Cloud Compute Services (Per KTU 2024 Module 4):**
> 1. **Elasticity** — Resources expand/contract with workload
> 2. **Pay-per-use** — Metered billing (per second/minute/invocation)
> 3. **Multi-tenancy** — Shared physical hardware, isolated virtual resources
> 4. **Self-service provisioning** — No human intervention from provider
> 5. **Service Level Agreement (SLA)** — Guaranteed uptime (e.g., **99.99%** for EC2)

## 1.5 Industry Reference Implementations

- **Amazon Web Services (AWS):** EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, Fargate
- **Microsoft Azure:** Virtual Machines, Azure Functions, AKS, Container Instances
- **Google Cloud Platform (GCP):** Compute Engine, Cloud Run, GKE, Cloud Functions
- **Open Source:** OpenStack (IaaS), Kubernetes (CaaS), Knative (Serverless)

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU examinations, AWS terminology is typically the **default reference** unless otherwise specified, as it dominates the global cloud market with approximately **31% market share (as of 2024)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Virtual Machines (EC2 — Elastic Compute Cloud)

### 2.1.1 Concept & Architecture

An **EC2 instance** is a virtualized computing environment that runs on a hypervisor (typically **Xen** in older AWS or **Nitro System** in current AWS). Each instance is launched from an **Amazon Machine Image (AMI)**, which contains the OS, application server, and applications.

**Operational Flow:**
1. User selects an **AMI** (template)
2. Chooses an **Instance Type** (CPU, RAM, storage, network)
3. Configures **Security Group** (firewall rules)
4. Launches → Hypervisor provisions VM on bare-metal host
5. User gets **Public/Private IP** + **DNS name**

### 2.1.2 Instance Type Families (AWS Reference)

| Family | Full Form | Use Case | Example Type |
|--------|-----------|----------|--------------|
| **T** | Burstable | Low-traffic web, dev/test | t3.micro, t3.medium |
| **M** | General Purpose | Balanced workloads | m5.large, m6i.xlarge |
| **C** | Compute Optimized | CPU-intensive tasks | c5.4xlarge, c7i.2xlarge |
| **R** | Memory Optimized | In-memory databases | r5.2xlarge |
| **X** | Memory Optimized (Extreme) | SAP HANA, large caches | x1.32xlarge |
| **P / G** | Accelerated (GPU) | ML training, graphics | p4d.24xlarge, g5.xlarge |
| **I** | Storage Optimized | NoSQL, data warehousing | i3.16xlarge |

### 2.1.3 Pricing Models (Critical for KTU 14-Mark Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4 EC2 Pricing Models:**
> 1. **On-Demand** — Pay per second/minute, no commitment. Most expensive. Best for short-term, unpredictable workloads.
> 2. **Reserved Instances (RI)** — 1-year or 3-year commitment. Up to **72% discount**. Best for steady-state workloads.
> 3. **Savings Plans** — Flexible commitment (1-3 years) across regions. Up to **66% discount**.
> 4. **Spot Instances** — Bid for spare capacity. Up to **90% discount**. Can be terminated with **2-minute notice**. Best for fault-tolerant, batch processing.

### 2.1.4 High Availability Concepts

- **Regions** — Geographic areas (e.g., `us-east-1`, `ap-south-1`)
- **Availability Zones (AZs)** — Isolated data centers within a region (typically **3 AZs per region**)
- **Edge Locations** — CDN endpoints for low-latency content delivery

> **Why AZs matter:** AZs are physically separate but connected with **low-latency links (< 1 ms)**. Deploying across multiple AZs provides **99.99% SLA**.

## 2.2 Containers & Orchestration

### 2.2.1 Container Fundamentals

A **container** is a lightweight, standalone, executable package that includes:
- Application code
- Runtime (e.g., Node.js, Python)
- System libraries
- Dependencies
- Configuration

Unlike VMs, containers **share the host OS kernel** and run as isolated processes.

| Feature | Virtual Machine | Container |
|---------|----------------|-----------|
| **Startup Time** | Minutes | Seconds/Milliseconds |
| **Size** | Gigabytes | Megabytes |
| **OS Overhead** | Full guest OS | Shares host kernel |
| **Isolation** | Strong (hypervisor) | Process-level |
| **Density per Host** | 10-20 VMs | 100-1000 containers |
| **Boot Time** | 30-60 seconds | < 1 second |

### 2.2.2 Container Services in Cloud

- **Amazon ECS** — AWS's proprietary container orchestrator (uses Docker)
- **Amazon EKS** — Managed **Kubernetes** service
- **AWS Fargate** — Serverless containers (no EC2 management)
- **Azure AKS / GCP GKE** — Managed Kubernetes on other clouds

> **Kubernetes** (often abbreviated **K8s**) is the de-facto standard for container orchestration, handling **auto-scaling, self-healing, service discovery, and rolling updates**.

## 2.3 Serverless Computing (FaaS)

### 2.3.1 Core Concept

In serverless, the cloud provider:
- **Dynamically allocates** compute resources on demand
- **Charges only for actual execution time** (e.g., per 100 ms)
- **Automatically scales** from zero to thousands of concurrent invocations

The user only provides:
- Function code
- Trigger event (HTTP request, queue message, file upload, schedule)

> [!NOTE]
> **"Serverless" does NOT mean "no servers"** — it means **"no server management for the user"**. Servers still exist; the provider handles all infrastructure.

### 2.3.2 AWS Lambda (Reference Implementation)

**Key Limits (must remember for KTU):**
- **Maximum execution time:** 15 minutes
- **Memory allocation:** 128 MB to **10,240 MB**
- **Deployment package size:** 50 MB (zipped), 250 MB (unzipped)
- **Concurrent executions:** 1,000 (default, can be increased)

**Billing Formula:**

$$ \text{Cost} = (\text{Number of invocations}) \times (\text{Price per invocation}) + (\text{GB-seconds}) \times (\text{Price per GB-second}) $$

**Where:**
- 1 GB-second = 1 GB of memory provisioned × 1 second of execution

### 2.3.3 Cold Start Problem

When a Lambda function is invoked for the first time (or after idle), the platform must:
1. Download the code
2. Start the runtime
3. Initialize the function

This latency (typically **100 ms to several seconds**) is called the **Cold Start**. Subsequent invocations are **"warm"** with much lower latency.

## 2.4 Auto Scaling & Load Balancing

### 2.4.1 Auto Scaling Types

| Type | Mechanism | Use Case |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **Reactive (Dynamic) Scaling** | Scale based on metrics (CPU > 70%) | Handling traffic spikes |
| **Predictive Scaling** | ML-based forecast of load | Anticipated events |
| **Scheduled Scaling** | Time-based (e.g., scale up at 9 AM) | Known business hours |

**AWS Auto Scaling Group (ASG) Components:**
- **Launch Template/Configuration** — What to launch
- **Min/Max/Desired capacity** — Boundaries
- **Scaling Policies** — When and how to scale
- **Health Checks** — Replace unhealthy instances

### 2.4.2 Load Balancers (ELB)

| Type | Layer | Protocol | Use Case |
|------|-------|----------|----------|
| **Application Load Balancer (ALB)** | Layer 7 | HTTP/HTTPS | Web apps, microservices, path-based routing |
| **Network Load Balancer (NLB)** | Layer 4 | TCP/UDP | High-performance, low-latency |
| **Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB)** | Layer 3 | IP | Third-party virtual appliances |
| **Classic Load Balancer (CLB)** | Layer 4/7 | TCP/HTTP | Legacy (being deprecated) |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Compute Services:**

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|-------------------|----------|---------------|
| **Lambda Cost** | $C = N \times P_{inv} + M \times T \times P_{GB\text{-}s}$ | N = invocations, M = GB memory, T = seconds |
| **GB-Seconds** | $GB\text{-}s = \frac{Memory_{MB}}{1024} \times Duration_{s}$ | Always normalize to GB |
| **SLA Uptime (3 nines)** | $\text{Downtime} = 365.25 \times 24 \times 60 \times (1 - 0.999) = 52.6 \text{ min/year}$ | 99.9% = 8.77 hours/year |
| **SLA Uptime (4 nines)** | $\text{Downtime} = 365.25 \times 24 \times 60 \times (1 - 0.9999) = 5.26 \text{ min/year}$ | 99.99% = 52.6 min/year |
| **Spot Savings** | $Savings\% = \frac{P_{OD} - P_{Spot}}{P_{OD}} \times 100$ | Up to 90% |
| **RI Discount** | $Discount\% = \frac{P_{OD} - P_{RI}}{P_{OD}} \times 100$ | Up to 72% for 3-year |
| **Concurrent Users Capacity** | $Capacity = \frac{Instances \times vCPU_{per\_inst}}{vCPU_{per\_user}}$ | Theoretical limit |
| **Cost per Hour (EC2)** | $C_{hr} = C_{compute} + C_{storage} + C_{data}$ | Sum all components |

> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN RULE:** All absolute values and pipe symbols have been replaced with `\vert` for table compatibility. For example, write $\vert x \vert$ instead of `|x|`.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Startups:** Use Spot + Lambda to keep infrastructure cost below **\$50/month**
- **Enterprises:** Use Reserved Instances for steady-state + Spot for batch processing
- **ML Workloads:** Use P/G instances for training, Lambda for inference
- **E-commerce:** Auto-scaling handles 10x traffic on Black Friday
- **IoT:** Serverless functions process millions of sensor events
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: EC2 Cost Calculation (14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A startup runs **3 m5.large** instances **24/7 for 30 days**. Calculate the monthly cost using:
> - On-Demand pricing: **\$0.096/hour**
> - 1-year Reserved (No Upfront): **\$0.062/hour**
> - Spot pricing: **\$0.038/hour**
>
> Which option is cheapest? What is the savings percentage?

### Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate total hours per month**

$$ H = 3 \text{ instances} \times 24 \text{ hr/day} \times 30 \text{ days} = 2160 \text{ instance-hours} $$

**Step 2: On-Demand Cost**

$$ C_{OD} = 2160 \times 0.096 = \$207.36 $$

**Step 3: Reserved Instance Cost (1-year)**

$$ C_{RI} = 2160 \times 0.062 = \$133.92 $$

**Step 4: Spot Instance Cost**

$$ C_{Spot} = 2160 \times 0.038 = \$82.08 $$

**Step 5: Savings Comparison**

$$ Savings_{RI} = \frac{207.36 - 133.92}{207.36} \times 100 = 35.42\% $$

$$ Savings_{Spot} = \frac{207.36 - 82.08}{207.36} \times 100 = 60.42\% $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Spot is cheapest at **\$82.08/month (60.42% savings)**, but carries the risk of 2-minute termination notice. RI offers **35.42% savings** with guaranteed capacity. The choice depends on workload fault-tolerance.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: AWS Lambda GB-Seconds Calculation

> **Problem:** A Lambda function is configured with **512 MB** memory. It runs **100,000 times** in a month, with each execution averaging **200 ms**.
> - Invocation price: **\$0.20 per 1 million**
> - Compute price: **\$0.0000166667 per GB-second**
>
> Calculate the total monthly bill.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Convert memory to GB**

$$ M_{GB} = \frac{512}{1024} = 0.5 \text{ GB} $$

**Step 2: Calculate execution time in seconds**

$$ T_s = \frac{200}{1000} = 0.2 \text{ seconds per invocation} $$

**Step 3: Calculate GB-seconds per invocation**

$$ GB\text{-}s_{single} = 0.5 \times 0.2 = 0.1 \text{ GB-seconds} $$

**Step 4: Total GB-seconds in month**

$$ GB\text{-}s_{total} = 100{,}000 \times 0.1 = 10{,}000 \text{ GB-seconds} $$

**Step 5: Invocation cost**

$$ C_{inv} = \frac{100{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 0.20 = \$0.02 $$

**Step 6: Compute cost**

$$ C_{compute} = 10{,}000 \times 0.0000166667 = \$0.1667 $$

**Step 7: Total monthly cost**

$$ C_{total} = 0.02 + 0.1667 = \$0.1867 $$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: SLA Downtime Calculation

> **Problem:** A compute service offers **99.95% SLA**. How much downtime is allowed per year?

### Solution:

**Step 1: Identify downtime percentage**

$$ Availability = 99.95\% \implies Downtime\% = 100 - 99.95 = 0.05\% $$

**Step 2: Convert to decimal**

$$ Downtime_{decimal} = \frac{0.05}{100} = 0.0005 $$

**Step 3: Calculate minutes in a year**

$$ Min_{year} = 365.25 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}960 \text{ minutes} $$

**Step 4: Calculate allowed downtime**

$$ D_{allowed} = 525{,}960 \times 0.0005 = 262.98 \text{ minutes} \approx 4.38 \text{ hours/year} $$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: VM vs Container vs Serverless

| Parameter | EC2 (IaaS) | ECS/EKS (CaaS) | Lambda (FaaS) |
|-----------|------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Startup Time** | 30-60 s | 1-5 s | 100 ms (warm), 1-3 s (cold) |
| **Max Runtime** | Unlimited | Unlimited | 15 minutes |
| **Control Level** | Full OS access | Container-level | Function-only |
| **Scaling Speed** | Minutes (with ASG) | Seconds (K8s HPA) | Milliseconds |
| **Pricing Granularity** | Per hour/second | Per second (Fargate) | Per 100 ms |
| **Idle Cost** | Yes (always running) | Yes (always running) | **Zero** |
| **Best For** | Stateful, long-running | Microservices, hybrid | Event-driven, sporadic |
| **Example Use** | Database server | API gateway backend | Image resize on upload |

## 3.5 Decision Tree: When to Use What?

```
┌─ Is the workload event-driven and short-lived (< 15 min)?
│    ├─ YES → Use AWS Lambda (Serverless)
│    └─ NO  → Continue ↓
│
├─ Does the app need OS-level customization (specific kernel modules, drivers)?
│    ├─ YES → Use EC2 (IaaS)
│    └─ NO  → Continue ↓
│
├─ Is the app containerized and needs orchestration?
│    ├─ YES → Use ECS / EKS (CaaS)
│    └─ NO  → Use Elastic Beanstalk (PaaS)
│
└─ Is it a steady-state long-running workload?
     ├─ YES → EC2 with Reserved Instances
     └─ NO with fault-tolerance → EC2 Spot Instances
```

## 3.6 Common KTU 14-Mark Problem Patterns

1. **Cost Comparison:** "Compare On-Demand vs Reserved vs Spot for X instances over Y months"
2. **Lambda Billing:** "Calculate monthly cost given memory, invocations, and duration"
3. **SLA Calculation:** "Compute downtime for a given SLA percentage"
4. **Scaling Strategy:** "Design an auto-scaling policy for given CPU thresholds"
5. **Architecture Design:** "Choose appropriate compute service for given scenario"
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Compute Service Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Cloud Compute Services] --> B[IaaS Virtual Machines]
    A --> C[CaaS Containers]
    A --> D[FaaS Serverless]
    A --> E[PaaS Managed Platforms]

    B --> B1[EC2 On Demand]
    B --> B2[EC2 Reserved]
    B --> B3[EC2 Spot]
    B --> B4[Bare Metal]

    C --> C1[Amazon ECS]
    C --> C2[Amazon EKS]
    C --> C3[AWS Fargate]
    C --> C4[Kubernetes]

    D --> D1[AWS Lambda]
    D --> D2[Azure Functions]
    D --> D3[Cloud Run]

    E --> E1[Elastic Beanstalk]
    E --> E2[App Engine]
    E --> E3[Azure App Service]
```

## 4.2 EC2 Instance Launch Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant API as AWS API
    participant H as Hypervisor Nitro
    participant BM as Bare Metal Host
    participant S as Storage EBS

    U->>API: 1 Select AMI and Instance Type
    API->>U: 2 Return Pricing Options
    U->>API: 3 Confirm Launch with Key Pair
    API->>H: 4 Provision VM on Host
    H->>BM: 5 Allocate vCPU and RAM
    H->>S: 6 Attach EBS Volume
    H->>U: 7 Return Public IP and DNS
    U->>API: 8 SSH Connect Port 22
    API->>U: 9 Connection Established
```

## 4.3 Auto Scaling Group Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LB[Application Load Balancer] --> ASG{Auto Scaling Group}

    ASG --> Inst1[EC2 Instance 1]
    ASG --> Inst2[EC2 Instance 2]
    ASG --> Inst3[EC2 Instance 3]
    ASG --> Inst4[EC2 Instance N]

    CW[CloudWatch Metrics] -->|CPU 70 percent| ASG
    CW -->|Network In| ASG
    CW -->|Custom Metric| ASG

    SNS[SNS Notification] -.->|Alert| Admin[Administrator]
    ASG -.->|Trigger| SNS
```

## 4.4 Serverless Event-Driven Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S3[S3 Bucket Upload] -->|Event| EB[EventBridge]
    API[API Gateway] -->|HTTP| EB
    SQS[SQS Queue] -->|Message| EB
    SCH[EventBridge Schedule] -->|Trigger| EB

    EB --> L1[Lambda Function 1]
    EB --> L2[Lambda Function 2]
    EB --> L3[Lambda Function 3]

    L1 --> DDB1[DynamoDB]
    L2 --> SES[SES Email]
    L3 --> SNS2[SNS Topic]

    L1 -.->|Logs| CL[CloudWatch Logs]
    L2 -.->|Logs| CL
    L3 -.->|Logs| CL
```

## 4.5 Multi-AZ High Availability Deployment

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    USER[End Users] --> R53[Route 53 DNS]

    R53 --> ALB1[ALB AZ 1]
    R53 --> ALB2[ALB AZ 2]

    ALB1 --> EC2A1[EC2 in AZ 1a]
    ALB1 --> EC2A2[EC2 in AZ 1b]

    ALB2 --> EC2A1
    ALB2 --> EC2A2

    EC2A1 --> RDS1[RDS Primary AZ 1]
    EC2A2 --> RDS2[RDS Standby AZ 2]

    RDS1 <-->|Synchronous Replication| RDS2

    S3ST[(S3 Cross Region Replication)]
    EC2A1 -.->|Backup| S3ST
    EC2A2 -.->|Backup| S3ST
```

## 4.6 Compute Service Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New Workload] --> Q1{Need OS control?}

    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is it long running?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Event driven?}

    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4{Predictable load?}
    Q2 -->|No| EC2SP[EC2 Spot]

    Q4 -->|Yes| EC2RI[EC2 Reserved]
    Q4 -->|No| EC2OD[EC2 On Demand]

    Q3 -->|Yes| Q5{Runtime less than 15 min?}
    Q3 -->|No| Q6{Containerized?}

    Q5 -->|Yes| LAMBDA[AWS Lambda]
    Q5 -->|No| EC2LT[EC2 Long Task]

    Q6 -->|Yes| Q7{Need K8s?}
    Q6 -->|No| PAAS[Elastic Beanstalk]

    Q7 -->|Yes| EKS[Amazon EKS]
    Q7 -->|No| ECS[Amazon ECS]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Serverless Computing. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Serverless Computing** is a cloud execution model in which the cloud provider dynamically allocates compute resources on-demand, and the user is charged only for the actual execution time of their code. The user writes and uploads **functions** (event-driven code snippets) and the provider handles all server provisioning, scaling, patching, and maintenance. The term "serverless" is misleading — servers still exist, but the user has **zero server management responsibility**. Examples include **AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, and Google Cloud Functions**. Key characteristics are:
> 1. **Event-driven execution** (triggered by HTTP, queue, schedule)
> 2. **Automatic scaling from zero to thousands**
> 3. **Pay-per-invocation billing** (typically per 100 ms)
> 4. **Built-in high availability and fault tolerance**

### Question 2: List any three EC2 instance type families with their use cases. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1. **T-family (Burstable):** T3, T4g — For low-traffic websites, development environments, and small databases with baseline CPU performance and burst capability.
> 2. **C-family (Compute Optimized):** C5, C6i, C7i — For CPU-intensive workloads such as batch processing, scientific modeling, gaming servers, and high-performance web servers.
> 3. **R-family (Memory Optimized):** R5, R6i — For memory-intensive applications such as in-memory databases (Redis, Memcached), real-time big data analytics, and high-performance caching.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **Question A: Compute Services — Pricing and SLA Analysis** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
> Explain the **four EC2 pricing models** with their discount percentages and ideal use cases. A company wants to run **5 m5.large instances 24/7 for 12 months** with predictable steady-state load. Recommend the most cost-effective model with justification.

**Model Answer (a):**

The four EC2 pricing models are:

**1. On-Demand Instances (No Discount):**
- Pay per second (Linux) or per hour (Windows)
- No long-term commitment
- Highest cost (baseline pricing)
- **Use case:** Short-term, unpredictable, spiky workloads; development and testing

**2. Reserved Instances (Up to 72% discount):**
- 1-year or 3-year commitment
- Three payment options: All Upfront, Partial Upfront, No Upfront
- Capacity reserved in specific AZ
- **Use case:** Steady-state workloads with predictable usage (databases, always-on apps)

**3. Savings Plans (Up to 66% discount):**
- 1-year or 3-year commitment based on dollar amount
- Flexibility across instance families, regions, and operating systems
- **Use case:** Workloads that may shift between instance types but with predictable spend

**4. Spot Instances (Up to 90% discount):**
- Bid for spare AWS capacity
- Can be terminated with **2-minute notice** by AWS
- **Use case:** Fault-tolerant, flexible workloads (batch jobs, CI/CD, ML training)

**Cost Calculation for 5 m5.large × 24/7 × 12 months:**

$$ H_{total} = 5 \times 24 \times 365 = 43{,}800 \text{ instance-hours} $$

| Model | Rate/hr | Annual Cost |
|-------|---------|-------------|
| On-Demand | \$0.096 | \$4,204.80 |
| 1-yr Reserved (No Upfront) | \$0.062 | \$2,715.60 |
| 3-yr Reserved (No Upfront) | \$0.043 | \$1,883.40 |
| Spot (variable) | \$0.038 | \$1,664.40 |

> **Recommendation:** For a **predictable steady-state load**, **3-year Reserved Instances (No Upfront)** are most cost-effective, providing **55.21% savings** over On-Demand while guaranteeing capacity. Spot is cheaper but inappropriate due to termination risk. 1-year RI offers 35.42% savings if 3-year commitment is undesirable.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
> A cloud service advertises **99.95% SLA**. Calculate the **maximum allowed annual downtime** in minutes. Explain the **multi-AZ deployment strategy** with a diagram and justify why it is critical for compute services.

**Model Answer (b):**

**SLA Calculation:**

$$ Downtime_{year} = 525{,}960 \times (1 - 0.9995) = 525{,}960 \times 0.0005 = 262.98 \text{ minutes} $$

This equates to approximately **4 hours 22 minutes 58 seconds per year**.

**Multi-AZ Deployment Strategy:**

A **Region** contains multiple **Availability Zones (AZs)**. Each AZ is one or more physically separate, independent data centers with redundant power, networking, and connectivity. AZs within a region are connected by **low-latency private fiber links** (typically < 1 ms RTT).

**Deployment Steps:**
1. Deploy EC2 instances across **at least 2 AZs** (e.g., AZ-1a and AZ-1b)
2. Place an **Application Load Balancer (ALB)** in front of all instances
3. Configure **Route 53** for DNS-based failover
4. Use **RDS Multi-AZ** for synchronous database replication
5. Enable **Auto Scaling** across AZs for elasticity

**Why It Is Critical:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Single-AZ deployment risks:**
> - Power outage in the data center → complete service downtime
> - Natural disaster (flood, earthquake) → service unavailable
> - Network failure → users disconnected
> - Hardware failure → no failover

> **Multi-AZ benefits:**
> - **High availability** — If one AZ fails, traffic reroutes to the other
> - **99.99% SLA** — Multi-AZ deployments qualify for higher SLA than single-AZ
> - **Zero-downtime maintenance** — AWS can patch underlying infrastructure
> - **Disaster recovery** — Built-in protection against localized failures

**Architecture Diagram (refer to Section 4.5):** The diagram shows users connecting via Route 53 to an ALB, which distributes traffic to EC2 instances spread across multiple AZs, with a primary-standby RDS configuration and S3 cross-region replication for backups.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

### **Question B: Container and Serverless Architectures** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
> Compare **Virtual Machines (EC2) and Containers (ECS/EKS)** across **six key parameters**. Explain the role of **Kubernetes** in container orchestration with at least **four core functions**.

**Model Answer (a):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | EC2 (Virtual Machines) | ECS/EKS (Containers) |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------|
| **Isolation** | Hardware-level (hypervisor) | Process-level (kernel namespaces + cgroups) |
| **Startup Time** | 30-60 seconds | 1-5 seconds |
| **Image Size** | 10-20 GB (full OS) | 50-500 MB (app + libs) |
| **Density per Host** | 10-20 VMs | 100-1000 containers |
| **OS Overhead** | Full guest OS per instance | Shared host kernel |
| **Boot/Update Speed** | Minutes | Seconds |

**Kubernetes (K8s) — Core Functions:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kubernetes is the de-facto industry standard for container orchestration.**

1. **Auto-Scaling (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler):** Automatically adjusts the number of running container instances (pods) based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics.

2. **Self-Healing:** Restarts failed containers, replaces unresponsive pods, and reschedules workloads to healthy nodes automatically.

3. **Service Discovery & Load Balancing:** Provides built-in DNS and load balancing so containers can find and communicate with each other without hard-coded IPs.

4. **Rolling Updates & Rollbacks:** Performs zero-downtime deployments by gradually replacing old container versions with new ones, with automatic rollback on failure.

5. **Storage Orchestration:** Automatically mounts persistent storage (EBS, EFS, S3) to containers as needed.

6. **Secret & Config Management:** Manages sensitive data (API keys, passwords) and configuration files without exposing them in container images.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
> Explain **AWS Lambda's billing model** with a worked example. A function is configured with **1024 MB memory**, invoked **500,000 times/month**, with each execution lasting **3 seconds**. Pricing: **\$0.20 per 1M invocations, \$0.0000166667 per GB-second.** Calculate the monthly bill.

**Model Answer (b):**

**AWS Lambda Billing Model:**

AWS Lambda charges for two components:

$$ C_{total} = C_{invocations} + C_{compute} $$

**1. Invocation Charges:** Per number of function executions
- \$0.20 per 1 million requests
- Free tier: 1 million requests/month

**2. Compute Charges:** Per GB-second (memory × duration)
- \$0.0000166667 per GB-second
- Free tier: 400,000 GB-seconds/month

**Where:**
$$ GB\text{-}s = \frac{Memory_{MB}}{1024} \times Duration_{seconds} $$

**Worked Example Calculation:**

**Step 1: Convert memory to GB**

$$ M_{GB} = \frac{1024}{1024} = 1.0 \text{ GB} $$

**Step 2: Compute GB-seconds per invocation**

$$ GB\text{-}s_{single} = 1.0 \times 3 = 3.0 \text{ GB-seconds} $$

**Step 3: Total GB-seconds per month**

$$ GB\text{-}s_{total} = 500{,}000 \times 3.0 = 1{,}500{,}000 \text{ GB-seconds} $$

**Step 4: Invocation cost**

$$ C_{inv} = \frac{500{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 0.20 = \$0.10 $$

**Step 5: Compute cost**

$$ C_{compute} = 1{,}500{,}000 \times 0.0000166667 = \$25.00 $$

**Step 6: Total monthly bill**

$$ C_{total} = 0.10 + 25.00 = \$25.10 $$

> **Note:** This exceeds the free tier (400,000 GB-seconds), so the full amount is charged. The key insight is that **memory is the most significant cost driver** in Lambda — doubling memory doubles the GB-second cost.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Compute Services Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include ALL cost components:** EC2 cost is not just per-hour rate — students forget to add **EBS storage, data transfer, and Elastic IP** costs. Always state: *"Assuming standard EBS and minimal data transfer..."*
>
> 2. **Confusing Spot and Reserved pricing:** Spot offers up to **90% discount** but NO capacity guarantee. Reserved offers up to **72% discount** with capacity guarantee. Examiners mark this distinction carefully.
>
> 3. **Mixing up GB-Seconds formula:** Many students compute **MB × seconds** instead of **GB × seconds**. Always divide memory by 1024 to convert MB to GB.
>
> 4. **SLA calculation errors:** Use **365.25 days** (accounting for leap years), not 365. For **99.99% SLA**, downtime is **52.6 minutes/year** — memorize this.
>
> 5. **Lambda limits:** Forgetting the **15-minute maximum execution time** and **10 GB memory cap** is a common error.
>
> 6. **Container vs VM comparison:** Always mention **kernel sharing** for containers vs **separate kernels** for VMs. Examiners specifically test this.
>
> 7. **Auto Scaling terminology:** "Horizontal scaling" (add more instances) is different from "Vertical scaling" (add more CPU/RAM to existing instance). EC2 Auto Scaling is **horizontal only**.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Compute Services:**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] **EC2 = IaaS** (Virtual Machines, full OS control)
- [ ] **ECS/EKS = CaaS** (Containers, shared OS kernel)
- [ ] **Lambda = FaaS** (Serverless, event-driven, max 15 min runtime)
- [ ] **Beanstalk = PaaS** (Managed platform for app deployment)
- [ ] **Fargate = Serverless Containers** (no EC2 management)

### Key Numerical Limits
- [ ] Lambda **max execution:** 15 minutes
- [ ] Lambda **max memory:** 10,240 MB (10 GB)
- [ ] Lambda **deployment size:** 50 MB zipped, 250 MB unzipped
- [ ] Spot **termination notice:** 2 minutes
- [ ] RI **max discount:** 72% (3-year, all upfront)
- [ ] Spot **max discount:** Up to 90%
- [ ] Typical **3-AZ** region architecture

### Critical Formulas
- [ ] **Lambda cost:** $C = N \times P_{inv} + GB \times s \times P_{GB\text{-}s}$
- [ ] **GB-Seconds:** $GB\text{-}s = \frac{MB}{1024} \times seconds$
- [ ] **SLA Downtime:** $D = 525{,}960 \times (1 - SLA)$
- [ ] **Discount %:** $\frac{P_{base} - P_{new}}{P_{base}} \times 100$

### Service Selection Mnemonic — "LECA"
- [ ] **L**ong-running + OS control → **E**C2
- [ ] **E**vent-driven + short tasks → **L**ambda
- [ ] **C**ontainerized + microservices → **E**CS/**E**KS
- [ ] **A**pp deployment without infra mgmt → Elastic **B**eanstalk (PaaS)

### Pricing Models Mnemonic — "ORSS"
- [ ] **O**n-Demand (full price, no commitment)
- [ ] **R**eserved (1-3 yr, up to 72% off)
- [ ] **S**avings Plans (flexible commitment)
- [ ] **S**pot (up to 90% off, 2-min termination)

### Quick Definitions to Memorize
- [ ] **Hypervisor:** Software that creates and runs VMs (e.g., AWS Nitro)
- [ ] **AMI:** Amazon Machine Image — template for EC2 instances
- [ ] **AZ:** Availability Zone — isolated data center within a region
- [ ] **ASG:** Auto Scaling Group — manages fleet of EC2 instances
- [ ] **ALB:** Application Load Balancer — Layer 7 HTTP/HTTPS
- [ ] **NLB:** Network Load Balancer — Layer 4 TCP/UDP
- [ ] **Cold Start:** First-time Lambda invocation latency
- [ ] **EFA:** Elastic Fabric Adapter — for HPC and ML
- [ ] **Placement Group:** Cluster of EC2 instances for low-latency communication
- [ ] **Dedicated Host:** Physical server dedicated to single customer
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT524 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Storage Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud Storage Services** are **on-demand, network-accessible, elastic, and metered data storage subsystems** delivered as a managed service over the Internet. They abstract the underlying physical storage hardware (HDDs, SSDs, tapes) and present logical storage primitives — **Object, Block, and File storage** — to consumers via standardized APIs (REST/SOAP) or industry protocols (iSCSI, NFS, SMB).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme Cloud Computing syllabus (PEERT524, Module 4)**, *Storage Services* is positioned as the data-persistence backbone of every IaaS/PaaS/SaaS offering. The formal definition, per the syllabus outcome **CO4**, is:

*"A logically pooled, geographically distributed, fault-tolerant storage fabric that delivers **5 nines (99.999%)** durability, multi-region replication, pay-per-GB billing, and policy-driven access control to tenants over standard network protocols."*

Key terminologies mandated by the KTU syllabus:
- **Object Storage** – Data managed as discrete, addressable units (objects) inside flat namespaces called *buckets* or *containers*.
- **Block Storage** – Raw, fixed-size storage volumes (typically **512 bytes – 16 MB blocks**) attached to a single VM instance.
- **File Storage** – Hierarchical POSIX/NTFS-style file systems shared across multiple clients using NFS/SMB.
- **Storage Tier** – Cost-vs-performance class (Hot, Cool/Cold, Archive).
- **Data Consistency Model** – Strong, eventual, or read-after-write.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Warehouse" Model

Imagine you run a **national logistics company**. Instead of owning warehouses, you rent space from a giant, automated warehouse provider (AWS, Azure, GCP). You never visit the warehouse; you send a courier (API call) with a barcode label (object key) and either:
- **Drop a box** (Object Storage — `S3 / Azure Blob`) — perfect for photos, videos, backups.
- **Rent a private locker bolted to your office desk** (Block Storage — `EBS / Azure Disk`) — perfect for databases that need low latency.
- **Rent a shared filing cabinet with folders** (File Storage — `EFS / Azure Files`) — perfect when multiple employees need to edit the same documents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary for First-Time Learners:**
> Cloud storage is *just a hard drive that lives on the Internet*. The "cloud" part means you don't own the disk, you rent it by the hour or by the gigabyte, and it automatically copies itself to multiple physical locations so a meteor could hit one data center and your data would still survive.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics You Must Memorize for KTU Exams

The following constants appear in nearly every KTU Numerical/Descriptive question on Module 4:

- **Durability of Object Storage:** **99.999999999 % (11 nines)** — industry-standard for top-tier cloud object stores.
- **Availability SLA:** Typically **99.9 %** (3 nines) to **99.99 %** (4 nines).
- **Maximum single object size:** **5 TB** (AWS S3 / Azure Blob).
- **Minimum billable block size:** **1 GB-month** for cold tiers; **per-request** for hot tiers.
- **Default block size for cloud block storage:** **4 KB – 16 MB** (configurable).
- **Replication factor (default):** **3 copies** across **3 Availability Zones**.


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Cloud Storage — Operational Breakdown

#### Pillar 1: Object Storage (e.g., AWS S3, Azure Blob, GCP Cloud Storage)

**Operational Logic (Why & How):**
- *Why?* Traditional file systems collapse when files exceed billions because directory trees become bottlenecks. Object stores use a **flat namespace** — every object has a unique key (URL) and an associated **metadata map** (size, MIME type, custom tags).
- *How?* When a `PUT` request arrives, the storage controller:
  1. Computes an MD5/SHA-256 hash for integrity.
  2. Generates a globally unique object key.
  3. Stores the object on **one of N storage nodes** using a **Consistent Hashing** ring.
  4. Replicates to **N+1 or N+2 nodes** based on durability class.
  5. Updates the index/metadata database (DynamoDB-style).

> [!NOTE]
> **Object Storage is ideal for:** static websites, backups, log archival, big-data lakes (data used by Spark/Hadoop), disaster recovery, and media streaming.

#### Pillar 2: Block Storage (e.g., AWS EBS, Azure Managed Disks, GCP Persistent Disk)

**Operational Logic:**
- *Why?* Operating systems and databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB) require **raw, low-latency, randomly writable byte-addressable** storage. Object storage is eventually consistent and not random-writable.
- *How?* A block volume is a virtual disk carved from a **RAID array of SSDs/HDDs** in the hypervisor's storage area network (SAN). It is exposed as `/dev/sda`, `/dev/xvdf`, etc., to the guest VM via iSCSI or NVMe-over-Fabrics.

**Key Properties:**
- **Single-tenant attachment** (one volume = one VM at a time, except for shared-NVMe clusters).
- **IOPS-bound** — performance measured in **Input/Output Operations Per Second** and **Throughput (MB/s)**.
- **Snapshots** are incremental, copy-on-write, point-in-time copies.

#### Pillar 3: File Storage (e.g., AWS EFS, Azure Files, GCP Filestore)

**Operational Logic:**
- *Why?* Legacy enterprise applications (SAP, Oracle E-Business Suite, content management systems) expect a **POSIX-compliant** shared file system that multiple VMs can mount simultaneously.
- *How?* Implemented as a **distributed parallel file system** (e.g., Lustre, GPFS, custom NFSv4 cluster). Data is striped across many storage nodes using a metadata server (MDS) + data server (DS) architecture.

### 2.2 Storage Service Tier Matrix

| Tier Class | Access Frequency | Retrieval Latency | Typical Use Case | Relative Cost (per GB/month) |
|------------|------------------|-------------------|------------------|------------------------------|
| **Hot / Standard** | Frequent (daily) | Milliseconds | Web content, analytics | **Highest** (~$0.023) |
| **Cool / Infrequent Access** | 1× per month | Milliseconds–seconds | Disaster recovery, backups | Medium (~$0.0125) |
| **Cold / Archive** | 1–2× per year | Minutes–hours | Compliance, long-term archival | **Lowest** (~$0.004) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *"How does the cost model of cold storage differ from hot storage?"*, always mention **retrieval fees** and **minimum storage duration penalties** (e.g., 90 days for S3 IA, 180 days for Glacier).

### 2.3 Consistency Models — A KTU Favourite

| Model | Definition | Example Service |
|-------|------------|-----------------|
| **Strong Consistency** | All clients see the same data immediately after a write completes | AWS EBS, Azure Disk |
| **Read-After-Write Consistency** | A read immediately after a write returns the new value (only for the writer) | AWS S3 (post-2020) |
| **Eventual Consistency** | Replicas converge within seconds; temporary staleness is acceptable | AWS S3 (older), DynamoDB |
| **Causal Consistency** | Operations causally related are seen in order | Some Azure Cosmos DB modes |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **exam-critical** for any KTU 2024 Scheme numerical on storage services.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Effective Storage Capacity** | $C_{\text{effective}} = (N_{\text{disks}} \times S_{\text{disk}}) \times \eta_{\text{RAID}}$ | $\eta_{\text{RAID}} = 0.5$ (RAID-1), $0.75$ (RAID-5), $1.0$ (RAID-0) |
| 2 | **Replication Factor Cost** | $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{logical}} \times R$ | $R = 3$ default in AWS S3, Azure Blob |
| 3 | **Durability (N-nines)** | $D = 1 - (1 - p)^{n}$ | $p$ = single-object loss probability, $n$ = number of replicas |
| 4 | **Availability (uptime %)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100$ | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair |
| 5 | **Annual Downtime** | $T_{\text{down}} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | Minutes/year for given availability |
| 6 | **Monthly Cost (Object Store)** | $C_{\text{monthly}} = (S_{\text{GB}} \times P_{\text{storage}}) + (N_{\text{req}} \times P_{\text{req}})$ | $P_{\text{storage}}$ per GB-month, $P_{\text{req}}$ per 10,000 requests |
| 7 | **IOPS Requirement** | $\text{IOPS}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{IOPS}_{i}$ | Sum across all VMs attached to a volume |
| 8 | **Throughput** | $T = \text{IOPS} \times \dfrac{B_{\text{block}}}{1024^2}$ | MB/s = IOPS × Block size in MB |
| 9 | **Erasure Coding Overhead** | $E = \dfrac{k}{k+m}$ | $k$ data shards, $m$ parity shards (e.g., RS 6+3 → $E = 0.667$) |
| 10 | **RTO / RPO Definitions** | $\text{RTO} = \text{Recovery Time Objective}$, $\text{RPO} = \text{Recovery Point Objective}$ | RPO measured in data loss tolerance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The durability equation $D = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$ is used by AWS/Azure engineers to **prove compliance with 11 nines** ($D \geq 0.99999999999$) during SLA negotiations with enterprise customers (banks, hospitals).

### 2.5 Content Delivery Networks (CDN) — Extension of Storage

A CDN is a **geographically distributed cache layer** sitting *in front of* object storage. When a user requests `/video.mp4`, the CDN edge server nearest to the user delivers the bytes, drastically reducing latency.

- **Cache Hit Ratio (CHR):** $\text{CHR} = \dfrac{\text{Cache Hits}}{\text{Cache Hits} + \text{Cache Misses}}$
- **Origin Shield:** A dedicated tier of caching between edge and origin (S3) to reduce origin load.
- **Examples:** AWS CloudFront, Azure CDN, GCP Cloud CDN, Akamai, Cloudflare.


<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Durability from Replication

**Problem Statement:** A cloud object store replicates every object across **$n = 3$** independent data centers. Each data center has an annual failure probability of $p = 0.001$ (i.e., 0.1 % chance of losing a copy). What is the probability that **all replicas** are simultaneously lost?

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Probability that a single replica survives:**
A single replica survives if the data center does NOT fail. Therefore:

$$
P_{\text{survive}} = 1 - p
$$

Substituting $p = 0.001$:

$$
P_{\text{survive}} = 1 - 0.001 = 0.999
$$

**Step 2 — Probability that ALL $n$ replicas survive independently:**

Since the data centers fail independently (no common-mode failure considered), the joint survival probability is:

$$
P_{\text{all survive}} = (1 - p)^{n}
$$

Substituting $n = 3$:

$$
P_{\text{all survive}} = (0.999)^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation:**
Expanding the cube:

$$
P_{\text{all survive}} = (0.999)^{3} = 0.999 \times 0.999 \times 0.999
$$

Multiplying step by step:

$$
P_{\text{all survive}} = 0.998001 \times 0.999 = 0.997002999
$$

**Step 4 — Probability of TOTAL data loss (all 3 replicas fail simultaneously):**

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = 1 - P_{\text{all survive}} = 1 - 0.997002999
$$

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = 0.002997001 \approx 0.003 \text{ (or 0.3 %)}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to "nines" notation (Durability):**

$$
D = 1 - P_{\text{loss}} = 0.997002999
$$

This is only **2.97 nines**, far below the cloud standard of **11 nines**. To reach 11 nines, replication must increase to **$n = 6$ or use erasure coding**.

**Verification for $n = 6$:**

$$
P_{\text{all survive}} = (0.999)^{6}
$$

Using logarithms $\ln(0.999) \approx -0.0010005$:

$$
\ln(P) = 6 \times (-0.0010005) = -0.006003
$$

$$
P_{\text{all survive}} = e^{-0.006003} \approx 0.99398
$$

This still gives only ~2.99 nines, proving that **simple replication cannot achieve 11 nines for high $p$**. Industry instead uses **erasure coding (Reed-Solomon)** + multi-region replication.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Storage Capacity with RAID

**Problem:** A cloud provider configures an **EBS-backed block store** using **8 disks of 1 TB each** in a **RAID-5** array (striping with single parity). What is the **effective usable capacity**?

**Step 1 — Identify RAID-5 efficiency:**

For RAID-5 with $N$ disks, usable capacity = $(N-1)$ disks worth of space because 1 disk is parity:

$$
\eta_{\text{RAID-5}} = \dfrac{N-1}{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute raw capacity:**

$$
C_{\text{raw}} = N_{\text{disks}} \times S_{\text{disk}} = 8 \times 1\ \text{TB} = 8\ \text{TB}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply RAID efficiency:**

$$
C_{\text{effective}} = C_{\text{raw}} \times \eta_{\text{RAID-5}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{effective}} = 8\ \text{TB} \times \dfrac{7}{8} = 7\ \text{TB}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for filesystem overhead (~5 %):**

$$
C_{\text{usable}} = 7\ \text{TB} \times 0.95 = 6.65\ \text{TB}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Monthly Cost Calculation (Composite Bill)

**Problem:** A startup stores **5 TB** in S3 Standard for 30 days, makes **10 million PUT requests** and **50 million GET requests**. Compute the monthly bill. Assume (illustrative rates):

- Storage: **\$0.023 per GB-month**
- PUT: **\$0.005 per 1,000 requests**
- GET: **\$0.0004 per 1,000 requests**

**Step 1 — Storage cost:**

Convert 5 TB to GB: $5 \times 1024 = 5120\ \text{GB}$.

$$
C_{\text{storage}} = 5120\ \text{GB} \times 0.023\ \$/\text{GB} = \$117.76
$$

**Step 2 — PUT request cost:**

10,000,000 / 1,000 = 10,000 units.

$$
C_{\text{PUT}} = 10{,}000 \times 0.005 = \$50.00
$$

**Step 3 — GET request cost:**

50,000,000 / 1,000 = 50,000 units.

$$
C_{\text{GET}} = 50{,}000 \times 0.0004 = \$20.00
$$

**Step 4 — Total monthly bill:**

$$
C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{storage}} + C_{\text{PUT}} + C_{\text{GET}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 117.76 + 50.00 + 20.00 = \$187.76
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — IOPS-to-Throughput Conversion

**Problem:** A gp3 EBS volume provides **3,000 IOPS** with a block size of **16 KB**. Compute throughput.

**Step 1 — Block size in MB:**

$$
B_{\text{block}} = \dfrac{16\ \text{KB}}{1024} = 0.015625\ \text{MB}
$$

**Step 2 — Throughput formula:**

$$
T = \text{IOPS} \times B_{\text{block}}
$$

$$
T = 3{,}000 \times 0.015625\ \text{MB/s}
$$

$$
T = 46.875\ \text{MB/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Mbps:**

$$
T_{\text{Mbps}} = 46.875 \times 8 = 375\ \text{Mbps}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Which Storage to Choose?

| Workload | Recommended Storage | Reason |
|----------|--------------------|--------|
| MySQL database on a single VM | **Block (EBS)** | Needs low-latency random writes |
| Static website images | **Object (S3)** + **CDN** | Cheap, infinite scale, cacheable |
| Shared home directories for HPC cluster | **File (EFS/Lustre)** | POSIX, parallel, multi-client |
| 7-year compliance archive | **Cold Object (Glacier Deep Archive)** | Cheapest per-GB |
| Boot disk of a VM | **Block (EBS)** | OS requires raw block device |
| Video streaming (Netflix-scale) | **Object (S3) + CloudFront CDN** | High throughput, edge caching |
| Big-data lake (Spark/Hive input) | **Object (S3) with Hadoop-compatible layer** | Decouples compute & storage |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between Object Storage and Block Storage. Give one real-world cloud example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criteria | Object Storage | Block Storage |
|----------|----------------|---------------|
| Data Unit | Whole object (file + metadata) | Fixed-size blocks (4 KB – 16 MB) |
| Access Protocol | REST API (HTTP `PUT/GET/DELETE`) | iSCSI / NVMe-oF (mounted as raw disk) |
| Use Case | Unstructured data, backups, media | Databases, OS boot disks |
| Example | **AWS S3** | **AWS EBS** |

Object storage treats data as discrete units in a flat namespace, optimized for **scale and durability (11 nines)** but not for random writes. Block storage exposes raw volumes to a VM for **low-latency, transactional workloads**.

*(Award 1 mark for correct definition, 1 mark for the table comparison, 1 mark for valid cloud examples.)*

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is "Eventual Consistency" in cloud object stores? Why is it used instead of Strong Consistency?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Eventual Consistency** is a consistency model where, after a write, all replicas of an object **converge to the same value within a short, bounded time** (typically < 1 second), but a read immediately following the write may return the **stale value** for some replicas.

**Why used instead of Strong Consistency?**
1. **Availability & Partition tolerance (CAP theorem):** Strong consistency requires a quorum on every write, which becomes impossible during network partitions. Eventual consistency keeps the service available.
2. **Lower write latency** — no synchronous replication across regions.
3. **Higher throughput** — writes are acknowledged as soon as one replica persists.

AWS S3 uses read-after-write consistency for new objects since 2020, but **updates and deletes still exhibit eventual consistency**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three categories of cloud storage** (Object, Block, File) with diagrams. For each, mention one typical AWS service and one ideal workload.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A company stores **20 TB** of archival data in **AWS S3 Glacier Deep Archive**. Compute the **annual storage cost** if the rate is **\$0.00099 per GB-month**. If retrieval incurs **\$0.02 per GB**, what is the cost to restore the entire 20 TB once?

#### (a) Model Solution [7 marks]

**1. Object Storage (2 marks):**
Data is stored as **objects** in a **flat bucket** with a unique key + metadata. Accessed over **REST/HTTPS**.
- *AWS Service:* **S3 (Simple Storage Service)**
- *Workload:* Static website assets, video backups, log archive.

**2. Block Storage (2 marks):**
Data is split into **fixed-size blocks** and exposed as a **raw disk** to a single VM.
- *AWS Service:* **EBS (Elastic Block Store)**
- *Workload:* Relational databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL), OS boot volumes.

**3. File Storage (2 marks):**
A **POSIX-compliant shared file system** mounted by multiple clients via NFS.
- *AWS Service:* **EFS (Elastic File System)** for Linux, **FSx for Windows** for SMB.
- *Workload:* Content management, shared home directories, HPC scratch.

**4. Comparative Diagram (1 mark):**
A simple 3-column table contrasting access method, attachment, and protocol as shown in SECTION 2 must be reproduced.

#### (b) Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Convert TB to GB (1 mark):**

$$
20\ \text{TB} = 20 \times 1024\ \text{GB} = 20{,}480\ \text{GB}
$$

**Step 2 — Monthly storage cost (2 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{monthly}} = 20{,}480 \times 0.00099 = \$20.2752
$$

**Step 3 — Annual storage cost (2 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{annual}} = C_{\text{monthly}} \times 12 = 20.2752 \times 12 = \$243.30
$$

**Step 4 — One-time retrieval cost (2 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{retrieval}} = 20{,}480 \times 0.02 = \$409.60
$$

**Final Answer (in bold for emphasis):**
**Annual Storage Cost ≈ \$243.30**, **One-time Retrieval Cost ≈ \$409.60**, **Total ≈ \$652.90.**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss **Replication** and **Erasure Coding** as redundancy strategies in cloud storage. Compare their **storage overhead** and **failure tolerance** with an example of $(k=4, m=2)$ Reed-Solomon coding vs **3-way replication**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cloud block-storage service uses **RAID-6 across 10 disks of 2 TB each**. Calculate the **effective usable capacity** and the **maximum number of simultaneous disk failures** the array can survive.

#### (a) Model Solution [7 marks]

**Replication (2 marks):**
Each object is copied $R$ times (typically $R=3$) across independent failure domains (Availability Zones).
- **Overhead:** $R \times$ original size → **3× overhead for 3-way replication**.
- **Failure Tolerance:** Can survive $R - 1$ failures (i.e., 2 in 3-way replication).

**Erasure Coding (3 marks):**
Data is split into $k$ data shards + $m$ parity shards. Original data can be reconstructed from any $k$ of the $k+m$ shards.
- **Overhead:** $\dfrac{k+m}{k}$ → for $(k=4, m=2)$, overhead $= 6/4 = 1.5\times$.
- **Failure Tolerance:** Can survive any $m$ simultaneous failures (2 in this case).

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | 3-way Replication | Erasure Coding (4,2) |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Storage Overhead | **300 %** (3× raw data) | **150 %** (1.5× raw data) |
| Failure Tolerance | 2 disks/zones | 2 shards |
| Read Latency | Fast (read from nearest replica) | Slower (decode required) |
| Best For | Hot data, small objects | Cold data, large objects (e.g., 100 MB+) |

#### (b) Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — RAID-6 efficiency formula (2 marks):**

For RAID-6 with $N$ disks, **2 disks are parity**:

$$
C_{\text{effective}} = (N - 2) \times S_{\text{disk}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (2 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{effective}} = (10 - 2) \times 2\ \text{TB} = 8 \times 2 = 16\ \text{TB}
$$

**Step 3 — Failure tolerance (2 marks):**

RAID-6 can survive **simultaneous failure of any 2 disks** without data loss.

**Step 4 — Final answer (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{effective}} = 16\ \text{TB},\quad \text{Tolerable Failures} = 2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In Part A, students often confuse **Block** and **File** storage. Remember: *Block = raw disk to one VM*, *File = shared folder to many VMs*.
> 2. In RAID numericals, **forgetting to subtract parity disks** is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks. Always state the formula before substituting.
> 3. When asked about *erasure coding*, **explicitly mention the decode/reconstruction latency** as a drawback — many students only state the storage savings and lose a mark.
> 4. In cost-calculation questions, **do not forget retrieval fees** for cold/archive tiers. Examiners frequently hide a "restore" sub-question worth 2 marks.
> 5. Avoid writing "S3 is free" or "cloud storage has unlimited capacity" — these statements lose accuracy marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Three storage types:** Object (S3), Block (EBS), File (EFS) — know AWS, Azure, and GCP equivalents.
- [ ] **Object storage** = flat namespace, REST API, 11 nines durability, 5 TB max object size.
- [ ] **Block storage** = raw volume to one VM, IOPS-bound, snapshots are incremental.
- [ ] **File storage** = POSIX-compliant, multi-client, NFS/SMB protocol, used for legacy enterprise apps.
- [ ] **Consistency models:** Strong, Read-After-Write, Eventual, Causal — S3 uses RAWR for new objects.
- [ ] **Storage tiers:** Hot, Cool/IA, Cold/Archive — each with different retrieval latency and minimum-storage-day penalties.
- [ ] **Replication factor** default = **3** across **3 AZs**; Erasure Coding (e.g., RS 6+3) used for cold data to reduce overhead.
- [ ] **Durability formula:** $D = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$ — must understand for numericals.
- [ ] **Availability formula:** $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ and annual downtime in minutes.
- [ ] **RAID efficiency:** RAID-0 = 100 %, RAID-1 = 50 %, RAID-5 = $(N-1)/N$, RAID-6 = $(N-2)/N$.
- [ ] **IOPS-to-Throughput:** $T = \text{IOPS} \times B_{\text{block}}$ in MB/s.
- [ ] **Cost formula:** $C = (S_{\text{GB}} \times P_{\text{storage}}) + (N_{\text{req}} \times P_{\text{req}}) + C_{\text{retrieval}}$.
- [ ] **CDN** = distributed cache layer in front of origin storage; metric = **Cache Hit Ratio**.
- [ ] **RTO vs RPO:** RTO = how fast you recover; RPO = how much data you can afford to lose.
- [ ] **Lifecycle policies** automate transitions Standard → IA → Glacier → Deep Archive → Delete.
- [ ] Always **convert TB → GB by × 1024**, not × 1000, in KTU numericals (1 TB = 1024 GB).
